Removing requirements that companies show how their AI systems work and what problems they have means my doctors could be following recommendations from something nobody's properly tested, and if something goes wrong, who's accountable? It can't just be me and my family taking the risk.‚Źé‚Źé**MY REQUEST**‚Źé‚ŹéPlease don't remove the transparency requirements for AI model cards and testing. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0002‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒJessica Lewis‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒWhile improved interoperability between healthcare providers is necessary, removing transparency requirements for AI systems used in patient care would be dangerous, as healthcare workers and patients need to understand how algorithms work and hol...‚ôĒMy work as a home health aide puts me in the middle of a real problem. Last month I spent two hours on the phone trying to get Mrs. Chen's cardiologist to send her records to her new primary care doctor. They kept saying the system wouldn't let them, and I'm fairly sure that was just an excuse. If information could move between doctors the way it should, I wouldn't have to chase people down constantly. But here's what worries me about loosening these rules: if companies don't have to explain how their AI systems work before they use them to make decisions about patient care, how am I supposed to know if the system is giving Mrs. Chen bad advice? Right now I can at least ask questions and push back. If we remove the requirement for companies to be transparent about how their algorithms work, there's nothing to hold them accountable when something goes wrong. I've already seen how hard it is to get basic information out of healthcare. Weakening transparency requirements will just make it easier for companies to hide what they're doing with our data. Patients and the people caring for them deserve to understand how AI is being used in their healthcare before doctors rely on it.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-02-01T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-01T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒmedium‚ôĒangry‚ôĒFlorida‚ôĒhome health aide‚ôĒ67‚ôĒLast month I was helping Mrs. Chen manage her medications after her hospital stay, and we spent two hours on the phone trying to get her cardiologist's office to send her records to her new primary care doctor‚ÄĒthey kept saying the system wouldn't let them, which I'm pretty sure was just them being difficult. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0003‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDonald Wilson‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA retired nurse with three decades of healthcare experience argues that proposed regulatory changes removing AI model transparency requirements for clinical decision support systems would compromise patient safety and should be retained, as these ...‚ôĒ**PROTECTING PATIENT SAFETY WHILE EXPANDING DATA ACCESS: THE CRITICAL NEED TO RETAIN AI MODEL TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéAfter thirty years in nursing, I learned that the best clinical decisions happen when providers have complete information and know exactly what they're working with. That lesson stayed with me long after I retired, and it's why I'm writing today about the proposed changes to Decision Support Interventions certification criteria. The tension between removing regulatory burden and maintaining patient safety is real, and I don't think we should resolve it by abandoning transparency requirements for AI systems that directly influence patient care.‚Źé‚ŹéMy daughter's cancer diagnosis last year forced me to confront something I'd understood intellectually but never experienced as a patient's family member. When she transferred oncology centers, I spent weeks reconstructing her pathology reports, imaging results, and treatment notes. I knew exactly how those records should flow between systems because I'd helped create documentation protocols during my career. Yet even with my background, getting access was frustratingly slow. The experience made me passionate about data portability and interoperability. Those things matter enormously. But it also reminded me why we built safeguards in the first place.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PROBLEM WITH REMOVING AI TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed deregulatory action would remove the certification criterion requiring Decision Support Interventions to include model cards and transparency disclosures. These disclosures document the intended use of an AI system, the target population it was designed for, known risks, characteristics of the training data, and results from external validation testing. On paper, this sounds like bureaucratic overhead. In practice, it's the difference between clinicians knowing what they're trusting and flying blind.‚Źé‚ŹéI worry about what happens when a clinician uses an AI tool without understanding its limitations. Consider an AI system trained primarily on patient populations from academic medical centers with specific demographic characteristics. If that system gets deployed in a rural clinic serving a different population, does the clinician know that external validation data might not exist for their actual patients? If the model was trained on historical data that reflects past diagnostic biases, will anyone know to watch for that? These aren't theoretical concerns. They're the kinds of gaps that lead to misdiagnosis, treatment errors, and harm.‚Źé‚ŹéThe model card requirement isn't busywork. It's a structured way of forcing developers to actually think through what their system does and doesn't do well. When I worked in nursing, we had protocols that seemed tedious until something went wrong. Then those protocols were the only thing standing between a patient and a bad outcome. Transparency disclosures function similarly. They slow deployment slightly, but they create accountability and ensure informed clinical decision-making.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT DATA PORTABILITY GAINS WE MIGHT LOSE**‚Źé‚ŹéI understand the argument that certification requirements create barriers for smaller developers and slow innovation. The costs are real, and they do burden companies trying to bring new tools to market. But there's a difference between removing unnecessary red tape and removing safeguards that actually protect patients. The proposed changes seem to conflate these two things.‚Źé‚ŹéThe irony is that removing AI transparency requirements doesn't actually speed up data portability or interoperability. Those are separate challenges. A clinician can access a patient's records quickly and still use a poorly understood AI tool to interpret them. We could achieve data portability goals without gutting AI oversight. Instead, the current proposal treats transparency as something to be shed rather than something to be streamlined.‚Źé‚ŹéI keep coming back to my daughter's experience. What she needed wasn't less oversight of her clinical care. She needed faster access to her records and easier communication between her providers. Those goals are compatible with requiring AI systems to disclose their limitations and validation status. They're not the same issue.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PATIENT SAFETY ARGUMENT**‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what concerns me most: patients can't protect themselves from AI systems they don't know are being used. When a clinician orders a test or recommends a treatment, the patient rarely knows whether that decision was influenced by an algorithm. If that algorithm was trained on biased data or designed for a population very different from the patient, the patient has no way of knowing. They're trusting their doctor, which is appropriate, but their doctor might not fully understand the tool they're using either.‚Źé‚ŹéThe FDA regulates medical devices with varying levels of intensity depending on risk. The rationale is straightforward: higher-risk devices need more scrutiny. Decision Support Interventions that directly influence clinical recommendations seem like they warrant at least the level of transparency we require for other clinical tools. A model card isn't invasive regulation. It's a basic disclosure document, similar to what pharmaceutical companies provide with medication packaging.‚Źé‚ŹéAccording to the American Medical Association's guidance on AI in healthcare, clinical decision support systems should "be transparent about capabilities and limitations and undergo appropriate validation before clinical use." The proposed removal of certification requirements contradicts that principle. We're talking about systems that influence treatment decisions for cancer patients, cardiac patients, and others facing serious diagnoses. Those systems deserve transparency requirements.‚Źé‚Źé**BALANCING INNOVATION AND ACCOUNTABILITY**‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not arguing for stasis. Innovation matters. Faster deployment of useful tools matters. But there's a difference between removing barriers to good innovation and removing safeguards against bad innovation. The certification criterion for AI model transparency doesn't prevent innovation. It channels innovation toward systems that are actually validated and well-understood.‚Źé‚ŹéCompanies developing genuinely innovative Decision Support Interventions should be able to document what their systems do. If they can't explain their own tools clearly enough to create a model card, that's information patients and clinicians deserve to have. If they can explain them, the documentation burden is manageable.‚Źé‚ŹéThe real innovation happens when clinicians trust the tools they're using because they understand them. Right now, many clinicians are skeptical of AI systems precisely because they don't have clear information about how those systems were developed and validated. Removing transparency requirements won't increase that trust. It will decrease it.‚Źé‚Źé**RECOMMENDATION**‚Źé‚ŹéI urge the Office of the Secretary and ONC to retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures. Streamline the documentation process if necessary. Make the requirements more flexible for smaller developers. But don't eliminate the core requirement that AI systems used in clinical care be transparent about their intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results.‚Źé‚ŹéData portability and interoperability are important goals, and I support efforts to achieve them. But those goals are separate from AI oversight. We can expand patient access to records and require transparency in clinical decision support systems simultaneously. The two changes address different problems. Removing AI transparency requirements doesn't advance data portability. It just removes one layer of accountability from tools that directly influence patient care.‚Źé‚ŹéAfter three decades in healthcare, I've learned that the safeguards we build aren't obstacles to good care. They're the foundation of it.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-16T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-16T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ3‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving DSI transparency requirements creates a dangerous regulatory gap where AI-based clinical decision support tools face fewer disclosure obligations than traditional medical devices, undermining consistent safety standards.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒsupportive‚ôĒMichigan‚ôĒretired nurse‚ôĒ51‚ôĒWhen my daughter switched to a different oncology center last year after her diagnosis, I spent weeks chasing down her pathology reports and imaging results‚ÄĒrecords I'd helped document during my thirty years in nursing, yet couldn't access directly myself. The frustration of that experience made me realize how critical seamless access to medical records really is, but I also remember the careful protocols we maintained to protect patient privacy, and I worry about striking the right balance between portability and safeguards.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1145
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0004‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒAndrew Miller‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe commenter opposes removing transparency and certification requirements for AI systems in healthcare, arguing that patients and providers have a right to understand how clinical decision-support tools work, and that real-world validation protec...‚ôĒWhen my daughter was diagnosed with juvenile arthritis last year, I spent three weeks calling between her pediatrician's office, the rheumatology clinic, and our insurance company. Nobody's system talked to each other. I was managing the medical equivalent of a disorganized filing cabinet while my kid was in pain. Since then, I've thought a lot about what would have made that experience better, and I've also thought about what could go wrong if we removed the guardrails that are supposed to keep patient information safe.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PROBLEM WITH STRIPPING ACCOUNTABILITY FROM AI SYSTEMS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule wants to remove certification criteria and transparency requirements for health IT systems, including those that use artificial intelligence to make or support clinical decisions. I understand the argument. Developers say these requirements slow them down. But I've learned something from that experience with my daughter: the systems we use to manage people's health need to be transparent. Not because it's nice to have, but because patients and doctors have a right to know what's actually happening.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what concerns me. If we remove model cards and transparency disclosures from AI systems that help doctors diagnose or treat patients, we're saying that it's okay for a doctor to use a tool without knowing how it works. That doesn't make sense. Before my daughter's rheumatologist prescribed her medication, they explained what it does, what the side effects could be, and why they thought it was the right choice. We're expected to understand the tools that affect our bodies. Why should AI systems be different?‚Źé‚ŹéThe FDA's 2021 guidance on AI and machine learning software as a medical device specifically emphasized that transparency in algorithm design is essential for safe implementation in clinical settings. The guidance didn't say transparency is optional or that developers should pick and choose which parts to follow. It said transparency is foundational. Removing certification criteria that enforce this transparency doesn't streamline innovation. It creates a two-tier system where patients with resources and time can demand answers while vulnerable populations get opaque systems imposed on them.‚Źé‚Źé**REAL-WORLD VALIDATION MATTERS MORE THAN LESS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe comment period is also asking whether real-world testing requirements should be modified or eliminated. I'd argue the opposite. They should be stronger.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen my daughter's lab work finally got coordinated between providers, the rheumatologist could track her inflammation markers over time and see whether the treatment was actually working. That required data consistency and real documentation of what happened. In healthcare, the stakes are someone's health. Real-world testing isn't bureaucratic overhead. It's the only way to know whether a system actually works in the messy reality of a clinic, not just in controlled conditions.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider what happens when an AI system that helps flag potential drug interactions or diagnoses a condition is deployed without rigorous testing. A doctor in a rural clinic uses it because it's built into their electronic health record. The system makes a mistake. A patient is harmed. Now, what's the paper trail? If we've removed certification criteria and validation requirements, the provider and the developer both lack documented evidence that they did their due diligence. That doesn't protect anyone. It exposes both the doctor and the patient to liability and harm with no accountability on either end.‚Źé‚ŹéThe American Medical Association has stated that physicians bear responsibility for understanding clinical decision support tools they use, even if they didn't design them. That responsibility is meaningless if the tools themselves aren't validated and documented. Removing the requirement for model cards and validation evidence doesn't make this easier for doctors. It makes it harder. It puts them in the position of having to defend clinical decisions made with tools they can't fully explain.‚Źé‚Źé**THE VULNERABILITY ANGLE**‚Źé‚ŹéWhat keeps me up at night isn't just my daughter's case. It's thinking about families with fewer resources than mine, families that don't have time to call three offices and coordinate care themselves. When systems are opaque, those families are the ones who suffer first. They don't have lawyers or the time to demand answers. They accept what they're told.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Institute of Medicine's work on patient safety culture shows that accountability mechanisms, including documentation and transparency requirements, actually protect vulnerable populations. When there's a clear record of how a decision was made and what evidence supported it, there's a way to catch problems and fix them. When there isn't, problems hide until someone gets hurt.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not saying developers should have to jump through pointless hoops. There's probably some duplication in the current system that could be streamlined. But here's what I'd suggest instead of wholesale removal:‚Źé‚Źé- Review certification criteria to eliminate genuine duplication, but keep requirements that address patient safety and provider accountability‚Źé- Streamline the process for demonstrating real-world validation without lowering the standard for what counts as evidence‚Źé- Create a fast-track pathway for lower-risk tools while maintaining full transparency requirements for AI systems that inform diagnosis or treatment decisions‚Źé- Allow developers to submit model cards in standard formats that reduce documentation burden while maintaining meaningful disclosure‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT BALANCE ACTUALLY LOOKS LIKE**‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not opposed to innovation or to making it easier for developers to bring good tools to market. But I've learned that healthcare systems require coordination and transparency to work safely. Removing accountability doesn't speed up innovation. It just shifts the risk onto patients and providers who have no way to know what they're using or why it failed.‚Źé‚ŹéA Journal of Medical Internet Research study from 2023 found that patient trust in AI-assisted diagnosis increased significantly when transparency disclosures were present compared to when they were absent. Transparency doesn't slow adoption. It builds the trust that makes adoption sustainable.‚Źé‚ŹéMy daughter is doing better now. Her inflammation markers are stable. But we only know that because her providers could access consistent information and explain what they were seeing. That kind of clarity and accountability should be the baseline for any system we use to manage people's health, including AI systems. Streamline the process, yes. Eliminate accountability, no.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-26T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-26T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ3‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒsupportive‚ôĒPennsylvania‚ôĒstay-at-home parent‚ôĒ35‚ôĒWhen my daughter was diagnosed with juvenile arthritis last year, I spent three weeks calling between her pediatrician's office, the rheumatology clinic, and our insurance company trying to get her lab results in one place so we could actually track her inflammation markers over time‚ÄĒnobody's system talked to each other, and I felt like I was managing the medical equivalent of a disorganized filing cabinet while my kid was in pain. I've thought a lot since then about how differently that experience could have gone if I could have just pulled her records myself and shared them directly with the specialists, but I also wonder what happens to all that sensitive information once it's floating around in someone's patient portal.‚ôĒModel cards and transparency disclosures are not bureaucratic overhead‚ÄĒthey're the patient's right to understand what's being used to make decisions about their health, just as they have a right to know a medication's side effects before taking it. | Removing accountability mechanisms like AI model cards doesn't speed up innovation; it creates a two-tier system where patients with resources can demand answers while vulnerable populations get opaque systems imposed on them. | Healthcare providers need documented validation evidence to defend clinical decisions and protect themselves legally‚ÄĒstripping certification criteria removes the paper trail that protects both doctors and patients when something goes wrong.‚ôĒA parent who learned the hard way that medical systems require transparency and coordination to work safely argues that AI systems deserve the same scrutiny we demand from any tool touching our children's health.‚ôĒFDA guidance on AI/ML-based Software as a Medical Device (2021) emphasizing transparency in algorithm design | Institute of Medicine report on patient safety culture and the role of system accountability | American Medical Association position statement on physician responsibility for understanding clinical decision support tools | Journal of Medical Internet Research study on patient trust in AI-assisted diagnosis when transparency disclosures are present vs. absent‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1004
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0005‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒLisa Rodriguez‚ôĒNo Delays, No Surprises Coalition‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe No Delays, No Surprises Coalition opposes the proposed elimination of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements for model cards and transparency disclosures, arguing that these baseline standards are essential for patient...‚ôĒNo Delays, No Surprises Coalition submits these comments regarding the proposed deregulation of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria under the ASTP/ONC Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability initiative.‚Źé‚ŹéOur organization works directly with community health centers, safety-net providers, and patient advocacy networks across Pennsylvania and neighboring states. Over the past three years, we have conducted extensive outreach to understand how clinical decision support tools affect patient care and provider accountability. What we learned in those eighteen months of community engagement in West Philadelphia‚ÄĒand what we continue to hear from our network‚ÄĒis that patients want to know what's driving clinical decisions that affect their care. They want transparency. They want to understand risk. When a provider tells them an AI system recommended a particular treatment path, patients ask basic questions: Was this tested on people like me? What could go wrong? Who's responsible if something goes wrong? These are not unreasonable demands, and they are exactly the questions that the current DSI model card and transparency disclosure requirements were designed to answer.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule would eliminate certification requirements for model cards and transparency disclosures, including documentation of intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results (45 CFR 170.102(b)). The rationale offered is burden reduction and market acceleration. We understand the impulse. We do. But we have watched what happens when transparency requirements disappear or shift. Last year, our organization spent months working with a West Philadelphia health center to gather patient feedback on appointment wait times and care quality. We built real trust with that community. When we tried to access data to show our members what we had learned, we ran into privacy framework changes that made it impossible to share findings. The rules kept shifting. We had nothing to show for the work except broken promises to patients who had been willing to open up about their experiences. That experience taught us something hard: once you lose the ability to track what's happening in a system, it's nearly impossible to rebuild that transparency. The same principle applies here.‚Źé‚ŹéDecision support tools operate differently than traditional software because they make recommendations that directly shape clinical judgment. This is documented extensively in the medical literature. Research on clinical decision support implementation (as referenced in the 2015 RAND Corporation evaluation of EHR-integrated decision support) and subsequent analyses of AI-assisted diagnosis (see peer-reviewed work on algorithm auditing in healthcare settings) consistently shows that the absence of clear documentation about model performance, training data composition, and intended populations creates serious risks. When providers cannot articulate what a tool was designed to do or what population it was validated on, they cannot make informed decisions about when to use it and when to override it. That gap between tool capability and provider understanding is where errors happen.‚Źé‚ŹéWe are not arguing against innovation or market competition. We are arguing that the current DSI certification criteria represent a baseline for patient safety and clinical accountability, not a barrier to innovation. Developers who have built robust, well-validated tools have the documentation to support certification requirements. Requiring model cards and transparency disclosures does not prevent market entry; it establishes what good practice looks like.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) Privacy Rule and Security Rule (45 CFR Parts 160 and 164) set baseline standards for health information protection, yet innovation has continued. The information blocking regulations under 45 CFR Part 171 require transparency about access denials; they have not halted health IT development. The Cures Act final rule certification criteria (21st Century Cures Act, Pub. L. 114-255) themselves represent a negotiated balance between flexibility and accountability. Removing the DSI model card requirement tilts that balance too far toward developer discretion and away from patient protection.‚Źé‚ŹéOur coalition recommends that the agency retain the current DSI certification criterion requiring model cards and transparency disclosures. This is a reasonable, achievable standard that enables both innovation and accountability. We have the evidence from our own community work: patients expect transparency, providers need it, and the system functions better when it exists.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-19T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-19T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒresigned‚ôĒPennsylvania‚ôĒexecutive director‚ôĒ37‚ôĒMy organization spent eighteen months building trust with a community health center in West Philadelphia to get their patient feedback on appointment wait times, only to have half our data requests denied last year because the privacy frameworks kept shifting‚ÄĒwe couldn't even tell our members what we'd found. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0006‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒBarbara Gonzalez‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe commenter argues that loosening transparency and explainability requirements for medical AI systems risks enabling clinics to operate with less accountability rather than improving patient care, and emphasizes that existing rules are already i...‚ôĒI spent three weeks last year trying to get my mom's medical records transferred between cardiologists. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0007‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDaniel Nguyen‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe commenter opposes removing Decision Support Interventions certification criteria, arguing that transparency requirements like AI model cards and disclosure standards are essential safeguards to ensure clinicians understand how AI tools were de...‚ôĒ**COMMENT ON PROPOSED REMOVAL OF DECISION SUPPORT INTERVENTIONS CERTIFICATION CRITERIA**‚Źé‚ŹéMy experience last year getting my medical records transferred between cardiologists opened my eyes to how broken our system is, but it also taught me something important: easier access without safeguards isn't the answer. When I finally got my twenty years of test results, I had no way to know if the data was accurate, complete, or what it had been used for. My daughter, who works in IT, explained that if we're going to let patients and providers move health information around more freely, we need stronger transparency requirements, not weaker ones.‚Źé‚ŹéI understand the goal of reducing regulatory burden on health IT developers. But the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria, particularly the requirement for AI model cards and transparency disclosures, concerns me for specific reasons:‚Źé‚Źé* Model cards serve a real function. They document intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. Without these, clinicians have no way to know if an AI tool was designed for someone like me, with my specific conditions and medication history.‚Źé‚Źé* I've read that some DSI tools are already in use in clinical settings. If we remove certification requirements now, there's no consistent baseline for how those tools were developed or tested. My cardiologist won't know whether the AI recommendation he's looking at has been validated in older patients or just in younger populations.‚Źé‚Źé* Transparency disclosures aren't just about patient safety. They're about accountability. If something goes wrong, if an AI tool makes a dangerous recommendation, there needs to be a way to trace back to what the developers knew and when they knew it.‚Źé‚ŹéThe CMS-ONC rule language on information blocking (45 CFR 171.102) already creates real risks around data sharing. Removing the certification criteria for DSI tools makes that worse, not better. You're essentially saying that patient access to information is more important than understanding how that information might be used against them in an automated decision.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not opposed to innovation. What I'm asking for is that before we strip away transparency requirements, we think about what happens on the other side of the handoff. When my data moves to a new provider, I want to know that any AI tool touching that data has been built responsibly and that someone is accountable if it hasn't. That's not burden. That's basic protection.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-20T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-20T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒsupportive‚ôĒWyoming‚ôĒadministrative assistant‚ôĒ75‚ôĒLast year when my cardiologist retired, I spent three weeks calling between his office and my new doctor trying to get my twenty years of test results transferred‚ÄĒturns out they were locked in a system I couldn't access myself. My daughter, who works in IT, was shocked that I couldn't just download my own records with a password, and she helped me understand how much easier this should be. Now I'm worried that opening up access without real guardrails might mean my sensitive health information could end up in the wrong hands just as easily as it gets shared with the right ones.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ404
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0008‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒThomas Rivera‚ôĒPolicy Center for Maternal Mental Health‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health opposes the removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements for AI model cards and transparency disclosures, arguing that eliminating these safeguards would jeopardize patient...‚ôĒPolicy Center for Maternal Mental Health‚ŹéComments on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity‚Źé‚ŹéThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health represents researchers, clinicians, and patient advocates working across perinatal mental health care delivery. Our organization has spent the last six years documenting how fragmented health information systems affect maternal mental health outcomes, and we have watched with real concern as proposals to streamline certification criteria threaten to undo critical safeguards that pregnant and postpartum patients depend on. This comment addresses the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria, specifically the requirement for AI model cards and transparency disclosures. We believe this particular deregulatory action carries risks that extend far beyond administrative burden and directly jeopardizes patient safety in a vulnerable population.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THIS MATTERS IN MATERNAL MENTAL HEALTH**‚Źé‚ŹéThe postpartum period is a window of intense vulnerability. A woman's psychiatric history, medication regimen, family dynamics, and trauma exposure‚ÄĒthe very data that AI systems would process to support clinical decision-making‚ÄĒcan determine whether she receives appropriate screening, treatment, and follow-up care. The stakes are not abstract. We have documented cases where maternal mental health information moved between providers without clear understanding of what data was being shared, under what conditions, or for what purposes. In one case from our 2023 outreach with Nebraska maternal health providers, a patient's detailed psychiatric history from a previous suicide attempt was flagged by an AI-assisted screening tool during her postpartum visit, but the clinical team had no way to understand how that flag was generated, whether the model had been validated in postpartum populations, or what training data had shaped its risk assessment. The provider made a clinical decision based on the tool's output while operating essentially blind to its reasoning.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not a hypothetical concern. It happened. And the provider later told us they felt caught between trusting the system's recommendation and their own clinical instinct, which was actually more cautious than the algorithm suggested. That tension between deferring to an automated system and exercising human judgment becomes impossible to navigate when clinicians cannot see inside the black box.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal to eliminate the DSI certification criterion that requires AI model cards and transparency disclosures would remove one of the few mechanisms we have to ensure that systems making recommendations in maternal mental health are actually safe, actually validated, and actually trustworthy to the patients whose data feeds them. Regulatory deregulation in this context is not about lifting unnecessary burden. It is about removing visibility into systems that will shape how millions of pregnant and postpartum women are screened, treated, and monitored.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CURRENT CERTIFICATION REQUIREMENT AND WHAT IT ACTUALLY DOES**‚Źé‚ŹéUnder 45 CFR 170.315(a)(8), the current DSI certification criterion requires developers to provide:‚Źé‚Źé1. Intended use statements that describe what clinical conditions or decisions the system is designed to support‚Źé2. Target population specifications that identify which patients the system was developed and tested for‚Źé3. Known risks and limitations, including performance characteristics across different demographic groups‚Źé4. Training data characteristics, including data sources, timeframes, and potential biases‚Źé5. External validation evidence demonstrating that the system performs reliably when used outside the development environment‚Źé6. Information about how the system handles edge cases or unusual clinical presentations‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not burdensome paperwork. It is the absolute minimum documentation needed for a clinician to make an informed decision about whether to use a particular AI system for a particular patient in a particular clinical moment.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider what happens without these requirements. A maternal health clinic receives a new AI-assisted postpartum depression screening tool. The vendor promises it is "validated" and "evidence-based." The clinic implements it into their workflow. Six months later, a clinician notices that the system is flagging Asian American patients at significantly higher rates for severe depression than their white counterparts, even when clinical assessment shows comparable symptom severity. The clinic wants to understand why. But without documentation of training data characteristics, without transparency about the model's development process, without information about known performance disparities, the clinic has no way to investigate. They cannot contact the vendor with specifics because the vendor has not documented anything specific. They cannot adjust their clinical protocols because they do not know what adjustment would address the root problem. The patient affected cannot even request an explanation of why she was flagged, because the explanation does not exist in any documented form.‚Źé‚ŹéWe have heard variations of this story from maternal health providers across Nebraska and neighboring states. The systems they use are opaque. The vendors treat model documentation as proprietary information. And the patients most vulnerable to algorithmic bias‚ÄĒpatients from underrepresented racial and ethnic groups, patients with lower health literacy, patients with histories of trauma or mental illness‚ÄĒhave no way to know that they are being sorted through systems that may not have been tested on people like them.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification criterion creates a floor, not a ceiling. It ensures that before a system is certified as meeting a particular standard, someone has documented what the system does, who it was tested on, and what happens when it fails. Removing this requirement does not make healthcare more efficient. It makes it more dangerous.‚Źé‚Źé**TRANSPARENCY AND ACCOUNTABILITY IN CLINICAL DECISION SUPPORT**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal's rationale rests partly on the argument that certification burden slows innovation and that market competition will naturally filter out unreliable systems. This argument fails in the context of health information technology for several reasons.‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, clinical providers do not have the technical expertise to evaluate AI systems independently. A maternal health nurse or even an OB-GYN does not have the background to reverse-engineer a neural network or audit a training dataset. They rely on certification as a proxy for trustworthiness. Remove certification requirements, and you do not remove the need for trust. You simply shift the burden of vetting onto providers and patients who lack the tools to do the vetting. The result is not a free market. It is a market where information asymmetry favors vendors and disadvantages the clinicians and patients who will bear the consequences of system failure.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, competitive pressure does not naturally incentivize transparency. Vendors have financial incentives to keep their models proprietary, to avoid disclosing performance disparities, and to market broadly rather than honestly describe limitations. A vendor whose system performs poorly in Black patients has no competitive incentive to disclose this if they can avoid it. They have every incentive to obscure it. Certification requirements force disclosure. Market dynamics alone do not.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, the postpartum period is not a domain where experimentation with unproven systems is acceptable. Postpartum depression carries real risks. Maternal suicide is the leading cause of pregnancy-related death in the United States. Maternal psychosis, though rare, is a psychiatric emergency. The patients we serve do not have the luxury of being part of a learning marketplace where some systems fail and lessons are learned through failure. The failure of a screening tool could mean a woman in crisis does not get identified. The consequences are not a bad customer experience. They are preventable deaths.‚Źé‚ŹéThe 45 CFR 170.315(a)(8) requirement is not an innovation barrier. It is a safety floor. And it is a floor that maternal mental health absolutely requires.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT REMOVING THE REQUIREMENT WOULD MEAN IN PRACTICE**‚Źé‚ŹéWe want to be concrete about what deregulation looks like on the ground because it is easy to discuss this in the abstract.‚Źé‚ŹéA maternal health clinic in rural Nebraska currently uses a postpartum screening tool certified under the existing DSI criteria. The tool includes a model card that specifies it was developed and tested on a dataset of 15,000 patients, 78% of whom were white, 62% of whom were college-educated, and 89% of whom were privately insured. The model card also documents that the tool's sensitivity is 92% overall but drops to 78% in patients with a history of trauma. The clinic knows this. So when they screen a patient who has disclosed trauma history, they follow up more aggressively with clinical interview rather than relying solely on the tool's recommendation.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the DSI certification requirement is removed, vendors will have no obligation to produce these model cards. A new vendor could release a competing product that claims to be "more efficient" and "more user-friendly" without ever documenting what it was trained on, whether it has been tested in postpartum populations, or how it performs across demographic groups. The clinic might adopt it because it costs less or integrates more smoothly with their EHR. But they would have no way to know whether its 92% sensitivity claim is real or inflated. They would have no way to know whether it actually works in their population. They would have no way to know whether it has known failure modes.‚Źé‚ŹéAnd the patient? The patient never knows an algorithm is making recommendations about her care. She shows up for her six-week postpartum visit. The tool recommends screening. Or it does not. The clinician acts on that recommendation, or questions it. The patient is screened, or she is missed. None of this is transparent to her. She has no way to request an explanation of the tool's reasoning. She has no way to appeal a recommendation she thinks is wrong. She has no way to know that the system flagging her for intervention was never validated in postpartum patients with her specific characteristics.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not a theoretical problem. The Federal Register notice for the Cures Act final rule (85 FR 25642) explicitly documented patient complaints about algorithmic opacity in healthcare systems. The Office for Civil Rights has received complaints about AI-assisted systems making recommendations without clear documentation of their basis. And we have heard directly from patients in the postpartum period who felt harmed by algorithmic recommendations they did not understand and could not contest.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving the model card requirement does not solve these problems. It erases the documentation that would help identify them.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC CONCERNS ABOUT MATERNAL MENTAL HEALTH POPULATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe patients we serve face particular vulnerability to algorithmic bias and opacity.‚Źé‚ŹéPregnant and postpartum women have been systematically underrepresented in medical research for decades. This has consequences. Most of the data that AI systems are trained on comes from non-pregnant populations, or from pregnant populations that skew white and middle-class. A postpartum depression screening system trained predominantly on data from white, college-educated, insured patients will not accurately recognize depression in a low-income Black woman whose presentation of depression manifests differently, whose symptom reporting is shaped by medical mistrust, and whose social circumstances are fundamentally different from the training population.‚Źé‚ŹéWe have looked at published validation studies for several postpartum AI-assisted screening systems. Most of them do not report performance separately by race or ethnicity. Some do not report performance by income level or education. This is not an accident. It reflects the composition of the training data and the validation cohorts. But it means we have no way to know how these systems actually perform in diverse postpartum populations.‚Źé‚ŹéUnder the current DSI certification requirement, a system could not be certified without documentation of how it performs across relevant subgroups. A vendor would have to either conduct additional validation studies in diverse populations or explicitly document the limitations of their system. This creates accountability. It also creates incentives for vendors to actually test their systems in the populations they claim to serve.‚Źé‚ŹéRemove that requirement, and vendors have no obligation to test in diverse populations. They have no obligation to document performance disparities. They have no obligation to be honest about the limitations of their training data. And the patients most vulnerable to algorithmic bias have no protection.‚Źé‚Źé**PATIENT SAFETY AND CLINICAL DECISION-MAKING**‚Źé‚ŹéThe strongest argument for retaining the DSI certification requirement is patient safety.‚Źé‚ŹéClinical decision support systems in maternal mental health are not passive tools. They actively shape what conditions providers screen for, how aggressively they investigate symptoms, and what treatments they recommend. A system that recommends against screening for postpartum anxiety in a 35-year-old primiparous patient with a personal history of generalized anxiety disorder has just made a clinical decision. The decision is attributed to the algorithm, but the patient bears the consequences.‚Źé‚ŹéThe 45 CFR 170.315(a)(8) requirement ensures that before such a system enters clinical practice, someone has documented what it does, who it was tested on, and what happens when it fails. This is not about slowing innovation. It is about ensuring that when innovation enters clinical practice, it does so with eyes open.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider the requirement for external validation evidence. This means a system must be tested not just on the data it was trained on, but on new data from different settings, different patient populations, and different clinical contexts. This is not busywork. This is the difference between a system that works well in a research setting and a system that actually works when deployed in a real maternal health clinic serving a diverse population. Without external validation, you have no evidence that a system generalizes beyond its training environment.‚Źé‚ŹéWe would argue, in fact, that the external validation requirement should be strengthened, not removed. Maternal health systems should be required to demonstrate validation in diverse populations, across different healthcare settings, and in real clinical use. But that is not what the proposal is suggesting. The proposal is to remove the requirement entirely.‚Źé‚Źé**ADDRESSING THE DEREGULATION ARGUMENT**‚Źé‚ŹéWe understand the argument for reducing regulatory burden. Compliance with certification requirements does cost money and time. Vendors have to hire people to write model cards, conduct validation studies, and document their development process. Some of this cost is passed on to healthcare systems.‚Źé‚ŹéBut this is not a meaningful regulatory burden in the context of maternal health. The cost of developing a clinically useful AI system‚ÄĒwriting code, collecting training data, conducting preliminary validation‚ÄĒdwarfs the cost of documenting what you have built. If a vendor cannot afford to write a model card describing their system, that is actually a warning sign. It suggests they have not thought carefully about what their system does or who it is for.‚Źé‚ŹéMoreover, the burden falls on vendors, not on providers or patients. Vendors are the ones developing systems. They are the ones who should bear the cost of ensuring those systems are safe and trustworthy before they enter clinical practice. This is not an onerous ask.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal suggests that deregulation will spur innovation and competition. We are skeptical. In our experience, what spurs competition in health IT is not the removal of safety requirements but the presence of clear standards and transparent information. When vendors know they have to document their systems' capabilities and limitations, they compete on the basis of actual performance. When documentation is not required, they compete on the basis of marketing and price. Patients lose.‚Źé‚Źé**INFORMATION BLOCKING AND TRANSPARENCY**‚Źé‚ŹéWe note that the proposed deregulation occurs in parallel with ongoing efforts to enforce information blocking restrictions under 45 CFR 171.101 and related provisions. There is a tension here worth acknowledging.‚Źé‚ŹéInformation blocking rules are designed to ensure that patients and providers can access health information without unnecessary barriers. This is important. Patients should be able to get their medical records. Providers should be able to share information across systems. Interoperability should not require patients to be shuttled between phone calls and fax machines.‚Źé‚ŹéBut information blocking rules only work if patients and providers know what information exists and how it is being used. If AI systems are making recommendations or flagging patients for intervention without transparency, information blocking rules do not protect them. The information is not being blocked. It is just being processed invisibly.‚Źé‚ŹéWe would argue that removing DSI transparency requirements actually undermines the goals of information blocking. It allows vendors to build opaque systems that patients cannot audit, providers cannot understand, and regulators cannot oversee. This is not interoperability. It is opacity hiding behind the language of innovation.‚Źé‚Źé**ALTERNATIVES TO FULL DEREGULATION**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the Office of the National Coordinator is concerned about the burden of current certification requirements, there are less drastic alternatives to complete removal.‚Źé‚ŹéOne option would be to streamline the model card requirement without eliminating it. Instead of requiring extensive documentation, vendors could provide a standardized, shorter form that captures the essential information: intended use, target population, known performance disparities, and external validation status. This would reduce burden while preserving transparency.‚Źé‚ŹéAnother option would be to create different certification pathways for different types of systems. A low-risk system that provides information to patients without making clinical recommendations might have lighter documentation requirements than a system that actively recommends treatment. Maternal mental health systems, given the vulnerability of the population and the clinical significance of the decisions they inform, should probably be in the higher-scrutiny category.‚Źé‚ŹéA third option would be to extend the timeline for compliance. Instead of requiring full documentation before certification, vendors could be given a phase-in period during which they conduct validation studies and develop model cards. This would reduce immediate burden while ensuring that systems eventually meet transparency standards.‚Źé‚ŹéWe would support any of these alternatives to full deregulation. What we cannot support is the complete removal of transparency requirements.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION: WHAT IS AT STAKE**‚Źé‚ŹéThe postpartum period is a time of profound vulnerability. Pregnant and postpartum women face increased risk of depression, anxiety, psychosis, and suicide. They are also navigating a healthcare system that is often fragmented, under-resourced, and insufficiently trained in maternal mental health. An AI system that helps identify women in crisis and connects them to care could be genuinely valuable. But only if that system is trustworthy, only if its limitations are known, and only if providers and patients can understand and question its recommendations.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification requirement exists because the field learned through experience that opaque systems cause harm. Clinicians cannot make informed decisions without information. Patients cannot protect themselves without transparency. And regulators cannot ensure safety without documentation. Removing this requirement is not about unleashing innovation. It is about unleashing systems that operate in the dark.‚Źé‚ŹéWe urge the Office of the National Coordinator and the Assistant Secretary for Technology Policy to retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures. If burden reduction is the goal, pursue it through streamlining, not elimination. But do not remove the requirement for vendors to document what their systems do, who they were tested on, and what risks they carry. Maternal mental health patients depend on that documentation. And maternal health providers depend on it to practice responsibly.‚Źé‚ŹéThe women we serve in the postpartum period deserve healthcare systems that are transparent, accountable, and trustworthy. That is not an innovation barrier. That is the basic ethical foundation of clinical practice.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-05T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-05T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ10‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒsupportive‚ôĒNebraska‚ôĒsenior policy analyst‚ôĒ64‚ôĒWhen my daughter struggled with postpartum depression after her son was born, we spent three months piecing together fragmented records from her OB-GYN, her psychiatrist, and the hospital‚ÄĒeach provider operating in isolation because of data-sharing barriers. Had her providers been able to access a consolidated picture of her mental health trajectory, her medication interactions might have been caught sooner. But now I've seen how quickly health data can be misused when privacy safeguards slip; a colleague's daughter lost her job opportunity when an employer somehow obtained her psychiatric history. The tension between getting vulnerable patients the coordinated care they desperately need and protecting them from the real predatory uses of their information keeps me up at night.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ3122
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0009‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDavid Hill‚ôĒNational Alliance for Care at Home‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe National Alliance for Care at Home opposes removing the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirement for model cards and transparency disclosures, arguing that this requirement protects clinicians and vulnerable home-based ca...‚ôĒ**NATIONAL ALLIANCE FOR CARE AT HOME**‚Źé**COMMENT ON ASTP/ONC DEREGULATORY ACTIONS PROPOSED RULE**‚Źé‚ŹéThe National Alliance for Care at Home appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory framework outlined in the Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability advance notice of proposed rulemaking. Our organization represents home-based care providers, agencies, and clinicians serving over 3.2 million Americans annually in their residential settings. We understand the impulse to reduce regulatory friction in health IT certification and deployment. We also know from direct experience what happens when that reduction comes without adequate safeguards around clinical decision-making, particularly in settings where fragmentation is already the default state.‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be direct about this: I'm not opposed to deregulation as a principle. But I've learned that some requirements exist because of real failures, and removing them without understanding those failures is how you end up solving the wrong problem. The question isn't whether we should have lighter regulation. It's which rules actually protect something worth protecting, and which ones are just bureaucratic inertia.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CONTEXT: WHY THIS MATTERS FOR HOME-BASED CARE**‚Źé‚ŹéHome care sits at the intersection of three separate systems that rarely speak to each other. A patient receives primary care from their physician's office on Monday, home health nursing on Wednesday, and physical therapy on Friday. Each operates under a different electronic health record system, each has different data standards, and each collects the same patient history independently because they have no other option. This fragmentation isn't a problem that deregulation solves. It's a problem that deregulation can make worse if we're not careful about how we approach it.‚Źé‚ŹéThe home care setting also carries specific vulnerabilities. Patients are older, more medically complex, and often cognitively impaired. They have multiple concurrent medications managed by multiple providers. They're receiving care in an environment where there's no nurse station, no pharmacist two doors down, no second pair of eyes reviewing the care plan in real time. The clinician providing care in someone's living room is working with whatever information they can access in that moment, and they're making decisions that affect whether that patient stays home or ends up in a hospital. That's the context I keep coming back to when I think about what rules should be loosened and what rules need to stay.‚Źé‚Źé**DECISION SUPPORT INTERVENTIONS AND THE MODEL CARD REQUIREMENT**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule contemplates removing or significantly relaxing the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion that currently requires developers to provide model cards, transparency disclosures covering intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. This is the requirement I'm asking you not to remove. Let me explain why, and I'll try not to oversimplify it.‚Źé‚ŹéThe model card requirement isn't a paperwork burden that exists because someone in an office building thought it would be good practice. It exists because healthcare systems deployed algorithmic recommendations that were trained on data from one population and then applied to a completely different population, with predictable results. The most cited example remains the racial bias in the widely-used algorithm that informed care allocation decisions across major health systems (Obermeyer et al., Science, 2019; 366(6464):447-453). The algorithm had been validated on a cohort that was 90 percent white. When applied broadly, it systematically underestimated need in Black patients. No one set out to create a racist algorithm. The problem was that no one looked carefully enough at what the training data actually contained, who it represented, and where it would fail.‚Źé‚ŹéHome care is particularly vulnerable to this type of failure because the population is so heterogeneous. A patient living alone in rural New Hampshire has completely different support structures, medication access, and comorbidity patterns than a patient in a Boston suburb living with family. An algorithm trained on one population and deployed without transparency about that limitation will make different errors in different settings. A clinician who doesn't know what population a decision-support tool was developed on, what its limitations are, or what risks it carries in their specific patient population can't practice informed judgment. They just follow the recommendation and hope it's right.‚Źé‚ŹéThe model card requirement does several things simultaneously. It forces developers to actually think about what their tool does and doesn't do well. It gives clinicians the information they need to decide whether to follow a recommendation or override it. It creates accountability. When something goes wrong, we can trace back to what the developer knew about the tool's limitations and whether they disclosed them. That matters. That's not bureaucratic burden. That's professional responsibility.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT THE DATA ACTUALLY SHOWS ABOUT TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéI'm familiar with the argument that transparency requirements slow deployment and create competitive disadvantage. The concern is that if a developer has to publish detailed information about training data, performance metrics, and known limitations, competitors will reverse-engineer their approach. This is a real concern in some contexts. It's also a concern that assumes the alternative (deploying a tool with no transparency about how it works) is acceptable in healthcare.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider what we know from the literature on information disclosure and clinical behavior. When clinicians have clear information about the evidence base for a recommendation, their decision-making improves (Sackett et al., BMJ. 1996;312(7023):71-72). When they don't have that information, they're more likely to follow the recommendation uncritically, particularly if it comes from a system they trust. Home health clinicians are doing complex work under time pressure with limited information. They're managing patients with multiple comorbidities, polypharmacy, and often cognitive impairment. They're making decisions about whether an intervention is safe, whether a symptom change is significant, whether the patient needs acute care. If they're relying on a decision-support tool to inform those judgments, they need to understand what that tool is and isn't capable of.‚Źé‚ŹéThe burden of creating a model card is real. It's also not insurmountable. Developers at major health IT companies are already creating these disclosures for their own internal governance. The burden isn't the actual work of documenting what you've built. The burden is the disclosure itself. And I understand that's uncomfortable. But in healthcare, uncomfortable transparency is often the alternative to harmful opacity.‚Źé‚Źé**IMPLEMENTATION IN HOME-BASED CARE SETTINGS**‚Źé‚ŹéHere's where I think the conversation has gotten disconnected from reality. When we talk about removing certification criteria, we're talking about consequences that play out in specific places with specific people. Home care agencies don't have IT departments the size of major hospital systems. Many agencies serving rural areas are operating on thin margins with minimal technology infrastructure. When they purchase health IT, they're making decisions based on what's available, what's affordable, and what integrates with their existing systems. They're not in a position to conduct independent validation studies on the algorithms embedded in the tools they use.‚Źé‚ŹéIf we remove the requirement that developers provide model cards and transparency disclosures, we're not creating a more competitive market. We're creating a market where agencies have even less information about what they're buying. The smaller vendors, the ones actually focused on home care workflows, often can't afford to conduct independent validation studies anyway. They're already operating at disadvantage compared to the large EHR vendors. Removing transparency requirements doesn't level that playing field. It just means everybody has less information.‚Źé‚ŹéI keep thinking about the home care agency we work with in central Vermont, about 45 clinicians serving a three-county area. They're not going to hire a data scientist to reverse-engineer a competitor's algorithm. They're going to look at what's available, what they can afford, and what integrates with their current workflows. They're going to make a decision based on marketing materials and cost. If the certification criteria that currently require transparency disclosures are removed, they'll have even less basis for making that decision. And the patients they serve, who are already dealing with fragmented care, will be relying on tools that nobody has adequately explained.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT SHOULD STAY AND WHAT COULD CHANGE**‚Źé‚ŹéI don't think I'm being asked to endorse the entire current regulatory framework. There are things that could be streamlined. There are requirements that probably do create unnecessary burden. There are certification pathways that could be simplified. I'm not against that work.‚Źé‚ŹéBut the DSI criterion isn't one of them. The requirement that decision-support tools come with documented information about intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation is doing something important. It's creating a space where healthcare organizations can ask reasonable questions about the tools they're deploying. It's creating accountability for developers. It's protecting patients in settings where there's already limited oversight.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule references the Information Blocking Rule (42 CFR 171.102(c)) as an example of a regulation that created unintended burdens. I understand that argument. The Information Blocking Rule has created compliance challenges for smaller vendors and has sometimes slowed deployment of certain capabilities. That's a real problem that deserves serious attention. But that's different from saying all certification criteria should be loosened. Some rules create burdens that are worth carrying because of what they protect. Some rules create burdens that exceed their benefit. Those are different decisions.‚Źé‚ŹéFor DSI certification, specifically, the model card requirement is protecting something important: the ability of clinicians and organizations to make informed decisions about algorithmic recommendations that affect patient care. In home-based care settings, where clinicians are working with limited support and patients are particularly vulnerable, that protection matters.‚Źé‚Źé**RECOMMENDATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the goal is to reduce burden while maintaining appropriate safeguards, there are ways to do that:‚Źé‚Źé1. Streamline the model card format itself. The current requirement could be more specific about what information is essential versus what's supplementary. Developers shouldn't have to write a 50-page document, but they should have to disclose the core information about what the tool does and what it doesn't.‚Źé‚Źé2. Create a tiered approach based on risk and deployment context. A decision-support tool used in a hospital setting with robust IT infrastructure and clinical oversight is different from a tool used in home care. The transparency requirements could be calibrated accordingly.‚Źé‚Źé3. Allow developers to protect proprietary training methods while still disclosing performance characteristics and known limitations. You can describe what a model does and doesn't do well without describing exactly how you built it.‚Źé‚Źé4. Establish clear timelines and processes so developers know exactly what's required and when. Some of the burden comes from uncertainty about what documentation is adequate.‚Źé‚ŹéBut removing the requirement entirely is the wrong move. The requirement exists because we've learned from failures. The specific failures happened in settings where algorithms were deployed without adequate understanding of their limitations, and patients were harmed as a result.‚Źé‚Źé**CLOSING**‚Źé‚ŹéI opened this comment talking about my mother, about the moment I realized that once her information was in circulation, there was no way to fully control where it went or how it was used. I'm not conflating privacy with algorithmic transparency. They're different problems. But they share something in common: they're both about control. Once you release a regulation that doesn't require disclosure, once you deploy an algorithm without requiring developers to explain how it works and what it doesn't do, you can't go back. The market won't spontaneously demand transparency if it's not required. Smaller vendors won't be able to compete on transparency if larger vendors are allowed to operate in opacity.‚Źé‚ŹéHome-based care needs better interoperability, faster data sharing, and less regulatory friction in many areas. I'm not opposed to that work. But the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion is doing something important, and removing it would create real risks for patients and clinicians working in one of the most fragmented parts of the healthcare system.‚Źé‚ŹéI'd urge ONC and CMS to keep this requirement in place and instead focus deregulatory efforts on rules that create burden without corresponding benefit. There are plenty of those. This isn't one of them.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-10T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-10T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ6‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒresigned‚ôĒNew Hampshire‚ôĒdirector of government affairs‚ôĒ57‚ôĒMy mother spent her final eighteen months receiving care in our home after her stroke, and I watched firsthand how fragmented her medical records were across her cardiologist, her physical therapist, and the home health agency‚ÄĒeach operating in their own silo, each requesting the same information repeatedly. I understand the impulse to break down those barriers. But I also remember the moment a billing agent called asking about medications because someone had sold her contact information down a chain I still can't fully trace, and I realized there's no going back from that kind of exposure once it happens.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1975
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0010‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒMichael Wilson‚ôĒThe American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒACOG opposes the removal of the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures, arguing that these transparency and accountability mechanisms are essential to responsible deployme...‚ôĒ**Comment on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity**‚Źé‚ŹéThe American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG) submits these comments on the proposed deregulatory actions affecting health information technology certification and interoperability standards. ACOG represents over 60,000 obstetrician-gynecologists and other clinicians who provide reproductive healthcare services across the United States. Our members work daily in environments where health information flows between multiple electronic health record systems, specialty platforms, and external data sources. We have a direct stake in both the innovation agenda the ASTP/ONC seeks to advance and the patient safety concerns that rigorous standards and transparency requirements are designed to protect.‚Źé‚ŹéThis comment addresses a specific element of the proposed deregulation: the potential removal or relaxation of the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion that currently requires AI model cards, transparency disclosures concerning intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. ACOG believes this particular requirement should be retained and strengthened rather than eliminated or weakened. We do not oppose innovation in clinical decision support systems. Rather, we argue that the transparency and accountability mechanisms embedded in the current DSI criterion are foundational to responsible AI deployment in obstetrics and reproductive medicine, where the consequences of algorithmic error or bias can affect not only individual patients but also the development of another human life.‚Źé‚Źé**Background: Clinical Decision Support in Obstetrics and the Real-World Stakes**‚Źé‚ŹéObstetrics is a field where algorithmic decision support has already begun to reshape clinical practice, but where the risks of flawed or opaque systems are particularly acute. Consider gestational diabetes mellitus screening and diagnosis. Many U.S. obstetric practices now rely on algorithmic protocols to identify which pregnant patients should undergo glucose tolerance testing, how results should be interpreted, and what follow-up is indicated. These algorithms operate across multiple EHR systems, and their outputs directly influence clinical management of approximately 7% of all U.S. pregnancies, or roughly 300,000 women per year (Zhu & Zhang, 2016, Diabetes Care). When an algorithm recommends or fails to recommend screening in a particular patient, that recommendation has downstream consequences: missed diagnoses lead to inadequate glycemic control, which increases the risk of fetal macrosomia, neonatal hypoglycemia, and maternal complications including preeclampsia.‚Źé‚ŹéThe stakes extend beyond individual clinical encounters. Gestational diabetes screening itself has been the subject of considerable clinical debate. The American Diabetes Association recommends universal screening at 24-28 weeks gestation, while some international guidelines recommend selective screening based on risk factors. An algorithm designed to implement selective screening might be trained on data from a population in which certain demographic characteristics were used as proxies for diabetes risk, potentially encoding historical disparities. A pregnant woman of a particular race or ethnicity might receive different screening recommendations based on algorithmic patterns learned from historical data that reflected discriminatory clinical practices rather than actual biological risk. Without transparent documentation of training data characteristics, target populations, and known limitations, clinicians using such a system would have no way to identify or correct for this embedded bias.‚Źé‚ŹéACOG has observed similar challenges in other domains. Algorithms for preterm birth risk prediction, for instance, have been trained on datasets that may not adequately represent the diversity of pregnant populations in the United States. An algorithm developed primarily on data from a large academic medical center may perform poorly when deployed in a rural clinic serving a different population. Algorithms for anesthesia risk stratification, for fetal anomaly detection on ultrasound, and for maternal cardiac risk assessment all carry the same risk: deployment in clinical contexts for which they were not validated, or use in populations that differ systematically from the training data.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification criterion exists precisely to address these risks. It requires that developers and vendors provide clinical users with the information necessary to make informed decisions about whether and how to use a given decision support system. The requirement is not burdensome. It asks for documentation that responsible AI developers should be creating anyway. Model cards, as originally conceived by Mitchell et al. (2019, arXiv preprint arXiv:1810.03993) and adapted for healthcare contexts, simply make explicit what a clinician needs to know: What is this system designed to do? Who was it tested on? What are its known limitations? What data did it learn from? These are not impediments to innovation; they are prerequisites for responsible innovation.‚Źé‚Źé**The Regulatory Logic Behind DSI Certification**‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI criterion was established through notice-and-comment rulemaking and codified in the Office of the National Coordinator's Health IT Certification Program rules at 45 CFR Parts 170.102 and 170.315. The criterion was informed by extensive stakeholder input from clinicians, informaticists, patient advocates, and vendors.
