It reflects a considered judgment that clinical decision support systems operating in healthcare settings warrant transparency requirements distinct from other software. This is not unique to healthcare. FDA regulation of software as a medical device (21 CFR Part 11 and related sections) similarly imposes documentation and traceability requirements that do not apply to general-purpose software.‚Źé‚ŹéThe logic is straightforward. Clinical decision support systems are often treated by end users as having a kind of authority. A clinician may be more likely to follow a recommendation generated by an algorithm, particularly if the algorithm comes from a reputable vendor or is integrated into their existing EHR workflow, than they would be to follow the same recommendation from a colleague or a textbook. This creates what might be called a "trust asymmetry": users may invest more confidence in the algorithm than the algorithm's actual validation evidence warrants. Transparency requirements are designed to counterbalance this asymmetry by ensuring that clinicians have access to the same evidence that informed the algorithm's development and validation.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider the clinical context in which obstetric decision support operates. An obstetrics department may implement an algorithm for early detection of preeclampsia, a condition that affects 3-5% of pregnancies and is a leading cause of maternal mortality and morbidity in the United States (ACOG Practice Bulletin 202, 2019). The algorithm may be embedded in the EHR in such a way that it generates alerts or recommendations at the point of care. A busy clinician seeing a patient in the third trimester may receive a flag indicating "high preeclampsia risk" without having immediate access to information about how that risk was calculated, what data the algorithm was trained on, or whether its performance has been validated in populations similar to the patient being evaluated. Under the current DSI criterion, the vendor would be required to provide documentation addressing these questions. Under a deregulated approach, the vendor might choose not to provide such documentation, leaving clinicians to make decisions based on incomplete information.‚Źé‚Źé**Specific Concerns About Removing the DSI Transparency Requirement**‚Źé‚ŹéACOG has three specific concerns about removing or substantially relaxing the DSI certification criterion.‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, removal of the transparency requirement would eliminate the primary mechanism by which clinicians can assess whether a decision support system is appropriate for their patient population. Obstetric practices vary considerably by geography, patient demographics, and available resources. A practice serving a predominantly rural, lower-income population may have different needs and constraints than a practice in an urban, affluent area. An algorithm for labor management or cesarean delivery timing that was validated on data from a large academic medical center may not generalize well to a smaller community hospital. The vendor may have no incentive to conduct validation studies in different populations if certification does not require it. Clinicians in those secondary populations would then be using systems they have no way to evaluate, based on evidence they cannot access.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, removal of the transparency requirement would reduce accountability in cases where algorithmic errors or biases cause patient harm. Suppose a pregnant patient of color receives a recommendation for early delivery based on an algorithm's assessment of her preeclampsia risk, and that recommendation is later found to have been based on training data that systematically overestimated risk in women of color. Under current rules, the vendor would be required to have disclosed the composition of the training data as part of the model card. This disclosure would make the source of bias visible and would establish a clear accountability pathway. Without such disclosure, identifying the source of bias becomes much more difficult, and patients harmed by algorithmic discrimination would have fewer tools to seek redress.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, removal of the DSI criterion would create a regulatory gap precisely as interoperability standards are maturing. The Health Information for Economic and Clinical Health (HITECH) Act of 2009 and subsequent regulations have established frameworks for data exchange and interoperability. The Trusted Exchange Framework and Common Agreement (TEFCA), authorized under 42 U.S.C. 300jj-52a and implemented through ONC guidance, creates new pathways for health information to flow between systems. The 21st Century Cures Act Information Blocking Rule, codified at 45 CFR Part 171, requires providers and vendors to make data available in standardized formats. These frameworks assume that data and algorithms will be subject to appropriate governance and accountability mechanisms. Removing transparency requirements for decision support systems would undermine the governance assumptions embedded in these broader interoperability frameworks. It would mean that data could flow more freely between systems while the algorithms that process that data would be subject to fewer transparency requirements. This is backwards.‚Źé‚Źé**The Distinction Between Innovation and Opacity**‚Źé‚ŹéACOG recognizes the ASTP/ONC's stated objective of reducing regulatory burden to accelerate innovation. We do not believe that the DSI transparency requirement is a meaningful impediment to innovation. The requirement does not prevent the development or deployment of novel decision support systems. It does not require FDA approval or clinical trial data. It does not mandate that vendors use particular machine learning frameworks or validation methodologies. It requires that vendors document what they have already built and how they have already tested it.‚Źé‚ŹéReal-world experience supports this assessment. Vendors have successfully complied with the DSI criterion for multiple years. They have implemented model card systems, documented training data characteristics, and disclosed known limitations. Some have gone further, voluntarily submitting their systems to external validation studies. None of these vendors has reported that the transparency requirement was a barrier to bringing products to market. The requirement has not prevented the proliferation of clinical decision support systems in obstetrics and other specialties. What it has done is create a mechanism by which clinicians can make informed choices about which systems to adopt.‚Źé‚ŹéThere is a difference between innovation and opacity. Innovation occurs when new tools are developed, tested, and deployed to solve real clinical problems. Opacity occurs when tools are deployed without clinicians knowing how they work or what their limitations are. The former is desirable; the latter is not. The DSI criterion does not prevent innovation. It prevents opacity.‚Źé‚Źé**Lessons from Related Regulatory Domains**‚Źé‚ŹéThe case for retaining the DSI transparency requirement becomes stronger when one considers how similar requirements have operated in related regulatory domains.‚Źé‚ŹéFDA regulation of laboratory-developed tests (LDTs) has long required developers to document clinical validity, analytical validity, and the intended use of the test. While FDA enforcement of LDT regulations has been inconsistent, the regulatory principle is clear: diagnostic tools used in clinical practice should be accompanied by documentation of their performance and limitations. This principle is so widely accepted that even vendors who are not subject to FDA jurisdiction often voluntarily provide such documentation, because clinicians expect it and require it before adopting a new test.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Clinical Laboratory Improvement Amendments (CLIA) regulations, codified at 42 CFR Part 493, similarly require that laboratories implement quality control and validation procedures for any test they offer, and that they maintain documentation of those procedures. These requirements have not prevented innovation in laboratory medicine. They have simply ensured that clinicians using laboratory tests can have confidence in their accuracy and reliability.‚Źé‚ŹéIn the realm of health IT interoperability, the requirement that vendors implement standards-based data exchange does not prevent innovation; it channels innovation in directions that are compatible with broader ecosystem needs. Similarly, transparency requirements for decision support systems do not prevent innovation; they channel innovation toward systems that can be responsibly deployed in clinical practice.‚Źé‚Źé**The Patient Safety Imperative in Obstetrics**‚Źé‚ŹéThere is a specific patient safety imperative in obstetrics that warrants retention of the DSI transparency requirement. Obstetrics is a field in which clinical decisions affect not only the pregnant patient but also the developing fetus or newborn. The stakes of algorithmic error are correspondingly high.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider fetal anomaly detection. Many obstetric practices now use algorithms to assist in the interpretation of prenatal ultrasound images, flagging potential anomalies for further clinical evaluation. An algorithm that has high sensitivity but low specificity in detecting, say, congenital heart defects might lead to unnecessary referrals and maternal anxiety. An algorithm that has low sensitivity might miss important anomalies, delaying diagnosis and treatment. Without documentation of the algorithm's performance characteristics in the specific population being evaluated, clinicians cannot calibrate their interpretation of the algorithm's output. They cannot know whether to trust a positive finding or to be reassured by a negative finding.‚Źé‚ŹéOr consider labor management. Some hospitals have implemented algorithms that recommend the augmentation or induction of labor based on patterns of fetal heart rate and uterine contractions. These algorithms are often trained on historical labor data that may reflect clinical practices and outcomes from a different era. An algorithm trained on data from the 1990s and early 2000s, when rates of cesarean delivery were lower and labor management practices were different, may not perform well when applied to current labor management. Without access to information about the training data and the algorithm's performance in contemporary populations, clinicians cannot appropriately calibrate their reliance on the algorithm's recommendations.‚Źé‚ŹéThe potential for harm is not merely hypothetical. Several peer-reviewed studies have documented cases in which clinical decision support systems deployed without adequate validation or transparency mechanisms have contributed to patient harm or have produced recommendations inconsistent with current evidence. A study by Char et al. (2018, JAMA Internal Medicine) examined algorithms for predicting patient mortality and found significant discrepancies between the algorithms' internal validation results and their performance in external populations. A systematic review by Gianfrancesco et al. (2018, Nature Medicine) found that machine learning algorithms trained on data from predominantly white populations often showed significantly worse performance when applied to patients of other races. These findings underscore the importance of transparency about training data and external validation.‚Źé‚Źé**The Information Blocking Rule and Its Implications for DSI Transparency**‚Źé‚ŹéAn important consideration in assessing the DSI transparency requirement is its interaction with the Information Blocking Rule established under the 21st Century Cures Act. The Information Blocking Rule, codified at 45 CFR Part 171, requires that developers, vendors, and providers not block the flow of health information. The Rule defines "information" broadly to include clinical data, but the question of whether algorithms and their documentation constitute "information" subject to the Rule has not been fully resolved.‚Źé‚ŹéIf decision support algorithms and their associated documentation (model cards, validation studies, training data characteristics) are considered "information" under the Information Blocking Rule, then developers would be required to make that information available to other developers and to clinicians who request it. This would provide a powerful market-based mechanism for ensuring transparency, supplementing the DSI certification requirement. Conversely, if the Information Blocking Rule is interpreted narrowly to cover only raw clinical data and not algorithmic documentation, then the DSI certification requirement becomes even more important as a mechanism for ensuring transparency.‚Źé‚ŹéACOG believes the ONC should clarify the relationship between the DSI certification requirement and the Information Blocking Rule. If the Rule is intended to cover algorithmic documentation, then the DSI requirement may be partially redundant with it. If the Rule is not intended to cover algorithmic documentation, then the DSI requirement serves an essential function that the Rule does not. Either way, the appropriate policy response is to retain the DSI requirement, at least until the ONC has clarified how the Information Blocking Rule applies to algorithms.‚Źé‚Źé**Market-Based Arguments for Deregulation and Why They Don't Fully Apply Here**‚Źé‚ŹéThe ASTP/ONC's proposal rests partly on market-based arguments: that removing regulatory requirements will increase competition, that vendors will compete on the basis of transparency and quality, and that market forces will drive innovation more efficiently than regulatory mandates. These arguments have some force in certain contexts. But they apply less convincingly to clinical decision support systems in healthcare.‚Źé‚ŹéThe market for clinical decision support is not perfectly competitive. Many obstetric practices use EHR systems from a small number of large vendors (Epic, Cerner, Athenahealth). These vendors have significant market power, and individual clinicians have limited ability to switch between systems based on the quality or transparency of embedded decision support features. A clinician who is dissatisfied with the transparency of the preeclampsia risk algorithm embedded in their EHR cannot easily switch to a different EHR simply to access a different algorithm. The switching costs are enormous, and the benefits of switching may not justify those costs.‚Źé‚ŹéFurthermore, the information asymmetry between vendors and clinicians is substantial. A vendor has full knowledge of how an algorithm works, what data it was trained on, and how well it performs in different populations. A clinician does not have this knowledge and cannot easily acquire it without the vendor's cooperation. In such an information asymmetry, markets often fail to produce optimal outcomes. Vendors may have little incentive to invest in transparency if they can market their products without it. Clinicians may adopt systems based on marketing claims rather than actual validation evidence. Patients may be harmed by algorithms that are used without appropriate understanding of their limitations.‚Źé‚ŹéRegulatory requirements like the DSI certification criterion are designed to counteract these market failures. They are not substitutes for market competition; they are complements to it. They set a baseline floor of transparency and accountability that allows markets to function more efficiently. They do not prevent vendors from competing on the basis of superior algorithms, better user interfaces, or more comprehensive validation evidence. They simply require that all vendors meet a baseline standard of transparency.‚Źé‚Źé**International Precedent and Regulatory Alignment**‚Źé‚ŹéIt is worth noting that other major regulatory jurisdictions are moving in the opposite direction from the proposed deregulation. The European Union's Artificial Intelligence Act (AI Act), which became enforceable in 2024, establishes transparency and documentation requirements for high-risk AI systems, including those used in healthcare. The AI Act requires that developers maintain documentation of training data, intended use, known limitations, and performance characteristics. These requirements are more stringent than the current U.S. DSI certification criterion, not less stringent.‚Źé‚ŹéThe UK's approach to regulating algorithms in healthcare, outlined in guidance from the UK Health Security Agency and the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE), similarly emphasizes transparency and validation. The guidance notes that algorithms used in clinical decision support should be accompanied by documentation of their performance, limitations, and appropriate use cases.‚Źé‚ŹéCanada's approach is similar. Health Canada's guidance on AI and machine learning in medical devices requires that developers provide evidence of analytical and clinical validity, and that this evidence be made available to clinicians and regulators.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the United States moves toward deregulation while international jurisdictions move toward stricter regulation, U.S. vendors will face a fragmented regulatory landscape. Vendors seeking to sell in Europe or Canada will have to meet the transparency requirements imposed by those jurisdictions. Vendors selling only in the United States would face no such requirements. This could create a competitive disadvantage for U.S. vendors operating internationally, and it could mean that U.S. clinicians using U.S.-developed algorithms have access to less transparency than their international counterparts.‚Źé‚Źé**Specific Recommendations**‚Źé‚ŹéACOG recommends the following:‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, the DSI certification criterion should be retained in its current form. The requirement that vendors provide model cards and transparency disclosures concerning intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results is essential to responsible AI deployment in healthcare.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, the ONC should consider strengthening the DSI criterion by making explicit the requirement that external validation studies be conducted in populations that differ from the training population, and that validation results be disclosed. Many current algorithms are validated only on the same dataset on which they were trained, which provides little information about how they will perform in real-world practice. External validation in diverse populations would provide much stronger evidence of an algorithm's suitability for clinical use.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, the ONC should clarify the relationship between the DSI certification requirement and the Information Blocking Rule. If algorithmic documentation is considered "information" under the Information Blocking Rule, then vendors would be required to make that information available to clinicians and other developers upon request. This would provide an additional mechanism for ensuring transparency beyond the certification requirement.‚Źé‚ŹéFourth, the ONC should establish clear standards for what constitutes adequate documentation of training data characteristics. Many vendors currently provide only high-level summaries of their training data (e.g., "the algorithm was trained on 100,000 patient records from a large academic medical center"). More specific documentation would be more useful to clinicians. At a minimum, vendors should be required to disclose the demographic characteristics of the training population, the clinical settings from which the training data were drawn, the time period over which the data were collected, and any known limitations or biases in the training data.‚Źé‚ŹéFifth, the ONC should establish mechanisms for ongoing monitoring of algorithm performance in real-world clinical settings. The current certification requirement ensures that algorithms are validated before deployment, but it does not require ongoing monitoring after deployment. As algorithms are used in new populations and new clinical contexts, their performance may degrade. Vendors should be required to monitor for such degradation and to inform clinicians when it occurs.‚Źé‚Źé**ACOG's Commitment to Supporting Responsible Innovation**‚Źé‚ŹéACOG is not opposed to innovation in clinical decision support. Our members are eager to adopt new tools that can improve care for pregnant patients. We have participated in pilot studies of novel decision support systems. We have worked with vendors to implement algorithms for screening, diagnosis, and management of obstetric conditions. We recognize that appropriately designed and validated decision support systems can improve clinical outcomes, reduce unnecessary testing, and support clinicians in making complex decisions.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat ACOG is opposed to is the deployment of decision support systems without adequate transparency and validation. We believe that responsible innovation is innovation that is accompanied by clear documentation of what the system is designed to do, how it was tested, what its limitations are, and what populations it has been validated in. This is not an impediment to innovation; it is a prerequisite for innovation that clinicians can confidently adopt.‚Źé‚ŹéACOG's members are prepared to implement and use more and better clinical decision support systems. We ask only that those systems come with the transparency and validation evidence that responsible clinical practice requires. The DSI certification criterion provides a mechanism for ensuring this transparency without imposing excessive burden on vendors. Removing the criterion would be a step backward for patient safety and for the responsible deployment of AI in obstetrics.‚Źé‚Źé**Conclusion**‚Źé‚ŹéThe decision to retain or eliminate the DSI certification criterion is ultimately a choice about what kind of innovation we want to encourage. We can pursue innovation that prioritizes speed and market entry, in which case we should eliminate the transparency requirement. Or we can pursue innovation that prioritizes patient safety and clinical utility, in which case we should retain it. ACOG believes the latter is the correct choice.‚Źé‚ŹéThe requirement that vendors provide model cards and transparency disclosures is not a regulatory burden that prevents innovation. It is a foundational accountability mechanism that enables responsible innovation. It allows clinicians to make informed decisions about whether and how to use a given decision support system. It provides a basis for identifying and correcting algorithmic bias. It creates an accountability pathway for cases where algorithmic errors harm patients. These are not obstacles to innovation; they are features of a regulatory system that is designed to support innovation in directions that benefit patients.‚Źé‚ŹéFor these reasons, ACOG respectfully urges the ASTP/ONC to retain the DSI certification criterion in its current form, and to consider the specific recommendations outlined above for strengthening and clarifying the criterion. We stand ready to work with the ONC, with vendors, and with other stakeholders to implement these recommendations in ways that support both innovation and patient safety.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-31T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-31T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ13‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒacademic‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒurgent‚ôĒTennessee‚ôĒPhD researcher‚ôĒ58‚ôĒWhen my daughter was diagnosed with gestational diabetes during her pregnancy last year, her OB pulled data from three different electronic health systems to coordinate care with maternal-fetal medicine and endocrinology‚ÄĒa coordination that simply wouldn't have happened five years ago under fragmented systems. I watched firsthand how interoperability standards, however imperfect, prevented the kind of dangerous gaps that used to send patients bouncing between providers with incomplete records. Now I'm genuinely uncertain whether loosening those standards will accelerate innovation or recreate the siloed chaos we've finally begun to escape.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ4054
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0011‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒKevin Walker‚ôĒNational Alliance for Care at Home‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe National Alliance for Care at Home opposes removing Decision Support Interventions transparency and model card requirements, arguing that disclosure of AI model training data, limitations, and validation results is essential for patient safety...‚ôĒJune 16, 2025‚Źé‚ŹéOffice of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology‚ŹéU.S. Department of Health and Human Services‚Źé200 Independence Avenue, S.W.‚ŹéWashington, D.C. 20201‚Źé‚ŹéRE: Comments on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity‚Źé‚ŹéThe National Alliance for Care at Home submits these comments regarding the proposed modifications to Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria under 45 CFR 170.315(b)(6). We represent over 200 member organizations providing home-based care services across 38 states, and our members work directly with patients and clinicians who depend on transparent, reliable clinical decision support systems.‚Źé‚ŹéMAINTAINING TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS FOR PATIENT SAFETY‚Źé‚ŹéWe support the intent to reduce unnecessary regulatory burden on health IT developers. However, the proposed removal of DSI model card requirements and transparency disclosures would eliminate critical safeguards without commensurate benefits. The current requirement that developers disclose intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results (45 CFR 170.315(b)(6)(ii)) serves a distinct function from other certification criteria. It is not primarily a technical interoperability standard. It is a transparency mechanism designed to prevent clinical harm.‚Źé‚ŹéMy organization has learned this lesson directly. Eighteen months ago, one of our partner agencies in Stamford, Connecticut implemented an AI-assisted care coordination system that lacked clear transparency documentation about its underlying model. The system recommended discharge planning for patients with complex comorbidities without disclosing that it had been trained predominantly on data from academic medical centers. Within six weeks, three patients experienced adverse events related to inadequate discharge planning for conditions the system had not been trained to recognize. The clinical team had no way to know what the system's actual limitations were because the developer had not provided this basic information. The federal requirement for model cards would have prevented this situation.‚Źé‚ŹéThe FDA's guidance on clinical decision support (FDA Guidance for Industry: Clinical Decision Support Software, September 2019) explicitly recognizes that transparency about AI model development and validation is necessary for clinicians to make informed decisions about when and how to use such systems. Similarly, the National Institute of Standards and Technology's AI Risk Management Framework (NIST AI RMF 1.0, January 2023) identifies model transparency as foundational to risk mitigation. These are not burdensome compliance obligations. They are basic professional communication.‚Źé‚ŹéSPECIFIC CONCERNS ABOUT REMOVAL‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving the model card requirement would disproportionately affect home-based care settings. Unlike hospital systems with large IT departments, home care agencies typically employ 50 to 150 clinicians and rely on external decision support tools to manage complex patients in unpredictable home environments. When a clinician working alone in a patient's home receives a recommendation from a DSI system, they have no institutional oversight, no second opinion, no algorithm explainability tools. They must understand what the system actually does, what data it was trained on, and what its documented limitations are. Without transparent disclosure, clinicians in these settings cannot safely evaluate whether a recommendation applies to their specific patient.‚Źé‚ŹéWe also note that the burden argument does not account for the actual cost of transparency. Developers already conduct validation studies and maintain internal documentation of their models. A model card requirement asks only that this information be shared with users in a standardized format (45 CFR 170.315(b)(6)(ii)(A) through (D)). This is not a new analysis. It is disclosure of work already performed.‚Źé‚ŹéRECOMMENDATION‚Źé‚ŹéWe recommend retaining the DSI certification criterion as currently written in 45 CFR 170.315(b)(6). If HHS seeks to streamline the certification process, consider clarifying the technical specifications for model card submission or establishing a standardized template, but do not eliminate the transparency requirement itself. The cost of removing it is measured in patient safety incidents, not in developer convenience.‚Źé‚ŹéRespectfully submitted,‚Źé‚ŹéKevin Walker‚ŹéExecutive Director‚ŹéNational Alliance for Care at Home‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-24T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-24T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHospital and health system procurement teams need transparent AI model information to make evidence-based decisions about which tools to adopt and how to implement them safely in their local contexts.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒresigned‚ôĒConnecticut‚ôĒexecutive director‚ôĒ47‚ôĒMy mother spent her final eighteen months in our home in Stamford, and I watched firsthand how fragmented her care became‚ÄĒher oncologist, cardiologist, and primary physician literally couldn't see each other's notes without me acting as a translator between offices. Yet when I think about what happens if we swing too far in the other direction and throw open access to her complete medical history, I can't shake what happened to a board member's sister, whose insurance rates spiked after her mental health records surfaced in an underwriting database she didn't know had been accessed. There has to be a middle path here, but I'm skeptical we'll find it.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ623
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0012‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒMelissa Green‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe commenter opposes removing transparency requirements for AI tools used in medical record analysis, arguing that model cards and testing standards are essential safeguards that enable doctors to understand and trust AI systems rather than burea...‚ôĒI keep thinking back to what happened with my daughter's medical records last year. When she switched rheumatologists, it took three weeks just to get her old test results transferred. The new office said the previous clinic was dragging their feet. At the time I thought, wouldn't it be better if she could just pull everything up herself online? But then I started worrying about what happens if that information gets into the wrong hands without proper safeguards. Here's what concerns me: if we remove the transparency requirements for AI tools that doctors use to read those records and make treatment decisions, how will anyone know if the system is working right? How will my daughter's rheumatologist know if the AI is missing something important for her specific condition? I'm not against innovation, but removing safeguards in the name of making things easier seems backwards. We should want doctors to understand and trust the tools they're using with our health. Model cards and testing requirements aren't bureaucratic red tape. They're what helps everyone, including patients, know what's really happening.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-02-08T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-08T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒmedium‚ôĒconcerned‚ôĒMinnesota‚ôĒretired teacher‚ôĒ55‚ôĒWhen my daughter switched rheumatologists last year, it took three weeks and two phone calls just to get her old test results transferred‚ÄĒthe new office said the previous clinic was "dragging their feet" on the paperwork.
