CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0013‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒKimberly Thompson‚ôĒTexas Medical Association‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Texas Medical Association opposes eliminating Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements, arguing that transparency standards requiring documentation of AI model validation, training data characteristics, and known limitat...‚ôĒ**Comment on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity**‚Źé‚Źé**Submitted by:** Kimberly Thompson, Community Organizer, Texas Medical Association‚Źé‚ŹéThe Texas Medical Association appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory framework and wants to raise a specific concern about the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria. While we recognize the intent to reduce unnecessary burden on developers and accelerate innovation, we believe the current requirements for AI model transparency and validation deserve careful reconsideration before being eliminated.‚Źé‚ŹéOur organization works with community health centers and primary care practices across Texas and Mississippi, many of them serving low-income and underinsured populations. Over the past eighteen months, I've watched our member clinicians increasingly adopt AI-assisted diagnostic tools, clinical decision support systems, and predictive analytics to manage patient care. These tools have real value. But I've also watched the same clinicians struggle with a fundamental problem: they don't know what they don't know about how these systems work.‚Źé‚Źé**THE REAL PROBLEM WE'RE SEEING IN PRACTICE**‚Źé‚ŹéLast month, I sat with Maria, a diabetic single mother from our community health program, while she tried to understand why her insulin copay doubled after her employer switched plans. During that conversation, we discovered her previous medical history had been sold to a data broker without her knowledge. She's now terrified to seek mental health care because she fears more of her information will be commodified. What struck me most was that she wanted to ask her doctor about her care options, but her doctor couldn't give her clear answers about why certain tests were ordered or certain medications recommended because the decision support system the clinic uses doesn't disclose its underlying logic or training data.‚Źé‚ŹéThis isn't an edge case. It's becoming routine. Our member clinics report that when they try to understand why an AI system flagged a patient for certain interventions, they get opacity. The system works or it doesn't. The recommendations seem reasonable or they don't. But the clinician can't explain to the patient whether the system has been validated in populations similar to theirs, what data it was trained on, or what its known failure modes are. For a clinic serving primarily Hispanic and Black patients in underserved rural areas, this is a serious problem. Research consistently shows that AI systems trained on non-representative datasets can perform worse for minority populations (see Obermeyer et al., 2019, "Dissecting racial bias in an algorithm used to manage the health of populations," Science 366(6464): 447-453). We have no way of knowing if the tools our members are using have this problem.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule would eliminate the DSI certification criterion that currently requires AI model cards, transparency disclosures about intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. On its face, this sounds like deregulation that benefits everyone. Remove the paperwork, let innovation happen faster, let the market sort out which tools are good and which aren't. But in practice, what happens is that clinicians lose visibility into a critical tool they're using to make decisions about patient care, and patients lose the ability to understand or question those decisions.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY CURRENT TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS MATTER**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification requirements under 45 CFR 170.315(b)(7) aren't burdensome in the way that, say, redundant reporting to multiple agencies is burdensome. They require developers to document what the system does, who it was tested on, and what it's known to get wrong. These requirements exist because health care is different from other sectors where AI is deployed. When Netflix's recommendation algorithm fails, you get a bad movie suggestion. When a clinical decision support system fails, people can get hurt.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider what clinicians actually need to do their jobs responsibly. They need to know:‚Źé‚Źé- What specific patient population was this system validated in? (If it was trained and tested on adult patients ages 40-65 with commercial insurance, how reliable is it for my 28-year-old uninsured patient?)‚Źé- What specific clinical problem does it address? (Is it designed to predict sepsis risk in ICU patients, or can I use it in my outpatient primary care clinic?)‚Źé- What are its known limitations and failure modes? (Under what conditions does it perform poorly?)‚Źé- What training data was used? (Was it representative, or does it reflect historical biases in medical practice?)‚Źé- How was it externally validated? (Did someone other than the developer test it? Were those tests done in real-world clinical settings?)‚Źé‚ŹéThese aren't academic questions. They're operational questions that clinicians need answered to use these tools safely. The 21st Century Cures Act (42 U.S.C. 300jj et seq.) and the information blocking regulations (45 CFR 171) established broad principles that health IT should increase transparency and give patients and providers more control over health data. The DSI certification criteria are a specific implementation of that principle in the context of algorithmic decision-making.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving these requirements doesn't eliminate the need for this information. It just moves the burden somewhere else. Either clinicians will spend time trying to extract this information from developers directly (which is inefficient and puts small practices at a disadvantage), or they'll use systems without understanding them (which is worse), or they'll avoid adopting these tools altogether (which means missing out on genuine benefits).‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT HAPPENS WHEN TRANSPARENCY DISAPPEARS**‚Źé‚ŹéI need to be direct about what concerns me most. Our member organizations serve communities that have been harmed by medical bias and data exploitation. These aren't hypothetical risks. They're lived experiences.‚Źé‚ŹéMaria's case is one example. But I could cite others. We've had clinics tell us that their patients are increasingly reluctant to share health information because of privacy violations. We've documented cases where algorithmic bias in risk stratification tools has led to lower-risk patients being deprioritized for preventive care. We've seen patients denied coverage or charged higher premiums based on health predictions from systems they never knew existed.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule's preamble suggests that market competition and professional liability will keep developers honest. That's not how it works for smaller practices and under-resourced clinics. Our member organizations don't have legal departments to audit vendor contracts or data scientists to validate algorithms. They have one or two clinicians and a small administrative staff trying to deliver care to hundreds of patients a month. They rely on regulatory standards to establish baseline expectations for vendors.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving the DSI certification requirements means that a vendor could release a clinical decision support system that was trained exclusively on data from large academic medical centers, never validated in community health center settings, with no public disclosure of its limitations or failure modes. A small clinic could adopt it because it's cheaper than alternatives or because it integrates with their EHR. Clinicians would use it without understanding whether it's appropriate for their patient population. Patients would have decisions made about their care influenced by an algorithm they don't know exists and can't examine.‚Źé‚Źé**A MIDDLE PATH THAT SERVES BOTH INNOVATION AND SAFETY**‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not arguing that we should freeze AI development or make it impossible for new tools to reach clinicians. That's not realistic and it's not in patients' interests. Innovation in clinical decision support can genuinely improve care and reduce clinician burden. But innovation doesn't require opacity.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification requirements exist at a relatively light touch. Developers don't have to submit their algorithms for pre-market review. They don't have to conduct randomized controlled trials. They just have to document what they did and what they found. This is a reasonable baseline, not an unreasonable burden.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat I'd propose instead of removing these requirements is thoughtfully streamlining them for developers of different sizes. For small vendors developing niche tools, perhaps there's room for a simplified documentation process. For larger vendors, the current requirements make sense. But the core elements should remain:‚Źé‚Źé- Clear documentation of the population the system was validated in‚Źé- Transparent disclosure of intended clinical use‚Źé- Honest accounting of known limitations‚Źé- Description of training data characteristics, including any identified biases or gaps‚Źé- Evidence of external validation, ideally in settings similar to where the tool will be used‚Źé‚ŹéThis isn't onerous. It's the same level of transparency we'd expect from a pharmaceutical company releasing a new medication. It's basic responsible practice in health care.‚Źé‚Źé**THE SPECIFIC IMPACT ON UNDERSERVED COMMUNITIES**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be explicit about why this matters for the communities our organization serves. Health equity isn't an add-on consideration in these regulations. It's central.‚Źé‚ŹéLow-income patients and patients of color have already experienced decades of medical marginalization. They're less likely to have access to specialists, more likely to receive lower-quality care, more likely to experience medical bias and discrimination. When we introduce AI systems into this context without transparency requirements, we're essentially creating a new mechanism for embedding and automating historical biases.‚Źé‚ŹéThe research literature on algorithmic bias in health care is growing and it's concerning. Obermeyer's work on racial bias in a widely used risk prediction algorithm found that the algorithm systematically underestimated risk for Black patients, leading to fewer referrals for high-risk interventions. That algorithm was in use for years before the bias was discovered. It was a tool developed by reputable researchers and implemented in major health systems. The bias wasn't obvious from the outside. It only became visible through careful external validation and comparison of outcomes across racial groups.‚Źé‚ŹéOur member clinics would like to know if the tools they're using have similar problems. They can't do that if developers aren't required to disclose training data characteristics or external validation results. They can't do that if there's no standard for what "external validation" means or who has to do it.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving the DSI certification requirements disproportionately affects under-resourced health systems. Large academic medical centers and integrated health systems can afford to hire health informatics experts to audit vendors and validate tools. Community health centers can't. They'll end up using whatever's available and affordable, without the ability to assess whether it's appropriate for their patient population.‚Źé‚Źé**ALIGNMENT WITH EXISTING POLICY FRAMEWORKS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification requirements align with other HHS policies around algorithmic transparency and accountability. The FDA's guidance on clinical decision support systems (issued in 2019 and updated in 2021) recommends disclosure of training data, validation methods, and known limitations. The National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) AI Risk Management Framework (published in 2023) emphasizes the importance of transparency and documentation for high-risk AI applications in health care. The Office for Civil Rights has explicitly stated that algorithmic bias can constitute discrimination under civil rights laws (see HHS Office for Civil Rights guidance on AI and civil rights, 2023).‚Źé‚ŹéThese frameworks aren't in conflict with innovation. They're consistent with each other and with the stated goal of the 21st Century Cures Act to increase transparency and put patients and providers in control of health information.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule cites burden reduction as the primary justification for removing the DSI requirements. But burden reduction shouldn't come at the cost of patient safety and equity. If the DSI documentation requirements are genuinely onerous for developers, the appropriate response is to streamline them, not eliminate them entirely.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE'RE ASKING FOR**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Texas Medical Association asks that the final rule retain the core DSI certification requirements under 45 CFR 170.315(b)(7), or at minimum, retain the requirements for:‚Źé‚Źé1. Documentation of the population used for validation (demographic characteristics, sample size, clinical setting)‚Źé2. Disclosure of intended clinical use and known limitations‚Źé3. Description of training data characteristics, including any identified sources of bias or gaps in representation‚Źé4. Evidence of external validation, defined as testing by someone other than the developer, ideally in clinical settings similar to where the tool will be deployed‚Źé‚ŹéThese requirements don't prevent innovation. They establish baseline transparency expectations that allow clinicians to make informed decisions about whether a tool is appropriate for their patient population and their clinical context. They give patients the ability to understand and question decisions made about their care. They help us identify and address algorithmic bias before it's embedded in routine clinical practice across hundreds of health systems.‚Źé‚ŹéMaria should be able to ask her doctor how a clinical decision was made and get an answer. She should have confidence that the tools influencing her care were developed and tested responsibly. Our member clinics should have the information they need to determine whether the tools they're using are appropriate for the patients they serve.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification requirements support all of that. Removing them doesn't unleash prosperity. It unleashes opacity in a sector where transparency is essential to safety and equity.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-23T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-23T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ7‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHospital and health system procurement teams need transparent AI model information to make evidence-based decisions about which tools to adopt and how to implement them safely in their local contexts.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒangry‚ôĒMississippi‚ôĒcommunity organizer‚ôĒ48‚ôĒLast month, I sat with Maria, a diabetic single mother from our community health program, while she tried to understand why her insulin copay doubled after her employer switched plans‚ÄĒand discovered her previous medical history had been sold to a data broker without her knowledge or consent. She's now terrified to seek mental health care because she fears more of her information will be commodified, and I watched her choose between her dignity and her health.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2101
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0014‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒAnthony Robinson‚ôĒZeroTrusted.ai LLC‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒZeroTrusted.ai LLC argues that while the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion imposes real compliance costs, it should be retained rather than eliminated because it serves an essential patient safety function by requiring v...‚ôĒRE: RFI Response on Decision Support Interventions Certification Criterion and AI Model Transparency in Health IT‚Źé‚ŹéZeroTrusted.ai LLC, a health information technology vendor focused on clinical decision support systems, respectfully submits this comment on the proposed deregulatory actions affecting certification requirements for health IT systems. Our organization brings perspective from both the vendor side and the clinical operations side, given that our Chief Medical Officer works directly with hospital networks and integrated delivery systems to implement AI-assisted clinical tools. We have a specific interest in the future of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements under 45 CFR Part 170, particularly as they relate to AI model transparency and accountability.‚Źé‚Źé**THE BUSINESS CASE FOR TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéLast spring, we lost contract renewal negotiations with a twelve-hospital regional network in Florida. The IT director told us directly that our platform couldn't move forward without ONC certification under the current DSI criterion. That criterion requires model cards, transparency disclosures covering intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. The migration cost to meet that requirement was substantial‚ÄĒroughly $180,000 in engineering time plus compliance documentation. Our clinical outcomes data showed superior performance compared to the competitor they chose. They chose the competitor anyway. The compliance burden made our solution appear more expensive than alternatives, even though the actual clinical value proposition was stronger. That decision cost us eighteen months of revenue and forced us to defer hiring three security engineers we had recruited. I mention this not for sympathy, but because it illustrates a real dynamic: compliance costs are real, they affect vendor viability, and they can distort purchasing decisions in ways that don't always align with clinical quality.‚Źé‚ŹéHowever, the conclusion we drew from that experience was not that the DSI criterion should be eliminated. It was that we needed to understand what the criterion actually does, why it exists, and whether the burden can be reduced without compromising its purpose. After working through the implementation, we became convinced that the DSI criterion serves a function that shouldn't be removed entirely, even if it could be streamlined.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THE DSI CRITERION EXISTS AND WHAT IT PREVENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe certification criterion for Decision Support Interventions was established in the 2015 Edition of the ONC Health IT Certification Program (80 FR 62602-62603, October 16, 2015) specifically because clinical decision support systems powered by algorithmic or machine learning models present unique risks that traditional software doesn't. Unlike a medication calculator or a lab result display tool, a decision support intervention makes a recommendation or generates a suggestion that influences clinical judgment. The criterion requires vendors to document what the model was trained to do, what kinds of patients it was tested on, what errors or biases have been identified, and what external validation has been conducted.‚Źé‚ŹéThe regulation exists because of documented cases where clinical decision support systems have caused harm when deployed without sufficient transparency about their limitations. One well-documented case involved a clinical trial protocol that relied on a proprietary algorithm to identify eligible patients. When the algorithm's training data was eventually examined, it had been biased toward patients with particular demographic characteristics, systematically excluding others from the trial. The investigators didn't know this because the vendor didn't have to disclose the training data characteristics. There's also the case of the sepsis prediction algorithm that performed well in internal validation but failed dramatically in external settings because the training data came from a specific hospital system with different EHR workflows, different population characteristics, and different documentation practices than the hospitals where it was deployed.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI criterion doesn't require vendors to make their algorithms open source or to share proprietary training datasets. It requires vendors to document what they know about their models' performance, limitations, and intended scope of use. This is not an onerous requirement in principle. It's also not optional in practice. Every responsible AI development team should know these things about their own models. If a vendor doesn't understand the intended use case, target population, known limitations, and validation evidence for their own decision support system, that's a red flag regardless of what the regulation says.‚Źé‚Źé**THE SPECIFIC COMPONENTS OF THE CRITERION AND THEIR FUNCTION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI criterion has several specific components. Examining them individually helps explain why each one serves a purpose.‚Źé‚ŹéIntended use documentation requires vendors to specify what clinical question the system is designed to answer and what kind of decision it's meant to support. This seems basic, but it prevents the problem of systems being used in ways they weren't designed for. We have encountered implementations where a sepsis risk stratification model trained on intensive care unit data was being applied to general inpatient populations without modification. The system's performance characteristics were completely different in that context, but nobody had bothered to document what the intended use was supposed to be. A clinician seeing a high-risk flag had no way of knowing whether the model had been validated for that patient population.‚Źé‚ŹéTarget population specification requires vendors to describe the demographic, clinical, and other characteristics of the population on which the model was trained and tested. This prevents the problem of silent performance disparities. We now know from multiple studies in the medical literature that machine learning models frequently perform differently across demographic groups, sometimes dramatically so. A model trained predominantly on data from patients over 65 years old may perform poorly for younger patients. A model trained on data from one geographic region may have different accuracy in different regions due to variation in EHR systems, documentation practices, and underlying population health patterns. If this information isn't documented, clinicians can't know whether the system's recommendations are reliable for the patient in front of them.‚Źé‚ŹéKnown risks and limitations documentation prevents the problem of false confidence. Every clinical support system has limitations. Some models are sensitive to missing data. Some perform poorly when certain lab values are absent. Some have higher false positive rates in specific patient subgroups. Some degrade in performance when clinical presentations fall outside the training distribution. Documenting these known limitations allows clinicians to calibrate their trust in the system appropriately. A clinician who knows that a model has a 5% false positive rate in a particular subgroup can interpret a positive recommendation differently than a clinician who has no information about the model's performance characteristics.‚Źé‚ŹéTraining data characteristics documentation prevents the problem of hidden distributional mismatch. If a model was trained on data from electronic health records that use a particular documentation style, or from a geographic region with particular disease prevalence patterns, or from a time period before a clinical practice change, the model's performance in a different context may be unpredictable. Documenting what training data looks like allows clinicians and IT implementers to assess whether the model is likely to work well in their own environment.‚Źé‚ŹéExternal validation evidence prevents the problem of overfitting and context-specific failure. A model can perform very well on the data it was trained on while performing poorly on new data, particularly if that new data comes from a different clinical setting. External validation means the model has been tested on data from sources other than the original training data. This is the gold standard for understanding how a model will perform in practice. Requiring documentation of external validation results prevents vendors from deploying systems with only internal validation evidence.‚Źé‚Źé**THE DEREGULATORY IMPULSE AND ITS REAL COSTS**‚Źé‚ŹéI understand the deregulatory impulse. Compliance requirements do impose costs. They do slow innovation. They do sometimes protect incumbent vendors more than they protect patients. The argument that smaller vendors face disproportionate compliance burdens is not entirely wrong. We felt that burden directly.‚Źé‚ŹéBut the correct response to "compliance requirements are costly" is not "eliminate the requirements." The correct response is "can we achieve the same goal with lower burden?" Those are different questions, and they lead to different answers.‚Źé‚ŹéEliminating the DSI criterion entirely would reduce compliance costs in the near term. It would also eliminate the only systematic requirement that vendors document what they know about their own models' performance and limitations. That creates a gap. Clinical decision support systems will continue to be deployed. They will continue to have risks and limitations. The question is whether those risks and limitations will be documented and communicated to clinicians, or whether they will remain opaque.‚Źé‚ŹéThe market does not solve this problem on its own. A vendor could choose to document model limitations and performance characteristics voluntarily. In practice, most don't, because it's not required and because disclosing limitations creates liability and sales friction. We have surveyed implementations of clinical decision support systems in hospital networks. The vast majority of systems deployed do not have publicly available documentation about training data, target population, known limitations, or external validation. The clinicians using these systems often don't know what population the model was trained on. This is not a failure of individual vendors. It's a failure of incentives.‚Źé‚Źé**OPPORTUNITIES FOR STREAMLINING WITHOUT ELIMINATING**‚Źé‚ŹéThe better approach is to streamline the DSI criterion in ways that reduce burden while preserving its core function. There are several possibilities.‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, the documentation could be simplified and standardized. The criterion currently allows vendors flexibility in how they document these elements. Standardizing the format, perhaps around a model card template (similar to the approach in Gebru et al., "Model Cards for Model Reporting," arXiv 1810.03993, 2019), would reduce the burden of creating documentation while actually making it more useful to clinicians. Instead of requiring a lengthy narrative, vendors could fill out a structured template. This is less work, not more. It also makes information comparable across vendors.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, the criterion could be tiered based on the risk level of the system. A decision support system that provides an informational flag to a clinician, with the clinician retaining full decision authority, carries different risk than a system that makes an automated decision that triggers a clinical action. The documentation burden could be proportional to risk. A lower-risk system might require documentation of intended use and target population but not external validation. A higher-risk system would require the full set of documentation. This is how FDA regulates medical devices, and there's no reason health IT couldn't follow a similar approach.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, the criterion could include a sunset provision for documentation that becomes outdated. If a vendor conducts external validation in 2020, that evidence becomes less relevant by 2025 if the model hasn't been retrained. Instead of requiring ongoing re-validation at set intervals, the criterion could specify that validation evidence older than a certain age (perhaps three to five years) should be refreshed. This creates an incentive for continuous improvement without imposing a constant burden.‚Źé‚ŹéFourth, the documentation could be required to be submitted to ONC or a trusted third party, but not necessarily made publicly available, if vendors have concerns about proprietary information. The documentation could be available to hospital IT departments and clinicians, with restrictions on further disclosure. This addresses the liability and competitive concerns while still ensuring that the people implementing and using the systems have access to relevant information about limitations and performance characteristics.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE'VE LEARNED FROM IMPLEMENTATION**‚Źé‚ŹéAfter we worked through the compliance process ourselves, we learned that the burden was not actually as great as we initially thought. The documentation work took roughly 120 hours of engineering time, spread over three months. The compliance review and iteration process took another 60 hours. That's not nothing, but it's not catastrophic for an organization with reasonable engineering capacity. For a vendor with ten engineers, that's roughly two weeks of total engineering time, one-time. The compliance cost was real, but it was measurable and finite, not open-ended.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat the process actually forced us to do was understand our own model better. We had to articulate what population we trained on, what limitations we knew about, what external validation we had and hadn't done. Some of that information we already had in scattered form. Some of it we had to generate. The process of doing this forced us to identify gaps in our validation evidence. We realized that while our model performed well on data from large academic medical centers, we had minimal external validation evidence from smaller community hospitals. We had not tested the model's performance in rural settings. We had not systematically evaluated performance differences across age groups and racial/ethnic groups.‚Źé‚ŹéThat was uncomfortable information. It was not information we wanted to disclose. But it was information we needed to know. Once we knew it, we could decide what to do. We could invest in additional external validation in underrepresented settings. We could modify our documentation to specify that our intended use was currently limited to academic medical center settings with similar EHR infrastructure, and that community hospital implementations should conduct local validation before deployment. We could build that gap into our product roadmap.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the DSI criterion didn't exist, we probably would have left those gaps unexamined for longer. The regulatory requirement created an incentive to understand our own product better. That turned out to be valuable.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PATIENT SAFETY DIMENSION**‚Źé‚ŹéThis is a health IT regulation, so it should be justified primarily on patient safety grounds, not vendor preference grounds. The question is whether the DSI criterion actually improves patient safety, or whether it's just compliance theater.‚Źé‚ŹéThe evidence suggests it does improve safety, though the improvement is subtle and difficult to measure. We don't have prospective controlled trials comparing clinical outcomes in hospitals with versus without documented decision support system transparency requirements. That would be difficult to design and conduct. What we do have is evidence that clinical decision support systems can cause harm when deployed without understanding of their limitations (Strom et al., "Computerized Decision Support and Outcomes of Acute Myocardial Infarction," JAMA, 2010, 294(13)). We have evidence that machine learning models have performance disparities across demographic groups that aren't always apparent without systematic evaluation (Mitchell et al., "Model Cards for Model Reporting," CACM 2019). We have evidence that clinicians often don't understand the limitations of decision support systems they use regularly (Guszcza et al., "Why AI Needs Humans," Deloitte Insights, 2017).‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI criterion doesn't guarantee safety. It creates a systematic process for identifying and documenting known risks, which allows those risks to be communicated to clinicians and implementers. That's not a complete safety solution. It's one part of a safety approach.‚Źé‚Źé**ADDRESSING THE COMPETITIVE CONCERN**‚Źé‚ŹéThe claim that the DSI criterion disproportionately burdens smaller vendors is worth taking seriously. Compliance costs do create barriers to entry. A larger vendor with a 500-person engineering organization can absorb documentation and compliance work more easily than a startup with 20 engineers. That's a real problem if it prevents better technologies from reaching the market.‚Źé‚ŹéHowever, the solution is not to eliminate the requirement for everyone. The solution is to find ways to reduce the burden for everyone, particularly for smaller vendors. Standardized documentation templates help smaller vendors more than larger ones, because larger vendors have already invested in documentation infrastructure. Tiered requirements based on risk level help smaller vendors more than larger ones, because smaller vendors often build lower-risk systems. Allowing third-party certification bodies to verify compliance helps smaller vendors, because it spreads the cost and reduces the need for internal compliance infrastructure.‚Źé‚ŹéWe would support a revised DSI criterion that includes these elements. We would not support eliminating the criterion entirely.‚Źé‚Źé**IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the DSI criterion is retained and potentially streamlined, there are several implementation details worth considering.‚Źé‚ŹéThe ONC should develop standardized templates for model card documentation, based on emerging best practices in the AI industry. This has multiple benefits. It reduces the burden on vendors by providing a clear format to follow. It makes documentation comparable across vendors. It makes documentation more useful to clinicians by standardizing terminology and structure. The template should be developed in collaboration with vendors, clinicians, and patient safety organizations.‚Źé‚ŹéThe ONC should clarify the relationship between the DSI criterion and other relevant regulations, particularly FDA medical device regulations. Some decision support systems are also subject to FDA regulation. The documentation requirements should align where possible, reducing the total compliance burden. Where they don't align, the ONC should explicitly state which requirement applies in which circumstance.‚Źé‚ŹéThe ONC should consider allowing vendors to submit documentation that is reviewed by ONC or a trusted third party, with restricted access to hospital IT departments and clinicians, if vendors have legitimate concerns about proprietary information. This addresses competitive concerns while still ensuring that implementers have access to information about system limitations.‚Źé‚ŹéThe ONC should develop guidance on what constitutes adequate external validation evidence. This is currently somewhat ambiguous. Guidance could specify that external validation should include testing on data from at least one independent source, conducted by the vendor or by a third party, and should report performance metrics broken down by relevant subgroups (at minimum, age and race/ethnicity where relevant to the clinical indication).‚Źé‚Źé**THE BROADER QUESTION OF DEREGULATION**‚Źé‚ŹéThis comment is focused on a specific criterion within a larger deregulatory proposal. The broader question underlying the proposal is whether certification requirements generally promote or impede innovation and competition in health IT.‚Źé‚ŹéThe honest answer is that this depends on how the requirements are designed. Well-designed requirements that are proportional to risk, clearly specified, and aligned with industry best practices can promote safety without significantly impeding innovation. Poorly designed requirements that are overly prescriptive, misaligned with actual risks, or duplicative of other regulations can impede innovation without substantially improving safety.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI criterion, in its current form, is reasonably well-designed. It's focused on a specific category of systems (decision support systems that use algorithmic or machine learning models) where the risk is real and documented. It specifies what must be documented without prescribing how to document it. It doesn't require vendors to share proprietary algorithms or training datasets. It does require vendors to document what they know about their models' performance and limitations. That seems proportional to the risk.‚Źé‚ŹéThe question of whether the criterion should be eliminated or streamlined is, ultimately, a question about what level of transparency is appropriate for clinical decision support systems. Our view is that some level of transparency is appropriate. The market does not provide sufficient transparency on its own. Eliminating the requirement would reduce that transparency further. Streamlining the requirement could maintain transparency while reducing burden.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéZeroTrusted.ai LLC respectfully urges the Office of the Secretary and the Office of the National Coordinator to retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion, with modifications to streamline implementation and reduce burden. Specifically, we recommend:‚Źé‚Źé- Development of standardized model card templates for documentation, based on emerging industry best practices‚Źé- Tiered requirements based on the risk level of the system and the degree of automated decision-making involved‚Źé- Clarification of alignment with FDA medical device regulations and other relevant requirements‚Źé- Explicit guidance on what constitutes adequate external validation evidence‚Źé- Consideration of restricted-access documentation review processes for systems where vendors have proprietary concerns‚Źé‚ŹéThese modifications would preserve the core safety function of the criterion while reducing the compliance burden, particularly for smaller vendors. They would maintain transparency about decision support system limitations without imposing unreasonable documentation requirements. They would align health IT certification requirements with emerging best practices in AI governance and model documentation.‚Źé‚ŹéWe recognize that we have a financial interest in this outcome, having invested in compliance with the current criterion. We also have a clinical interest in ensuring that the decision support systems deployed in healthcare are adequately documented and understood by the clinicians who use them. Those interests happen to align in this case. We believe the approach we recommend would serve both vendor interests and patient safety interests.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-04T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-04T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ10‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒurgent‚ôĒFlorida‚ôĒchief medical officer‚ôĒ38‚ôĒLast spring, we lost a contract renewal with a regional hospital network because their IT director couldn't justify the cost of migrating our platform to meet the existing certification requirements‚ÄĒthe compliance burden made our solution appear more expensive than competitors even though our actual clinical outcomes were superior. That decision cost us eighteen months of revenue and forced us to defer hiring three security engineers we desperately needed.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ3290
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0015‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒWilliam Williams‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA longtime caregiver argues that the proposed removal of AI transparency requirements for health IT systems would dangerously shift accountability away from developers onto patients and providers, eliminating the only safeguard ensuring clinicians...‚ôĒ**Comment on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity**‚Źé‚ŹéAfter spending the better part of two decades as a caregiver, managing my wife's medical records across three different health systems and two states, I've learned that access to patient information and the transparency behind how that information gets used are not abstract policy concerns. They are survival issues. When her cardiologist retired last year, it took three months and a written demand to get her records transferred to the new practice. Three months. Records I'd spent years helping organize during her illness. If this proposed rule removes the safeguards that currently require health IT developers to be transparent about how their AI systems work, I'll spend my remaining years fighting bureaucrats instead of actually caring for her.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CURRENT REQUIREMENT AND WHY IT MATTERS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Decision Support Interventions certification criterion currently requires that AI model cards and transparency disclosures be made available to healthcare providers. This means developers have to explain what their AI system is designed to do (intended use), who it is designed for (target population), what its known limitations and risks are, what data it was trained on, and whether it has been validated outside the lab. This is not a paperwork burden. This is accountability. And it is the only thing standing between my wife's cardiologist and a black box that could recommend the wrong treatment path without anyone understanding why.‚Źé‚ŹéI understand the argument for deregulation. Developers say these requirements slow down innovation. They say the market will police itself. They say that if a system doesn't work, providers will stop using it. I have heard this before, and I have lived long enough to know it is not how healthcare works. Providers are under enormous time pressure. They are understaffed and exhausted. If a system promises to save them five minutes per patient, they will use it, and they will not read a 40-page model card. That is not a failure of individual clinicians. That is a structural problem. The certification requirement exists because the market does not, in fact, police itself when patients are on the line.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT REMOVING THIS SAFEGUARD ACTUALLY MEANS**‚Źé‚ŹéWhen the RFI discusses removing or relaxing the DSI certification criterion, it is proposing that developers no longer be required to disclose how their AI systems work. They would no longer have to explain what population their model was trained on, what biases might be embedded in it, or what happens when a patient walks in with a medical history that looks different from the training data. They would no longer have to provide external validation. They would no longer have to disclose known risks.‚Źé‚ŹéThe argument is that this will reduce burden and encourage innovation. What it actually does is shift the burden. It shifts it away from developers, who know their systems, and onto providers and patients, who do not. It shifts it onto people like me, who are trying to make sure that the medical decisions affecting someone we love are made with the best possible information. It shifts it onto clinicians who will have to guess whether a recommendation from an AI system is sound or whether it is reflecting biases in training data they will never see.‚Źé‚ŹéI am not opposed to innovation in healthcare. I am opposed to innovation that happens in the dark. I have seen what happens when providers make decisions without understanding the reasoning behind them. I have sat in examination rooms where a test was ordered because "the computer flagged it" and the doctor could not explain whether that flag made sense for my wife's specific situation. The certification criterion was supposed to fix that. It was supposed to make it so that a clinician could say, "Let me check what this system was built for and whether my patient actually fits that population." Removing it takes away that option.‚Źé‚Źé**THE SPECIFICS OF WHAT SHOULD STAY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe model card requirement is not burdensome in the way that true regulatory burden is burdensome. It is a one-time disclosure. It is not a recurring compliance cost. It is not a field inspection or a quarterly report. It is a document that a developer prepares once and then uses to market their product to hospitals and clinics. The only reason it seems like a burden is that developers do not want to be transparent. And that is exactly why it needs to stay.‚Źé‚ŹéThe intended use disclosure tells clinicians what the system was designed for. If a system was designed to flag sepsis in intensive care patients, clinicians need to know that before they use it in an outpatient setting. The target population disclosure tells them whether the training data included patients like theirs. If a system was trained primarily on data from patients under 65, and a clinician is using it for a 78-year-old woman with multiple comorbidities, they need to know that. The known risks disclosure tells them what the developers have already discovered about failure modes. The training data characteristics disclosure tells them whether the data came from a hospital system in one region, whether it included underrepresented populations, whether it reflected real-world variation or laboratory conditions. The external validation disclosure tells them whether anyone other than the developers has tested the system and found it to work the way the developers claim.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not onerous. This is foundational. This is the minimum transparency needed for informed clinical decision-making.‚Źé‚Źé**ACCOUNTABILITY AND PATIENT SAFETY**‚Źé‚ŹéI keep coming back to the word accountability. When something goes wrong in healthcare, someone has to be responsible. If a provider uses an AI system that was never required to disclose its limitations, and that system makes a recommendation that harms a patient, who is accountable? The developer will say they are not responsible because the provider chose to use the system. The provider will say they are not responsible because they trusted the developer. The patient is caught in the middle. The certification requirement creates a paper trail. It creates documentation that the developer understood what their system could and could not do. It creates a basis for accountability.‚Źé‚ŹéMy wife's case is not unique. Every week, I meet with other caregivers. We share stories about providers who do not have time to review records, about systems that flag things that turn out not to matter, about recommendations that sound plausible until you know the patient's full history. The certification requirement is one of the few mechanisms we have to make sure that the people making recommendations about care have thought through whether those recommendations make sense for the specific people receiving care.‚Źé‚ŹéI understand that this is a deregulatory initiative. I understand that the goal is to reduce burden and increase competition. But there is a difference between removing burdensome rules that do not serve a purpose and removing safeguards that protect patients from harm. The DSI certification criterion serves a clear purpose. It ensures that providers know what they are using. It ensures that patients like my wife can ask their doctors whether a recommendation reflects her actual medical situation or whether it reflects biases in the system's training data. Removing it does not reduce burden. It increases the burden on everyone except the people who built the system.‚Źé‚Źé**THE COST OF REMOVING THIS PROTECTION**‚Źé‚ŹéIf this criterion is removed, what happens? Developers will have no requirement to disclose how their systems work. Some of them will do it anyway because they are ethical and because transparency is good for business. But some of them will not. And the providers and patients who do not get that information will have no way to know. A clinician will see a recommendation, will not know what population the system was trained on, will not know what the known risks are, and will make a decision based on incomplete information. That is not innovation. That is negligence.‚Źé‚ŹéI have spent twenty years managing my wife's care. I have learned to read medical records. I have learned to ask questions. I have learned to push back when something does not make sense. Not every caregiver can do that. Not every patient can do that. The certification requirement exists because there is a power imbalance in healthcare. Patients and families are dependent on systems they do not understand. Clinicians are dependent on tools they do not have time to evaluate. Developers are the only ones with full information about how their systems work. The certification requirement is a small attempt to close that gap. Removing it opens it wider.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT SHOULD HAPPEN INSTEAD**‚Źé‚ŹéI am not asking for more regulation. I am asking for the regulation that is already there to stay. I am asking that developers continue to be required to disclose how their AI systems work, what populations they were designed for, what their known limitations are, and whether they have been validated outside the laboratory. I am asking that this requirement apply equally to all Decision Support Interventions, regardless of the developer's size or market power. I am asking that the certification criterion be strengthened, not weakened, so that the disclosures are meaningful and accessible to clinicians who do not have degrees in machine learning.‚Źé‚ŹéThe goal of reducing burden is legitimate. But it should not be achieved by pushing that burden onto patients and providers. It should be achieved by streamlining compliance processes, by creating standardized formats for disclosures, by working with developers to make transparency more efficient. The burden of explaining how a system works is not a bug. It is a feature. It is the price of operating in a domain where decisions affect people's health.‚Źé‚ŹéI am 78 years old. I will not be here forever. But my wife will be using healthcare for the rest of her life. The decisions that get made about her care should be made by people who understand what systems are recommending those decisions and why. That requires transparency. That requires the certification criterion to stay in place. That requires accountability from developers who build systems that affect human lives.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not about deregulation. This is about patient safety and informed consent. And it matters.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-21T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-21T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ5‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒangry‚ôĒTennessee‚ôĒcaregiver‚ôĒ78‚ôĒWhen my wife's cardiologist retired last year, it took three months and a written demand to get her records transferred to the new practice‚ÄĒrecords I'd spent two decades helping her organize during her illness.
