CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0016‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒCharles Johnson‚ôĒXSELL Technologies‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒXSELL Technologies opposes weakening or removing the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion for AI-assisted clinical tools, arguing that the current transparency and validation requirements are essential for patient safety and that...‚ôĒ**XSELL Technologies Comments on Decision Support Interventions Certification Criteria**‚Źé‚ŹéXSELL Technologies submits these comments in response to the proposed deregulatory actions affecting Health IT certification criteria. Our organization has spent the last six years helping smaller health IT vendors and provider organizations navigate compliance requirements, and we want to address what we see as a critical issue: the proposal to remove or weaken the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion for AI-assisted clinical tools.‚Źé‚ŹéWe understand the impulse to reduce certification burden. Last year, we watched one of our smaller clients‚ÄĒa provider network in rural Colorado‚ÄĒspend $80,000 and six months of staff time certifying a legacy EHR module that represented less than 2% of their actual clinical workflow. The cost-to-benefit ratio was genuinely painful to watch. We get why vendors and providers want relief. But the DSI criterion is not the place to find it. The current requirement for AI model cards, transparency disclosures, and external validation is working, and removing it would create real patient safety risks that will cost far more than the compliance savings.‚Źé‚Źé**Why the DSI Criterion Matters**‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI criterion, as currently structured under 45 CFR 170.315(a)(1), requires vendors to disclose the intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results for any AI-based clinical decision support. This is not bureaucratic theater. It is the only systematic way that clinicians, health system administrators, and patients can understand what an algorithm actually does, who it was tested on, and where it might fail.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider the real-world implications. In 2022, the FDA and CMS both identified serious issues with AI algorithms used in clinical settings (see FDA guidance on AI/ML in medical devices, published December 2021). Sepsis prediction tools, for example, often perform differently across racial groups because training data reflects historical disparities in care. A clinician using such a tool without knowing its validation limitations could inadvertently perpetuate harm. The DSI criterion creates accountability by requiring vendors to disclose these limitations upfront.‚Źé‚ŹéWe have also seen smaller vendors use the DSI certification process not as a burden, but as a competitive advantage. By obtaining certification and transparently publishing their model cards, they differentiate themselves from uncertified competitors. One vendor we work with in the diabetes management space gained significant market traction by being transparent about their algorithm's performance in Type 2 versus Type 1 populations. The certification process forced rigor; the transparency created trust.‚Źé‚Źé**Specific Concerns with Removal**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the DSI criterion is removed or substantially weakened, three things will happen:‚Źé‚Źé1. Vendors will deploy AI tools without standardized transparency requirements, making it impossible for providers to compare safety profiles or for patients to understand what's influencing clinical decisions about their care.‚Źé‚Źé2. Smaller vendors will face greater pressure to compete on price alone, because they cannot differentiate on safety or transparency. This consolidates the market toward larger vendors with existing customer bases, not toward innovation.‚Źé‚Źé3. Liability exposure shifts entirely to providers. Without certified, transparent AI tools, health systems implementing AI decision support will bear full responsibility if the algorithm fails. Ironically, this increases provider compliance costs rather than reducing them.‚Źé‚Źé**Our Recommendation**‚Źé‚ŹéRetain the DSI certification criterion. If the concern is compliance cost, consider tiered approaches: allow vendors to certify at different levels of rigor, or create exemptions for lower-risk decision support tools (e.g., reminders with no treatment recommendations). But do not remove the requirement for transparency disclosures and external validation.‚Źé‚ŹéWe also recommend clarifying that compliance with the DSI criterion does not preclude innovation. Vendors should be able to use modern machine learning techniques, including ensemble methods and deep learning, as long as they disclose performance characteristics and known limitations. The criterion constrains transparency, not technology.‚Źé‚ŹéThe deregulatory impulse is sound. Reducing unnecessary burden helps smaller organizations compete. But patient safety in AI-assisted healthcare depends on the information asymmetry being closed. The DSI criterion does that. Removing it trades short-term compliance relief for long-term patient and provider risk.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-27T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-27T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒsupportive‚ôĒColorado‚ôĒcompliance officer‚ôĒ36‚ôĒLast year, I spent six months shepherding our smaller clients through a certification audit for a legacy EHR module that represented maybe 2% of their actual workflow‚ÄĒthe compliance costs alone ran them $80,000, and watching them debate whether to absorb it or discontinue the module altogether made me realize how badly these requirements are calibrated to real business constraints.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ659
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0017‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒMichelle Martinez‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA patient with rheumatoid arthritis argues against removing certification requirements for clinical decision support AI systems, contending that while data interoperability improvements are necessary, eliminating transparency mandates for AI tools...‚ôĒ**COMMENT TO THE OFFICE OF THE NATIONAL COORDINATOR FOR HEALTH INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY**‚Źé‚Źé**RE: RFI on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions**‚Źé‚ŹéWhen my rheumatologist retired last year, I spent three months trying to get my imaging records transferred to my new doctor. Three months. My condition was flaring the whole time, and treatment kept getting delayed because the new clinic couldn't see what the old one had documented about my disease progression over the previous five years. I finally had to drive forty miles to the old office myself, sit in their records department, and get copies burned onto a disc. That was in 2023. We're supposed to have solved this problem by now.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm writing because I'm concerned about where this deregulation effort is headed, particularly around the proposed removal of certification criteria for Decision Support Interventions (DSI). I understand that reducing burden on developers can spur innovation. I get that. But I also know what it feels like to have your medical information trapped in silos while you're the one suffering the consequences. If we're going to actually fix that problem‚ÄĒmake data move between providers the way it should‚ÄĒwe can't do it by stripping away the safeguards that keep AI systems honest about what they're doing and who they might hurt.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PROBLEM I EXPERIENCED, AND WHY IT MATTERS FOR THIS RULE**‚Źé‚ŹéLet me be clear about what happened to me. My rheumatologist's office didn't refuse to share my records because they wanted to block information. They had legitimate workflow reasons. The old EHR system wasn't set up to send imaging reports in a format the new clinic could use. There were technical incompatibilities. There was staff turnover. There was no financial incentive to make the transfer smooth. I understand all of that. But none of those reasons made my flare less painful or my treatment less delayed. The patient pays the price for system fragmentation, not the providers.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is where I get worried about what the proposed changes might do. Yes, we need interoperability. Yes, we need data portability. But if we remove the transparency requirements for AI-driven clinical decision support tools in the process, we're trading one problem for another. We're saying it's okay for algorithms to make recommendations about my care as long as developers don't have to tell anyone how those algorithms actually work, what population they were tested on, or what risks they carry.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm a veteran. I've seen enough institutional failures to know that good intentions and light-touch regulation don't always end the same way. The current DSI certification criterion requires model cards and transparency disclosures. Developers have to document the intended use, target population, known risks, and training data characteristics. They have to show external validation. This isn't bureaucratic busywork. This is what keeps a tool from recommending the wrong treatment for the wrong patient.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THE DECISION SUPPORT INTERVENTIONS CRITERION MATTERS**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to understand what's going into my care decisions. Not because I distrust my doctors. I trust my rheumatologist completely. But AI systems don't think like doctors. They find patterns in data. They're trained on populations that may not look like me. They can fail in ways that aren't obvious until someone gets hurt.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criterion that's under review does three specific things:‚Źé‚Źé1. It requires developers to disclose intended use and target population. This means if an AI tool was trained on data from younger patients, we actually know that before it's used to make recommendations for someone my age.‚Źé‚Źé2. It requires documentation of known risks. Every tool has failure modes. Every algorithm has edge cases. Making developers put those in writing means clinicians can say, "Okay, this tool is great for most patients, but I need to be careful with this person because of X."‚Źé‚Źé3. It requires external validation. This isn't the developer testing their own tool on their own data. This is independent verification that the thing actually works the way it's supposed to work on real patients in real clinical settings.‚Źé‚ŹéWithout these requirements, what happens? Developers can still build AI systems. Clinicians can still use them. But nobody has to tell the patient, the doctor, or anyone else what assumptions the system is making about them. Nobody has to say what populations it was trained on or what it's bad at. That's the part that keeps me up at night.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT DEREGULATION COULD MEAN FOR PATIENT SAFETY**‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not opposed to innovation. I know that removing compliance burden can help smaller developers bring new tools to market. I understand the economic argument. But there's a difference between removing unnecessary red tape and removing the one thing that lets patients and clinicians understand what a tool is actually doing.‚Źé‚ŹéThink about my situation again. Imagine if, instead of just struggling with data transfer, I'd had a clinical decision support system recommending my treatment. Imagine that system was built on data that included mostly younger, less complicated cases. Imagine it recommended a medication that's usually fine but carries higher risks for someone with my comorbidities. Now imagine nobody has to tell my doctor about the population that system was trained on, or what the known risks are for patients like me. That's the scenario we're creating if we strip the DSI certification criterion.‚Źé‚ŹéThe problem isn't that the criterion is too strict. The problem isn't that it's slowing down innovation in any meaningful way. The problem, if there is one, is that compliance costs money. Developers have to hire someone to write model cards. They have to conduct external validation studies. That's not nothing. But that's also not a reason to eliminate transparency entirely. That's a reason to maybe streamline the process, clarify the requirements, or offer guidance. It's not a reason to say, "Never mind, developers don't have to explain how their tools work anymore."‚Źé‚Źé**THE DIFFERENCE BETWEEN REMOVING BURDEN AND REMOVING ACCOUNTABILITY**‚Źé‚ŹéI keep coming back to something I read in the rule notice. It talks about "reducing burden on developers" and "promoting innovation and market competition." Those are legitimate goals. But they're not the only goals. Patient safety is also a goal. Informed clinical decision-making is a goal. Accountability is a goal. And right now, the DSI certification criterion is what delivers on those goals.‚Źé‚ŹéIf we remove it, what's the alternative? Are developers going to voluntarily provide model cards and transparency disclosures? Some might. The good ones probably will. But the ones that see compliance as pure cost will skip it. They'll build tools that work on the bench, maybe work in their own testing, and we won't know what they actually do until they're already in use at scale.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Department of Health and Human Services and ONC have said they want to "unleash prosperity." I get that. I want prosperity too. But prosperity that comes at the cost of patients not knowing how their care is being decided isn't the kind of prosperity I'm interested in. There's a reason Congress passed the 21st Century Cures Act. There's a reason the Cures Act has specific requirements for clinical decision support transparency. It's because we learned the hard way that when you let developers build black boxes, patients and clinicians suffer.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT I'M ASKING FOR**‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what I'd like to see instead of removing the DSI certification criterion: Keep it. Make it work better. If there are parts of it that are genuinely unnecessary or duplicative, streamline those. If there are ways to make compliance cheaper or easier without eliminating transparency, do that. But don't remove the requirement that developers explain what their tools do, who they were tested on, and what they're bad at.‚Źé‚ŹéThe data that needs to be disclosed under the current criterion is:‚Źé‚Źé- Intended use and target population‚Źé- Known risks and limitations‚Źé- Training data characteristics‚Źé- External validation results‚Źé‚ŹéThis isn't asking for trade secrets. This isn't asking for proprietary algorithms to be published. This is asking for honesty about what a tool does and doesn't do. That's a bare minimum for something that's going to influence my care.‚Źé‚ŹéI also want to see clarity on what "innovation" means in this context. If innovation means better tools that work for more patients, I'm all for it. If innovation means tools that nobody understands and nobody can hold accountable, that's not innovation. That's just risk shifting. It's shifting the risk from developers and clinicians onto patients like me.‚Źé‚Źé**CLOSING THOUGHTS**‚Źé‚ŹéWhen I was finally able to get my records from my old rheumatologist, I got them on a disc. That disc sat on my desk for a week before I could drive it to the new clinic. A week. In 2023, in a major metropolitan area, with two well-established medical offices. That's how fragmented our system is. That's the real problem we need to solve.‚Źé‚ŹéBut solving it by removing transparency from clinical decision support tools isn't the answer. We can fix data interoperability and information blocking without gutting the safeguards that keep AI systems accountable. We should. My flare last year is over, but the next one is coming. When it does, I want my doctors to have access to all my records. And I want them to understand, completely and transparently, how any AI system is helping them decide what to do about it.‚Źé‚ŹéKeep the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion. Make it work better if you need to. But don't remove it.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-31T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-31T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ5‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒurgent‚ôĒMinnesota‚ôĒveteran‚ôĒ62‚ôĒWhen my rheumatologist retired last year, I spent three months calling between clinics trying to get my imaging records transferred so my new doctor could see my progression‚ÄĒmeanwhile my condition was flaring and treatment kept getting delayed. I finally had to drive forty miles to the old office and sit in their records department myself to get copies on a disc. If providers could actually share my data seamlessly like they claim they want to, I wouldn't have lost months of care coordination, but I'm worried that making it easier to move my information around might mean less control over who's actually looking at it.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1577
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0018‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDorothy King‚ôĒDental Standards Institute‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Dental Standards Institute opposes removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements, arguing that model card documentation is essential for ensuring AI clinical tools are transparent, equitable, and safe‚ÄĒparticularly ...‚ôĒ**DENTAL STANDARDS INSTITUTE**‚Źé**Comment on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Dental Standards Institute submits this comment in response to the proposed deregulatory framework affecting health information technology certification criteria. Our organization represents dental practitioners, public health advocates, and community health workers across Indiana and bordering regions. We work directly with low-income families navigating healthcare access, and we have a specific concern about the removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements.‚Źé‚Źé**BACKGROUND: TRUST AND TRANSPARENCY IN VULNERABLE COMMUNITIES**‚Źé‚ŹéOver the past eighteen months, our organization coordinated a dental clinic expansion into two underserved neighborhoods on Indianapolis's southeast side. Both neighborhoods have median household incomes below the federal poverty line, and roughly 65 percent of residents are uninsured or underinsured. When we launched the clinics, we expected the primary barrier to be financial. We were partly right, but not in the way we anticipated.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat we actually encountered was something harder to quantify but more damaging to overcome: families were avoiding treatment not because they couldn't afford it, but because they feared their information would be flagged. Some worried that seeking dental care would trigger immigration enforcement queries. Others feared that documenting their income or household composition would affect their eligibility for other assistance programs. A few families explicitly said they didn't trust that the clinic's confidentiality promises were genuine. One woman told our outreach coordinator: "I've heard too many promises before."‚Źé‚ŹéThe clinic was explicitly confidential. We had processes in place. We had posted notices. It didn't matter. The barrier wasn't policy; it was trust, and trust doesn't get rebuilt by issuing more assurances. It gets rebuilt slowly, through demonstrated consistency and through knowing that the systems people depend on have been designed with their vulnerability in mind.‚Źé‚ŹéIt took four months of presence in the community, conversations with religious leaders, and one-on-one conversations with families before we saw consistent clinic attendance. We learned during that time that transparency about how systems work and what safeguards exist matters enormously to people who have been burned before. When a person from a marginalized community can see that something has been independently verified, that someone outside the system has checked it, that reduces the perceived risk of using it. The opposite is also true: when safeguards are removed or made opaque, people withdraw.‚Źé‚Źé**THE DSI CERTIFICATION REQUIREMENT AND PATIENT SAFETY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule would remove the certification criterion requiring Decision Support Interventions to include model cards containing information about intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. This criterion currently appears in 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1)(ii) and supporting guidance documents. We believe removing it would be a mistake, particularly in dental and primary care settings serving vulnerable populations.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's why this matters beyond abstract principle: artificial intelligence systems used in clinical decision support are opaque by nature. When a dentist uses an AI-assisted diagnostic tool to evaluate whether a patient has early periodontal disease or caries risk, neither the dentist nor the patient can easily understand how the system reached that conclusion. The system was trained on some dataset, optimized according to some objective function, and tuned by someone with particular assumptions about what matters. Those choices shape what the tool will recommend.‚Źé‚ŹéIf that tool was trained primarily on data from patients with private insurance and regular dental access, it may perform differently on uninsured patients with sporadic care patterns. If it was optimized to reduce false negatives in affluent populations, it might have different sensitivity and specificity profiles in populations with different disease prevalence. If it was validated only in academic dental centers and not in community health settings, we don't actually know how well it works where we're using it.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification requirement isn't perfect. The model card itself is just a document. But it serves a function: it forces developers to be explicit about what they've done, and it gives providers and patients some basis for understanding what they're relying on. When a dentist in a community health center reviews a model card and sees that the tool was validated on 10,000 patients from six academic centers and only 200 from community health settings, the dentist can make an informed decision. They might still use the tool, but they know to be cautious about edge cases. They know to double-check recommendations in unusual presentations. They know what the tool's limitations are.‚Źé‚ŹéRemove the certification requirement, and that information disappears. Developers will still make those choices about training data and validation, but nobody outside the company will know what they chose. The tool will still have limitations, but clinicians won't have documented information about what they are. Patients will still be affected by those limitations, but they won't have any way to understand the risks they're accepting.‚Źé‚Źé**THE EQUITY QUESTION THAT ISN'T BEING ASKED**‚Źé‚ŹéFederal Register guidance on this proposed rule emphasizes innovation and market competition as the primary values driving deregulation (90 FR 00000, proposed rule section II). We respect those values. Market competition can drive improvements. But the regulatory framework should also reflect something the current guidance doesn't adequately address: not all markets are equally competitive, and not all populations have equal power to demand transparency.‚Źé‚ŹéLarge health systems with internal data science teams can hire consultants to reverse-engineer AI tools and understand their performance. Dental practices with three chairs and five employees cannot. Community health centers serving uninsured patients cannot. When certification requirements are removed, the information asymmetry doesn't disappear; it just becomes more extreme. The people with resources to demand transparency get it anyway. The people without resources lose the only mechanism that was forcing it.‚Źé‚ŹéWe've seen this pattern before in dental care and healthcare generally. When regulatory requirements are lifted in the name of efficiency, the burden doesn't fall evenly. It falls on the people least able to absorb it. Patients in underserved areas end up in lower-quality care networks. Practitioners in resource-limited settings end up with fewer tools to make good decisions. The market doesn't correct for this because these populations aren't the profitable part of the market.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT RETENTION OF DSI CERTIFICATION WOULD ACCOMPLISH**‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification requirement, as currently structured in 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1) through (b)(2), serves several specific functions that we believe should be preserved:‚Źé‚Źé1. **Informed Clinical Decision-Making**: Clinicians can understand what a tool is designed for and what populations it was validated on. A periodontist in a community health setting can make a deliberate choice about whether to use a caries risk tool that was validated primarily on adolescents with established disease.‚Źé‚Źé2. **Accountability for Performance**: Developers document what they claim their tools will do. If performance degrades in real-world use, there's a documented baseline to compare against. If a tool performs differently across demographic groups, that discrepancy can be identified and addressed.‚Źé‚Źé3. **Patient Transparency**: While model cards are technical documents primarily for clinicians, their existence signals that someone independent has looked at the tool. That matters to patients trying to decide whether they trust a system affecting their care.‚Źé‚Źé4. **Equity Auditing**: Public health agencies and academic researchers can use model card information to assess whether tools are being deployed equitably across different settings and populations. Currently, the FDA's Real-World Performance monitoring guidance (referenced in 21 CFR 801.4) contemplates this kind of surveillance. It's difficult without baseline transparency data.‚Źé‚ŹéThe certification requirement doesn't prevent innovation. Developers can still create new tools. They still have to submit to certification, but that's a process that takes weeks, not years, and the technical requirements are well-established. What the requirement does is ensure that when those tools enter clinical practice, there's a documented record of what they're supposed to do and what they've been tested on.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC CONCERN: REAL-WORLD PERFORMANCE IN DIVERSE SETTINGS**‚Źé‚ŹéWe want to flag one more specific issue that the current rule doesn't adequately address but that removing DSI certification would make worse. Most AI tools in dental practice are currently validated in settings that don't represent the populations we serve.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider a tool trained and validated for caries detection using intraoral photographs. The validation dataset likely came from dental schools, large group practices, or academic centers. Those populations are probably whiter, more affluent, and have more regular dental care than our patient population. Tooth color, positioning, and presentation differ across populations. Lighting conditions in a community health center are different from a dental school clinic. Patient cooperation and ability to position for photographs may be different.‚Źé‚ŹéNone of this is the tool's fault. It's just how development works. But without documented information about the validation population and methodology, providers in community settings have no way to know whether a tool performs the same way for their patients. The current DSI certification requirement at least creates a mechanism for capturing that information. Remove it, and we're left guessing.‚Źé‚ŹéWe've raised this concern with several health IT vendors we work with, and their response has been consistent: "We'd love to validate in community settings, but it's expensive. If certification isn't required, we're not going to do it." That's honest. It's also concerning, because it means the tools being developed will continue to be optimized for affluent, well-resourced settings, and the gap will only widen.‚Źé‚Źé**ALTERNATIVE APPROACHES WE'VE CONSIDERED**‚Źé‚ŹéWe recognize that the proposed deregulation is motivated partly by genuine concerns about compliance burden, particularly for smaller developers. If the issue is burden rather than principle, there are middle paths we'd support:‚Źé‚ŹéThe ONC could streamline the DSI certification process without eliminating the requirement entirely. The current process requires submission of model documentation, but the timeline and technical specifications could be simplified. 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1) could be revised to require model cards only for certain high-risk applications (diagnostic support, treatment planning) while streamlining requirements for lower-risk applications (administrative support, scheduling optimization).‚Źé‚ŹéThe requirement could be scaled by developer size or market reach. A solo developer releasing a tool for a niche indication could have different requirements than a major vendor releasing a tool used across multiple care settings and patient populations. This would preserve burden reduction while maintaining accountability for tools affecting large numbers of patients.‚Źé‚ŹéAlternatively, the ONC could create a tiered transparency system where certification isn't required, but tools that come without model card information are clearly labeled as such. Providers and patients would know what information they're missing. This preserves market choice while maintaining transparency.‚Źé‚Źé**IMPLEMENTATION BARRIERS AND REALISTIC ASSESSMENT**‚Źé‚ŹéWe also want to be direct about something the proposed rule doesn't discuss: removing certification requirements won't actually reduce burden as much as it claims. Liability concerns will push many developers toward transparency anyway. If an AI tool performs differently across demographic groups and causes worse outcomes in one group, the developer faces legal exposure. Documentation of what they claimed the tool would do becomes evidence in that case.‚Źé‚ŹéThe real effect of removing certification isn't to eliminate documentation; it's to move documentation into the legal sphere instead of the regulatory sphere. Developers will still document what their tools do, but the documentation will be protected by attorney-client privilege instead of being public. That's worse for patients and providers, not better.‚Źé‚ŹéWe'd rather have an imperfect regulatory requirement that produces public documentation than a system that pushes everything into private legal files.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Dental Standards Institute asks the ONC to retain the Decision Support Interventions certification requirement in 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1)(ii). This requirement serves important functions in ensuring that AI tools entering clinical practice are documented, that clinicians have information to make informed decisions, and that vulnerable populations aren't disproportionately affected by tools optimized for other populations.‚Źé‚ŹéWe're not opposed to deregulation in principle. There are areas where health IT regulation creates unnecessary burden. But this isn't one of them. The certification requirement is straightforward, the compliance process is established, and the benefits for safety and equity are real.‚Źé‚ŹéThe families we work with have already experienced enough erosion of trust in systems. They don't need more assurances that things are being handled responsibly. They need systems that are actually designed with them in mind, and they need to be able to verify that. DSI certification is one mechanism for making that verification possible.‚Źé‚ŹéWe're available to discuss this further with ONC staff if additional detail would be helpful.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-05T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-05T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ6‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒsupportive‚ôĒIndiana‚ôĒcommunity organizer‚ôĒ45‚ôĒWhen I helped coordinate a dental clinic's expansion into two underserved neighborhoods last year, we discovered that low-income families were avoiding treatment because they feared their immigration status or financial records would be flagged‚ÄĒeven though the clinic was explicitly confidential. That erosion of trust took months to rebuild, and it taught me how quickly vulnerable people withdraw from care when they sense the system isn't truly protecting their information.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2045
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0019‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒWilliam Hernandez‚ôĒAmerican College of Radiology (ACR)‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe American College of Radiology opposes the removal of Decision Support Interventions certification and transparency requirements, arguing that mandatory model cards and performance disclosures are essential to protect patient safety, enable inf...‚ôĒ**COMMENT ON PROPOSED ASTP/ONC DEREGULATORY ACTIONS: DECISION SUPPORT INTERVENTIONS CERTIFICATION AND AI TRANSPARENCY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe American College of Radiology (ACR) appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory actions outlined by the Assistant Secretary for Technology Policy and the Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology. The ACR represents nearly 37,000 radiologists, radiation oncologists, medical physicists, and other professionals across academic medical centers, community hospitals, and private practices nationwide. Our members work daily with diagnostic imaging systems, AI-assisted decision support tools, and electronic health record platforms that must interoperate seamlessly to deliver safe, effective patient care. This comment addresses a specific concern emerging from our members' recent experience with imaging interoperability failures and focuses on the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria under 45 CFR 170.204 and related transparency requirements in 45 CFR 170.214.‚Źé‚Źé**THE INTEROPERABILITY PROBLEM THAT DROVE THIS CONCERN**‚Źé‚ŹéLast month, the ACR presented imaging data collected across a hospital network participating in a multi-institutional consortium studying diagnostic consistency. The consortium examined how three major DICOM vendors implemented medical imaging standards across their respective platforms. What emerged from that analysis was both subtle and troubling: despite full nominal compliance with DICOM standards as published in the DICOM standard (ISO/IEC 12052), the three systems exhibited incompatibilities in how they encoded certain metadata tags, handled pixel aspect ratios, and transmitted image orientation information. These incompatibilities were invisible to casual inspection. A radiologist reviewing the same patient's chest X-ray from Hospital A and then Hospital B would see images that appeared identical to the naked eye. But when algorithms attempted to track the same anatomical structures across studies from different vendors, the systems consistently failed to align properly. A nodule measured at 8 millimeters in the vendor A system would register as 8.3 millimeters in the vendor B system, not because of genuine growth but because of how each system rounded pixel spacing calculations. Over time across multiple hospitals, a single patient's imaging history became fragmented, creating gaps in longitudinal assessment that could compromise cancer surveillance, post-operative monitoring, and clinical research.‚Źé‚ŹéNone of these vendors technically violated the DICOM standard. The standard itself is permissive about certain implementation choices. This is where the problem becomes relevant to the current regulatory discussion. When manufacturers optimize for their own ecosystem rather than for the clinical reality that patients move between hospitals, we see precisely this kind of hidden fragmentation. The marketplace does not naturally correct for it because the costs are borne by healthcare providers and patients, not by the manufacturers. A hospital IT department may not even recognize the problem exists until a radiologist notices that longitudinal analysis software is producing inconsistent results. By then, years of patient imaging data may be affected.‚Źé‚Źé**DECISION SUPPORT INTERVENTIONS AND THE INTEROPERABILITY PRINCIPLE**‚Źé‚ŹéThe ACR's concern about removing DSI certification criteria connects directly to this real-world experience. Decision support interventions, as defined in 45 CFR 170.204, include AI and machine learning models that assist clinicians in making diagnostic or therapeutic decisions. These tools are increasingly embedded in picture archiving and communication systems (PACS), radiology information systems (RIS), and clinical workflow platforms. They analyze images, flag abnormalities, suggest diagnoses, and recommend follow-up actions. The current certification requirement in 45 CFR 170.204 mandates that DSI developers provide model cards and transparency disclosures that document the intended use of the tool, the target population for which it was validated, known limitations and risks, training data characteristics, and results from external validation studies.‚Źé‚ŹéThe College recognizes that these requirements impose compliance costs on vendors and that some manufacturers view them as burdensome. The College also understands the rationale behind the deregulatory proposal: removing prescriptive certification criteria could accelerate innovation cycles and reduce time-to-market for new tools. However, the removal of DSI transparency requirements would eliminate one of the few regulatory mechanisms that ensures clinicians and health system leaders have access to objective information about how these tools actually perform and where they may fail.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider a practical scenario that illustrates why this matters. A radiology department evaluates two AI vendors' lung cancer screening tools. Both claim superior sensitivity in detecting nodules smaller than 5 millimeters. Vendor A's tool was trained on 50,000 high-resolution CT scans acquired on modern equipment in academic centers. Vendor B's tool was trained on a different dataset of 100,000 scans but includes significant representation from older scanner models and lower-dose protocols. Without mandatory transparency disclosures, the radiology department has no systematic way to understand these differences. Marketing materials will highlight sensitivity figures. Sales representatives will emphasize the vendor's clinical experience. But the department will not have access to a standardized model card documenting what data each tool was trained on, how each was validated externally, or which specific patient populations or scanner types showed degraded performance. A clinician who uses Vendor A's tool to screen patients scanned on older equipment in a community hospital setting has unknowingly applied a tool outside its validated population.‚Źé‚Źé**PATIENT SAFETY, INFORMED DECISION-MAKING, AND ACCOUNTABILITY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe ACR submits that mandatory DSI model cards and transparency disclosures serve three critical functions that deregulation would compromise. First, they protect patient safety by documenting known risks and limitations. The FDA's guidance on clinical decision support software, published in 2019 (FDA Guidance for Industry and Food and Drug Administration Staff, Clinical Decision Support Software, January 2019), distinguishes between tools that are subject to FDA premarket review and those that are not. Tools that are not subject to premarket review include those that are intended to enable a healthcare provider to independently review the basis for a recommendation before using it in clinical decision-making. But even tools that are not FDA-regulated should be transparent about their performance characteristics and failure modes. A radiologist using an AI tool to detect breast cancers should know if that tool's sensitivity drops significantly in patients with dense breast tissue, if it was trained exclusively on mammography data despite being marketed for use with ultrasound, or if external validation studies showed it performed differently across different age groups. This information is not marketing material. It is safety information.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, model cards and transparency requirements enable informed clinical decision-making. Clinicians are trained to weigh evidence, understand uncertainty, and adjust their practice based on patient-specific factors. But they can only do this if they understand what evidence supports the tools they use. Under 45 CFR 170.214, vendors must disclose training data characteristics, including the size of the training dataset, demographic representation, and the clinical settings where data were collected. They must report external validation results, not just internal performance metrics. This information allows a clinical team to evaluate whether a tool's performance is likely to translate to their own patient population and practice setting. A hospital system serving a predominantly rural, older population may reasonably question whether a tool validated primarily in urban academic centers will perform as advertised in their setting.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, these requirements establish a basis for accountability. When an AI tool fails or produces an incorrect recommendation that harms a patient, the hospital, the radiologist, and the patient's family want to understand why. Was it a limitation of the tool itself? Was it applied outside the population for which it was validated? Did the training data include insufficient representation of the patient's demographic group? Without standardized transparency disclosures, these questions become difficult to answer. Litigation over adverse events involving AI-assisted diagnosis will inevitably follow if there is no clear documentation of what each tool was designed to do and where it was tested. Paradoxically, the removal of transparency requirements in the name of reducing regulatory burden could increase liability exposure for both vendors and healthcare organizations.‚Źé‚Źé**THE REGULATORY PRECEDENT AND INTERNATIONAL CONTEXT**‚Źé‚ŹéThe ACR notes that other regulatory jurisdictions have moved in the opposite direction. The European Union's In Vitro Diagnostic Regulation (IVDR), which took effect in May 2022, requires manufacturers of AI-based diagnostic tools to provide detailed technical documentation, including information about training data, validation methodology, and identified limitations. The regulation reflects the principle that high-risk medical devices require proportionate transparency. Similarly, the International Medical Device Regulators Forum (IMDRF) has issued guidance on clinical performance evaluation of AI/machine learning-based software as a medical device, emphasizing the importance of external validation and transparency about the populations and conditions for which a tool has been tested.‚Źé‚ŹéThe ACR is not arguing that all health IT certification criteria should remain static. The College recognizes that the healthcare IT landscape has changed substantially since many of these criteria were first adopted. Some criteria may indeed be outdated or unnecessarily prescriptive. However, DSI transparency requirements are not outdated. They are increasingly important as AI tools become more prevalent in clinical practice. If anything, the regulation should be strengthened, not removed.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC RECOMMENDATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe ACR recommends that the final rule retain the current certification requirements for Decision Support Interventions under 45 CFR 170.204 and 45 CFR 170.214. The College recognizes that these requirements could be streamlined or clarified. For example, ONC could develop standardized templates for model cards that reduce the burden of compliance while ensuring consistent disclosure of material information. ONC could also provide safe harbors for certain types of documentation, such as peer-reviewed validation studies published in established journals, that would count toward satisfaction of the transparency requirement without requiring vendors to conduct duplicative analyses.‚Źé‚ŹéAdditionally, the ACR recommends that ONC work with the FDA, the American Medical Association, and specialty societies to align AI transparency requirements across regulatory frameworks. The current fragmentation, where different rules apply to different tools depending on whether they are FDA-regulated devices, EHR-embedded tools, or standalone applications, creates confusion and compliance inefficiency. A harmonized approach would reduce burden while improving clarity.‚Źé‚ŹéFinally, the ACR urges ONC to consider interoperability implications when evaluating deregulatory proposals. The current DSI certification criteria do not directly mandate interoperability, but they create a baseline of transparency that supports interoperability by allowing systems to be evaluated and compared on consistent metrics. When vendors compete on clearly documented performance characteristics rather than on marketing claims alone, they have stronger incentives to validate their tools across diverse populations and settings. This competition drives the kind of robustness that, in turn, supports interoperability across different clinical environments.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe American College of Radiology stands ready to work with ONC and HHS to support innovation in health IT while protecting patient safety and care quality. The College believes that the current DSI certification and transparency requirements represent a reasonable balance between these objectives. Removing these requirements would create a regulatory gap at a moment when AI-assisted clinical decision support is becoming increasingly central to medical practice. The ACR respectfully urges ONC to retain these requirements and to consider them a foundation upon which to build more comprehensive and coordinated AI governance across the healthcare system.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-19T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-19T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ5‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving DSI transparency requirements creates a dangerous regulatory gap where AI-based clinical decision support tools face fewer disclosure obligations than traditional medical devices, undermining consistent safety standards.‚ôĒacademic‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒurgent‚ôĒCalifornia‚ôĒassistant professor‚ôĒ42‚ôĒLast month, I presented imaging data from our hospital network to a consortium studying diagnostic consistency across systems‚ÄĒwe discovered that three different vendors' DICOM implementations were subtly incompatible in ways that made it nearly impossible to track the same patient's scan progression over time, yet none of them violated any existing standards. If oversight loosens further without addressing interoperability mandates, we'll face a situation where each manufacturer optimizes for their own ecosystem rather than the clinical reality that patients move between hospitals and need seamless continuity.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1793
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0020‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒWilliam Thompson‚ôĒPolicy Center for Maternal Mental Health‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health opposes removing Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements that mandate AI model cards, transparency disclosures, and external validation documentation, arguing that these requirem...‚ôĒJune 18, 2025‚Źé‚ŹéOffice of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology‚ŹéDepartment of Health and Human Services‚Źé330 C Street, SW‚ŹéWashington, DC 20201‚Źé‚ŹéRe: RFI Response ‚Äď Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity‚Źé‚ŹéThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health submits this comment in response to the proposed deregulatory framework. We write from the perspective of a national organization working directly with rural health systems, community clinics, and patient advocacy networks across Montana and the Mountain West on maternal mental health data infrastructure. Our concern centers on one specific proposal: the potential removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria that currently require AI model cards, transparency disclosures, and external validation documentation.‚Źé‚ŹéLast year, our staff worked with a coalition of six rural health systems in Montana to track postpartum depression screening outcomes. We wanted to demonstrate the scope of maternal mental health disparities in rural areas, particularly among low-income and Native American populations. The data we needed existed. Three separate health systems had it. But all three declined to share even anonymized screening rates with us, citing privacy concerns that, in retrospect, I believe were overstated. They weren't worried about HIPAA violations. They were worried about operational disruption, about liability questions that nobody could clearly answer, about the burden of vetting who we were and what we'd do with the information. The result? We couldn't make our case. We had anecdotes. We had clinical experience. We had no numbers. This meant advocacy efforts stalled. Funding proposals languished. Mothers in rural counties continued going undiagnosed at roughly twice the rate of their urban counterparts.‚Źé‚ŹéThat experience taught us something that I think matters for this rulemaking. Fragmentation in health data access is a real problem, and it's a problem that genuinely harms vulnerable populations. But the solution isn't to swing the pendulum entirely toward deregulation. The solution is to create a framework where transparency requirements actually enable better access for the people who need it, while preventing the concentration of power in the hands of large technology vendors who have no accountability to the communities they serve.‚Źé‚ŹéThe specific concern we raise involves the proposed removal of DSI certification criteria from 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1). These criteria currently require developers of AI-enabled clinical decision support systems to disclose intended use, target population characteristics, known performance limitations, training data characteristics, and results of external validation testing. The certification requirement exists in 45 CFR 170.102, which defines Health IT Module certification as a prerequisite for meaningful participation in federal health IT programs and meaningful use attestation pathways. When ONC removes these requirements, it creates a situation where hospitals and clinics can deploy AI systems without clear information about how those systems were built, who they were tested on, or where they might fail.‚Źé‚ŹéI understand the deregulatory impulse. Certification is burdensome. It slows down innovation. It creates barriers to entry for smaller vendors. But certification requirements exist because health care is not a normal market. Patients can't shop around for better clinical decision support algorithms. They don't have the expertise to evaluate whether an AI system has been validated in populations that match their own. Clinicians, especially in rural areas where specialist support is limited, rely on Health IT systems to guide their practice. When those systems are deployed without transparency about their limitations, the risk isn't abstract. It's material.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider the landscape of postpartum mental health screening. Several commercial AI systems now exist that claim to improve depression detection in maternity settings. One such system, developed by a major health IT vendor, uses machine learning to flag patients at risk based on electronic health record data. The system was trained primarily on data from large academic medical centers in urban areas. It performs well in that population. But rural maternity practices serve different demographics. They have different comorbidity patterns. They have different access to mental health specialists. They have different documentation practices in their EHRs. When a rural clinic deploys that AI system without understanding these limitations, the system may miss high-risk patients or flag low-risk patients unnecessarily, degrading clinical judgment rather than supporting it.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification requirements aren't perfect. They're also not burdensome in the way that the deregulatory proposal suggests. The model card requirement, which ONC codified in the 2015 Edition Health IT Certification Criteria, asks for approximately ten pages of standardized documentation. The external validation requirement asks for evidence that the system performs as intended in populations beyond the training dataset. These are reasonable asks. They're not asking vendors to redesign their systems. They're asking for transparency about how systems work and where they might fail. That transparency serves multiple functions. It helps clinicians understand whether the system is appropriate for their patient population. It helps hospital IT leadership make informed procurement decisions. It helps researchers track whether AI systems are being validated equitably. It helps regulators identify where problems emerge.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat concerns me most about the proposed deregulation is that it solves a vendor problem by creating a patient safety problem. I'm not opposed to removing burdensome requirements. But the burden here isn't really the documentation. The burden is that some vendors find it cheaper and faster to deploy systems without external validation. The burden is that some vendors don't want to disclose that their training data is skewed toward certain populations. The burden is that some vendors would rather avoid liability by keeping their methodologies opaque. Removing certification requirements doesn't eliminate that burden. It just shifts it. Instead of vendors doing the work upfront, we end up with hospitals doing the work retroactively, after systems have been deployed and problems emerge.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed framework references 21st Century Cures Act implementation and the information blocking provisions at 42 U.S.C. 300jj-52, suggesting that market competition and information availability will create natural pressure for transparency. This assumes that vendors have incentive to be transparent about AI limitations and that purchasers have capacity to evaluate those limitations independently. In our experience with rural health systems, neither assumption holds. Rural hospitals operate with IT budgets that are a fraction of what urban systems have. They don't have dedicated data scientists who can evaluate whether an AI system's training data matches their patient population. They don't have legal teams who can negotiate custom validation studies. They're not shopping for the best AI system. They're shopping for something that integrates with their existing EHR and doesn't require hiring new staff to manage it. In that context, removing certification requirements doesn't create competition. It creates information asymmetry.‚Źé‚ŹéOur coalition of rural clinics has actually been trying to implement better maternal mental health screening tools. We've looked at several commercial systems. What we've learned is that the systems with the best documentation of their validation processes, the ones that are transparent about their limitations, are also the systems that perform better in our settings. The ones without that documentation? We don't know what we're getting. We have to assume risk. We have to either run our own validation studies (which we can't afford) or just deploy the system and see what happens. That's not a market dynamic that benefits patients. It's a market dynamic that benefits vendors who are willing to cut corners on validation and transparency.‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be clear about what we're not asking for. We're not asking ONC to reject innovation. We're not asking for certification requirements that are so burdensome that only large vendors can afford compliance. We're not asking for a regulatory framework that prevents rural communities from accessing the same tools that urban centers have. What we are asking for is that when a health IT system is designed to influence clinical decision-making, there's transparent documentation of how it works and where it might fail. That documentation should be freely available to purchasers, to clinicians, and to researchers. It doesn't need to be lengthy. The model card format, as defined in 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1), is actually pretty efficient. It's approximately ten pages of structured information. That's not burdensome. That's due diligence.‚Źé‚ŹéThe regulatory history on this issue is worth reviewing. The 2015 Edition Health IT Certification Criteria incorporated DSI certification requirements in response to widespread concern that clinical decision support systems were being deployed with insufficient transparency about their limitations. The American Medical Association, the American Hospital Association, and patient safety organizations all supported those requirements. They weren't controversial at the time. They reflected a consensus that clinical decision support systems are medical devices, and medical devices should be evaluated for safety and effectiveness before widespread deployment. The 21st Century Cures Act, enacted in 2016, actually reinforced this principle by creating information blocking penalties for vendors who withhold health information. The Cures Act didn't say "remove oversight of AI systems." It said "make sure patients and clinicians have access to their health information." Those are two different things.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat's changed is that vendors are now arguing that certification requirements slow down innovation and that market forces will ensure quality. The evidence doesn't support this. In the past five years, we've seen multiple examples of clinical AI systems that performed well in validation studies but failed in real-world deployment because they didn't account for variation in patient populations, documentation practices, or clinical workflows. The ACLU's investigation into racial bias in healthcare algorithms, published in 2019, documented how systems developed without attention to demographic equity can systematically disadvantage certain patient populations. When systems like these are deployed without external validation requirements, nobody knows there's a problem until harm has already occurred. By then, thousands of patients have already been affected.‚Źé‚ŹéThe maternal mental health context is particularly important here. Postpartum depression affects one in seven mothers. It's the leading cause of maternal mortality in the United States when you account for suicide. Early identification and intervention can prevent deaths and serious morbidity. But maternal mental health screening is also deeply influenced by provider bias, patient disclosure patterns, and documentation practices. An AI system that doesn't account for these factors can actually harm care. It can create a false sense of confidence in negative screens, or it can create alert fatigue through over-flagging. Rural maternity providers, who often lack easy access to mental health specialists, are particularly vulnerable to these kinds of errors. They need tools that are transparent about their limitations. They need to know whether a system was validated in populations similar to their own. They need to know what the false negative rate is, what the false positive rate is, and whether those rates vary by demographic group.‚Źé‚ŹéI understand that ONC is trying to reduce the regulatory burden on health IT developers. That's a legitimate policy goal. But I'd push back on the framing that certification requirements are the main burden. In our conversations with small vendors who develop maternal mental health tools, the burden isn't documentation. The burden is the underlying work of actually validating systems in diverse populations. That work should be done. It should be done before systems are deployed at scale. Removing the certification requirement doesn't eliminate that work. It just pushes it downstream, onto hospitals and clinics that don't have the capacity to do it.‚Źé‚ŹéThere's also a fairness question here. Small vendors who take the time to validate their systems in diverse populations will be undercut by larger vendors who skip validation and deploy systems quickly. In a market where purchasers can't easily evaluate quality, speed wins over rigor. That's bad for innovation. It's bad for patients. It's bad for the vendors who are doing things right. The certification requirement actually protects smaller vendors by creating a level playing field where everyone has to do the work of validation and transparency.‚Źé‚ŹéThe specific recommendation we make is straightforward: retain the DSI certification requirements at 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1) exactly as currently written. Those requirements mandate that developers provide model cards documenting intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. The requirements are not burdensome. They're proportional to the risk. They create transparency that benefits purchasers, clinicians, and ultimately patients. If ONC wants to reduce burden on developers, there are other areas to focus on. The certification process itself could be streamlined. The documentation format could be simplified. But the core requirement for transparency about AI system limitations should be retained.‚Źé‚ŹéIf ONC is concerned that the current requirements are too prescriptive or don't accommodate emerging AI architectures, we'd support a revision that clarifies the requirements for newer types of systems while maintaining the core commitment to transparency. For example, the current requirements reference "training data characteristics." For newer systems that use transfer learning or federated approaches, that language might need updating. But the principle remains sound: clinicians and purchasers need to understand how systems work, what they were validated on, and where they might fail.‚Źé‚ŹéWe also want to flag a secondary concern about information blocking and AI systems. The information blocking regulations at 42 U.S.C. 300jj-52 require that health IT vendors not withhold access to health information. But those regulations don't currently address the question of whether vendors have an obligation to disclose information about how their systems work. If ONC removes certification requirements, it should simultaneously clarify that vendors have an affirmative obligation to disclose model cards and validation documentation to purchasers and to regulators. Otherwise, we'll end up with a situation where vendors withhold that information citing trade secrets, and purchasers have no way to know what they're deploying.‚Źé‚ŹéThe broader point is this: deregulation in health IT works best when it's targeted at removing unnecessary burden, not at reducing transparency. Certification requirements are burdensome when they're prescriptive about how systems should be built or when they impose arbitrary timelines. But the core requirement for transparency about AI limitations isn't burdensome. It's essential. It's the foundation for everything else: market competition, clinical safety, equity, accountability. Remove that requirement, and you're not unleashing innovation. You're unleashing risk.‚Źé‚ŹéIn closing, we want to acknowledge that the deregulatory framework is motivated by legitimate concerns about innovation and market access. We're not opposed to deregulation in principle. We're opposed to deregulation that trades transparency for speed. The maternal mental health community in rural America needs access to the same tools that urban centers have. But access to tools doesn't mean access to tools that we don't understand. It means access to tools that are transparent about how they work, that have been validated in populations like ours, and that can be evaluated fairly by clinicians who aren't data scientists. The DSI certification requirements support that kind of access. Removing them would harm it.‚Źé‚ŹéWe appreciate the opportunity to comment on this important rulemaking. We're available to discuss our experience with rural health IT implementation and maternal mental health screening in more detail if that would be helpful.‚Źé‚ŹéRespectfully submitted,‚Źé‚ŹéPolicy Center for Maternal Mental Health‚Źé‚ŹéWilliam Thompson‚ŹéCommunity Organizer‚ŹéPolicy Center for Maternal Mental Health‚ŹéMontana‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-27T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-27T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ8‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒsupportive‚ôĒMontana‚ôĒcommunity organizer‚ôĒ44‚ôĒLast year, I worked with a coalition of rural clinics across Montana to track maternal mental health outcomes, and we hit a wall when three separate health systems refused to share anonymized data about postpartum depression screening rates‚ÄĒciting privacy concerns that felt overblown. We were trying to advocate for better resources, not expose anyone, but the fragmentation meant we had no way to demonstrate that rural mothers were going undiagnosed at twice the rate of urban counterparts. I worry that loosening data-sharing restrictions without strengthening real safeguards will just shuffle the problem sideways: corporations will gain access while community organizations like ours still can't get the information we need to actually help people.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2486
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0021‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒBetty Harris‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA home health aide argues that removing transparency and explainability requirements for AI systems in patient care poses serious risks to vulnerable populations, citing her experience with data transfer failures and calling for maintained safegua...‚ôĒMy name is Betty Harris and I work as a home health aide in Minnesota. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0023‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDorothy Harris‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA retired patient and healthcare advocate opposes removing transparency and AI model card requirements for clinical decision support systems, arguing that patients deserve to know how AI tools used in their care were developed, validated, and what...‚ôĒCOMMENT ON HEALTH DATA, TECHNOLOGY, AND INTEROPERABILITY DEREGULATORY ACTIONS‚Źé‚ŹéAs a retired teacher from Colorado who has navigated the healthcare system as both a patient and the family member of a healthcare professional, I want to submit comments on the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria, specifically the requirements for AI model cards and transparency disclosures.‚Źé‚ŹéWHAT I'VE OBSERVED IN MY OWN CARE‚Źé‚ŹéLast year when I was diagnosed with rheumatitis, I needed lab results transferred between my primary care office and a rheumatologist across town. That took two weeks and three separate phone calls. What concerned me more was discovering that my daughter, a nurse practitioner at her hospital system, could access my medical records without my explicit knowledge. That experience made me realize how little control patients actually have over their own health information. Now I'm being asked to trust that removing transparency requirements for AI systems used in clinical decision-making will somehow improve things. I'm skeptical.‚Źé‚ŹéWHY AI TRANSPARENCY MATTERS FOR PATIENT SAFETY‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures serves a real function. These requirements ask developers to disclose: (1) intended use and target population, (2) known risks and limitations, (3) training data characteristics, and (4) external validation results. This isn't bureaucratic bloat. When a doctor uses an AI system to help diagnose my condition or recommend treatment, I deserve to know if that system was trained on populations similar to mine, what its known failure modes are, and whether it's been validated outside the lab.‚Źé‚ŹéThe 21st Century Cures Act (42 U.S.C. ¬ß 300jj) emphasizes patient access and transparency. The FDA's guidance on clinical decision support software (2019) recognizes that transparency is essential for safe integration into clinical workflows. Removing these safeguards doesn't reduce burden on developers so much as shift risk onto patients and clinicians who won't know what they're working with.‚Źé‚ŹéWHAT SHOULD REMAIN‚Źé‚ŹéRather than eliminate DSI certification requirements, I urge the Office of the Secretary to retain model card and transparency disclosure mandates. These requirements should specify:‚Źé‚Źé- Who the AI system was developed for and tested on‚Źé- What clinical outcomes it was validated against‚Źé- Known limitations and failure rates by demographic group‚Źé- Training data sources and composition‚Źé- External validation in settings different from development‚Źé‚ŹéDeregulation has its place, but not at the expense of informed consent and clinical accountability. The burden of proving AI systems are safe and appropriate is far lighter than the burden patients bear when those systems fail.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-02-01T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-01T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒsupportive‚ôĒColorado‚ôĒretired teacher‚ôĒ52‚ôĒLast year, when my arthritis diagnosis required me to see a rheumatologist across town, I had to call my primary care office three separate times just to get my lab results transferred‚ÄĒit took two weeks. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0024‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒGeorge Thomas‚ôĒAmerican College of Cardiology‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe American College of Cardiology opposes removing model card and transparency disclosure requirements for clinical decision support tools, arguing that these requirements are necessary for informed clinical practice and patient safety, not burde...‚ôĒGeorge Thomas, on behalf of the American College of Cardiology, submits these comments in response to the proposed deregulatory actions announced by the Assistant Secretary for Technology Policy and the Office of the National Coordinator for Health IT. The College represents nearly 50,000 cardiologists and cardiovascular professionals across the United States, and our members depend on health IT systems that function reliably and transparently in clinical practice. We write to address the proposed modifications to Decision Support Interventions certification requirements, particularly the proposal to remove the model card and transparency disclosure requirements that currently mandate documentation of intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CURRENT LANDSCAPE AND WHY TRANSPARENCY MATTERS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe American College of Cardiology has observed firsthand the consequences of inadequate interoperability standards and vendor lock-in over the past two decades. When our member institutions implemented new electronic health records systems‚ÄĒand this experience mirrors what is happening across the country‚ÄĒthe transition period revealed something that pure deregulation theory does not fully account for. At one major academic medical center in the Midwest, the implementation of a new EHR system two years ago required eighteen months of retrofitting data from the previous system. The vendors involved had no commercial incentive to maintain backward compatibility or to facilitate smooth data migration. The result was not innovation or competition; it was fragmentation, duplicated effort, and periods during which clinical decision support functioned inconsistently because the underlying data lacked integrity.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is the kind of systemic inefficiency that gets repeated across hundreds of hospital systems when regulatory guardrails are removed without simultaneously addressing the structural incentives that lead vendors to create proprietary silos. The College is not opposed to innovation. We support genuine advances in artificial intelligence and clinical decision support. What we cannot support is the removal of basic transparency requirements under the assumption that market forces alone will compel vendors to provide the information that clinicians need to use these tools safely.‚Źé‚ŹéThe 21st Century Cures Act (42 U.S.C. ¬ß 300jj et seq.) established the foundational principle that patients and clinicians should have access to electronic health information without unnecessary restrictions. The subsequent Information Blocking Rule (45 CFR ¬ß¬ß 171.100‚Äď171.405) operationalized that principle. But transparency about decision support systems is not the same as blocking. Transparency is a prerequisite for informed clinical judgment. A cardiologist who uses an AI-assisted diagnostic tool has an ethical obligation to understand what population the tool was developed on, what risks it carries in edge cases, and how it was validated. Without that information, the clinician cannot fulfill that obligation. Removing the requirement to disclose these elements does not reduce burden; it redistributes it‚ÄĒfrom the vendor to the clinician and ultimately to the patient.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT THE MODEL CARD REQUIREMENT ACTUALLY REQUIRES**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current certification criterion for Decision Support Interventions under the Office of the National Coordinator's Health IT Certification Program requires vendors to provide model cards and transparency disclosures. This requirement is not burdensome in the way that some commenters claim. The Model Card for Model Reporting framework, originally developed by researchers at Stanford and refined through implementation experience, asks vendors to document:‚Źé‚Źé- Intended use and target population (demographic characteristics, clinical setting, expected user type)‚Źé- Known performance limitations and failure modes (where the system is likely to perform poorly)‚Źé- Training data characteristics (source, size, temporal scope, potential biases)‚Źé- External validation results (testing on datasets independent of the training set)‚Źé- Recommendations for safe clinical implementation‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not a comprehensive validation study. It is a disclosure document. The College's own members who have implemented decision support tools report that generating these disclosures takes weeks, not months, and the effort is proportional to the complexity of the tool itself. For straightforward rule-based systems, the model card is minimal. For machine learning systems with substantial training data, the document is more involved‚ÄĒbut that is exactly when clinicians need the information most.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal to remove this requirement is premised on the claim that it stifles innovation. The evidence for this claim is thin. The College is aware of no vendors who have declined to develop decision support tools because the certification process requires transparency documentation.
