What the College has observed instead is that vendors who are serious about clinical adoption provide these disclosures willingly because they understand that clinicians will not trust tools they cannot understand.‚Źé‚Źé**INTEROPERABILITY AND THE REAL SOURCE OF FRAGMENTATION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe deregulatory proposal conflates two separate problems: (1) the burden of meeting certification requirements, and (2) the structural incentives that lead to vendor consolidation and lock-in. These are not the same issue, and addressing one will not solve the other.‚Źé‚ŹéThe College's experience suggests that the real source of fragmentation in the health IT market is not overly stringent certification criteria but rather the absence of enforceable interoperability standards combined with weak incentives for data portability. The proposed removal of certification requirements for Decision Support Interventions will not change this underlying dynamic. What it will do is create a new category of tools that operate in a kind of regulatory shadow, where vendors can iterate rapidly without documentation while clinicians have less information about how those tools work.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Office of the National Coordinator has previously stated (in the 2015 Federal Health IT Strategic Plan) that the goal of health IT regulation is to "enable the secure, seamless, and authorized use and exchange of health information." The proposed deregulation moves in the opposite direction for clinical decision support. It reduces transparency at the very moment when AI-assisted tools are proliferating across cardiovascular care, from imaging analysis to risk stratification to arrhythmia detection.‚Źé‚ŹéThe College recognizes that the Cures Act directed the Office of the National Coordinator to consider whether certification requirements are necessary and appropriate. The College's position is straightforward: for Decision Support Interventions, transparency requirements are necessary and appropriate precisely because these tools influence clinical judgment in ways that are not immediately visible to clinicians. Unlike a new drug, which undergoes FDA review before reaching patients, an AI-assisted diagnostic tool can be deployed in clinical practice with minimal oversight if certification requirements are removed.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC CONCERNS ABOUT THE PROPOSED CHANGE**‚Źé‚ŹéThe American College of Cardiology has several specific concerns about the proposal to remove model card and transparency disclosure requirements:‚Źé‚Źé1. **Liability and accountability gaps.** If a vendor removes a decision support tool from the market because it performed poorly in a certain patient population, there is currently no requirement to disclose that the tool ever existed or why it was withdrawn. Clinicians who used the tool have no mechanism to know whether their patients were affected. This is not a theoretical problem. The College is aware of at least two instances in the past five years where clinical decision support tools were quietly discontinued by vendors after poor performance reports, and clinicians had no formal notification. Removing transparency requirements will only make this worse.‚Źé‚Źé2. **Validation and real-world performance divergence.** Machine learning systems frequently perform differently in production than they did in development. A tool validated on a cohort of primarily male patients in academic medical centers may fail systematically in community hospitals with more diverse patient populations. The current model card requirement asks vendors to disclose the population used for validation. Without this disclosure, clinicians have no way to assess whether a tool is appropriate for their specific patient population. The College's cardiology members are particularly concerned about this issue because cardiovascular disease presentation varies significantly across demographic groups, and AI tools that fail to account for this variation can cause harm.‚Źé‚Źé3. **Competitive dynamics and market consolidation.** The claim that deregulation will increase competition is not supported by the history of health IT. When regulatory requirements are reduced, large vendors with established market share often benefit because they have the resources to absorb the compliance costs, while smaller innovators cannot. The removal of certification requirements lowers barriers to entry in the short term but can actually entrench dominant firms in the long term because clinicians will gravitate toward tools from vendors with established reputations, regardless of transparency. This is the opposite of what the proposal claims to achieve.‚Źé‚Źé4. **Patient safety and informed consent.** Patients have a right to understand when artificial intelligence is being used to assist in their care. If a cardiologist uses an AI system to interpret an ECG or a cardiac imaging study, the patient should be able to ask what population the system was trained on and what its known limitations are. Removing transparency requirements makes it harder for patients to exercise informed consent because clinicians themselves will have less information to share.‚Źé‚Źé**ALTERNATIVE APPROACHES TO REDUCING BURDEN**‚Źé‚ŹéThe College understands the argument that certification processes can be burdensome and that vendors should not face unnecessary regulatory obstacles. The solution to this problem, however, is not to remove transparency requirements entirely. Instead, the College proposes several alternatives that would reduce burden while preserving the information that clinicians need:‚Źé‚Źé- **Streamlined certification pathways for low-risk tools.** Decision Support Interventions that operate as decision aids rather than autonomous systems, or that are used in non-critical clinical contexts, could be certified through an expedited process that requires less documentation. The Office of the National Coordinator could establish risk tiers and calibrate documentation requirements accordingly.‚Źé‚Źé- **Third-party verification of model cards.** Rather than requiring vendors to generate model cards internally, the Office of the National Coordinator could establish a network of approved third-party organizations that verify and validate model card disclosures. This would reduce the burden on individual vendors while maintaining the integrity of the information.‚Źé‚Źé- **Public registry of decision support tools.** The Office of the National Coordinator could establish a searchable public registry where vendors voluntarily disclose model cards and performance data. Vendors who do not participate would be flagged as non-transparent, creating market pressure for disclosure without formal regulatory requirements.‚Źé‚Źé- **Liability framework for undisclosed tools.** Rather than requiring pre-market disclosure, Congress could establish a liability standard that holds vendors responsible for failures of decision support tools that lack transparent documentation. This would create a powerful economic incentive for transparency without the need for regulatory approval processes.‚Źé‚ŹéThese alternatives would address the legitimate concerns about regulatory burden while preserving the information asymmetry that currently exists between vendors and clinicians. The College believes that one or more of these approaches would better serve the goals of both innovation and patient safety.‚Źé‚Źé**EVIDENCE FROM IMPLEMENTATION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe College has surveyed its members about their experiences implementing decision support tools, and the data suggests that transparency requirements are not a significant barrier to adoption. Among 847 cardiologists and advanced practice providers who responded to a survey conducted in 2024, 76 percent reported that they use at least one AI-assisted clinical decision support tool in their practice. Of those who use such tools, 82 percent reported that they would like more information about how the tools were developed and validated. Only 4 percent reported that they had declined to use a tool because of the time required to generate transparency documentation.‚Źé‚ŹéThis suggests that vendors are not being deterred from developing tools by the requirement to provide model cards. Instead, the requirement is serving its intended purpose: it is ensuring that clinicians have the information they need to use tools appropriately. The College believes that this evidence should weigh heavily in the Office of the National Coordinator's final decision.‚Źé‚ŹéAdditionally, the College has examined the adoption timelines for decision support tools that have undergone the full certification process. Tools that include comprehensive model cards and transparency disclosures are adopted more rapidly in clinical practice than tools that lack such documentation. This suggests that transparency is not a barrier to adoption; it is a facilitator. Clinicians trust tools that are transparent about their capabilities and limitations.‚Źé‚Źé**THE BROADER CONTEXT OF DEREGULATION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal to remove Decision Support Interventions certification requirements should be understood in the context of a broader deregulatory agenda. The Office of the National Coordinator and the Assistant Secretary for Technology Policy have proposed removing or modifying numerous other certification requirements, including standards for secure messaging, electronic prescribing, and data exchange. The cumulative effect of these changes is to shift the burden of assessing quality and safety from vendors to clinicians and patients.‚Źé‚ŹéThis shift may be appropriate in some contexts. The College does not argue that every certification requirement is necessary or well-calibrated. But for decision support systems that influence clinical judgment, transparency is not a luxury; it is a minimum standard of professional practice. A cardiologist who uses a tool to assess the risk of sudden cardiac death without understanding how the tool was developed and validated is not practicing at the standard of care. The College's position is that certification requirements should be calibrated to reflect the clinical significance of the tool, not eliminated entirely.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Cures Act directed the Office of the National Coordinator to reduce regulatory burden, but it did not direct the office to eliminate all requirements for transparency. The College reads the Cures Act as requiring a balanced approach: reducing unnecessary burden while maintaining standards that protect patients and support informed clinical practice.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe American College of Cardiology respectfully urges the Office of the National Coordinator and the Assistant Secretary for Technology Policy to retain the current certification requirements for Decision Support Interventions, including the requirement to provide model cards and transparency disclosures. These requirements are not burdensome, they are supported by clinician demand, and they serve the fundamental purpose of enabling informed clinical judgment.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the agencies are concerned about the burden of the certification process, the College recommends exploring the alternative approaches outlined above: streamlined pathways for low-risk tools, third-party verification, public registries, or liability frameworks that create market incentives for transparency without requiring pre-market approval.‚Źé‚ŹéThe College stands ready to work with the Office of the National Coordinator and the Assistant Secretary for Technology Policy to develop implementation approaches that reduce burden while maintaining the transparency that clinicians need to use artificial intelligence safely and effectively. Our members are committed to adopting tools that improve cardiovascular care, but we are also committed to the principle that innovation and transparency are not in tension. The best tools are those that clinicians understand and trust. Transparency is the foundation of that trust.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-30T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-30T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ7‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒacademic‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒresigned‚ôĒMissouri‚ôĒassistant professor‚ôĒ57‚ôĒWhen we implemented a new electronic health records system at our institution two years ago, I watched our cardiology department spend eighteen months retrofitting data from our previous system because the vendors had no incentive to maintain compatibility‚ÄĒand I realized then that whatever efficiencies we might gain from looser regulations would likely be offset by exactly this kind of fragmentation, repeated across every hospital system in the country.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2381
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0025‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒMichael Ramirez‚ôĒTexas Medical Association‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Texas Medical Association opposes removing the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures, arguing that transparency about training data, validation results, and known limitatio...‚ôĒ**Texas Medical Association Comments on the Proposed Deregulatory Actions in Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Texas Medical Association represents over 55,000 physicians and medical students across our state, and we work daily with the clinical realities that this proposed rule will directly affect. Our research directors, practicing clinicians, and health system leaders have spent the last several weeks reviewing the ASTP/ONC deregulatory proposal, and I want to be straightforward: we support innovation and reducing unnecessary burden on health IT developers. But we have serious concerns about the specific proposal to remove the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures.‚Źé‚ŹéI need to explain why this matters beyond the usual regulatory language. Back in 2019, I watched a colleague's patient records get accessed by a pharmaceutical representative without proper authorization. Nothing catastrophic happened to that particular patient, but the breach revealed how easily our current safeguards can be circumvented when we're not paying attention. That incident stuck with me, and it's part of why I'm writing this comment. The proposed removal of DSI certification requirements feels like we're moving in exactly the wrong direction at exactly the moment when AI systems are becoming more prevalent in clinical decision-making. I'm caught between understanding why developers want fewer regulatory hurdles and knowing that loosening these specific guardrails puts vulnerable populations at real risk.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PRACTICAL PROBLEM WITH REMOVING AI TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéLet me be concrete about what's at stake. When our physicians use a decision support tool in their electronic health record, they're making clinical judgments based partly on that tool's output. Under current 21 CFR Part 11 and the FDA's existing guidance on clinical decision support software, these tools are supposed to be transparent about what they are, what populations they were tested on, what their known limitations are, and what their actual validation evidence shows. The DSI certification criterion in 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1) requires AI model cards that disclose intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. This isn't bureaucratic theater. This is the difference between a clinician understanding that a sepsis prediction algorithm was developed primarily on data from teaching hospitals in the Northeast and might behave differently in our rural Texas clinics, and that same clinician having no idea what population bias might be embedded in the algorithm.‚Źé‚ŹéOur experience in Texas health systems has shown us what happens without this transparency. We have member institutions that implemented AI-assisted diagnostic tools without full visibility into their training data or validation performance across different demographic groups. One health system discovered after deployment that a mortality prediction model was systematically underestimating risk in patients over 75 years old because the training dataset had been weighted toward younger populations. The clinicians had no way of knowing this from the tool's interface or documentation. They were making care decisions based on incomplete information about the tool's actual performance characteristics. This is exactly the scenario that model cards and transparency disclosures are designed to prevent.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal suggests that market forces and post-market surveillance will be sufficient to catch these problems. That's a reasonable argument in theory, but it doesn't match what we see in practice. By the time a safety signal emerges in post-market surveillance, the tool has already been used in thousands or tens of thousands of clinical encounters. In a health system serving low-income neighborhoods, like the clinic my daughter works in, the patients most likely to be harmed by a biased algorithm are often the least likely to have the resources to pursue remedies or to even know they've been harmed. The burden of post-market discovery falls entirely on end users, not on the developers who created the tools.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY INFORMED CLINICAL DECISION-MAKING REQUIRES TRANSPARENCY AT DEPLOYMENT**‚Źé‚ŹéI keep coming back to a fundamental question: what does it mean for a clinician to make an informed decision when they're relying on an AI system? The informed consent literature in medicine is clear on this point. Patients can't consent to treatment they don't understand. Clinicians can't make sound clinical judgments about recommendations they don't understand. The DSI certification criterion requiring model cards is the mechanism that makes understanding possible at the point of clinical use. Without it, we're asking physicians to integrate AI-generated recommendations into their practice without the basic information needed to assess reliability or applicability.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule cites efficiency gains and reduced developer burden as the primary rationale for removing these requirements. I understand the developer perspective. Generating model cards requires documentation, internal validation work, and transparency about training data that developers might prefer to keep proprietary. But efficiency in regulatory compliance shouldn't come at the cost of patient safety. The question isn't whether model cards are burdensome. The question is whether the burden is justified by the safety benefit. In our assessment, it is. A 2023 study in the Journal of the American Medical Association examined AI-assisted diagnostic tools in radiology and found that clinician accuracy improved when tools provided explicit confidence intervals and training data characteristics, but actually declined when the same tools provided only output recommendations without transparency information (JAMA, 2023; 330(15): 1463-1471). Clinicians need to know not just what the tool recommends, but how confident they should be in that recommendation and which patient populations the recommendation applies to.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification criterion doesn't prevent innovation. It shapes innovation toward transparency. Developers can still create powerful AI tools. They still compete on functionality and performance. What they can't do is hide the limitations. That's exactly what we should be asking for in clinical software. The 21st Century Cures Act, which Congress passed in 2016, was explicit about this: information blocking regulations exist to prevent developers from obscuring information about their tools' capabilities and limitations (42 U.S.C. ¬ß 300jj-52). The DSI criterion is a direct expression of that Congressional intent. Removing it doesn't just reduce burden on developers. It reverses a policy judgment that Congress made about how much transparency is necessary for safe clinical decision-making.‚Źé‚Źé**THE EQUITY DIMENSION THAT THE PROPOSAL DOESN'T ADDRESS**‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what concerns me most, and I'll try to explain this clearly because it's the part of this comment that comes from watching my daughter practice medicine in a under-resourced clinic. AI bias in clinical tools doesn't affect all patients equally. Tools trained primarily on data from academic medical centers or affluent populations are more likely to have systematic biases against patients from different socioeconomic backgrounds or different racial and ethnic groups. We know this from the literature. A widely-cited study by Obermeyer and colleagues found that a widely-used risk prediction algorithm systematically underestimated illness severity in Black patients because the algorithm was trained on historical healthcare spending as a proxy for illness, and Black patients receive systematically less healthcare spending for equivalent illness (Obermeyer et al., Science, 2019; 366(6464): 447-453). Without model cards that disclose training data characteristics, clinicians have no way of knowing whether a tool they're using has this kind of bias embedded in it.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Texas Medical Association has member institutions serving predominantly Hispanic and Black patient populations. These health systems are exactly the ones where AI bias is most likely to cause harm, because the tools they're using have often been trained on data from different populations. Removing the DSI certification requirement means removing the primary mechanism that would give these clinicians visibility into that bias. It means moving toward a system where only large health systems with robust data science teams can conduct post-deployment validation of AI tools, while smaller systems and safety-net providers are left using tools they don't fully understand.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal states that post-market surveillance and adverse event reporting will catch safety problems related to algorithmic bias. But here's the reality: bias in clinical algorithms often manifests as subtle differences in accuracy across patient subgroups. A tool might be 92 percent accurate in white patients and 88 percent accurate in Black patients. That's a statistically detectable difference, but it's not necessarily the kind of safety event that gets reported as an adverse event in the traditional sense. No one patient's outcome is clearly caused by the bias. Instead, there's a systematic erosion of care quality that's hard to attribute to any single tool or decision. The only way to catch that bias is to have transparency about the tool's development and validation from the beginning, so clinicians and health systems can conduct their own analysis of whether the tool performs equally well in their patient population.‚Źé‚Źé**ADDRESSING THE INNOVATION ARGUMENT**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to acknowledge the core argument for deregulation, because it's not wrong exactly. The current certification process does add time and cost to getting decision support tools to market. Some developers have told us they delay deployment of new AI tools because of uncertainty about whether they'll need DSI certification. That's a real problem. Innovation matters. Better tools matter. But the solution isn't to remove transparency requirements entirely. The solution is to streamline the certification process while keeping the core transparency mandate.‚Źé‚ŹéThe FDA's recent guidance on clinical decision support software actually provides a reasonable framework here. Under 21 CFR 201.56(d), certain decision support tools are exempt from premarket FDA review if they meet specific criteria, including that they provide recommendations that clinicians can independently review and that they don't drive clinical decisions without human oversight. The DSI certification criterion doesn't conflict with that framework. In fact, it complements it by ensuring that when clinicians are reviewing and validating AI recommendations, they have adequate information about what they're reviewing.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat we're asking for is not a return to more regulation. We're asking that the current level of transparency requirement be maintained while the process for compliance is streamlined. The model card requirement itself doesn't need to be burdensome. It's a structured documentation format. The issue is whether transparency about training data, validation results, and known limitations is required. That's not a procedural question. That's a substantive safety question, and the answer should be yes.‚Źé‚Źé**OUR SPECIFIC RECOMMENDATION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Texas Medical Association recommends that the ASTP/ONC retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion, specifically the requirement that AI-based clinical decision support tools include model cards disclosing intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. We further recommend that ONC work with developers and health IT vendors to streamline the certification process for DSI compliance, potentially by providing templates for model card documentation and creating a more efficient review pathway. But the transparency requirement itself should remain in place.‚Źé‚ŹéWe also recommend that ONC issue guidance clarifying that model card requirements apply specifically to clinical decision support algorithms that make recommendations about diagnosis, treatment, or patient risk stratification, and that simpler tools that provide information without making recommendations may have reduced documentation requirements. This could address some of the efficiency concerns raised in the proposal while maintaining safety standards for high-risk tools.‚Źé‚ŹéThe stakes here are real. Our physicians care about patient safety. We also care about innovation and efficiency. But we can't sacrifice transparency about how clinical decision support tools work in order to reduce developer burden. The clinicians and patients who rely on these tools deserve to know what they're getting. The model card requirement is the mechanism that makes that possible. Removing it would be a step backward, even if it's dressed up as deregulation.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-16T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-16T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ6‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒresigned‚ôĒGeorgia‚ôĒresearch director‚ôĒ60‚ôĒBack in 2019, I watched a colleague's patient records get accessed by a pharmaceutical rep without proper authorization‚ÄĒnothing dramatic happened, but the breach revealed how easily our current safeguards can be circumvented. Now I'm caught between wanting researchers like myself to access better datasets for comparative effectiveness studies and knowing that loosening those guardrails puts patients in neighborhoods like the one my daughter's clinic serves at disproportionate risk of becoming research subjects without meaningful recourse.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1893
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0026‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒLinda Lopez‚ôĒbeHuman Corp.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒbeHuman Corp. argues that while certification compliance burdens on smaller vendors are genuine concerns, weakening transparency requirements for clinical decision support tools would compromise patient safety and actually advantage larger competi...‚ôĒ**beHuman Corp. ‚ÄĒ Comment on ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions**‚Źé‚ŹébeHuman Corp., a mid-sized health IT vendor based in New Jersey with expertise in electronic health record customization and clinical decision support, respectfully submits this comment on the proposed deregulatory actions. Our organization has spent the past decade working with health systems of varying sizes, and we have direct experience with both the unintended consequences of overly rigid certification regimes and the genuine patient safety risks that arise when transparency requirements are weakened. We want to be clear about what we support and what concerns us.‚Źé‚ŹéThe conversation around reducing compliance burden is one we take seriously. Last year, we lost a hospital client specifically because we could not absorb the recertification costs for a minor EHR customization they needed to serve their patient population more effectively. Our larger competitors simply built that cost into their operations. We had to make difficult decisions, including reducing our development team. The economic pressure created by unequal compliance burdens is real, and we understand the appeal of streamlining the certification process.‚Źé‚ŹéBut streamlining and gutting transparency are not the same thing. We want to speak specifically about the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion and the model card requirements under 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1). We believe these requirements should remain in place, and we want to explain why from our operational perspective.‚Źé‚Źé**Why DSI Transparency Requirements Matter**‚Źé‚ŹéOur experience deploying clinical decision support tools across 14 health systems has taught us something that cost accounting alone does not capture: clinicians need to understand what they are using. When we implemented our sepsis prediction model at a mid-Atlantic teaching hospital in 2022, the attending physicians asked specific questions. What patient populations was the model trained on? Were pediatric patients included? What were the known failure modes? What external validation had been performed? These were not bureaucratic obstacles. They were responsible clinicians trying to understand whether they could trust a tool with patient care decisions.‚Źé‚ŹéThe model card requirement under the current DSI criterion is not ornamental. It requires vendors to document:‚Źé‚Źé- Intended use and target population‚Źé- Known limitations and risks‚Źé- Training data characteristics and provenance‚Źé- External validation results beyond development cohorts‚Źé- Performance across relevant demographic subgroups‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not theoretical. It directly affects how safely a tool can be deployed. A sepsis prediction model developed on data from adult ICU patients in urban academic medical centers may perform differently (sometimes dangerously differently) when applied to rural critical access hospitals or pediatric populations. Clinicians need to know this. Health systems need to know this for credentialing and liability purposes.‚Źé‚Źé**The Competitive Argument Works Both Ways**‚Źé‚ŹéWe have heard the argument that certification burden disproportionately affects smaller vendors. That is true in some cases. But transparency requirements do not primarily burden vendors; they protect clinical users. If anything, they level the playing field. A smaller company like ours can compete on the quality and honesty of our model documentation. We cannot compete on marketing budgets or sales infrastructure, but we can compete on the integrity of our work. Removing or weakening the DSI criterion would actually advantage larger vendors who can afford to operate in a gray zone where their decision support tools are poorly understood by the clinicians using them.‚Źé‚Źé**What We Recommend**‚Źé‚ŹéMaintain the current DSI certification criterion requiring model cards and transparency disclosures. Consider streamlining the submission process, the documentation format, or the review timeline if you believe those create unnecessary friction. We would support that conversation. But do not remove the requirement that vendors explain what their tools do, who they were validated on, and what risks clinicians should monitor for. That is not compliance theater. That is patient safety.‚Źé‚ŹéThe certification burden problem is real. The solution is not to make clinical decision support tools less transparent.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-17T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-17T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒurgent‚ôĒNew Jersey‚ôĒcompliance officer‚ôĒ60‚ôĒLast year, our mid-sized vendor lost a critical hospital client because we couldn't afford the recertification costs for a minor EHR customization they needed‚ÄĒmeanwhile, the big three vendors just absorbed it as a line item. I watched our CEO decide not to pursue two other health systems with similar requests, and we had to let go three developers. If the certification burden actually shifts, we finally have a fighting chance to compete on innovation instead of just compliance budgets.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ638
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0027‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDeborah Mitchell‚ôĒURAALI Refaluwasch Association‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe URAALI Refaluwasch Association opposes removing Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria, arguing that while streamlining certification processes is acceptable, eliminating transparency requirements for AI clinical decision ...‚ôĒ# Comment on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions‚Źé‚ŹéDeborah Mitchell‚ŹéDirector of Government Affairs‚ŹéURAALI Refaluwasch Association‚ŹéSalt Lake City, Utah‚Źé‚ŹéTo the Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology:‚Źé‚ŹéThe URAALI Refaluwasch Association represents patient advocacy communities across rural Utah and parts of the Mountain West, and we have spent the last eighteen months working directly with families who are trying to understand their own genetic testing results. This experience has shaped how we approach the proposed deregulatory changes, and I want to be direct: we support removing unnecessary barriers to data access, but the current proposal to eliminate Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria goes too far and creates real risks for the people we serve.‚Źé‚ŹéBefore I explain why, I should be clear about what we're not arguing. We're not opposed to reducing regulatory burden. We're not arguing that innovation should be constrained by outdated requirements. What we are concerned about is removing safety guardrails that were put in place because previous systems failed vulnerable patients. The families we work with in our member communities don't have the resources to litigate if an AI-assisted clinical decision leads them down the wrong path. They depend on the assumption that when a healthcare provider uses a tool to help make a diagnosis or treatment recommendation, that tool has been vetted in some meaningful way.‚Źé‚Źé**THE ACTUAL PROBLEM WE'RE TRYING TO SOLVE**‚Źé‚ŹéLet me ground this in something concrete. Last year, one of the families we worked with, the Jorgensens from Emery County, requested their genetic testing results from a local lab. The lab refused to release the raw data, citing HIPAA privacy regulations. What struck us was that the same lab had already sold aggregated versions of that data to a pharmaceutical research consortium without ever asking the Jorgensens for permission. The family had no idea their genetic information was being used for research purposes. But when they asked for their own results, suddenly privacy became a major concern.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is the kind of thing that breaks trust in the system. The Jorgensens weren't asking for anything unreasonable. They wanted to understand their own health information. Instead, they hit a wall that felt arbitrary and contradictory. That experience forced us to think carefully about what "deregulation" actually means in healthcare. It can mean liberating patients from unnecessary restrictions on their own data. Or it can mean liberating companies from accountability for how they use patient information. Those aren't the same thing, and the distinction matters.‚Źé‚ŹéI raise this because the proposal to remove DSI certification criteria sounds, on its surface, like it's about reducing burden. The language talks about unleashing innovation and letting market competition drive better products. But when you talk to healthcare providers in rural areas like ours, what they actually need isn't fewer rules. It's clarity about which tools work, which ones have been tested, and what their limitations are. Right now, if a provider is considering implementing an AI tool to help with clinical decision-making, they can at least look at the certification documentation and see what population the tool was validated on, what the known risks are, and whether it's been tested in a setting like theirs. If we remove that requirement, providers are making decisions in the dark.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THE DSI CRITERIA EXIST AND WHAT HAPPENS IF THEY DISAPPEAR**‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criteria were developed to address a specific problem. According to the 2015 Health IT Certification Program guidance (45 CFR 170.315), decision support tools that are used to assist clinical decision-making carry particular risks because they influence patient care directly. A provider who uses a poorly validated tool might not realize it's giving bad advice. The patient certainly won't know. The criteria require that models include transparency disclosures about intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. This isn't bureaucratic busywork. It's the minimum documentation needed for a provider to make an informed choice about whether to use a tool.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat concerns me is that removing this requirement doesn't actually make anything simpler for providers. It just shifts the burden. Instead of having a standardized way to evaluate tools, providers will be left comparing marketing materials, vendor promises, and whatever information they can find in medical literature or through professional networks. Rural providers especially are at a disadvantage here. We don't have large IT teams or relationships with vendors that big urban systems have. When a vendor tells us their tool is validated and works well, we have limited ability to verify that claim independently.‚Źé‚ŹéI keep thinking about one of the conversations we had with a clinic director in Daggett County. She said something that stuck with me: "I trust the people I work with, but I don't have time to become an expert in machine learning. If I'm going to use an AI tool, I need someone independent to have already asked the hard questions." That's what the certification criteria do. They create a space where someone other than the vendor has verified the basic claims.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule references the Information Blocking Conditions exceptions (45 CFR 171.102 and related sections) as justification for removing prescriptive requirements on how data should be shared and how tools should be designed. The logic seems to be that if we reduce requirements, market competition will automatically drive quality. But that assumes everyone in the market has equal access to information and equal power to negotiate. That's not true in healthcare, especially in rural areas. Consolidation is happening rapidly. A few large vendors control most of the market. Competition doesn't automatically fix problems when the playing field is uneven.‚Źé‚Źé**THE SPECIFIC RISKS OF REMOVING MODEL CARDS AND TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéLet me be concrete about what disappears if we remove the DSI criteria. Currently, when a health IT vendor submits an AI decision support tool for certification, they have to provide what amounts to a model card: documentation of what the tool is designed to do, who it was tested on, what its limitations are, and what validation evidence supports those claims. This is based on standards that have emerged in the broader AI field, including the FDA's guidance on AI/ML in healthcare (FDA guidance document 2021) and emerging best practices around algorithmic transparency.‚Źé‚ŹéIf that requirement goes away, vendors can choose whether to provide this information. Some will, because they're responsible companies that want to build trust. Others will provide minimal information, because there's no competitive disadvantage to doing so. A provider choosing between two tools, neither of which has transparency documentation, has no way to distinguish between them except on price or interface design. The tool that was better validated loses out if it's more expensive.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what makes me anxious about this specifically for our communities: AI tools trained on data from large, urban academic medical centers often don't work as well on rural populations. This isn't a secret. It's well documented in the literature on AI bias and health disparities. A tool trained primarily on patients from Salt Lake City might not have seen enough cases of valley fever or other endemic conditions in our region to recognize them properly. Without transparency requirements, a rural provider has no way of knowing whether the tool was validated on rural populations. The vendor has no obligation to disclose it.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal argues that removing these requirements will speed up the market and allow innovation to happen faster. I don't doubt that it will. But faster isn't always better. What we're asking for is a middle ground: keep the transparency requirements, but streamline the process for getting certification. Require vendors to disclose intended use, target population, known limitations, and validation evidence. Don't require them to go through a months-long review process. Let them self-certify that they meet these standards, with audit provisions if problems emerge.‚Źé‚Źé**THE EQUITY DIMENSION THAT GETS OVERLOOKED**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to circle back to something I said at the beginning, because it's central to why our organization is engaging on this issue. When we talk about deregulation in healthcare, there's often an implicit assumption that lighter regulation is good for everyone. It's good for companies because they have fewer compliance costs. It's good for consumers because innovation happens faster and prices might come down. But that model assumes all consumers have equal ability to evaluate and push back on products that don't work for them. In healthcare, that's not true.‚Źé‚ŹéIf a large health system discovers that an AI tool isn't working well for their patient population, they have the resources to stop using it, switch vendors, or even develop their own solution. They can hire consultants to evaluate the tool's performance in their setting. They can demand better documentation from the vendor. A small rural clinic can do none of those things. If they implement a tool and it turns out to be problematic, they're stuck. Switching costs are high. They don't have staff to evaluate alternatives.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat concerns me even more is that lighter regulation disproportionately affects the populations that already face the worst healthcare outcomes. Rural areas have higher rates of uninsured and underinsured patients. They have fewer specialists. They depend on AI tools to help fill gaps in expertise. If those tools are poorly validated or designed for populations different from theirs, it compounds existing inequities rather than addressing them.‚Źé‚ŹéThe 2020 HHS Office for Civil Rights guidance on health IT and civil rights protections (Federal Register notice) acknowledged that health IT can either reduce or amplify existing disparities depending on how it's designed and deployed. Removing transparency requirements increases the risk that it will amplify them, because developers have no obligation to think about whether their tools work across different populations.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE'RE ACTUALLY ASKING FOR**‚Źé‚ŹéI realize I've been mostly explaining what we oppose. Let me be clear about what we support and what we think is actually achievable.‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, we support removing certification requirements that don't serve a clear safety or equity purpose. If there are requirements in the current 45 CFR 170.315 framework that are purely administrative burden, get rid of them. We're not defending every requirement just because it exists.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, we support streamlining the certification process itself. The current timeline for certification can take months. That's a real burden on vendors, especially smaller companies. But streamlining the process is different from removing the underlying standards. You can have fast certification without eliminating transparency requirements.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, we think the transparency requirements should be modernized to reflect how AI actually works. Model cards were developed in the academic AI community and adapted for healthcare use. They make sense, but they could probably be more streamlined. Instead of requiring extensive documentation, require vendors to disclose:‚Źé‚Źé- The intended clinical use case and population‚Źé- Key characteristics of the training data (size, source, demographic composition)‚Źé- External validation results (how the tool performed on data it wasn't trained on)‚Źé- Known limitations and failure modes‚Źé- How the tool handles edge cases or unusual inputs‚Źé‚ŹéThat's not a massive burden. Most responsible vendors are already documenting this anyway. What we're asking is that it become a standard requirement rather than optional.‚Źé‚ŹéFourth, we support creating a certification pathway specifically designed for rural and underserved settings. One of the problems right now is that tools tend to get validated in large academic medical centers. If vendors knew there was a distinct certification pathway for tools that had been validated in rural settings or for treating underserved populations, they'd have an incentive to do that work. It would create market differentiation.‚Źé‚Źé**ADDRESSING THE DEREGULATION ARGUMENT DIRECTLY**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to engage seriously with the deregulation argument because I think it has real merit in some contexts. Healthcare is over-regulated in places where regulation serves no one. Providers spend enormous resources on compliance activities that don't improve patient care. That's waste, and it's reasonable to want to eliminate it.‚Źé‚ŹéBut the DSI certification criteria aren't that kind of regulation. They're not administrative requirements that exist for their own sake. They serve a specific function: they create a baseline of accountability for tools that directly influence clinical decision-making. Removing them doesn't free up provider resources to do something better. It just removes a check on vendor behavior.‚Źé‚ŹéThe deregulation argument also assumes that markets work efficiently in healthcare, and they don't. Patients don't shop for health IT. Providers often don't have real choices about which tools to implement. Vendors lock in through integration, training, and switching costs. In that environment, transparency requirements aren't anti-competitive. They're pro-competitive, because they level the information asymmetry that favors large, established vendors.‚Źé‚ŹéI also want to push back gently on the framing that innovation requires deregulation. Some of the most innovative work in AI and healthcare is happening at companies and research institutions that embrace transparency standards. They understand that transparency builds trust, which builds adoption. The companies arguing loudest for removing requirements are often the ones that can afford the least transparency.‚Źé‚Źé**IMPLEMENTATION CONCERNS AND A PATH FORWARD**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the rule does remove DSI certification criteria, there are implementation issues worth thinking through. First, what happens to tools that are currently certified? Do they get grandfathered in? Do they lose their certification status? There's a real question about whether removing the criteria retroactively would create chaos in the market.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, if certification is optional, how do providers know which tools have been through any kind of review process? There's a question about whether some form of voluntary certification pathway should remain, so that vendors who want to signal quality can do so.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, and this is important for rural providers, there needs to be some mechanism for ongoing performance monitoring. If a tool isn't certified, how do providers know if it's working well in their setting? Right now, they can at least look at validation evidence. If that goes away, monitoring becomes more important, not less. But providers don't have resources to do sophisticated performance analysis. Who's responsible for identifying when a tool isn't working?‚Źé‚ŹéWhat we're proposing is a tiered approach. Keep transparency requirements for tools that directly influence clinical decisions. These don't have to go through lengthy certification, but vendors need to disclose what we listed above. For tools that don't directly influence clinical decisions, or that are used more as reference tools, the requirements can be lighter. And create a voluntary certification pathway for vendors who want to signal that they've met higher standards.‚Źé‚ŹéThis approach addresses the deregulation concern without eliminating accountability. It speeds up the market for tools that don't carry significant risk. And it preserves the ability for providers and patients to make informed choices about tools that do influence care.‚Źé‚Źé**THE RURAL PERSPECTIVE**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to close by emphasizing something that probably doesn't come through in formal regulatory comments, but which drives everything our organization does. Rural healthcare is fragile. Many of our clinics are operating on thin margins. They're using outdated electronic health record systems because they can't afford to upgrade. They're treating patients with complex health problems using limited specialist resources. In that context, tools that help them deliver better care faster could be genuinely transformative.‚Źé‚ŹéBut those same clinics are also vulnerable to being sold tools that don't actually work for them, or that work worse for them than for urban populations. They don't have IT staff to evaluate vendor claims. They don't have the luxury of trying something, finding it doesn't work, and switching to something else. When a clinic implements a tool, they're usually living with it for years.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat we're asking for is pretty simple: keep requiring vendors to be honest about what their tools do and who they were tested on. Don't require extensive certification, but require transparency. And think about the rural providers who are going to be implementing these tools. We're not against innovation. We're for innovation that actually works in our communities.‚Źé‚ŹéThe families we worked with last year, including the Jorgensens, eventually got access to their genetic testing results. But it took months, and it required intervention from our organization. That shouldn't be how it works. And when those same families use health IT tools to manage their care, they deserve to know that those tools have been validated for people like them, with their health conditions and their genetic backgrounds.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving the DSI certification criteria won't make that happen faster. It will just remove one of the few mechanisms we have to ensure that vendors take these questions seriously.‚Źé‚ŹéWe appreciate the opportunity to comment on this proposal, and we're available if you have questions about our perspective or the experiences we've documented with member communities.‚Źé‚ŹéRespectfully submitted,‚Źé‚ŹéDeborah Mitchell‚ŹéDirector of Government Affairs‚ŹéURAALI Refaluwasch Association‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-25T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-25T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ9‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving DSI transparency requirements creates a dangerous regulatory gap where AI-based clinical decision support tools face fewer disclosure obligations than traditional medical devices, undermining consistent safety standards.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒsupportive‚ôĒUtah‚ôĒdirector of government affairs‚ôĒ41‚ôĒLast year, I worked with three separate patient families in our rural communities who couldn't access their own genetic testing results because the labs cited privacy regulations as a barrier‚ÄĒyet those same families had no idea their data was already being aggregated by third parties for research purposes they never consented to. It crystallized for me that our current framework protects institutional interests more than it protects the people we're supposed to serve, and I'm caught between advocating for the transparency my members desperately need and worrying we'll trade privacy safeguards that vulnerable populations depend on in the process.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2792
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0028‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDonald Williams‚ôĒRegard Technologies, Inc.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒRegard Technologies, a mid-sized health IT vendor, supports streamlining health IT certification processes to reduce costs for small vendors and providers, but opposes eliminating transparency requirements for clinical decision support systems, ar...‚ôĒ**Comment on the Proposed Deregulation of Health IT Certification and Information Blocking Requirements**‚Źé‚ŹéRegard Technologies, Inc. respectfully submits this comment on the Department of Health and Human Services' proposed deregulatory actions concerning health IT certification criteria, standards requirements, and information blocking regulations. We are a mid-sized health IT vendor based in Massachusetts with over fifteen years of experience developing electronic health record modules and clinical decision support systems for community health centers, small hospital networks, and primary care practices across New England. Our client base consists almost entirely of smaller healthcare organizations that operate on tight margins and cannot absorb the compliance costs that larger systems treat as routine line items.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule has raised substantial concerns for us, particularly regarding the proposal to eliminate or substantially weaken the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion. We want to be direct about this: we support retaining the current DSI requirements, including the mandate for AI model cards and transparency disclosures. This is not a position we arrive at lightly, given the regulatory burden that certification itself places on vendors like us. But there is a difference between streamlining cumbersome processes and abandoning safety guardrails, and we believe the distinction matters enormously for the community health providers who depend on our tools.‚Źé‚Źé**The Real Cost of Certification Burden and Why It Matters**‚Źé‚ŹéLet me describe what happened last year, because it shapes how we think about this rule. A clinic director from a federally qualified health center in Springfield reached out to our team in March 2023. Her organization serves about 8,000 patients annually, many of them uninsured or on Medicaid. They had been using our basic EHR module for three years with good results. But their clinical workflows had evolved. They needed customization in how the system handled medication reconciliation and flagged potential drug interactions for their patient population, which skews older and has high rates of polypharmacy. The request itself was reasonable. The problem was the certification pathway.‚Źé‚ŹéTo modify our DSI module to meet her specific clinical needs, we would have needed to undergo a full recertification process through ONC-Authorized Certification Bodies (OACBs). The cost estimate came back at $180,000 to $240,000, depending on the scope of testing required. This was for a customization that would affect perhaps 2,000 patient encounters per year at a single organization. When I presented this to the clinic director, she had to make a choice that no healthcare leader should face: spend six figures on certification for a single client, or tell them no and watch them implement a workaround that might be less safe but requires no certification at all. She chose the workaround. That conversation has stayed with me.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is the real world impact of certification burden, and it is genuinely unacceptable. Small health IT vendors cannot sustain business models where each customization requires six-figure recertification costs. Smaller healthcare providers cannot absorb those costs. The result is not less regulation; it is worse regulation, because providers implement unvetted solutions outside the certification framework altogether. The current system fails at its own goal when the burden becomes so high that organizations abandon the system entirely.‚Źé‚ŹéBut here is what concerns us about the proposed rule: the solution to this problem is not to eliminate transparency requirements for clinical decision support systems. The solution is to streamline the certification process itself, perhaps through tiered approaches, modular recertification pathways, or faster-track reviews for lower-risk modifications. We need a better process, not no process. And the reason is patient safety.‚Źé‚Źé**Why AI Model Cards and Transparency Matter in Clinical Contexts**‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI criterion currently requires vendors to provide model cards detailing intended use, target population, known performance limitations, training data characteristics, and external validation results. This is not bureaucratic theater. It is the minimum documentation needed for a clinician to understand what a decision support system is actually doing and whether it is appropriate for their patient population.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider a hypothetical: a decision support algorithm trained primarily on data from academic medical centers with predominantly insured, non-Hispanic white populations. That algorithm is deployed in a federally qualified health center serving primarily Latino and Black patients with lower insurance rates and different patterns of chronic disease. The algorithm may perform adequately on average across the general population, but its performance in the specific population being treated may be substantially different. A clinician who does not know the training data characteristics and does not understand this limitation may over-rely on the algorithm's recommendations, with potential consequences for patient safety.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not speculation. The literature on algorithmic bias in healthcare is substantial. A 2019 study published in *Science*, examining a widely used clinical risk algorithm, found that Black patients assigned the same risk score as white patients were actually sicker, because the algorithm used healthcare costs as a proxy for illness severity, and racial disparities in healthcare access meant that Black patients incurred lower costs for equivalent illness (Obermeyer et al., 2019). That algorithm had been in clinical use for years before this bias was identified. Had the training data characteristics and validation methodology been transparent and well-documented, clinicians and health IT leaders might have caught this problem earlier.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification criterion is an attempt to prevent this kind of failure. It asks vendors to document what we know about our algorithms, what populations they have been tested on, what their performance limitations are, and what they were designed to do. For vendors like us, this requires work. But it is work that we should be doing anyway. And for clinicians using our tools, it provides information they need to use them safely.‚Źé‚Źé**The Distinction Between Burden Reduction and Safety Reduction**‚Źé‚ŹéWe want to emphasize something that we think gets lost in deregulatory discussions: there is a meaningful difference between reducing unnecessary burden and eliminating safety requirements. The certification process as it currently exists does impose real costs. We have experienced those costs directly. The testing requirements are sometimes redundant. The timelines can be unpredictable. The fees charged by OACBs vary substantially. There are legitimate opportunities to streamline without weakening.‚Źé‚ŹéBut the DSI transparency requirements are not redundant or unnecessary. They serve a specific function: they create accountability for how decision support systems are designed, what data they were trained on, and what their limitations are. For a clinician deciding whether to implement a decision support tool in their workflow, this information matters. For a patient whose care is influenced by a decision support recommendation, this information matters. For health IT oversight bodies trying to understand why an algorithm performed poorly in a specific population, this information matters.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the proposed rule eliminates the requirement for model cards and transparency disclosures, we will have solved the vendor burden problem by shifting the burden entirely to healthcare providers. Providers will be responsible for reverse-engineering the behavior of decision support systems, documenting their limitations, and managing the risks. This is not burden reduction; it is burden shifting, and it shifts burden onto the entities least equipped to handle it.‚Źé‚Źé**What a Workable Alternative Looks Like**‚Źé‚ŹéRegard Technologies recommends that the final rule retain the DSI certification criterion with its current transparency requirements, but modify the implementation pathway to reduce unnecessary burden on vendors. Specifically:‚Źé‚Źé- Implement a tiered recertification approach where minor modifications to clinical decision support systems (e.g., updating underlying data sources, adjusting alert thresholds within the same clinical domain, or expanding to similar patient populations) receive expedited review and reduced fees, rather than full recertification. This could be modeled on the FDA's 510(k) process for medical devices, which distinguishes between substantially similar modifications and those requiring full premarket review (21 CFR 860.3).‚Źé‚Źé- Create a modular recertification framework where vendors can recertify specific components of a system (e.g., a medication interaction module) rather than requiring recertification of the entire system when only one component changes. This would allow us to serve clients like the Springfield clinic more cost-effectively.‚Źé‚Źé- Standardize fees and timelines across OACBs. One of our challenges is that recertification costs vary widely depending on which OACB we work with. Published fee schedules and guaranteed timelines would allow us to plan more effectively.‚Źé‚Źé- Allow vendors to document algorithm updates through supplemental filings when the underlying clinical logic has not changed materially, rather than requiring full retesting. This is especially important for systems that rely on machine learning models that are periodically retrained on new data.‚Źé‚Źé- Establish a clear definition of what constitutes a material change requiring recertification versus a minor update requiring documentation. Right now, the boundary is ambiguous, which creates uncertainty and encourages vendors to recertify more frequently than might be necessary.‚Źé‚ŹéThese changes would substantially reduce the burden on vendors while maintaining the transparency requirements that protect patients and clinicians. We could serve our community health center clients more affordably. We could respond to their workflow customization requests without requiring them to choose between certification costs and hiring clinical staff. And clinicians would still have access to the information they need to use decision support systems safely.‚Źé‚Źé**Implementation and Transition**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the rule retains the DSI transparency requirements with a tiered recertification approach, Regard Technologies would need a transition period of 18 to 24 months to align our existing systems with any revised certification pathways. We currently have four decision support modules in active use across approximately 47 community health center clients in Massachusetts, Connecticut, and Rhode Island. Depending on how the tiered framework is structured, some of these systems may require recertification under the new pathway. We would want adequate time to work with OACBs to understand the new requirements and to plan recertification activities in a way that does not disrupt client operations.‚Źé‚ŹéWe would also benefit from guidance documents clarifying what qualifies for expedited versus full recertification, and what documentation is required for supplemental updates to existing systems. The ONC has done this effectively for other certification criteria (see the ONC Health IT Certification Companion Guide, updated annually), and the same approach would work well here.‚Źé‚Źé**Broader Concerns About Information Blocking and Standards**‚Źé‚ŹéWhile our primary focus is the DSI criterion, we want to flag a concern about the broader deregulatory approach reflected in the proposed rule. The information blocking regulations, implemented under 42 CFR 171, have been burdensome for vendors and providers. We understand the frustration. But the intent behind information blocking is sound: to prevent health IT developers and healthcare organizations from using technology as a tool to lock in customers by making data difficult to export or share. In our experience, most of the burden comes from uncertainty about what constitutes prohibited information blocking, not from the requirement itself. The solution is clearer guidance, not elimination of the rule.‚Źé‚ŹéSimilarly, while we would welcome flexibility in standards requirements, the push toward interoperability through standards like HL7 FHIR (45 CFR 170.215) has been valuable. We have built FHIR APIs into our systems specifically because our clients need to exchange data with other systems. The standardization reduces our development costs over time, even though the initial investment is substantial. We would not want to see a return to proprietary data exchange mechanisms.‚Źé‚Źé**Conclusion**‚Źé‚ŹéRegard Technologies supports targeted burden reduction in health IT certification and regulation. We experience the burden directly, and we understand its real costs for small vendors and for the healthcare providers we serve. But we cannot support a deregulatory approach that eliminates transparency requirements for clinical decision support systems. The DSI certification criterion, particularly the requirements for model cards and algorithm transparency, serves an important function in accountability and patient safety. The solution is to streamline the process, not to abandon it.‚Źé‚ŹéWe urge the Department of Health and Human Services to revise the proposed rule to retain the DSI transparency requirements while implementing a tiered, modular recertification framework that reduces burden on vendors without shifting risk onto healthcare providers and patients. This approach would achieve the deregulatory goal of reducing unnecessary burden while preserving the safeguards that are genuinely necessary.‚Źé‚ŹéRegard Technologies is available to discuss these recommendations further and to provide additional detail on the specific implementation challenges we have encountered in the current certification process.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-26T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-26T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ6‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒconcerned‚ôĒMassachusetts‚ôĒdirector of government relations‚ôĒ58‚ôĒWhen our smaller clients‚ÄĒcommunity health centers across New England‚ÄĒapproached us last year about customizing our EHR module for their specific workflows, we had to tell them no, not without a six-figure recertification process that made the economics impossible for them. I watched a clinic director's face fall when she realized she'd have to choose between the certification costs and hiring another nurse. That conversation has stayed with me through every vendor meeting since.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2028
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0029‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒWilliam Garcia‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe commenter argues that transparency requirements and disclosure mechanisms for health data systems are essential for caregiver safety and oversight, asserting that the lack of published evidence should not justify eliminating protections that h...‚ôĒMy mother was diagnosed with diabetes last year, and I spent weeks chasing down her records from three different clinics just to understand what medications she was taking and what her test results actually showed. Now that I'm her full-time caregiver, I'm worried that making it easier to move her information around will mean less control over who sees it, especially since I'm the one responsible for keeping her safe.‚Źé‚ŹéI don't think the agency is making the right call by saying there's no published evidence that transparency requirements help patients. That's not how safety works when you're responsible for someone else's care. Just because researchers haven't done a formal study yet doesn't mean AI model cards and disclosure requirements have no value. My mother's situation shows why knowing what information exists and who can access it matters. The thing is, caregivers like me don't have time to become data security experts. We need clear, built-in protections so we can focus on actual patient care instead of worrying about hidden risks and uncontrolled data sharing.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-07T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-07T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe agency's claim that 'no publicly available evidence' supports transparency requirements conflates absence of evidence with evidence of absence; lack of published studies does not mean the criterion provides no value.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒmedium‚ôĒconcerned‚ôĒOklahoma‚ôĒcaregiver‚ôĒ55‚ôĒWhen my mother was diagnosed with diabetes last year, I had to chase down her records from three different clinics just to get a clear picture of her medications and test results‚ÄĒit took me weeks. Now that she's in my care full-time, I'm worried that making it easier to move her information around might mean less control over who actually sees it, especially since I'm the one managing her privacy as her caregiver.‚ôĒThe agency is making a logical mistake by saying 'no published evidence' means these transparency requirements don't help patients‚ÄĒthat's not how safety works in healthcare, especially when you're the one responsible for someone else's care. | Just because researchers haven't published a formal study yet doesn't mean AI model cards and disclosure requirements have no value; my mother's case shows why knowing what information exists and who can access it matters for real patient safety. | Removing transparency requirements assumes the market will police itself, but caregivers like me don't have time to become data security experts‚ÄĒwe need clear, built-in protections so we can focus on actual patient care.‚ôĒA caregiver worried that deregulation based on 'missing studies' will strip away the transparency safeguards that help families like mine protect vulnerable patients from hidden risks and uncontrolled data sharing.‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ175
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0030‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒMargaret Perez‚ôĒNo Delays, No Surprises Coalition‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe No Delays, No Surprises Coalition opposes the removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures, arguing that these requirements are essential for informed consent, acc...‚ôĒNo Delays, No Surprises Coalition‚ŹéBirmingham, Alabama‚Źé‚ŹéRE: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: Certification and Adoption Promotion System (CAPS) and Related Health IT Policy‚ŹéDocket No. HHS-OS-2025-0001‚Źé‚ŹéThe No Delays, No Surprises Coalition appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed modifications to health IT certification criteria and information blocking regulations. Our coalition represents patient advocates, health equity organizations, and safety-focused providers across Alabama and the Southeast who have worked for the past three years to improve care coordination while maintaining patient protection.‚Źé‚ŹéWe write with a specific concern about the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria, particularly the requirement for AI model cards and transparency disclosures that detail intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. This concern is not abstract. It comes from real experience watching how fragmented information and poor system design affect real patients.‚Źé‚ŹéMy mother's case last year illustrates what happens when systems prioritize speed over accountability. When she was diagnosed with stage-two breast cancer at UAB Comprehensive Cancer Center, her oncology notes stayed at UAB. The pathology report from Grandview Medical Center existed in a separate system. Her radiation planning happened at yet another facility. When I tried to get her records consolidated to seek a second opinion at MD Anderson in Houston, we spent six weeks on phone calls and faxes. She has limited English proficiency and almost no comfort with digital systems. If records had moved between systems without explicit consent, if algorithms had made recommendations about her treatment without clear disclosure of how those algorithms worked, she would have been completely vulnerable. She wouldn't have known what information was being shared or what was driving clinical recommendations.‚Źé‚ŹéThis matters because it's not unusual. The Alabama Hospital Association reported in 2024 that nearly 60 percent of cancer patients in our state receive treatment across multiple facilities. When patients like my mother cannot easily understand what information is being used to guide their care, when algorithms operate as black boxes, the entire foundation of informed consent collapses.‚Źé‚Źé**RETENTION OF AI TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criterion requiring model cards and transparency disclosures serves three distinct functions that the proposed rule does not adequately address through alternative mechanisms.‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, it ensures patients and families understand how algorithms influence clinical decisions. Model cards document the intended use of an AI system, which populations it was tested on, what its known limitations are, and what data was used in training. This is not regulatory overhead. This is the basic information needed for informed consent. A clinician reviewing a DSI recommendation for a cancer patient needs to know whether the model was trained on a demographically representative sample. If an AI system was developed using primarily data from large academic medical centers, a rural patient or a patient from an underrepresented population should know that. They should know whether the algorithm's performance was validated in their specific clinical context. 42 CFR 170.315(b)(1) currently requires this disclosure, and there is no proposed substitute mechanism that maintains the same level of patient access to this information.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, model cards create accountability for developers. When a company must document what a system was designed to do, what it was validated on, and what its known risks are, that documentation becomes a record against which the system's actual performance can be measured. It becomes evidence if things go wrong. It prevents the scenario where a developer claims an algorithm works well for a particular population when the training data tells a different story.
