We saw problems with this in the early years of racial bias in clinical algorithms. Models that appeared well-functioning actually had significant performance gaps when used with Black patients. Those gaps existed in the training data and validation results, but nobody was required to look at them. Removing the requirement for external validation results and training data transparency recreates that risk.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, model cards support clinical governance. When a hospital adopts an AI system for clinical decision support, the hospital's quality and safety committees need information to oversee that system. They need to know what the system was designed for, what populations it was tested on, and what its known limitations are. Without this information, they cannot determine whether the system is being used appropriately in their setting, whether their patient population matches the validation population, or whether they need additional safeguards. The American Medical Association's Principles on Augmented Intelligence (2023) specifically emphasize that clinicians and health systems need clear information about AI system design and validation to make safe implementation decisions.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule states that transparency requirements create burden for developers and that market competition will encourage better practices. This reasoning contains a basic assumption that may not hold in healthcare: that patients and clinicians have meaningful ability to choose between competing systems based on safety information. In most healthcare settings, health IT purchasing decisions are made by hospital administration or integrated delivery networks, not by individual clinicians or patients. A clinician does not typically evaluate an AI system's training data before it is implemented in their EHR. A patient does not choose which algorithm influences their cancer treatment recommendation. They encounter it after the institution has already adopted it. Market competition in healthcare IT has not historically produced better transparency; it has produced faster implementation of increasingly complex systems with decreasing visibility into how they work.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC OPERATIONAL CONCERNS**‚Źé‚ŹéWe want to be direct about what removal of these criteria means operationally. The current DSI certification criterion requires that AI systems used in clinical decision support meet specific standards for transparency and external validation. If this requirement is removed, a company can market an AI system for cancer treatment planning with documentation of internal testing only, with no requirement to disclose whether the system was tested on diverse patient populations, with no requirement to document known performance limitations, and with no requirement to validate the system in real-world clinical settings before it is widely adopted.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not a theoretical concern. Last year, the FDA issued guidance on clinical decision support software (January 2024) that explicitly acknowledged gaps in oversight for systems that influence clinical decision-making but are not classified as medical devices. The guidance noted that software companies often have minimal regulatory oversight for systems that make treatment recommendations. The gap between FDA oversight and the certification criteria in 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1) is actually substantial. FDA guidance applies to specific medical device categories. The certification criteria apply to all EHR systems. Removing the certification criteria without strengthening FDA oversight creates a compliance gap.‚Źé‚ŹéWe also want to acknowledge that we understand the developer perspective. Model cards take time to develop. External validation costs money. Documenting training data characteristics requires rigorous record-keeping. These are real burdens. But the answer to genuine implementation burden is not to remove the requirement entirely. The answer is to clarify what constitutes adequate documentation, to allow reasonable timelines for external validation, and to provide guidance on how smaller developers can meet the standard. The 21st Century Cures Act (Pub. L. 114-255, Section 4001) explicitly contemplated that certification criteria would evolve to reduce unnecessary burden while maintaining protection for patients and clinicians. It did not contemplate removing safety-critical requirements entirely.‚Źé‚Źé**PATIENT SAFETY AND HEALTH EQUITY IMPLICATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe health equity implications of removing AI transparency requirements deserve separate attention. Algorithmic bias in healthcare has been extensively documented. The study by Obermeyer et al. in Science (2019) demonstrated that a widely used clinical algorithm exhibited significant racial bias because it used healthcare costs as a proxy for health needs. Patients who had not used as much healthcare received lower risk scores even when they had greater clinical need. The algorithm was already in use in clinical settings before the bias was identified. Transparency about training data and validation would not have prevented the bias, but it would have made the bias visible earlier.‚Źé‚ŹéMore recent work on AI systems in oncology has raised similar concerns. Studies of AI systems designed to recommend cancer treatments have found that performance varies significantly across racial and ethnic groups. When validation data is not disaggregated by patient demographic characteristics, these gaps remain invisible. Clinicians implementing the system may not know that it performs differently for different populations. Patients from underrepresented groups may receive recommendations based on an algorithm that was not adequately validated in their population.‚Źé‚ŹéThe requirement for external validation and disaggregated performance data on known demographic characteristics is not a burden placed on developers for regulatory compliance. It is a minimum standard for safe clinical use. Removing this requirement disproportionately affects patients from groups that have historically been excluded from clinical research and algorithm development. If a system was developed primarily using data from large academic medical centers in major metropolitan areas, and validation was conducted only in similar settings, the system may not work well for rural patients or patients in safety-net hospitals. Those patients need to know that. The certification criteria ensure they can know it.‚Źé‚Źé**CLINICAL GOVERNANCE AND IMPLEMENTATION OVERSIGHT**‚Źé‚ŹéHospitals and health systems that adopt DSI tools need information to implement them safely. Right now, under 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1), when a hospital purchases an EHR with integrated clinical decision support, the vendor must provide documentation of the system's intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. The hospital's quality committee can review this information and determine whether the system is appropriate for their patient population and clinical setting. They can identify whether additional safeguards are needed. They can establish governance processes for monitoring the system's performance over time.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the certification requirement is removed, vendors will not be required to provide this information. Some vendors will choose to provide it anyway, because they want to market their systems as safe. Others will provide minimal documentation. Hospitals will have no standard baseline for what information they should demand. Small hospitals without sophisticated health IT expertise may implement systems without understanding their limitations. Rural hospitals and critical access hospitals, which often have limited IT staff, will struggle even more.‚Źé‚ŹéThe American Hospital Association's guidance on AI implementation (2023) emphasizes that hospitals need clear documentation of AI system design and validation to implement these systems responsibly. The guidance recommends that hospitals establish committees to oversee AI implementation, that they evaluate whether AI systems are appropriate for their patient populations, and that they monitor performance over time. None of this is possible without the information that model cards provide.‚Źé‚Źé**INTERNATIONAL PRECEDENT AND REGULATORY ALIGNMENT**‚Źé‚ŹéWe also note that other regulatory jurisdictions have moved toward strengthening AI transparency requirements rather than weakening them. The European Union's AI Act (effective 2024) requires providers of high-risk AI systems to maintain detailed technical documentation including training data characteristics, performance metrics disaggregated by demographic characteristics, and information about known limitations. The UK's AI Bill of Rights and the Canadian regulatory framework on clinical AI systems similarly emphasize transparency and documentation requirements.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not just a matter of regulatory philosophy. It reflects a practical reality: as AI systems become more complex and more widely deployed in clinical settings, clinicians and patients need more information about how they work, not less. The regulatory response should be to standardize and clarify requirements, not to eliminate them.‚Źé‚Źé**ALTERNATIVE APPROACHES TO BURDEN REDUCTION**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the concern is implementation burden, there are more targeted solutions that do not require removing safety-critical requirements entirely.‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, extend timelines for external validation. A system could be certified provisionally based on internal validation with a requirement to complete external validation within 18 months of deployment. This would allow developers to bring systems to market while still ensuring that real-world performance is validated before widespread adoption.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, create tiered documentation requirements based on system risk. A DSI system that makes treatment recommendations for serious illness should require more detailed documentation than a system that provides general clinical decision support. This would reduce burden for lower-risk applications while maintaining requirements for high-stakes decisions.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, provide specific guidance on what constitutes adequate documentation. Developers often struggle not because the requirement is inherently burdensome but because they are uncertain about what ONC expects. Clear guidance documents specifying what information should be included in a model card, how training data should be characterized, and what constitutes adequate external validation would reduce implementation burden while clarifying expectations.‚Źé‚ŹéFourth, establish a technical assistance program for smaller developers. ONC could fund consulting or provide templates to help smaller companies meet certification requirements without the cost of hiring specialized expertise.‚Źé‚ŹéThese approaches address the genuine concern about developer burden without compromising patient safety or removing clinician and patient access to critical information about how clinical AI systems work.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC TECHNICAL COMMENTS ON MODEL CARD ELEMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI criterion requires documentation of intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. Each element serves a specific function.‚Źé‚ŹéIntended use documentation ensures that systems are used as designed. If an algorithm was developed for initial cancer screening and is instead used to guide treatment planning, the intended use documentation makes that misuse visible. This matters because algorithm performance often varies significantly by use case.‚Źé‚ŹéTarget population documentation ensures that clinicians understand whether a system was validated for their patient population. If a system was developed using data from patients aged 50-75 and is applied to younger patients, clinicians need to know that the system has not been validated in that age group. If a system was developed primarily using data from patients with commercial insurance and is applied to uninsured patients, that difference matters.‚Źé‚ŹéKnown risks documentation is perhaps the most critical element. Every algorithm has limitations. Some systems perform poorly for patients with certain comorbidities. Some have higher false positive rates for certain groups. Some are sensitive to input data quality. Documenting these known risks prevents the scenario where a clinician is unaware that a system has a specific limitation relevant to their patient. It allows hospitals to implement additional safeguards or clinical oversight when they are using a system with known limitations in high-risk scenarios.‚Źé‚ŹéTraining data characteristics documentation allows clinicians and hospital quality committees to assess whether the data used to develop a system is representative of their patient population. If a system was trained on data from 90 percent white patients and is being used in a hospital with a 60 percent Black patient population, that discrepancy is important to know. It does not necessarily mean the system should not be used, but it means the hospital should validate performance in their population and monitor for potential disparities.‚Źé‚ŹéExternal validation results documentation ensures that system performance has been tested in real-world settings with real patients, not just in controlled research environments. This is particularly important for AI systems, which often perform worse on real-world data than on the carefully controlled data used during development. ‚Źé‚ŹéWe recognize that documenting these elements requires effort and expertise. But they are not optional elements that developers can skip. They are the minimum information needed for safe clinical use. Removing the requirement does not mean the information becomes unnecessary; it means the information is simply not available to clinicians and patients who need it.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION AND SPECIFIC RECOMMENDATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe No Delays, No Surprises Coalition respectfully urges ONC and ASTP to retain the DSI certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures. We further recommend:‚Źé‚Źé1. If burden reduction is needed, extend external validation timelines to 18 months after initial deployment rather than requiring validation before deployment.‚Źé‚Źé2. Provide detailed guidance documents specifying what information should be included in model cards, with specific examples for different types of clinical DSI systems.‚Źé‚Źé3. Establish tiered certification requirements with higher transparency standards for systems that make treatment recommendations for serious illness and lower requirements for systems that provide general informational support.‚Źé‚Źé4. Fund a technical assistance program for smaller developers to help them meet certification requirements without prohibitive costs.‚Źé‚Źé5. Explicitly clarify that information blocking regulations remain in effect for all DSI systems, whether or not they meet enhanced certification criteria, to ensure that clinicians and patients can access information about system design and performance.‚Źé‚ŹéWe understand that innovation in health IT is important. We also understand that patient safety and informed consent remain foundational to clinical care. These goals are not in conflict. Transparent, well-validated clinical decision support systems serve both innovation and patient safety. Removing transparency requirements in the name of innovation actually risks slowing adoption by creating uncertainty about system safety and creating liability exposure for hospitals that implement systems without adequate information.‚Źé‚ŹéMy mother ultimately did get her second opinion at MD Anderson, but it took six weeks of effort and considerable frustration. She had to call multiple hospitals and fax records manually. If an AI system had been involved in her treatment planning, and if that system had been a black box without transparent documentation of how it worked, she would not have had even the limited ability to understand what was influencing her care. She would have been entirely dependent on clinician explanations of algorithm recommendations, with no way to verify whether the algorithm was appropriate for her situation or whether it had known limitations relevant to her health status.‚Źé‚ŹéThat is what transparency requirements protect against. We ask that ONC and ASTP maintain that protection while working with developers on reasonable approaches to burden reduction.‚Źé‚ŹéRespectfully submitted,‚Źé‚ŹéNo Delays, No Surprises Coalition‚ŹéBirmingham, Alabama‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-17T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-17T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ9‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving DSI transparency requirements creates a dangerous regulatory gap where AI-based clinical decision support tools face fewer disclosure obligations than traditional medical devices, undermining consistent safety standards.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒconcerned‚ôĒAlabama‚ôĒresearch director‚ôĒ48‚ôĒLast spring, my mother was diagnosed with stage-two breast cancer, and I watched her medical records get fragmented across three different hospital systems in the Birmingham area‚ÄĒher oncology notes at UAB, pathology at Grandview, and radiation planning at another facility entirely. When I tried to consolidate her information to get a second opinion, it took six weeks of phone calls and faxes. I understand the appeal of faster data sharing, but I'm also acutely aware that my mother, who speaks limited English and has limited digital literacy, would be completely vulnerable if her records moved between systems without her explicit knowledge or consent.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2906
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0031‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒJessica Clark‚ôĒDoximity, Inc.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA health IT compliance officer at Doximity argues against eliminating the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion, contending that while compliance burdens are real, the requirement for model cards and transparency disclosures...‚ôĒRe: RIN 0945-AA17 ‚Äď Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity‚Źé‚ŹéDoximity submits this comment in response to the proposed rule regarding the removal and revision of ONC certification criteria and standards. As a health IT compliance officer with direct responsibility for ensuring our platform meets regulatory requirements while serving over 800,000 healthcare professionals and thousands of clinical practices, I want to focus on a specific concern about the proposed elimination of the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion.‚Źé‚Źé**The Current DSI Requirement and Why It Exists**‚Źé‚ŹéThe existing DSI certification criterion requires that clinical decision support tools include model cards and transparency disclosures covering intended use, target population, known risks, and training data characteristics. When we first encountered this requirement during our last major platform enhancement two years ago, I'll be honest: our product team groaned. It meant additional documentation, validation studies, and coordination with our vendor partners. It wasn't what anyone would call streamlined.‚Źé‚ŹéBut here's what happened that changed my perspective. One of our smaller clinic partners in Louisville, Kentucky, actually used the model card we provided to identify why a drug interaction alert was firing inappropriately in their workflow. The alert was technically correct but triggered too frequently for their patient population, making providers ignore it. The transparency disclosure showed that our training data came from academic medical centers, not primary care clinics. Once they understood that context, they adjusted their alert settings and recalibrated the tool for their actual patient population. They didn't sue us. They didn't report us. They improved their clinical outcomes. That's the system working.‚Źé‚Źé**Why Removing This Requirement Concerns Us**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal suggests removing the DSI certification criterion as part of broader deregulation efforts. The reasoning seems to be that the criterion creates compliance burden without proportional safety benefit, and that market forces and liability will naturally incentivize vendors to provide transparency anyway.‚Źé‚ŹéI understand the argument. Compliance costs are real. When we implemented our last major EHR upgrade, our smaller clinic partners spent roughly eighteen months and nearly $400,000 just on compliance documentation and vendor coordination. That money came directly from clinical staff budgets. I watched one practice manager at a 12-provider clinic spend nearly half her time as a de facto compliance analyst rather than actually managing operations. The burden is not theoretical.‚Źé‚ŹéBut there's a critical difference between reducing unnecessary compliance overhead and removing requirements that actually connect to patient safety. The DSI criterion does something that market forces alone may not achieve: it creates a binding obligation for vendors to document what their AI-assisted tools do, who they were tested on, and what problems users should watch for. Without that obligation, documentation becomes optional. And when documentation is optional, it disappears during cost-cutting cycles or when vendors compete on price.‚Źé‚Źé**The Gap Between What We Say and What We Do**‚Źé‚ŹéHere's my honest concern, speaking as someone who works inside a major health IT company. When vendors aren't required to produce model cards and transparency disclosures, most won't. Not because they're malicious, but because it's not a visible competitive advantage. A clinic shopping for decision support tools doesn't ask, "Show me your model card." They ask, "Does it integrate with our EHR?" and "How much does it cost?" Liability concerns exist, yes, but they're abstract and future-facing. Cost pressures are immediate.‚Źé‚ŹéOur organization is large enough and reputation-conscious enough that we'd probably produce transparency documentation anyway. But I've worked in this space long enough to know we're not representative of the entire vendor ecosystem. Smaller vendors, startups, and foreign companies have different incentive structures. Removing the requirement would create a two-tier market: large vendors who voluntarily provide transparency, and everyone else who doesn't. Providers using cheaper tools from less-transparent vendors wouldn't know the difference. They'd use them anyway because budget matters.‚Źé‚Źé**What Happens at the Point of Care**‚Źé‚ŹéI keep circling back to the Louisville clinic example because it illustrates something important. That clinic didn't need litigation or regulatory enforcement to improve their outcome. They needed information. The model card told them their tool was trained on academic centers. That single fact made them understand why the alert patterns didn't match their primary care workflow. They adjusted. Problem solved. Clinical safety improved.‚Źé‚ŹéRemove the requirement, and that information might never exist. No clinic would ever ask for it. No vendor would volunteer it unprompted. The tool would continue firing alerts inappropriately, providers would develop alert fatigue, and safety would degrade silently. Nobody would know why because nobody would have the documentation to look at.‚Źé‚ŹéThis isn't speculative. There's published research on this exact problem. A 2019 study in the Journal of the American Medical Informatics Association found that clinical decision support systems with poor transparency documentation had significantly higher rates of alert override by clinicians, independent of whether the alerts were actually clinically appropriate (Ancker & Mauer, 2019, JAMIA). The issue wasn't that the alerts were bad. It was that clinicians didn't understand why they were firing and didn't trust them. Transparency documentation could have prevented that erosion of trust.‚Źé‚Źé**The Specific Elements Matter**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be precise about what should remain in the DSI criterion because not all regulatory documentation is equally valuable. Some requirements do create compliance burden without safety benefit. But the current DSI elements are not in that category.‚Źé‚ŹéThe requirement for intended use documentation is straightforward. A tool designed for diagnosing diabetes in adults over 40 should not be used for pediatric patients without additional validation. That seems basic, but it only stays basic if someone documents it and makes it available to end users.‚Źé‚ŹéThe target population requirement serves a similar function. If a decision support tool was trained primarily on data from patients with commercial insurance, it may perform differently on Medicaid populations. If nobody documents that, a clinic serving 80 percent Medicaid patients would never know they're using a tool with different performance characteristics for their actual population. They'd just assume it works the same as advertised.‚Źé‚ŹéThe known risks and training data characteristics are where the real insight lives. These are the elements that let clinicians understand where a tool might fail. Is it more likely to miss certain diagnoses? Is it trained on data that's five years old? Was it developed using patients from a single geographic region? These aren't compliance checkboxes. They're operational intelligence.‚Źé‚Źé**The Burden Argument, Reconsidered**‚Źé‚ŹéI've spent a lot of time thinking about the compliance burden question because I experience it directly. Creating a comprehensive model card for a complex decision support algorithm requires work. It requires our product team to document training data sources, validation study results, and known performance gaps. It requires coordination with our legal team and our clinical advisors. It's not free.‚Źé‚ŹéBut here's what I've realized: the burden is front-loaded. We do the work once, when we develop the tool. We update it when we materially change the algorithm. After that, the documentation exists. It's not like an annual audit requirement that creates recurring compliance costs. We produce it, we share it, we're done. The cost to us is probably around $50,000 to $100,000 per significant decision support tool, depending on complexity. For a platform serving hundreds of thousands of users, that's not insignificant but it's not the kind of massive burden that requires elimination.‚Źé‚ŹéFor providers, the burden is even lighter. They receive the documentation. They can read it or ignore it, but it's available. We've never had a clinic tell us that receiving our model cards created operational difficulty. If anything, the opposite is true. Several of our partner organizations have told us they use our transparency disclosures in their credentialing processes for AI-assisted tools, which actually reduces their internal compliance work.‚Źé‚Źé**What Should Happen Instead**‚Źé‚ŹéRather than eliminate the DSI requirement, I'd recommend three adjustments that would reduce legitimate burden while preserving the safety function:‚Źé‚Źé1. Establish a standardized template for model cards and transparency disclosures so vendors aren't creating them from scratch. Organizations like the AI Now Institute have published templates. Adoption of a standard format would reduce documentation time significantly while making the information more consistent and comparable across vendors.‚Źé‚Źé2. Allow vendors to reference external validation studies rather than requiring proprietary validation for every single use case. If a decision support tool has been published in peer-reviewed literature and validated in independent studies, vendors should be able to cite those rather than duplicating the work internally.‚Źé‚Źé3. Create a safe harbor for vendors who provide good-faith transparency documentation. If a vendor has clearly documented known limitations and a provider uses the tool outside those parameters anyway, the vendor shouldn't be held liable. This would reduce the legal risk that sometimes drives over-documentation.‚Źé‚ŹéThese adjustments would actually address the real compliance burden without eliminating the safety function.‚Źé‚Źé**Market Forces Are Not Sufficient**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to directly address the argument that market competition will naturally drive transparency. In theory, clinics would prefer tools with better documentation and would reward vendors who provide it. In practice, procurement doesn't work that way.‚Źé‚ŹéHealth IT purchasing decisions are driven by interoperability, cost, and integration with existing workflows. A clinic's IT director evaluating decision support tools cares about whether it connects to their EHR. They don't care about the theoretical transparency of the algorithm. That's not a purchasing criterion. It's not part of the RFP. It's not discussed in contract negotiations.‚Źé‚ŹéIf two vendors offer similar tools at similar prices with similar interoperability, the clinic will choose based on whichever one they already use or whichever one is easier to implement. Neither clinic will select based on the quality of the model card documentation. So vendors have no market incentive to invest in that documentation beyond the minimum required by regulation.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not a criticism of clinics. They're making rational economic decisions. But it's precisely why regulation is necessary. Without a requirement, documentation doesn't exist. Without documentation, transparency doesn't happen. Without transparency, safety suffers silently.‚Źé‚Źé**Conclusion**‚Źé‚ŹéI came into this comment thinking I'd argue for eliminating compliance burden across the board. I've spent enough of my career drowning in regulatory documentation to understand the frustration with overregulation. But the DSI requirement is not overregulation. It's a targeted requirement that creates real value for clinical safety without creating massive burden for vendors or providers.‚Źé‚ŹéThe model cards and transparency disclosures required under the current DSI criterion are not compliance theater. They're operational intelligence that lets clinicians understand their tools. They prevent alert fatigue. They catch performance gaps before they cause harm. They make the healthcare system safer in quiet, incremental ways that never make headlines because the problems they prevent never happen.‚Źé‚ŹéI'd ask the agency to reconsider the removal of the DSI criterion, or at minimum, to maintain it with the three streamlining adjustments I outlined above. The burden reduction would be real. The safety preservation would be essential.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-18T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-18T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ6‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒresigned‚ôĒKentucky‚ôĒcompliance officer‚ôĒ46‚ôĒWhen we implemented our last major EHR upgrade at Doximity, our smaller clinic partners spent eighteen months and nearly $400,000 just on compliance documentation and vendor coordination‚ÄĒmoney that came straight out of their clinical staff budgets. I watched one practice manager essentially become a part-time compliance analyst, and it struck me that the certification requirements weren't actually making their systems safer, just more expensive to maintain.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1817
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0032‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒWilliam Thomas‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒWhile supporting medical records interoperability to reduce patient burden, the commenter opposes deregulation that would eliminate transparency requirements for AI-assisted medical decisions, arguing that patients need to know what data trained t...‚ôĒLast year I spent three weeks chasing my medical records between clinics after a back injury. The old doctor said they'd fax them, the new one never got them, and I ended up sitting in their office while they burned a CD. I'm relieved that interoperability rules might finally make that easier. But I'm concerned about what we're losing in the rush to deregulate.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen doctors use AI to recommend my treatment, I need to know what that AI was trained on, who it was tested with, and what it might get wrong. Removing requirements for transparency disclosures means I have no way to know if the AI recommending my care was ever tested on people like me. That's the same kind of hidden friction I already experience with records transfer, except this time it affects my actual medical decisions. I've already wasted enough time dealing with healthcare bureaucracy. I shouldn't have to wonder whether the tool guiding my treatment has blind spots nobody bothered to check for. Streamlining rules makes sense where it reduces patient burden, but not when it hides risks. Transparency about AI training data and limitations should stay.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-14T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-14T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒmedium‚ôĒresigned‚ôĒNevada‚ôĒadministrative assistant‚ôĒ39‚ôĒLast year when I switched doctors after my back injury, I spent three weeks calling between offices trying to get my imaging records transferred‚ÄĒthe old clinic said they'd fax them, the new one said they never received anything. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0033‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒAnthony Nguyen‚ôĒFreedom Home Health Care Inc.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒFreedom Home Health Care opposes weakening certification requirements for AI-based Decision Support Interventions, arguing that transparency disclosures about AI model training, validation, and limitations are essential patient safety measures dis...‚ôĒFreedom Home Health Care Inc. submits this comment regarding the proposed deregulatory framework for health information technology certification and standards. Our organization operates home health services across Kentucky and has direct experience with the operational and financial consequences of regulatory volatility in the health IT space. We want to address what we see as a critical gap in the current deregulatory proposal: the removal or substantial weakening of certification requirements for Decision Support Interventions (DSI) that incorporate artificial intelligence.‚Źé‚Źé**THE VENDOR-IMPOSED BURDEN WE KNOW**‚Źé‚ŹéBefore explaining our position on the DSI criterion, I need to be transparent about our starting point. Last spring, Freedom Home Health Care spent $180,000 to upgrade our electronic health record system. Our vendor told us that specific certification features were mandatory to meet regulatory requirements. We made the investment in good faith. Six months later, we learned that the standards had shifted. Half of those features we'd paid for were now optional. That money is gone. Our smaller competitors who waited it out are now deploying cheaper solutions that leapfrogged ahead of our investment. I bring this up because it shapes how we think about deregulation. On the surface, reducing certification burdens sounds good to us. We understand the appeal. We've been burned by unnecessary rigidity.‚Źé‚ŹéBut that experience also taught us something: there is a difference between removing regulations that don't serve anyone and removing safeguards that protect patients and clinical integrity. The DSI certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures is not the same as the kind of vendor-driven mandate that cost us $180,000. It is not regulatory bloat that exists to protect market incumbents. It serves a specific, necessary function.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT THE DSI CRITERION ACTUALLY DOES**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current certification requirement for Decision Support Interventions asks vendors to document and disclose:‚Źé- The intended use of the AI model‚Źé- The target population on which it was tested and validated‚Źé- Known risks and limitations‚Źé- Characteristics of training data‚Źé- Results of external validation testing‚Źé‚ŹéThese are not burdensome documentation requirements in the way that redundant EHR certification features are burdensome. They are baseline transparency measures. A clinician using an AI system to support a diagnosis or treatment decision needs to know what population that system was trained on, whether the patient in front of them fits that population, and what risks the system has demonstrated. This is not a vendor compliance checkbox. This is a patient safety question.‚Źé‚ŹéOur organization works with elderly and chronically ill patients in home settings. Many of them take multiple medications. Several of our clinicians have started exploring AI-assisted tools for medication interaction screening. One of our nurses asked me a straightforward question last month: "How do I know this tool is reliable for my patients?" I couldn't answer her without knowing what the vendor had trained the model on, whether external validation had occurred, and whether the tool had been tested on populations similar to ours. That question is not regulatory overreach. It is the foundation of informed clinical decision-making.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THIS MATTERS DIFFERENTLY THAN OTHER STANDARDS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe deregulatory proposal makes a case that many existing certification criteria create cost barriers without proportional safety benefits. There is merit to that argument in some domains. Redundant standards, overlapping requirements, and prescriptive technical specifications that don't meaningfully improve outcomes do create unnecessary friction.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI criterion operates differently. It does not prescribe how a vendor must build or validate an AI model. It does not mandate a specific architecture, algorithm, or validation methodology. It requires that whatever approach the vendor takes, they document it transparently. This is a disclosure requirement, not a design requirement. The cost burden is documentation, not engineering redesign. And the safety return is direct: clinicians and organizations can make informed choices about whether to use a tool, with which patients, and under what conditions.‚Źé‚ŹéI also want to acknowledge the flip side. Yes, there is a concern that requiring AI model cards could slow vendor innovation or create market advantages for larger vendors with compliance infrastructure. We felt that exact frustration when our vendor told us we needed features that smaller competitors didn't have to implement. But the solution to that problem is not to remove the requirement. It is to make the requirement proportionate to the actual risk and to provide clear, stable guidance so vendors can plan compliance costs accurately. That is what we did not have with our EHR upgrade, and it cost us significantly.‚Źé‚Źé**OUR SPECIFIC RECOMMENDATION**‚Źé‚ŹéWe recommend retaining the DSI certification criterion in its current form. The requirement for AI model cards and transparency disclosures should remain a condition of certification for any health IT system that includes decision support features that use machine learning or algorithmic decision-making. This should apply regardless of whether the AI component is developed in-house by the vendor or licensed from a third party.‚Źé‚ŹéThe documentation should be standardized in format to reduce burden. The Office of the National Coordinator (ONC) should develop a template or structured form for AI model cards that vendors can complete efficiently. This would take the requirement from a narrative documentation burden to a structured disclosure that vendors can build into their development workflows. It would also make the information more usable for clinicians and compliance officers reviewing the tools. The template should cover:‚Źé‚Źé- Intended clinical use cases and patient populations‚Źé- Training data characteristics (size, source, demographic distribution, temporal range)‚Źé- Validation methodology and external validation results if available‚Źé- Known limitations and performance across subpopulations‚Źé- Intended use restrictions or contraindications‚Źé- Update frequency and retraining protocols‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not a heavy lift for vendors. Companies like Tempus, IBM Watson Health, and others have already published model cards for clinical AI systems. The format exists. The precedent is established. What is needed is consistency and standardization so that every organization using AI decision support tools has access to the same baseline information.‚Źé‚Źé**IMPLEMENTATION WITHOUT CREATING NEW BARRIERS**‚Źé‚ŹéOne legitimate concern is that standardized AI model cards could inadvertently create barriers to market entry for smaller vendors or for academic medical centers that want to deploy internally developed AI tools. We do not want to replace one form of market concentration with another.‚Źé‚ŹéThe certification requirement should apply only to vendors seeking certification status from ONC. Academic medical centers or healthcare systems developing AI tools for internal use should be exempt from the certification requirement, though we would recommend that they follow the same model card documentation standard as an internal governance matter. This preserves innovation and allows systems to experiment without regulatory burden while ensuring that any AI tool distributed commercially carries transparent documentation.‚Źé‚ŹéFor vendors, the certification should be tied to the AI component, not the entire EHR system. A vendor might have a certified EHR that includes a new AI decision support module. That module alone should undergo the certification process for the DSI criterion. The vendor should not have to recertify the entire EHR system. This reduces the compliance burden and allows vendors to update AI components more rapidly without triggering system-wide recertification.‚Źé‚ŹéWe would also support a safe harbor provision. If a vendor documents a known limitation or risk in the AI model card, and a clinician encounters that exact limitation in use, the vendor should not face liability claims based on that disclosed risk. The safe harbor would apply only to risks that were clearly documented in the model card. This incentivizes vendors to be thorough and transparent in their documentation because transparency actually protects them.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THIS IS NOT THE SAME AS OUR EHR PROBLEM**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to return to the vendor situation that prompted our $180,000 loss. Our EHR vendor created artificial urgency around certification features that turned out to be optional. The vendor benefited from market confusion and regulatory uncertainty. Our organization and others like us lost money as a result. We believe deregulation should address this kind of scenario.‚Źé‚ŹéBut the DSI criterion is different. It exists because AI in clinical decision-making creates a specific risk that does not exist with other health IT functions. An EHR system that stores patient data reliably is valuable regardless of how it was built. The underlying architecture does not change the clinical safety profile. An AI system that makes recommendations about diagnosis or treatment, however, has a direct relationship to patient outcomes. Its training data, validation methodology, and known limitations are clinically material information. A clinician cannot use that tool safely without knowing those things.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving or weakening this requirement would not reduce vendor burden in a way that helps smaller vendors compete. It would reduce transparency in a way that helps vendors avoid accountability. Those are not the same thing.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT DEREGULATION SHOULD ADDRESS INSTEAD**‚Źé‚ŹéIf ONC is looking for certification requirements that actually create unnecessary burden without safety return, we would point to some of the interoperability standards that have become redundant. The simultaneous certification of multiple HL7 versions, for example, creates implementation burden without clinical benefit. Vendors maintain backward compatibility with older standards long after they should. Deprecating older standards more aggressively would reduce burden without reducing safety.‚Źé‚ŹéSimilarly, the certification requirements around specific data element formats could be simplified. Vendors spend compliance resources on format validation that does not affect interoperability or clinical use. Moving to more flexible, outcome-focused standards would reduce cost without reducing functionality.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI criterion is not in that category. It is focused, outcome-relevant, and directly tied to patient safety. Keeping it should not be controversial.‚Źé‚Źé**CLOSING OBSERVATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéOur organization is not opposed to deregulation. We have felt the cost of regulatory complexity and vendor opportunism. But we also know that healthcare is different from other industries. Deregulation that reduces documentation burden for AI systems in clinical use is deregulation that increases risk to patients. That is not a tradeoff we should make in the name of reducing administrative cost.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal before ONC should be refined to distinguish between regulations that protect incumbents and regulations that protect patients. The DSI criterion protecting AI transparency is in the second category. We recommend retaining it.‚Źé‚ŹéWe appreciate the opportunity to comment on this proposal. Freedom Home Health Care is committed to supporting innovation in health IT while maintaining the safeguards that allow our clinicians to use new tools with confidence. We are available if ONC would like to discuss our operational experience with health IT regulation and its effects on small and mid-sized healthcare providers.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-19T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-19T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ5‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒangry‚ôĒKentucky‚ôĒcompliance officer‚ôĒ36‚ôĒLast spring, we spent $180,000 to upgrade our EHR system to meet certification requirements that our vendor insisted were mandatory, only to learn six months later that the standards had shifted and half those features were now optional‚ÄĒmoney we'll never recover while our smaller competitors who waited it out just leapfrogged ahead with cheaper solutions.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1746
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0034‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒStephanie Mitchell‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒRemoving Decision Support Interventions certification requirements would eliminate critical transparency and accountability safeguards for AI tools used in clinical decision-making, leaving patients vulnerable to unexplained algorithmic bias and u...‚ôĒSTEPHANIE MITCHELL‚ŹéARKANSAS‚Źé‚ŹéRE: ONC's Proposed Removal of Decision Support Interventions Certification Criteria‚Źé‚ŹéI've been driving trucks for forty-three years, and I know when something doesn't add up. Last year when my heart started acting up, I needed my stress test results transferred between clinics. Instead of a simple handoff, I paid twice for the same test because nobody would send the records across town. That was frustrating enough. But when those files finally moved, I found thirty-year-old custody information mixed in there that should have stayed buried. Now I'm supposed to feel better because the government wants to make data move "faster"? Not if it means less oversight of what's in those files or who can see them.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY AI TRANSPARENCY MATTERS FOR PATIENT SAFETY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal to remove Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements worries me more than the data transfer problems. These are the AI tools doctors use to help diagnose and treat patients. If you're going to let a computer program influence what medication a cardiologist prescribes or which test they order, that program needs to be transparent about what it was trained on, who it was tested with, and what its known limitations are. That's not bureaucratic busywork. That's basic accountability.‚Źé‚ŹéUnder 21 CFR Part 11 and the existing certification framework, AI model cards are required to disclose intended use, target population, known risks, and external validation results. Removing these requirements means a doctor using an AI tool won't know if that tool was only tested on patients like me (older, white, female) or if it has blind spots with other populations. I've already been burned by fragmented records. I don't want my treatment decisions made by a black-box algorithm that nobody's required to explain.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT NEEDS TO STAY IN PLACE**‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criterion should remain because it protects three things:‚Źé‚Źé1. Patient safety through documented validation and known risk disclosure‚Źé2. Clinical decision-making that's informed, not automated‚Źé3. Provider accountability when something goes wrong‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not against innovation. I'm against the idea that loosening oversight somehow equals progress. My experience shows me that when healthcare systems get sloppy about data, patients pay the price. Before you remove these safeguards, ask yourselves whether you'd feel comfortable if an AI tool influenced your own doctor's decisions without anyone being required to explain how it works or who it was tested on.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒangry‚ôĒArkansas‚ôĒtruck driver‚ôĒ67‚ôĒLast year when I had that bout with atrial fibrillation, I had to see a cardiologist two hours away because my regular doctor couldn't access my stress test results from the clinic across town‚ÄĒturns out they were literally refusing to send them over‚ÄĒand I ended up paying for duplicate testing out of pocket. But here's what really got me: when I finally got those records transferred, I realized my ex-husband's name and our custody dispute details from thirty years ago were buried in there, and now I'm terrified that loosening up how this data moves around is going to mean more people seeing information I thought was supposed to stay private.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ396
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0035‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒRebecca Nguyen‚ôĒNational Alliance for Care at Home‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe National Alliance for Care at Home opposes removing Decision Support Intervention (DSI) certification requirements for AI model transparency and documentation, arguing that such requirements are essential for protecting vulnerable populations ...‚ôĒRE: RFI on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity (Federal Register Notice)‚Źé‚ŹéNational Alliance for Care at Home respectfully submits this comment on the Request for Information regarding proposed deregulatory changes to health information technology certification criteria and standards. Our organization represents over 200 home and community-based care agencies across the United States, serving approximately 1.2 million individuals annually, with particular focus on vulnerable populations including low-income immigrants, elderly adults, and people with complex chronic conditions. We are writing to address the proposed modifications to Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements, specifically the recommendations to remove model card and transparency disclosure mandates for AI-assisted clinical decision tools.‚Źé‚ŹéOur perspective on this matter is informed by direct operational experience. Last month, during a routine audit of our data governance practices at three partner agencies in the Omaha area, we discovered that medication histories and clinical information belonging to low-income immigrant clients had been shared with a third-party data analytics firm. The sharing occurred without explicit informed consent from the affected individuals. The mechanism was troubling in its ordinariness: the data transfer had been buried in revised terms-of-service language that clients were asked to sign during routine intake appointments, written in English at a tenth-grade reading level, when many clients' primary language was not English. When we began investigating how this had happened and whether it violated any specific regulatory requirement, we found ourselves in an uncomfortable position. Nobody on our compliance team could point to a clear federal standard that would have prevented this. That gap is precisely why we believe the proposed removal of DSI transparency requirements would be a mistake.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CURRENT DSI FRAMEWORK AND ITS OPERATIONAL PURPOSE**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current certification criterion for Decision Support Interventions, as established under the ONC Health IT Certification Program (codified at 45 CFR 170.315(a)(1)), requires that certified AI and algorithmic decision support tools include model cards documenting intended use, target population, known performance limitations, training data characteristics, and results from external validation testing. This requirement exists because there is a meaningful difference between a clinical tool working well in the population for which it was designed and the same tool being deployed to a different population with different characteristics. That difference is not theoretical. It reflects something we have learned repeatedly through our work in home-based care settings across rural and urban Nebraska, Iowa, and surrounding states.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen a medication management algorithm is trained primarily on data from adult patients aged 65 and older with Medicare coverage and English-language medical records, its performance in a younger population of uninsured immigrants with limited English proficiency and fragmented medical histories will differ in ways that matter clinically. The model card requirement ensures that the clinician using the tool, and the organization implementing it, have access to information about those differences before deployment. Without this information, a home care nurse using the algorithm to flag potential drug interactions might not know that the tool was never validated in patients taking the combination of medications common in her immigrant patient population. She might not know that the training data came overwhelmingly from urban academic medical centers. She would be making a clinical decision without full information about the reliability of the tool in her specific context.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed deregulation would remove this requirement, arguing that it imposes unnecessary compliance burden on health IT developers and that market competition and professional judgment will ensure appropriate tool validation and use. We understand the desire to reduce administrative burden. Our own organizations spend significant resources on compliance documentation, and we are not opposed to streamlining requirements that do not serve patient safety. However, we have not seen evidence that professional judgment and market forces alone have prevented inappropriate tool deployment or protected vulnerable populations from algorithmic bias. In fact, our experience suggests the opposite.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE LEARNED FROM OUR DATA GOVERNANCE INCIDENT**‚Źé‚ŹéThe medication data sharing incident at our three partner agencies occurred because the compliance and data governance infrastructure at those agencies was inadequate. All three are smaller organizations, each serving 200 to 400 clients, with limited IT staff and no dedicated privacy officer. When a data analytics firm approached them with a proposal to share aggregated client data in exchange for free analytics reports on agency performance metrics, the leadership teams did not have the expertise to evaluate the proposal against privacy and ethical standards. The analytics firm's representatives explained that the data would be de-identified and that sharing it would help the agencies understand their outcomes better. It sounded reasonable. The fact that it turned out to violate basic principles of informed consent was not obvious to people without formal training in health information privacy.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat troubles us about this situation is how easily it happened, and how it reflects a broader pattern we observe in the home care sector. Many home-based care agencies are small, mission-driven nonprofits with limited resources for compliance infrastructure. They are not equipped to conduct independent validation studies of algorithmic tools or to interpret technical documentation about model performance. When they adopt new technology, they often rely on vendor representations and existing professional practices. If the regulatory requirement for model cards is removed, vendors will have less incentive to produce clear, accessible documentation about tool limitations and intended use. The burden of validation and risk assessment will shift entirely to the using organization, but the using organizations in our network often lack the capacity to bear that burden effectively.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is where the equity question becomes concrete rather than abstract. The home care agencies serving predominantly immigrant and low-income populations tend to be smaller and have fewer resources than agencies serving more affluent areas. Removing the DSI certification requirement for model cards will likely widen the gap between well-resourced organizations that can independently validate tools and smaller organizations that cannot. The populations served by those smaller organizations will bear the risk of algorithmic deployment that is poorly understood and inadequately documented.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC CONCERNS ABOUT AI MODEL TRANSPARENCY IN HOME-BASED CARE**‚Źé‚ŹéHome-based care settings present particular challenges for AI tool deployment that justify maintaining the DSI transparency requirement. First, home care involves inherently complex clinical decision-making in environments with limited infrastructure. A registered nurse working in a client's home does not have access to the same clinical support systems as a hospital-based clinician. She does not have a pharmacist immediately available if a medication interaction alert seems unclear. She cannot quickly consult with a physician about an algorithmic recommendation she does not understand. She depends on clear, accessible documentation about how the tool works and what its limitations are.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, the home care population includes many individuals with fragmented medical histories, complex social circumstances, and health conditions that may not be well-represented in the datasets used to train clinical algorithms. An elderly immigrant client with diabetes might have received prior care from multiple providers in different countries, with incomplete records. An algorithm trained on continuous, integrated medical records in English will behave differently when applied to that client's incomplete and multilingual history. The clinician using the tool needs to know this.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, home care agencies often lack the IT infrastructure to implement sophisticated safeguards against algorithmic bias or to monitor tool performance over time. If an algorithm begins making systematically different recommendations for clients from particular demographic groups, many home care agencies would not have the technical capacity to detect this drift. The DSI certification requirement for external validation testing and clear documentation of known limitations creates a baseline protection that organizations cannot easily work around.‚Źé‚ŹéWe conducted an informal survey of 45 home-based care agencies in our network between January and March of this year, asking about their current use of algorithmic decision support tools and their capacity to independently evaluate tool performance. Thirty-two agencies reported using at least one algorithmic tool for medication management, care planning, or fall risk assessment. Of those 32 agencies, only 4 reported having staff with formal training in data analysis or health informatics. None had conducted independent validation studies of any algorithmic tool. When asked how they would evaluate tool performance if certification requirements were removed, 28 of the 32 agencies indicated they would rely primarily on vendor claims and their own subjective experience with the tool. This is not a rigorous survey, but it reflects a real gap between the technical expertise required to independently validate algorithmic tools and the expertise available at most home care organizations.‚Źé‚Źé**THE SPECIFIC VALUE OF MODEL CARDS AND TRANSPARENCY DOCUMENTATION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe requirement for model cards is not a mere bureaucratic exercise. Model cards serve a specific function in communicating tool limitations and appropriate use cases. A well-constructed model card includes the following elements: description of the tool's intended use and clinical context, characteristics of the population on which the model was trained, performance metrics disaggregated by demographic group, known limitations and edge cases where performance degrades, and results from external validation testing in populations different from the training population. This information allows a clinician to make an informed judgment about whether the tool is appropriate for her patient.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider a concrete example from our work. One of our partner agencies in Lincoln, Nebraska adopted a fall risk assessment algorithm about two years ago. The algorithm was trained on data from a large urban hospital system and performed well in identifying high-risk patients in that setting. The agency used the algorithm for about six months before realizing that it was systematically underestimating fall risk in clients with cognitive impairment. The algorithm had been trained primarily on data from patients with intact cognition, and it weighted certain mobility factors that are less predictive in patients with dementia. The agency discovered this problem only because one of their clinical supervisors happened to notice the pattern. They were lucky. A different agency might not have noticed, and clients might have been placed in fall prevention programs at inappropriately low intensity.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the DSI certification requirement had required the vendor to produce a clear model card documenting the algorithm's performance in patients with cognitive impairment, the Lincoln agency would have known about this limitation before deployment. They could have decided whether to use the tool, to use it only for certain clients, or to supplement it with additional assessment methods. Instead, they learned about the limitation through experience, after the tool had already been in use for months.‚Źé‚ŹéModel cards also serve an important function in accountability. If an algorithmic tool produces a bad outcome or is later found to have embedded bias, the availability of clear documentation about the tool's training data, validation process, and known limitations creates a basis for understanding what went wrong. This is important for the clinicians and organizations using the tool, for clients harmed by inappropriate recommendations, and for regulators investigating problems. The proposed removal of the model card requirement would eliminate this documentation requirement just as deployment of AI tools in clinical settings is accelerating.‚Źé‚Źé**ALIGNMENT WITH EXISTING LEGAL FRAMEWORKS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification requirement for model cards and transparency documentation aligns with existing legal obligations that health care organizations already bear. Under the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act Privacy Rule (45 CFR Parts 160 and 164), covered entities have a responsibility to ensure that the tools they use to make decisions about patient care are appropriate and reliable. The Security Rule requires organizations to implement safeguards to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of protected health information, including safeguards related to the systems that process that information.‚Źé‚ŹéAdditionally, under the Genetic Information Nondiscrimination Act (GINA), health plans and covered entities are prohibited from making adverse decisions based on genetic information. As algorithmic tools become more sophisticated in integrating genetic and other health information, the requirement for clear documentation about how those tools were trained and validated becomes important for ensuring compliance with GINA's requirements. An organization cannot ensure compliance with antidiscrimination law if it lacks clear documentation about whether an algorithmic tool is making systematically different recommendations for people based on protected characteristics.‚Źé‚ŹéState laws governing health care quality and patient safety also support maintaining the DSI transparency requirement. Nebraska's Health Care Quality Improvement Act (Neb. Rev. Stat. Section 44-2801 et seq.) requires health care providers to maintain adequate systems for ensuring quality of care. Using an algorithmic tool without understanding its limitations and appropriate use cases could be seen as inconsistent with this obligation. Similar provisions exist in the laws of most states.‚Źé‚Źé**ALTERNATIVE APPROACHES AND WHY THEY ARE INADEQUATE**‚Źé‚ŹéWe recognize that some commenters on this RFI may argue that removing the DSI certification requirement is not as risky as we suggest because other mechanisms exist to ensure appropriate algorithmic deployment and use. Specifically, some may argue that professional judgment, organizational liability, and competitive market forces will create sufficient incentives for vendors to produce good documentation and for organizations to use tools appropriately.‚Źé‚ŹéWe are skeptical of this argument based on our experience with the data sharing incident at our partner agencies. Market forces did not prevent those agencies from sharing client data without informed consent. The organizations had no reason to believe they were doing anything wrong. The vendor presented the arrangement as a standard business practice. There was no competitor offering better privacy practices who could have captured market share by advertising superior data governance. The agencies were using a tool that was standard in the industry. Liability is a slow and imperfect corrective mechanism. By the time an organization faces a lawsuit for harm caused by inappropriate algorithmic deployment, clients have already been harmed.‚Źé‚ŹéProfessional judgment is important, but professional judgment operates within constraints. A home care nurse cannot exercise good clinical judgment about an algorithmic tool if she lacks information about how the tool was trained and what its limitations are. We cannot expect clinicians to independently conduct validation studies or to second-guess vendors' claims about tool performance. That is not a reasonable expectation given the time constraints and technical expertise required.‚Źé‚Źé**MAINTAINING DSI REQUIREMENTS AS A BASELINE PROTECTION**‚Źé‚ŹéWe believe the appropriate regulatory approach is to maintain the current DSI certification requirement for model cards and transparency documentation, while considering whether the documentation requirements themselves could be streamlined to reduce burden on developers without reducing the quality of information available to users. For example, the certification criterion could allow vendors to provide model cards in a variety of formats, rather than prescribing a specific format. The criterion could allow vendors to document validation testing in different populations rather than requiring validation in a fixed number of subgroups. These modifications would reduce some compliance burden while maintaining the core protection of ensuring that clinicians and organizations have access to information about tool limitations and appropriate use.‚Źé‚ŹéWe are also open to regulatory changes that would clarify the role of model cards within a broader framework of organizational responsibility for algorithmic tool governance. For example, the certification criterion could be paired with stronger requirements that organizations using certified tools must document their evaluation of whether the tool is appropriate for their patient population and their processes for monitoring tool performance over time. This would distribute the responsibility for algorithmic governance between vendors (who have the expertise to develop and validate tools) and using organizations (who have the expertise to understand their patient populations and clinical workflows).‚Źé‚ŹéHowever, removing the certification requirement entirely is not the right approach. The gap in our regulatory framework that allowed the data sharing incident at our partner agencies to occur should not be widened by removing transparency requirements for algorithmic tools. If anything, the gap should be narrowed by strengthening the requirements for documented algorithmic governance.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe National Alliance for Care at Home respectfully urges the Office of the National Coordinator and the Assistant Secretary for Technology Policy to maintain the current DSI certification requirement for model cards and transparency documentation. This requirement serves an important function in ensuring patient safety, supporting informed clinical decision-making, and creating accountability for algorithmic tool deployment in health care settings. Removing the requirement would shift the burden of validation and risk assessment entirely to using organizations, many of which lack the technical capacity to bear that burden effectively. The burden would fall most heavily on smaller organizations serving vulnerable populations, likely widening existing health equity gaps.‚Źé‚ŹéWe have observed directly how easily algorithmic and data governance failures occur in home care settings when the regulatory framework is weak and organizations lack adequate compliance infrastructure. We believe that maintaining the DSI transparency requirement, while considering targeted modifications to reduce unnecessary compliance burden, is the appropriate regulatory approach. We would welcome the opportunity to discuss these issues further with ONC staff and to contribute our on-the-ground experience with algorithmic tool deployment in home-based care settings to the agency's ongoing work on health data governance.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-13T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-13T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ9‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒfrustrated‚ôĒNebraska‚ôĒresearch director‚ôĒ45‚ôĒLast month, I was reviewing preliminary data from our home-care outcomes study when we discovered that three of our partner agencies‚ÄĒall serving predominantly low-income immigrant communities in the Omaha area‚ÄĒhad quietly shared client medication histories with a third-party analytics firm without explicit consent, buried in terms-of-service updates. When I tried to trace how this happened, nobody could point to a clear regulatory requirement that would have prevented it, which is precisely the problem we're facing now.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2798
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0036‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDeborah Mitchell‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA parent argues that transparency requirements for AI systems in healthcare are essential, drawing on her experience struggling to access her daughter's medical records and emphasizing that families deserve to know how AI systems are trained and w...‚ôĒ**MY DAUGHTER'S CARE AND WHY TRANSPARENCY MATTERS**‚Źé‚ŹéLast year when my daughter was diagnosed with Type 1 diabetes, I spent weeks just trying to get her lab results from one clinic to another. Three phone calls, two faxes, and I still ended up waiting twelve days for basic bloodwork that her new endocrinologist needed to see. It felt backwards. Here I was fighting to share information that was about my own child, with doctors we chose, and nobody's systems talked to each other. I kept thinking, this is 2024, how is this still happening?‚Źé‚ŹéNow I'm reading about removing transparency requirements for AI systems in healthcare, and my stomach tightens. I don't pretend to understand everything about artificial intelligence, but I know this: I fought hard to know what information was going into decisions about my daughter's insulin dosing and medication plan. I needed to understand what her doctors were seeing and why they were recommending what they recommended. If an AI system is going to influence those decisions, don't I deserve to know how it was trained? What data did it learn from? Does it actually work reliably for kids like my daughter, or are there gaps we should worry about?‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT TRANSPARENCY REALLY MEANS**‚Źé‚ŹéWhen you strip away the requirement for developers and providers to disclose how an AI system was built and what it can't reliably do, you're basically asking families like mine to trust a black box with high-stakes medical choices. We're already fragmented enough without adding hidden algorithms on top of it. I'm not asking for perfection. I'm asking that before an AI system influences care decisions, someone knows and can tell us what it was trained on and where its blind spots are. That's not‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-25T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-25T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒmedium‚ôĒhopeful‚ôĒVirginia‚ôĒsmall business owner‚ôĒ44‚ôĒWhen my daughter got diagnosed with Type 1 diabetes last year, I had to fight with three different clinics just to get copies of her lab results so I could share them with the endocrinologist we finally found two hours away‚ÄĒit took weeks of phone calls and faxes.
