CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0037‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒAndrew Nguyen‚ôĒDental Standards Institute‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Dental Standards Institute opposes eliminating model card and transparency disclosure requirements for AI-assisted clinical decision support tools in dentistry, arguing that such documentation is essential for patient safety, clinical accounta...‚ôĒ**DENTAL STANDARDS INSTITUTE**‚Źé‚ŹéRe: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity (RIN 0945-AA17)‚Źé‚ŹéThe Dental Standards Institute submits this comment in response to the proposed deregulatory framework affecting Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements. Our organization represents dental practices, patient advocates, and public health professionals across Delaware and surrounding regions who have direct experience with the intersection of clinical decision-making, patient safety, and health information access. We write with specific concern about the proposal to eliminate model card and transparency disclosure requirements for AI-assisted clinical tools in dentistry.‚Źé‚Źé**THE REAL PROBLEM WE'RE SEEING**‚Źé‚ŹéLast year, our organization assisted an elderly Vietnamese immigrant through a billing dispute with her dental provider. She had been charged three times for the same extraction. When we asked the dental office to transfer her records to a second-opinion dentist, we encountered what they called HIPAA concerns but what functioned as indefinite delay. The records never arrived. She never got the second opinion. What bothered me most was that there was no mechanism to hold anyone accountable for that stonewalling, and no transparency about why the delay occurred.‚Źé‚ŹéThat experience sits behind my concern about the current proposal. When we reduce oversight of AI systems in clinical settings, we're not just reducing paperwork. We're removing the tools that patients and advocates like us have to understand what went wrong when something does go wrong. Model cards are not bureaucratic theater. They document what a tool is actually designed to do, who it was tested on, what its failure modes are. Without that documentation, when an AI system makes a recommendation that harms a patient, we have no way to trace whether that harm was predictable, preventable, or the result of the system being used outside its intended population.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THIS MATTERS FOR PATIENT SAFETY**‚Źé‚ŹéDentistry is not immune to AI-related risks. A DSI tool trained primarily on data from younger, insured patients with straightforward pathology may perform poorly for older patients, patients with complex medical histories, or patients from populations underrepresented in training data. I'm not speculating here. In 2022, research published in the Journal of Dental Research identified significant performance gaps in caries detection algorithms when tested on diverse patient populations (Schwendicke et al., 2022). The tools performed adequately on the training population but diverged sharply for patients over 65 and for patients with restorations or crown work. Without model card requirements documenting the training population and known performance limitations, a dental practice would have no way to know they should apply additional scrutiny when using that tool with elderly patients.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification criterion requires that vendors disclose:‚Źé‚Źé- Intended use and target population‚Źé- Known performance limitations and failure modes‚Źé- Training data characteristics and representativeness‚Źé- External validation results and confidence intervals‚Źé- Recommendations for clinical oversight and human review thresholds‚Źé‚ŹéThese aren't elaborate requirements. They're the basic facts that a responsible clinician should know before trusting a tool with patient care decisions. Removing them doesn't reduce burden on vendors. It shifts the burden onto patients, who lose the ability to understand what tool was used to inform their care and what that tool's actual limitations are.‚Źé‚Źé**THE INTEROPERABILITY PARADOX**‚Źé‚ŹéThere's an irony in the framing of this deregulation. The preamble talks about unleashing innovation and market competition. But without transparency standards, how does a patient shop for care? How does a dental practice evaluate competing DSI tools? Right now, if two vendors are offering caries detection systems, a practice can compare their model cards. We can see that one was validated on a dataset of 50,000 images from six different practices and one was validated on 8,000 images from a single practice. We can see where each tool's confidence drops. That's how markets actually work. Removing that transparency doesn't create competition. It creates information asymmetry where only vendors understand their own products.‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be clear about what I'm not arguing for. I'm not opposed to deregulation generally. I understand the case for reducing unnecessary approval processes, streamlining certification timelines, and removing redundant documentation. But transparency and safety documentation aren't the same as approval burden. A model card doesn't require FDA review. It doesn't slow deployment. It's a technical document that vendors should be producing anyway if they're serious about clinical utility.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC CHANGES WE'RE ASKING FOR**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Dental Standards Institute respectfully recommends that the final rule retain the DSI certification criterion requiring:‚Źé‚Źé1. Model cards documenting intended use, target population, and known performance limitations. These should be made available to purchasing practices and, upon patient request, to patients whose care involved the tool.‚Źé‚Źé2. Training data transparency sufficient for clinicians to evaluate whether the tool was trained on populations similar to their patient population. This doesn't require releasing proprietary data. It requires disclosing basic facts about dataset composition, size, and validation methodology.‚Źé‚Źé3. External validation evidence from independent sources. A vendor's own testing is necessary but insufficient. Tools should demonstrate performance across diverse settings and populations before they're used in clinical care.‚Źé‚Źé4. Clear guidance on human review thresholds. When should a clinician override the tool's recommendation? When should a second opinion be sought? These should be documented and part of the certification record.‚Źé‚ŹéThese requirements can coexist with a streamlined approval timeline. The European Union's In Vitro Diagnostic Regulation (IVDR), which came into effect in 2022, demonstrates that you can have both faster market entry and transparency requirements. Performance data and intended use documentation don't slow approval. They clarify what's being approved.‚Źé‚Źé**THE LARGER PATTERN**‚Źé‚ŹéWhat concerns me, having worked on patient advocacy for fifteen years, is the pattern of deregulation that sounds like it's about efficiency but functions as a shift in power. When we remove transparency requirements, we're not reducing burden. We're reducing visibility. The burden of understanding what's happening moves from vendors, who have the technical knowledge, to patients and advocates, who don't have access to the information.‚Źé‚ŹéI've watched this happen in other contexts. In 2019, when the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services simplified prior authorization requirements, the stated goal was reducing administrative burden on providers. The practical effect was that patients had less visibility into why their care was being delayed or denied. The prior authorization letter was often the only document where a patient could see the reasoning. Remove that requirement and the reasoning becomes invisible.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not saying that's the intent here. I'm saying it's the effect. When AI systems make recommendations about patient care, and those systems lack transparent documentation of their limitations and training data, patients lose the ability to understand the decisions affecting their treatment. That's not a reduction in burden. That's a reduction in accountability.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Dental Standards Institute supports innovation in clinical decision support. We want vendors to develop better tools faster. But faster deployment shouldn't mean less transparency. Those aren't opposites. And for patients like the woman we helped last year, transparency is the only leverage they have when something goes wrong.‚Źé‚ŹéWe ask that you retain the model card and transparency requirements for DSI certification. These are not burdensome. They are the baseline documentation that responsible vendors should provide anyway. Removing them doesn't unleash innovation. It unleashes opacity.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-29T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-29T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ4‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒfrustrated‚ôĒDelaware‚ôĒcommunity organizer‚ôĒ54‚ôĒLast year, I helped an elderly Vietnamese immigrant navigate a dental billing dispute‚ÄĒshe'd been charged three times for the same extraction‚ÄĒand when I tried to get her records transferred to a second opinion dentist, I hit a wall of bureaucratic silence. The dental office claimed HIPAA concerns, but what they really meant was they could stall indefinitely without consequences. Now I'm watching the conversation shift toward "streamlining" access, and I'm terrified we're about to hand these gatekeepers even more control over who gets to see what, while patients like her have even less recourse.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1200
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0038‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒWilliam Lopez‚ôĒNo Delays, No Surprises Coalition‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe No Delays, No Surprises Coalition urges the agency to retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion requiring AI model transparency and documentation, arguing that while broader deregulation of health IT interoperability is...‚ôĒ**No Delays, No Surprises Coalition**‚Źé**Comments on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions**‚Źé‚ŹéThe No Delays, No Surprises Coalition appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory actions affecting health information technology certification and information blocking requirements. Our coalition represents a diverse membership of patient advocates, health equity organizations, consumer protection groups, and community health providers across Washington State and beyond. We have a direct stake in how these rules evolve, given that our members interact daily with the consequences of both fragmented data systems and inadequate safeguards around algorithmic decision-making in healthcare.‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be direct about something that shaped my perspective on this proposal. Last year, my mother was diagnosed with late-stage diabetes, and I watched her primary care doctor spend nearly an hour on the phone trying to access her lab results from a clinic fifteen minutes away. Those results existed in digital form, but they were locked behind incompatible systems. The frustration was real, and I understand viscerally why people want to tear down barriers to data sharing. At the same time, I've seen what happens when data flows without guardrails. In my role here at the coalition, I've reviewed cases where aggressive data-sharing policies enabled discriminatory pricing algorithms that targeted low-income neighborhoods with inflated health plan costs. Both problems are real. Both matter. This is why I'm writing to urge the agency to retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures, even as we work to streamline other regulations.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PROBLEM WITH REMOVING DSI TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current certification criterion for Decision Support Interventions, codified in 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1), requires health IT developers to provide model cards documenting intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. The proposed rule appears to contemplate removing or substantially weakening this requirement as part of the broader deregulatory agenda. We believe this would be a mistake, and I want to explain why in concrete terms rather than abstract principle.‚Źé‚ŹéMy mother's case illustrates the stakes. Her physician had access to multiple AI-assisted diagnostic tools embedded in the EHR. One flagged her for potential kidney disease based on her creatinine levels and age. Another suggested a different medication pathway based on her comorbidities. The doctor made good clinical decisions, but she told me afterward that she had no idea how those tools were trained, what populations they were validated on, or whether the risk estimates applied to women over sixty-five. She was using black-box algorithms to inform treatment decisions affecting someone she cared deeply about. That's the current reality in many clinics. According to a 2023 analysis published in JAMA Network Open, approximately 40 percent of U.S. hospitals now use some form of AI-assisted clinical decision support, yet fewer than half can articulate what training data their systems employed (Rajkomar et al., 2023). Without transparency requirements, this opacity deepens.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what concerns me most. The proposed deregulation treats AI model transparency as a burden on developers rather than as essential infrastructure for patient safety and informed clinical decision-making. But certification isn't just bureaucratic overhead. When a health IT vendor provides a model card showing that an algorithm was trained on a majority-white patient population, clinicians can adjust their reliance on that tool for diverse patients. When a model card documents known risks, doctors can watch for those specific complications. When external validation data is available, providers can understand how the algorithm performs in their specific setting. This isn't theoretical. The FDA's proposed framework for clinical decision support software (announced in January 2024 guidance) explicitly emphasizes the need for documentation of algorithm performance across demographic groups precisely because algorithms trained on homogeneous datasets often fail for underrepresented populations.‚Źé‚ŹéI also keep coming back to accountability. If an AI tool contributes to a misdiagnosis or a treatment error, who is responsible? Under current rules, the vendor must have documented what they knew and what they validated. That documentation creates a paper trail. Remove the requirement, and you remove the accountability mechanism. A patient harmed by an algorithm trained on inadequate data and validated only in academic medical centers has no recourse if the vendor never documented those limitations in the first place. This isn't a theoretical concern either. The recent case of Optum's sepsis prediction model, which was found to systematically underestimate risk in Black patients because it was trained on a majority-white cohort, shows what happens when algorithmic development proceeds without rigorous transparency and validation across demographic groups. That model was already in use at hundreds of hospitals before the bias was identified (Obermeyer et al., 2019, in Science).‚Źé‚Źé**INTEROPERABILITY AND DATA ACCESS ARE NOT THE SAME AS UNMONITORED ALGORITHMS**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to acknowledge something important. The coalition supports faster data sharing. We support removing barriers to interoperability. My mother shouldn't have had to wait hours for lab results that existed in digital form. That's genuinely frustrating, and we need better standards and faster timelines for information sharing under the information blocking rule. Several of our member organizations have submitted comments supporting expanded use of FHIR APIs and faster TEFCA implementation. That's not where we're pushing back.‚Źé‚ŹéBut interoperability and algorithmic transparency are different regulatory problems. The deregulatory proposal seems to lump them together. The justification offered is that certification requirements slow down innovation and that market competition will drive quality without government oversight. I understand the logic, but it misses something crucial. Health care isn't a normal market. Patients don't choose algorithms. Clinicians often don't know they're using them. And the harms from algorithmic bias compound over time in ways that market competition alone cannot fix. A provider might use a biased algorithm for years without realizing it because the bias is subtle and affects a demographic minority in their patient population. By the time a competitor offers a better product, real harm has accumulated.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification criterion is actually quite modest. It doesn't require vendors to make their training data or proprietary code public. It doesn't mandate continuous monitoring. It doesn't prevent vendors from using proprietary machine learning approaches. It simply requires documentation of what you know about your algorithm's performance. That's not a heavy lift. It's basic professional responsibility. And it's essential for the kind of trustworthy AI that patients and clinicians actually need.‚Źé‚Źé**THE EQUITY DIMENSION**‚Źé‚ŹéLet me turn to something that's central to our coalition's mission. The organizations we represent have documented how data-sharing initiatives without adequate safeguards disproportionately harm vulnerable populations. This matters for the DSI criterion specifically because algorithmic bias follows wealth and power. Well-resourced health systems are more likely to invest in rigorous algorithm validation. They're more likely to conduct subgroup analyses. They're more likely to have internal oversight. Smaller providers, rural health systems, and community health centers are more likely to adopt tools from lower-cost vendors who cut corners on validation and transparency. If you remove the certification requirement, you create a two-tier system where affluent patients benefit from vetted algorithms while everyone else gets whatever's cheapest.‚Źé‚ŹéI've seen this pattern in other sectors. When the FTC investigated discriminatory pricing algorithms in insurance, they found that companies were more aggressive about deploying untested algorithms in zip codes with lower median incomes and higher proportions of communities of color. Those algorithms were never formally validated across demographic groups. Nobody documented the risks. And by the time the FTC acted, years of damage had been done. The health care system has fewer protections against algorithmic discrimination than the insurance market does. Removing DSI certification requirements would make that problem worse, not better.‚Źé‚ŹéOur coalition includes representatives from community health centers serving primarily low-income and immigrant populations. They've told us directly that they're concerned about adopting new health IT systems without understanding how the algorithmic components will affect their patients. They don't have the resources to conduct independent validation studies. They can't hire data scientists to audit algorithms. They rely on certification and transparency requirements to give them some baseline assurance that a tool has been tested. Remove that requirement, and you're telling them to either fall further behind in health IT adoption or accept tools they can't evaluate. That's not market competition. That's a choice between obsolescence and risk.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE'RE ASKING FOR**‚Źé‚ŹéOur recommendation is straightforward. Retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion in 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1) with its current transparency requirements. Make the implementation more flexible if you need to. Allow vendors to provide model cards in different formats if the current template is cumbersome. Extend timelines for compliance if vendors need more time to document their algorithms. But don't remove the requirement altogether. The burden is not substantial relative to the stakes, and the benefits for patient safety and equity are real.‚Źé‚ŹéAt the same time, we support the broader deregulatory goals where they make sense. The information blocking timelines can be streamlined. The TEFCA implementation schedule can be accelerated. The certification requirements for basic data exchange functionality can be simplified. We're not saying every rule is perfect or that no burden reduction is warranted. We're saying that this particular requirement serves a function that market competition cannot provide on its own, and that function matters most for the patients who have the least power to protect themselves.‚Źé‚ŹéThere's also a practical consideration. The health care system is already dealing with significant algorithmic bias. The VA's algorithm for allocating resources systematically underestimated the needs of Black veterans because it was trained on historical spending patterns that reflected prior discrimination. The University of California's algorithm for identifying high-risk patients was found to contain significant racial bias. These aren't edge cases. They're the current reality. Adding DSI transparency requirements didn't cause these problems. But removing them would make it easier for similar problems to persist undetected. We know from the FDA's work on software validation that documentation requirements, far from slowing innovation, actually help vendors identify and fix problems earlier in the development cycle.‚Źé‚Źé**IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the agency is concerned about burden, consider a more targeted approach. You could exempt certain categories of decision support from the model card requirement. You could allow vendors to submit documentation less frequently if they're not updating their algorithms. You could create a streamlined certification pathway for vendors whose algorithms have been independently validated in peer-reviewed literature. But the baseline requirement that vendors document what they know about their tools' performance, limitations, and validation should remain.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm also mindful that this comment comes from someone whose organization cares deeply about data access and interoperability. We're not opposed to deregulation on principle. We want faster data sharing. We want lower barriers to innovation in the health IT market. But we also remember that deregulation in health care has consequences that ripple through vulnerable communities. The financialization of health care, the consolidation of hospital systems, the rise of algorithmic management of clinical decisions, the increasing use of AI in eligibility determinations and treatment recommendations, all of these have happened against a backdrop of reduced regulatory oversight. And all of them have contributed to widening health disparities. We're asking for a modest preservation of one transparency requirement as part of an otherwise deregulatory agenda. That seems like a reasonable balance.‚Źé‚Źé**CLOSING**‚Źé‚ŹéThe No Delays, No Surprises Coalition is named for a simple principle. Patients and providers deserve clear information about what's happening in health care systems. They deserve transparency about how decisions are being made. They deserve to know when algorithms are involved and what those algorithms are designed to do. None of that requires eliminating the DSI certification criterion. It requires keeping it. We urge the agency to retain this requirement while proceeding with other deregulatory measures that reduce unnecessary burden without compromising patient safety or equity.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-12T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-12T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ6‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒsupportive‚ôĒWashington‚ôĒdirector of government affairs‚ôĒ46‚ôĒLast year, when my mother was diagnosed with late-stage diabetes, I watched her primary care physician spend forty minutes on the phone trying to access her previous lab results from a clinic across town‚ÄĒresults that were already digitized but locked behind incompatible systems. Meanwhile, I've also seen firsthand how aggressive data-sharing initiatives in other sectors have disproportionately exposed low-income communities to discriminatory pricing and targeting, which made me realize we can't simply unlock health records without robust safeguards in place first.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1956
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0039‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒEdward Gonzalez‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe commenter opposes removing AI model card transparency requirements from clinical decision support tools, arguing that documentation of how AI tools are designed, validated, and limited is essential for doctors to make informed decisions about ...‚ôĒMy daughter's A1C records took three weeks to move between her pediatrician and the endocrinologist at the children's hospital last year. Three weeks. We paid for those tests twice because the first provider couldn't share them digitally, and we needed them for her care plan. I get frustrated thinking about that, and I also understand why there are rules keeping medical records from being treated like a commodity. But what I'm most concerned about right now is what happens to the safeguards if we strip away the transparency requirements for AI tools that doctors are using to make decisions about her care.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed deregulation would remove the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion that currently requires AI model cards. These cards document what a tool is actually designed for, who it works best on, what its known limitations are, and how it was trained. When my daughter's endocrinologist adjusts her insulin doses, I need to know if the clinical decision support tool they're using has been validated in kids like her, or if it was trained mostly on adults. I need to know if the model card says it works better for certain populations. Right now, that information has to be disclosed. Under the proposed changes, it wouldn't.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not opposed to innovation. I understand that certification requirements add time and cost for companies developing these tools. But there's a difference between removing unnecessary red tape and removing the documentation that lets doctors actually understand what they're deploying. If we eliminate the model card requirement in the name of deregulation, we're essentially saying a clinical tool doesn't need to tell us how it was built, what it's good at, or what it misses. That's not freedom. That's opacity.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification criterion isn't perfect, but it serves a real function. It makes developers think carefully about their training data. It forces them to validate their tools across different patient populations. It gives doctors the information they need to use these tools responsibly. According to the ONC's own documentation, these transparency requirements are the backbone of accountable AI deployment in healthcare settings.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm asking you to keep this criterion in place. Reduce other burdens if you need to. But don't remove the requirement that companies tell us how their AI tools actually work. My daughter's care depends on doctors making informed decisions, and informed decisions require transparency.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-26T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-26T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒsupportive‚ôĒFlorida‚ôĒelementary school teacher‚ôĒ43‚ôĒMy daughter was diagnosed with type 1 diabetes last year, and I watched my wife spend hours on the phone trying to get her A1C records transferred between the pediatrician and the endocrinologist at the children's hospital‚ÄĒrecords we literally paid for and needed for her care plan. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0040‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒBarbara Ramirez‚ôĒJohns Hopkins University and Medicine‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒJohns Hopkins University argues that transparency requirements for clinical decision support systems, including AI model cards disclosing training data, intended use, and known limitations, should be retained because they are essential safeguards ...‚ôĒ**Comment on the Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity**‚Źé‚ŹéThe undersigned submits this comment on behalf of Barbara Ramirez, Assistant Professor at Johns Hopkins University and Medicine, in response to the proposed deregulatory actions announced by the Office of the Assistant Secretary for Technology Policy and the Office of the National Coordinator for Health IT.‚Źé‚Źé**BACKGROUND AND CONCERN**‚Źé‚ŹéLast year, our research group at Johns Hopkins was pursuing a project that mattered‚ÄĒgenuinely mattered‚ÄĒto patient outcomes in chronic disease management. A postdoctoral fellow had built preliminary models for decision support algorithms intended to help clinicians assess treatment sequencing in patients with complex comorbidities. The work was rigorous, incremental, and aimed at a real clinical problem. Within months, three major health information vendors consolidated their market position and locked their proprietary data formats behind tighter restrictions. Our institution's legal and compliance teams advised us that we could not compete with the consolidated players under the existing framework, and more importantly, we could not access the interoperable data we needed to validate our models externally. The postdoc took a position in industry. Eighteen months of research effort dissolved. A tool that might have improved clinical decision-making never reached development.‚Źé‚ŹéThis experience is not unique, and it is not primarily about competitive advantage for our institution. It is about what happens to innovation and patient safety when market consolidation outpaces regulatory clarity. The proposed deregulatory actions raise urgent questions about whether removing certification criteria and transparency requirements will accelerate beneficial innovation or whether it will entrench market power and shrink the space where academic researchers and smaller innovators can contribute to patient care.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CASE FOR RETAINING AI MODEL TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéJohns Hopkins respectfully urges the Office of the National Coordinator and CMS to retain the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures. This is not a request to block innovation. It is a request to ensure that the innovation we enable serves patient safety and clinical accountability.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification criterion requires developers to disclose intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. These requirements exist because clinical decision support systems operate in environments where errors propagate directly to patient harm. A clinician relying on a recommendation from a black-box algorithm has no way to recognize when that recommendation is outside the system's validated domain or when the underlying training data contained biases that render it unsafe for a particular patient population.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider the mechanics of how AI-assisted clinical tools fail. In 2019, researchers at the University of California examined a widely deployed risk-prediction algorithm used in U.S. hospitals and found that the model systematically underestimated risk for Black patients by approximately 50 percent because it was trained on historical data in which healthcare spending served as a proxy for health need (Obermeyer et al., "Dissecting racial bias in an algorithm used to manage the health of populations," Science 2019). The algorithm had been deployed across hundreds of hospitals. Clinicians using it had no documentation of its training data characteristics, no disclosure of its target population, no warning about known disparities. The harm was systemic and largely invisible until external researchers specifically looked for it.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not a hypothetical concern. It is the documented failure mode of unvetted decision support systems in clinical practice. The proposed deregulation does not specify what transparency mechanisms should replace the current DSI criterion. If the answer is "market competition will drive transparency," the evidence from deployed health IT systems suggests otherwise. Vendors do not voluntarily disclose limitations that competitors might use to market alternative products. Hospitals do not systematically audit the racial or demographic performance of algorithms before deploying them. The burden of discovering algorithmic bias falls on external researchers working with whatever documentation is available, which is often minimal.‚Źé‚Źé**TRANSPARENCY SUPPORTS INFORMED CLINICAL DECISION-MAKING**‚Źé‚ŹéClinicians are already overwhelmed. They operate under time pressure, cognitive load from electronic health record interfaces, and competing demands. In this context, a clinician using a decision support tool needs to know: Is this recommendation based on clinical trials, observational data, or extracted patterns from billing records? Was this algorithm tested on patient populations similar to the one in front of me? What are the known failure modes?‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI criterion does not require perfect transparency or eliminate clinical judgment. It requires that developers document what they know about their own systems and communicate that information in a standardized format (the model card, as specified in ONC guidance). A clinician still decides whether to follow the recommendation. But the clinician makes that decision with information rather than blind faith.‚Źé‚ŹéCompare this to a regulatory regime without model transparency requirements. A clinician encounters a recommendation from a decision support system. The system's internal logic is proprietary. The training data is proprietary. External validation, if it exists, may not be published or may be published only in venues the clinician does not access. The clinician has no structured way to understand whether this recommendation is reliable for this patient. The clinician must choose between (1) ignoring the system entirely, which defeats its purpose, or (2) trusting it on faith, which is what the system's vendor prefers but which is not safe medical practice.‚Źé‚Źé**ACCOUNTABILITY AND LIABILITY IMPLICATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéTransparency requirements create accountability pathways. If a clinician uses a decision support system that causes harm, and that system had no disclosed training data characteristics or external validation, then it becomes difficult to determine where responsibility lies. Did the vendor sell a system they knew was untested? Did the hospital deploy a system without adequate vetting? Did the clinician misuse a system that was appropriate for a different population? Without transparent disclosures, all these questions become murky.‚Źé‚ŹéThe model card requirement clarifies responsibility. A vendor that discloses its training data, intended use, and known limitations has created a factual record. If that system is then used outside its validated domain and causes harm, the accountability chain is clearer. Conversely, a vendor that refuses transparency creates opacity that benefits no one except the vendor itself. Patients and clinicians bear the downstream risk.‚Źé‚Źé**MARKET CONSOLIDATION AND THE PROBLEM OF PROPRIETARY LOCK-IN**‚Źé‚ŹéThe experience at Johns Hopkins also reflects a broader market dynamic that deregulation may worsen. When health IT vendors consolidate, they often adopt proprietary data standards and restrict interoperability. This is rational from a business perspective: if your market position is strong, interoperability dilutes your competitive advantage. But it also reduces the ability of external researchers, smaller competitors, and academic institutions to validate claims, develop alternatives, or contribute incremental innovations.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current regulatory framework includes information blocking rules (42 CFR Part 2, and more broadly the 21st Century Cures Act, Pub. L. 114-255) that require certified health IT systems to make data available for interoperability. These rules are imperfect and enforcement is inconsistent, but they create a legal obligation that clinicians and institutions can invoke when vendors resist data sharing. Removing certification requirements may eliminate the lever that forces interoperability.‚Źé‚ŹéIf decision support systems become classified as non-certified tools, vendors have less incentive to comply with interoperability standards. A vendor might argue that since the system is not subject to certification, it is not subject to information blocking rules. The system becomes a closed appliance, accessible only to the vendor's preferred partners, if at all. This is good for the vendor's market position. It is bad for external validation, bad for market competition, and bad for the broader ecosystem of innovation.‚Źé‚Źé**EVIDENCE FROM INTERNATIONAL REGULATORY APPROACHES**‚Źé‚ŹéThe European Union's regulatory approach to AI in healthcare, outlined in the proposed AI Act (currently in legislative process), includes transparency and documentation requirements for high-risk AI systems, which includes clinical decision support. The EU is not blocking innovation. It is requiring that developers document what they know about their systems and make that documentation available to regulators and, in some cases, to the public. The rationale is straightforward: systems that affect human health require transparency proportionate to their risk.‚Źé‚ŹéThe United States has historically taken a lighter-touch approach than the EU, and there are legitimate reasons for that choice. But "light-touch" should not mean "no touch." A minimum floor of transparency‚ÄĒmodel cards, intended use documentation, training data characteristics, external validation results‚ÄĒis compatible with innovation. It is not compatible with vendors claiming their systems are completely proprietary black boxes that no one, including the purchaser, is allowed to examine.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC RECOMMENDATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéJohns Hopkins offers the following specific recommendations:‚Źé‚Źé1. Retain the DSI certification criterion requiring model cards and transparency disclosures, or adopt an equivalent requirement that applies to all clinical decision support systems regardless of certification status.‚Źé‚Źé2. If ONC and CMS move forward with deregulation, establish a minimum transparency floor that applies to all decision support systems used in clinical settings. This floor should include: intended use, target population, training data characteristics (including data source, time period, and demographic composition), validation methodology and results, known performance disparities by demographic group, and documented limitations.‚Źé‚Źé3. Require that transparency disclosures be made available to healthcare providers and, to the extent consistent with legitimate intellectual property protection, to external researchers. A clinician purchasing a decision support system should be able to obtain documentation of how that system was built and tested.‚Źé‚Źé4. Maintain the connection between health IT certification and interoperability obligations. If a vendor chooses to seek certification for its decision support system, it should be required to support industry-standard data exchange formats (FHIR, as specified in the 21st Century Cures Act Final Rule, 85 FR 25642). If a vendor chooses not to seek certification, it should not be required to comply with interoperability standards, but it should be transparent about this choice and its implications.‚Źé‚Źé5. Establish a process for external validation of decision support systems, particularly those making recommendations in high-stakes clinical domains (oncology, cardiovascular medicine, critical care). This process should be led by academic medical centers and professional societies, not by vendors. The goal is not to create a new regulatory bottleneck but to build a library of independently validated systems that clinicians can trust.‚Źé‚Źé**THE DISTINCTION BETWEEN BURDEN AND SAFEGUARD**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed deregulation frames transparency requirements as "burden." From a vendor perspective, they are. Documenting how a system was built, what data it was trained on, and what its limitations are requires work. But burden and safeguard are not opposites. They can be the same thing viewed from different angles. A seatbelt is a burden on the driver. It is also a safeguard.‚Źé‚ŹéThe question is whether the benefit of removing this burden exceeds the cost of losing this safeguard. Johns Hopkins believes it does not. Clinical decision support systems are not consumer electronics. They are tools that clinicians use to make recommendations that affect patient health. The burden of transparency is proportionate to the stakes.‚Źé‚ŹéFurthermore, the transparency burden is not as heavy as vendors claim. The ONC has published guidance on model cards for health AI (available in the proposed National Institute of Standards and Technology AI Risk Management Framework). A vendor that has actually developed a rigorous decision support system already has most of the required information. Documenting it is not creating new knowledge; it is making existing knowledge explicit. For vendors that resist transparency, the question becomes: What are they hiding?‚Źé‚Źé**INNOVATION VERSUS MARKET CONSOLIDATION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe deregulation proposal frames removing certification requirements as necessary to unleash innovation. But innovation can take many forms. Some innovations benefit patients. Others benefit vendors. Some innovations benefit both. The question is what regulatory approach actually produces the innovations that matter.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider what happened in our laboratory. We were attempting an innovation: using machine learning to improve treatment sequencing in complex patients. This innovation required access to real-world data, the ability to validate our models against external datasets, and the freedom to publish our results. Regulatory transparency requirements would not have blocked this innovation. They would have supported it by ensuring that the proprietary systems we relied on were interoperable and documented.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat actually blocked our innovation was not regulatory burden. It was market consolidation combined with proprietary lock-in. Three vendors consolidated. They adopted proprietary data formats. Their legal teams said no to external data access. Our institution's legal team advised us to back away. The innovation died not because of regulation but because of the absence of regulatory constraints on proprietary behavior.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving certification requirements will not change this dynamic. If anything, it will accelerate it. Vendors will have less incentive to comply with interoperability standards because they will no longer be certified systems subject to information blocking rules. Consolidation will continue. Proprietary lock-in will deepen. Smaller innovators and academic researchers will face even more barriers.‚Źé‚Źé**DISTINGUISHING INNOVATION FROM DEREGULATION**‚Źé‚ŹéTrue innovation in health IT comes from new insights, new data, new methods, and new applications. It does not come primarily from removing documentation requirements. A vendor that cannot innovate unless it is freed from the requirement to document its training data and disclose its limitations is not actually innovating. It is hiding.‚Źé‚ŹéThe vendors most enthusiastically supporting deregulation are not the innovative startups. They are the consolidated incumbents with large market share and proprietary ecosystems. They have already achieved market dominance. Deregulation protects that dominance. It does not create space for new entrants.‚Źé‚ŹéStartups and academic researchers often support maintaining transparency requirements because transparency creates a level playing field. If all systems must disclose their methods and validation results, then a startup with a better idea can compete on the merits. If some systems are opaque black boxes while others are transparent, the incumbents with the resources to maintain complexity and secrecy have an advantage.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PATIENT SAFETY ARGUMENT**‚Źé‚ŹéUltimately, this comment returns to patient safety. The DSI certification criterion requiring transparency exists because clinical decision support systems affect patient health. When these systems fail‚ÄĒwhen they make recommendations outside their validated domain, when they embed historical biases, when they are used in populations different from their training data‚ÄĒpatients are harmed.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current regulatory framework is imperfect. ONC certification does not guarantee that a system is safe. It ensures that certain minimum standards have been met: the system is documented, it has been tested, the vendor has disclosed what it knows about limitations. This is a floor, not a ceiling. But it is a necessary floor.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving this floor in the name of deregulation trades a known safeguard for a speculative benefit. The speculation is that vendors will innovate more rapidly if freed from transparency requirements. The safeguard that is lost is the assurance that clinicians purchasing decision support systems have basic information about how those systems work.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéJohns Hopkins University and Medicine respectfully urges the Office of the National Coordinator and CMS to retain the DSI certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures, or to establish an equivalent transparency requirement that applies to all clinical decision support systems.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not a request to block innovation or to protect incumbent vendors. It is a request to ensure that the innovation we enable serves patient safety and clinical accountability. The postdoctoral fellow who left our laboratory last year was attempting an innovation that could have improved patient care. She was blocked not by regulatory burden but by market consolidation and proprietary lock-in. Removing transparency requirements will not solve this problem. It will make it worse.‚Źé‚ŹéThe path forward is not deregulation but smarter regulation: maintaining transparency requirements while reducing other burdens, ensuring that interoperability standards are enforced consistently, and creating space for external validation and competitive entry. This approach protects patients, supports clinicians, and enables the innovations that actually matter.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-14T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-14T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ8‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving DSI transparency requirements creates a dangerous regulatory gap where AI-based clinical decision support tools face fewer disclosure obligations than traditional medical devices, undermining consistent safety standards.‚ôĒacademic‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒangry‚ôĒIllinois‚ôĒassistant professor‚ôĒ54‚ôĒLast spring, I watched a promising postdoc in my lab abandon her research on interoperability standards because the institution's legal team said we couldn't compete with three major players who'd just consolidated under looser oversight‚ÄĒthey'd already locked in proprietary data formats we couldn't access. She took a position in industry instead, and we lost eighteen months of work on a problem that actually matters to patient care.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2602
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0041‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒPatricia Wilson‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA stroke survivor and veteran argues against removing the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion from healthcare IT regulations, contending that transparency requirements for AI systems used in clinical decision-making are essentia...‚ôĒRe: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity (RFI)‚Źé‚Źé**MY EXPERIENCE WITH FRAGMENTED RECORDS**‚Źé‚ŹéLast year, I had a stroke. I was 61. I spent three months seeing a neurologist, a cardiologist, a physical therapist, and an endocrinologist, and not one of them could pull up my previous imaging from the hospital stay or my lab work from two years before. I described my medications from memory while my daughter searched her email for old discharge summaries. One doctor ordered a repeat CT scan because he couldn't access the original. Another prescribed something that conflicted with what I was already taking because nobody had the full picture. That's when I started paying attention to what "interoperability" actually means, because I was living the cost of it not working.‚Źé‚ŹéNow this rule is moving toward something that sounds better on the surface. Patients can get their records. Data can move between providers. I want that. I need that. But the proposal includes removing something called the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion, which is where health systems have to disclose what they know about the AI tools they're using to make decisions about patient care. That requirement includes things like model transparency cards, information about what populations an AI system was tested on, what its known risks are, how it was trained, and whether it's been validated outside the lab.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not a technologist. I'm a veteran with a medical history, and I've started to understand what it means when those safeguards disappear.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PROBLEM WITH REMOVING TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what I'm concerned about. When you remove the requirement for Decision Support Interventions certification criteria, you're removing the requirement for transparency about how AI is being used in my care. That sounds abstract. Let me make it concrete.‚Źé‚ŹéMy VA psychiatric history spans 15 years. I have diagnoses in my records that I've worked through, that I've been treated for, and that I manage now. When I had my stroke, one of the medications prescribed was based partly on an algorithm. I didn't know that. The doctor didn't tell me which parts of the recommendation came from human judgment and which parts came from a machine learning model trained on data that might not have included people like me. Veterans. People with PTSD. People with chronic pain. People who've had certain diagnoses and recovered.‚Źé‚ŹéThe certification criterion that's being removed required health IT developers to disclose the training data characteristics, the intended use, the target population, and known risks. That's not bureaucratic overhead. That's informed consent. That's the difference between me knowing what's happening in my medical care and not knowing.‚Źé‚ŹéIf we remove that requirement, what happens next? Developers can still use AI. They just don't have to tell anyone what it was trained on or what its limitations are. They don't have to disclose if it works worse for certain populations. They don't have to document what it was designed to do versus what it might do in the real world. And when something goes wrong, or when an algorithm makes a recommendation that doesn't fit my actual situation, there's no paper trail. There's no accountability.‚Źé‚ŹéI've read enough to know that the intention here is to reduce burden on developers. I get it. Certification is work. But certification exists because we've learned hard lessons about what happens when medical decisions are made without transparency. I'm not asking for perfection. I'm asking for honesty about what's being used to treat me.‚Źé‚Źé**INFORMATION BLOCKING CUTS BOTH WAYS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe rule also talks about information blocking safeguards. Right now, there are regulations that prevent health systems from blocking patient access to records. That's good. I want my records. I want them portable. I want them to follow me from provider to provider without me having to call and beg.‚Źé‚ŹéBut opening up that access without strong safeguards about how the data can be used and where it can go is a different problem.‚Źé‚ŹéMy VA psychiatric history. My diabetes diagnosis. My medications for anxiety. These things follow me in the medical record. They're necessary for good care. But they're also things that could be held against me. I'm 62. I'm thinking about what happens if I need a new job. Employers still discriminate against people with psychiatric histories. They do. I've seen it happen to other veterans. I've watched it happen.‚Źé‚ŹéIf my health data becomes more accessible, more portable, that's good for care coordination. But if there's no requirement for AI systems to be transparent about how they're using that data, if there's no paper trail showing how decisions were made, then my information is floating around in systems I can't see, being used in ways I don't understand, to make decisions that affect me.‚Źé‚ŹéThe information blocking safeguards that exist now are there because someone fought for them. Because patients got hurt. Because data that should have been private ended up in the wrong hands. I'm not asking to block my own records. I'm asking to know how they're being used.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT THE CERTIFICATION CRITERION ACTUALLY DOES**‚Źé‚ŹéLet me walk through what the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion requires, because it matters.‚Źé‚ŹéFirst: AI model cards. These are documents that describe what an AI system does, what it was designed for, and what it's not good at. They include the training data characteristics. Was it trained on data from a diverse population, or was it trained on data from one hospital system in one region? Was it trained on mostly younger patients or older patients? Was it tested on people with my conditions, or was it tested on a different population entirely? That matters. A model trained on data from 30-year-olds might not work the same way for a 62-year-old.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond: intended use and target population. The certification criterion requires developers to say what the system is supposed to do and who it's supposed to do it for. If an AI system is designed to help with diagnosis in primary care, it should say that. If it hasn't been tested in emergency departments, it should say that too. If it works better for some racial groups than others, that needs to be documented. Right now, that's required. Under the proposed rule, it's optional.‚Źé‚ŹéThird: known risks. Every medical intervention has risks. Medications have side effects. Surgeries have complications. AI systems have failure modes. They can miss things. They can be overconfident. They can work differently in different settings. The certification criterion requires developers to document what they know about what can go wrong. Without that requirement, a developer could know that their system has a 15 percent false negative rate in a certain population and choose not to tell anyone.‚Źé‚ŹéFourth: external validation. This means testing the system outside the lab, in the real world, with real patients, in different settings. A system might work perfectly when it's tested by the people who built it on carefully selected data. It might fail when a real hospital uses it with their actual patient population. The certification criterion requires evidence that it works beyond the original testing environment. Without it, we're back to trusting developers to do the right thing on their own.‚Źé‚ŹéI understand why developers find this burdensome. It takes work. It costs money. It means you have to test your system thoroughly and document what you find. But that work exists because patients have been hurt by medical decisions made without transparency. I've lived through the consequence of fragmented care. I don't want to also live through the consequence of opaque AI.‚Źé‚Źé**THE SPECIFIC CONCERN ABOUT REMOVING THIS CRITERION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal would remove the requirement for Decision Support Interventions certification entirely. Let me be clear about what that means. It means a health system could implement an AI system to help with medication recommendations, diagnostic support, triage decisions, or resource allocation without any requirement to disclose how that system works, what data it was trained on, or what its limitations are.‚Źé‚ŹéThe certification criterion I'm talking about is in the current rules under 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1). It requires health IT developers to attest that their Decision Support Interventions meet specific standards. Those standards include the transparency disclosures I mentioned. Under the proposed rule, that criterion would be removed entirely from the certification requirements.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat replaces it? Nothing. Developers could still build AI systems. They just wouldn't have to prove they're transparent about it. They wouldn't have to document training data, intended use, target population, or known risks. They wouldn't have to show external validation. The market would supposedly reward good systems and punish bad ones, but that assumes patients know what they're being treated by. Most of the time, we don't even know an AI system is involved in our care.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm skeptical of that logic. I'm skeptical because I spent three months rotating between specialists last year, and none of them told me what was driving their recommendations. I'm skeptical because I'm a veteran with a psychiatric history, and I know how that information gets used against people. I'm skeptical because the burden of bad medical decisions falls on patients, not developers.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THIS MATTERS FOR PATIENT SAFETY**‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what keeps me up at night. My stroke could have been worse. I got lucky. But during those three months of care, there were several moments where a decision was being made about my treatment, and nobody had the full picture. If an AI system had been involved in one of those decisions, if it had been trained on data that didn't include people like me, if it had been tested only in a different setting, if nobody knew its limitations, that outcome could have been different.‚Źé‚ŹéPatient safety isn't just about individual mistakes. It's about systems that make it possible to catch mistakes, to understand where they came from, to learn from them. When you remove transparency requirements, you remove the ability to audit decisions. You remove the ability to know if something went wrong because the doctor made a mistake or because the AI system failed or because the AI system was used in a way it wasn't designed for.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current certification criterion for Decision Support Interventions exists because we've learned that patients get hurt when medical decisions are opaque. It's not a perfect system. But it's better than no system at all.‚Źé‚ŹéI've read some of the deregulation arguments. They say certification is slowing down innovation. Maybe that's true. But I'm not sure I want faster innovation if it means faster deployment of AI systems nobody understands. I want innovation that includes transparency. I want developers who are confident enough in their systems to document them fully.‚Źé‚Źé**THE RISK WITH INFORMATION BLOCKING CHANGES**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule also weakens information blocking safeguards. The current rules prevent health systems from blocking patient access to their records. That's important. I want my records. I want to be able to get them and take them somewhere else.‚Źé‚ŹéBut information blocking safeguards also include requirements about how data can be used and shared. When those safeguards are weakened, data flows more freely. That sounds good. But it also means my psychiatric history, my chronic condition diagnoses, my medication list, all of that is moving through systems I can't see, being used for purposes I don't fully understand.‚Źé‚ŹéThe certification requirement for Decision Support Interventions is connected to this. If an AI system is using my data to make recommendations about my care, I should know about it. I should know what data it's using, how it was trained, what its limitations are. That transparency is part of what keeps information blocking from becoming information abuse. It's the difference between my data being used to help me and my data being used in ways I didn't consent to.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen you remove transparency requirements while also loosening information blocking safeguards, you're creating a situation where data flows freely but its use is opaque. That's not innovation. That's just risk transferred to patients.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT I'M ASKING FOR**‚Źé‚ŹéI'm asking that the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion be retained. Specifically, I'm asking that the requirement for AI model cards and transparency disclosures remain in place. This should include:‚Źé‚Źé1. Disclosure of training data characteristics. What populations was this system trained on? What was the demographic breakdown? What geographic regions were included?‚Źé‚Źé2. Clear documentation of intended use and target population. What is this system designed to do? Who is it designed for? What is it not designed for?‚Źé‚Źé3. Comprehensive documentation of known risks and limitations. What can go wrong? Where has this system been tested and where hasn't it? What are its failure modes?‚Źé‚Źé4. Evidence of external validation. Has this system been tested outside the lab? Does it work in real-world settings? Does it work equally well for different populations?‚Źé‚Źé5. Ongoing monitoring and reporting. After deployment, is there tracking of how well the system actually performs? Are there mechanisms to report problems?‚Źé‚ŹéThese requirements don't prevent innovation. They require that innovation be transparent. They require that developers understand their own systems well enough to document them. They require that patients and clinicians know what's involved in medical decisions.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not asking for perfection. I'm asking for honesty.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PRACTICAL IMPACT**‚Źé‚ŹéLet me give you an example of what this looks like in practice. I'm on a medication that helps with my blood pressure and my mood. It was prescribed by my cardiologist after my stroke. He told me it was the right choice for my situation. I trusted him. But what if, in another health system, an AI system recommends the same medication but for different reasons? What if that system was trained on data from a younger population? What if it doesn't account for the interaction with my other medications? What if nobody knows because transparency isn't required?‚Źé‚ŹéThat's not theoretical. That happens. Medications get prescribed based on algorithmic recommendations. Treatments get recommended based on AI systems. Right now, there's at least a requirement that if you're using an AI system to help with those decisions, you have to document how it works. You have to show that you tested it. You have to disclose its limitations.‚Źé‚ŹéUnder the proposed rule, you don't have to do any of that. You can use the same AI system, make the same recommendations, affect the same patients, but without any transparency requirement. That's not innovation. That's just hiding.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not opposed to AI in healthcare. I'm opposed to using AI without knowing what it does. I'm opposed to removing safeguards that exist because patients have been hurt. I'm opposed to loosening requirements while also making data more portable. That's a recipe for data being used in ways I didn't consent to for purposes I don't understand to make decisions about my care that I can't audit.‚Źé‚Źé**ACCOUNTABILITY AND LEARNING**‚Źé‚ŹéHere's another thing that matters. When something goes wrong in medicine, we investigate. We figure out what happened. We learn from it. We change processes so it doesn't happen again. That requires transparency. It requires documentation. It requires being able to trace decisions back to their source.‚Źé‚ŹéIf an AI system makes a bad recommendation, and a patient gets hurt, how do we investigate? Where do we look? What documentation exists? Right now, the certification criterion requires that documentation to exist. Developers have to prove they know how their systems work. If something goes wrong, there's a paper trail.‚Źé‚ŹéRemove that requirement, and you remove the ability to investigate. A bad outcome could come from a doctor's mistake, or from an AI system's failure, or from the AI system being used in a way it wasn't designed for, or from a combination of things. Without transparency, you can't tell. Without documentation, you can't learn.‚Źé‚ŹéThat's not just a patient safety issue. That's a public health issue. We get better at medicine because we investigate failures and learn from them. We remove transparency requirements, we lose that ability.‚Źé‚Źé**THE BROADER CONTEXT**‚Źé‚ŹéI know this rule is framed as deregulation. Reduce burden on developers. Increase competition. Let the market work. I understand that argument. I'm not opposed to competition. I'm not opposed to innovation. But I've lived through fragmented healthcare. I've watched what happens when systems don't talk to each other. I've also watched what happens when safeguards are removed in other industries. They get removed because people argue that the market will provide oversight. Then years later, you find out the market didn't provide oversight. People got hurt. And now you're trying to put safeguards back in place, and it's much harder.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm asking you not to make that mistake with AI in healthcare. Keep the transparency requirements. Keep the certification criterion. Let developers innovate. But make them do it transparently. Make them document what they've built. Make them test it in the real world. Make them disclose what they know about limitations.‚Źé‚ŹéThat's not anti-innovation. That's pro-patient. And frankly, it should be pro-developer too. A developer who's confident in their system should be willing to document it. A system that works well should be able to show that it works. The transparency requirement isn't a barrier to good innovation. It's a filter that keeps bad innovation from hurting patients.‚Źé‚Źé**MY SPECIFIC ASKS OF THE AGENCY**‚Źé‚ŹéI want the Office of the National Coordinator to retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion at 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1). I want that criterion to continue requiring:‚Źé‚ŹéAttestation that health IT developers have created and included model transparency cards with their systems. These cards should document training data characteristics, intended use, target population, known risks, and external validation evidence. I want this requirement to stay in place.‚Źé‚ŹéI want information blocking safeguards to remain robust. When data becomes more portable, the risk of it being used inappropriately increases. We need strong safeguards to ensure that patient data isn't used without consent or in ways that harm patients.‚Źé‚ŹéI want ongoing monitoring and accountability mechanisms. If an AI system is being used in clinical care, there should be ways to track how well it actually works, to report problems, to investigate failures. That's not possible without documentation.‚Źé‚ŹéI want patient transparency. If an AI system is involved in a recommendation about my care, I want to know that. I want to know what data it used, what it was trained on, what its limitations are. That's basic informed consent.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéI had a stroke. I'm 62. I'm a veteran. I'm working on recovering. I'm also thinking about what happens next. I'm thinking about what kind of healthcare system I want to be part of. I'm thinking about what kind of safeguards I need to trust that my records are being used to help me, not harm me.‚Źé‚ŹéThe certification criterion for Decision Support Interventions is one of those safeguards. It requires transparency. It requires documentation. It requires accountability. It's not perfect. But it's better than nothing. And removing it while also loosening information blocking safeguards is a mistake.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm asking you to keep that criterion in place. I'm asking you to keep transparency requirements for AI systems used in clinical care. I'm asking you to make sure that when my data becomes more portable, it's protected by strong safeguards about how it can be used.‚Źé‚ŹéInnovation is good. But transparent innovation is better. Safe innovation is best. Please don't remove the safeguards that make that possible.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-18T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-18T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ10‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒurgent‚ôĒTexas‚ôĒveteran‚ôĒ62‚ôĒAfter my stroke last year, I spent three months rotating between four different specialists, and not one of them could access my previous imaging or lab work‚ÄĒI had to describe my medications from memory while my daughter frantically searched her email for old discharge summaries.
