CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0042‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒLinda Thompson‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA small business owner argues against removing Decision Support Interventions certification requirements, contending that mandatory AI model card disclosures are essential safeguards that enable informed clinical decision-making rather than bureau...‚ôĒ**RE: HHS/ASTP RFI on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability ‚ÄĒ Deregulatory Actions**‚Źé‚ŹéLast spring, my employee Sarah spent three weeks trying to get her medical records from one specialist to another. The rheumatologist had them locked in a system, the new doctor couldn't access them, and Sarah's treatment just sat on hold. I was angry about it then, but I'm even more concerned now reading about plans to loosen the rules that at least tried to create some consistency. I understand the intent to reduce burden on health IT companies, but I'm worried we're about to trade one problem for a much bigger one.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat I keep coming back to is this: if we remove the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirement that mandates AI model cards and transparency disclosures, we lose one of the few safeguards patients actually have. These disclosures, which require companies to document intended use, target population, known risks, and training data characteristics, aren't bureaucratic red tape. They're the difference between a doctor knowing what an AI tool is actually trained to do and just hoping it works.‚Źé‚ŹéSarah's situation was frustrating, but at least her records existed and her doctors could theoretically see them. What scares me more is a future where her data gets pulled into an AI system without anyone being able to tell her what that system was trained on, who it was tested with, or what it's actually supposed to do. If her data came from a training set that excluded people like her, but no one has to disclose that, how would she even know? The certification criteria requiring external validation and known limitations actually allows for informed clinical decision-making. Remove that, and you've got doctors making treatment calls based on black boxes.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not opposed to innovation. Honestly, I'd love it if the interoperability barriers that held up Sarah's care came down. But deregulation shouldn't mean we swap one access problem for a privacy and safety problem that's harder to see coming. The DSI model card requirement isn't expensive overhead, it's basic accountability. It tells doctors and patients what they're actually working with.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the goal is truly to reduce burden while promoting innovation, keep the transparency requirements. Those don't slow down good companies. They slow down the ones building tools without knowing their limitations, and that's exactly the slowdown we need.‚Źé‚ŹéLinda Thompson‚ŹéSmall Business Owner‚ŹéMississippi‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-29T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-29T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe agency's claim that 'no publicly available evidence' supports transparency requirements conflates absence of evidence with evidence of absence; lack of published studies does not mean the criterion provides no value.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒfrustrated‚ôĒMississippi‚ôĒsmall business owner‚ôĒ40‚ôĒLast spring, my bookkeeper Sarah needed records from her rheumatologist to share with a new specialist, and it took three weeks and two phone calls just to get a CD in the mail‚ÄĒmeanwhile her treatment was on hold. I was furious on her behalf, but honestly relieved our small business health plan at least gave her options. Now I'm reading that some of these barriers might come down, which sounds great until I realize the same rules loosening those barriers could let our plan's administrator sell her data to anyone willing to pay.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ399
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0043‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDonna Jackson‚ôĒHealthcare Leadership Council‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Healthcare Leadership Council opposes removing Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements for AI model transparency and disclosure, arguing that transparency documentation is essential for patient safety and equity without...‚ôĒJune 16, 2025‚Źé‚ŹéOffice of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology‚ŹéU.S. Department of Health and Human Services‚Źé200 Independence Avenue, S.W.‚ŹéWashington, D.C. 20201‚Źé‚ŹéRe: RFI on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity‚Źé‚ŹéThe Healthcare Leadership Council, on behalf of our membership and the communities we serve, appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory framework. We represent health systems, provider networks, and community health organizations across the country, and we work directly with patients and families navigating one of the most complex information ecosystems in the American healthcare system.‚Źé‚ŹéWe support regulatory reform where it reduces unnecessary burden and enables genuine innovation. But we have significant concerns about the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria, specifically the requirement for AI model cards and transparency disclosures. This is not a call for regulatory maximalism. This is a call for proportionality.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PROBLEM WE'RE TRYING TO SOLVE‚ÄĒAND THE ONE WE MIGHT CREATE**‚Źé‚ŹéLast year, our organization worked with a patient whose mother suffered a stroke in March. The family is Latinx, primarily Spanish-speaking, and they spent four weeks trying to move medical records between a hospital and an outpatient rehabilitation clinic thirty miles away. The records got stuck in faxes that went nowhere. Requests were lost. Someone told them the hospital had "different systems" and couldn't transfer the information electronically. What looked like a technical problem was, from the family's perspective, a wall. I understand why we want to remove barriers to data flow. I genuinely do.‚Źé‚ŹéBut I also remember a conversation I had last fall with a privacy advocate whose teenage daughter's mental health records were leaked to her school without proper consent. The daughter is seventeen. The records included notes about suicidal ideation. The school counselor saw them, the school nurse saw them, and by lunch period the shame had spread through her peer group. The daughter didn't speak to her mother for three months. She's back in therapy now, but the trust in her original provider is gone. Her mother told me: "I would have given anything for there to be more friction in that system. More questions. More checkpoints. More proof that someone was paying attention."‚Źé‚ŹéThose are the two truths we're holding. Data portability is essential. Patients should not be trapped by information blocking. But transparency requirements for clinical AI systems are not the same thing as friction. They are the opposite. They are the mechanism by which we ensure that when data moves, and when AI systems make recommendations about care, someone is actually looking at what's happening.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT THE DSI CERTIFICATION CRITERION ACTUALLY DOES**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current requirement under 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1) and its associated guidance documents requires that health IT systems incorporating Decision Support Interventions include model cards, transparency disclosures, and documentation of intended use, target population, known risks, and training data characteristics. This is not new regulation. The rule has been in place since the 2015 Edition Health IT Certification Criteria (81 FR 1494). Developers have had a decade to implement these requirements. The infrastructure exists.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat the criterion does is require that developers document what their system actually does. Not in marketing language. In technical language. Who was this system trained on? What population was used to validate the model? What are the known failure modes? What is the system intended to do, and what should it not be used for? A cardiologist using an AI system to flag potential atrial fibrillation needs to know whether that system was trained predominantly on data from patients over sixty, or whether it includes validation data from younger populations with different presentations. A primary care physician using a system to recommend screening thresholds needs to know if the training data included diverse socioeconomic populations, or if the recommendations might be biased toward higher-income cohorts.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not theoretical. The literature is clear on this point. A 2023 study published in JAMA Network Open found that commercial AI systems used in radiology were trained on datasets that were 75 percent male, skewing their sensitivity for conditions that present differently in women (Gianfrancesco et al., JAMA Netw Open. 2023;6(7):e2326340). Without transparency about training data, a radiologist using that system has no way to know they should be more cautious with female patients. The certification criterion doesn't prevent the use of that system. It requires that the developer disclose the limitation so the clinician can account for it.‚Źé‚ŹéWe are not asking for perfection. We are asking for transparency. There is a difference.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THIS MATTERS FOR PATIENT SAFETY AND EQUITY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe concern we hear from the deregulation proposal is that certification criteria create compliance costs and slow innovation. We understand that argument. We also know that it's incomplete. The argument assumes that removing requirements will speed innovation in a direction that benefits patients. But innovation without accountability can move in any direction. It can move toward systems that improve care. It can also move toward systems that are cheaper to deploy because they were trained on narrower datasets. It can move toward systems that are optimized for administrative efficiency rather than clinical accuracy. Without a requirement to document and disclose what the system actually does, the market pressure goes toward speed and cost, not transparency.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider what happened in the criminal justice context when risk assessment algorithms were deployed without transparency requirements. ProPublica's 2016 investigation of the COMPAS algorithm found it had significantly higher false positive rates for Black defendants than white defendants, yet the algorithm's developers refused to disclose their training methodology or validation data on the grounds that it was proprietary (ProPublica, Machine Bias, 2016). Without transparency requirements, that system continued to be used in courts across the country for years before the bias was exposed by independent researchers. The same dynamic exists in healthcare. Without transparency requirements, a biased clinical AI system can spread through multiple health systems before anyone realizes it's systematically misidentifying conditions in certain populations.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) and the Health Information Technology for Economic and Clinical Health Act (HITECH Act) established privacy and security requirements for health information (42 U.S.C. 1320d, et seq.). Those requirements didn't prevent innovation or reduce access. They created a framework within which innovation happens responsibly. The DSI certification criterion works the same way. It doesn't prevent AI systems from being used in clinical settings. It requires that they be transparent about what they do and what their limitations are.‚Źé‚Źé**OUR RECOMMENDATION: RETAIN THE DSI CERTIFICATION CRITERION WITH CLARIFICATION**‚Źé‚ŹéWe recommend that the final rule retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion as currently written in 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1). If there are aspects of the criterion that create genuine compliance challenges, those should be clarified, not removed. We offer the following specific suggestions:‚Źé‚Źé- The model card documentation should be permitted in any format that meets the transparency intent, not a single mandated template. Developers have flexibility in how they present the information, but the required elements (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) must be present and accessible to clinicians.‚Źé‚Źé- For health IT systems that incorporate multiple DSI components, the requirement should allow for consolidated documentation rather than separate model cards for each component, provided that the clinician can still access the relevant information for each clinical use case.‚Źé‚Źé- The validation data requirement should specify that external validation means validation on data that was not used in model development or internal testing, but should not require validation in every possible population. Documentation of populations where validation was not performed is sufficient, provided the developer discloses this gap.‚Źé‚Źé- The timeline for compliance should remain as currently structured, with flexibility for developers who can demonstrate good-faith efforts to meet the requirement but face technical challenges. This is not a request to eliminate the requirement. It is a request to structure implementation in a way that accounts for the real complexity of health IT system architecture.‚Źé‚ŹéThese clarifications would reduce compliance burden without eliminating the transparency that clinicians need to use these systems safely. They also align with how other healthcare regulations actually work. The FDA requires transparency about device performance, but allows flexibility in how that transparency is demonstrated, provided the information is accurate and clinically relevant (21 CFR 807.87).‚Źé‚Źé**THE BROADER QUESTION ABOUT DEREGULATION AND MARKET DYNAMICS**‚Źé‚ŹéWe want to be direct about something that gets lost in the technical conversation about certification criteria. The argument for deregulation assumes that removing requirements will result in better outcomes because markets will self-correct. If a health IT system is biased or unreliable, the argument goes, providers won't use it, so developers won't make it, so the market will naturally select for quality.‚Źé‚ŹéThat is not how healthcare markets work. Providers adopt health IT systems based on cost, integration with existing infrastructure, vendor relationships, and administrative requirements. Clinicians often have limited input into those decisions. Patients have almost no input. A biased AI system that is 20 percent cheaper than an unbiased alternative will get adopted in under-resourced health systems serving low-income populations. That's not market failure. That's market logic. The market will optimize for the buyer's constraints, not the patient's safety.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is why we have health IT certification criteria in the first place. The 21st Century Cures Act and the subsequent ONC Health IT Certification Program were designed to address exactly this problem: ensuring that health IT systems meet minimum standards for safety, security, and interoperability, regardless of market pressures (42 U.S.C. 300jj et seq.). The DSI criterion is part of that framework. Removing it because compliance costs money is like removing building codes because construction is expensive. The cost exists because we decided that safety matters.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE WOULD LOSE**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the DSI certification criterion is removed, here is what changes. Health IT developers will no longer be required to document what their AI systems actually do. A clinician will install a system and know only what the vendor tells them in marketing materials. They will not know the population used for training. They will not know the known failure modes. They will not know whether the system was externally validated on diverse populations, or validated only on the developer's internal test set.‚Źé‚ŹéThis creates liability problems for providers. If a system fails in a way that the developer knew about but did not disclose, the provider is exposed to malpractice liability for using a system without understanding its limitations. But more importantly, it creates patient safety problems. The patient whose records are leaked is harmed. The patient whose condition is missed because the AI system was trained on a population that doesn't match theirs is harmed. The patient whose care is delayed because the AI system recommended an inappropriate intervention is harmed.‚Źé‚ŹéWe have not seen evidence that the DSI certification criterion is preventing innovation. We have seen evidence that it is enabling informed clinical decision-making. Those are not the same thing, and we should not confuse them.‚Źé‚Źé**CLARIFICATION ON INFORMATION BLOCKING AND DATA PORTABILITY**‚Źé‚ŹéWe want to be clear that we are not opposing the broader deregulatory goals around information blocking and data portability. The information blocking regulations under 45 CFR 171 have created some unnecessary friction in legitimate clinical workflows. There are cases where providers should be able to share information more freely without the current documentation burden. We support streamlining those processes where the streamlining actually reduces burden without reducing transparency.‚Źé‚ŹéBut the DSI certification criterion is not an information blocking regulation. It is not preventing data portability. It is a transparency requirement for clinical decision support systems. Removing it would not enable any additional data sharing. It would simply remove the requirement that developers document what their systems do. Those are separate policy questions, and they should not be conflated.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Healthcare Leadership Council has worked with thousands of patients and families navigating the healthcare system. We have seen what happens when information flows freely and what happens when it gets blocked. We have also seen what happens when clinical tools lack transparency. Both matter. The goal is not to maximize flow at the expense of safety, and it is not to maximize transparency at the expense of access. The goal is to find the right balance.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criterion, as currently written, represents a reasonable balance. It requires transparency without preventing innovation. It enables informed clinical decision-making without blocking data sharing. We recommend that it be retained, with the clarifications we have outlined above.‚Źé‚ŹéWe are available to discuss these recommendations with ONC staff and appreciate the opportunity to contribute to this important conversation.‚Źé‚ŹéSincerely,‚Źé‚ŹéDonna Jackson‚ŹéCommunity Organizer‚ŹéHealthcare Leadership Council‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ7‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒconcerned‚ôĒDelaware‚ôĒcommunity organizer‚ôĒ46‚ôĒLast spring, I was helping a woman from our Latinx outreach program navigate her mother's care after a stroke, and we hit a wall trying to get her medical records transferred between providers‚ÄĒthe friction was clearly intentional, designed to lock families in. But when I think about removing those guardrails entirely, I keep coming back to a different conversation I had with a privacy advocate whose teenage daughter's mental health records were leaked to her school without consent, and the shame nearly cost them their relationship. We need real data portability, absolutely, but not if it means trading one kind of entrapment for exposure that could follow someone for life.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2118

CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0045‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDeborah Johnson‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA former nurse argues that transparency requirements for AI systems in healthcare are essential safeguards that enable doctors and patients to understand how treatment recommendations are made and catch potential errors, warning that removing thes...‚ôĒLast spring, my daughter had to switch rheumatologists because her new doctor had more experience with lupus. The old office kept saying their system was "incompatible" with the new one, and it took six weeks to get her records transferred. By then she'd already repeated labs and missed a month of treatment planning. I kept thinking how much better things would have gone if information could move as easily as she could pick up the phone. I spent thirty years as a nurse, and that experience taught me something: the gaps in information are where mistakes happen.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm concerned about removing transparency requirements for AI systems used in healthcare. When doctors can't see how an AI model was trained, what data it learned from, or where it might fail, they're making decisions blind. In my daughter's case, if a lupus algorithm had been recommending her treatment plan and neither her doctor nor she understood how that system worked or what it was trained on, how would they know whether to trust it? How would they catch an error? Transparency isn't red tape. It's the difference between a doctor who can make an informed choice about whether to follow a recommendation and a doctor who's just guessing.‚Źé‚ŹéPatients and healthcare providers need to work together, and that only happens when everyone understands what's influencing the decisions being made. Stripping away those safeguards would repeat the same mistake that delayed my daughter's care, except at a scale that affects thousands of vulnerable people. The right approach to innovation includes keeping the guardrails that let us ask questions and get second opinions. That's not slowing things down. That's healthcare done right.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-20T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-20T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒmedium‚ôĒsupportive‚ôĒMaryland‚ôĒretired nurse‚ôĒ41‚ôĒLast spring, my daughter switched rheumatologists because her new doctor was supposedly better with lupus cases, but it took six weeks to get her old records transferred‚ÄĒthe first office kept saying the system was "incompatible." CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0046‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒBetty Clark‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA social worker urges CMS to retain Decision Support Interventions certification requirements for AI transparency and model documentation, arguing that removing these safeguards would leave vulnerable patients without protection against algorithmi...‚ôĒRe: CMS-0042-NC, Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions‚Źé‚ŹéWorking in social work for the past eight years, I've watched the disconnect between what healthcare systems say they're doing with patient data and what actually happens on the ground. Last month, a client of mine discovered that her psychiatric history had been shared with her employer's HR department. When I asked the hospital about consent, the documentation was vague at best. She never agreed to that disclosure. That same client's medical records took two weeks to transfer to a specialist three hours away, but somehow her sensitive mental health information moved somewhere in days. The speed of one kind of sharing and the opacity of another is what keeps me up at night about this proposed rule.‚Źé‚ŹéI understand the appeal of deregulation. Healthcare systems are drowning in administrative burden, and I get that. But I'm deeply concerned about removing the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion that requires AI model cards and transparency disclosures. These aren't bureaucratic obstacles. They're the only real protection patients have when an algorithm is making or influencing their care decisions. A model card should document intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. These specifics matter because algorithms can fail in ways that are invisible to patients. An AI trained mostly on data from one demographic group might recommend different treatments to someone from a different background without anyone noticing.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what I'm asking for: keep the DSI certification requirements intact. Don't remove them in the name of innovation. I've seen what happens when systems prioritize speed over safeguards, and it's the people with the least power who get hurt. The patients I work with are already vulnerable. They're navigating complex medical systems while managing trauma, poverty, or cognitive impairment. They're not in a position to audit algorithms or demand transparency. That's supposed to be the government's job.‚Źé‚ŹéMy specific concerns are straightforward. First, without model transparency requirements, clinicians have no way to understand why an AI tool is recommending what it's recommending. Second, patients have no meaningful way to know what data trained these systems or whether the system was even tested on people like them. Third, there's no accountability mechanism if something goes wrong. If a tool harms someone, how do they know? How do we know?‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not against technology. I'm against the idea that progress means removing the guardrails before we've figured out how to keep people safe. The current DSI certification criterion exists because we learned hard lessons about what happens without it. Please don't erase that learning.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-21T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-21T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe agency's claim that 'no publicly available evidence' supports transparency requirements conflates absence of evidence with evidence of absence; lack of published studies does not mean the criterion provides no value.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒconcerned‚ôĒWisconsin‚ôĒsocial worker‚ôĒ42‚ôĒLast month, I was helping a client navigate care after her daughter's hospitalization, and she needed her records transferred to a specialist three hours away‚ÄĒit took two weeks of phone calls between facilities before we got them. But what worried me more was discovering that the same hospital had shared her psychiatric history with her employer's HR department without clear consent documentation. If moving records becomes easier but the guardrails disappear, I'm terrified families like hers will find their most sensitive information circulating places they never agreed to.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ434
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0047‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒNancy Davis‚ôĒThe Pharmacy Health Information Technology Collaborative‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Pharmacy Health Information Technology Collaborative opposes removing Decision Support Intervention (DSI) certification requirements and AI model card disclosure standards, arguing that while regulatory streamlining is appropriate, transparenc...‚ôĒ**The Pharmacy Health Information Technology Collaborative Comments on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Pharmacy Health Information Technology Collaborative appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory actions outlined by the Assistant Secretary for Technology Policy and the Office of the National Coordinator for Health IT. Our membership includes pharmacy benefit managers, clinical informatics experts, health economists, and technology developers across the medication management ecosystem. We have reviewed the proposed changes with particular attention to Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements and the potential removal of model card and transparency disclosure standards for artificial intelligence in healthcare settings.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Collaborative supports a balanced regulatory approach that genuinely reduces unnecessary burden while preserving safeguards essential to patient protection and clinical accountability. We are concerned that the current proposal, as drafted, conflates two distinct regulatory problems: outdated compliance infrastructure that impedes innovation, and foundational transparency requirements that enable safe AI deployment. Removing DSI certification criteria entirely, particularly the model card and disclosure requirements, would address neither problem effectively and would instead create new risks that fall disproportionately on patients and clinicians least equipped to evaluate algorithmic reliability.‚Źé‚Źé**Understanding the Problem We Actually Have**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Collaborative's perspective on this issue is grounded in concrete experience with medication therapy management across competing pharmacy benefit manager platforms. Last year, our team analyzed claims data for a Louisville hospital system and discovered something that troubled us immediately: three separate pharmacy benefit managers were each running drug interaction screening on the same patients through incompatible systems. Same patients. Different platforms. No shared data. This created duplicate work, redundant alerts that clinicians learned to ignore, and blind spots where a genuine interaction might slip through because the prescriber was looking at an incomplete medication history.‚Źé‚ŹéThat discovery shaped how we think about regulation in this space. The fragmentation wasn't protecting anyone. It was protecting market position. But the answer wasn't to eliminate all rules. The answer was smarter rules that forced interoperability while allowing competition to drive innovation in how systems actually functioned. We need to know what medication history we're missing, and we need vendors to be accountable for the answers their algorithms give us.‚Źé‚ŹéThis same logic applies directly to AI-assisted clinical decision support. When a pharmacy benefit manager's algorithm recommends against a medication fill, or when a hospital's medication interaction checker suggests a particular drug combination is contraindicated, clinicians need to understand what assumptions and data shaped that recommendation. They need to know if the algorithm was trained on a population that resembles their patient, what edge cases the developers already know about, and what validation studies actually tested the tool's performance.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification framework, established under 45 CFR Part 170, requires AI model cards that disclose intended use, target population characteristics, known risks, training data sources, and external validation results. This is not bureaucratic overhead. It is the documentation that allows clinicians to make informed decisions about whether to override, accept, or seek human review of an algorithmic recommendation. Removing this requirement would not accelerate beneficial innovation. It would accelerate opacity.‚Źé‚Źé**The Distinction Between Outdated Compliance and Necessary Transparency**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Collaborative recognizes that health IT certification processes have accumulated regulatory cruft. Certain documentation requirements are genuinely redundant. Some compliance pathways require vendors to submit identical information to multiple agencies. Some standards mandates no longer reflect how data actually flows across modern health systems. These are legitimate targets for streamlining, and we support ONC and CMS in identifying and removing them.‚Źé‚ŹéBut transparency about AI behavior is not compliance cruft. It is foundational infrastructure for safe clinical deployment.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider the distinction carefully. A vendor might reasonably argue that being required to certify compliance with three different standards bodies for the same data exchange format is wasteful. The Collaborative agrees. ONC should consolidate that requirement. But a vendor arguing that patients should not know what data trained their medication recommendation algorithm is making a different claim entirely. They are arguing that clinical accountability is optional if it creates friction in the sales process.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Federal Register notice accompanying the proposed rule changes suggests that DSI certification requirements create barriers to market entry and slow innovation cycles (90 FR 4521). The Collaborative has reviewed the supporting analysis and finds the evidence for this claim thin. The actual barrier to entry in the health IT market is not model card documentation. It is integration complexity, regulatory fragmentation across state pharmacy boards, and the consolidated market power of large PBMs that make it difficult for new vendors to achieve scale. Removing transparency requirements will not solve any of those problems.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat it will do is create information asymmetry between vendors and clinicians. Vendors will know exactly what their algorithms do, under what conditions they work well, and where they fail. Clinicians will be left guessing, or will be forced to run parallel validation studies on every new tool they consider adopting. That is not innovation. That is externalized risk.‚Źé‚Źé**Real-World Experience with Algorithmic Accountability**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Collaborative's concerns are not theoretical. We have consulted with pharmacy directors and clinical informaticists at health systems trying to implement AI-assisted medication management tools. The conversation almost always follows a pattern. The vendor provides marketing materials highlighting accuracy metrics. When clinicians ask what population those metrics were tested on, the vendor becomes vague. When they ask what drug-drug interactions the algorithm is known to miss, the vendor discusses "edge cases" without specificity. When they ask whether the tool was validated on patients with renal impairment or hepatic dysfunction, the vendor suggests running a local pilot study.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not how evidence-based medicine works. We do not deploy cardiac drugs without knowing how they perform in heart failure populations. We do not use antibiotics without understanding resistance patterns. We should not be deploying clinical decision support algorithms without understanding their limitations and validation performance across relevant patient populations.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule changes would make this problem worse. Under the current DSI certification framework codified at 45 CFR 170.315(a)(4), vendors must disclose model development processes, training data characteristics, and external validation results. Health systems can use this information to make informed implementation decisions. Removing the requirement does not free vendors to innovate. It frees them to avoid accountability.‚Źé‚Źé**What the Literature Actually Shows About AI Safety in Healthcare**‚Źé‚ŹéRecent peer-reviewed studies on AI deployment in healthcare settings provide relevant context. A 2023 systematic review in Nature Medicine found that algorithmic decision support tools show significant performance degradation when applied to patient populations different from their training cohorts (Gianfrancesco et al., Nature Medicine 2023). A 2022 analysis in JAMA Internal Medicine documented that clinicians using AI-assisted diagnostic tools without access to model documentation showed higher rates of inappropriate override of algorithmic recommendations, suggesting that lack of transparency led to either false confidence or inappropriate skepticism (Rajkomar et al., JAMA Intern Med 2022).‚Źé‚ŹéThe FDA's 2023 guidance on AI/ML-based Software as a Medical Device (FDA-2023-D-2976) explicitly recommends that manufacturers provide detailed documentation of algorithm performance across demographic and clinical subgroups. This is not coming from ONC alone. This is coming from the agency responsible for drug and device safety, and it reflects a consensus that transparency about algorithmic limitations is essential to safe clinical deployment.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Collaborative understands the impulse behind the proposed deregulation. Certification processes should be efficient. Redundant standards should be eliminated. But the solution to regulatory inefficiency is not to eliminate requirements that serve a patient safety function. The solution is to streamline the process of meeting those requirements.‚Źé‚Źé**A More Targeted Approach to Deregulation**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Pharmacy Health Information Technology Collaborative proposes a more surgical approach to the deregulatory goals outlined in the proposed rule. Rather than removing DSI certification requirements entirely, ONC should:‚Źé‚Źé1. Streamline the submission process for AI model cards by establishing a standardized template aligned with emerging industry frameworks like the Model Card for Model Reporting (Mitchell et al., 2019, ArXiv) and the NIST AI Risk Management Framework (NIST AI 600-1, 2023). This reduces compliance burden without eliminating accountability.‚Źé‚Źé2. Create a tiered certification pathway based on algorithmic risk level. Lower-risk decision support tools (those that provide filtered information displays or alerts without blocking clinical action) might require less extensive validation documentation than higher-risk tools (those that automatically deny coverage or block medication dispensing). This allows vendors to move faster when safety risk is genuinely lower.‚Źé‚Źé3. Consolidate DSI certification requirements across CMS, ONC, and state pharmacy boards into a single submission process with mutual recognition. The current fragmentation creates duplicate work. Eliminating it would reduce burden without reducing transparency.‚Źé‚Źé4. Establish a public registry of AI model cards for decision support tools used in pharmacy benefit management. This allows health systems to compare tools transparently and creates market incentives for vendors to invest in robust validation. Transparency becomes competitive advantage rather than compliance burden.‚Źé‚Źé5. Require vendors to disclose material changes to algorithm training data or performance characteristics within 30 days, similar to FDA adverse event reporting requirements. This creates ongoing accountability without requiring recertification every time a vendor updates their model.‚Źé‚ŹéThese approaches would address the stated regulatory burden while preserving the transparency infrastructure that makes safe clinical deployment possible. They recognize that innovation and accountability are not opposing values. They are complementary.‚Źé‚Źé**The Specific Risk of Removing DSI Model Card Requirements**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Collaborative wants to be explicit about why removing the model card disclosure requirement specifically is problematic. A clinical decision support tool's model card contains information that clinicians need to use the tool responsibly:‚Źé‚Źé- **Intended use specification**: What clinical decision is the tool designed to support? If a vendor says their tool is designed to identify medication interactions but it was actually trained to predict insurance denials, that is a fundamental mismatch that changes how clinicians should interpret its recommendations.‚Źé‚Źé- **Target population characteristics**: What demographic and clinical groups was the algorithm trained on? If a medication interaction algorithm was trained on a predominantly white, Medicare population, clinicians managing a younger, more diverse population need to know that their patients fall outside the tool's validation cohort.‚Źé‚Źé- **Known limitations and failure modes**: What types of interactions does the algorithm miss? What comorbidities confound its recommendations? What medication classes has it been tested on, and which have been excluded from testing?‚Źé‚Źé- **Training data sources and characteristics**: What medication claims database was used to train the algorithm? How recent is the data? Does it reflect current prescribing patterns? If the training data is five years old, clinicians need to know that the tool may not capture newer drug interactions discovered since training.‚Źé‚Źé- **External validation results**: Has an independent research team tested the algorithm on data not used in training? What were the results? If external validation shows lower performance than vendor-reported metrics, that discrepancy is critical for clinicians to understand.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving the requirement to disclose this information does not accelerate innovation. It accelerates deployment of tools that clinicians do not fully understand, in healthcare systems that cannot adequately evaluate their performance, for patient populations that may fall outside their validation scope. That is a recipe for harm.‚Źé‚Źé**Addressing the Competitive Concerns**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Collaborative recognizes that vendors raise legitimate concerns about competitive advantage. If Model A's vendor discloses that their algorithm was trained on 10 million medication claims from a specific PBM, while Model B's vendor refuses to disclose training data sources, Model A appears at a disadvantage because proprietary information has been revealed.‚Źé‚ŹéThis concern is real. It is also solvable without eliminating transparency requirements. ONC could allow vendors to submit model cards under confidential treatment, with public disclosure limited to essential safety information (intended use, target population, known limitations) while protecting proprietary details about training methodology and data sources. Similar approaches have been used successfully in FDA's 505(b)(2) pathway and in FTC review of trade secret claims.‚Źé‚ŹéThe key insight is that clinicians do not need to know a vendor's exact training methodology to use their tool responsibly. They need to know whether their patient population resembles the tool's validation cohort, and what limitations the developers have already identified. That information can be disclosed while protecting legitimate business interests.‚Źé‚Źé**Implementation Concerns with Full Deregulation**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Collaborative is also concerned about the implementation timeline for the proposed changes. The notice indicates that DSI certification requirements would be removed effective immediately upon final rule publication. This creates a cliff where tools currently in the market under certification requirements would suddenly be operating in an unregulated environment, with no transition period for health systems to assess implications.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the final rule proceeds with DSI deregulation, the Collaborative respectfully requests that ONC establish a transition period of at least 18 months, during which vendors could continue to provide model card documentation voluntarily, and health systems could use that period to evaluate tools for continued use. This would allow market-driven reassessment without creating a sudden regulatory gap.‚Źé‚Źé**What We Are Actually Recommending**‚Źé‚ŹéTo be direct: The Pharmacy Health Information Technology Collaborative recommends that ONC retain DSI certification requirements with the modifications outlined above. We support streamlining the certification process, consolidating redundant standards, and creating tiered pathways based on risk level. We do not support removing the requirement that vendors disclose how their algorithms work and what they have been validated to do.‚Źé‚ŹéThis position reflects our experience in the medication management ecosystem. We have seen what happens when multiple vendors operate without shared data standards. We have seen what happens when clinicians do not understand the limitations of the tools they are using. We have seen medication errors that resulted from algorithmic recommendations that clinicians could not adequately evaluate because they lacked information about the algorithm's training and validation.‚Źé‚ŹéRegulation in healthcare is not inherently good or bad. It is good when it prevents harm. It is bad when it creates burden without corresponding safety benefit. The DSI model card requirement falls in the first category.‚Źé‚Źé**Conclusion**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Pharmacy Health Information Technology Collaborative appreciates ONC and CMS for seeking input on how to reduce regulatory burden while promoting innovation. The goal is right. The proposed approach is blunt. We have offered more targeted alternatives that would reduce burden on vendors while preserving transparency that serves patient safety. We stand ready to work with ONC on implementation of a modified deregulatory approach that achieves both objectives.‚Źé‚ŹéThe medication management ecosystem is ready for innovation. Clinicians want better tools. Vendors want to compete. But that competition will be most beneficial when it happens on the basis of genuine performance differences that clinicians can evaluate, not on the basis of opacity that favors vendors over users. Retaining DSI model card requirements, while streamlining the certification process, creates exactly that environment.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-06T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-06T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ8‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒacademic‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒhopeful‚ôĒKentucky‚ôĒhealth economist‚ôĒ31‚ôĒWhen I was analyzing claims data for a hospital system in Louisville last year, I discovered that three competing pharmacy benefit managers were each maintaining separate patient medication histories‚ÄĒwe're talking about the same patients being duplicatively screened for drug interactions across incompatible systems. It struck me then that the regulatory fragmentation forcing these silos wasn't protecting anyone; it was just creating blind spots where real harm could happen, and I've been convinced ever since that we need smarter rules, not fewer rules, if we're going to unlock the kind of interoperability that actually serves patients.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2443
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0048‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDonna Rodriguez‚ôĒXSELL Technologies‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA small rural health IT vendor argues that removing transparency and model card requirements from ONC certification will harm smaller competitors by allowing well-resourced vendors to operate without clear standards, ultimately reducing market com...‚ôĒ**RE: Deregulation of Health IT Certification ‚Äď Transparency Requirements Must Remain**‚Źé‚ŹéLast year our team spent six months and nearly $200,000 getting our EHR module through ONC certification under 45 CFR Part 170. We're a 40-person shop serving smaller rural clinics across Texas. During that process, I watched a competitor with a budget ten times ours roll out functionally identical capabilities in half the time. The difference wasn't innovation or better engineering. It was that they could afford compliance consultants and navigated the regulatory pathway faster because they had resources we didn't. What struck me then, and what worries me now about these proposed removals, is that stripping out model cards and transparency disclosures won't level the playing field for smaller vendors like us. It will do the opposite.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PARADOX OF DEREGULATION FOR SMALLER VENDORS**‚Źé‚ŹéI hear the argument that model cards and transparency documentation add unnecessary burden. But I've lived on both sides of that burden. Those requirements exist because providers and health IT directors like myself need a way to verify what we're actually deploying in clinical settings. When a vendor won't disclose how their AI system makes clinical decisions, that's not a compliance inconvenience. That's a fiduciary risk. My clinic directors have a duty to patients to understand the tools they're using, and right now, model transparency is the only accountability mechanism that lets us do that.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what actually happens when you remove standardized certification criteria: the opacity increases, not decreases. Vendors with massive compliance budgets can afford to hire armies of lawyers and engineers to prove their systems work in the absence of clear standards. Smaller innovators like us get locked out because we can't navigate undefined pathways. The vendors who benefit most are the ones with the deepest pockets, not the ones building better products for underserved clinics. You end up with more consolidation, not less.‚Źé‚ŹéPatient safety has to come first. Removing transparency requirements under the banner of innovation just shifts the risk from vendors onto providers and patients who have no way to make informed decisions. The certification burden I'm concerned about isn't documentation. It's the burden of operating in a market where only the biggest players can afford opacity.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-01T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-01T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒmedium‚ôĒangry‚ôĒTexas‚ôĒhealth IT director‚ôĒ38‚ôĒLast year I spent six months and nearly $200K getting our EHR module through certification, only to watch a competitor with ten times our budget roll out basically the same functionality in half the time because they could afford the compliance consultants we couldn't. My team was furious‚ÄĒwe're the ones actually innovating for smaller clinics, but the certification process made it impossible for us to compete fairly.‚ôĒRemoving model cards and transparency disclosures eliminates the accountability mechanism that allows health IT directors like myself to actually verify what we're deploying in clinical settings‚ÄĒand that's dangerous when patient safety is on the line. | Smaller health IT vendors and innovative clinics depend on clear, standardized certification criteria to compete fairly; without model cards, only vendors with massive compliance budgets can navigate the opacity, which actually *increases* consolidation rather than reducing burden. | Healthcare providers have a fiduciary duty to patients to understand how AI systems work before deployment. Stripping transparency requirements doesn't reduce burden‚ÄĒit shifts risk from vendors onto providers and patients who have no way to make informed decisions.‚ôĒDonna frames transparency requirements as a necessary guardrail that protects smaller innovators and clinicians from being locked into black-box systems controlled by well-funded competitors.‚ôĒONC Health IT Certification Program requirements (45 CFR Part 170) on model transparency and validation documentation‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ369
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0049‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒMatthew Nelson‚ôĒVerve, LLC‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒVerve, LLC opposes removing the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion requiring AI model transparency and disclosure, arguing that while some regulatory streamlining is justified, transparency requirements serve critical patient s...‚ôĒRe: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity‚Źé‚ŹéVerve, LLC appreciates the opportunity to respond to the proposed deregulatory framework. Our organization operates as a healthcare consulting and technology implementation firm supporting hospital systems across the Pacific Northwest, and we work directly with CFOs, IT directors, and clinical leadership navigating the intersection of innovation, patient safety, and regulatory compliance. The experiences I've had over the past five years in my role at a 400-bed teaching hospital have given me a front-row seat to how certification requirements affect not just vendor selection, but what kinds of solutions actually make it to market in the first place.‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be direct about something that concerns me in the current proposal. The suggested rollback of certification criteria for Decision Support Interventions, particularly the requirements around AI model transparency and disclosure, moves in a direction I think carries real risk. This isn't a blanket argument against deregulation. I genuinely believe some of the current certification burdens are counterproductive, and I've watched smaller vendors price themselves out of the market because of compliance costs they simply can't absorb. But there's a difference between streamlining approval processes and removing safeguards that actually protect both patients and providers. The DSI certification criterion requiring model cards, intended use documentation, target population specifications, known risks, and training data characteristics isn't bureaucratic overhead‚ÄĒit's foundational to responsible AI deployment in clinical settings.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PROBLEM WE ACTUALLY FACE**‚Źé‚ŹéLet me start with the situation that brought this into sharp focus for our organization. Last spring, our orthopedic surgery department identified a documentation system from a smaller vendor that could integrate directly with our physical therapy workflow in ways our current certified EHR simply couldn't manage. The vendor had built something genuinely innovative. Their system used natural language processing to extract relevant clinical data from surgical notes and auto-populate therapy intake forms, reducing data entry time by roughly 60 percent based on their pilot data. The workflow integration was elegant. Our department heads were excited about it for the first time in years.‚Źé‚ŹéBut when we ran the numbers on implementation, the certification and interoperability requirements made a pilot prohibitively expensive. The vendor would have needed to pursue full ONC certification, which meant investing in model validation studies, external testing, documentation of training data characteristics, and third-party audits. For a company with eight developers and two clinical staff, that was somewhere north of $400,000 and eighteen months of delay. We couldn't ask them to take that on for a 400-bed hospital pilot. So instead, we spent $180,000 on workarounds‚ÄĒcustom API development, manual data mapping, middleware solutions that were clunky and expensive but didn't trigger certification requirements because they weren't making clinical decisions.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what bothers me about that outcome: we ended up with a more expensive, less integrated solution that actually introduced more manual steps into clinical workflows. The vendor's AI-driven system would have been cleaner and probably safer because it was designed as a system rather than patched together. But the certification burden didn't push us toward better solutions; it pushed us toward more fragmented ones.‚Źé‚ŹéThat experience made me a believer in reducing unnecessary certification barriers. But it also made me acutely aware that some requirements exist for reasons that matter.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THE DSI CRITERION MATTERS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Decision Support Interventions certification criterion requiring AI model transparency is not an arbitrary check box. It exists because clinical decision support tools operate in an environment where the stakes are different from other software domains. When a recommendation algorithm suggests a dosing adjustment, recommends additional imaging, or flags a potential drug interaction, that recommendation gets embedded in clinical workflow. Providers are busy. They're making decisions under time pressure. They rely on decision support systems to be reliable in specific ways for specific populations.‚Źé‚ŹéThe requirement for model cards and transparency disclosures serves three distinct purposes, and I think each one is important enough to be worth preserving.‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, there's the patient safety piece. Model cards that document intended use, target population, known limitations, and training data characteristics give providers concrete information about whether a tool is appropriate for their patient population. This matters. An AI model trained primarily on data from academic medical centers may perform very differently on a patient population that's older, has more comorbidities, or has different demographic characteristics than the training set. A community hospital in rural Oregon can look at that documentation and make an informed decision about whether the tool is suitable for their patient base. Without that transparency, providers are flying blind. They're assuming a tool works broadly when it might work well for some patients and poorly for others.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, there's clinical accountability. When something goes wrong, providers and hospitals need to understand how a decision support tool made its recommendation. If a patient has an adverse event and there's a question about whether a clinical decision support recommendation contributed to that event, the model card and training data documentation provides the foundation for understanding what happened. It's not perfect accountability, but it's meaningful. It allows for honest investigation rather than just speculation. That matters for quality improvement and for malpractice defense, but more importantly, it matters for figuring out what actually went wrong so you don't repeat the mistake.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, there's informed clinical decision-making. This is the piece I keep coming back to. Providers need to know what they're trusting. If a recommendation comes from a model trained on a specific data set with known limitations, that context changes how a provider should weight that recommendation. A provider who knows a model was trained on 85 percent male patients and their patient is female might approach the recommendation differently than a provider who doesn't know that. The transparency isn't just regulatory compliance; it's information that should be part of the clinical decision-making process.‚Źé‚ŹéI acknowledge that this documentation requirement creates compliance costs, especially for smaller vendors. But the cost of removing this requirement isn't zero either. It's just invisible because it lands on the provider side, in the form of risk we're not managing well.‚Źé‚Źé**THE MARKET DYNAMICS PROBLEM**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal suggests that reducing certification burdens will increase vendor competition and drive innovation. I think that analysis is partially right and partially incomplete. Yes, lower barriers to entry will allow more vendors to enter the market. But there's a difference between market entry and market participation in clinical settings.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what I've observed: the vendors who can absorb certification costs are usually the larger ones. They have compliance teams, they have revenue to invest in regulatory requirements, they have enough customers to distribute those costs across. When you lower barriers for everyone, you might think that helps smaller vendors. And in some cases it does. But in healthcare specifically, the absence of third-party validation becomes a problem for provider procurement.‚Źé‚ŹéHospital IT committees have to answer for the decisions they make. If we adopt a tool that's certified by ONC, we can point to that certification as evidence of due diligence. If we adopt a tool that's not certified but complies with a lower regulatory threshold, we're making a different kind of bet. We're betting that our internal evaluation processes are sufficient. Many hospital systems will simply opt for the certified tools because they're easier to justify to compliance committees and to boards of directors. The regulation that feels burdensome to vendors can actually feel protective to providers.‚Źé‚ŹéThe result is that removing certification requirements doesn't necessarily create a more level playing field. It might create a two-tier market where large vendors have certified products, smaller vendors have non-certified products, and providers in risk-averse organizations stick with the certified options anyway. The burden gets transferred rather than eliminated.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE'RE ASKING FOR**‚Źé‚ŹéVerve's position is straightforward: retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion with its current transparency and disclosure requirements. I'm not arguing for expanding certification requirements. I'm arguing against removing one that serves a specific, important function.‚Źé‚ŹéThis isn't a request to maintain the status quo for the sake of status quo. If there are ways to streamline the certification process for DSI tools‚ÄĒif there are ways to reduce the time, cost, or complexity of demonstrating compliance with the model card requirement‚ÄĒthose changes should be considered. The goal should be to maintain the transparency requirement while reducing unnecessary process burden.‚Źé‚ŹéSome possibilities worth exploring:‚Źé‚Źé- Establish standardized templates for model cards that reduce documentation burden while ensuring essential information is captured. If vendors knew exactly what format was required and could use a standard template, the compliance cost would drop significantly.‚Źé- Allow third-party validation by health IT consulting firms or academic institutions rather than requiring a single certification pathway. This could create competitive pressure on the cost of validation while maintaining quality.‚Źé- Create a tiered approach where simpler decision support tools with lower-risk applications have reduced documentation requirements compared to high-risk tools. A system that flags potential typos in medication orders is different from a system recommending chemotherapy regimens.‚Źé- Establish a registry where model cards are published and accessible, allowing providers to make informed decisions without requiring each hospital to do individual validation work.‚Źé‚ŹéThe point is to separate the transparency requirement from the process requirement. The transparency is important. The specific process might be optimizable.‚Źé‚Źé**THE RISK OF THE ALTERNATIVE**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be honest about what concerns me if we move in the opposite direction. If we remove the DSI certification criterion and its transparency requirements, we're essentially saying that providers should evaluate AI decision support tools the same way they evaluate other software. But that's not quite right, because the stakes are different.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen we evaluate an EHR system, we look at functionality, usability, integration, cost, and vendor stability. Those are legitimate evaluation criteria. But we don't typically need to understand the mathematical properties of how the system makes decisions because EHRs mostly don't make decisions‚ÄĒthey record and display information. Decision support systems are different. They're making recommendations that influence clinical decisions.‚Źé‚ŹéWithout transparency requirements, what's the mechanism for providers to understand how a tool works? We're relying on vendor marketing, on our IT department's technical evaluation, and on post-implementation experience. That's weaker than it sounds.‚Źé‚ŹéVendor marketing isn't going to highlight limitations. IT departments don't have clinical expertise to evaluate whether a tool's performance is adequate for their patient population. Post-implementation experience is important, but it's also reactive. By the time you discover a problem, you've already deployed the tool, trained staff on it, and embedded it in workflows.‚Źé‚ŹéI've seen this dynamic play out with other health IT tools. A vendor makes claims about a system's capabilities. The hospital adopts it. Six months later, clinical staff find out the system doesn't work well in specific scenarios that weren't addressed in the implementation process. By then, you've invested in training, workflows have been built around it, and changing course is expensive. If there had been clearer upfront documentation about the system's limitations, that implementation would have gone differently.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC CONCERNS ABOUT THE PROPOSAL**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal language suggests that removing certification requirements will "unleash innovation and competition." I think there are real benefits to reducing unnecessary compliance burden. But I also think the framing understates the costs of removing transparency requirements.‚Źé‚ŹéOne thing that's missing from the proposal is discussion of how providers will make decisions about AI tools in a less-regulated environment. The assumption seems to be that market competition will drive quality and that providers will be able to evaluate tools adequately. But healthcare IT procurement isn't a perfectly functioning market. Providers don't have perfect information. Vendors have strong incentives to make their tools look better than they are. There's a significant information asymmetry.‚Źé‚ŹéThe FDA regulates medical devices because there's a recognition that patients and providers can't always evaluate medical products adequately on their own. They rely on expert review to identify risks that aren't obvious. That same logic applies to AI decision support systems. A hospital CFO can't look at a neural network and determine whether it will perform well on their patient population. That requires specialized expertise.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal mentions that providers will have post-market surveillance mechanisms and can report problems after they occur. That's true, but it's a weak form of oversight. Adverse events that are clearly caused by a software system get reported. Subtle performance degradation in specific patient populations doesn't. Systematic bias that affects a small percentage of decisions but compounds over time might not be detected for months or years.‚Źé‚Źé**THE INTEROPERABILITY ANGLE**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to acknowledge that interoperability and the ability to integrate diverse systems is genuinely important. Our orthopedic department's problem was fundamentally an interoperability problem. The vendor's system was better, but it couldn't talk to our EHR in a way that made integration feasible.‚Źé‚ŹéBut I don't think interoperability and transparency requirements are in conflict. A system can be transparent about how it makes decisions while also adhering to modern interoperability standards. In fact, transparency and interoperability are complementary. If a system's decision-making process is well-documented, it's actually easier to integrate it with other systems because the integration team understands what the system does and how it does it.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal seems to frame regulation as a zero-sum choice between innovation/interoperability on one side and transparency/safety on the other. I think that's a false dichotomy. You can have certification requirements that ensure transparency while also allowing flexibility in how vendors achieve interoperability. You can have model card requirements while also streamlining the process for creating and validating those cards.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT THIS MEANS FOR PATIENT SAFETY**‚Źé‚ŹéHere's where I think the proposal needs more explicit engagement. The framing emphasizes burden reduction and innovation, which are legitimate regulatory considerations. But there's no serious discussion of what happens if transparency requirements go away and an AI decision support system causes harm.‚Źé‚ŹéLet's say a hospital adopts an uncertified AI system. The system works well for most patients but performs poorly for a specific demographic group‚ÄĒsay, older patients or patients with certain comorbidities. This isn't discovered during implementation because the hospital's initial patient volumes in that specific group are small. Six months into deployment, the system has influenced thousands of clinical decisions. Then adverse events start accumulating in that population.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat happens next? The hospital needs to understand why the system failed. If there's a model card documenting the system's training data and performance characteristics, that's a starting point for investigation. If there's no such documentation, the investigation is much harder. The vendor might not cooperate. The hospital might not have access to information about how the system works. The investigation becomes adversarial rather than collaborative.‚Źé‚ŹéMore importantly, how does the field learn from the problem? If the issue is documented and shared‚ÄĒif there's a mechanism for other hospitals to understand what went wrong‚ÄĒthen other implementations can be modified or cancelled before similar harm occurs. If the information is proprietary and not shared, then each hospital that adopts the system has to discover the problem independently.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal mentions that providers will have recourse through existing liability mechanisms. That's true, but liability mechanisms are backward-looking. They compensate harm after it occurs. Transparency requirements are forward-looking. They prevent harm by ensuring that providers understand the tools they're deploying.‚Źé‚Źé**RECOMMENDATIONS FOR MODIFICATION**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the regulatory framework is going to move toward deregulation, I would strongly recommend that any changes to the DSI certification criterion preserve the core transparency requirements. Here's what I think should be non-negotiable:‚Źé‚Źé- Any AI decision support system should include documentation of its intended use, target population, known limitations, and training data characteristics. This documentation should be available to providers before implementation.‚Źé- External validation data should be required for systems making high-risk clinical recommendations. This doesn't necessarily mean ONC certification, but it does mean independent verification that the system performs adequately for the intended use.‚Źé- There should be a mechanism for reporting and sharing information about system performance problems and adverse events. This could be a formal registry or an informal network, but providers need to be able to learn from each other's experiences.‚Źé- Vendors should be required to document any changes to system performance or recommendations and to notify providers when significant changes are made.‚Źé‚ŹéThese requirements aren't trivial, but they're also not the entire certification burden. They're focused on transparency and accountability rather than process compliance. A vendor could potentially meet these requirements with less expense and time than current certification requires.‚Źé‚Źé**THE SMALLER VENDOR PERSPECTIVE**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to circle back to the smaller vendor problem because I think it's real and important, but I'm not convinced that removing transparency requirements is the right solution.‚Źé‚ŹéThe vendor we wanted to work with‚ÄĒthe one that built the workflow integration system‚ÄĒwasn't struggling because of model card requirements specifically. They were struggling because the total certification process was expensive and they didn't have the resources to navigate it. If we could streamline certification while preserving transparency, that vendor would be better served than if we removed transparency requirements but kept the rest of the process burdensome.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat would actually help smaller vendors is:‚Źé- Clearer guidance on what certification requires, so they can plan resources more accurately‚Źé- Standardized templates and tools for compliance documentation‚Źé- Reduced or tiered requirements based on risk level‚Źé- Access to subsidized or pro-bono help with certification from organizations with compliance expertise‚Źé‚ŹéThose changes would reduce burden without removing safeguards. They're harder to implement than blanket deregulation, but they solve the actual problem smaller vendors face.‚Źé‚Źé**IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the decision is made to preserve the DSI transparency requirements, there are some implementation questions worth addressing:‚Źé‚ŹéThe current model card requirement is based on FDA guidance for AI/ML systems in medical devices (FDA Proposed Modification to Software as a Medical Device Action Plan, 2021). The FDA's approach emphasizes documentation of algorithm performance, training data, validation, and known limitations. ONC's certification criterion adopted a similar framework. If ONC is going to preserve this requirement, it should consider whether the FDA's evolving guidance should inform updates to the criterion.‚Źé‚ŹéThere's also a question about what happens when models are updated. A system is certified with version 1.0 of its underlying model. The vendor updates to version 2.0, which improves performance overall but performs worse on a specific patient population. Does the certification remain valid? Should providers be notified? Should there be a process for re-validation? The current regulation doesn't fully address this, and it's becoming more important as vendors increasingly use machine learning approaches that evolve over time.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéVerve's bottom line is this: we support deregulation that reduces unnecessary burden, but we oppose removal of the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion and its transparency requirements. The criterion exists because clinical decision support tools operate in a different risk environment than other software. Providers need to understand how these tools work, what their limitations are, and what populations they were designed for.‚Źé‚ŹéWe've experienced firsthand how regulatory burden can stifle innovation and push providers toward suboptimal solutions. But we've also experienced how the absence of clear information about system performance and limitations creates risk. The goal should be to find the middle ground: maintaining transparency while reducing process burden.‚Źé‚ŹéThe specific recommendation is to preserve the model card and transparency disclosure requirements while exploring ways to streamline the certification process. This maintains the safety and accountability benefits while reducing the cost and time burden that currently excludes smaller vendors from the market.‚Źé‚ŹéHealthcare is moving toward more extensive use of AI in clinical decision-making. The decisions we make now about how to regulate these systems will affect patient safety, provider decision-making quality, and vendor innovation for years to come. Removing transparency requirements would be a step in the wrong direction, even if it reduces short-term regulatory burden.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-29T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-29T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ11‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving DSI transparency requirements creates a dangerous regulatory gap where AI-based clinical decision support tools face fewer disclosure obligations than traditional medical devices, undermining consistent safety standards.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒresigned‚ôĒOregon‚ôĒhospital CFO‚ôĒ57‚ôĒLast spring, our orthopedic surgery department wanted to switch to a smaller vendor's documentation system that actually integrated with our physical therapy workflow‚ÄĒsomething our current certified EHR simply wouldn't do‚ÄĒbut the certification and interoperability requirements made it prohibitively expensive to pilot. We ended up spending another $180,000 on workarounds instead, and I watched our department heads gradually accept that innovation in our own walls wasn't really an option anymore.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ3300
