CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0050‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒSandra Davis‚ôĒThe Society of Thoracic Surgeons‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Society of Thoracic Surgeons opposes eliminating certification requirements for clinical decision support tools, arguing that the current model card transparency requirement‚ÄĒwhich mandates disclosure of intended use, target population, trainin...‚ôĒRe: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity‚Źé‚ŹéThe Society of Thoracic Surgeons submits this comment on the proposed deregulatory framework outlined in the Notice of Proposed Rulemaking published January 28, 2025 (90 FR 5847). We represent over 7,000 board-certified thoracic surgeons, cardiothoracic surgeons, and allied specialists across the United States, including many based in Colorado who directly experience the clinical consequences of fragmented health information systems. Our members operate in diverse settings‚ÄĒacademic medical centers, community hospitals, independent surgical practices‚ÄĒand have invested significantly in health information technology infrastructure. We write to address a specific concern about the proposed elimination of Decision Support Interventions certification criteria that we believe warrants reconsideration.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CLINICAL CONTEXT**‚Źé‚ŹéWhen a patient arrives for evaluation of a complex thoracic condition, my colleagues often work with imaging data, pathology reports, prior operative notes, and imaging algorithms that assist in surgical planning. These tools are no longer optional extras in our field. They're integral to how we assess candidacy for minimally invasive versus open approaches, estimate operative risk, and counsel patients about outcomes. Last year, I watched our surgical team at Denver Health spend three weeks reconstructing a patient's prior imaging history after she transferred from an employer-sponsored clinic. The imaging was sitting in a separate system. No one could access it. We eventually pieced together what should have been instantaneous retrieval, but the delay meant we began her evaluation without complete information about the natural history of her disease. That wasn't acceptable, and I keep thinking about how many other patients experience something similar without anyone documenting the gap.‚Źé‚ŹéNow here's what worries me about the current proposal: the impulse to remove certification requirements for decision support tools makes intuitive sense from a regulatory burden perspective. Fewer rules mean faster innovation. Fewer certification boxes mean companies get to market quicker. But when those tools are used in surgical decision-making, the stakes are not theoretical. A thoracic surgeon considering whether to recommend surgery for a patient with borderline operability needs to know what population that algorithm was trained on. If it was developed using data predominantly from academic centers, it may not perform the same way in community hospital populations. If the training dataset excluded women over 65, it might systematically misclassify risk in that group. These aren't edge cases. Women represent about 40 percent of our lung cancer cases now, and our patient population skews older.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT THE CURRENT CERTIFICATION CRITERION ACTUALLY REQUIRES**‚Źé‚ŹéUnder the existing 45 CFR 170.315(a)(1) certification criterion for Decision Support Interventions, health IT developers must provide an AI model card that includes intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and evidence of external validation. This is not an unreasonable burden. It's documentation that responsible developers should already be maintaining. The criterion doesn't prevent innovation. It doesn't require specific performance thresholds. It simply requires transparency about what the tool does, who it was tested on, and what limitations exist.‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be honest about something: I initially thought the deregulatory framing was reasonable. Certification requirements do impose costs, and those costs ultimately pass to providers. We understand the argument that market competition and liability exposure create sufficient incentives for quality without regulatory mandates. But I've also sat in our quality committee meetings where we review algorithm performance across our patient populations. I've seen where they work well and where they fail. The problem is that without transparency requirements, we don't have a systematic way to know which is which until we've already made clinical decisions based on incomplete information.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Department's proposed rule cites the Administrative Procedure Act and the Regulatory Flexibility Act as justification for reducing burden on health IT developers (89 FR 86347, November 8, 2024). I'm sympathetic to that. But the rule also fails to address the burden shift that occurs when certification requirements are eliminated. That burden doesn't disappear. It moves to healthcare providers and patients. Hospitals and surgical practices now bear the responsibility for validating decision support tools in their own populations. We lack the technical expertise and statistical infrastructure that larger integrated health systems might have. Smaller practices, rural providers, community hospitals‚ÄĒthey're left deciding whether to implement tools they don't fully understand or to continue without decision support capabilities.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PRIVACY AND EQUITY CONCERN**‚Źé‚ŹéThere's another dimension to this that extends beyond surgical decision-making. My mother's experience with data misuse made this personal for me. When her file was accidentally sent to a debt collector, there was no obvious recourse. She wasn't a healthcare professional. She didn't know how to file a complaint with the state medical board or pursue damages for unauthorized disclosure. She was just a patient who had to spend weeks calling offices to verify that her full medical history‚ÄĒincluding psychiatric notes and reproductive health information‚ÄĒhad been transmitted to someone collecting her medical bills. That happened under the current regulatory framework. I shudder to think what happens when we further reduce transparency requirements and oversight.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule doesn't address data governance adequately. When decision support tools use patient data for training, validation, or refinement, patients deserve to know how their information is being used. The current certification criterion for DSI doesn't directly govern data governance, but it does create accountability through transparency. Developers must disclose training data characteristics. That disclosure mechanism creates a paper trail. It gives regulators something to audit. It gives providers a way to ask questions. Remove that requirement, and we lose one of the few levers we have for understanding how patient data flows through these systems.‚Źé‚ŹéI keep coming back to equity. The Health IT Certification Program has historically struggled to ensure that certified tools perform equitably across demographic groups (see the Office of the National Coordinator's 2023 report on health IT certification and health equity). Removing the model card requirement doesn't solve that problem. If anything, it makes it worse. A developer can now bring a tool to market without disclosing whether it was tested on diverse populations, whether it performs differently for Black patients versus white patients, whether it systematically over- or underestimates risk in women. We'll only discover these problems after deployment, in real patient care, when harm may already have occurred.‚Źé‚Źé**REGULATORY PRECEDENT AND WHAT OTHER AGENCIES ARE DOING**‚Źé‚ŹéThe FDA's approach to software as a medical device offers a useful comparison. When the FDA regulates clinical decision support software that impacts patient safety, it requires manufacturers to provide information about software validation, performance characteristics, and intended use (21 CFR 860.7(d)). The FDA doesn't micromanage algorithm development, but it does require transparency about what the software is designed to do and evidence that it actually does it safely and effectively. The ONC's current DSI criterion is less demanding than FDA requirements, yet the proposed rule would eliminate even that floor.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Veterans Health Administration took a different approach. In 2022, VHA established internal governance standards for clinical AI tools that require documentation of training data, performance testing across demographic subgroups, and ongoing monitoring after deployment (VHA Directive 1137, March 2022). VHA isn't a regulatory agency. It's a healthcare provider. Yet VHA leadership recognized that transparent governance protects both patients and the organization from liability. There's a lesson there. Certification requirements aren't just regulatory burden. They're a mechanism for ensuring that tools are actually suitable for their intended use.‚Źé‚Źé**THE INFORMATION BLOCKING CONNECTION**‚Źé‚ŹéHere's where I think the proposal misses something important about the relationship between certification requirements and information blocking rules. The information blocking regulations at 42 CFR 171.100 require certain actors to provide access to electronic health information without unnecessary delay or interference. But those regulations don't specify how actors should handle requests for information that flows through decision support tools. If a patient requests access to data that informed a clinical decision made with an algorithm, what exactly does the provider have to disclose? The current certification requirement for DSI creates a common understanding: the model card tells the provider what the algorithm does, and that information can be disclosed to the patient. Remove certification, and we lose that common language. Providers will make inconsistent decisions about what to disclose.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not arguing that the information blocking rules should govern algorithm transparency. That's the wrong regulatory lever. But information blocking and certification requirements work together. They create a system where providers understand what their tools are doing and can explain that to patients. Eliminate certification, and you weaken the entire information blocking framework without necessarily intending to.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE'RE ASKING FOR**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Society of Thoracic Surgeons asks that the final rule retain the current Decision Support Interventions certification criterion at 45 CFR 170.315(a)(1). We're not asking for more stringent requirements. We're asking for the current requirement to remain in place.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the Department is concerned about burden on developers, there are ways to streamline the certification process without eliminating transparency requirements altogether. The criterion could be modified to allow developers to submit model cards using a standardized template, reducing documentation burden while maintaining information transparency. Certification bodies could be permitted to expedite review for tools in lower-risk categories. Developers could be allowed to submit updated model cards when tools are refined, rather than recertifying from scratch. These adjustments address burden concerns without sacrificing patient safety or transparency.‚Źé‚ŹéThe rule as proposed assumes that market forces alone will incentivize developer transparency and tool validation. That assumption is questionable in healthcare. Providers often lack the technical expertise to evaluate decision support tools independently. Patients have limited ability to understand algorithm performance. Liability exposure provides some incentive for quality, but litigation occurs after harm. We need transparency mechanisms that operate prospectively, before tools are deployed in clinical care. Certification requirements provide that mechanism.‚Źé‚Źé**IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéOne practical point: if the Department does proceed with the deregulatory approach, it should at least require some alternative mechanism for transparency. Perhaps developers could publish model cards voluntarily on a registry maintained by ONC. Providers could query that registry to understand what tools are available and what their characteristics are. This would reduce regulatory burden on developers while preserving information flow to providers and patients. It's not ideal‚ÄĒvoluntary standards are weaker than certification requirements‚ÄĒbut it's better than complete elimination of transparency requirements.‚Źé‚ŹéThe COVID-19 pandemic accelerated adoption of clinical decision support tools in many surgical specialties. Our field saw rapid deployment of tools for patient risk stratification, operative planning, and outcome prediction. In the urgency of the moment, some of that deployment occurred without adequate validation in diverse populations. We're now dealing with the consequences. Some tools perform well across our patient populations. Others have systematic biases we're still discovering. A certification requirement creates a mechanism for learning from that experience and improving future tools. Eliminate it, and we lose that learning opportunity.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Society of Thoracic Surgeons supports regulatory efficiency and market competition in health IT. We also support patient safety, transparent clinical decision-making, and equitable care. These goals don't have to be in tension. A streamlined certification process for decision support tools can achieve both. Eliminating certification requirements altogether shifts burden to providers and patients without clear benefit. We ask the Department to reconsider that aspect of the proposed rule and to retain transparency requirements for clinical decision support tools, even as you pursue broader deregulatory goals.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-22T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-22T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ6‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒresigned‚ôĒColorado‚ôĒcampaign director‚ôĒ43‚ôĒWhen my mother was diagnosed with Stage III lung cancer last year, her surgical team at Denver Health couldn't access her complete imaging history from her previous employer's clinic‚ÄĒit took three weeks of phone calls to reconstruct what should have been instantaneous. Now I'm watching proposed changes that could open those records faster, but I keep thinking about the gynecologist's office that accidentally sent her full file to a debt collector, and wondering if speed is worth the risk for people without legal resources to fight misuse.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1877
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0051‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒLisa Martin‚ôĒTheragen, Inc.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTheragen, a genomics-focused healthcare provider, supports targeted deregulation of health IT certification but strongly opposes removing Decision Support Intervention (DSI) standards, arguing that transparency requirements for clinical algorithms...‚ôĒ**COMMENT ON HEALTH DATA, TECHNOLOGY, AND INTEROPERABILITY: ASTP/ONC DEREGULATORY ACTIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéTheragen, Inc. appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory actions regarding health IT certification requirements. We are a 180-person genomics-focused healthcare provider based in Connecticut with significant operational experience in EHR implementation and workflow optimization across precision medicine settings. Our perspective is shaped by eighteen months of intensive work to implement a certified EHR system that, while compliant with current certification standards, has become increasingly misaligned with the clinical and technical requirements of our genomics practice.‚Źé‚ŹéThe tension we face is real and worth articulating plainly. On one hand, we understand and support the deregulatory impulse. Certification requirements have genuine costs. We spent roughly $2.3 million implementing our current system, not counting internal staff time and workflow disruption. We've been told by at least three vendors that certification adds 18-24 months to their development cycles and tens of millions of dollars in annual compliance overhead. That burden is real. But we also need to be honest about what happens when we remove safeguards without thinking through the downstream consequences.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PROBLEM WITH CURRENT CERTIFICATION REQUIREMENTS FOR OUR ORGANIZATION**‚Źé‚ŹéOur experience implementing a certified EHR has shown us that some certification requirements impose costs that don't scale well for mid-market providers. The 2015 Edition certification criteria, in particular, created a situation where we were locked into a vendor relationship despite outgrowing the platform. The switch-away costs are prohibitive. We looked at three alternative platforms that would serve our genomics workflows far better than our current system, but the interoperability gaps between our certified EHR and those platforms, combined with the cost of re-certification and re-validation, made migration economically infeasible for an organization our size.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is the part where deregulation sounds appealing. If certification requirements were loosened, smaller, more innovative vendors could enter the market without the same compliance burden. We might have had more viable migration options. The market might be more dynamic. We get it. And there's truth to this argument.‚Źé‚ŹéBut here's where we need to pump the brakes, and it's specifically about Decision Support Interventions (DSI) in certified EHR systems, which is where our deepest concern lies.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY DECISION SUPPORT INTERVENTIONS ARE DIFFERENT**‚Źé‚ŹéGenomics is inherently complex. Our clinical workflows depend increasingly on algorithmic decision support, particularly around variant interpretation, pharmacogenomic recommendations, and risk stratification. These aren't simple alerts. They're sophisticated models trained on genetic databases, clinical outcomes data, and sometimes proprietary training sets. When a clinician receives a recommendation from our EHR about whether a genetic variant is pathogenic or how it might affect drug metabolism, they're receiving information that directly influences treatment decisions. That carries genuine patient safety implications.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification criterion requires vendors to provide model cards and transparency disclosures covering intended use, target population, known risks, and training data characteristics. This is not onerous. It's not preventing innovation. What it does is create a baseline expectation that someone, somewhere, has documented what a model is supposed to do, what it was trained on, and what its limitations are. When a clinician in our system looks at a pharmacogenomic alert, they can see whether that model was validated in populations that match their patient's ancestry and age group. That matters for patient safety.‚Źé‚ŹéWe are deeply concerned about proposals to eliminate or significantly weaken the DSI certification criterion. The argument for doing so typically goes like this: certification is slowing innovation, vendors should be trusted to self-regulate, and the market will punish vendors whose models perform poorly. There's a superficial appeal to that logic. But it doesn't hold up when you think about how healthcare actually works.‚Źé‚Źé**THE SPECIFIC RISK WITH REMOVING DSI CERTIFICATION STANDARDS**‚Źé‚ŹéConsider the information asymmetry problem. Our clinicians use EHR-embedded decision support every day. Most of them are not machine learning engineers. They don't have the technical literacy to independently validate whether an algorithm is working as intended or whether it's making systematic errors in particular subpopulations. They're trusting that the model has been thoughtfully developed and that its limitations are known. If we remove certification requirements for DSI, we remove the expectation that vendors document and disclose those limitations. We don't remove the risk. We just push it onto clinicians and patients.‚Źé‚ŹéWe've also seen, repeatedly, that self-regulation in healthcare IT doesn't work well in practice. This isn't a cynical view. It's based on what we've observed in our own vendor relationships. When we ask vendors about the specifics of their algorithms, the transparency varies wildly. Some vendors provide detailed documentation. Others tell us it's proprietary and they can't share details. Some have clearly never documented their models' limitations because they've never been required to. If we remove the certification requirement, there's no baseline expectation anymore. Vendors with strong governance will continue to document and disclose. Vendors without that culture will optimize for cost and speed to market. Clinicians will have no way to know which is which.‚Źé‚ŹéThe market doesn't punish poor algorithmic performance in healthcare the way it does in other sectors. A poorly performing recommendation engine on an e-commerce platform loses revenue immediately. A poorly performing clinical decision support algorithm might injure patients for months or years before anyone connects the dots. By then, the vendor's already moved on. The liability is diffuse. The accountability is weak.‚Źé‚ŹéThere's also a fundamental question about informed consent and professional autonomy. Our clinicians have a right to understand, at some baseline level, what they're being advised to do and why. If we're embedding algorithmic recommendations into their workflow, we have an obligation to be transparent about how those recommendations were developed and validated. That's not a regulatory burden. That's basic professional ethics.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE'RE PROPOSING: RETAIN AND CLARIFY DSI STANDARDS**‚Źé‚ŹéWe recommend that the final rule retain the current DSI certification criterion and, if anything, clarify it further. The criterion is not preventing innovation. It's creating a baseline expectation of transparency and documentation. That's worth keeping.‚Źé‚ŹéSpecifically:‚Źé‚Źé- Maintain the requirement that vendors document model cards covering intended use, target population, known risks, and training data characteristics for all DSI systems in certified EHR systems.‚Źé‚Źé- Clarify that "known risks" includes not just performance metrics but also documented limitations in specific populations or clinical scenarios. This forces vendors to be honest about where their models work well and where they don't.‚Źé‚Źé- Require vendors to disclose the external validation status of models. If a model has been validated only in academic settings and not in real-world clinical practice, clinicians need to know that.‚Źé‚Źé- Establish a clear expectation that these disclosures are accessible to clinicians in a standard format, not buried in dense technical documentation or hidden behind vendor gatekeeping.‚Źé‚ŹéThese aren't burdensome requirements. They're the minimum baseline for responsible deployment of algorithmic decision support in clinical settings.‚Źé‚Źé**ADDRESSING THE INNOVATION ARGUMENT**‚Źé‚ŹéWe anticipate the response: this will slow innovation and prevent smaller vendors from entering the market. We don't think that's true, but we want to address it directly.‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, documentation and transparency are not the same as technical complexity. A small vendor building a new clinical decision support tool can document what it does and how it was trained. That doesn't require a massive compliance infrastructure. It requires intellectual honesty about the model's performance and limitations.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, the vendors most likely to be disadvantaged by clear DSI standards are not the innovative disruptors. They're the vendors who cut corners on validation and documentation because it's cheaper. We should want to disadvantage those vendors. They're not competitors. They're risks to patient safety.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, our experience suggests that providers actually want transparency from their vendors. We've spent countless hours trying to extract technical details from our current vendor about how their decision support algorithms work. It's frustrating. We'd be better served by a vendor who proactively documented this information as part of their certification process. That vendor would be more trustworthy, not less competitive.‚Źé‚Źé**THE BROADER CONTEXT: SELECTIVE DEREGULATION**‚Źé‚ŹéWe recognize that this comment focuses narrowly on one certification criterion. But that narrowness is intentional. The broader deregulatory proposal seems to assume that all certification requirements are equally burdensome and that removing them uniformly will increase competition and innovation. We don't think that's true.‚Źé‚ŹéSome certification requirements are genuinely expensive and duplicative. The security testing requirements, for example, overlap significantly with HIPAA compliance. There's a legitimate argument for streamlining those. Other requirements, like the electronic health information export requirement, are foundational to interoperability and deserve to stay. And still others, like the DSI criterion, have direct patient safety implications that justify their continued existence.‚Źé‚ŹéEffective deregulation isn't about removing all requirements. It's about being strategic about which requirements to keep and which to change. We're concerned that the current proposal doesn't make those distinctions carefully enough.‚Źé‚Źé**OUR OPERATIONAL EXPERIENCE WITH DECISION SUPPORT TRANSPARENCY**‚Źé‚ŹéLet me ground this in a specific example from our work. Last year, we implemented a pharmacogenomic recommendation system within our EHR. The system flags potential drug-gene interactions based on a model trained on PharmGKB data and our own clinical outcomes database. The model performs very well overall. But we discovered, through internal validation, that its performance is notably weaker in patients of East Asian ancestry, specifically because the underlying training data had limited representation from that population.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is exactly the kind of finding that should be disclosed to clinicians. Our East Asian patients deserve to know that a recommendation they're receiving comes from a model that was trained on limited data from their population. That's not a reason to avoid the recommendation. It's a reason to be more cautious in interpreting it and to pay closer attention to individual patient factors.‚Źé‚ŹéWith current DSI certification standards, we're required to document and disclose this limitation. Our model card explicitly notes the ancestry composition of the training data and flags the performance gap. Clinicians using the system can see this information. It informs how they interpret recommendations.‚Źé‚ŹéIf DSI certification requirements were removed, we could still choose to disclose this information. But there would be no requirement to do so. And frankly, not all vendors would make the same choice. Some would omit the information to avoid raising concerns about their model's performance. And there would be no way for clinicians or patients to know that the information had been omitted.‚Źé‚Źé**THE DIFFERENCE BETWEEN REASONABLE DEREGULATION AND DEREGULATION THAT CREATES RISK**‚Źé‚ŹéThis brings us to a fundamental distinction that we think the agency needs to make. There's a difference between removing regulatory burden that doesn't serve patient safety or clinical utility, and removing safeguards that actually matter.‚Źé‚ŹéThe burden of obtaining separate ONC certification for products that already comply with HIPAA security requirements is probably duplicative. That's reasonable to streamline. The requirement to support specific outdated technical standards that have been superseded by newer, better standards is worth reconsidering. The pressure on vendors to maintain backwards compatibility with EHR systems from ten years ago creates unnecessary cost and slows adoption of better technology. Those are the kinds of deregulatory actions that make sense.‚Źé‚ŹéBut the requirement that vendors document and disclose how their clinical decision support algorithms work is different. It's not duplicative. It doesn't constrain innovation. It creates transparency about a technology that directly affects clinical decisions and patient outcomes. Removing it would be deregulation in service of vendor convenience, not patient benefit or market efficiency.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WOULD ACTUALLY HELP US**‚Źé‚ŹéSince we're in the position of having written a substantial comment on deregulation, we want to be clear about what would actually help our organization and organizations like ours. It's not the removal of DSI standards. It's:‚Źé‚Źé- Clearer interoperability standards that would make it easier for us to migrate away from our current vendor. We're currently locked in not because of certification requirements, but because of proprietary data formats and incomplete API support. If the final rule mandated broader use of HL7 FHIR for clinical data exchange and required vendors to support standardized export formats, we could more easily transition to a platform better suited to our genomics workflows.‚Źé‚Źé- Reduced burden around the security testing requirements, which are genuinely duplicative with HIPAA compliance. We've already invested heavily in HIPAA security infrastructure. Separating ONC certification security testing from HIPAA compliance would be helpful.‚Źé‚Źé- Clearer guidance on what constitutes appropriate external validation for clinical decision support systems. Right now, the standard is somewhat ambiguous. Is validation in published literature sufficient? Does it need to be prospective? How recent does it need to be? Clearer guidance would actually be more helpful than removing the requirement entirely.‚Źé‚Źé- Standards for how vendors should handle updates and retraining of models in production. We worry about model drift, where a model's performance degrades over time as the population changes. But there's no clear standard for how often models should be revalidated or what triggers should require model updates. That's an area where clearer standards would be better than deregulation.‚Źé‚Źé**PATIENT SAFETY ISN'T A SECONDARY CONSIDERATION**‚Źé‚ŹéWe want to be direct about something that we think gets lost in the deregulation discussion. Patient safety isn't a secondary consideration that we should weigh against convenience or cost. It's a primary obligation.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen we embed algorithmic recommendations into clinical workflows, we're not just offering information. We're shaping clinical decision-making. Clinicians are cognitively busy. They're managing multiple patients, multiple systems, multiple sources of information. When an EHR-embedded recommendation appears in their workflow, it carries weight. There's evidence that clinicians tend to follow algorithmic recommendations at higher rates than they follow recommendations from other sources, sometimes without fully understanding the basis for the recommendation.‚Źé‚ŹéThat's not a criticism of clinicians. It's a reflection of how human attention and trust work. It's also why transparency about how those recommendations were developed and validated is important. It's not a burden on vendors. It's a responsibility.‚Źé‚ŹéWe've seen, in our own experience, what happens when that transparency is lacking. We've encountered vendor recommendations that we later discovered were based on outdated evidence or limited validation. We've found ourselves in conversations with clinicians who were applying recommendations in ways the vendor clearly didn't intend, because the intended use case wasn't clearly documented. These are problems that transparency standards actually solve.‚Źé‚Źé**THE RISK OF A RACE TO THE BOTTOM**‚Źé‚ŹéOne more concern, and then we'll wrap up. There's a real risk that if DSI standards are removed, we'll see a race to the bottom among vendors. Vendors who maintain high documentation and validation standards will be at a competitive disadvantage relative to vendors who cut those corners. Over time, the market will favor the cheaper, less rigorous vendors. That's not because rigor is inherently bad for innovation. It's because without regulatory standards, rigor becomes an uncompensated cost.‚Źé‚ŹéWe've seen this dynamic play out in other healthcare IT contexts. HIPAA compliance is a good example. HIPAA standards created a baseline expectation that all vendors had to meet. That baseline didn't prevent innovation. It prevented a race to the bottom where vendors competed on who could cut the most security corners. The market is better for it.‚Źé‚ŹéDSI standards would work the same way. They'd create a baseline expectation that all vendors have to document and disclose how their algorithms work. Vendors would still compete on innovation, on user experience, on clinical utility. But they wouldn't compete on who could be least transparent about their algorithms' limitations.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION AND SPECIFIC RECOMMENDATION**‚Źé‚ŹéTheragen supports thoughtful deregulation of health IT requirements where regulation is genuinely duplicative, outdated, or not serving patient benefit. We support efforts to reduce unnecessary burden on vendors and accelerate innovation. But we strongly recommend that the final rule retain the DSI certification criterion requiring vendors to document and disclose model cards, intended use cases, target populations, known risks, and training data characteristics.‚Źé‚ŹéThis criterion is not preventing innovation. It's creating accountability. That's worth keeping. Everything else in the deregulatory proposal can move forward. This one element should remain.‚Źé‚ŹéIf there are concerns about how the DSI criterion is being interpreted or implemented, we'd support clarifying guidance rather than removal. But the fundamental requirement for transparency should stay.‚Źé‚ŹéHealthcare IT is moving toward increasing reliance on algorithmic decision support. That's not a bad thing. Algorithms can help clinicians make better decisions if they're developed thoughtfully and deployed transparently. But that transparency requires standards. It requires documentation. It requires disclosure. And it requires regulatory expectation that vendors will meet those standards.‚Źé‚ŹéWe've invested heavily in our current EHR system. We're locked in to our vendor in ways that are frustrating. We understand the appeal of deregulation. But we're asking the agency to be thoughtful about which regulations to remove. Keep this one. It matters.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-02-07T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-07T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ9‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒconcerned‚ôĒConnecticut‚ôĒhealth IT director‚ôĒ42‚ôĒMy team spent eighteen months implementing a certified EHR system that we've now outgrown, but the certification requirements made it prohibitively expensive to migrate to a platform better suited to our genomics workflows‚ÄĒwe're essentially locked in with a vendor whose innovation has stalled because the barrier to entry keeps smaller, more nimble competitors out of the market entirely.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2779
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0052‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒCarol Rodriguez‚ôĒSouth Carolina Primary Health Care Association‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe South Carolina Primary Health Care Association supports targeted deregulation but opposes removing Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements for AI clinical tools, arguing that transparency mandates like model cards are e...‚ôĒ**COMMENT ON HEALTH DATA, TECHNOLOGY, AND INTEROPERABILITY: ASTP/ONC DEREGULATORY ACTIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéSouth Carolina Primary Health Care Association‚Źé‚ŹéThe South Carolina Primary Health Care Association appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory framework. Our organization represents 51 federally qualified health centers and primary care clinics across South Carolina, serving approximately 850,000 patients annually, many of whom are uninsured, underinsured, or have limited English proficiency. For more than a decade, our members have operated in environments where fragmented data systems create real clinical risks. We've also watched the promises of interoperability initiatives repeatedly fail to materialize in ways that actually improve care at the clinic level. What we're concerned about now is that in moving to reduce regulatory burden, we may inadvertently remove the few guardrails that currently prevent vendors from deploying AI-assisted clinical decision support tools without adequate transparency or evidence of safety.‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be direct about this: we support deregulation in some areas. Certification requirements that create unnecessary technical bottlenecks or impose compliance costs that don't measurably improve patient outcomes should be reconsidered. Overly prescriptive standards that lock providers into outdated interoperability models deserve scrutiny. The regulatory burden on EHR vendors is real, and we've heard repeatedly from our members about implementation timelines that stretch years and cost millions. But the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria, specifically the requirement for AI model cards and transparency disclosures, is not the right direction. This isn't about preserving regulatory complexity for its own sake. It's about preserving a basic requirement that clinicians and their patients understand what an AI tool is actually doing.‚Źé‚Źé**The Problem We've Seen in Real Practice**‚Źé‚ŹéLast year, I watched a colleague's elderly mother‚ÄĒa diabetes patient with limited English proficiency‚ÄĒreceive contradictory medication advice because her records were scattered across three different clinic systems that couldn't communicate. When we finally consolidated everything, we discovered a serious drug interaction that had gone undetected for months. The irony wasn't lost on me: better data integration could have prevented harm, yet I'm now worried that opening these systems up without proper safeguards means her data could end up in the hands of insurers or employers looking to deny her coverage.‚Źé‚ŹéThat experience crystallized something I've been thinking about for years. Fragmentation is a real problem. But fragmentation exists partly because clinicians and their patients can actually see what's happening with their data. A provider knows they're using three systems. A patient can theoretically track where their records have gone. There's friction, but there's also transparency. What concerns us is replacing that friction with a system that feels seamless on the surface but obscures where data flows and how algorithms are making decisions about patient care.‚Źé‚ŹéIn our network, we've started piloting AI-assisted diagnostic tools for early detection of diabetic complications. These tools are valuable. They help our providers catch problems earlier. But we've learned something important: the tools that our clinicians actually trust and use correctly are the ones where we can explain what the tool sees, what it's trained on, and what its limitations are. We've had to push vendors hard for this information. Some have been responsive. Others have treated model documentation as proprietary information they won't share. When vendors refuse transparency, our clinicians either don't use the tool or use it in ways that bypass its intended safeguards. That's not safe. That's just friction in a different form.‚Źé‚Źé**What the Proposed Rule Would Do**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule would eliminate the requirement for Decision Support Interventions certification, which currently mandates that AI tools in clinical settings include model cards with information about intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. The justification is that this requirement imposes unnecessary burden on developers and slows innovation. We understand the burden argument. But we've read the RFI documents, and we think the proposed approach conflates two different problems.‚Źé‚ŹéOne problem is genuine: some standards are outdated or overly prescriptive. The requirement that all EHR systems support a specific FHIR implementation guide, for instance, has created situations where vendors spend resources on compliance with outdated technical specifications rather than on interoperability that actually works. That kind of regulatory deadweight should be removed.‚Źé‚ŹéThe other problem is different: the current certification framework for DSI is actually doing something important. It's not creating technical friction. It's creating transparency friction. Vendors don't like it because they'd prefer to keep model details proprietary. Providers would benefit from removing it because it reduces certification timelines. But patients and clinicians would lose something real, which is any systematic requirement that the tools making decisions about their care be documented and validated.‚Źé‚Źé**Transparency as a Safety Requirement, Not a Burden**‚Źé‚ŹéWe need to distinguish between regulations that create technical burden and regulations that create transparency requirements. The DSI certification criterion does not require vendors to use a specific technical architecture, support a particular data format, or implement any particular interoperability protocol. It requires documentation. It requires that vendors be able to articulate what their tool does, who it was designed for, what its performance looks like in different populations, and what risks have been identified.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not the same as saying vendors must use a particular data standard or follow a specific implementation pathway. Model card documentation is scalable. A vendor can document a decision support tool in a model card format in a matter of weeks, not months. The burden argument doesn't hold up when you look at actual implementation timelines in our network. What actually takes months is integrating a tool into an EHR workflow, training clinicians, and testing it in a live environment. The model card is a one-time documentation effort that, once done, can be reused and updated as the model evolves.‚Źé‚ŹéMore importantly, transparency about AI model characteristics is not a burden on vendors. It's a safety requirement for clinicians. According to the FDA's draft guidance on clinical decision support software (published in January 2021), transparency about model performance, training data, and known limitations is essential for clinicians to make informed decisions about when and how to use the tool. The guidance specifically recommends that developers provide information about the populations used in training and validation, algorithm performance across demographic subgroups, and known failure modes. The DSI certification criterion is aligned with this FDA guidance.‚Źé‚ŹéWe've also looked at what other high-stakes domains do. In aviation, every piece of software that affects flight safety requires documentation of its design, testing, and known limitations. In pharmaceutical manufacturing, process control algorithms are validated and documented. In medical device software, manufacturers must provide evidence of validation across intended use populations. Healthcare AI is not some special domain where transparency should be lower. If anything, it's a domain where the stakes are higher because the consequences of model failure are direct patient harm.‚Źé‚Źé**The Risk of Removing Transparency Requirements**‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what concerns us about removing the DSI certification criterion without replacement: vendors will have zero systematic incentive to document their models or validate them across different patient populations. This isn't speculation. We've already seen it happen. We've had vendors deploy decision support tools in our clinics and, when we asked for documentation of model performance in patients over 65, in patients with limited English proficiency, or in patients with multiple comorbidities, they told us that information wasn't available. The tool was trained on a population that didn't match our patient demographics, but there was no systematic requirement to tell us that.‚Źé‚ŹéOne of our member clinics in Charleston used a tool for predicting hospital readmission risk. The tool worked well overall, but when we looked at the underlying data, we discovered it had been trained primarily on patients from urban academic medical centers with good follow-up care. Our patients‚ÄĒmany of whom are rural, have transportation challenges, and have inconsistent access to primary care‚ÄĒhad systematically worse outcomes with the tool's predictions. The tool was flagging low-risk patients as high-risk because it was interpreting barriers to follow-up care (which we could address with care coordination) as clinical risk factors (which the tool couldn't distinguish). Without the model card requirement, we never would have known this.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criterion exists because of exactly this kind of problem. It ensures that before a tool is deployed, vendors have to articulate what population it's designed for, what data it was trained on, and what performance looks like across different subgroups. This matters for equity. Our patient population is 68% African American and 12% Hispanic. Tools trained on datasets that don't represent these populations can perform worse. The certification requirement pushes vendors to either validate their tools across our patient populations or explicitly document that they haven't. That's not a burden. That's a prerequisite for safe deployment.‚Źé‚Źé**What We'd Propose Instead**‚Źé‚ŹéWe don't think the answer is to keep the current regulatory framework exactly as is. Some aspects of the DSI certification process could be streamlined. The timelines could be shortened. The process could be more transparent about what vendors are actually being asked to document. But the core requirement‚ÄĒthat AI tools used in clinical decision-making be documented and validated‚ÄĒshould remain.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what we'd recommend:‚Źé‚Źé1. Keep the DSI certification criterion but simplify the documentation requirements. A model card doesn't need to be a 100-page technical document. It could be a standardized form that asks vendors to document: (a) the clinical problem the tool is designed to address, (b) the population and setting it was developed in, (c) the performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value) in the development population and in any validation studies, (d) known performance differences across demographic subgroups if the tool has been evaluated in those subgroups, and (e) documented failure modes or limitations.‚Źé‚Źé2. Establish clear timelines for certification. Vendors should have a clear pathway to submit a tool for certification and receive a decision within 60 days. If the decision is a request for additional information, vendors should have 30 days to respond. This is faster than current timelines but still allows for meaningful review.‚Źé‚Źé3. Create a public database of certified DSI tools with their model cards. This isn't proprietary information. Once a tool is certified and deployed, the documentation should be publicly available so other health systems can learn from validation studies and real-world implementation experience. This accelerates learning across the healthcare system.‚Źé‚Źé4. Require periodic revalidation. An AI tool that performs well in 2024 might perform differently in 2026 if the patient population changes or if the underlying data distributions shift. Every three years, vendors should submit updated performance data or confirm that the model remains valid. This isn't a major burden (it's mostly about running the model on recent data and reporting metrics), but it ensures that tools remain fit for purpose.‚Źé‚Źé5. Establish a post-market surveillance mechanism. If a tool is deployed in 50 different health systems and we start seeing systematic failures in certain populations, there should be a way for that information to feed back into the certification system. This is standard in medical device regulation and should be standard for clinical AI.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not a regulatory expansion. It's a refinement of the existing requirement. The burden is minimal compared to the benefit of ensuring that clinicians have the information they need to use AI tools safely.‚Źé‚Źé**Addressing the Innovation Concern**‚Źé‚ŹéI know the counterargument: this will slow innovation. Vendors will be less likely to develop new tools if they have to document and validate them. I understand that concern, but I think it's overblown. Pharmaceutical companies develop new drugs under much more stringent requirements. Medical device manufacturers develop new diagnostic tools under FDA oversight. Innovation doesn't require the absence of transparency requirements. It requires clear timelines, reasonable standards, and market incentives.‚Źé‚ŹéIn fact, transparency requirements can accelerate innovation. When vendors know exactly what documentation is expected, they can plan for it. When there's a public database of certified tools with documented performance, other vendors can learn from that work rather than reinventing the wheel. When validation is required, the tools that do get deployed are actually used more effectively by clinicians, which creates more positive feedback and more demand for the next generation of tools.‚Źé‚ŹéWe've also learned something from our experience with EHR interoperability standards. The vendors that complained most about FHIR requirements five years ago are now the ones investing most heavily in FHIR capabilities because they've realized that interoperability is a competitive advantage. Once the standards were clear and stable, innovation actually accelerated. The same thing could happen with DSI certification. Once vendors understand what documentation is required, they can build it into their development process and move forward.‚Źé‚Źé**The Equity Risk**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to come back to the equity issue because it's not getting enough attention in the deregulation discussion. Our patient population includes a lot of people who are at risk of being harmed by AI tools that have been validated only on narrow populations. Limited English proficiency, unstable housing, chronic disease burden, transportation challenges‚ÄĒthese are all factors that change how an AI tool performs in practice. Without transparency requirements, vendors have no incentive to test their tools in these populations.‚Źé‚ŹéIn fact, there's an economic incentive not to. Testing a tool in multiple populations is more expensive. If a vendor can deploy a tool without having tested it broadly, they save money. They pass that cost onto the health system that has to figure out whether the tool works for their specific population. That's not market-driven innovation. That's cost-shifting to providers and risks to patients.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification requirement is one of the few regulatory mechanisms we have to push back against this incentive structure. It says: before you deploy this tool, you need to know whether it works for the populations you're claiming to serve. That's not overregulation. That's basic due diligence.‚Źé‚Źé**How This Connects to the Broader Deregulation Agenda**‚Źé‚ŹéWe're not opposed to deregulation. Some of the other proposed changes in the rule make sense. Removing the requirement for direct review of certain non-critical data elements, streamlining the certification process for tools that have minimal clinical impact, clarifying the information blocking standards to make them easier to comply with‚ÄĒthese are all reasonable moves that could reduce burden without compromising safety.‚Źé‚ŹéBut the DSI certification criterion is not a good candidate for removal. It's not creating technical burden. It's not slowing deployment of tools that are already validated. It's not imposing costs on vendors that don't benefit patients. It's requiring transparency about how AI tools work, which is exactly the kind of regulatory requirement that should survive a deregulation effort.‚Źé‚ŹéWe also want to flag something that's not directly in the rule but is related: the proposal to reduce information blocking enforcement. We support clarifying the information blocking standards and making compliance easier. But we're concerned that without concurrent requirements for transparency about how data is being used, loosening information blocking rules could lead to data flows that benefit vendors and payers more than patients and providers.‚Źé‚ŹéFor example, if a vendor can collect clinical data from one health system and use it to train tools for another vendor, without the original patients' knowledge or consent, that's a problem. The fact that the data isn't "blocked" doesn't mean it's being used appropriately. We'd recommend that any loosening of information blocking requirements be paired with stronger transparency requirements about secondary uses of data. That's not anti-deregulation. That's ensuring that the deregulation achieves its stated goal of benefiting patients and providers, not just vendors.‚Źé‚Źé**Specific Recommendations for the Final Rule**‚Źé‚ŹéBased on our experience, here's what we'd ask the agency to consider:‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, retain the DSI certification criterion but establish clear, streamlined documentation requirements using a standardized model card format. The documentation should be proportionate to the clinical risk of the tool‚ÄĒhigh-risk tools (those that make diagnoses or treatment recommendations) require more comprehensive validation; lower-risk tools (those that flag data for clinician review) require less.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, establish firm timelines for certification review. Vendors should receive a decision within 60 days of submission. If additional information is requested, vendors should receive a decision within 30 days of providing that information. This creates predictability while maintaining meaningful review.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, require that model cards be made publicly available once a tool is certified. This creates transparency for other health systems and supports learning across the healthcare ecosystem.‚Źé‚ŹéFourth, establish a three-year revalidation requirement for certified tools. This ensures that tools continue to perform as intended as patient populations and clinical contexts change.‚Źé‚ŹéFifth, create a post-market surveillance mechanism where health systems can report performance issues with certified tools, and the agency can use this information to update certifications or issue guidance about appropriate use.‚Źé‚ŹéSixth, pair any loosening of information blocking requirements with new transparency requirements about secondary uses of patient data. If vendors are going to use data from one health system to train tools for other systems, patients need to know about this and have the ability to opt out.‚Źé‚Źé**Conclusion**‚Źé‚ŹéWe support the goals of the deregulation effort: reducing unnecessary burden, accelerating innovation, and enabling market competition. But we want to be clear that transparency requirements for AI tools used in clinical decision-making are not unnecessary burden. They're prerequisites for safe deployment of tools that have the potential to affect patient outcomes.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criterion has been in place for several years. It has not slowed innovation. It has not prevented vendors from deploying tools. What it has done is ensure that before tools are deployed, vendors have documented what they do, who they work for, and what their limitations are. That's exactly the kind of regulatory requirement that should survive a deregulation effort.‚Źé‚ŹéWe appreciate the opportunity to comment and welcome the opportunity to discuss these recommendations with the agency and with other stakeholders.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-28T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-28T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ9‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHospital and health system procurement teams need transparent AI model information to make evidence-based decisions about which tools to adopt and how to implement them safely in their local contexts.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒfrustrated‚ôĒIndiana‚ôĒresearch director‚ôĒ55‚ôĒLast year, I watched a colleague's elderly mother‚ÄĒa diabetes patient with limited English proficiency‚ÄĒreceive contradictory medication advice because her records were scattered across three different clinic systems that couldn't communicate. When we finally got everything consolidated, we discovered a serious drug interaction that had gone undetected for months. The irony wasn't lost on me: better data integration could have prevented harm, yet I'm now worried that opening these systems up without proper safeguards means her data could end up in the hands of insurers or employers looking to deny her coverage.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2963
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0053‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒJoseph Allen‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒWhile interoperability between healthcare institutions is important, patients must retain direct access to their own medical records without excessive delays or fees, and hospitals should be required to maintain transparency about how AI systems i...‚ôĒWorking as a home health aide for the past eight years, I've watched how healthcare records move between offices, and I've seen what happens when they don't. Last week Mrs. Chen needed her cardiologist to review her old test results before an appointment, but the clinic wanted two weeks and fifty dollars to release them. She's 84 and couldn't wait. What worries me most about making it easier for hospitals to share data among themselves is that patients still won't be able to get their own information when they need it.‚Źé‚ŹéBut there's something bigger here. When hospitals use AI systems to decide on treatment or flag health risks, patients deserve to know how reliable those systems actually are. Mrs. Chen should know if the medication recommendation her doctor's using was tested on people her age, or if it's based on data that doesn't represent her. That's not a paperwork burden. That's safety. Removing transparency requirements means vulnerable patients won't understand what's driving decisions about their care. The easier path for hospitals shouldn't hide how their tools work. It should make sure patients can actually question what affects their health.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-26T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-26T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒmedium‚ôĒconcerned‚ôĒVirginia‚ôĒhome health aide‚ôĒ67‚ôĒLast Tuesday, I was helping Mrs. Chen get ready for her cardiologist appointment, and she asked me to grab her records from her previous doctor because the new office said they needed them‚ÄĒbut when we called, they told us it would take two weeks and cost fifty dollars.
