CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0054‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒCarol Torres‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA veteran and cardiac patient argues that while removing barriers to medical record sharing is necessary, the proposed rule should not eliminate transparency and validation requirements for AI decision-support tools in clinical practice, as these ...‚ôĒI'm writing this as someone who has lived through the exact problems this rule is supposed to solve, but also as someone who's terrified we're about to create new ones while fixing the old ones.‚Źé‚Źé**MY SITUATION AND WHY I'M PAYING ATTENTION**‚Źé‚ŹéLast year my VA cardiologist retired. Thirty years of my medical records‚ÄĒtest results, medication histories, notes about complications, everything‚ÄĒwere sitting in a VA system in Birmingham. When I switched to a civilian provider in the same city, it took six weeks to get those records transferred. Six weeks. My new doctor couldn't see what medications I'd been on, what my ejection fraction was in 2015, whether I'd had any allergic reactions documented. So she ordered duplicate tests. I paid out of pocket for tests I'd already had done, and I spent the whole time worried that something critical might be missed because the system was broken.‚Źé‚ŹéI get it now. The fragmentation is real. The delays are infuriating. And I understand why the government wants to loosen things up, get the data moving, let patients see what's theirs. That's not crazy. But I'm also someone who spent twenty-three years in military service where privacy and security of information mattered. Not in some abstract way. In a way that affected whether I could do my job, whether my family was safe. So when I read that this rule is about removing safeguards around how AI systems make recommendations to doctors, I get anxious. I get anxious because I can see how quickly "remove burden" becomes "remove accountability."‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT CONCERNS ME ABOUT REMOVING THE DSI CERTIFICATION CRITERION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe rule wants to get rid of something called the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion. This is the part that requires companies making AI tools for doctors to be transparent about what their AI actually does. They have to disclose the intended use, the target population, what risks they know about, what data the system was trained on, whether it's been validated outside the lab. This sounds boring. It sounds like bureaucratic paperwork. But it's not. It's the thing that stops a company from selling a black-box algorithm to a hospital and having nobody understand what it's actually recommending or why.‚Źé‚ŹéI know enough about technology to understand that AI systems can be trained on biased data. They can perform differently for different populations. A model trained mostly on data from younger patients might not work well for someone like me, seventy-eight years old with a complicated cardiac history. If the company doesn't have to tell my doctor that, how would my doctor know? How would I know? The rule says this is about reducing burden on developers. But the burden isn't on the developers. The burden is on patients if something goes wrong.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology (ONC) set up the 2015 Edition Health IT Certification Criteria partly to make sure that when new tools came into clinical practice, there was some documentation of what they were supposed to do and what they weren't supposed to do. The DSI criterion was added in the 21 CFR Part 11 amendments and refined in subsequent years because people kept finding out too late that algorithms were making recommendations in ways nobody intended. The rule right now wants to remove this. I'm not saying the paperwork was perfect. But removing it entirely is like saying we don't need to know what's in a medication before a doctor prescribes it.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PROBLEM WITH SAYING PATIENTS CAN JUST REVIEW THE DECISIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe rule seems to assume that if a patient gets access to their records, they can just look at what the AI recommended and decide whether it's okay. That's not realistic. I have a high school diploma and a military service record. I don't understand how machine learning works. I know my own body. I know that I get lightheaded when my medications interact a certain way. I know that I'm stubborn and I don't always follow medical advice. But I don't know statistics. I don't know how to evaluate whether an algorithm's confidence interval is narrow enough to trust.‚Źé‚ŹéEven my new cardiologist in Birmingham, who is smart and well-trained, told me she doesn't have time to fully understand every AI tool that gets integrated into her practice. She relies on the fact that these tools are supposed to be tested, supposed to have documentation, supposed to be used only for what they're actually validated for. If we remove those requirements, we're not empowering patients. We're just making it so that nobody really knows what's happening. The patient doesn't know. The doctor doesn't fully know. The hospital doesn't know. And the company making the tool is the only one with full information, which is exactly the opposite of accountability.‚Źé‚ŹéI've thought about this a lot since my transfer problem, and I keep coming back to one thing: transparency has to work both ways. I want my medical records to move freely between providers. I want that fragmentation fixed. But I don't want that freed-up information to move into systems where nobody's checking whether those systems actually work, whether they're biased, whether they're appropriate for my age and my specific conditions. Those aren't contradictory goals. But this rule treats them like they are.‚Źé‚Źé**THE ACTUAL REQUIREMENT ISN'T AS BURDENSOME AS THE RULE SUGGESTS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe documentation that the DSI criterion requires isn't exotic. It's not asking companies to do something they shouldn't already be doing if they're serious about safety. Companies need to document what population their AI was designed for. That's basic. If you're making a tool for cardiac patients, you should know whether you tested it on patients like me, older patients with multiple comorbidities. You should know if your training data was mostly younger people or if it included a representative sample. This isn't regulatory overreach. This is basic due diligence.‚Źé‚ŹéThe criterion also requires documentation of known limitations and risks. Again, this should be standard. If your model has a 15 percent false positive rate in a certain subgroup, your customers need to know that. If your model was trained on data from a specific region or health system and might not generalize to another context, your customers need to know that. The companies making these tools already have this information. The requirement just makes sure they share it with the people who are actually using the tools in clinical practice.‚Źé‚ŹéExternal validation is part of the requirement too. That means the tool has been tested on data it wasn't trained on, ideally in a different health system or population. This is how you find out whether something that looked good in the lab actually works in the real world. It takes time and money. I understand that. But the alternative is what we already have happening in health care, which is that hospitals buy software that nobody has really validated outside the vendor's own testing environment, and then patients like me are the experiment.‚Źé‚ŹéI looked at what the ONC actually specified in the 2015 Edition Health IT Certification Criteria and subsequent guidance. The requirement for AI model cards and transparency disclosures is detailed in the certification procedure requirements, and it's not onerous. It's a structured way of documenting things that responsible companies should be documenting anyway. The rule would essentially say companies don't have to do this anymore, at least not in any standardized way. They could still do it if they want. But there would be no requirement. No common standard. No way for a hospital or a doctor to know if they're getting a tool that's been properly evaluated or one that's just being pushed out to see what happens.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THIS MATTERS FOR PEOPLE MY AGE**‚Źé‚ŹéI'm seventy-eight. In a few years, I might have multiple conditions. I might be taking a dozen medications. I might be seeing specialists in different health systems. The AI tools that get recommended to my doctors could significantly affect my care. And because I'm older, I'm in a smaller population for a lot of these tools. Developers might not have tested their algorithms on enough elderly patients to know how well they work for me specifically. That's not their fault necessarily. But I need to know that. My doctors need to know that. And if we remove the requirement to document this, we're just hoping nobody figures out too late that the tool doesn't work well for older patients.‚Źé‚ŹéI've read some of the literature on this. A study in JAMA in 2019 showed that an algorithm widely used to assess patient risk was significantly biased against Black patients because it was trained on biased data. Nobody knew until researchers looked at it carefully. Another study in Health Affairs looked at how often AI tools in health care are validated outside their original context, and the answer is not often enough. These are the kinds of problems that transparency and documentation requirements are supposed to catch before deployment, not after.‚Źé‚ŹéThe rule says this is about innovation and reducing burden. I understand the impulse. But I also know what happens when you deregulate something without a good safety mechanism in place. You get innovation, sure. But you also get corners cut. You get companies pushing out tools that aren't fully tested because there's no requirement to fully test them. And then you get patients, especially older patients in smaller populations, who end up using tools that were never properly validated for them.‚Źé‚Źé**THE BALANCE BETWEEN ACCESS AND SAFETY ISN'T AN EITHER-OR**‚Źé‚ŹéI want my records to be accessible. I want them to move between providers without six-week delays. I want my new doctor to be able to see what medications I've been on without ordering duplicate tests. Those things are good. They should happen. But I don't think they should happen at the cost of removing oversight of the AI tools that my doctors will use to make decisions about my care. These are separate issues. We can fix the fragmentation problem and keep the transparency requirements for decision support systems. We can do both.‚Źé‚ŹéThe rule frames this as if we have to choose. Either we reduce burden on developers and innovators, or we keep patients safe. But that's not how this actually works. What keeps patients safe is making sure that the tools being used in clinical practice are doing what they're supposed to do, for the populations they're supposed to do it for, with full knowledge of their limitations. That's not anti-innovation. That's pro-innovation done responsibly. There are plenty of companies making AI tools for health care that are perfectly willing to document their models, show their validation studies, disclose their limitations. Those companies should welcome a requirement like this. It levels the playing field against companies that are trying to cut corners.‚Źé‚ŹéI keep thinking about my situation last year. I was frustrated with the VA, frustrated with the delays, frustrated that my records were stuck in limbo. But if I found out afterward that my new doctor had been using an AI tool to make recommendations about my cardiac care, and that tool had never been validated on patients my age, and nobody ever told her that, and that tool made a recommendation that turned out to be wrong, I wouldn't think "oh, at least we reduced the regulatory burden on the developer." I would think "nobody was checking whether this thing actually worked."‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT NEEDS TO HAPPEN**‚Źé‚ŹéThe rule should keep the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion. It should keep the requirement for AI model cards and transparency disclosures. It should require documentation of intended use, target population, known limitations, training data characteristics, and external validation. These aren't burdensome requirements. They're basic accountability measures.‚Źé‚ŹéThe rule can still address the fragmentation and access problems. Those are real problems. The ONC and CMS can still work on removing unnecessary barriers to data sharing. They can still promote interoperability and patient access to records. But they should do that separately from removing safety requirements for AI tools. You can have both faster data access and AI tools that are properly documented and validated. The two things aren't mutually exclusive.‚Źé‚ŹéI understand that innovation matters. I understand that burden on developers is a real cost. But the burden on patients if an inadequately tested AI tool makes a bad recommendation is also a real cost. It might be a bigger cost. And that burden falls on people like me, people who don't have the technical expertise to understand what the tool is doing or whether it's appropriate for our particular situation.‚Źé‚ŹéThe rule right now is about unleashing prosperity. I get that framing. But prosperity means different things to different people. For me, prosperity means knowing that the tools my doctor uses to help make decisions about my care have been properly tested and documented. It means knowing that if a tool was only tested on younger patients, my doctor knows that. It means having confidence that the person making the recommendation, whether that's a doctor or an AI tool, actually understands the limitations of what they're recommending.‚Źé‚Źé**MOVING FORWARD**‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not opposed to innovation. I'm not opposed to reducing unnecessary burden. But I am opposed to removing accountability mechanisms that protect patients. The DSI certification criterion isn't perfect. Maybe it needs to be refined or updated. Maybe there are ways to make the documentation process more efficient. But removing it entirely is the wrong answer.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm going to submit this comment because I've lived through the problems that this rule is trying to solve. I know what it's like when information is fragmented and stuck in limbo. But I also know what it's like to put your trust in systems that are supposed to work, and to find out later that they don't, or that nobody was really checking whether they did. That's what I'm worried about here. That's why I think the transparency requirements for AI need to stay.‚Źé‚ŹéThe rule should move forward with fixing the access and interoperability problems. But it should keep the safeguards that make sure the tools being used in clinical practice are actually safe and appropriate. That's not asking for the moon. That's asking for basic accountability in a system that's going to affect real people's health.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-05T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-05T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ7‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving DSI transparency requirements creates a dangerous regulatory gap where AI-based clinical decision support tools face fewer disclosure obligations than traditional medical devices, undermining consistent safety standards.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒconcerned‚ôĒAlabama‚ôĒveteran‚ôĒ78‚ôĒWhen my VA cardiologist retired last year, I had to switch to a civilian provider in Birmingham, but it took six weeks to get my records transferred‚ÄĒrecords I'd accumulated over thirty years of military service. My new doctor had to order duplicate tests because the old files were stuck in limbo, and I worried the whole time that something critical might slip through the cracks. Now that I'm finally getting settled with this new practice, I'm terrified that opening up access to my medical history could mean insurance companies or employers might see things they shouldn't, especially after spending my career in sensitive positions where privacy mattered.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2392
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0055‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒLisa Young‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA caregiver argues that Decision Support Intervention certification requirements mandating AI model cards and transparency disclosures should be retained because they ensure clinicians understand algorithm limitations and enable patient safety acc...‚ôĒRE: Docket No. HHS-OS-2025-0002, Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity‚Źé‚ŹéLast spring, I was sitting in my mother's oncology clinic waiting room when something became very clear to me. Her new oncologist was ordering redundant tests, asking questions her rheumatologist had already answered months earlier, and most troubling, didn't have access to notes about her previous severe reactions to certain drug classes. It took us three weeks of phone calls and faxes to track down those old records ourselves. By the time we did, the oncology team had already flagged a potential drug interaction during routine review. The records existed. They just weren't talking to each other. I left that clinic furious, convinced that seamless data sharing was the only answer to fragmented care.‚Źé‚ŹéThen my sixteen-year-old son's therapist mentioned something in passing. She said his mental health notes, once entered into an interoperable network, could theoretically be accessed by any provider connected to that system. I asked what "any provider" meant. She wasn't entirely sure. That conversation changed how I think about this problem. I realized I'd been so focused on getting my mother's records to her oncologist that I hadn't thought about who else might see my son's therapy notes if we opened those doors wider. Not everyone in a health system should read a teenager's mental health documentation. Not everyone needs to. The more I've thought about this, the more I've come to believe that the real answer isn't choosing between fragmentation and transparency. It's about building systems that move information where it's actually needed, with safeguards that keep people from being exposed to unnecessary access.‚Źé‚ŹéThat brings me to why I'm writing this comment. The current certification requirements for Decision Support Interventions (DSI) require AI model cards and transparency disclosures that lay out what an algorithm was designed to do, who it's meant for, what risks it carries, and how it was trained. I've read some of the arguments for removing these requirements. The reasoning goes that they slow down innovation and create burden on developers. I understand that argument. But after watching my mother's care fragment across systems, and thinking about my son's privacy, I believe these requirements are doing something important that gets lost when we focus only on speed and burden reduction.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THIS MATTERS FOR PATIENT SAFETY**‚Źé‚ŹéLet me be direct about what happened with my mother's care. The oncologist prescribed a chemotherapy regimen without full information. The drug interaction that was eventually flagged in hindsight wasn't a minor concern. It was significant enough that her oncology team had to reconsider the entire treatment plan once they finally had access to her rheumatology notes. She had to wait an additional two weeks for a revised protocol. At seventy-three years old, with stage III cancer, two weeks is not insignificant. Time matters in cancer care.‚Źé‚ŹéNow, AI algorithms are making similar kinds of decisions in clinics every day. They're recommending treatment pathways. They're flagging which patients might benefit from certain interventions. They're helping providers make choices about medication, dosing, and sequencing. These aren't minor recommendations. They're clinical decisions that affect real people. The difference between an algorithm that knows a patient's full medical history and one that doesn't could be as consequential as the difference between my mother getting the right chemotherapy protocol on time and getting a delayed, revised one.‚Źé‚ŹéThe model card requirement exists because it forces developers to ask themselves hard questions before an algorithm goes into clinical use. What population was this trained on? If it was trained primarily on patients from academic medical centers in urban areas, will it work as well for rural patients? What happens if someone using this algorithm has a contraindication that the training data didn't capture? These aren't theoretical concerns. They're real questions about how AI performs when it meets actual patients who don't fit neatly into training datasets.‚Źé‚ŹéI keep coming back to something the oncologist said when we finally had my mother's full records. She said, "This changes everything about how I'd approach her treatment." An algorithm that doesn't know about previous adverse reactions, previous diagnoses, or previous treatment responses can't make that kind of informed adjustment. It might recommend something that looks optimal on paper but that's actually dangerous for this specific patient. Model cards and transparency disclosures don't solve that problem entirely, but they make it visible. They force the algorithm developer to say what the algorithm can and cannot do, what it was trained on, and what gaps exist in its knowledge.‚Źé‚Źé**THE INFORMED DECISION-MAKING PIECE**‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what troubles me about removing these certification requirements. A clinician using an AI tool needs to know whether that tool has been validated in populations that look like their patient. They need to know what training data went into it. They need to know what the algorithm is actually measuring and what it might miss. Right now, if a provider is using a DSI tool that has an approved model card, that provider can read what the intended use is, what the target population was, what the known risks are, and what external validation has been done. That information helps the provider make a judgment about whether the algorithm is appropriate for their specific patient.‚Źé‚ŹéTake away the model card requirement, and what happens? A provider still has to use some algorithm to assist with clinical decisions. But now they're using it without transparent information about what it can do and what it can't. That's not deregulation that unleashes prosperity. That's deregulation that leaves providers and patients vulnerable. I'm not saying that removing one certification criterion will cause immediate harm. What I'm saying is that the requirement exists because someone, somewhere, realized that transparency about AI in clinical settings saves lives.‚Źé‚ŹéMy experience has taught me that information fragmentation is real and dangerous. But so is information opacity. The answer isn't to swing the pendulum from "your data is locked in silos" all the way to "your data is everywhere and the algorithms using it are black boxes." The answer is to build systems where data moves where it needs to go, but where the tools using that data are transparent about what they're doing and why.‚Źé‚ŹéI work as a caregiver. That means I spend time in clinics, in hospitals, in medical offices. I see how providers struggle with incomplete information. I also see how providers struggle with too much information and too little transparency about what the information means. An algorithm is supposed to help with that. It's supposed to take all the data and make sense of it in a way that a human mind overwhelmed by charts and notes can't quite manage. But if the algorithm is doing that work invisibly, if there's no transparency about how it works or what it might miss, then it's not helping. It's just adding another layer of opacity.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT MODEL CARDS ACTUALLY DO**‚Źé‚ŹéLet me walk through what the model card requirement actually entails, because I think this gets lost in the deregulation conversation. A model card for a DSI tool includes several specific elements:‚Źé‚Źé- The intended use of the algorithm (what clinical problem is it supposed to solve)‚Źé- The target population (who is it designed for)‚Źé- The performance metrics (how well does it actually work)‚Źé- The known risks and limitations (what can go wrong)‚Źé- The training data characteristics (what data was used to build this)‚Źé- External validation results (has anyone other than the developer tested this)‚Źé‚ŹéThese aren't abstract requirements. They're concrete questions that force developers to think about whether their algorithm is actually ready for clinical use. They're also information that clinicians need to make informed decisions about whether to use the algorithm for a specific patient.‚Źé‚ŹéI've been thinking about what it would mean if we removed this requirement. Let's say a company develops an AI algorithm to help identify patients at risk for adverse drug reactions. That sounds useful. That sounds like something my mother's oncologist could have used. But what if the algorithm was trained primarily on data from patients under sixty-five? What if it was validated only in academic medical centers? What if the developers know it performs less well in certain racial or ethnic groups but they don't disclose that because there's no requirement to do so?‚Źé‚ŹéWithout a model card requirement, a provider using that algorithm might not know any of that. They might think they're using a tool that works well for all patients when actually it works well for young patients in academic centers but less well for older patients or patients from different backgrounds. That's not innovation. That's introducing bias into clinical care while calling it deregulation.‚Źé‚ŹéThe model card requirement doesn't prevent innovation. It doesn't stop companies from developing new algorithms. What it does is require them to be honest about what their algorithm can do and what it can't. That's not a burden. That's a baseline standard of transparency that keeps patients safe.‚Źé‚Źé**ACCOUNTABILITY AND REAL-WORLD CONSEQUENCES**‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what I haven't been able to stop thinking about since my mother's experience. When something goes wrong in clinical care, there needs to be a way to trace back what happened and why. If a provider uses an algorithm and that algorithm makes a recommendation that leads to harm, how do we investigate what went wrong? If there's a model card, if there's documentation about what the algorithm was supposed to do and what it was trained on, then we can actually investigate. We can ask, was this algorithm used outside its intended population? Was it used for a purpose it wasn't designed for? Did the developer fail to disclose known limitations?‚Źé‚ŹéWithout that documentation, the investigation is much harder. The algorithm becomes a black box. The provider might say, "The algorithm recommended this." The developer might say, "The algorithm works as designed." And the patient is left without a clear understanding of what went wrong or why.‚Źé‚ŹéI think about accountability a lot in my work as a caregiver. When something doesn't go right, families want to know why. They want to understand what happened and how to prevent it from happening again. That's not unreasonable. That's basic respect for the patient and family. The same principle applies to AI in healthcare. When an algorithm makes a recommendation that contributes to a bad outcome, there needs to be transparency about what the algorithm was supposed to do, what it actually did, and whether the developer disclosed the relevant limitations.‚Źé‚ŹéThe model card requirement creates that accountability. It creates a paper trail. It forces developers to be explicit about what they know and what they don't know about their algorithm's performance. If you remove that requirement, you remove that accountability. You're asking patients and providers to trust algorithms without any transparent basis for that trust.‚Źé‚Źé**THE BROADER PICTURE ON INTEROPERABILITY**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to circle back to where I started, because this connects to the broader deregulation proposal. I absolutely believe that health information should move more freely between providers. I want my mother's oncologist to have her rheumatology notes automatically, not three weeks later. I want clinicians to have complete information about their patients. That's not controversial. That's just good medicine.‚Źé‚ŹéBut interoperability and transparency about AI aren't in tension with each other. They're complementary. We need both. We need data to flow where it's needed. And we need the tools that use that data to be transparent about how they're using it, what they're good at, and what they might miss. Those two goals work together.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat I'm worried about is a version of deregulation that says, "We're going to make data flow freely and we're going to get rid of certification requirements for AI tools." That combination could actually make things worse. You'd have more data available, which is good. But you'd have less transparency about how AI is using that data, which is bad. The net effect might be more efficient fragmentation of care, but not better care.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT SHOULD STAY IN PLACE**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Decision Support Interventions certification criterion requiring model cards and transparency disclosures should stay. Here's why, concretely:‚Źé‚Źé1. Patient safety depends on knowing what an algorithm can and cannot do. Model cards create that knowledge.‚Źé‚Źé2. Informed clinical decision-making requires providers to understand the limitations of tools they're using. Model cards document those limitations.‚Źé‚Źé3. Accountability for algorithm performance requires transparency about how the algorithm was developed and validated. Model cards provide that transparency.‚Źé‚Źé4. Real-world clinical performance differs from training performance, and that gap needs to be documented. External validation results in model cards capture that gap.‚Źé‚Źé5. Bias and disparities in algorithm performance need to be disclosed so providers can use tools appropriately. Model cards require disclosure of known performance differences.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not saying the current model card requirement is perfect. I'm sure there are ways to make it less burdensome for developers while keeping it meaningful for patient safety. Maybe the requirement could be streamlined. Maybe there are ways to make it easier for smaller developers to comply. Those are reasonable conversations to have. But removing the requirement entirely isn't the answer. That's throwing the baby out with the bathwater.‚Źé‚Źé**FINAL THOUGHT**‚Źé‚ŹéWhen I left my mother's oncology clinic that day, frustrated by the fragmentation of her care, I wanted everything to be connected. I wanted perfect interoperability where all her records were always available to all her providers. I still want that. But I've learned something since then. I've learned that connectivity without transparency can create new kinds of harm. I've learned that the tools using all that connected data need to be trustworthy, and trustworthiness requires transparency.‚Źé‚ŹéThe model card requirement for DSI tools is one way to create that transparency. It's not perfect, but it's something. It's a way of saying that when AI is making recommendations about human health, we deserve to know what that algorithm was trained on, what it's supposed to do, what it might miss, and what the developer knows about its performance. That's not a burden on innovation. That's a baseline standard of respect for patients and the providers trying to care for them.‚Źé‚ŹéMy mother's care eventually worked out. Her oncologist adjusted the treatment plan once she had the full information. She's in remission now. But that adjustment took time. It added stress. It created uncertainty. If the system had been transparent from the beginning, if the algorithm recommending the initial protocol had come with clear documentation about what it could and couldn't do, maybe that adjustment could have happened sooner. That's what I mean by keeping the model card requirement. It's not about slowing innovation. It's about making sure innovation actually works for patients.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-16T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-16T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ8‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒurgent‚ôĒNorth Dakota‚ôĒcaregiver‚ôĒ38‚ôĒLast spring, I discovered my mother's oncologist had never received her full medical history from her previous rheumatologist‚ÄĒrecords that were directly relevant to her chemo tolerance‚ÄĒand by the time we tracked down those notes ourselves, a critical drug interaction had already been flagged in hindsight. The frustration of watching her care fragment across systems made me desperate for seamless data sharing, but then my teenage son's therapist mentioned that his notes could theoretically be accessed by any provider in a connected network, and suddenly I realized I was trading one kind of vulnerability for another.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2465
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0056‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDeborah Adams‚ôĒbeHuman Corp.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒbeHuman Corp., a multi-specialty primary care practice, argues that transparency requirements for clinical decision support tools should be retained because they enable responsible clinical practice and do not actually drive vendor switching costs...‚ôĒ**COMMENT ON PROPOSED DEREGULATORY ACTIONS FOR HEALTH IT CERTIFICATION CRITERIA**‚Źé‚ŹébeHuman Corp. respectfully submits this comment regarding the proposed modifications to health IT certification requirements, specifically addressing the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion and the transparency standards that accompany AI-assisted clinical decision-making tools.‚Źé‚ŹéOur organization operates a multi-specialty primary care practice in Cedar Rapids, Iowa, serving approximately 12,000 active patients across three locations. I have worked as the practice manager for eight years, and for the past year I have managed our transition to a new EHR system. That transition gives me perspective on both the costs of compliance and the genuine value that well-designed transparency requirements provide to practices like ours when we evaluate new technology.‚Źé‚Źé**THE COST OF SWITCHING IS REAL, BUT TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS ARE NOT THE PROBLEM**‚Źé‚ŹéWhen we made the decision last April to leave our previous EHR vendor, the decision itself was almost impossible. We had been with that system for eight years. Their annual certification maintenance costs had climbed to nearly $40,000 per year, which for a practice our size represents a meaningful percentage of our IT budget. The vendor's recertification requirements had become increasingly prescriptive, and they seemed designed more to lock practices in than to protect patients. But here's what I want to be clear about: the problem was not that we had to understand what the system was doing or how it worked. The problem was that we felt trapped.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen we finally switched systems, the transition itself was expensive. We spent roughly $60,000 on staff training and data migration services. We experienced workflow disruptions for six weeks. Several staff members who had mastered shortcuts in the old system had to relearn how to work efficiently. That was painful. But what helped us through that transition was knowing clearly what our new system would do and what it would not do. Our new vendor provided us with detailed documentation about their clinical decision support algorithms, including their intended uses, the populations they were designed for, and their known limitations. That transparency helped us train our clinicians appropriately. It helped us understand which recommendations we should rely on and which ones needed additional clinical judgment.‚Źé‚ŹéI mention this because I want to distinguish between two different kinds of burden. One kind of burden is the cost and complexity of switching systems, which is real and significant. The other kind of burden is understanding what a clinical tool is designed to do and what risks it carries. Those are not the same thing, and removing transparency requirements does not actually reduce switching costs. It just leaves providers like us in the dark about the tools we are using.‚Źé‚Źé**DECISION SUPPORT TOOLS ARE NOT NEUTRAL INFRASTRUCTURE**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule appears to treat certification requirements as though they uniformly slow innovation and increase costs for vendors. In some cases, this is true. But clinical decision support tools are not the same as other health IT infrastructure. They make recommendations that influence patient care. They are trained on data that may or may not be representative of the populations we serve. They have failure modes. They have edge cases where they perform poorly. A practice manager in Iowa needs to know these things.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen we were evaluating our current EHR system, one of the decision support modules was designed to flag patients at risk for type 2 diabetes based on clinical and demographic factors. The vendor was transparent about the training data: the model had been developed and validated on a large commercial claims database that skewed toward insured patients in urban areas. The vendor was honest that the model's performance in rural populations was unknown. This mattered to us. Our patient population is about 35 percent rural, and we have a disproportionate number of uninsured and underinsured patients. That transparency allowed us to make an informed decision about how much weight to give that particular recommendation in our workflows. We decided to use it as a screening tool but to apply additional clinical judgment for patients outside the training population. Would we have made that decision differently if we didn't know the model's limitations? Probably. And patient care might have suffered for it.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is what the existing Decision Support Interventions certification criterion actually does. It requires vendors to provide model cards that document intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. These are not abstract compliance burdens. They are the information that responsible clinicians need to use AI-assisted tools appropriately (see 21 CFR 868.1800 and the FDA guidance on clinical decision support software, 2019).‚Źé‚Źé**THE TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENT DOES NOT PREVENT MARKET ENTRY**‚Źé‚ŹéOne argument in the proposed rule is that certification requirements create barriers to market entry for smaller vendors and innovative startups. I understand this concern in theory. But in practice, I have not found that detailed documentation about a clinical tool's performance characteristics prevents smaller vendors from competing.‚Źé‚ŹéIn fact, our experience was the opposite. When we were evaluating EHR systems, we looked at three vendors. One was a large established vendor. Two were smaller companies that had built modern, cloud-based systems designed specifically for practices our size. Both smaller vendors provided us with clear documentation about their decision support algorithms. One of them won our business partly because their transparency was exceptional. They had conducted external validation studies on their algorithms in diverse patient populations, and they were willing to share those results. They also acknowledged limitations honestly. That vendor is now capturing market share in the small-to-medium practice space, and their transparency about their tools is part of their competitive advantage, not a barrier to their success.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving the transparency requirement would not make it easier for startups to compete. It would make it easier for all vendors, large and small, to avoid accountability. The vendors most likely to benefit from deregulation would be those with the most to hide about their algorithms' limitations. That does not seem like a recipe for innovation that serves patients or practices well.‚Źé‚Źé**CERTIFICATION REQUIREMENTS AND SWITCHING COSTS ARE SEPARABLE PROBLEMS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule bundles together several different regulatory burdens and presents them as a single problem. But they are not the same problem, and they should not be solved with a single solution.‚Źé‚ŹéThe switching costs that made it so difficult for us to leave our previous vendor were driven by factors that have nothing to do with decision support transparency requirements:‚Źé‚Źé- The vendor's proprietary data formats made it difficult and expensive to export our clinical records. Standardized data exchange formats like HL7 FHIR would have reduced this burden significantly (see the ONC Health IT Certification Program requirements, 45 CFR 170.102).‚Źé- The vendor's refusal to support interoperability standards meant we had to pay for custom integration services to connect their system to our lab, pharmacy, and hospital partners. Requiring vendors to support modern interoperability standards would address this directly.‚Źé- The vendor's annual recertification requirements were designed to force ongoing payments rather than to ensure safety. Streamlining recertification timelines, perhaps to every two or three years, would reduce this burden without compromising patient safety.‚Źé‚ŹéNone of these problems would be solved by removing the decision support transparency requirement. In fact, removing that requirement would address a problem we do not have while leaving the actual barriers to switching intact.‚Źé‚Źé**PATIENT SAFETY AND INFORMED DECISION-MAKING DEPEND ON TRANSPARENCY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe existing DSI criterion requires vendors to provide information about training data characteristics, intended use, target population, known risks, and external validation results. These requirements exist because clinical decision support tools can fail in ways that are not obvious to clinicians. A tool that works well for one population may perform poorly for another. A tool trained on data from a specific geographic region or insurance type may not generalize to different settings. These are not theoretical risks. They have been documented repeatedly in the literature on algorithmic bias in healthcare (see Obermeyer et al., "Dissecting racial bias in an algorithm used to manage the health of populations," Science, 2019, and related FDA guidance on AI/ML software modification 21 CFR Part 11).‚Źé‚ŹéWhen we use a clinical decision support tool without understanding its limitations, we risk making decisions that are not in our patients' best interest. A clinician who does not know that a diabetes risk model was trained on insured, urban populations may overweight that model's recommendations for a rural, uninsured patient. A clinician who does not know that a sepsis prediction algorithm was developed in an academic medical center may misapply it in a community hospital setting. These are not abstract compliance concerns. They are patient safety issues.‚Źé‚ŹéThe transparency requirement is not burdensome for vendors who have confidence in their tools. It is only burdensome for vendors whose tools have significant limitations they do not want to disclose. And if a tool has significant limitations that cannot be disclosed, that suggests the tool should not be certified for clinical use in the first place.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE RECOMMEND**‚Źé‚ŹébeHuman Corp. urges the Office of the Secretary and ONC to retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion in its current form, including the requirements for model cards and transparency disclosures. We further recommend that the agency consider the following modifications to reduce unnecessary burden while preserving patient safety:‚Źé‚Źé1. Streamline the recertification timeline for decision support tools that have not changed materially. A tool that has been stable for two years should not require the same level of recertification as a newly released tool. This would reduce vendor burden without compromising safety.‚Źé‚Źé2. Provide clear, specific guidance about what constitutes adequate external validation. Vendors should know upfront what evidence is required, so they can plan accordingly rather than discovering requirements late in the development process.‚Źé‚Źé3. Consider tiered requirements based on the risk level of the decision support tool. A tool that provides suggestions for patient education materials carries different risks than a tool that influences medication dosing. Not all tools need the same level of documentation.‚Źé‚Źé4. Require interoperability standards and data export capabilities as a condition of certification, rather than as separate requirements. This would address the actual switching costs that practices face without compromising transparency about clinical tools.‚Źé‚ŹéOur organization has direct experience with both the costs of vendor lock-in and the value of transparency about clinical decision support tools. These are not contradictory positions. We can reduce unnecessary regulatory burden while preserving the transparency requirements that enable responsible clinical practice. We urge the agency to distinguish between these issues and to retain the DSI criterion as a safeguard for patient safety and informed clinical decision-making.‚Źé‚ŹéThank you for the opportunity to comment on this proposal.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-05T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-05T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ5‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒconcerned‚ôĒIowa‚ôĒpractice manager‚ôĒ38‚ôĒLast spring, our clinic switched EHR systems after eight years with a vendor whose certification requirements had become prohibitively expensive to maintain‚ÄĒwe were paying nearly $40,000 annually just to stay compliant with their specifications. The transition itself cost us about $60,000 in training and data migration, but what struck me was how locked in we'd felt before that decision, unable to even consider alternatives because switching seemed financially impossible for a practice our size.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1782
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0057‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDonald Garcia‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe commenter argues against removing AI transparency requirements for clinical decision support systems, contending that documentation of model training data, intended use, target populations, and known risks is essential safety information for c...‚ôĒ**Re: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability; ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions**‚Źé‚ŹéWhen my daughter was diagnosed with Type 1 diabetes last year, I spent three weeks calling between her pediatrician, the endocrinologist, and urgent care. None of them could see her blood work or medication history without me hand-delivering printed records I'd compiled myself. I finally created a shared spreadsheet to track everything, but it struck me how fragile that system was‚ÄĒher health information scattered across my email and Google Drive when it should have been moving seamlessly from one provider to the next. That experience taught me something important: interoperability matters more in practice than it does in theory.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CASE FOR KEEPING AI TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéI'm concerned about removing the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion that currently requires AI model cards and transparency disclosures. The proposed changes would eliminate requirements for documenting intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. I understand the deregulatory impulse, but this specific requirement isn't bureaucratic overhead‚ÄĒit's a safety guardrail that affects real patient care.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what troubles me. When an AI system is helping a clinician decide my daughter's insulin dosing or flagging potential drug interactions, I want to know:‚Źé‚Źé- What population was this model trained on? (Does it work for pediatric patients, or just adults?)‚Źé- What are the known failure modes? (When does it give wrong recommendations?)‚Źé- Has anyone validated it outside the developer's lab?‚Źé- What data went into building this?‚Źé‚ŹéThese aren't academic questions. They go directly to whether a tool is safe for my kid. The current DSI criterion under 45 CFR 170.315(a)(1) requires exactly this documentation. Removing it would mean developers could deploy AI systems without disclosing these basics to clinicians or patients.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THIS MATTERS FOR INFORMED DECISION-MAKING**‚Źé‚ŹéThe rule change proposes this under the logic that certification criteria create burden and slow innovation. I get it. But certification isn't the same as approval‚ÄĒit's transparency. A clinician should know what they're putting their trust in. I should know whether the system recommending my daughter's care has been tested on kids like her or only on adult populations.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Federal Register notice (89 FR 23456, March 29, 2024) frames removing the DSI criterion as reducing unnecessary barriers. But the criterion doesn't prevent anyone from building or using AI tools. It just requires honesty about how they work and what they're good at. That's accountability, not barrier-building.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-01T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-01T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒsupportive‚ôĒColorado‚ôĒstay-at-home parent‚ôĒ54‚ôĒWhen my daughter was diagnosed with Type 1 diabetes last year, I spent three weeks coordinating between her pediatrician, the endocrinologist, and the urgent care clinic‚ÄĒcalling each one repeatedly because none of them could access her previous blood work or medication history without me hand-delivering paper records I'd printed myself. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0058‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒEmily Allen‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒPatients should have the right to understand how AI systems work before they're used in their medical care, and removing transparency requirements like model cards undermines accountability when AI-assisted diagnoses go wrong.‚ôĒMy daughter's recent experience getting her child's heart test results transferred between cardiologists opened my eyes to how complicated medical records still are. Now doctors are using artificial intelligence to help make decisions about people's health, and I keep coming back to this question: shouldn't patients have a right to know how those systems actually work before they're used on them? I'm not against progress. I just think removing requirements for model cards and transparency reports takes away accountability when something goes wrong. If an AI recommendation leads to a wrong diagnosis, how will a patient or family ever understand why it happened? That feels like trading one problem for another.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-22T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-22T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒmedium‚ôĒhopeful‚ôĒOregon‚ôĒretired nurse‚ôĒ77‚ôĒLast year when my daughter switched cardiologists, we spent weeks trying to get my grandchild's test results transferred‚ÄĒthe old office kept saying the new doctor had to request them officially. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0059‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDaniel Davis‚ôĒDental Standards Institute‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Dental Standards Institute urges the Office of the National Coordinator to retain current Decision Support Intervention certification requirements mandating model cards and transparency disclosures for AI-assisted clinical tools, arguing that ...‚ôĒJune 18, 2025‚Źé‚ŹéOffice of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology‚ŹéDepartment of Health and Human Services‚Źé200 Independence Avenue, S.W.‚ŹéWashington, D.C. 20201‚Źé‚ŹéRe: RFI on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity‚Źé‚ŹéThe Dental Standards Institute submits this comment in response to the Request for Information published in the Federal Register on May 20, 2025, regarding potential modifications to certification criteria and standards requirements for health information technology developers and providers. We write with particular attention to proposed changes affecting Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria and the transparency disclosures required of AI-assisted clinical tools.‚Źé‚ŹéOur organization represents a coalition of dental practitioners, patient advocates, and community health workers across low-income and underserved communities. We work directly with patients whose health information has been breached, misused, or shared without meaningful consent. Last year, our clinic partners in Orangeburg, South Carolina, discovered that patient dental records had been shared with data brokers who sold this information to third parties. One woman learned her employer knew details about her root canal treatment only when her supervisor referenced the procedure during a performance review, questioning why she'd missed work for what he termed "unnecessary dental work." This wasn't a hypothetical scenario about privacy erosion. It happened to real patients in our care.‚Źé‚ŹéThat experience shapes how we approach any regulatory proposal touching health data and AI in clinical settings. We are not opposed to innovation or to reducing unnecessary regulatory burden. We understand that excessive compliance costs can slow beneficial technology development and limit access to tools that could improve care, especially in under-resourced settings. But we also know that without clear, enforceable standards for how AI systems work and what data they use, innovation often proceeds at the expense of the people least able to protect themselves.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CURRENT DSI CERTIFICATION REQUIREMENT AND WHY IT MATTERS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current certification criterion for Decision Support Interventions requires that AI-assisted clinical tools include model cards and transparency disclosures documenting intended use, target population, known risks and limitations, characteristics of training data, and results of external validation testing. This requirement exists in the ONC Health IT Certification Program (45 CFR Part 170, Subpart C, Section 170.315(b)(6)). We understand that some developers argue this documentation burden is excessive and that market competition alone would drive quality and transparency. We disagree, and our disagreement is grounded in what we've actually observed in health data markets.‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, the dental care market is not a market where patient choice and competition naturally enforce transparency. Most patients don't shop for dental care based on what AI systems their provider uses, or how those systems were trained, or what populations they were validated against. They go to the dentist their insurance covers, or the one they can afford, or the one in their neighborhood. They certainly don't ask to see a model card before their dentist uses an AI tool to assess their risk for periodontal disease or recommend treatment options. The idea that transparency emerges naturally from competition assumes patients have information and choice. They often have neither.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, the dental field has already seen what happens when vendors push tools into clinical settings without robust validation standards. Over the past three years, we've documented at least a dozen instances where AI-assisted diagnostic tools performed significantly differently across racial and ethnic groups, with lower sensitivity in detecting early-stage disease in Black and Latino patients. These weren't tools that were maliciously designed to discriminate. They were tools trained on datasets that didn't adequately represent diverse populations, and the developers either didn't know it or didn't disclose it. The model cards and external validation data required by the current DSI criterion would have flagged these disparities before deployment.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Federal Register notice on Health IT Certification Program (88 FR 3066, January 19, 2023) emphasized that certification criteria exist to ensure health IT developers meet baseline standards for safety, effectiveness, and interoperability. The DSI criterion isn't a convenience or a nice-to-have. It's a floor. And that floor exists because without it, vulnerable patients end up relying on systems they don't understand, trained on data they don't know about, with risks they were never told existed.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT THE PROPOSED CHANGES WOULD MEAN FOR PATIENT SAFETY AND ACCOUNTABILITY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe RFI asks whether DSI certification criteria should be modified or removed entirely. It suggests that the documentation requirements create compliance costs that slow innovation without clear evidence that the requirements improve clinical outcomes. We want to address this directly: the absence of evidence that documentation prevents harm is not evidence that documentation is unnecessary.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider what happens when an AI system trained primarily on patient data from suburban practices with predominantly white, insured populations gets deployed in a rural dental clinic serving agricultural workers and low-income families. The system was validated on one population. It's now being used on another. Without required external validation disclosures, the dentist using the tool has no systematic way to know whether the recommendations it generates are reliable for their patient population. They might believe the system works because a vendor told them it did. They might discover it doesn't work only after it's harmed patients.‚Źé‚ŹéThis isn't theoretical. In 2023, a widely-used AI system for caries risk assessment was found to have substantially lower specificity in detecting early caries in children with high-sugar diets, a population common in low-income communities. The system had been trained primarily on data from children in private practices where dietary counseling had been provided. When it was used in community health centers where many children had limited access to preventive care, it systematically overestimated risk for some children and underestimated it for others. The tool was already in use in dozens of clinics before anyone documented this validation gap. If the current DSI certification requirement had been in place at the time of deployment, external validation across diverse populations would have been mandatory before certification, and this disparity would have been caught before patients were harmed.‚Źé‚ŹéWe also want to address the argument that removing documentation requirements would increase innovation and market entry for smaller developers. We don't think this is wrong as a general principle. But the DSI criterion doesn't require developers to conduct expensive clinical trials or build infrastructure. It requires them to document what they've already done: what population they trained their system on, what risks they've identified, what external testing has shown. This is documentation, not research. A small developer who builds a careful, responsible AI tool shouldn't find it burdensome to say so.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat we worry about is that removing this requirement would create a two-tier market: responsible developers who document their work and don't get certified, therefore can't be purchased by large health systems with compliance requirements, and less careful developers who avoid documentation and can be purchased more cheaply by under-resourced clinics that have no capacity to do independent validation. This doesn't help smaller developers. It helps the ones who cut corners.‚Źé‚Źé**PATIENT AGENCY AND THE LIMITS OF MARKET SOLUTIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe RFI also raises the question of whether patients and clinicians can adequately assess AI tool quality without certification criteria. The underlying assumption seems to be that transparency about tool performance would emerge naturally if regulation didn't mandate it, and that patients and clinicians would use that information to make better choices.‚Źé‚ŹéWe've spent the last eighteen months working with patients who discovered their health information had been sold without their knowledge or consent. Many of them told us something we found striking: even when they found out what happened, they had little practical recourse. The data had already been shared. They couldn't un-share it. They couldn't sue effectively because they didn't have the resources for litigation. They couldn't switch providers because they lived in areas with limited dental access. What they could do was ask their dentist why it happened, and many dentists themselves didn't know. They'd signed vendor agreements that included data-sharing language, but they hadn't read them closely or hadn't understood the implications.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is the population we worry about when we consider removing transparency requirements for AI systems. These are not patients who have the time, knowledge, or resources to independently evaluate whether a vendor's claims about an AI system are true. They're not going to request external validation studies. They're not going to ask about the racial composition of the training dataset. They're going to trust their dentist, and their dentist is going to trust the vendor, and somewhere in that chain of trust, accountability disappears.‚Źé‚ŹéThe 21st Century Cures Act (42 U.S.C. 300jj et seq.) was built on the premise that health information should be more transparent and more accessible to patients. The information blocking provisions in 45 CFR Part 171 reflect a commitment to ensuring that patients and providers can access and use health data without unnecessary barriers. We don't see how removing certification requirements for the AI systems that analyze that data is consistent with the underlying principle. If anything, it cuts against it. It says patients have a right to their data but not a right to know how that data is being used to train the systems that will recommend their treatment.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC CONCERNS ABOUT REMOVING THE MODEL CARD REQUIREMENT**‚Źé‚ŹéThe model card requirement in 45 CFR 170.315(b)(6)(i) asks developers to document:‚Źé‚Źé1. Intended use and target population‚Źé2. Characteristics of the training data, including population demographics‚Źé3. Known risks and limitations‚Źé4. Results of external validation testing‚Źé5. Performance metrics across subgroups‚Źé‚ŹéWe want to be specific about why each of these matters, because the RFI suggests they might be redundant or unnecessary.‚Źé‚ŹéIntended use and target population: We've seen vendors market AI tools for general clinical use that were actually trained and validated only in specific contexts. A tool trained to assess caries risk in adults was marketed to pediatric practices. A tool trained on data from patients with private insurance was used in federally qualified health centers. The dentists using these tools often didn't realize the mismatch. Requiring documented intended use would at least create a clear record of what the developer said the tool was for, which could be compared to how it's actually being used.‚Źé‚ŹéTraining data characteristics: This is where disparities hide. If a developer trains an AI system on data that's 85% white, 70% insured, 60% from suburban practices, that's important information. It doesn't mean the tool is bad. But it means anyone using it should know that the tool was built on the experiences of a particular population and might not perform the same way for other populations. Without documented training data characteristics, this information stays proprietary, and patients and clinicians don't get to know it.‚Źé‚ŹéKnown risks and limitations: We've reviewed vendor marketing materials for a dozen AI-assisted clinical tools. Almost none of them mention risks or limitations. They mention features and benefits. The model card requirement forces developers to articulate what they know their system can't do or might do badly. This is essential information for clinical decision-making. A dentist needs to know that a tool is less reliable for patients with certain medical histories, or that it's been tested less thoroughly in certain age groups. Without documented limitations, the dentist assumes the tool works equally well for all patients, which may not be true.‚Źé‚ŹéExternal validation: This is the most important part. A developer can claim their tool works well. But external validation means independent testing on data the developer didn't create and didn't control. It's the difference between a company saying "our product is good" and a third party saying "we tested this product in our setting and here's what we found." External validation across diverse populations catches the kinds of disparities we've seen in AI-assisted dental tools. It's not a burdensome requirement. It's a basic accountability mechanism.‚Źé‚Źé**ADDRESSING THE BURDEN ARGUMENT**‚Źé‚ŹéThe RFI emphasizes that compliance costs can slow innovation and that developers, especially smaller ones, may be discouraged from entering the market if certification requirements are too onerous. We understand this concern, and we don't dismiss it. But we want to offer some pushback on the premise.‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, the DSI certification requirement has been in place since 2015. The dental technology market hasn't collapsed. Developers continue to create and deploy AI-assisted tools. Some of them have gone through the certification process without apparent difficulty. The burden, if it exists, hasn't prevented innovation.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, the documentation required by the model card is something responsible developers have incentive to do anyway. If you're building a tool for clinical use, you need to know what population you trained it on, what risks exist, and whether it works in diverse settings. A developer who skips these steps isn't being more efficient. They're being reckless. Requiring documentation doesn't add much cost. It just ensures that the documentation exists and is available.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, we should acknowledge that there are real compliance costs to certification, and some of them fall disproportionately on smaller developers. But the solution isn't to remove transparency requirements. It's to streamline the certification process itself, to provide guidance and support to developers, and to consider tiered certification pathways for different types of tools and different levels of risk. A tool that recommends treatment options for a patient with low dental disease burden is lower risk than a tool that diagnoses oral cancer. Both might benefit from transparency requirements, but the level of external validation required could be different.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Health IT Certification Program, administered by ONC under 42 U.S.C. 300jj-52, already includes pathways for different certification levels and different types of developers. The regulations already recognize that one-size-fits-all approaches don't work. We should use those existing pathways to address burden concerns rather than removing the transparency requirements that protect patients.‚Źé‚Źé**THE EQUITY DIMENSION**‚Źé‚ŹéWe want to return to where we started: the experience of patients in low-income communities whose health information was shared without their knowledge or consent.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen regulatory requirements are weakened or removed, the burden of that change doesn't fall equally. Large health systems with resources to conduct independent validation of AI tools can do so and can demand that vendors provide the documentation they need. Smaller clinics, community health centers, and safety-net providers don't have those resources. They rely on certification criteria to establish a baseline of transparency and accountability. When that baseline is removed, the gap between what large systems can demand and what smaller systems can access widens. Patients in under-resourced communities end up using tools that have been less carefully vetted and about which less is known.‚Źé‚ŹéWe've seen this pattern in health IT generally. When certification requirements are weak, vendors target under-resourced settings with cheaper products that have fewer features and less rigorous validation. We don't want to see this happen with AI-assisted clinical tools. We want to see the opposite: we want to see transparency requirements that ensure every patient, regardless of where they receive care, is treated using tools that have been carefully evaluated and whose risks and limitations are known.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is fundamentally an equity issue. The model card requirement in the current DSI certification criterion is an equity tool. It ensures that the standards applied to AI systems are the same whether they're deployed in a suburban practice or a community health center. Removing that requirement would likely increase disparities in tool quality and validation across different healthcare settings.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE'RE ASKING FOR**‚Źé‚ŹéWe are asking the Office of the National Coordinator to maintain the current DSI certification criterion requiring model cards and transparency disclosures. Specifically, we ask that 45 CFR 170.315(b)(6) remain in effect with its current requirements for documentation of intended use, target population, training data characteristics, known risks and limitations, and external validation results.‚Źé‚ŹéWe also ask that ONC consider the following refinements to address legitimate burden concerns:‚Źé‚ŹéThe agency could provide standardized templates for model cards that developers could use to document required information more efficiently. This would reduce compliance costs without reducing transparency.‚Źé‚ŹéONC could establish tiered external validation requirements based on tool risk level. Lower-risk tools might require validation in one or two additional settings. Higher-risk tools would require more extensive external validation.‚Źé‚ŹéThe agency could create a technical assistance program to help smaller developers understand certification requirements and prepare documentation. This would lower barriers to market entry without lowering standards.‚Źé‚ŹéONC could establish a database where certified tools and their model cards are publicly accessible. This would help clinicians and patients understand what tools are available and how they were validated. It would also create competitive pressure on developers to have strong validation results.‚Źé‚ŹéWe recognize that the current regulatory environment has costs and that some of those costs fall on developers who are trying to innovate responsibly. But we also know that removing transparency requirements would impose different costs, and those costs would be borne primarily by patients who have the least ability to protect themselves. We believe the current DSI certification criterion represents a reasonable balance between supporting innovation and protecting patients. We ask that ONC preserve it.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe conversation about deregulation in health IT often assumes that removing rules is good for innovation and that keeping rules is bad for innovation. We don't think it's that simple. Rules can be poorly designed and can create unnecessary burden. But rules can also establish baseline standards that protect vulnerable people and create conditions under which responsible innovation can flourish.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI model card requirement is a well-designed rule. It doesn't prevent innovation. It doesn't require expensive clinical trials. It requires documentation of work that responsible developers should be doing anyway. It creates transparency about how AI systems work and who they work for. And it provides particular protection for patients in under-resourced communities who don't have the capacity to independently evaluate whether the tools their clinicians use are safe and effective.‚Źé‚ŹéWe ask that ONC retain this requirement. We also ask that the agency work with developers and stakeholders to address legitimate concerns about compliance burden through streamlined guidance, technical assistance, and tiered requirements rather than through removal of the transparency standards themselves. Innovation and accountability don't have to be in tension. But when they are, patients should come first.‚Źé‚ŹéThank you for the opportunity to comment.‚Źé‚ŹéRespectfully submitted,‚Źé‚ŹéDaniel Davis‚ŹéCommunity Organizer‚ŹéDental Standards Institute‚ŹéOrangeburg, South Carolina‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-26T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-26T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ10‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHospital and health system procurement teams need transparent AI model information to make evidence-based decisions about which tools to adopt and how to implement them safely in their local contexts.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒhopeful‚ôĒSouth Carolina‚ôĒcommunity organizer‚ôĒ47‚ôĒLast year, I worked with a coalition of low-income patients at our clinic in Orangeburg who discovered their dental records had been shared with a data broker without consent‚ÄĒthey only found out when one woman's employer started asking oddly specific questions about her root canal history during a performance review. That experience taught me that access and transparency aren't opposites; they're two sides of the same coin, and we need rules that don't force vulnerable people to choose between knowing what's happening to their information and actually getting care.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ3048
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0060‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒJoseph Nelson‚ôĒNORC at the University of Chicago‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒNORC at the University of Chicago opposes deregulation of AI clinical decision support systems, arguing that eliminating the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirement for model cards and transparency disclosures would create i...‚ôĒSubmitted to the Federal Docket on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability‚Źé‚ŹéTwo years ago, a woman in Arkansas received a diagnosis that should have triggered the fastest possible path to treatment. Instead, she faced a six-week delay while her oncology team manually compiled genetic marker data from three separate laboratory databases. Each lab had discontinued data-sharing arrangements following recent regulatory relaxation. Each was protecting proprietary algorithms as competitive leverage. The postdoctoral fellow at NORC at the University of Chicago who has spent every day since wrestling with that delay understands something that markets alone do not always price correctly: fragmentation of medical infrastructure kills people in ways that balance sheets never capture.‚Źé‚ŹéThis comment addresses the proposed deregulatory framework outlined in the ASTP/ONC Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability notice. Specifically, it concerns the proposal to relax or eliminate the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion that currently requires AI model cards and transparency disclosures. That criterion mandates disclosure of intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. The request invites input on whether deregulation increases innovation and competition or, conversely, creates conditions for market consolidation and opacity that ultimately harm patients and clinicians. NORC recommends retaining the DSI certification requirement in its current form.‚Źé‚ŹéThe case for deregulation rests on a reasonable intuition: regulatory burden stifles innovation, certification costs exclude smaller vendors, and competition should be trusted to police quality.
