That intuition has merit in many contexts. But healthcare decision support is not an ordinary market. The asymmetry between developer knowledge and end-user knowledge is profound. A clinician using an AI system to interpret imaging or predict patient deterioration cannot easily verify whether the model was trained on representative populations, whether it performs equally across demographic groups, or whether the developers disclosed known failure modes. The clinician has no access to the training data. She cannot independently validate external testing. She relies entirely on transparency disclosures to make an informed choice about deploying that tool in her clinic.‚Źé‚ŹéRemove that requirement and something else replaces it: competitive incentives to conceal weakness. A developer with a model that performs poorly in certain populations has every reason to simply not disclose that fact. A vendor whose AI system fails in ways that could expose the hospital to liability has no requirement to warn users. Market competition does not solve this problem because the information asymmetry prevents the market from working. Hospitals cannot choose between two AI systems if neither system is required to disclose what each one can and cannot do. Clinicians cannot make informed decisions about patient safety if the tools they use are black boxes by design.‚Źé‚ŹéThe empirical record bears this out. Research published in JAMA Network Open in 2023 examined AI clinical decision support systems across multiple studies and found that 67 percent of published models lacked adequate reporting of validation data, 71 percent did not report performance across demographic subgroups, and 44 percent provided no information about training data characteristics. This is not because developers are malicious. It is because no one required them to do otherwise. The moment transparency becomes optional, transparency becomes rare. Developers who compete on price have little incentive to bear the costs of comprehensive documentation and external validation if their competitors do not. The result is a race to the bottom in disclosure, not a race to the top in quality.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criterion exists to correct that market failure. It requires that before an AI system can be certified as health IT, the developer must provide a model card documenting intended use, target population, known performance limitations, training data characteristics, and results from external validation in populations different from the training set. This is not an onerous requirement. Model cards are becoming standard practice across the industry, supported by guidance from the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) and adopted voluntarily by leading AI developers. The requirement is not burdensome. It is a floor, not a ceiling.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat would happen if this floor is removed? First, transparency would become optional. Developers competing on cost would drop documentation to reduce overhead. Those competing on features would prioritize new functionality over validation in diverse populations. The result would be a market in which some vendors maintain rigorous disclosure practices and others do not, but purchasers cannot distinguish between them because no standard applies to all. Hospitals and clinics would face a choice: pay a premium for vendors who voluntarily disclose, or save money with vendors who do not. Cost pressure would drive consolidation toward the latter. The vendors maintaining high transparency standards would lose market share. They would either reduce their own disclosure practices to compete or exit the market. Over time, the average quality of transparency would fall, not rise.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, patient safety would suffer in ways that are difficult to quantify but not difficult to predict. An AI system trained predominantly on imaging data from academic medical centers, where the patient population skews older and whiter than the general population, may perform differently when deployed in a community clinic serving younger patients with greater ethnic diversity. If the developer was not required to test in diverse populations and disclose the results, the clinic would deploy the system without knowing that it works less well for their patient population. Clinicians would trust the tool more than they should. Patients would receive care calibrated to populations they do not resemble. Harms would accumulate slowly, attributed to individual clinical judgment rather than to systematic bias in the underlying algorithm.‚Źé‚ŹéThe case for deregulation assumes that market forces will police quality. But markets require transparency to function. They require that buyers can observe and compare quality. They require that bad actors face consequences for poor quality. Healthcare decision support does not meet those conditions. Clinicians cannot observe the quality of an AI system directly. They cannot test it comprehensively against their own patient populations before deploying it. They cannot easily switch to a competitor if problems emerge. They are locked in by switching costs, integration with existing workflows, and time constraints. The market does not work under those conditions. Deregulation would not unleash competition. It would enable consolidation and opacity.‚Źé‚ŹéNORC further notes that the regulatory framework being proposed would eliminate transparency requirements precisely when AI systems are becoming more widely used in clinical settings and when evidence of performance disparities across populations is mounting. A systematic review published in Nature Medicine in 2022 examined 228 AI clinical decision support systems and found that only 6 percent reported disaggregated performance data by race or ethnicity. When researchers conducted independent testing of several widely used systems, they found substantial performance gaps. An AI system for predicting sepsis risk, deployed across a large hospital network, performed substantially worse for Black patients than white patients because the training data reflected historical patterns in which Black patients received less aggressive treatment. Developers did not set out to create a biased system. But they did not discover the bias because they did not test for it, and they were not required to disclose it.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving the DSI certification requirement would make such failures more likely to go undetected. It would create stronger incentives for developers to avoid testing in diverse populations, because testing costs money and creates the risk of discovering problems that must then be disclosed. The rational response of a competitive firm is to test less, not more. The rational response is to document less. The result is a healthcare system in which biases become embedded deeper in the infrastructure because the infrastructure itself is designed to conceal them.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal to deregulate decision support systems should be understood in the context of broader trends in health information technology. Over the past decade, as barriers to entry have fallen and venture capital has flowed into digital health, the market has become increasingly concentrated. According to data from CB Insights, the five largest health IT vendors now control more than 40 percent of the market. Epic Systems, Cerner (now part of Oracle), Athena Health, and two others dominate the landscape. When consolidation reaches this level, competition cannot be relied upon to drive quality. The firms that dominate the market have little incentive to innovate on transparency or validation. They have every incentive to protect proprietary algorithms and resist disclosure.‚Źé‚ŹéSmaller vendors, who might have competitive incentives to differentiate on transparency, face a different problem: they cannot afford the compliance costs of certification if certification is mandatory. But certification costs are not the problem being addressed here. The DSI criterion does not require expensive third-party testing or lengthy approval processes. It requires documentation. It requires that developers disclose what they have already discovered about their systems' performance. The burden is real but modest. Removing the requirement would not help small vendors. It would help large vendors who want to reduce transparency without facing competitive disadvantage.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is where the sister's case becomes relevant to policy. Two years ago, when her oncologist wanted to cross-reference genetic markers against three laboratory databases, the databases had recently stopped sharing data. They had not stopped sharing for technical reasons. They had stopped sharing because regulatory requirements around information blocking had been relaxed, giving each lab more latitude to treat its database as proprietary. Each lab had developed proprietary algorithms for interpreting genetic markers. Each lab could now protect those algorithms by refusing to share raw data with competitors. The result was fragmentation. The oncologist could not get comprehensive information quickly. The patient lost six weeks. The cancer progressed.‚Źé‚ŹéThat patient's case illustrates what happens when deregulation prioritizes developer advantage over clinical utility. The same logic applies to decision support systems. If transparency requirements are eliminated, developers will be free to treat their algorithms as black boxes. They will have no obligation to disclose performance limitations. They will have no incentive to validate in diverse populations. Hospitals will deploy systems they do not fully understand. Clinicians will trust tools more than the evidence warrants. Patients will receive care based on algorithms trained on populations they do not resemble. The harms will be distributed across many patients and many institutions, making them harder to detect and attribute.‚Źé‚ŹéNORC recommends retaining the DSI certification criterion without modification. The criterion should continue to require that AI clinical decision support systems be accompanied by model cards documenting intended use, target population, known performance limitations across demographic groups, training data characteristics, and results from external validation. This is not a burden on innovation. It is a floor beneath which innovation should not fall. It is a requirement that developers document what they have already discovered about their systems' performance. It protects patients and clinicians. It enables informed decision-making. It creates accountability for systems that affect health and safety.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the concern is that certification costs exclude small vendors, the solution is not to eliminate transparency requirements. The solution is to streamline the certification process, reduce fees, or provide grant funding to smaller developers. These approaches would increase competition while maintaining the transparency floor. Eliminating transparency requirements would not increase competition. It would enable consolidation and reduce the information available to purchasers and end-users.‚Źé‚ŹéNORC further recommends that if any aspects of the DSI criterion are modified, the modifications should strengthen rather than weaken transparency requirements. Specifically, the criterion should be expanded to require that developers test and report performance across demographic groups defined not only by race and ethnicity but also by age, sex, insurance status, and comorbidity burden. Current guidance requires testing across "meaningful populations," but developers have considerable discretion in interpreting what is meaningful. Explicit requirements would reduce that discretion and increase the likelihood that performance disparities are detected and disclosed.‚Źé‚ŹéThe broader regulatory framework being proposed appears to prioritize developer burden reduction over patient safety and clinician decision support. That prioritization is misaligned with the core function of health IT. The purpose of health IT is to improve patient outcomes and support clinicians in delivering better care. That purpose is not served by reducing transparency. It is served by increasing transparency. It is served by requiring that developers disclose what they know about how their systems work and where they might fail. The market for health IT is not ordinary. It cannot be trusted to police quality without transparency requirements. Removing those requirements would not unleash innovation. It would unleash opacity.‚Źé‚ŹéIn conclusion, NORC urges the Department of Health and Human Services and the Office of the National Coordinator to maintain the DSI certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures. The criterion is not a burden on innovation. It is a protection for patients and clinicians. It enables informed decision-making about tools that affect health and safety. It creates accountability for systems deployed across healthcare. Removing the criterion would fragment the information available to purchasers and end-users. It would create incentives for developers to conceal rather than disclose performance limitations. It would enable consolidation and opacity in a market already dominated by a small number of large vendors. It would make healthcare less safe, not more innovative.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-07T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-07T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ7‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe agency's claim that 'no publicly available evidence' supports transparency requirements conflates absence of evidence with evidence of absence; lack of published studies does not mean the criterion provides no value.‚ôĒacademic‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒangry‚ôĒArkansas‚ôĒpostdoctoral fellow‚ôĒ46‚ôĒWhen my sister was diagnosed with late-stage ovarian cancer two years ago, her oncologist wanted to cross-reference her genetic markers against three different lab databases to identify the most targeted treatment option, but couldn't‚ÄĒthe labs had stopped sharing data after regulatory requirements were relaxed, each protecting their proprietary algorithms as a competitive advantage. She ended up waiting six weeks while her team pieced together information manually, time we didn't have, and I've spent every day since furious that what looked good on paper as "market freedom" actually fragmented the very infrastructure that could have saved her life.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2179
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0061‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒThomas Anderson‚ôĒBroadspire, Inc.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒWhile supporting deregulation of duplicative EHR compliance costs, Broadspire argues that removing AI transparency requirements in clinical settings would harm patient safety and disadvantage smaller providers who cannot afford to deploy unvalidat...‚ôĒRe: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity‚Źé‚ŹéOver the past year, Broadspire's three-facility system spent roughly $400,000 just maintaining EHR certification compliance, with half that burden coming from vendors who effectively lock us into their ecosystem because switching would mean starting over with regulatory paperwork. I understand the instinct to cut redundant burden, and some of that compliance layering genuinely is wasteful. But I need to be direct: removing transparency requirements for AI systems in clinical settings is not the same as eliminating duplicative EHR certification paperwork. Model cards and validation documentation are not vendor gatekeeping. They are how clinicians like our medical staff know whether an algorithm is safe for our patient population before we deploy it. Our smaller affiliated clinic learned this the hard way last year when they tried implementing a regional EHR solution but couldn't afford the transition costs. Weakening AI transparency criteria will make that problem worse, not better. Smaller providers and regional vendors cannot absorb the cost of deploying unvalidated systems. What I'm asking is that you distinguish between redundant burden and essential safeguard.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-13T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-13T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒmedium‚ôĒconcerned‚ôĒMississippi‚ôĒchief medical officer‚ôĒ46‚ôĒMy hospital system spent nearly $400,000 last year just to maintain EHR certification compliance across our three facilities, and half of that went to vendors who basically held us hostage because switching systems would mean starting from scratch with all the regulatory paperwork. When our smaller affiliated clinic tried to implement a more affordable regional solution last year, they couldn't because the certification requirements made it economically impossible for anyone but the big vendors to support them.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to understand how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before clinical deployment‚ÄĒremoving model cards eliminates the accountability mechanisms that protect patient safety. | Transparency requirements like model cards are not duplicative burden; they are essential safeguards that enable informed clinical decision-making and help providers identify when AI systems may not be appropriate for their patient populations. | While I understand the cost pressures vendors face, eliminating certification criteria for AI transparency will disproportionately harm smaller healthcare systems and regional vendors who cannot absorb compliance costs‚ÄĒthe opposite of market competition.‚ôĒAs a CMO managing compliance costs across multiple facilities, I recognize that transparency requirements for AI systems are fundamentally different from redundant EHR certification paperwork‚ÄĒthey protect patients and clinicians, not just regulators.‚ôĒONC Health IT Certification Program requirements for AI/ML transparency (21st Century Cures Act Final Rule)‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ184
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0062‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDonald Miller‚ôĒTexas Medical Association‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Texas Medical Association opposes removing the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures, arguing that documented information about algorithmic training data, validation,...‚ôĒ**RE: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity**‚Źé‚Źé**Docket No. HHS-OS-2024-0010**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Texas Medical Association appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory framework. We represent over 55,000 physicians across Texas, ranging from solo practitioners in rural communities to large integrated health systems managing millions of patient records annually. Our members work in environments where the gap between regulatory intent and clinical reality has grown increasingly stark. We understand the impulse to reduce certification burden, and we don't dismiss the legitimate costs of compliance. But we need to be direct: removing the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion, particularly the requirement for AI model cards and transparency disclosures, would hollow out one of the few mechanisms we have to ensure that algorithmic tools used in patient care don't harm the people we serve.‚Źé‚ŹéLet me be concrete about why this matters. The patient I mentioned earlier, the one whose drug interaction was finally caught when her records consolidated, would likely have benefited from explicit documentation about what her AI-assisted diagnostic tools were trained on and what populations they were validated in. Her endocrinologist used a clinical decision support system that flagged potential interactions, but that system had been trained primarily on data from academic medical centers with patient populations that looked very different from her own. Without transparency about those limitations, the tool appeared more authoritative than it was. The model card requirement in the current DSI criterion exists precisely because of scenarios like this. It's not bureaucratic overhead. It's a paper trail that lets clinicians make informed decisions about when to trust an algorithm and when to override it.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CURRENT LANDSCAPE OF AI IN CLINICAL DECISION SUPPORT**‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criterion requires that AI models used in clinical decision support include publicly available documentation of intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results (45 CFR 170.102(c)). This isn't onerous compared to what drug companies face under FDA review. The model card itself is typically 10 to 15 pages of structured information. Our members who've reviewed these documents tell us they actually use them. They check whether a tool was validated in their patient population. They note where external validation differed from internal testing. They understand the limits of the tool's applicability. Without this documentation, clinicians are left guessing about whether a black-box recommendation applies to their specific patient in their specific context.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat concerns us is that the deregulatory proposal appears to treat AI tools in clinical settings as equivalent to other software that might have lower stakes. A bug in a scheduling system is a headache. A bug in a clinical decision support system that systematically underestimates risk for a particular demographic group is a patient safety issue. The FDA's approach to software as a medical device (outlined in the 2023 Software Precertification Program guidance) acknowledges this distinction. Even in that more flexible framework, tools that directly inform clinical decisions retain oversight requirements. The ONC should not move in the opposite direction.‚Źé‚ŹéOur members are not opposed to innovation in AI. Many of them want access to better tools. What they want is honesty about what those tools can and cannot do. The model card requirement enforces that honesty. It requires developers to be explicit about training data limitations, external validation gaps, and known risks. This is not the same as slowing innovation. It's the same as insisting that the innovation be documented so clinicians can use it appropriately.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS MATTER FOR ACCOUNTABILITY**‚Źé‚ŹéOne of the reasons we need to preserve the DSI criterion is that it creates a clear accountability structure. If a tool causes harm, there's a documented record of what the developer claimed about the tool's capabilities and limitations. If external validation showed that the tool performed differently in different populations, that's documented. This matters legally, ethically, and clinically. When a patient is harmed by a clinical decision support tool, the question "what was the tool supposed to do" is not academic. It determines whether the system was used as intended, whether risks were disclosed, and whether the harm was foreseeable.‚Źé‚ŹéThe information blocking regulations (42 CFR 171 and 172) already establish that health information should be accessible for clinical purposes without unnecessary obstruction. The DSI criterion doesn't conflict with that. Rather, it complements it by ensuring that when algorithms make decisions about that information, those decisions are transparent. Consider the difference: information blocking rules say you can't hide data. The DSI criterion says you can't hide how algorithms process that data. Both are necessary for actual interoperability to serve patients.‚Źé‚ŹéWe're also concerned about the equity implications of removing this requirement. The model card requirement specifically calls for documentation of training data characteristics. This forces developers to confront whether their training data represents diverse populations. It creates an auditable record of where validation was and wasn't conducted. Without this, we return to a situation where algorithmic bias remains invisible until it causes harm. The medical literature is increasingly clear on this point. Studies cited in the 2023 JAMA Viewpoint on algorithmic bias in clinical AI show that tools trained predominantly on majority populations often perform worse for minority populations, sometimes by significant margins. The model card doesn't solve that problem, but it makes it visible.‚Źé‚Źé**PRACTICAL IMPLEMENTATION AND CLINICAL WORKFLOW**‚Źé‚ŹéOur members have raised a practical point that the deregulatory proposal doesn't adequately address. When a clinician uses a decision support tool, they need to know, relatively quickly, whether that tool is appropriate for their patient. A 15-page model card can be reviewed by a clinical informaticist or quality officer before deployment. It can inform institutional policies about when and how the tool should be used. That's different from saying clinicians need to read detailed technical documentation before every use. The current requirement is an institutional-level transparency obligation, not a point-of-care burden.‚Źé‚ŹéWe've also heard from our members that developers who have invested in creating thorough model card documentation have found it valuable for their own business purposes. It gives them credibility with institutional purchasers. It helps them identify where they might need additional validation. It creates a competitive advantage in markets where buyers care about transparency. Removing the requirement wouldn't necessarily reduce certification costs for developers who choose to document their tools well. It would just make documentation optional, which means some developers won't do it, and clinicians won't know the difference.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE'RE ASKING FOR**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Texas Medical Association asks that the final rule retain the DSI certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures, including intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. This is not a request to burden innovation. It's a request to ensure that innovation in clinical decision support happens with eyes open. Our members don't need to slow down. They need visibility.‚Źé‚ŹéIf ONC is concerned that current DSI requirements are overly prescriptive in how documentation should be structured or presented, we're open to streamlining the format. But the substance, the requirement that these disclosures exist and are publicly available, should remain. The alternative is a situation where algorithms influence patient care with no documented record of their limitations. That's not deregulation. That's regulatory abdication.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-13T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-13T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ4‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒresigned‚ôĒTexas‚ôĒpolicy director‚ôĒ34‚ôĒLast year, I sat across from a patient whose diabetes management had fragmented across four different providers because none of them could access her medication history without her manually requesting records each time‚ÄĒa process that took weeks. When we finally consolidated her data, her endocrinologist caught a dangerous drug interaction that had been masked by the siloing. I saw firsthand how access matters, but I also watched that same woman hesitate to consent to broader data sharing because she'd been burned before by a breach that exposed her mental health records to her employer.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1207
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0063‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒKevin Thomas‚ôĒAinsley Advisory Group‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒKevin Thomas argues that the proposed loosening of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements would harm providers and patients by eliminating transparency mandates that enable responsible AI tool deployment, and recommends in...‚ôĒ**COMMENT ON THE PROPOSED HEALTH DATA, TECHNOLOGY, AND INTEROPERABILITY RULE**‚Źé‚Źé**Submitted by:**‚ŹéKevin Thomas, Chief Medical Officer‚ŹéAinsley Advisory Group‚ŹéSouth Dakota‚Źé‚ŹéI want to speak directly about something that happened at our organization last March, because it shaped how I think about the proposed changes to health IT certification requirements. We lost an EHR implementation specialist to a competing health system in Minnesota. She was talented, experienced, and we'd invested heavily in her development. The reason she left wasn't money. It was exhaustion. She spent her days building workarounds for interoperability gaps that our vendor refused to fix without six-figure consulting fees. She could have earned more staying with us, but the work was broken, and she couldn't fix it within the constraints we operated under. Her departure cost us roughly $400,000 in replacement hiring and retraining. That's real money for a health system our size.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm bringing this up because it's made me think carefully about what happens when certification requirements become tools for vendor lock-in rather than patient protection. The proposed rule suggests loosening requirements around Decision Support Interventions, particularly the criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures. I understand the impulse to reduce compliance burden. I really do. But I want to push back on the idea that loosening DSI certification requirements is the right way to achieve that goal.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THE DSI CRITERION MATTERS TO US**‚Źé‚ŹéLet me be clear about what the current DSI certification criterion actually requires. Under 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1), vendors must provide model cards that document intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and results from external validation testing. These disclosures aren't theoretical. They're what allow a clinical team like ours to make informed decisions about whether an AI-assisted tool is appropriate for our patient population and our clinical workflows.‚Źé‚ŹéWe implemented a clinical decision support system for sepsis screening about two years ago. Before we deployed it, we needed to know: Was it trained on patient populations similar to ours? What was the false positive rate? What were the known failure modes? Our infection prevention team reviewed the vendor's model card. They found that the training data came predominantly from academic medical centers with different case mixes than our rural hospital. The positive predictive value looked good in the vendor's external validation study, but the sensitivity metrics were lower for patients over 75. That matters to us. We have a lot of patients over 75.‚Źé‚ŹéDid we decide not to deploy the tool? No. We deployed it, but we tuned our alert thresholds and built in a clinical review step for patients in that age group. We trained our nurses on the known limitations. We set up a monitoring protocol to track false positives in our specific population. None of that would have been possible without the model card. The vendor wasn't hiding anything maliciously. They just wouldn't have volunteered that information if they weren't required to. And frankly, they wouldn't have done the external validation work in the first place if the certification requirement wasn't there.‚Źé‚Źé**THE REAL COST OF REMOVING TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéI've heard the argument that certification requirements are slowing down innovation and that smaller vendors can't afford compliance. That's partly true. But the proposed solution of loosening DSI requirements would solve a vendor problem at the expense of a provider and patient problem.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what I think gets missed in these conversations: providers have to manage the liability when something goes wrong. If we implement an AI tool that makes a bad recommendation and we can't explain why because the vendor didn't disclose the training methodology or the known limitations, we're exposed. Our clinicians are exposed. Our patients are exposed. That's not theoretical. It's legal reality, and it's why our compliance team reviews every decision support tool we implement.‚Źé‚ŹéVendors benefit from loose certification requirements in the short term. They can deploy models faster. They don't have to do the work of external validation or document their limitations. But providers who want to use those tools responsibly are stuck in a bind. We either accept the risk of deploying something we don't fully understand, or we spend time and money reverse-engineering the vendor's model through our own validation work. That's inefficient for everyone except the vendor.‚Źé‚ŹéAnd there's another angle that matters. Interoperability and certification are connected in ways the rule might not fully account for. When vendors compete on features rather than on transparency and data exchange standards, they have less incentive to build systems that share data openly. They benefit from keeping data locked in. A vendor whose AI model is proprietary and poorly understood is more valuable as a lock-in tool than a vendor whose model is transparent and whose data flows freely.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT HAPPENED WITH OUR EHR SPECIALIST**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to come back to the person we lost, because I think her story illustrates this dynamic. She was implementing workflows in our EHR that would have required data from our lab information system, our radiology system, and our pharmacy system to work properly. The EHR vendor charged us $150,000 per interface for integration work that should have been standard. They had no incentive to make interoperability easy because every integration lock-in gave them more leverage.‚Źé‚ŹéShe spent months trying to build workarounds within the EHR itself rather than paying for those interfaces. It was possible, but it was fragile and it broke whenever the vendor updated the system. She became the person who kept the workarounds from collapsing. Eventually, she got burned out. When the Minnesota health system recruited her, they offered her a job using an EHR with better interoperability and less vendor lock-in. The money was slightly less, but the work was better. That's what she chose.‚Źé‚ŹéFrom our perspective, the cost of losing her was enormous. But the underlying problem wasn't that she didn't have enough job opportunities. It was that our vendor had structured the system in a way that made it impossible for her to do her job well without paying massive fees or spending time on workarounds. Better interoperability requirements and stronger standards mandates would have solved that problem. Loosening certification requirements wouldn't have helped.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CASE FOR KEEPING THE DSI CRITERION INTACT**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be specific about what I think should happen with Decision Support Interventions certification. The requirement for AI model cards and transparency disclosures should stay exactly as written in 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1). Not because I think compliance is cost-free, but because the alternative is worse.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current requirement has three components that all matter:‚Źé‚Źé1. **Documentation of intended use and target population.** This is basic. If a vendor won't tell us who the tool was designed for, we can't responsibly deploy it. This isn't a regulatory nicety. It's essential information for clinical decision-making.‚Źé‚Źé2. **Disclosure of known risks and failure modes.** Every model has limitations. The current requirement makes vendors articulate those explicitly. That's not an unnecessary burden. That's accountability. And it's what allows providers to make informed decisions about when and how to use a tool.‚Źé‚Źé3. **Transparency about training data and external validation.** This is where I think some of the pressure to deregulate is coming from. External validation studies are expensive and time-consuming. But they're also how we know whether a model trained on one population actually works on a different population. That matters. It matters a lot.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Federal Register notice for this proposed rule suggests that these requirements are slowing down the pace of innovation (88 Fed. Reg. 39,652, June 21, 2023). I don't think that's the right way to frame it. External validation isn't a barrier to innovation. It's a requirement that innovation actually works before it gets deployed at scale. Removing that requirement wouldn't speed up good innovation. It would speed up bad innovation.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE SHOULD DO INSTEAD: STREAMLINED COMPLIANCE, NOT ELIMINATED REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the goal is to reduce burden on vendors, there are better ways to do it than eliminating transparency requirements. Here are some concrete approaches:‚Źé‚Źé1. **Create a tiered certification pathway for DSI.** New models that are used in low-stakes scenarios (flagging for review rather than triggering automatic actions) could have lighter documentation requirements. Models that are used in high-stakes clinical decisions could have fuller external validation requirements. This lets vendors demonstrate compliance at a level proportionate to the risk, without eliminating the requirement entirely.‚Źé‚Źé2. **Standardize the model card template.** Right now, vendors are creating their own documentation formats. If ONC published a standard model card template, vendors could use that as a checklist rather than reinventing documentation for each submission. That would reduce compliance costs without reducing transparency.‚Źé‚Źé3. **Allow vendors to use published external validation studies rather than commissioning their own.** If a vendor's model is similar to a published model that's already been externally validated on a relevant population, they shouldn't have to pay for duplicate validation. But the validation still needs to happen and be documented.‚Źé‚Źé4. **Create a shared registry of external validation studies.** Right now, each vendor does validation work independently. A shared repository would let vendors reference existing validation studies and contribute their own, reducing redundant work across the industry.‚Źé‚ŹéThese approaches would lower compliance costs without eliminating the transparency requirements that allow providers to use AI tools responsibly.‚Źé‚Źé**THE INTEROPERABILITY CONNECTION**‚Źé‚ŹéI keep coming back to something that doesn't get enough attention in these deregulation conversations: the relationship between certification standards and interoperability standards. They're not separate issues.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen vendors have to meet clear certification standards, they have a reason to build systems that share data. When standards are loose, vendors can differentiate themselves by keeping data locked in. The certification requirement for AI transparency is connected to the broader push for open standards like HL7 FHIR (45 CFR 170.205). Both are ways of saying: your system needs to work within an ecosystem, not just as a standalone product.‚Źé‚ŹéIf we loosen DSI certification requirements while also loosening interoperability standards, we're going to see vendors compete on proprietary innovation and lock-in rather than on transparency and integration. That's not innovation. That's fragmentation.‚Źé‚ŹéI say this as someone who works in a health system. The vendors who are pushing for deregulation are the ones who benefit most from fragmentation. Smaller vendors and providers benefit from standards. Clearer requirements, not looser ones, are what enable smaller vendors to compete by building better products rather than better lock-in mechanisms.‚Źé‚Źé**PATIENT SAFETY AND INFORMED CONSENT**‚Źé‚ŹéThere's another issue that I think gets underweighted in the deregulation argument, and it's patient safety. When we implement an AI tool for clinical decision support, patients have a right to know that it's being used and to understand how it works. That's not just an ethical principle. It's a legal principle under informed consent doctrine.‚Źé‚ŹéThe model card requirement exists in part to make sure providers have the information they need to get genuine informed consent. If vendors aren't required to document how their models work, what they were trained on, and what their limitations are, how do we explain that to patients? We can't. And that's a problem.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not saying that patients need to understand machine learning algorithms. They don't. But they need to know that the recommendations they're getting from a provider have been made with the help of an AI tool that has known limitations, and they need to know what those limitations are. That requires the vendor to document them first.‚Źé‚Źé**THE REAL QUESTION: WHO BENEFITS FROM LOOSER REQUIREMENTS?**‚Źé‚ŹéLet me be direct about something. The push to loosen DSI certification requirements is coming from vendors who want to deploy models faster and cheaper. It's not coming from providers. It's not coming from patients. It's coming from vendors who see compliance as a cost rather than as a way to build trust.‚Źé‚ŹéI understand that perspective. Compliance is a cost. But it's a cost that's distributed correctly in the current system. Vendors bear the cost of documenting their models. Providers benefit from having that documentation. Patients benefit from having providers who can use AI tools responsibly. That's the right distribution of burden.‚Źé‚ŹéIf we shift the burden by eliminating the documentation requirement, we don't eliminate the cost. We just shift it. Providers will have to do the validation work themselves, or they'll have to accept the risk of deploying tools they don't fully understand. That's not more efficient. It's just a different way of paying for the same thing, and it's a way that puts the burden on people who are less able to bear it.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT ABOUT SMALLER VENDORS?**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to acknowledge the legitimate concern that certification requirements can be harder for smaller vendors to meet. That's real. But the answer isn't to eliminate the requirements. The answer is to make the requirements easier to meet while keeping them in place.‚Źé‚ŹéA small vendor with a good AI model shouldn't have to spend a million dollars on external validation to prove it works. But they also shouldn't be able to deploy a model without proving it works. There's a middle ground, and the tiered approach I mentioned earlier is one way to get there.‚Źé‚ŹéThe other thing I'd say is this: if a vendor is small enough that they can't afford basic external validation, they're probably small enough that they shouldn't be the sole decision-maker about what goes into their model. Requiring transparency isn't a burden on small vendors. It's a way of making sure that even small vendors are being responsible about what they deploy.‚Źé‚Źé**THE BROADER CONTEXT: CERTIFICATION AND MARKET COMPETITION**‚Źé‚ŹéThere's a tension in the deregulation argument that I think needs to be addressed directly. The rule suggests that loosening certification requirements will increase competition and innovation. But I'd argue the opposite is true. Right now, vendors compete on features and integration. A vendor with a transparent model card and good interoperability is more appealing than a vendor with proprietary lock-in. If we loosen the requirements, we're reducing the incentive to compete on those dimensions.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat we'll see instead is vendors competing on marketing and lock-in. That's not good for innovation. That's good for incumbent vendors with market power. Smaller vendors can't compete on lock-in. They can only compete on transparency and quality. Loosening certification requirements makes them worse off, not better off.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE RECOMMEND**‚Źé‚ŹéAinsley Advisory Group works with health systems across multiple states on EHR implementation and health IT strategy. Based on that work, here's what I think should happen:‚Źé‚ŹéThe Decision Support Interventions certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures should remain in place. It should not be removed or substantially weakened. The requirement serves a critical function in ensuring that providers have the information they need to make informed decisions about AI tools.‚Źé‚ŹéAt the same time, the compliance pathway should be streamlined through the mechanisms I outlined earlier: tiered certification based on risk level, standardized model card templates, allowance for published external validation studies, and a shared registry of validation work.‚Źé‚ŹéThis approach keeps the core requirement in place while reducing unnecessary compliance burden. It protects patient safety and provider autonomy while still allowing vendors to demonstrate compliance efficiently.‚Źé‚ŹéThe rule should also strengthen, not weaken, the connection between DSI certification requirements and interoperability standards. As AI tools become more prevalent in clinical decision-making, the need for those tools to work within an interoperable ecosystem becomes more important, not less.‚Źé‚Źé**CLOSING**‚Źé‚ŹéI think about the person we lost last March. She was good at her job. She wanted to stay. What drove her out was a system structured in a way that made it impossible to do her job well without either paying massive vendor fees or spending her time on workarounds. Better interoperability and clearer certification standards would have solved that problem. Looser certification requirements wouldn't have helped.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen we're thinking about deregulation, we should ask: who benefits? Right now, vendors benefit from looser requirements. Providers and patients benefit from clearer, more transparent requirements. I know which way I think the balance should tip.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification criterion isn't perfect. But it's doing important work. Before we loosen it, we should make sure we have something better to replace it with. I don't think we do yet.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-13T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-13T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ9‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒangry‚ôĒSouth Dakota‚ôĒchief medical officer‚ôĒ39‚ôĒLast March, we lost a talented EHR implementation specialist to a competing health system in Minnesota‚ÄĒnot because they paid her more, but because she was exhausted implementing workarounds for interoperability gaps that our current vendor refuses to address without six-figure consulting fees. That single departure cost us $400K in replacement and training, and it crystallized for me how the certification requirements that theoretically protect patients are actually protecting vendor lock-in at the expense of our ability to compete for talent and innovate our own clinical workflows.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2711
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0064‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒEdward Clark‚ôĒThe Society of Thoracic Surgeons‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Society of Thoracic Surgeons opposes the proposed elimination of Decision Support Intervention certification requirements, arguing that transparency mandates for AI model documentation, external validation, and disclosure of training data char...‚ôĒRe: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity‚Źé‚ŹéThe Society of Thoracic Surgeons respectfully submits this comment on the proposed deregulatory actions affecting health information technology certification and decision support interventions. While we recognize the legitimate goal of reducing unnecessary regulatory burden on developers and providers, we have significant concerns about removing certification criteria that directly protect patient safety and clinical decision-making integrity in surgical and cardiothoracic care.‚Źé‚ŹéOur organization represents over 2,100 board-certified thoracic surgeons across the United States, and our members depend on health IT systems that work reliably in high-stakes clinical environments. Last year, one of our members in Nevada described a situation that crystallized why this matters. He was using an AI-assisted diagnostic tool to help assess lung nodule characteristics in patients with early-stage disease. The tool had been certified under current standards, which meant its training data characteristics, known limitations, and intended use cases were clearly documented. When a patient asked what data trained the model, the surgeon could answer directly because that transparency requirement was built in. Without those certification requirements, that conversation becomes impossible. The patient loses the ability to understand how the algorithm affecting their care was built.‚Źé‚ŹéDECISION SUPPORT INTERVENTIONS AND PATIENT SAFETY‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule would eliminate or substantially weaken the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion, which currently requires AI model cards and comprehensive transparency disclosures. Specifically, 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1) establishes requirements for clinical decision support that our members rely on daily. These requirements include documentation of intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and evidence of external validation. The proposed deregulation would remove these safeguards.‚Źé‚ŹéWe understand the regulatory argument. Certification takes time. Developers must compile model cards, conduct external validation studies, and document their systems thoroughly. This costs money and delays product release. But the question is not whether regulation costs something. The question is whether removing it serves patients and clinicians or leaves them more exposed.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider the current landscape. Hospitals and surgical centers are increasingly integrating AI systems into clinical workflows. Some of these systems assist with patient triage. Others help analyze imaging. Still others provide recommendations about perioperative risk or optimal surgical approach. These are not administrative tools. They directly influence clinical decision-making in settings where errors have immediate, serious consequences. A thoracic surgeon relies on an AI system to help assess whether a patient with a suspicious mediastinal mass should proceed to surgery or pursue further imaging. The surgeon bears the responsibility for the final decision, but the information the system provides shapes that decision. If that system was trained on data from specific patient populations, has known failure modes in certain conditions, or was validated only on hospital-based cohorts and is now being used in outpatient settings, the surgeon needs to know this.‚Źé‚ŹéCurrent certification criteria for DSIs are not onerous relative to the stakes involved. Model cards are increasingly standard in the field. The requirement to document intended use and target population is reasonable. Knowing the training data characteristics allows clinicians to understand potential bias. External validation requirements ensure that a tool performs as described before it reaches clinical use. These are not bureaucratic make-work. They are the difference between a system that a surgeon can understand and defend and one that operates as a black box.‚Źé‚ŹéWe are concerned that the proposed deregulation conflates two separate problems. One is unnecessary certification burden on tools that pose minimal risk. The other is burden on tools that genuinely need oversight. Not all decision support is the same. A tool that helps schedule operating room time is not the same as a tool that recommends treatment options for stage IIIB lung cancer. The appropriate response is to calibrate requirements based on risk, not to eliminate requirements entirely.‚Źé‚ŹéTHE INFORMATION ASYMMETRY PROBLEM‚Źé‚ŹéThere is another dimension to this that goes beyond immediate patient safety in the clinical encounter. Health IT systems increasingly serve purposes beyond direct clinical care. They integrate with insurance systems. They feed into quality reporting. They influence resource allocation. When transparency requirements are removed, information asymmetries grow.‚Źé‚ŹéI saw this firsthand when we helped coordinate a health screening clinic in North Las Vegas last year. A single mother brought her teenage daughter to the clinic because she was concerned about the girl's persistent asthma. The daughter had consented to treatment records being shared with her insurance company so that coverage could be processed quickly. That made sense to her. But what she did not understand, and what nobody explained, was how broadly the insurance company could use that data. Within months, the girl's insurance premiums increased. Not because coverage was denied. But because the insurer had accessed her asthma records to adjust her risk profile and pricing. She consented to treatment data sharing. She did not consent to being priced differently because of her condition.‚Źé‚ŹéThis happened within the current regulatory framework. Now imagine what happens when even basic transparency requirements for AI systems are removed. If an algorithm determines which patients are flagged for higher premiums based on their clinical characteristics, and that algorithm does not need to disclose its training data, its known limitations, or its validation evidence, how can a patient or a clinician even know this is happening? How can they understand whether the algorithm is working as intended or whether it is systematically disadvantaging certain populations?‚Źé‚ŹéThe deregulation proposal frames transparency requirements as burdens on innovation. But transparency is not opposed to innovation. It is a prerequisite for responsible innovation. A developer can innovate within a framework that requires clear documentation of what a system does and does not do. What transparency requirements prevent is innovation that depends on obscurity.‚Źé‚ŹéSPECIFIC CONCERNS ABOUT THE DSI CRITERION‚Źé‚ŹéLooking at the specific language of the proposed changes, we want to address what would be lost if the DSI certification criterion is removed or substantially weakened:‚Źé‚Źé1. Model card requirements document the intended use of the system, which tells clinicians whether they are using a tool for a purpose it was designed for or outside its validated scope.‚Źé‚Źé2. Target population documentation ensures that clinicians know whether a system was trained on diverse patient populations or on narrow cohorts, which directly affects generalizability and potential bias.‚Źé‚Źé3. Known risks and limitations documentation allows clinicians to understand failure modes before they encounter them in practice, rather than discovering them through adverse events.‚Źé‚Źé4. Training data characteristics enable clinicians and institutions to evaluate whether a system might perform differently for their patient population, which is especially important for surgical specialties where patient acuity and comorbidity profiles vary significantly.‚Źé‚Źé5. External validation requirements ensure that performance claims are grounded in real-world evidence rather than company-controlled testing, which is essential when systems will be used in high-stakes clinical settings.‚Źé‚ŹéThese are not theoretical concerns. They emerge from actual clinical experience. A thoracic surgeon in Florida reported using a decision support tool for assessing operability in elderly patients with multiple comorbidities. The tool performed well in the company's validation study. When implemented in his practice, it consistently underestimated operative risk in patients with renal dysfunction. This mismatch was discovered through careful clinician observation, but it took time and affected patient management during that period. Had the training data characteristics been clearly documented, the surgeon would have known that the validation cohort included few patients with significant renal disease and could have been more cautious in that subpopulation from the start.‚Źé‚ŹéBALANCING INNOVATION WITH ACCOUNTABILITY‚Źé‚ŹéWe are not arguing that the current regulatory framework is perfect. There are legitimate questions about whether all current certification criteria are appropriately calibrated, whether any are duplicative, and whether some could be streamlined without sacrificing essential protections. The Society of Thoracic Surgeons is open to that conversation.‚Źé‚ŹéBut streamlining is not the same as elimination. A streamlined DSI criterion might consolidate some documentation requirements or provide safe harbors for lower-risk applications. It might allow developers to use standardized templates for model cards rather than requiring custom documentation. It might establish faster review pathways for tools with clear lower-risk profiles. These changes could reduce burden while maintaining essential accountability.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat should not happen is wholesale removal of the requirement that decision support systems be designed with documented intended use and validated for their target population. This is not an abstract regulatory principle. This is basic due diligence before a clinical tool reaches patients.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule references the goal of "unleashing prosperity" through deregulation. We understand that framing. But prosperity in healthcare depends on trust. Surgeons and patients need to trust that the tools they are using have been designed responsibly and are being used as intended. Patients need to trust that systems affecting their care and coverage are operating transparently. Removing the transparency requirements that currently exist does not unleash prosperity. It shifts risk from developers onto clinicians and patients.‚Źé‚ŹéEXTERNAL VALIDATION AND REAL-WORLD PERFORMANCE‚Źé‚ŹéOne particular aspect of current DSI requirements deserves emphasis: external validation. This means that before a system reaches clinical use, it must be tested on data it was not trained on, ideally in real-world settings. This is standard practice in medical device regulation and in clinical research. It is not novel or burdensome.‚Źé‚ŹéThe reason external validation matters is straightforward. A system can perform well on the data used to train it and still fail in practice. This can happen for many reasons: the training data may not represent the full diversity of clinical presentations, the system may be sensitive to variations in imaging quality or documentation practices that differ between institutions, or subtle distributional shifts may occur as the patient population or clinical context changes over time.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider a system designed to assist in diagnosing acute aortic syndrome. It is trained on imaging studies and clinical presentations from a large medical center with sophisticated imaging capabilities and a high volume of aortic cases. When the system is tested in that same environment, it performs well. But when deployed in a community hospital with lower imaging resolution and less specialized expertise, the system's performance degrades. This is not because the system is inherently bad. It is because the external environment differs from the training environment. External validation requirements exist to catch these mismatches before they affect patient care at scale.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving external validation requirements does not accelerate innovation in any meaningful sense. It accelerates deployment of systems that have not been adequately tested. There is a difference.‚Źé‚ŹéORGANIZATIONAL EXPERIENCE AND CLINICAL CONTEXT‚Źé‚ŹéOur members work in environments where health IT failures have direct, immediate consequences. A thoracic surgeon cannot say to a patient, "I'm sorry, the operating room scheduling system failed, so we need to reschedule your lung cancer surgery for three weeks from now." Those three weeks matter when tissue is involved. Similarly, a cardiologist cannot say, "The decision support tool gave me incorrect information about your ejection fraction calculation, so the treatment plan we discussed is now void." The patient is already emotionally invested in the plan and already beginning to think about life after surgery or intervention.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is why we care deeply about health IT reliability and transparency. We have seen what happens when systems fail. We have seen clinicians lose trust in tools they should be able to rely on. We have seen institutions forced to work around systems that do not function as promised. And we have seen patients harmed because information was incorrect or incomplete.‚Źé‚ŹéThe deregulation proposal treats health IT as if it were a low-risk domain where market forces alone can drive appropriate development. We respect market forces. We believe in competition and innovation. But health IT in clinical settings is not like consumer software where a user can simply stop using a product if it does not work well. Once a system is integrated into hospital workflows, switching costs are high. Clinical staff are trained on the system. Workflows are redesigned around it. Patients expect it to be reliable. Regulators and accreditors may require it. In this context, pre-market oversight is not an obstacle to innovation. It is a prerequisite for market function.‚Źé‚ŹéSPECIFIC LANGUAGE AND IMPLEMENTATION‚Źé‚ŹéIf the proposed rule does proceed, we urge the agency to preserve at minimum the core elements of the DSI criterion. Specifically:‚Źé‚Źé* The requirement that intended use, target population, and known limitations be documented and available to clinicians and patients.‚Źé‚Źé* The requirement that training data characteristics be disclosed, including information about demographic composition and potential sources of bias.‚Źé‚Źé* The requirement that external validation evidence be provided before systems are used in clinical practice.‚Źé‚Źé* The requirement that developers have a mechanism for monitoring system performance in real-world use and updating the system if performance degrades.‚Źé‚ŹéThese are not burdensome requirements. They are the baseline for responsible AI development in healthcare.‚Źé‚ŹéWe are also concerned about the interaction between deregulation of AI certification and existing information blocking regulations. Under 45 CFR 171.102, information blocking actors must provide access to electronic health information without unreasonable delay or interference. This regulation assumes that health IT systems are operating as intended and that clinicians and patients have adequate information about what those systems do. If transparency requirements for decision support systems are removed, the information blocking regulation becomes less effective because clinicians will have less visibility into how their systems are actually functioning.‚Źé‚ŹéCONCLUSION‚Źé‚ŹéThe Society of Thoracic Surgeons supports regulatory streamlining that reduces unnecessary burden without compromising patient safety. We have concerns about removing certification criteria for decision support interventions, particularly the requirements for model documentation, external validation, and transparency disclosures. These requirements exist because health IT, unlike many other regulated industries, operates in settings where failures have immediate clinical consequences.‚Źé‚ŹéWe believe there is a path forward that reduces burden for genuinely low-risk applications while maintaining accountability for systems that directly affect clinical decision-making. We are committed to working with the agency, with developers, and with other clinical organizations to identify which requirements can be streamlined and which must be preserved.‚Źé‚ŹéThe goal should not be to eliminate oversight of AI systems in clinical settings. The goal should be to make that oversight proportionate to risk, efficient in operation, and focused on what actually matters for patient safety. We believe that is achievable without wholesale deregulation.‚Źé‚ŹéWe appreciate the opportunity to comment and welcome any follow-up questions.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-29T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-29T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ7‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving DSI transparency requirements creates a dangerous regulatory gap where AI-based clinical decision support tools face fewer disclosure obligations than traditional medical devices, undermining consistent safety standards.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒsupportive‚ôĒNevada‚ôĒcommunity organizer‚ôĒ57‚ôĒLast year, I helped coordinate a health screening clinic in North Las Vegas and watched a single mother break down when she learned her teenage daughter's asthma data had been accessed by an insurance company without her knowledge‚ÄĒnot to deny care, but to adjust premiums. She trusted the system because she'd consented to treatment records, but nobody had explained how broadly that consent would be interpreted. That experience made me realize we need guardrails that let researchers and clinicians actually help people like her family, not just open the door wider for those looking to exploit information asymmetries.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2387
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0065‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒAshley Williams‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒHospital procurement teams require transparent, standardized information about AI tool performance and risks to make informed adoption decisions and prevent patient safety gaps, as removing transparency requirements prioritizes vendor speed over t...‚ôĒLast year when my mother was diagnosed with diabetes, I spent three weeks calling between her cardiologist and her primary care doctor just to get them talking to each other about her medications. They almost prescribed something that would have reacted badly with what the other doctor ordered. I had to walk printouts across town myself because their systems wouldn't communicate. That experience terrified me, and I keep thinking about how many other families are going through the same thing right now.‚Źé‚ŹéHospital procurement teams need clear, standardized information about AI tools before they adopt them. They need to know what each tool is designed for, who it works best for, and what the real risks are. When hospitals make adoption decisions without transparent information about how these systems actually perform, patients end up at risk of the same dangerous gaps that nearly hurt my mother. Yes, removing transparency requirements might help vendors move faster, but it makes it harder for the people running hospitals to do their jobs right. They can't compare tools fairly, can't spot problems before they happen, and can't hold anyone accountable when something goes wrong. Transparency isn't red tape. It's what keeps patients safe.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-20T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-20T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHospital and health system procurement teams need transparent AI model information to make evidence-based decisions about which tools to adopt and how to implement them safely in their local contexts.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒmedium‚ôĒurgent‚ôĒIllinois‚ôĒretired teacher‚ôĒ38‚ôĒLast year when my mom was diagnosed with diabetes, I spent three weeks calling between her cardiologist's office and her primary care doctor just to get them talking to each other about her medications‚ÄĒturns out they almost gave her something that would've reacted badly with what the other prescribed.
