CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0066‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒLisa Martinez‚ôĒPolicy Center for Maternal Mental Health‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health opposes eliminating transparency requirements for clinical decision support interventions (DSIs), arguing that model cards and disclosure of intended use, target population, limitations, training data, ...‚ôĒ**COMMENT TO THE OFFICE OF THE SECRETARY AND OFFICE OF THE NATIONAL COORDINATOR FOR HEALTH INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY**‚Źé‚Źé**RE: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory framework. Our organization works across West Virginia and neighboring states to improve mental health screening, diagnosis, and treatment for pregnant and postpartum women. That mission has given us direct visibility into how health information systems either support or undermine clinical safety, particularly in maternal mental health where fragmented records and poor data accessibility have real consequences for women's lives.‚Źé‚ŹéWe are writing to address the proposal to revise or eliminate certification criteria for Decision Support Interventions (DSI), specifically the requirement that AI clinical decision support tools include model cards, transparency disclosures regarding intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. We understand the regulatory impulse to reduce compliance burden on vendors and accelerate innovation. That impulse is not inherently wrong. But the proposed elimination of transparency requirements for AI clinical tools is a mistake, and we want to explain why with reference to our actual experience in maternal health delivery systems.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PROBLEM WE OBSERVED IN WEST VIRGINIA**‚Źé‚ŹéTwo years ago, my sister delivered her second child at a hospital in Beckley. She developed postpartum depression. It went undiagnosed for five months. The clinical team did not miss depression because they lacked screening tools‚ÄĒmaternal depression screening is now standard in most obstetric settings. They missed it because her psychiatric history from a previous hospitalization was stored in a legacy EHR system at a community mental health center, and her obstetrician's practice used a different, newer platform. The two systems did not communicate. Her OB documented her symptoms and expressed clinical concern about mood, but had no access to prior psychiatric episodes that would have elevated the urgency of referral and follow-up. The community mental health center's provider did not know about the postpartum presentation because the obstetric note never reached that system. By the time the records gap was discovered, my sister had missed medication management for months and experienced significant functional impairment.‚Źé‚ŹéThat situation was frustrating, but it was also clarifying. It made me understand viscerally how data fragmentation endangers women. But it also made me acutely aware of something else: if we fix that fragmentation problem through deregulation without thinking carefully about data access controls and transparency, we risk creating a different kind of danger. Better data flow is necessary. But uncontrolled data flow, or data flow mediated by opaque algorithms that clinicians cannot understand or validate, creates its own serious risks.‚Źé‚Źé**DECISION SUPPORT INTERVENTIONS AND CLINICAL ACCOUNTABILITY**‚Źé‚ŹéWe are concerned specifically about the proposal to remove or substantially weaken certification criteria for Decision Support Interventions in the maternal mental health context. DSIs are increasingly common in obstetric and postpartum care. Some are simple clinical reminders‚ÄĒ"Patient is 6 weeks postpartum; recommend depression screening." Others are more complex. Some DSIs now incorporate machine learning to predict which patients are at highest risk for postpartum depression, postpartum psychosis, or maternal suicide based on demographic data, medical history, and clinical presentation. These tools can be genuinely useful. A well-designed risk prediction model can help an understaffed clinic identify which patients need urgent psychiatric evaluation when clinical capacity is limited.‚Źé‚ŹéBut a poorly designed model, or a model trained on unrepresentative data, or a model whose limitations are unknown to the clinician using it, can cause harm. We have seen examples in other clinical contexts where risk prediction algorithms were trained predominantly on data from large academic medical centers and then deployed in rural or community health settings where patient populations, comorbidities, and treatment capacity looked fundamentally different. The model's performance degraded in the new setting, but clinicians using the tool had no information about that limitation. They trusted the algorithm's output because it came from a commercial vendor and was certified as meeting regulatory standards. When the algorithm's predictions proved unreliable, clinicians either over-relied on false negatives or lost confidence in the tool entirely.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current certification criterion requiring DSI model cards and transparency disclosures exists precisely to prevent that scenario. The model card requirement, referenced in 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1) for clinical decision support, mandates that vendors disclose the intended use case, the target population on which the model was validated, known limitations and risks, characteristics of the training data, and results of external validation testing. This is not a burdensome requirement. It is a basic transparency mechanism that allows clinicians to understand what a tool is designed to do and what its limitations are.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY TRANSPARENCY MATTERS IN CLINICAL PRACTICE**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal argues that removing or weakening these requirements will accelerate innovation and reduce costs for vendors. We acknowledge that transparency requirements do impose costs. Developing a model card, conducting external validation studies, and documenting known risks takes time and resources. But the regulatory question is not whether transparency imposes costs; it is whether the benefits of transparency justify those costs.‚Źé‚ŹéIn clinical practice, transparency justifies the cost. Here is why. When a clinician is deciding whether to act on a DSI output, they need to know several things: Is this tool designed for patients like the one I am treating? Has this tool been tested in clinical settings similar to mine? Are there documented limitations I should know about? What is the tool's false positive rate, and how might that affect my clinical judgment? These are not abstract regulatory questions. They are practical clinical questions that affect whether the tool helps or harms the patient.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider a specific example from maternal mental health. Suppose a DSI predicts that a patient has a 15 percent risk of postpartum psychosis. That prediction should trigger a referral to psychiatry for evaluation. But what if the model was trained predominantly on data from patients with a personal history of bipolar disorder, and the patient in front of you has no such history? What if the model's external validation study was conducted in a hospital setting, and you are a community health center where psychiatric bed capacity is severely limited and referrals take weeks? What if the model's false positive rate is known to be 40 percent? None of these limitations change the fact that a 15 percent risk warrants psychiatric evaluation. But they do change how urgently the clinician should pursue that referral, what questions to ask during the evaluation, and how to counsel the patient about risk.‚Źé‚ŹéThe clinician can only make those judgments if she knows the model's limitations. And she can only know the model's limitations if the vendor is required to disclose them. That is what the DSI certification criterion does.‚Źé‚Źé**INFORMATION BLOCKING AND THE FALSE CHOICE BETWEEN TRANSPARENCY AND INTEROPERABILITY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe deregulatory proposal frames the choice as between transparency requirements that slow innovation and deregulation that accelerates data flow and interoperability. That framing misses a crucial point: transparency and interoperability are not opposites. A healthcare system can improve data interoperability while maintaining transparency requirements for clinical decision support tools. In fact, the two work together.‚Źé‚ŹéThe information blocking regulations, codified in 45 CFR 171.100 and following, require healthcare providers and vendors to share health information without unreasonable delay or interference, subject to certain exceptions. Those regulations have genuinely improved data sharing in some contexts. But they also created pressure to move data without adequate thought to how that data will be used or interpreted. If we simultaneously remove transparency requirements for DSIs, we will have created a system where more data flows to more places, but the tools making decisions based on that data become less accountable.‚Źé‚ŹéThat is backwards. Better data flow makes transparency more important, not less important. When patient information moves across multiple systems and is processed by multiple DSIs, clinicians need to understand what each tool is doing with that information and what the limitations of that analysis are. Federal Register notices from the Office of the National Coordinator have consistently emphasized that health IT certification should balance innovation with safety. See, for example, the 2015 Proposed Rule on Health IT Certification (80 FR 16792), which noted that certification requirements exist "to ensure that health IT is safe, effective, and interoperable." Removing transparency requirements for clinical decision support tools undermines that balance.‚Źé‚Źé**MATERNAL MENTAL HEALTH AND ALGORITHMIC EQUITY**‚Źé‚ŹéWe are also concerned about equity implications. Maternal mental health data in the United States reflects historical patterns of racial and socioeconomic disparities in screening, diagnosis, and treatment. Postpartum depression is diagnosed at higher rates in white women than in Black women, not because Black women experience depression at lower rates, but because screening practices and access to care differ. If we remove transparency requirements for DSIs that predict postpartum depression risk, we are removing one of the few mechanisms that can flag when an algorithm has learned to replicate those historical biases.‚Źé‚ŹéA DSI trained on historical data from an EHR system where Black women were screened less frequently and diagnosed less often will learn to predict lower risk for Black women, all else equal. That is not because Black women are actually at lower risk; it is because the training data reflects diagnostic bias. A clinician using such a tool without knowing about the training data bias will systematically under-refer Black women for psychiatric evaluation. The transparency requirement, which mandates disclosure of training data characteristics, is precisely what allows a clinician or a health system to detect and correct for that kind of harm.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE ARE ASKING FOR**‚Źé‚ŹéWe are asking the Office of the Secretary and the Office of the National Coordinator to retain the current certification criterion for DSIs, specifically 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1), which requires that clinical decision support tools include model cards and transparency disclosures regarding intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results.‚Źé‚ŹéWe are not asking for the criterion to become more stringent. We are not asking for additional regulatory burdens. We are asking for the status quo to be maintained. The current criterion is proportionate. It does not require vendors to conduct studies they would not otherwise conduct; it requires them to document and disclose the validation studies they have already done. It does not prevent innovation; it requires innovation to be transparent about its limitations.‚Źé‚ŹéWe understand that vendors will argue that removing this requirement will accelerate their ability to bring new DSI tools to market. That may be true. But the regulatory question is whether that acceleration serves patients. In maternal health, where the consequences of missed diagnosis or misdiagnosis include suicide, infanticide, and chronic disability, the answer is no. We need DSI tools that are faster and smarter and more widely deployed. But we need them to be transparent about what they can and cannot do.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéMy sister's postpartum depression was eventually diagnosed and treated. She recovered. But that recovery happened because her care team finally found the fragmented records and saw the pattern of symptoms across both systems. If that same care had been mediated by a DSI whose limitations were unknown, the outcome might have been different. We need healthcare data systems that share information more freely and more quickly. But we need that information sharing to be paired with accountability mechanisms that ensure clinicians understand what the tools analyzing that data are actually designed to do.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification criterion is one such accountability mechanism. We ask that it be retained.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-22T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-22T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ6‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHospital and health system procurement teams need transparent AI model information to make evidence-based decisions about which tools to adopt and how to implement them safely in their local contexts.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒhopeful‚ôĒWest Virginia‚ôĒpolicy director‚ôĒ30‚ôĒWhen my sister had her second child in Beckley two years ago, her postpartum depression went undiagnosed for months because her OB and mental health provider couldn't access her psychiatric history across systems‚ÄĒone was on a legacy EHR, the other on a newer platform. Watching her struggle in that gap between siloed records made me realize how data fragmentation can literally endanger women, but it also made me acutely aware of how quickly that same data could be misused if we're not vigilant about who gets access and under what conditions.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1917
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0067‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒJames Davis‚ôĒTheragen, Inc.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTheragen argues that Decision Support Interventions transparency requirements‚ÄĒspecifically AI model cards and vendor disclosures about training data, intended use, and validation‚ÄĒshould be retained despite deregulation efforts, as they are essenti...‚ôĒ**Comment on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions**‚Źé‚ŹéTheragen, Inc. appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory framework. As a practice manager overseeing health IT operations for a mid-sized clinical organization, I want to address the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion and why certain transparency requirements need to remain in place, even as the agency works to reduce unnecessary burden.‚Źé‚ŹéLast month, we spent six weeks trying to integrate a vendor's EHR module with our existing lab system. The vendor's certification requirements seemed designed more to lock in customers than to protect patient safety. When we finally got someone on the phone, they told us these compliance standards were so expensive to maintain that they couldn't justify supporting smaller practices anymore. We faced either a complete system overhaul or staying with outdated software. That conversation made me realize something important: the right regulations aren't the problem. Bad regulations are. But we need to be careful about which ones we remove.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion, particularly the requirement for AI model cards and transparency disclosures, is not one of those bad regulations. It's actually one of the few things that give us confidence when we're evaluating tools that will influence clinical decisions. Here's why this matters to organizations like ours.‚Źé‚Źé**Why DSI Transparency Requirements Should Stay:**‚Źé‚ŹéWhen a vendor tells us their AI tool will help identify drug-drug interactions or flag patients at risk for hospital readmission, we need to know three things. First, who was this tool trained on? If the training data came from a hospital system in Massachusetts, does it work equally well for our Arkansas patient population? Second, what does the vendor actually claim this tool can do, and what are its known limitations? Third, how was it validated? Was it tested on data similar to what we see in our clinic, or was validation done on some completely different population?‚Źé‚ŹéRight now, without DSI model card requirements, vendors can tell us almost nothing. One vendor last year showed us their decision support tool and when I asked about training data characteristics, they said that information was proprietary. We couldn't evaluate whether it was safe for our patient population. Another vendor couldn't explain what their tool's intended use case actually was. Were we supposed to use it as a screening tool or a diagnostic tool? The difference matters enormously for patient safety.‚Źé‚ŹéThe burden argument doesn't hold up here. Model cards and transparency disclosures aren't expensive to create if vendors are actually developing responsibly. If a vendor can't explain how their tool works and what it's been validated on, that's a red flag about their development process, not proof that the regulation is too stringent. The real cost comes from bad AI decisions that harm patients or that we have to work around because we don't trust the tool.‚Źé‚ŹéThe CFR sections 170.315(a) and related certification criteria exist because we learned from experience that vendors will cut corners when oversight is light. I've watched that happen in our own procurement process. When we removed certain requirements to speed up a vendor contract, we spent months afterwards dealing with data quality problems and clinical workflow issues we could have caught upfront.‚Źé‚Źé**Recommended Path Forward:**‚Źé‚ŹéRather than eliminating DSI transparency requirements, consider streamlining the certification process itself. Allow vendors to submit model cards in flexible formats. Accept third-party validation studies instead of requiring vendor-specific testing. Create tiered certification levels so that vendors serving smaller practices can meet a simplified standard while those serving larger health systems meet a more rigorous one.‚Źé‚ŹéThe deregulation agenda should focus on process efficiency, not on removing safeguards that actually protect patient safety. Theragen and organizations like ours will support certification reforms that cut unnecessary administrative overhead. But transparency about how AI tools work should stay. That's not regulatory burden. That's basic due diligence.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-12T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-12T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHospital and health system procurement teams need transparent AI model information to make evidence-based decisions about which tools to adopt and how to implement them safely in their local contexts.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒangry‚ôĒArkansas‚ôĒpractice manager‚ôĒ41‚ôĒLast month, our smaller practice spent six weeks wrestling with a vendor's certification requirements just to integrate their EHR with our existing lab system‚ÄĒrequirements that frankly seemed designed more to lock us in than to protect patient safety. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0068‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒBarbara Robinson‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒPatients deserve transparency and accountability requirements for AI systems used in healthcare decisions, just as they receive clear explanations for medications and medical procedures, to ensure they understand how artificial intelligence affect...‚ôĒMAKING SURE PATIENTS KNOW HOW AI WILL AFFECT THEIR CARE‚Źé‚ŹéLast year I switched rheumatologists because my arthritis got worse, and it was a nightmare getting my medical records transferred between offices. Three weeks and two phone calls just to move some lab results. My old doctor's office claimed they were being difficult about it, so I ended up paying for copies myself and repeating tests I'd already done. That experience taught me something important: when information doesn't move smoothly between providers, patients suffer. We pay the price, literally and with our health.‚Źé‚ŹéNow I'm reading about plans to loosen rules around how doctors share information, and I understand the goal is to make things easier. But I'm worried we're going the wrong direction. The same lesson from my records transfer applies to artificial intelligence in healthcare. If we remove requirements that make AI systems transparent and accountable, patients like me won't know how decisions about our care are actually being made.‚Źé‚ŹéHERE'S WHAT CONCERNS ME MOST‚Źé‚ŹéBefore my doctor prescribed a new medication, she explained how it works and what the side effects might be. That's basic. But when hospitals or clinics start using AI to decide what treatment I get, who gets approved for what drug, or how my care is prioritized, nobody's required to show me how that system works. There's no transparency report. No model card explaining what data was used to train it or whether it was tested fairly on patients who look like me. That's different from a medicine where we know the ingredients. With AI, we're left in the dark.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not asking for perfection or to stop innovation. I'm asking for the same clarity doctors owed me about my own medical records. If we're going to let AI make decisions about patient care, we need clear rules requiring those systems to show their work before they're used on real people. That's not a burden. That's basic trust.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-10T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-10T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒmedium‚ôĒsupportive‚ôĒIdaho‚ôĒretail worker‚ôĒ63‚ôĒLast year when my arthritis got worse, I had to switch from my longtime doctor to a rheumatologist in Boise, and it took three weeks and two phone calls just to get my lab results transferred over‚ÄĒmy new doctor said the old office was being difficult about it. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0069‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒBetty Allen‚ôĒAVIA Nexus Collaborative‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒAVIA Nexus Collaborative opposes the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria, arguing that AI model transparency requirements are essential for patient safety, preventing data monopolization, and maintaining...‚ôĒSubmitted to Federal Docket Management System‚Źé‚ŹéAVIA Nexus Collaborative respectfully submits this comment on the proposed deregulatory framework for Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability. The Collaborative represents researchers, clinicians, and data governance specialists working at the intersection of academic medicine and health IT implementation. Our members have direct experience with how regulatory requirements function in practice, and we write to address a specific concern: the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria, particularly the requirement for AI model cards and transparency disclosures.‚Źé‚ŹéThe background here matters because it shapes how we interpret what deregulation actually means in practice. Last year, a colleague's NIH-funded research project on clinical decision support nearly collapsed when the only validated dataset in our field was locked behind proprietary licensing agreements, even though that dataset had been built with public research dollars across a decade of work. The research team had two choices: pay licensing fees that consumed their entire grant budget or abandon the project. Three of four planned studies were shelved. This wasn't a failure of the market to innovate. It was a failure of accountability mechanisms that should have been transparent from the start. Had the original AI model undergone DSI certification with clear documentation of training data provenance, validation results, and known performance limitations, the licensing situation would have been easier to navigate, and the intellectual property claims would have been more defensible. Transparency doesn't constrain innovation. It channels it productively.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule seeks to remove several certification criteria under the assumption that doing so will reduce burden on developers and accelerate innovation. We understand that logic, and we don't dispute that some regulatory requirements can be unnecessarily duplicative or outdated. The Health IT Certification Program has indeed grown complex over time, and streamlining makes sense in principle. However, the DSI certification criterion is not one of those rules that has become obsolete through technological maturation. It serves a function that hasn't been replaced by market mechanisms or alternative oversight structures. Specifically, the requirement that AI models document intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and evidence of external validation does three things that matter enormously for the healthcare system: it ensures that clinical users can make informed decisions about when and how to apply the tool, it creates accountability when the tool fails or causes harm, and it prevents the kind of data monopolization that we witnessed firsthand.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider what the healthcare landscape looks like without this requirement. A hospital adopts a clinical decision support tool for sepsis screening. The vendor claims it works across multiple patient populations and has been validated on large datasets. No one asks to see the model card. No one knows that the training data was 73 percent male and only 12 percent of cases involved patients over age 80. Six months later, the hospital discovers the tool performs poorly in their elderly female population. By then, clinical workflows have been redesigned around the tool, staff have been trained on its recommendations, and changing course is expensive and disruptive. If the DSI certification criterion had remained in place, the hospital would have had access to that performance data before implementation. The vendor would have been required to disclose it. This isn't regulatory overreach. This is basic accountability.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule cites the CMS Conditions of Participation and other existing requirements as sufficient safeguards (86 Federal Register 45678, August 3, 2023). We've read those regulations closely. The CMS CoPs under 42 CFR Part 482 require hospitals to have policies for clinical decision support systems, but they don't mandate transparency about how those systems work or require documentation of their performance characteristics. The information blocking regulations under 45 CFR Part 171 address data flow but say nothing about AI model validation. The Office of the National Coordinator's 2015 Edition Cures Update standards (85 Federal Register 25642) set interoperability requirements but are silent on model card documentation. None of these regulations require what the DSI criterion requires: systematic, standardized disclosure of model performance across relevant patient subgroups and documented external validation before clinical use.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Collaborative recognizes the appeal of a lighter regulatory footprint. Developers spend resources on compliance. Certification takes time. But deregulation should be measured against what it costs, not just what it saves. Three specific concerns follow:‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, patient safety depends on clinicians understanding the limitations of the tools they use. A 2023 study in JAMA Internal Medicine found that clinicians overestimated the accuracy of algorithmic recommendations when model limitations were not disclosed (Gianfrancesco et al., 2023). When performance data was made available, clinicians adjusted their reliance appropriately. The DSI certification criterion exists because transparency changes behavior in ways that improve outcomes. Removing it shifts the burden to individual hospitals to conduct their own validation studies, which most cannot afford. Smaller health systems and rural providers will be at particular disadvantage. They'll adopt tools with no systematic understanding of how those tools perform in their own patient populations.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, the data consolidation problem will accelerate. When AI models are not required to disclose their training data characteristics, there's no market pressure on vendors to use diverse, representative datasets. There's pressure to use whatever data is cheapest and easiest to obtain. This creates a two-tier system: large vendors with proprietary datasets build tools optimized for populations that happen to be overrepresented in their training data, while smaller developers cannot compete without access to those same datasets. The intellectual property claims become stronger, licensing fees increase, and public research infrastructure gets repurposed as a tollbooth. We've seen this happen. We watched it happen to colleagues. It's not theoretical.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, accountability becomes impossible. If a tool causes harm and a lawsuit follows, what evidence will exist about how the tool was developed, what data it was trained on, and whether it was validated before deployment? Without the DSI criterion, that documentation is voluntary. Vendors will claim trade secrets. Hospitals will claim they relied on vendor claims. No one will be accountable. The model card requirement exists specifically to create a paper trail that allows both regulatory oversight and civil liability to function. Removing it doesn't eliminate risk. It just moves it outside the system where it can be managed.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Collaborative proposes a middle path that the proposed rule should consider: retain the DSI certification criterion for AI-based clinical decision support but streamline the documentation process itself. The current criterion requires substantial formatting compliance that adds burden without adding safety value. Instead, establish a simplified model card standard that captures the essential information: intended use and target population, validation results from external datasets, known performance limitations and subgroup performance differences, training data characteristics at a high level (size, temporal range, clinical setting, demographic composition), and a statement of any conflicts of interest related to model development or validation. This can be completed in 10 to 15 pages rather than the 50-page documentation packages vendors currently produce. The American Medical Association's Principles for Augmented Intelligence and Machine Learning in Healthcare (AMA Code of Medical Ethics, 2023) already call for exactly this kind of transparency. ONC's own guidance on responsible AI (ONC Playbook for Health Equity in AI, 2024) makes the same argument.‚Źé‚ŹéVendors will say this still adds cost. It does. But the cost is not disproportionate to the benefit. A health system implementing a clinical decision support tool spends millions on integration, training, and workflow redesign. Asking the vendor to document what the tool actually does and where it might fail costs tens of thousands at most. That's not a burden. That's due diligence. And due diligence is what distinguishes a regulated market from a caveat emptor market where buyers beware because sellers aren't required to tell them anything.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Collaborative stands ready to work with ONC and CMS on a revised DSI certification criterion that maintains the transparency requirement while reducing unnecessary compliance burden. Our members have implemented these requirements in real clinical settings. We know what information matters and what doesn't. We know what documentation is used in practice and what sits in filing cabinets. We can help design a streamlined process that preserves accountability without creating waste. But removing the requirement entirely would be a mistake. It would trade short-term regulatory relief for long-term problems: patient safety risks, data consolidation, and the erosion of accountability mechanisms that keep the health IT market functioning as something other than a series of black boxes that hospitals are required to trust but not allowed to understand.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-02-05T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-05T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ4‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe agency's claim that 'no publicly available evidence' supports transparency requirements conflates absence of evidence with evidence of absence; lack of published studies does not mean the criterion provides no value.‚ôĒacademic‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒangry‚ôĒRhode Island‚ôĒPhD researcher‚ôĒ41‚ôĒWhen my lab's NIH grant was nearly derailed last year because a biotech firm had proprietary access to the only validated dataset in our field‚ÄĒone built with public research dollars‚ÄĒI watched colleagues simply abandon their projects rather than pay the licensing fees. That's when I realized how quickly market consolidation disguises itself as innovation, and how reduced oversight transforms public research infrastructure into private tollbooths.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1412
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0070‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒMelissa Lopez‚ôĒPolicy Center for Maternal Mental Health‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health opposes removing Decision Support Interventions certification requirements for AI model transparency, arguing that standardized documentation of how AI tools are trained, validated, and limited is essen...‚ôĒJune 18, 2025‚Źé‚ŹéOffice of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology‚ŹéU.S. Department of Health and Human Services‚Źé200 Independence Avenue, S.W.‚ŹéWashington, D.C. 20201‚Źé‚ŹéRE: RFI Response on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions (Federal Register Volume 90, Issue 101)‚Źé‚ŹéThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health submits this comment in response to the proposed regulatory changes affecting Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria and information blocking standards. We do so with genuine ambivalence, because we understand the regulatory burden argument, and we've watched smaller health systems struggle with compliance costs. But we also can't ignore what happens when transparency requirements disappear.‚Źé‚ŹéLast year, we coordinated with a coalition of maternal health advocates across three Pennsylvania hospital systems. Each system was collecting postpartum depression screening data independently, using different validated instruments and different follow-up protocols. None of the systems could share results, even in anonymized form, because they operated under different privacy frameworks and had no standardized way to exchange DSI model information. One patient we interviewed attempted suicide between two hospital visits. The first system had conducted a PHQ-9 screening two weeks earlier; the second system had no access to that result and conducted its own screening without context. Both systems operated competently within their own walls. The problem wasn't negligence. It was fragmentation enabled by the absence of common transparency standards.‚Źé‚ŹéThat experience forced us to think harder about what "deregulation" actually means in this context. Removing the DSI certification criterion that requires AI model cards and transparency disclosures would lower compliance costs for vendors. We get that. But it would also remove one of the few mechanisms currently available to ensure that clinicians and health systems have access to critical information about how an AI tool was trained, what population it was validated on, what its known limitations are, and what it's actually intended to do. The regulation we're discussing is 45 CFR 170.315(b)(7), which establishes the certification requirement for decision support intervention systems. Removing this would align with the broader deregulatory agenda, but it would also remove standardized accountability.‚Źé‚Źé**Why Model Transparency Matters for Patient Safety**‚Źé‚ŹéThe concern isn't abstract. When a clinician uses an AI-assisted screening tool to decide whether a postpartum patient needs psychiatric referral, that clinician needs to know several things that only a proper model card can communicate:‚Źé‚Źé- What population was the model trained on? If the training data skewed toward white, insured patients from urban academic medical centers, the tool's performance on rural, low-income, or Black and Hispanic patients may differ significantly. Studies on bias in maternal health AI tools have found performance gaps as wide as 15-20 percentage points across racial groups (see the work by Char et al. on racial disparities in algorithmic decision-making in healthcare, Journal of the American Medical Association, 2023).‚Źé‚Źé- What are the known failure modes? Every algorithm has them. Some postpartum depression screening tools perform poorly on patients with comorbid substance use disorder. Others miss suicidality in adolescent mothers. These aren't flaws; they're limitations that competent clinicians need to know about so they can apply human judgment appropriately.‚Źé‚Źé- What was the external validation? There's a difference between an algorithm that performed well on the dataset it was trained on and one that was independently validated on a separate population. The 21st Century Cures Act Information Blocking Rule, codified at 42 CFR Part 2, Part 164, and Part 318, already requires information blocking actors to provide access to EHI without delay. But that rule doesn't require standardization of how AI tools communicate their own limitations.‚Źé‚Źé- What is the tool actually intended for? Is it a screening instrument meant to flag patients for further evaluation, or is it a diagnostic tool meant to replace clinical judgment? That distinction matters enormously for how a clinician uses the output.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen these details aren't standardized and disclosed, clinicians often don't know they don't know. They assume a tool performs the way they'd want it to perform. They apply it to populations it wasn't designed for. They miss its failure modes. And when something goes wrong, it's hard to trace back to a problem with the tool itself because there's no standardized documentation of what the tool was supposed to do in the first place.‚Źé‚Źé**The Interoperability Problem Runs Deeper Than Burden**‚Źé‚ŹéThe deregulatory proposal frames certification requirements as a burden that stifles innovation. That's true in some cases. Smaller vendors do face real compliance costs. But the proposal also assumes that removing transparency requirements will somehow improve data sharing and interoperability. We don't think that's how it works in practice.‚Źé‚ŹéThe three hospital systems we worked with last year all wanted to share patient data and decision support information. But they couldn't create a meaningful exchange because they had no standardized way to communicate what their respective DSI tools actually did. System A's postpartum depression screening algorithm had been validated on a sample of 8,000 patients in a Midwestern academic medical center. System B's algorithm had been validated on 2,000 patients in a rural clinic network. System C used a validated paper instrument paired with a simple rule-based decision tree. Without standardized model cards (the kind currently required by 45 CFR 170.315(b)(7)), the systems couldn't trust each other's data. A positive screen from System A's algorithm might mean something entirely different from a positive screen from System C's rule-based approach. So the data sat in separate silos.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving the certification requirement wouldn't have solved that problem. It would have made it worse. Instead of fragmented systems that at least have some standardized documentation available to vendors, we'd have fragmented systems with no documentation at all.‚Źé‚ŹéThe interoperability standards that do exist, like HL7 FHIR and the Trusted Exchange Framework and Common Agreement (TEFCA) referenced at 45 CFR 171.100 et seq., don't address the decision support layer. They address data exchange. But if the data being exchanged includes outputs from AI tools that have no standardized documentation, the exchange doesn't actually improve clinical decision-making. It just moves fragmented information faster.‚Źé‚Źé**What We're Actually Asking For**‚Źé‚ŹéWe're not asking for unchanged regulation. We're asking for smarter regulation that distinguishes between burden that protects patient safety and burden that doesn't. The DSI certification criterion isn't primarily a burden on innovation. It's a mechanism for ensuring that when a health system or vendor deploys an AI tool, there's a standardized way for other systems to understand what that tool does and doesn't do.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the goal is to reduce burden on vendors, the regulation could be modified in several ways that don't require removing transparency entirely:‚Źé‚Źé- Tiered certification requirements based on the risk level of the DSI and the size of the vendor deploying it. A small vendor building a screening tool for internal use might face different documentation requirements than a vendor selling nationally to hundreds of health systems.‚Źé‚Źé- Standardized model card templates that make compliance easier without reducing the actual information disclosed. The current regulation doesn't mandate a specific format. Many vendors already use templates based on the Model Card framework published by Mitchell et al. (Model Cards for Model Reporting, 2019). Standardizing this would reduce compliance burden while maintaining transparency.‚Źé‚Źé- Clear timelines for compliance and pathways for vendors to request extensions or modifications based on resource constraints.‚Źé‚ŹéBut removing the requirement entirely would trade patient safety for regulatory simplification. And in maternal mental health, where we're already losing patients to preventable suicides and where racial disparities in screening and referral are well documented, that trade doesn't make sense.‚Źé‚Źé**The Equity Angle**‚Źé‚ŹéOne more thing needs to be said clearly: maternal mental health disparities are partly a technology problem. Black and Hispanic mothers are screened for postpartum depression at lower rates than white mothers. When they are screened, they're less likely to be referred to treatment. Some of that is bias on the part of clinicians. Some of it is structural. But some of it is that the screening tools and decision support systems being used in many health systems were validated on predominantly white samples.‚Źé‚ŹéIf we remove the requirement for vendors to disclose what populations their DSI tools were validated on, we remove one of the few incentives for vendors to build tools that work across racial groups. And we make it harder for health systems to identify and correct for that bias when it exists.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Cures Act and subsequent guidance from ONC have emphasized the importance of equity in health IT (see ONC's Health IT Certification Program: 2024 Edition Base EHR Definition, which includes equity considerations). The DSI certification requirement is one of the few places where that commitment is actually operationalized. Removing it would be a step backward.‚Źé‚Źé**Conclusion**‚Źé‚ŹéWe respectfully urge HHS and ONC to retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion requiring model cards and transparency disclosures. The regulation can be streamlined. The compliance burden can be reduced through tiered requirements, standardized templates, or extended timelines. But the core requirement that AI tools be documented in a standardized way should remain.‚Źé‚ŹéPatient safety depends on it. Interoperability depends on it. And equity depends on it.‚Źé‚ŹéRespectfully submitted,‚Źé‚ŹéMelissa Lopez‚ŹéCampaign Director‚ŹéPolicy Center for Maternal Mental Health‚ŹéPhiladelphia, Pennsylvania‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-22T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-22T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ5‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒconcerned‚ôĒPennsylvania‚ôĒcampaign director‚ôĒ58‚ôĒLast year, I worked with a coalition advocating for maternal mental health screening standards, and we discovered that three separate health systems in our region were collecting identical postpartum depression data but couldn't share it‚ÄĒeven anonymously‚ÄĒbecause of conflicting privacy frameworks. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0071‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒKaren Anderson‚ôĒBroadspire, Inc.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒRemoving AI model transparency requirements would harm regional health systems by eliminating standardized disclosure information needed to independently evaluate tools and make evidence-based procurement decisions, forcing smaller organizations t...‚ôĒ**PROCUREMENT TRANSPARENCY FOR REGIONAL HEALTH SYSTEMS**‚Źé‚ŹéOur regional clients have been consolidating onto large vendor platforms over the past year, not because those systems fit their specialty workflows better, but because avoiding recertification headaches became cheaper than managing compliance gaps. I've watched implementation costs spike while actual clinical function declined. They're paying for features they'll never use because the certified system can't be tailored to how their providers actually work.‚Źé‚ŹéIf ONC removes certification requirements for AI model transparency disclosures, regional health systems lose the standardized information we need to evaluate tools independently. Procurement teams require clear disclosure of intended use, target populations, training data characteristics, and known risks to make evidence-based decisions about what fits our specialty practices. Without that transparency, smaller systems end up choosing between expensive bloated solutions from major vendors or implementing poorly-understood systems that create clinical gaps and compliance risk.‚Źé‚ŹéPer the 21st Century Cures Act implementation requirements, those model cards exist for a reason. Removing them doesn't reduce burden on us. It just shifts the risk onto organizations without resources to reverse-engineer vendor claims.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-06T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-06T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHospital and health system procurement teams need transparent AI model information to make evidence-based decisions about which tools to adopt and how to implement them safely in their local contexts.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒmedium‚ôĒsupportive‚ôĒOhio‚ôĒcompliance officer‚ôĒ40‚ôĒWhen our smaller regional clients started switching to the big EHR vendors last year just to avoid recertification headaches, I watched our implementation costs spike while their actual clinical workflows got worse‚ÄĒthey were paying for features they didn't need because the certified system couldn't be modified for their specialty practices. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0072‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒAndrew Thompson‚ôĒNo Delays, No Surprises Coalition‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe No Delays, No Surprises Coalition urges the Office of the National Coordinator to retain Decision Support Interventions (DSI) model card and transparency requirements rather than eliminate them, arguing that removing these certification criter...‚ôĒ**No Delays, No Surprises Coalition Comments on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity**‚Źé‚ŹéThe No Delays, No Surprises Coalition appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory actions affecting health information technology standards and certification criteria. Our coalition represents over 180 member organizations across Kentucky and neighboring states, including patient safety networks, health equity advocates, consumer protection groups, and clinician organizations. We have a particular interest in how changes to Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements will affect patient safety and equitable access to reliable clinical decision support tools.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CASE FOR RETAINING DSI MODEL CARD AND TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule would eliminate the requirement that DSI developers submit model cards documenting intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results (45 CFR 170.315(b)(6)). We urge the Office of the National Coordinator to retain these transparency requirements rather than remove them entirely. This is not an abstract concern about regulatory burden. Last year, my mother was prescribed a diabetes management algorithm that her endocrinologist relied on heavily‚ÄĒbut when we asked what population the algorithm was trained on and whether it had been validated in rural, lower-income patients, no one could answer. Her clinic didn't have access to the underlying model card. She's a 68-year-old woman living in a rural county with limited specialist access; the algorithm had been developed using data from urban academic medical centers. We never learned whether it accounted for the medication availability constraints we face in our region or whether it was validated for patients with her specific comorbidity profile.‚Źé‚ŹéThis experience shaped how I think about what deregulation should and shouldn't do. Removing certification criteria doesn't actually reduce burden if it shifts the burden onto patients and clinicians trying to figure out whether a tool is safe for their population. It just makes the burden invisible.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI model card requirement exists because AI-assisted clinical decision support is not the same as traditional clinical software. When a provider uses a DSI to recommend a medication, adjust a dosage, or flag a patient for intervention, they're relying on the developer's representation about what that tool can and cannot do reliably. According to the 2023 FDA guidance on clinical decision support (FDA-2019-D-5529, Federal Register 88:44230), developers have responsibility for the clinical validity and safety of their tools. But clinical validity cannot be assessed if clinicians and their patients have no way to know what the tool was trained on, what populations it was tested in, or what failure modes it exhibits.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current model card requirement is not burdensome in the way the rule proposes. It is genuinely necessary. A model card typically includes:‚Źé‚Źé- Intended use statement (e.g., "recommended for adult patients age 18-75 with Type 2 diabetes and no kidney disease")‚Źé- Target population description and any subpopulation limitations‚Źé- Known performance gaps (e.g., "validation study showed lower accuracy in patients taking ACE inhibitors")‚Źé- Training data characteristics (data sources, time period, sample size, demographic composition)‚Źé- External validation results (performance on datasets outside training data)‚Źé- Conflict of interest disclosures‚Źé‚ŹéNone of these items is redundant. Each one addresses a specific risk. When a model card is missing or vague, clinicians cannot make informed decisions about deployment. They cannot flag patients who fall outside the intended population. They cannot counsel patients about the tool's limitations. And they cannot audit outcomes later to see whether the tool performed as expected.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC CONCERNS ABOUT REMOVING ACCOUNTABILITY MECHANISMS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule suggests that removing certification criteria will "unleash innovation" and reduce compliance costs. But the real-world effect is to remove the documentation requirements that enable accountability. Consider what happens when an algorithm causes harm. Under current rules, a clinician, patient, or regulator can request the model card and assess whether the DSI was deployed appropriately for that patient's characteristics. Without model cards, there is no basis for that assessment. The developer can claim the tool was never intended for that use case, but the clinician has no way to verify what the tool was actually intended for.‚Źé‚ŹéThis creates particular risks in rural and underserved communities. Rural providers often lack in-house informatics expertise. They rely on certification criteria and transparency disclosures to evaluate tools before purchasing or implementing them. When those disclosures are optional, vendors have less incentive to provide them. Rural clinics then face a choice: implement tools without understanding their limitations, or forego decision support entirely. Neither option is acceptable.‚Źé‚ŹéThe coalition is also concerned about equity implications. AI bias in clinical decision support is well-documented (Obermeyer et al., Science 2019; Gianfrancesco et al., Nature Medicine 2018). Tools trained on data from large academic medical centers often perform worse for patients in rural areas, patients with lower incomes, and patients from underrepresented racial and ethnic groups. Without model cards that explicitly document subpopulation performance, these disparities remain invisible until they harm patients. By the time harm is detected, patients have already been affected.‚Źé‚Źé**RECOMMENDED APPROACH**‚Źé‚ŹéWe recommend that the final rule retain the DSI model card requirement in 45 CFR 170.315(b)(6) and consider strengthening it rather than eliminating it. Specifically:‚Źé‚Źé1. Clarify that model cards must be updated when external validation reveals performance gaps in specific subpopulations, with updates made available to existing users.‚Źé‚Źé2. Require certification bodies to verify that model card claims are supported by actual validation studies, not just developer assertions.‚Źé‚Źé3. Establish a public registry where model cards are available to clinicians and patients, similar to how clinical trial registries work.‚Źé‚Źé4. Extend the requirement to cover DSI tools used in quality measurement, population health management, and referral routing, not just diagnostic and treatment recommendations.‚Źé‚ŹéThe burden of maintaining a model card is minimal compared to the burden of managing a patient harmed by an undisclosed algorithm limitation. A well-designed transparency requirement creates accountability without preventing innovation. It simply requires developers to document what they claim their tools can do and to validate those claims across relevant populations.‚Źé‚ŹéDeregulation works best when it removes rules that actually prevent good outcomes. The DSI model card requirement prevents bad outcomes. It should remain.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-02-06T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-06T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ3‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHospital and health system procurement teams need transparent AI model information to make evidence-based decisions about which tools to adopt and how to implement them safely in their local contexts.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒconcerned‚ôĒKentucky‚ôĒsenior policy analyst‚ôĒ44‚ôĒWhen my mother was diagnosed with late-stage diabetes last year, her endocrinologist couldn't access her complete medication history from her previous provider because of fragmented records‚ÄĒbut I watched her struggle with the same frustration patients face when their data sits in silos. Now I'm concerned that opening access pathways without proper safeguards could mean her genetic predisposition information or mental health records end up weaponized by insurers or employers, particularly given what we're seeing happen to rural communities in our region who can least afford the consequences of a data breach.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1021
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0073‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒKaren Young‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe commenter opposes deregulatory proposals that would eliminate transparency requirements for AI systems in healthcare, arguing that removing model cards and certification criteria removes patient accountability mechanisms and informed consent p...‚ôĒRE: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity‚Źé‚ŹéLast month I spent three hours on hold with my rheumatologist's office trying to get my lab results transferred to a new specialist. I'm worried about artificial intelligence systems making decisions about my care, and I have no way to know how they were built, who they were tested on, or what could go wrong. The proposal to eliminate model cards and transparency disclosures is another way to hide information from patients. It's the same logic that kept my records from me: we control the information, so we control the conversation.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what this looks like in practice. The FDA's 2021 guidance on AI/ML-based Software as a Medical Device explicitly emphasized that algorithmic transparency is essential for clinical decision-making. The American Medical Association's Journal of Ethics has documented how patient autonomy erodes when health systems lack explainability standards. The ONC's 2023 Health IT Certification Program Report found that patient complaints about lack of transparency in AI-assisted diagnoses were among the top concerns reported. These aren't abstract concerns. They're about whether I can understand the logic behind a recommendation that affects my treatment.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen a provider or a tech company tells me they're removing certification criteria to "reduce burden," what I hear is: we want less oversight. What I understand from my own experience is that less oversight means I have fewer ways to advocate for myself when something goes wrong. I couldn't even get my own records without fighting. How am I supposed to hold a hospital accountable for an AI system I'm not allowed to understand?‚Źé‚Źé**CLARIFYING 'ACCESS' MEANS CLARIFYING WHO OWNS PATIENT DATA**‚Źé‚ŹéThe RFI asks how to clarify the definitions of 'access' and 'use' in information blocking regulations. Here's what I need: a definition that puts patients first. Access should mean I can get my information without fees, delays, or corporate gatekeeping. Use should mean that any system touching my health decisions operates under standards I can verify, not hidden behind proprietary claims.‚Źé‚ŹéRight now, the system works like this:‚Źé‚Źé- Providers claim they "don't do" transfers, charge extraction fees, and call it policy‚Źé- Tech companies build AI systems with no requirement to disclose training data, testing populations, or known failure modes‚Źé- Patients get caught between bureaucracies with no way to see what's actually happening to their information or their care decisions‚Źé- When something goes wrong, we're told the system is too complex to explain‚Źé‚ŹéThat's not innovation. That's opacity dressed up as progress.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT PATIENTS NEED**‚Źé‚ŹéBefore an AI system touches my health decisions, someone has to tell me how it works, who it was tested on, and what could go wrong. That's not asking for perfection. That's asking for the same informed consent that's supposed to be fundamental to medicine. The Commonwealth Fund has documented how patient trust in health IT systems erodes without explainability standards. Trust matters. It affects whether people seek care, whether they follow recommendations, whether they feel safe in the system.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving model cards doesn't unleash prosperity. It unleashes risk for patients while protecting companies from accountability. I've already spent too much time fighting for access to information I created. Don't make it worse by letting AI systems operate in the dark.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-05T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-05T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒangry‚ôĒPennsylvania‚ôĒcustomer service representative‚ôĒ40‚ôĒLast month, I spent three hours on the phone with my rheumatologist's office trying to get my lab results transferred to a new specialist, only to be told they "don't do that" and that I'd have to pay $50 for paper records‚ÄĒrecords I'd already paid for when the tests were run.
