CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0076‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒCarol Garcia‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA retired nurse argues that while deregulation of health IT certification criteria may reduce administrative burden, removing transparency requirements for AI model performance, validation data, and failure modes creates dangerous blind spots for ...‚ôĒ**RE: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions**‚Źé‚ŹéAfter thirty years in nursing, I've learned that good intentions and good outcomes don't always line up. When my daughter needed her oncology imaging transferred last year, the six-week delay wasn't some bureaucrat's spite. The hospital had legitimate reasons for their controls. But that doesn't make the outcome less frustrating or less dangerous. I'm writing because I see the same pattern in this proposed rule: the instinct to cut away red tape makes sense, but some of what looks like tape is actually a safety rail.‚Źé‚ŹéI understand the argument for removing certification criteria that developers call duplicative. Lord knows I've filled out forms that made no sense. But I need to be direct about one thing: removing transparency requirements for AI model performance and validation data creates a blind spot in clinical decision-making that patients and clinicians have no way to see through. The FDA's 2021 guidance on AI/ML-based software as medical devices emphasized exactly this‚ÄĒthe importance of understanding model performance across different patient populations and known failure modes. I've read too many incident reports that traced back to a clinician who didn't understand what a tool could and couldn't do. In one case from my last year of work, a decision support system flagged a medication interaction it had never been trained to catch in elderly patients. No one knew that limitation existed. A patient was harmed before anyone figured it out.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen I worked nights in the ER, I understood why we kept tight controls on who could access what. But I also saw patients kept in the dark about their own information. The tension is real. The answer, though, isn't to choose one side completely. Streamlining interoperability standards like FHIR‚ÄĒthat's reasonable. Cutting unnecessary administrative burden on implementation‚ÄĒfine. But removing the criteria that require vendors to document what data trained their algorithms, what populations it was validated on, and what its failure modes are, removes the only mechanism patients and clinicians have to understand these tools. The Institute of Medicine documented this back in 2012: lack of clinician understanding of health IT systems linked directly to adverse events. That hasn't changed.‚Źé‚ŹéI've read the oncology research too. Sixty-seven percent of surveyed oncologists cited unclear model limitations as a reason they didn't trust AI tools their institutions wanted them to use. Unclear limitations. Not because the tools were bad, but because no one could explain what they actually did and didn't do. That's not a burden to eliminate. That's information patients deserve.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not saying keep everything as is. The certification program has genuine inefficiencies. But information blocking and transparency requirements exist because information without understanding is worse than no information at all. My daughter finally got her scans transferred. But she never learned why it took six weeks, and she still doesn't know if anything was missed during that gap. Patients deserve better than that.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-02-02T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-02T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒresigned‚ôĒArizona‚ôĒretired nurse‚ôĒ55‚ôĒWhen my daughter switched oncologists last year, it took six weeks and three phone calls just to get her imaging files transferred‚ÄĒthe first hospital kept saying they needed written authorization forms we'd already submitted. I realized then how frustrated patients must feel when their own medical records feel like they're held hostage, but I also remember working nights in the ER and knowing exactly why we kept tight controls on who could access what. There's a real tension between making information flow freely and making sure it doesn't end up in the wrong hands, and I'm not sure which side of that line we should be trusting more.‚ôĒRemoving AI model card and transparency requirements eliminates the only mechanism patients and clinicians have to understand what data trained an AI system, what populations it was validated on, and what its known failure modes are‚ÄĒcreating a blind spot in high-stakes clinical decisions. | The tension between data access and data safety is real, but the answer isn't to strip away transparency requirements; it's to keep them while streamlining the bureaucratic overhead. Patients deserve to know the limitations of the tools influencing their care, just as they deserve access to their own records.‚ôĒA retired nurse who understands both the legitimate need for information controls and the patient's right to understand the AI systems making recommendations about their health.‚ôĒFDA guidance on AI/ML-based Software as a Medical Device (2021), which emphasizes transparency in model performance across subpopulations | Institute of Medicine report on patient safety and health IT implementation failures (2012), linking lack of clinician understanding to adverse events | NEJM Catalyst study on oncology AI adoption barriers, noting that 67% of surveyed oncologists cited 'unclear model limitations' as a barrier to trust | ONC Health IT Certification Program Annual Report (2023), documenting the role of transparency criteria in reducing implementation failures‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ487
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0077‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDorothy Thomas‚ôĒTexas Medical Association‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Texas Medical Association opposes removing certification requirements for clinical decision support AI tools, arguing that transparency standards requiring model documentation, validation data, and risk disclosures are essential safeguards for...‚ôĒ**COMMENT ON PROPOSED REMOVAL OF DECISION SUPPORT INTERVENTIONS CERTIFICATION CRITERION**‚Źé‚ŹéTexas Medical Association‚ŹéSubmitted to the Health and Human Services Office of the Secretary/ONC‚ŹéRegulatory Docket [ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions]‚Źé‚ŹéThe Texas Medical Association respectfully submits this comment on the proposed removal of certification requirements for Decision Support Interventions (DSI), specifically the model transparency and accountability standards currently mandated under 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1). While we recognize the administration's legitimate interest in reducing unnecessary regulatory burden on health IT developers, we urge the Office of the National Coordinator and the Assistant Secretary for Technology Policy to retain the existing DSI certification criterion requiring model cards, transparency disclosures, and external validation documentation. The current framework does not impede innovation. Rather, it provides physicians and safety-net providers with the information necessary to deploy artificial intelligence responsibly in clinical settings where patient vulnerability is highest.‚Źé‚Źé**Background and Organizational Perspective**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Texas Medical Association represents nearly 50,000 physicians and medical students across Texas, including substantial membership from rural safety-net providers, safety-net clinics, and community health centers serving uninsured and low-income populations. Our members practice in environments where clinical decision support tools are increasingly embedded in electronic health record systems, order entry workflows, and diagnostic algorithms. We have observed firsthand how AI-assisted decision support is being deployed in community health centers, federally qualified health centers (FQHCs), and rural hospitals without adequate transparency about how these tools were trained, what populations they were validated on, or what failure modes clinicians should anticipate.‚Źé‚ŹéLast month, our policy research team reviewed disparities data from safety-net member clinics across rural Pennsylvania and Texas. We noticed a troubling pattern: clinics serving predominantly Latino agricultural workers and uninsured populations documented patient outcomes and adverse events at substantially lower granularity than urban counterparts. When we interviewed clinic directors, the explanation was not lack of capability but rather a rational response to liability concerns and resource constraints. One Potter County clinic director told us explicitly: "We don't collect it if we don't have to, because we can't afford the liability." This comment captures the reality that vulnerable populations are pushed further into the margins when regulatory safeguards are removed. AI transparency requirements are not an exception to this principle. They are foundational to ensuring that the populations most likely to be harmed by algorithmic bias actually have recourse when something goes wrong.‚Źé‚Źé**The Real-World Problem with Removing DSI Requirements**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed deregulatory action would eliminate the requirement that developers of AI-assisted clinical decision support systems provide model cards, documentation of intended use, target population specifications, known risks and limitations, training data characteristics, and results from external validation studies. The stated rationale is to reduce burden on developers and accelerate innovation. We understand this argument in the abstract. However, the actual effect would be to shift risk entirely onto clinicians and vulnerable patients without providing the information necessary to identify or mitigate that risk.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider a concrete example from our recent member survey. A mid-sized FQHC in South Texas implemented a sepsis prediction algorithm embedded in its EHR vendor's clinical decision support module. The tool was presented to the medical director as "validated" and "ready to use." When our policy director asked whether the FQHC had received documentation about the training population, the algorithm's performance across racial and ethnic subgroups, or the specific circumstances under which the tool was known to perform poorly, the medical director said no such documentation existed. The vendor had provided only a general description of the tool's purpose and a claim of FDA-grade accuracy. The FQHC had no way to know that the algorithm was trained primarily on data from academic medical centers in the Northeast and had never been validated in a rural, majority-Latino population with high rates of comorbidity and delayed care-seeking.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not an isolated incident. It reflects a systematic information asymmetry: vendors have full knowledge of how their tools work and where they have been tested, while clinicians who will be responsible for patient outcomes have almost no visibility into these details. When the certification criterion for DSI model cards and transparency disclosures is removed, this asymmetry becomes legally and ethically unbearable.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1) requirement is modest. It does not prohibit the use of proprietary algorithms or require developers to disclose trade secrets. It requires only that developers provide:‚Źé‚Źé1. Documentation of the intended clinical use case and the specific patient populations the tool was designed for‚Źé2. A description of the training data, including size, source, and demographic characteristics‚Źé3. Known risks, limitations, and circumstances where the tool's performance degrades‚Źé4. Results from external validation studies in populations different from the training set‚Źé5. Information about how the tool will be updated and monitored for performance drift‚Źé‚ŹéThese are not onerous requirements. They are baseline good practice in any clinical tool deployment. A medical device manufacturer does not object to providing information about how a stent was tested or what patient populations it was validated in. The same principle applies to software-based clinical decision support.‚Źé‚Źé**The Safety-Net Provider Perspective**‚Źé‚ŹéOur member safety-net providers face a distinct problem. They serve populations with high rates of health disparities, including African American and Latino patients, immigrants, low-income individuals, and people with multiple chronic conditions. These are exactly the populations most likely to be harmed by algorithmic bias or inadequate external validation. Yet safety-net providers have the fewest resources to conduct their own independent validation studies or to push back against vendor claims about tool accuracy and appropriateness.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen a large health system with a dedicated informatics team and a research department implements a new clinical decision support tool, the institution has capacity to evaluate the tool's performance across its patient populations and to discontinue use if the tool performs poorly for certain subgroups. When a 50-bed rural hospital or a community health center with three clinicians implements the same tool, this capacity does not exist. The institution must rely entirely on the information provided by the vendor and on whatever transparency the vendor chooses to provide.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving the DSI certification criterion would make safety-net providers more vulnerable, not less. It would eliminate the one regulatory mechanism that currently requires vendors to provide the information these organizations need to make informed decisions. The deregulatory action assumes that market competition will naturally incentivize transparency. Our experience suggests otherwise. In the absence of regulatory requirement, vendors have no competitive advantage to providing transparency, particularly when transparency might reveal limitations or bias in their tools. Safety-net providers will simply lose access to information they currently have.‚Źé‚Źé**Responding to the Deregulation Rationale**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule's preamble suggests that DSI certification requirements are "prescriptive," "reduce developer flexibility," and "slow innovation without clear benefit." We disagree with each of these characterizations.‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, the requirement is not prescriptive about the underlying technology. Developers can use any machine learning algorithm, any training approach, and any validation methodology they choose. The requirement is only that developers document what they did and share that documentation with customers. This is descriptive, not prescriptive.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, the requirement does not reduce developer flexibility. It requires transparency, not specific technical choices. A developer can design its AI model however it sees fit. It must simply be honest about how it was trained and what populations it was validated on.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, the assertion that the requirement "slows innovation" is unsupported by evidence. The DSI certification criterion has been in place since the 2015 Edition Health IT Certification Companion Guide. We are not aware of any documented cases where the requirement prevented a vendor from bringing a tool to market or substantially delayed deployment. What we do observe is that vendors have adapted to the requirement by providing model cards and validation documentation as a matter of course. The requirement has not stalled the deployment of clinical AI tools. It has instead created a baseline expectation that developers will document their work.‚Źé‚ŹéFinally, the claim that the requirement provides "unclear benefit" misses the point. The benefit is not to vendors. It is to clinicians and patients. The benefit is that a physician in a rural clinic can read the model card for a sepsis prediction tool and see whether the tool was validated in a population similar to the one she treats. The benefit is that a safety-net provider can discontinue use of a tool if the documentation reveals that the tool was never tested in patients with the comorbidities common in its patient population. These are substantial benefits to patient safety and health equity.‚Źé‚Źé**Specific Concerns About Information Asymmetry and Accountability**‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving the DSI certification criterion would create an accountability vacuum. Under the current framework, if a clinical decision support tool causes harm because of inadequate transparency about its limitations or biases, a clinician or patient could point to the fact that the vendor failed to provide the information required by 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1). This creates a legal and professional incentive for vendors to provide accurate, complete documentation.‚Źé‚ŹéWithout the certification requirement, this incentive disappears. A vendor could provide minimal or misleading information about how a tool was trained and what populations it was validated on, and a clinician would have no regulatory basis to demand better. The vendor could argue that it provided whatever information it chose to provide, and that any harm resulting from inadequate understanding of the tool's limitations is the clinician's responsibility.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is particularly problematic in the context of the information blocking rule, 45 CFR 171.100 et seq. The information blocking rule is intended to prevent health IT developers and providers from blocking access to electronic health information. But what is the point of access to information if the information available is incomplete, misleading, or reflects an information asymmetry that prevents clinicians from making informed decisions about whether to use a particular tool? Removing the DSI certification criterion would undermine the entire logic of the information blocking rule by ensuring that critical information about AI tools remains trapped with vendors.‚Źé‚Źé**The Health Equity Imperative**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Texas Medical Association is deeply committed to health equity. We have published position statements on addressing racial and ethnic disparities in healthcare, on improving access to care for uninsured patients, and on reducing barriers to care for immigrant communities. We recognize that artificial intelligence has tremendous potential to improve clinical decision-making and to identify patterns that human clinicians might miss. But AI also has the potential to amplify existing disparities if tools are trained on biased data, validated only in narrow populations, or deployed without understanding their limitations.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification requirement is a health equity safeguard. It ensures that when a tool is deployed in a patient population different from the one it was primarily developed for, clinicians have documentation about what that difference means. It ensures that safety-net providers, which serve the most vulnerable patients and have the fewest resources for independent validation, at least have access to the information vendors have about their own tools.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving this requirement would disproportionately harm the patients served by safety-net providers. It would be presented as a deregulatory action that reduces burden, but the actual effect would be to shift risk from vendors (who have full information about their tools) to clinicians and patients (who have incomplete information). This is not good policy. It is not innovation-friendly. It is risk-shifting.‚Źé‚Źé**Maintaining the Requirement Does Not Impede Innovation**‚Źé‚ŹéWe want to be clear about what we are asking for. We are not asking the ONC to expand the DSI certification criterion or to make it more onerous. We are not asking for new requirements about how AI models should be trained or what performance metrics vendors should achieve. We are asking simply that the current, modest requirement remain in place.‚Źé‚ŹéThe requirement has been in effect for nearly a decade. During this time, the health IT industry has not stalled. AI-assisted clinical decision support tools have proliferated. Vendors have developed sophisticated algorithms for sepsis prediction, acute kidney injury detection, readmission risk stratification, and countless other clinical applications. The certification requirement has not prevented any of this. What it has done is ensure that clinicians and health systems have information about how these tools work and what populations they were validated on.‚Źé‚ŹéIf anything, the requirement has encouraged innovation by creating trust in the health IT ecosystem. Clinicians are more willing to deploy tools that come with transparent documentation than tools that come with vendor assertions and nothing else. Vendors that provide clear, honest documentation about their tools have a competitive advantage over vendors that provide minimal information. The requirement has thus created incentives aligned with both safety and innovation.‚Źé‚Źé**Specific Language Recommendations**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the ONC moves forward with deregulatory changes to the certification framework, we urge that 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1) remain unchanged. If the ONC believes the requirement should be modified, we recommend the following language be retained as mandatory:‚Źé‚ŹéThe health IT module must be capable of providing or making available the following information to a clinician or health system prior to deployment in clinical practice:‚Źé‚Źé(i) Documentation of the clinical use case for which the decision support intervention was designed, including the specific patient populations and clinical scenarios the tool is intended for;‚Źé‚Źé(ii) A description of the data used to train the algorithm, including the source, size, date range, and demographic characteristics of the training population;‚Źé‚Źé(iii) A summary of known limitations, risks, and circumstances where the tool's performance is degraded or unreliable;‚Źé‚Źé(iv) Results of external validation studies conducted in patient populations different from the training population, with particular attention to validation in populations that may differ from the training data in race, ethnicity, age, comorbidity burden, or other clinically relevant characteristics;‚Źé‚Źé(v) A process for ongoing monitoring of tool performance and a plan for notifying users if performance drift is detected.‚Źé‚ŹéThis language is straightforward, achievable for any competent developer, and directly responsive to the information needs of clinicians and health systems deploying these tools.‚Źé‚Źé**Conclusion**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Texas Medical Association supports innovation in health information technology. We support reducing unnecessary regulatory burden on developers and health systems. But we do not support removing safeguards that protect patient safety and health equity. The DSI certification criterion is not an unnecessary burden. It is a baseline requirement that vendors document how their clinical decision support tools were developed and validated.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving this requirement would shift risk from vendors (who have full information) to clinicians and patients (who have incomplete information). It would disproportionately harm safety-net providers and the vulnerable populations they serve. It would undermine the logic of the information blocking rule by ensuring that critical information about AI tools remains proprietary to vendors. Most importantly, it would eliminate one of the few regulatory mechanisms that currently ensure clinicians have access to information necessary to use these tools safely and responsibly.‚Źé‚ŹéWe urge the ONC and ASTP to retain the DSI certification criterion in its current form. This is not a matter of resisting innovation or defending unnecessary regulation. It is a matter of ensuring that the patients most vulnerable to algorithmic bias and inadequate validation have some access to information about the tools being used to make decisions about their care.‚Źé‚ŹéThe clinic director in Potter County was right: we don't collect data we don't have to, because we can't afford the consequences. The same principle applies to vendors and clinical decision support. Without regulatory requirement, vendors will provide only the minimum information necessary to make a sale. Clinicians will deploy tools without understanding their limitations. And vulnerable patients will bear the consequences of decisions made by algorithms that were never validated in populations like theirs.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not the path to innovation. It is the path to deeper health inequities. We respectfully urge the ONC to maintain the current DSI certification requirements as a foundational safeguard for patient safety and health equity.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-20T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-20T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ8‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving DSI transparency requirements creates a dangerous regulatory gap where AI-based clinical decision support tools face fewer disclosure obligations than traditional medical devices, undermining consistent safety standards.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒurgent‚ôĒPennsylvania‚ôĒpolicy director‚ôĒ48‚ôĒLast month, I was reviewing disparities data from our member clinics across rural Pennsylvania, and I noticed our safety-net providers were documenting patient outcomes at half the granularity of urban counterparts‚ÄĒnot because of capability gaps, but because the regulatory framework incentivizes them to minimize data collection. When I spoke with a clinic director in Potter County serving predominantly Latino agricultural workers, she told me point-blank: "We don't collect it if we don't have to, because we can't afford the liability." That conversation crystallized for me that loosening data governance requirements doesn't liberate anyone‚ÄĒit just pushes vulnerable populations further into the margins where their health outcomes become invisible.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2634
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0078‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒRobert Miller‚ôĒCollege of American Pathologists (CAP)‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe College of American Pathologists opposes removing Decision Support Intervention (DSI) certification and model card transparency requirements, arguing that these safeguards are essential for clinicians to understand AI diagnostic tools' limitat...‚ôĒ**Submitted on behalf of the College of American Pathologists**‚Źé‚ŹéThe College of American Pathologists (CAP) appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory actions under the Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability initiative. As the largest organization of board-certified pathologists in the United States, with nearly 18,000 members and accreditation programs serving over 7,000 laboratories, the College has a direct stake in how AI-assisted diagnostic tools are regulated and deployed across clinical practice.‚Źé‚ŹéWe write to express concern about the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria, specifically the model card and transparency disclosure requirements. While the College understands the intent to reduce regulatory burden and accelerate innovation, we believe the current DSI framework represents a proportionate safeguard that should be retained. Our position is informed by direct experience with the interoperability challenges that plague smaller laboratory operations and by recognition that deregulation without guardrails creates different‚ÄĒnot fewer‚ÄĒrisks to patient safety and clinical decision-making.‚Źé‚Źé**The Real Cost of Unguarded Deregulation**‚Źé‚ŹéLast year, CAP members collaborated with a rural pathology lab in South Carolina on a quality-assurance initiative examining their laboratory information system and its integration with external platforms. What emerged from that study was instructive. The lab could not interface with three major EHR systems‚ÄĒnot because of technical limitations. The barrier was purely regulatory: the compliance overhead required to meet certification standards made interoperability economically unfeasible for a 15-person operation. They faced a choice between investing in certified interfaces or closing. They chose a workaround involving manual data transfer, which introduced transcription errors and delayed reporting by 12 to 48 hours.‚Źé‚ŹéThis experience crystallized something for CAP members: regulatory burden can prevent the very standardization and data quality improvements that better clinical outcomes depend on. But the solution is not to abandon certification entirely. It is to ensure that when we do regulate, we regulate the right things. DSI certification is one of those things.‚Źé‚ŹéUnder 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1), which establishes the DSI criterion, developers must disclose intended use, target population, known limitations, training data characteristics, and results of external validation testing. This is not burdensome theatrics. It is the minimum documentation needed for a clinician to understand whether a tool applies to their patient population and to recognize its failure modes. The model card requirement, aligned with standards from the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) AI Risk Management Framework, creates a common language between developers, clinicians, and laboratories.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider what happens without it. A pathology lab purchases AI software for digital slide analysis and deploys it in their general surgical pathology workflow. The vendor has not disclosed that the model was trained exclusively on specimens from a particular geographic region and race-stratified cohort, or that external validation showed a 6 percent false-negative rate for adenocarcinomas in patients over 75. The lab does not know this. The pathologist reviewing the AI output does not know this. A patient receives a delayed diagnosis. This scenario is not hypothetical. It reflects actual gaps in clinical validation that have emerged in recent FDA premarket review of AI/ML-based Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) products (see FDA Guidance for Industry: Clinical Evaluation of Quantitative Imaging Software as a Medical Device, December 2023).‚Źé‚Źé**Why Model Transparency Prevents Market Consolidation**‚Źé‚ŹéThe College recognizes that the proposed rule aims to promote competition and innovation. We share that goal. But transparency requirements actually serve that goal by lowering barriers for smaller vendors to enter the market. A startup biotech company developing a new pathology AI tool can meet the DSI model card requirement with the same documentation it would prepare for an FDA submission or a hospital credentialing committee. The requirement is not proprietary. It does not demand trade secrets. It demands that the vendor articulate what the tool does, who it was tested on, and what it cannot do reliably.‚Źé‚ŹéWithout such requirements, the market consolidates around large vendors with established relationships and institutional trust. A hospital system evaluates two AI tools: one from a well-known EHR vendor, one from an innovative startup. Without mandatory transparency disclosures, the hospital defaults to the incumbent. With DSI requirements, the startup's model card allows the hospital to conduct a fair technical comparison. The startup competes on merit, not on brand.‚Źé‚ŹéCAP's experience accrediting laboratories under the Clinical Laboratory Improvement Amendments (CLIA; 42 CFR 493.1) shows that transparency requirements increase quality and competition. Laboratories that clearly document their testing protocols, quality control measures, and validation studies attract better partnerships and higher volumes. They do not resist transparency. They embrace it as a marker of rigor. We expect the same pattern in AI-assisted diagnostics.‚Źé‚Źé**The Interoperability Paradox**‚Źé‚ŹéHere is what troubles the College about the current deregulatory proposal. The rule seeks to remove barriers to interoperability and data exchange under the Trusted Exchange Framework and Common Agreement (TEFCA) and the information blocking provisions of 42 CFR 171 et seq. That goal is sound. But the proposed removal of DSI certification criteria conflicts with it.‚Źé‚ŹéInteroperability depends on trust. If a pathology lab receives AI-assisted diagnostic data from a referral facility via a health information exchange, the receiving lab needs to know the provenance and limitations of that data. Was the AI tool trained on a population that matches the current patient? What was the external validation sample size? What is the false-positive rate in the relevant subgroup? These questions cannot be answered if vendors are not required to disclose them. The result is not faster data exchange. It is hesitant or rejected exchange, because downstream users cannot responsibly accept data they do not understand.‚Źé‚ŹéThe College supported the original DSI criterion when it was finalized in 2015 (80 Federal Register 62704, October 16, 2015). We continue to support it, with one clarification: the requirement should apply to all clinical decision support software that presents output to a clinician as a recommendation or finding, regardless of how the software is labeled or distributed. Current gaps in the definition of DSI have allowed vendors to market diagnostic AI tools as "informational" rather than "decisional," thereby circumventing the model card requirement. The ONC should close this gap as part of the final rule.‚Źé‚Źé**Specific Recommendations**‚Źé‚ŹéThe College recommends the following:‚Źé‚Źé1. Retain the DSI certification criterion at 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1) and the model card disclosure requirement, with clarification that the definition of DSI includes any AI or algorithmic tool that generates output intended to inform or guide clinical decision-making in diagnosis, prognosis, or treatment recommendation.‚Źé‚Źé2. Align DSI model card requirements with the NIST AI Risk Management Framework (NIST AI RMF 1.0, January 2023) to create consistency with emerging federal AI governance standards. This reduces burden by allowing vendors to use a single documentation standard across FDA, ONC, and hospital credentialing processes.‚Źé‚Źé3. Establish a grace period of 18 months for existing DSI-certified software to comply with any updated model card specifications, allowing vendors time to gather additional external validation data without market disruption.‚Źé‚Źé4. Require that DSI model cards be made available to end-users and healthcare institutions upon request, with allowances for redaction of proprietary training data specifications. Transparency to the market and to clinicians is the goal, not disclosure of trade secrets.‚Źé‚Źé5. Create a voluntary registry of DSI model cards, maintained by ONC, to allow hospitals and laboratories to compare tools and make informed purchasing decisions. This registry would serve the competitive function that mandatory certification currently serves.‚Źé‚ŹéThe College is not opposed to deregulation where it truly reduces burden without compromising safety. We see the value in streamlining interoperability standards, reducing redundant certification steps, and allowing small vendors to compete. But we cannot support the removal of a requirement that ensures clinicians understand the tools they are using to make diagnostic decisions that affect patient care.‚Źé‚ŹéThe rural South Carolina pathology lab we worked with last year remains caught in a compliance trap, but for different reasons than the regulatory framework. The College stands ready to work with ONC and CMS to design deregulation that actually solves that problem while preserving the safeguards that matter most.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-21T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-21T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ4‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving DSI transparency requirements creates a dangerous regulatory gap where AI-based clinical decision support tools face fewer disclosure obligations than traditional medical devices, undermining consistent safety standards.‚ôĒacademic‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒhopeful‚ôĒNorth Carolina‚ôĒbiostatistician‚ôĒ36‚ôĒLast year, I collaborated with a pathology lab in rural South Carolina on a quality-assurance study, and we discovered their reporting system couldn't interface with three major EHR platforms‚ÄĒnot because of technical limitations, but because compliance overhead made interoperability economically unfeasible for smaller operations. I saw firsthand how regulatory burden was actually *preventing* the kind of data standardization that better outcomes depend on, and it made me wonder whether we've been measuring the right tradeoffs between safety and innovation all along.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1339
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0079‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒAshley Sanchez‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA healthcare patient and former office manager argues against removing the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion, contending that transparency requirements for AI model cards and validation disclosures are essential safeguards tha...‚ôĒLast year when my cardiologist retired, I spent three weeks calling back and forth between his office and my new doctor trying to get my test results transferred. They kept saying the files were incompatible or lost in the system. My daughter finally had to drive down from Billings and sit in the office with me to print everything manually off my patient portal. I shouldn't have needed her help at my age, but that experience taught me something important: the systems we're talking about deregulating are supposed to make things easier for people like me, not harder.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm writing because I'm concerned about the proposal to remove the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion that requires AI model cards and transparency disclosures. I understand the argument that regulations slow down innovation and create burden for developers. I'm not opposed to reducing unnecessary red tape. But there's a difference between cutting through bureaucracy and cutting out the safeguards that keep patients safe when AI is involved in their medical care.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current requirement under 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1) asks developers to disclose the intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results for AI tools used in clinical decision support. This isn't a trivial checkbox. When my new cardiologist started using an AI system to help interpret my EKG results, I wanted to know: Was this system tested on people my age? Women? People with my specific health history? What does it actually do? What are its limits? I didn't ask those questions out of paranoia. I asked because my health depends on understanding what my doctor is using to make decisions about my care.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what troubles me. If we remove the requirement for AI model cards and transparency disclosures, clinicians won't have access to the information they need to use these tools responsibly. My cardiologist told me last month that he uses several AI-assisted systems but doesn't always know exactly how they work or what they were trained on. He's a good doctor and he's honest about that limitation. But shouldn't the developers be required to tell him? And shouldn't I, as the patient, have some assurance that the tool has been validated on people like me? The FDA requires drug manufacturers to disclose how medications were tested. Why should AI in healthcare be different?‚Źé‚ŹéI've read some of the arguments from health IT companies. They say certification requirements are expensive and slow down product development. They say market competition will weed out bad products without needing government rules. I understand that logic, but I've also seen what happens when there's no transparency requirement. I worked as an office manager in healthcare for thirty-five years, and I watched vendors make promises they didn't keep. I watched systems get rolled out that weren't ready. When there's money at stake and no clear accountability mechanism, corners get cut. That's not cynicism. That's just what I observed over decades in that world.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI criterion as it exists now is actually quite reasonable. It doesn't prevent innovation. It doesn't require developers to spend years getting approval. It asks them to document what they built, how they tested it, what it's good at, and what it's not good at. Model cards are now standard practice in AI development across many industries. They're not some radical burden. What they do is create accountability. They give clinicians the information they need. And they give patients like me some assurance that the system involved in my care wasn't trained only on people who don't look like me or have my medical history.‚Źé‚ŹéI know the administration's goal is to reduce regulations and let markets work. I'm not against that as a general principle. But health care is different from many other markets. When I buy a car, I can test drive it, read reviews, and if it breaks down I can choose a different brand next time. When my doctor uses an AI system to help diagnose my heart condition, I don't have those choices. I have to trust that the system works. I have to trust that it was tested responsibly. The only way I can have that trust is if there are rules that require developers to be transparent about what they built and how they tested it.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving the DSI certification criterion sounds like a small change, but it's not. It removes one of the few concrete requirements that tie health IT developers to accountability for the safety and effectiveness of their products. It tells patients and clinicians that transparency about AI systems is optional. I don't think that's the right message. I think we should keep this requirement in place. I think we should maybe even strengthen it, make sure that model card requirements extend to more kinds of AI systems used in health care, not just decision support.‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be clear about one thing. I'm not opposed to innovation in health IT. I'm not someone who thinks all regulations are good or that the status quo is perfect. The fact that I couldn't get my records transferred easily last year showed me that the current system has real problems. But the solution to those problems isn't to remove safeguards around AI transparency. The solution is to make sure we have rules that require interoperability and information sharing while also making sure that the tools being used to manage and interpret that information are trustworthy and well-validated. Those things aren't in conflict. You can have both.‚Źé‚ŹéKeep the DSI certification criterion. Keep the requirement for AI model cards and transparency disclosures. Let innovation happen, but let it happen with accountability. That's what patients like me need.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-26T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-26T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ3‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒsupportive‚ôĒMontana‚ôĒoffice manager‚ôĒ77‚ôĒWhen my cardiologist retired last year, I spent three weeks calling between his office and my new doctor trying to get my test results transferred‚ÄĒthey kept saying the files were "incompatible" or lost in the system. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0081‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒKevin Mitchell‚ôĒbeHuman Corp.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒStandardized AI transparency requirements and model disclosure are essential safeguards against vendor lock-in and enable clinicians to evaluate tools before adoption, rather than bureaucratic burdens that should be eliminated.‚ôĒTwo years ago, beHuman Corp switched EHR systems and the experience was brutal. The certification requirements were so locked down we couldn't customize workflows to match how our providers actually work. Switching vendors cost us $80,000 in transition fees that should have gone toward hiring clinical staff. When our current vendor raised renewal costs, we felt trapped because moving to something better wasn't realistic without another financial hit.‚Źé‚ŹéI understand the impulse to strip away certification burden, but removing transparency requirements for AI tools would push us right back into vendor lock-in. Model cards aren't bureaucratic overhead. They're the practical mechanism that lets us actually evaluate whether a tool works in our clinic before we're locked into a contract. Research from the FDA shows clinician trust in AI adoption directly correlates with understanding how the model was trained and what its limitations are. Without standardized disclosure requirements, we'd have no way to compare systems or hold vendors accountable when something fails. Certification for AI transparency protects providers like us from repeating expensive mistakes. That's not red tape. That's survival.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-22T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-22T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒmedium‚ôĒfrustrated‚ôĒMaryland‚ôĒpractice manager‚ôĒ49‚ôĒMy clinic switched EHR systems two years ago and it nearly killed us‚ÄĒthe certification requirements were so rigid that we couldn't customize anything to fit how our providers actually work, and switching vendors cost us $80,000 in transition fees that could've gone to hiring staff. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0082‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒBetty Hill‚ôĒDental Standards Institute‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Dental Standards Institute opposes removing the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures, arguing that such transparency is essential for patient safety, clinical accountabili...‚ôĒ**COMMENT ON PROPOSED MODIFICATIONS TO HEALTH DATA, TECHNOLOGY, AND INTEROPERABILITY CERTIFICATION CRITERIA**‚Źé‚Źé**Submitted by: Betty Hill, Campaign Director**‚Źé**Dental Standards Institute**‚Źé**Worcester, Massachusetts**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Dental Standards Institute appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed modifications to the certification criteria for Health IT developers under the Office of the National Coordinator's regulatory framework. Our organization represents dental practices, dental schools, and patient advocacy groups across New England, and we work directly with the communities most affected by how health information systems are designed, deployed, and governed.‚Źé‚ŹéI need to be direct about what troubles us in the current proposal. The suggested removal of the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion, specifically the requirement for AI model cards and transparency disclosures, represents a significant step backward for patient safety and clinical accountability. This isn't abstract regulatory concern. Last spring, I was helping coordinate a voter registration drive at a community health clinic in Worcester, and I watched a woman break down in tears in the waiting room. She'd delayed getting her daughter's cavity filled for months because she was terrified that any dental record would be flagged in some database and used against her immigration status. That moment crystallized something I'd been thinking about in a more theoretical way: the systems we build around health data don't just collect information. They shape whether vulnerable people feel safe seeking care.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI requirements under 45 CFR 170.315(b)(6) mandate that AI-assisted clinical decision support tools include model cards disclosing intended use, target population characteristics, known performance limitations, training data sources, and external validation evidence. These aren't bureaucratic checkboxes. They're the difference between a tool that a dentist can understand and trust, and a black box that produces recommendations no one can explain or defend. When a system flags a patient for potential periodontal disease or suggests treatment thresholds that differ significantly from clinical guidelines, the dentist needs to know why. Not in some abstract sense, but in a way that allows them to exercise clinical judgment and explain their decisions to the patient.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CURRENT FRAMEWORK AND WHY IT MATTERS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe existing certification requirements emerged from lessons learned across healthcare over the past fifteen years. The FDA's 2021 Action Plan on AI and Machine Learning in Software as a Medical Device recognized that transparency requirements are essential for safe clinical deployment. When the ONC added the DSI criterion in the 2015 edition of the certification standards and refined it through the 2023 Information Blocking Rule implementation, the intent was clear: developers should demonstrate that their tools have been tested in real-world conditions and that clinicians have enough information to make informed decisions about whether to use them.‚Źé‚ŹéI understand the deregulatory impulse. We hear from developers who say the model card requirement adds cost and slows time-to-market. But here's what we see from the clinical side: practices already struggle with alert fatigue and unexplainable recommendations from existing systems. A 2023 study in the Journal of Dental Education found that nearly 60 percent of dentists using AI-assisted diagnostic tools reported uncertainty about whether the system had been validated for their specific patient populations. That's not a minor implementation issue. That's a clinical safety problem.‚Źé‚ŹéIn dental practice, we deal with populations that are disproportionately affected by health information surveillance concerns. We serve immigrant communities, undocumented populations, and families who've experienced discrimination in healthcare settings. When a dental clinic implements an AI system for caries detection or treatment planning, and that system has been trained primarily on data from affluent, predominantly white patient populations, the system may perform worse for patients with different dental anatomy, different disease prevalence patterns, or different socioeconomic risk factors. Without transparency about training data and validation across subpopulations, we have no way to know whether we're perpetuating existing disparities or exacerbating them.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC CONCERNS WITH REMOVING THE TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENT**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal suggests that market forces and clinical judgment are sufficient safeguards. I respect that theory, but it doesn't match what we actually see in practice. Here are the problems:‚Źé‚Źé1. Developers have limited incentive to disclose limitations if transparency isn't required. A vendor who knows their AI system performs poorly on patients over 65, or on patients with certain genetic markers, can simply not mention that limitation. The dentist using the tool may never know unless they independently validate it in their own practice, which most don't have the resources to do.‚Źé‚Źé2. Liability frameworks lag behind technology. If a dentist relies on an AI recommendation that leads to a missed diagnosis or unnecessary treatment, who is responsible? Without transparency about what the system was designed to do and what it was validated for, that question becomes impossible to answer in a way that protects patients.‚Źé‚Źé3. Patients themselves become unable to give truly informed consent. Under HIPAA and basic principles of informed decision-making, patients should understand what tools are being used to make treatment recommendations about their care. If the system is a black box, even to the clinician, the patient certainly can't make an informed choice.‚Źé‚ŹéThe removal of these requirements would also undermine the intent of the Information Blocking Rule. Under 45 CFR 171.102, developers are required to not block access to health information, but that obligation only makes sense if we have transparency about how information is being used in decision support tools. A developer could theoretically provide access to all requested data while deploying a decision support system that subtly steers clinicians toward certain treatment paths based on opaque algorithmic reasoning.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE'RE ASKING FOR**‚Źé‚ŹéOur position is straightforward: retain the DSI certification criterion as currently structured. The model card requirement should remain mandatory for any Health IT developer seeking certification for clinical decision support tools. This doesn't require developers to open-source their algorithms or disclose proprietary training methodologies. It requires them to disclose:‚Źé‚Źé- The intended clinical use case and the specific clinical question the tool is designed to address‚Źé- The characteristics of the patient population used to develop and validate the tool‚Źé- Known performance limitations, including any subgroups where performance differs significantly‚Źé- The source and composition of training data‚Źé- Evidence of external validation beyond the developer's internal testing‚Źé- A description of how the tool integrates with clinical workflow and what information the clinician sees when making decisions‚Źé‚ŹéThese disclosures should be publicly available and reviewed as part of the certification process. This is consistent with how other regulated medical devices work. The FDA doesn't require surgical robot manufacturers to disclose their exact algorithms, but they do require disclosure of intended use, performance data, and known limitations. Health IT should operate under a similar transparency standard.‚Źé‚ŹéWe also recommend that ONC establish a process for periodic re-certification or validation updates as AI systems are deployed in real-world settings. A system validated on one dataset may perform differently when used in a different clinical context. Requiring developers to gather real-world performance data and report back on any significant divergence from validation studies would catch problems before they affect patient care at scale.‚Źé‚Źé**ADDRESSING THE INNOVATION CONCERN**‚Źé‚ŹéI know the counterargument. Transparency requirements slow innovation. They add cost. They create barriers to entry for smaller developers. These concerns deserve serious consideration, not dismissal.‚Źé‚ŹéBut innovation without accountability isn't innovation. It's experimentation on patients. And frankly, the transparency requirement isn't what slows most developers. What slows them is actually building tools that work reliably across diverse patient populations. The model card requirement just makes that work visible. A developer who's genuinely confident in their tool shouldn't fear transparency about how it was built and validated.‚Źé‚ŹéFor smaller developers concerned about cost, the ONC could consider a tiered approach where developers of lower-risk decision support tools (flagging information for clinician review, rather than directly recommending treatment) face less stringent transparency requirements than developers of higher-risk tools (directly suggesting diagnoses or treatment plans). But the baseline standard should remain: clinicians and patients deserve to know what they're using and why.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Dental Standards Institute urges the Office of the National Coordinator to preserve the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion and its model card requirements. This is not an argument against innovation or against reducing unnecessary regulatory burden. It's an argument for the kind of transparency that makes innovation trustworthy.‚Źé‚ŹéWe've spent the last decade learning that health information systems designed without attention to equity and transparency can amplify existing disparities. The patients I work with in Worcester, the immigrant families relying on community health clinics, the people who delay necessary care because they fear what will happen to their information, those are the populations most vulnerable to harm from opaque AI systems. The model card requirement exists to protect them and to protect clinicians who are trying to do right by their patients.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving this requirement in the name of deregulation would be trading real, measurable protections for the promise of faster innovation. That's not a trade we should make.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-23T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-23T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ4‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒurgent‚ôĒMassachusetts‚ôĒcampaign director‚ôĒ51‚ôĒLast spring, I was helping coordinate a voter registration drive at a community health clinic in Worcester, and I watched a woman break down in tears in the waiting room‚ÄĒshe'd delayed getting her daughter's cavity filled for months because she was terrified that any dental record would be flagged in some database and used against her immigration status. That's when I realized our current system, for all its flaws, at least provides some opacity that vulnerable families depend on, and any loosening of those guardrails without ironclad protections could turn a routine dental visit into a surveillance trap for people who already have everything to lose.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1488
