CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0084‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒCharles Hill‚ôĒNational Alliance for Care at Home‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe National Alliance for Care at Home opposes removing Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements for AI tools in home care, arguing that transparency mandates requiring vendors to disclose training data, performance limitati...‚ôĒJune 20, 2025‚Źé‚ŹéNational Alliance for Care at Home‚ŹéBurlington, Vermont‚Źé‚ŹéRe: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity; RFI Comment‚Źé‚ŹéThe National Alliance for Care at Home appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory framework for health information technology certification and standards. Our organization represents over 350 home- and community-based care providers across Vermont, New Hampshire, and upstate New York, serving approximately 18,000 older adults and individuals with disabilities. We employ more than 4,200 direct care workers and coordinate care delivery across residential settings, assisted living facilities, and in-home care environments where families like those we serve depend on accurate information exchange to make critical decisions about their loved ones' health and safety.‚Źé‚ŹéThe deregulatory proposal raises significant questions about how to balance innovation incentives with the accountability mechanisms that protect vulnerable populations. We want to be clear: we are not opposed to regulatory modernization, and we understand the genuine burden that excessive certification requirements place on smaller health IT developers. The home care sector has been starved of technology investment for years precisely because the compliance costs were prohibitive for companies focused on our market. We have seen promising innovations fail to reach our providers because they could not justify the certification expense. So when we raise concerns about removing certain oversight requirements, we do so from a position of genuine support for bringing more tools into home care, not from reflexive opposition to change.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat we cannot support is removing the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion that currently requires AI model cards, transparency disclosures about intended use, target population, known risks, and training data characteristics. Our concern here is not abstract. It is rooted in what happened to my mother two years ago, and what we see happening to our members' families every month.‚Źé‚Źé**THE SPECIFIC PROBLEM: WHAT TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS ACTUALLY PROTECT**‚Źé‚ŹéWhen my mother transitioned to assisted living, her new care team was using an AI-assisted scheduling system to coordinate medication administration across a 120-resident facility. The system had been marketed as improving medication safety by flagging potential drug interactions. What nobody disclosed was that the model had been trained primarily on data from a much younger population (average age 52) and had never been externally validated in a population over 75. When my mother was prescribed a new medication for atrial fibrillation, the system missed a critical interaction because the interaction pattern was statistically rare in the training cohort and the model had not been designed to account for age-related pharmacokinetic changes. The facility's nurse relied on the system's recommendation. My mother ended up in the hospital.‚Źé‚ŹéThis was not a case of bad faith. The company that built the system was not trying to harm anyone. They had simply never been required to disclose what population they trained on, what they knew about performance limitations, or what risks were known. The facility had no way to know they should not rely on the system for that particular decision. There was no model card. There was no transparency disclosure. There was no external validation data available to anyone.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criterion exists because situations like this one happen. It exists because the populations we serve in home and community care are not the young, relatively healthy populations that most commercial health IT systems are designed around. Older adults take more medications. They have more complex disease interactions. They have different patterns of health care utilization. They are more likely to experience cognitive decline that affects their ability to communicate with automated systems. They are more likely to be on Medicare Advantage plans with network restrictions that an algorithm trained on commercial insurance populations has never encountered.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen we say we need DSI certification to remain in place, we are saying: require developers to tell us what they know about how their system actually works, who it works for, and who it does not work for. That is not burden for its own sake. That is the baseline information that allows care teams to make informed decisions about whether to use a tool, how to use it, and when not to trust it.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT THE DATA SHOWS ABOUT AI DEPLOYMENT IN CARE SETTINGS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe home care sector has been an early adopter of AI-assisted tools, in part because we are desperate for solutions to workflow problems that have become untenable. We have implemented AI scheduling systems in 47 of our member organizations. We have deployed clinical decision support tools in 23 facilities. We have tested predictive models for fall risk, hospital readmission, and medication adherence. We have learned some hard lessons.‚Źé‚ŹéIn 2023, we conducted a survey of 156 home care and assisted living providers who had implemented some form of AI-assisted clinical decision support. We asked them: "Did the vendor provide you with a model card or formal documentation of what population the model was trained on?" Sixty-three percent said no. We asked: "Did the vendor provide you with information about known performance limitations or risks?" Forty-eight percent said no. We asked: "Did you have access to external validation data before implementation?" Only nineteen providers said yes. Most of those 19 had conducted their own validation because the vendor had not provided it.‚Źé‚ŹéWe also asked a follow-up question: "Has this lack of information ever affected your clinical decision-making?" Forty-one providers reported instances where they had over-relied on a system recommendation because they did not know about performance limitations. Twenty-eight reported that they had later discovered the system performed poorly for a specific subpopulation in their facility (often older adults, often individuals with multiple comorbidities, often racial or ethnic minorities).‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not a theoretical problem. This is what happens when vendors are not required to disclose what they know.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THIS MATTERS FOR EQUITY AND VULNERABLE POPULATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe populations we serve are disproportionately affected by health inequities. Sixty-eight percent of our clients are on Medicaid. Forty-two percent are people of color. Thirty-five percent live alone with no family caregiver available. These are the populations least likely to have the knowledge, resources, or advocacy power to catch problems when an AI system makes a bad recommendation.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen an algorithm trained on a population that is younger, wealthier, and whiter is deployed in a facility where most residents are older, poorer, and more racially diverse, the risk of disparate performance is not speculative. It is documented in the literature. The study by Obermeyer et al. in Science (2019) showing that a widely used health risk algorithm systematically underestimated risk for Black patients because it was trained on cost as a proxy for health need. The analysis by Gianfrancesco et al. in Nature Medicine (2018) showing that mental health AI tools had significantly higher false-positive rates for African Americans. The work by Buolamwini and Gebru in the Proceedings of Machine Learning Research (2018) documenting racial and gender bias in commercial face recognition systems.‚Źé‚ŹéThese are not edge cases. They are the documented, peer-reviewed reality of how AI systems perform when they are deployed without transparency about their training data and validation populations. And home care is exactly the setting where these biases matter most, because our clients have the least ability to advocate for themselves if a system is systematically making bad recommendations for their demographic group.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen we ask for DSI certification to remain in place, we are asking for the minimum protection: require vendors to tell us what populations they trained on, what we should and should not expect the system to do, and what external validation shows about performance across different demographic groups. That information then allows care teams to use the system responsibly, to monitor for disparate performance, and to intervene when something is not working.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CERTIFICATION REQUIREMENT ACTUALLY SOLVES A REAL COORDINATION PROBLEM**‚Źé‚ŹéThere is a secondary reason why DSI certification matters in the home care context, and it relates to something the deregulatory proposal does not seem to account for: most home care providers do not have sophisticated health IT infrastructure or data science expertise.‚Źé‚ŹéOur member organizations range from small family-owned operations with 12 employees to larger networks with 500 employees. The median organization has 67 employees. That median organization does not have a data science team. It does not have a chief information officer. It has one person who manages IT, who also manages the electronic health record, who also handles technical support. That person is not equipped to conduct an independent validation of a machine learning model. They cannot read a model card they do not receive. They cannot evaluate bias in a system they have never seen documented.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat DSI certification does is create a credible signal. When a vendor says "this system is DSI certified," it means an independent body has verified that the vendor has provided model documentation, disclosed training data characteristics, identified known risks, and provided external validation. It means a care team can say: "I do not have the expertise to evaluate this myself, but an independent reviewer has checked that the vendor is being transparent about what they know."‚Źé‚ŹéRemove that certification requirement, and you remove that signal. You are left with a care team that needs to make a deployment decision with no way to verify vendor claims. The vendor can say "we validated this on a representative population" and there is no way for a facility administrator to know if that is true, if "representative" means anything specific, or if the validation was done independently.‚Źé‚ŹéIn a market with sophisticated institutional buyers (hospital systems, large health plans), maybe this is not a problem. Those organizations have data science teams. They can demand vendor documentation and evaluate it themselves. But in home care, you have smaller organizations making decisions about systems that will directly affect vulnerable older adults, with no infrastructure to independently verify vendor claims. In that context, DSI certification is not a burden on the vendor. It is a protection for the client.‚Źé‚Źé**ADDRESSING THE INNOVATION ARGUMENT**‚Źé‚ŹéWe understand the argument for removing certification requirements. It takes time and money to get a system certified. Smaller developers face higher costs as a proportion of revenue. This creates a barrier to entry that may prevent innovation in underserved markets like home care. We have seen this firsthand. A promising scheduling optimization tool for home care agencies did not reach the market because the developer could not justify the certification cost. That is a real loss.‚Źé‚ŹéBut the solution to that problem is not to remove certification. It is to reform it in ways that reduce burden without eliminating accountability. Consider what the existing DSI criterion actually requires (as specified in 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1)):‚Źé‚ŹéThe system must provide, in a manner that is understandable to human reviewers, documentation that includes the intended use of the decision support intervention, the intended user, the clinical context in which the decision support intervention is appropriate, the training data characteristics, the performance metrics, and the known limitations and risks.‚Źé‚ŹéThat documentation requirement is not onerous. It is not asking the vendor to conduct a year-long validation study. It is asking them to document what they already know about their own system: what it was designed to do, who it was designed for, what data it was trained on, how well it works, and what risks they are aware of. A competent data scientist can produce this documentation in a few days.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat is onerous is the certification process itself, not the documentation requirement. The certification process takes months and involves third-party testing. That is where the cost comes in. If the goal is to reduce burden without eliminating accountability, the solution is not to remove the documentation requirement. It is to streamline the certification process. Allow vendors to self-attest that they have provided the required documentation. Allow third-party certification on a voluntary basis for vendors who want to signal higher confidence. Create a tiered system where simple systems (decision trees, rule-based tools) have lighter certification requirements than complex systems (deep learning models).‚Źé‚ŹéThe deregulatory proposal seems to assume that the choice is binary: either keep the current DSI certification or remove it entirely. That is not actually the case. There are ways to reduce burden while maintaining transparency.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE ARE HEARING FROM OUR MEMBER ORGANIZATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéWe surveyed our member organizations about their views on this proposal. The survey was not scientific, and the sample is not representative of all home care providers nationally. But the responses are instructive.‚Źé‚ŹéWe asked: "Should certification requirements for AI-assisted clinical decision support tools be removed?" Seventy-three percent said no. We asked: "Should vendors be required to provide documentation about what population their model was trained on?" Ninety-one percent said yes. We asked: "Should vendors be required to disclose known limitations and risks?" Ninety-four percent said yes.‚Źé‚ŹéWe also asked an open-ended question: "What would make it easier for you to responsibly deploy AI tools in your organization?" The most common response (mentioned by 67 of 156 respondents) was: "We need clear documentation from vendors about what the system is designed to do and what it is not designed to do. We need to know if the system has been validated in a population like ours."‚Źé‚ŹéThat is not asking for the world. That is asking vendors to be transparent about what they know.‚Źé‚Źé**THE INFORMATION BLOCKING REGULATION INTERSECTION**‚Źé‚ŹéThere is a secondary concern we want to raise, because the deregulatory proposal mentions loosening information blocking regulations and it intersects with AI deployment in ways that are not fully addressed.‚Źé‚ŹéUnder 45 CFR 171.100 et seq., vendors are generally prohibited from engaging in information blocking, which includes withholding health information in a manner that interferes with, prevents, or materially discourages access to that information by another person. The regulation has exceptions, including an exception for protecting privacy and security.‚Źé‚ŹéIn practice, vendors have sometimes cited information blocking exceptions to refuse to share training data information with providers. The argument goes: "We cannot disclose what population we trained on because that information could reveal proprietary insights about our model." That argument is technically defensible under information blocking exceptions. But it creates a perverse incentive where vendors can hide information about their systems by claiming it is proprietary.‚Źé‚ŹéIf DSI certification requirements are removed, and if information blocking exceptions remain as broad as they currently are, there is nothing preventing vendors from refusing to disclose training data characteristics, performance limitations, or validation information. The vendor can say: "That information is proprietary. We are not required to disclose it." And a care team has no recourse.‚Źé‚ŹéWe are not asking for the deregulatory proposal to expand information blocking enforcement. We are asking that if certification requirements are being loosened, the agency should simultaneously tighten the information blocking exceptions to prevent vendors from hiding behind claims of proprietary protection when information is material to patient safety.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE ARE RECOMMENDING**‚Źé‚ŹéBased on our experience and the feedback from our member organizations, we recommend the following:‚Źé‚Źé1. Retain the DSI certification criterion requiring AI model documentation, but streamline the certification process to reduce cost and timeline. Allow vendors to self-attest compliance with documentation requirements. Reserve third-party certification for vendors seeking higher assurance signals.‚Źé‚Źé2. Clarify that the information blocking exceptions do not apply to vendor disclosures of training data characteristics, known performance limitations, and external validation information when those disclosures are requested by the provider or care team deploying the system. Vendors should not be able to hide material safety information behind proprietary claims.‚Źé‚Źé3. Create a home care specific guidance document that clarifies what DSI compliance looks like for different types of AI tools (scheduling systems, clinical decision support, predictive analytics). Many smaller vendors do not understand what the requirement actually asks for, and providing clearer guidance would reduce compliance burden.‚Źé‚Źé4. Establish a process for care teams to report instances where an AI system performed poorly for specific populations, and require vendors to respond to those reports and update their documentation if warranted. This creates an ongoing feedback mechanism that improves system performance without requiring extensive upfront validation.‚Źé‚Źé5. If the agency does move forward with removing DSI certification, establish a voluntary certification program operated by the home care industry itself (perhaps through organizations like ours) that allows vendors to signal compliance with transparency standards. This preserves the signaling function even if certification is no longer mandatory.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéWe support health IT innovation. We want more tools available in home care. We want to see smaller developers succeed in this market. We also want to make sure that when tools are deployed, care teams know what they are using, why it works, what its limitations are, and who it is designed for. Those are not contradictory goals. They are both essential.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification requirement is not perfect. It could be streamlined. The certification process could be faster and cheaper. But the transparency that it requires is not a burden. It is a baseline protection for populations that cannot protect themselves. My mother could not read the model card on the scheduling system that made a recommendation about her medication. She could not evaluate whether the system had been tested in people her age. She relied on a care team to do that for her, and the care team had no information to work with.‚Źé‚ŹéThat should not happen again. Not in home care. Not for the 2.8 million older adults who receive care from our members every year. Retain the DSI requirement. Streamline the process. But do not remove the transparency.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-02-01T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-01T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ9‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒresigned‚ôĒVermont‚ôĒexecutive director‚ôĒ63‚ôĒWhen my mother was moved to assisted living two years ago, I spent weeks filing requests just to understand which provider networks actually served her Medicare Advantage plan‚ÄĒinformation that should have been transparent from the start. The fragmentation I witnessed firsthand is precisely what concerns me about loosening oversight: we'll gain speed at the cost of visibility, and it's always the families least equipped to navigate bureaucracy who suffer the consequences.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2929
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0085‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒKimberly Moore‚ôĒSouth Carolina Primary Health Care Association‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe South Carolina Primary Health Care Association opposes removing Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements and AI transparency standards, arguing that model cards, external validation, and disclosure of training data chara...‚ôĒRe: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity; Request for Information (RFI)‚Źé‚ŹéSouth Carolina Primary Health Care Association submits these comments regarding the proposed deregulatory actions affecting Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria and AI transparency requirements in health information technology. Our organization represents 200 primary health care centers across South Carolina and serves as a practical testing ground for the tensions that arise when regulatory frameworks attempt to balance innovation with patient safety.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CASE FOR RETAINING DSI CERTIFICATION AND MODEL CARD REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéLast month, one of our member clinics in rural Beaufort County encountered a situation that crystallizes why I believe the current DSI certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures remains necessary. A diabetic patient came to the clinic with a new prescription from another provider, and the clinic's AI-assisted decision support system flagged a potential interaction with the patient's existing medications. The system worked because the developers had documented their training data characteristics, disclosed the patient populations for which the model was validated, and listed known limitations. The prescriber was able to verify whether the interaction warning applied to this specific patient given her age, kidney function, and other conditions the model had explicitly been trained on. Without those transparency disclosures, the clinic staff would have had to make a judgment call with less information. That feels like a reasonable standard to maintain.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat gets me is that the same week, we discovered something else at another site in our network. A woman's mental health history had been shared with her employer's occupational health vendor when consolidated EHR records were accessed without explicit patient consent protocols. She found out when she was denied a promotion. The vendor had access to information about her depression diagnosis and antidepressant use. I've spent fifteen years in health policy, and I still don't have a clear answer for how we balance the need for fluid data access with the need to keep people safe from discrimination. But I know that removing transparency requirements for AI systems won't solve that problem. It will make it worse.‚Źé‚ŹéThe tension here is real, and I think it's important to name it directly. We need interoperability. We need clinical decision support that works across fragmented systems. We need innovation in health IT. But we also need to know what's happening when algorithms are making recommendations that affect patient care. The current DSI certification criterion requiring AI model cards, documentation of intended use, target populations, known risks, and training data characteristics is not a burden that prevents innovation. It is a floor that says developers must understand their own systems well enough to explain them.‚Źé‚Źé**PATIENT SAFETY AND INFORMED CLINICAL DECISION-MAKING**‚Źé‚ŹéThe certification requirement in 45 CFR 170.315(b)(6) has been in place since 2015, and in that time, the health IT industry has not reported that model cards are impossible to produce. What has happened is that the industry has gotten better at producing them. Developers now expect to document their models. This is not a heavy lift for companies that are serious about building trustworthy systems. It is, however, a meaningful filter for systems that are not.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider the specifics of what the current criterion requires. Developers must disclose the intended use of the model, the target population it was trained on, known risks and limitations, and characteristics of the training data. This is not asking for proprietary algorithms or trade secrets. It is asking developers to be transparent about what their model is designed to do and for whom. A clinical decision support system designed to assist in sepsis management should disclose whether it was trained primarily on ICU populations or whether it included primary care settings. If it was trained on data from a specific region or health system, that should be stated. If there are known limitations in performance for certain age groups or racial groups, those should be disclosed.‚Źé‚ŹéI think about this from the perspective of a clinician using the system. If I'm making a treatment decision for a 78-year-old patient with multiple comorbidities, I need to know whether the AI model was trained on a population similar to my patient. I need to know what the developers know about how the system performs in that context. This is not regulation for regulation's sake. This is the minimum information necessary for informed clinical decision-making.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Food and Drug Administration has recognized similar principles in its guidance on clinical decision support software (FDA, Clinical Decision Support Software, 2019). While FDA does not regulate all clinical decision support systems, the agency's framework distinguishes between systems that provide recommendations based on transparent algorithms and those that function as black boxes. The principle is sound: when a system is providing input into a clinical decision, transparency about how the system works is a patient safety issue.‚Źé‚Źé**EXTERNAL VALIDATION AND REAL-WORLD PERFORMANCE**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current criterion also requires external validation of AI models. This means that before a system is certified, developers must demonstrate that the model performs adequately when tested on data it was not trained on. This is a check against overfitting and a practical test of whether the system generalizes beyond its development environment.‚Źé‚ŹéWe have learned from the AI ethics literature over the past few years that models can perform very differently depending on the data they are tested on. A study by Obermeyer et al. in *Science* (2019) demonstrated that a widely used algorithm for allocating healthcare resources had racial bias because it was trained on healthcare spending as a proxy for healthcare need. Spending patterns reflect historical inequities in access, not actual need. The algorithm would have been used for years without anyone knowing about this bias if external validation had not been performed. That is what external validation catches. It is not a theoretical requirement. It is a practical necessity.‚Źé‚ŹéWe cannot reliably assess whether an AI system will work fairly across different populations without testing it on those populations. If a system is developed in an urban academic medical center with a patient population that is 60 percent white and 70 percent insured, we need to know how it performs in a rural clinic with a patient population that is 40 percent uninsured and 30 percent Hispanic. External validation on diverse populations is the way to find out.‚Źé‚Źé**ACCOUNTABILITY AND CLINICAL GOVERNANCE**‚Źé‚ŹéThere is another reason the DSI certification criterion matters, and it relates to accountability. When something goes wrong with an AI system in clinical practice, someone needs to be able to explain what happened. If a system recommends a treatment that causes harm, the clinician, the health system, and potentially the patient will want to understand why the system made that recommendation.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not hypothetical. At another member clinic, a clinical decision support system recommended a higher dose of a blood thinner for a patient than the clinician had intended. The clinician caught the error before administering the medication, but the incident raised questions. The clinic requested documentation from the vendor about how the system made dosing recommendations. The vendor provided limited information, claiming that the algorithm was proprietary. The clinic could not perform a full safety review. This is a problem. Clinicians need to be able to understand and verify the recommendations their tools are giving them, especially when safety is at stake.‚Źé‚ŹéThe accountability gap is not just a clinical problem. It is a legal problem. When a health system uses an AI system for clinical decision support and an adverse event occurs, the health system may face liability questions. If the system cannot be explained or validated, the risk profile changes significantly. The current certification criterion provides a foundation for accountability. It ensures that developers have documented their systems and that external parties can verify how those systems work.‚Źé‚Źé**THE RISK OF REMOVING TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be direct about what I think will happen if the DSI certification criterion is removed or significantly weakened. I think we will see an initial surge in new clinical decision support products. The barrier to entry will be lower. Small companies and startups will develop systems that they believe will help clinicians. Some of those systems will be good. But some will not be. And we will not know which is which until something goes wrong.‚Źé‚ŹéThe problem is not that bad actors will deliberately create harmful systems. Most developers care about building tools that work. The problem is that without external validation and transparency requirements, bad systems will look the same as good systems from the outside. A clinician will not be able to tell the difference. A health system will not be able to tell the difference. A patient will not be able to tell the difference.‚Źé‚ŹéWe have seen this pattern before in health IT. When EHR certification requirements were weakened in certain areas, the market responded with products that met the minimum technical requirements but did not necessarily serve clinicians or patients well. Some of those systems are still in use today, creating workarounds and inefficiencies that cost the healthcare system billions of dollars.‚Źé‚Źé**EQUITY AND VULNERABLE POPULATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe South Carolina Primary Health Care Association serves vulnerable populations. Many of our patients are low-income, uninsured, or underinsured. Many are in rural areas with limited access to specialists. Many are from racial and ethnic groups that have historically been underrepresented in medical research and in the datasets used to train AI systems.‚Źé‚ŹéFor these populations, the transparency requirements in the DSI certification criterion are not a burden. They are a protection. If a clinical decision support system is being used to make recommendations about treatment for our patients, we need to know that the system was tested on populations like ours. We need to know what the system's limitations are. We need to know whether the developers are aware of any disparities in how the system performs.‚Źé‚ŹéThe history of healthcare innovation is full of examples of new technologies that promised to help everyone but ended up benefiting some populations more than others. AI systems trained on biased datasets or tested only on narrow populations will likely follow the same pattern. The transparency requirements in the current criterion are one of the few mechanisms we have to catch these problems before they become embedded in clinical practice.‚Źé‚Źé**INTEROPERABILITY WITHOUT SACRIFICING TRANSPARENCY**‚Źé‚ŹéI understand the impulse behind the deregulatory proposal. There is a genuine need for faster innovation in health IT. There is a genuine need for better interoperability. There is a genuine need for smaller companies and startups to be able to compete with large vendors. These are all important goals.‚Źé‚ŹéBut I do not think achieving these goals requires removing transparency requirements for AI systems. The transparency requirements do not prevent interoperability. They do not prevent innovation. They add a step to the development process, but it is a step that good developers are already taking.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat the transparency requirements do is ensure that when a new system enters the market, it has been subjected to basic scrutiny. The system has been tested on external data. The developers have documented what the system is designed to do and for whom. The system has been examined by people other than the developers who built it. This is not a heavy lift. It is a reasonable floor.‚Źé‚Źé**ALTERNATIVES THAT PRESERVE PATIENT SAFETY**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the goal is to reduce burden on developers while maintaining patient safety, there are alternatives to removing the DSI certification criterion. One approach would be to streamline the external validation process. Instead of requiring developers to conduct their own external validation studies, the ONC could establish a process for shared external validation. Developers could submit their systems for validation by independent testing organizations. This would spread the cost and the burden across multiple developers and would likely be more efficient than having each developer validate independently.‚Źé‚ŹéAnother approach would be to differentiate between different types of clinical decision support systems. A system that is providing informational support to a clinician, such as flagging relevant drug interactions, might have lighter requirements than a system that is providing a specific recommendation about treatment. The current criterion could be modified to reflect this distinction without being eliminated entirely.‚Źé‚ŹéA third approach would be to allow developers to use real-world evidence from clinical practice as a form of validation, rather than requiring controlled validation studies. Once a system is in use, health systems could submit data about how the system is performing. This would provide ongoing validation and would allow the ONC to update the certification based on real-world evidence.‚Źé‚Źé**REGULATORY CERTAINTY AND MARKET CONFIDENCE**‚Źé‚ŹéOne benefit of maintaining the DSI certification criterion is regulatory certainty. If a system meets the current requirements, health systems and clinicians know what they are getting. They know the system has been tested. They know it has been reviewed by independent parties. They know the developers have documented their work. This creates confidence in the market.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the certification requirements are removed, there will be a period of uncertainty. Health systems will not know which systems have been tested and which have not. Clinicians will not know which systems are reliable and which are not. This uncertainty may actually slow innovation, as health systems become more cautious about adopting new systems.‚Źé‚Źé**ALIGNMENT WITH INTERNATIONAL STANDARDS**‚Źé‚ŹéIt is worth noting that other countries and regions are moving in the opposite direction. The European Union's proposed AI Act includes transparency requirements for high-risk AI systems, including those used in healthcare. The UK's approach to AI governance emphasizes transparency and explainability. Canada has published guidance on the use of AI in healthcare that emphasizes the need for external validation and documentation. If the United States removes these requirements, we risk falling behind international standards and potentially creating barriers to the adoption of US-developed systems in other markets.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe South Carolina Primary Health Care Association supports innovation in health IT. We support faster approval processes for new systems. We support reducing unnecessary regulatory burden. But we do not support removing requirements that ensure AI systems used in clinical practice are transparent, validated, and accountable.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures has been in place for a decade. It has not prevented innovation. It has not prevented the development of new clinical decision support systems. What it has done is ensure that when those systems are used in clinical practice, clinicians and patients have some assurance that the systems have been tested and that the developers understand how they work.‚Źé‚ŹéWe urge the ONC to retain this requirement. If modifications are needed to reduce burden or streamline the process, we are open to discussing those modifications. But the fundamental principle of transparency and external validation should be preserved. The stakes are too high and the populations we serve are too vulnerable to do otherwise.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-03T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-03T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ8‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒurgent‚ôĒNorth Dakota‚ôĒsenior policy analyst‚ôĒ45‚ôĒLast month, I reviewed a case file where a rural clinic in our network couldn't access fragmented patient records across three different EHR systems, and a diabetic patient nearly missed a critical drug interaction‚ÄĒbut I'm equally troubled by what happened when we tried to consolidate those same records without explicit patient consent protocols. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0086‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒMark Miller‚ôĒSouth Carolina Primary Health Care Association‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe South Carolina Primary Health Care Association opposes eliminating Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements for AI clinical decision support tools, arguing that mandatory model cards and transparency disclosures are esse...‚ôĒSouth Carolina Primary Health Care Association‚ŹéResearch and Clinical Affairs Division‚ŹéDenver, Colorado‚Źé‚ŹéRE: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity‚ŹéRIN 0955-AA05 / CMS-0057-IFC‚Źé‚ŹéThe South Carolina Primary Health Care Association (SCPHCA) appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed modifications to health information technology certification criteria and standards. Our organization represents 80+ federally qualified health centers and rural primary care clinics across South Carolina and the Delta region, serving approximately 340,000 patients annually, many of whom are uninsured or underinsured. I write from my perspective as research director, having spent the last eighteen months analyzing care patterns across our network, and I want to address the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements.‚Źé‚Źé**The Case for Maintaining DSI Model Card Requirements**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule would eliminate the requirement that AI-assisted clinical decision support tools provide model cards and transparency disclosures, including information on intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results (45 CFR 170.102(h), as proposed). I understand the deregulatory impulse. Developers have told us that these requirements add months to deployment timelines and create documentation burdens. I get that. But our experience in rural health suggests the opposite approach is actually safer for both patients and providers.‚Źé‚ŹéLast year, our network adopted a clinical decision support system to help identify early-stage kidney disease in our patient population. The vendor provided a basic overview of the tool's general functionality. What we didn't receive until we requested it independently was documentation showing that the model had been trained predominantly on data from urban academic medical centers with relatively homogeneous patient demographics. Our Delta region patient population is 65% African American, with different disease prevalence patterns and different medication use profiles than the training data. When we ran preliminary validation against our own patients, we found the tool's positive predictive value dropped by 14 percentage points compared to the vendor's published performance metrics. Had we deployed this system without that scrutiny, we would have had delayed diagnoses for patients who could least afford them. This wasn't a failure of the tool itself. It was a failure of transparency.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criteria requiring model cards and external validation data aren't bureaucratic obstacles. They're safety infrastructure. Clinicians in rural and underserved settings often don't have the technical expertise or time to independently reverse-engineer how an AI system works or whether it's been validated in populations similar to theirs. We rely on documentation. We rely on knowing what the training data looked like. We rely on understanding where the tool might fail. When that information is absent, we either spend months conducting our own validation (which smaller clinics can't afford to do) or we deploy systems blind.‚Źé‚Źé**What Removal Would Mean for Patient Safety and Equity**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed elimination of these requirements would create a two-tier system. Large health systems with data science teams will continue to validate tools internally, at considerable expense. Rural clinics and small primary care networks will make deployment decisions based on marketing claims and vendor assurances. That's not deregulation in service of innovation. That's regulatory capture dressed up as market freedom.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm aware of the concern that transparency requirements slow adoption of beneficial tools. That's real. But the solution isn't to eliminate the requirements. It's to streamline the process. The Trusted Exchange Framework and Common Agreement (TEFCA) implementation (42 CFR Part 171) has shown that standardized documentation formats can actually accelerate deployment by reducing negotiation and custom documentation work. The same principle applies here.‚Źé‚ŹéWe recommend retaining the DSI certification criteria requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures. The clinical decision-making that happens in our clinics‚ÄĒthe diagnosis a patient receives, the treatment they're offered‚ÄĒdepends on information quality. Removing the requirement that AI systems disclose how they were built and where they might fail doesn't unleash innovation. It unleashes liability and undermines patient safety.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-26T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-26T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒconcerned‚ôĒColorado‚ôĒresearch director‚ôĒ58‚ôĒLast year, I reviewed preliminary data from our rural clinics showing that uninsured patients in the Delta region were receiving significantly delayed diagnoses compared to their insured counterparts‚ÄĒa disparity we might have caught months earlier with more systematic access to comparable datasets across providers. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0087‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒMichael Jones‚ôĒAd Hoc LLC‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA specialty cardiology practice opposes removing Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements, arguing that model cards and transparency disclosures are essential for clinicians to safely evaluate whether AI-assisted diagnostic ...‚ôĒRe: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity‚Źé‚ŹéAd Hoc LLC is a three-location cardiology practice based in Massachusetts with 48 staff members and approximately 12,000 active patients. I've served as practice manager for the past six years and have direct experience with the operational and financial realities of health IT transitions in a specialty care setting. Our comment addresses the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements, specifically the model card and transparency disclosure criteria, because we believe this particular deregulation would create genuine patient safety risks that outweigh any vendor burden reduction.‚Źé‚Źé**THE REAL COST OF OUR EHR TRANSITION ATTEMPT**‚Źé‚ŹéLast spring, we spent eighteen months and nearly $40,000 attempting to migrate from our incumbent vendor to a competitor whose clinical workflows actually matched cardiology subspecialty practice. We were driven by legitimate frustration: our existing EHR forced us into generic workflows that didn't align with how we actually practice. The new vendor promised customization and better decision support integration. What we encountered instead were certification gaps that made data exchange technically possible but financially ruinous for our size. We ultimately abandoned the transition and renegotiated with our original vendor from a weakened negotiating position, incurring an additional $15,000 in unexpected licensing fees. The point here is that we understand the appeal of reducing vendor burden. But we also understand what happens when clinical decision support tools enter practice without transparent documentation of their training data, intended use populations, known limitations, and external validation status.‚Źé‚ŹéDuring our transition evaluation process, we discovered that neither vendor could clearly articulate how their AI-assisted diagnostic support tools were validated, what populations they were trained on, or what failure modes we might encounter in our patient population. One vendor's cardiology-specific recommendation engine had been trained predominantly on data from academic medical centers with different patient demographics and disease prevalence than our community-based practice serves. We had no way to assess whether that mismatch mattered clinically. Without model cards and transparency documentation, we were being asked to trust a black box. Removing the DSI certification criterion would mean vendors have no obligation to provide that documentation at all.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THE CURRENT CRITERION MATTERS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criterion, as currently codified in 45 CFR 170.315(a)(4), requires vendors to disclose model cards containing information about intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. This isn't regulatory theater. It's the minimum documentation necessary for clinicians to make informed decisions about whether to accept, override, or ignore algorithmic recommendations. The FDA's guidance on clinical decision support (FDA 2019) and the NIST AI Risk Management Framework (NIST AI 600-1, 2023) both emphasize the importance of transparency documentation for tools that influence clinical judgment. Removing this requirement doesn't eliminate the risk that AI decision support tools will fail in unanticipated ways. It simply removes visibility into that risk.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider what happens in practice. A cardiologist reviewing a recommendation from a decision support tool needs to know: Was this algorithm trained on similar patients to mine? What was the validation sample size? Were there subgroup performance differences by age, sex, or comorbidity? What are the known false negative rates in different clinical contexts? The current criterion compels vendors to answer those questions. Without it, vendors have economic incentive to remain silent, and we have no way to know whether a tool is safe for our specific patient population.‚Źé‚Źé**OUR SPECIFIC RECOMMENDATION**‚Źé‚ŹéWe urge the Office of the Secretary and ONC to retain the current DSI certification criterion, including the requirement for model cards and transparency disclosures. This is one area where deregulation creates genuine clinical harm without corresponding benefit. Vendors do not need to remove this requirement to innovate. They need to innovate in ways that are clinically transparent.‚Źé‚ŹéIf there is concern that the current documentation requirements are overly prescriptive or create unnecessary technical burden, we would support a targeted revision that:‚Źé‚Źé- Clarifies which elements of model card documentation are mandatory versus optional‚Źé- Allows vendors flexibility in documentation format (provided all required information is present and accessible)‚Źé- Establishes a reasonable timeline for external validation data (e.g., within 18 months of market release for tools making clinical claims)‚Źé- Permits aggregated or de-identified training data disclosure where proprietary concerns exist‚Źé‚ŹéWhat we cannot support is the wholesale removal of vendor transparency obligations. We've spent $40,000 and eighteen months learning that vendor promises about clinical utility don't substitute for documented evidence. Our patients deserve better.‚Źé‚ŹéAd Hoc LLC‚Źé‚ŹéMichael Jones, Practice Manager‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-28T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-28T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒurgent‚ôĒMassachusetts‚ôĒpractice manager‚ôĒ48‚ôĒLast spring, our three-location practice spent eighteen months and nearly $40,000 trying to swap from our incumbent EHR vendor to a competitor whose workflow actually matched our cardiology subspecialty‚ÄĒonly to hit a wall of interoperability requirements that made the transition technically feasible but financially ruinous for a practice our size. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0088‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒRobert Wilson‚ôĒNational Alliance for Care at Home‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe National Alliance for Care at Home opposes eliminating Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements, arguing that mandatory transparency disclosures about clinical decision support tools are essential to protect vulnerable h...‚ôĒ**NATIONAL ALLIANCE FOR CARE AT HOME**‚Źé**COMMENT ON PROPOSED ASTP/ONC DEREGULATORY ACTIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe National Alliance for Care at Home appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed revisions to health IT certification criteria and information blocking regulations. Our organization represents over 8,000 home care agencies, hospice providers, and care coordination networks serving seniors and disabled individuals across all 50 states. We have followed the evolution of certification requirements since the HITECH Act of 2009 and the subsequent adoption of the 21st Century Cures Act provisions. We offer this comment with genuine respect for the Office of the National Coordinator's efforts to reduce unnecessary regulatory burden, while raising specific concerns about removing Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria that we believe protect vulnerable populations from real harms.‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be direct about what prompted us to comment. Three years ago, our member agencies began reporting incidents where clinical decision support tools recommended by hospital discharge planners were producing care recommendations inconsistent with a patient's documented preferences or actual home environment. One case involved a 78-year-old woman with advanced COPD for whom an AI-assisted tool flagged "high readmission risk" based on prior hospitalizations, without accounting for her documented preference to remain at home and her family's ability to provide daily monitoring. The tool's training data, it turned out, came primarily from urban hospital populations and had never been validated on home-based care trajectories. When the tool's logic was finally disclosed to us under the current certification transparency requirements (found at 45 CFR 170.405(b)), we discovered the known limitation in target population applicability had never been communicated to the discharging physician. This is not a hypothetical concern. Home care patients are older, more medically complex, and often live in rural settings that differ substantially from the hospital-derived datasets that train many clinical support tools. Without the current DSI certification criterion requiring model cards, transparency disclosures of intended use, target population specifications, and external validation results, we cannot reliably identify when tools are being applied outside their validated scope.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule would eliminate the DSI certification criterion entirely, replacing mandatory disclosures with voluntary industry reporting to the FDA's Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) database. I understand the deregulatory rationale. Certification is costly. It slows time to market. But I want to explain why this particular removal troubles us. Home care operates differently than hospital settings in ways that affect how decision support tools perform. Hospital systems have IT infrastructure, trained personnel to interpret tool outputs, and the ability to override recommendations in real time. Home care agencies often have fewer resources. A visiting nurse in rural Oklahoma sees a patient for two hours per week. That nurse relies on decision support tools to flag safety issues between visits. When a tool's training data excluded home care populations, or when its performance on certain demographic subgroups was never tested, the nurse has no way of knowing this without model transparency. The current certification requirement in 45 CFR 170.405(b) mandates disclosure of training data characteristics and external validation results. Moving to purely voluntary reporting means these disclosures become optional, market-dependent, and inaccessible through a centralized certification process that regulators and advocates can audit.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Federal Register notice references reducing burden on developers, and we take that seriously (88 Fed. Reg. 55274, August 16, 2023). But we want to propose an alternative that preserves accountability without eliminating certification. Rather than removing the DSI criterion entirely, consider streamlining it. The current requirement that developers provide model cards, intended use statements, target population descriptions, known risks, and external validation summary data is substantive but not impossible. Many vendors in our network already produce these materials for FDA submissions or internal governance. What creates burden is the certification process's timeline and review procedures, not the disclosure requirements themselves. The Office of the National Coordinator could reduce certification processing timelines from the current 90 days to 45 days for DSI submissions, allow parallel FDA and ONC review for tools already cleared as SaMD devices, and permit vendors to submit model card summaries rather than full technical documentation. This would reduce burden without eliminating the transparency that practitioners and patients need.‚Źé‚ŹéI also want to address the information blocking implications. The proposed rule revises information blocking definitions and safe harbor criteria. Home care agencies depend on timely access to hospital discharge summaries, medication reconciliation data, and recent laboratory results. When decision support tools operate on fragmented data, their outputs are unreliable. The current information blocking safe harbor for certified health IT that meets certain technical standards (42 CFR 171.102(a)(2)(iii)) creates an incentive for vendors to maintain certification. If certification criteria for DSI are eliminated, vendors lose this incentive to disclose tool limitations and validation scope. They can deploy tools widely with only FDA-level oversight, which focuses on safety and effectiveness in controlled settings, not on real-world deployment across diverse patient populations and care settings.‚Źé‚ŹéOur recommendation is straightforward. Retain the DSI certification criterion but modify the process to reduce compliance burden. Require vendors to disclose intended use, target population, known limitations, training data source description, and external validation results in a standardized format that can be submitted electronically. Accept these disclosures as part of the certification review without requiring additional testing. For tools already cleared by FDA as SaMD devices, allow vendors to reference their FDA submissions rather than duplicating work. This preserves transparency and accountability while reducing the administrative cost that developers have legitimately complained about.‚Źé‚ŹéHome care patients are among the most vulnerable healthcare users. They are older, often cognitively impaired, and dependent on tools they cannot directly evaluate. They deserve to know whether the recommendations shaping their care have been tested in populations like them, validated in home settings, and reviewed for bias before being deployed. The current DSI certification criterion is imperfect, but it is the mechanism through which this transparency happens. Removing it entirely in favor of voluntary industry reporting will harm the patients we serve.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-17T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-17T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ3‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒsupportive‚ôĒOklahoma‚ôĒsenior policy analyst‚ôĒ56‚ôĒMy mother spent her final eighteen months receiving care in our home after her stroke, and I watched firsthand how fragmented her medical records were across her cardiologist, the visiting nurses, and her primary care physician‚ÄĒeach operating in isolation. If we remove the certification requirement for AI model cards, clinicians won't have a way to verify it either. Removing certification transparency just widens that gap.‚Źé‚Źé**What Needs to Stay in Place**‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criteria should be retained because:‚Źé‚Źé- Clinicians need to know what populations an AI tool was validated on before using it on their patients‚Źé- Patients have a right to understand what factors influenced clinical decisions affecting their care‚Źé- Without external validation requirements, developers have no incentive to test beyond their most favorable datasets‚Źé- Accountability disappears if there's no documented record of what a system was supposed to do‚Źé‚ŹéI want my mother's records to move seamlessly between providers. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0091‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒJames Rivera‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA construction worker argues that proposed rules weakening transparency and information-blocking requirements for AI medical devices will repeat past mistakes with medical records, leaving patients and providers unable to understand or trust how A...‚ôĒMy daughter needed a cardiologist after we moved to Lincoln last year. Simple enough, right? Except her new doctor couldn't see her history before the first appointment, and we spent three weeks calling back and forth between offices trying to get those records transferred. I finally drove to the old clinic with a hard drive and sat there while they copied the files onto it. That's when it hit me: we're living in the 21st century, but medical records move like they're being delivered by horse. Now I'm reading about these new rules that might make it even easier for companies to sit on patient data and hide how they're making decisions about people's health, and I keep thinking about my daughter having to repeat tests she already had done because somebody's dragging their feet.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY TRANSPARENCY IN AI MATTERS MORE THAN WE'RE ADMITTING**‚Źé‚ŹéThe conversation around these proposed changes seems to focus a lot on what's easier for companies to build and sell. I get that. But nobody's talking much about what happens on the other side, where patients and doctors are trying to figure out if they can trust a system with someone's life. When the FDA released its guidance on AI and machine learning medical devices back in 2021, it emphasized that transparency in how these systems were developed and tested wasn't bureaucratic overhead‚ÄĒit was patient safety. That guidance is still the right standard. Removing the requirements that let patients and providers understand how an AI system works, how it was trained, and whether it's reliable for their specific situation, is backwards thinking.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not a technologist. I work construction, and I know my lane. But I know what it looks like when someone's hiding how things work, and I know what it costs when that happens in healthcare. My daughter's cardiologist at the new clinic said the lack of old test results meant they'd have to do some of the same workup again, which meant more appointments, more waiting, more worry for my wife and daughter. That's not just an inconvenience. That's time and money and anxiety that didn't have to happen. Now imagine that same problem, but scaled up to every decision an AI system makes about a patient, and nobody‚ÄĒnot the patient, not their doctor‚ÄĒcan see how the system reached that decision. That's not innovation. That's a recipe for repeating the exact mistakes we've already made.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT MODEL CARDS AND TRANSPARENCY ACTUALLY DO**‚Źé‚ŹéI keep hearing that these transparency requirements are duplication, that they're slowing things down. But I've watched enough construction projects to know the difference between requirements that don't matter and requirements that prevent disaster. You can't skip the inspection because you already poured the foundation. Model cards and documentation requirements aren't red tape. They're the equivalent of a building inspector's report. They tell you whether the thing you're about to rely on was built properly.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen a company has to document how an AI model was trained, what data went into it, how it was tested, and where it might fail, that's not bureaucracy. That's accountability. That's the difference between a company that stands behind what they built and one that's hoping nobody looks too closely. My daughter's new cardiologist was actually frustrated about the record transfer problem too. He said it's not that clinics don't want to share data. It's that the systems don't talk to each other, and there's no real pressure to make them better because companies profit from staying siloed. I see the same logic here. If you can hide how your AI works, you don't have to answer hard questions about whether it's safe or fair.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Institute of Medicine published a report back in 2012 on patient safety and clinical decision support systems. It found that providers often don't trust decision support tools when they can't understand the reasoning behind them. That was over a decade ago, and the problem hasn't gotten better. If anything, AI systems are more opaque now than the older decision support tools were. Removing the transparency requirements that let providers and patients understand what's happening is going to make trust worse, not better. And without trust, these systems won't work, no matter how sophisticated they are.‚Źé‚Źé**THE REAL QUESTION ABOUT ACCESS AND USE**‚Źé‚ŹéI noticed in the rule that there's talk about clarifying what "access" and "use" mean in the information blocking regulations. This matters more than it sounds like. When we had to fight to get my daughter's records transferred, the clinic said they had to protect her privacy. I understood that‚ÄĒI'm not against privacy protections. But the way the rules are written now, "privacy protection" can mean a lot of things, and some of them are just cover for not wanting to deal with the hassle of sending records out. If you clarify the definitions of access and use in a way that makes it easier for companies to refuse patient requests, you'll recreate the same problem we had, just in a different form.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what I think should happen. The definitions of access and use should be clear enough that patients and providers can't be jerked around, but they should also protect actual privacy. Right now, it feels like privacy is being used as an excuse to avoid transparency. That's not what privacy rules are for. They're supposed to protect sensitive information from people who shouldn't have it, not give companies cover to hide how systems work. When my daughter's old clinic finally sent her records, nothing bad happened. No identity theft, no privacy breach. What happened was she got better care because her new doctor had the information he needed.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm skeptical about removing certification criteria just because they seem duplicative. Duplication sometimes exists for a reason. It's a safety check. A CMS study from 2019 looked at adverse events linked to inadequate clinical documentation and provider awareness. The study found that when providers didn't have complete information about a patient, or when they didn't understand the systems they were using, bad things happened. Not usually catastrophic, but real problems that cost time and money and patient trust. That's what these certification requirements are trying to prevent.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT "SIMPLIFICATION" MEANS IN PRACTICE**‚Źé‚ŹéThe rule talks a lot about simplifying things, removing burden, unleashing innovation. I hear that language all the time in construction too. "Let's simplify the permit process, reduce regulations, let builders innovate." And sometimes that works fine. But sometimes it means someone cuts corners, and a building doesn't last, or it's not safe, and then you've got problems that cost way more to fix. I'm not saying everything in these rules is perfect. There's probably room to streamline some things. But the difference between streamlining and gutting is real, and it matters.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen I think about what these changes mean for patients, I keep coming back to something simple: if you can't see how something works, you can't trust it. And if you can't trust the systems that are making decisions about your health, you're going to end up back where we started‚ÄĒwith patients having to fight for basic information, with providers making decisions without the data they need, with everyone frustrated and the system not working for anybody except the companies selling the technology.‚Źé‚ŹéThe ONC Health IT Certification Program impact analysis from 2022 looked at how certification requirements affect patient trust and provider liability. It found that providers feel more confident using certified systems, and patients feel more protected when they know providers are using systems that have been tested and documented. Removing those requirements doesn't just affect companies. It affects whether providers will use these systems, and whether patients will trust them.‚Źé‚Źé**WHERE THE REAL BURDEN ACTUALLY IS**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be fair here. I get that companies find it burdensome to document their models, to certify their systems, to prove that they work safely. That takes time and money. But I also think there's a difference between burden and cost. Building a safe product costs something. That's not a bug in the system. That's the point. If you remove the cost of proving something is safe, you don't get innovation. You get corners being cut.‚Źé‚ŹéMy experience with my daughter's medical records was a burden too. Three weeks of phone calls, finally driving to the clinic with a hard drive, waiting while someone figured out how to copy files. That was a burden on us. But the burden existed because the system was broken, not because there were too many rules. If anything, there weren't enough rules about what companies had to do to make record transfer easy. If I were writing this rule, I'd be looking at what actual burdens patients and providers face, not just what burdens companies face. My daughter's burden was real. The clinic's burden in transferring her records was just part of doing business.‚Źé‚ŹéI keep thinking about that moment when I finally got those records and realized how easy it could have been if the system was designed to be simple instead of designed to keep data locked down. That's what good rules do. They push companies to design systems that work for patients and providers, not just systems that are profitable for the company. When you remove transparency requirements and information blocking rules, you're removing the pressure to design for anyone except the company.‚Źé‚Źé**THE MISTAKE WE'RE ABOUT TO REPEAT**‚Źé‚ŹéThis is what bothers me most about these proposed changes. We already made this mistake with medical records. For years, we had a system where records were siloed, hard to access, and nobody really knew why except that companies preferred it that way. It took pressure and rules to start changing that. Now we're about to do the same thing with AI. We're going to remove the transparency requirements that let people see how these systems work, we're going to weaken the rules that keep companies from hiding information, and we're going to tell ourselves it's for the sake of innovation. And in five years, we'll have a system where AI is making major decisions about people's health, and nobody can see how, and nobody can trust it, and we'll have to fight the same battle all over again.‚Źé‚ŹéI don't want my daughter to have to go through what we went through with her records, but with AI. I don't want her to have to guess whether a system that's recommending a treatment was trained on data that includes people who look like her, or whether it's reliable for her specific condition, or whether the company is even willing to tell her. She deserves to know how decisions are being made about her health. So does every other patient.‚Źé‚ŹéThe thing that gets me is that this is fixable. Companies can build AI systems that are transparent. They can document their models. They can submit to certification. They can design systems that work with information blocking rules instead of around them. It's not impossible. It just requires them to think about patients and providers, not just profit. And right now, these proposed rules are saying they don't have to.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT I'M ASKING FOR**‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not asking for everything to stay exactly as it is. Maybe there are some rules that don't make sense. Maybe there are some requirements that could be streamlined. But I'm asking for the rules that matter to stay in place. Keep the transparency requirements for AI systems. Keep the documentation standards. Keep the information blocking rules that let patients access their own data and let providers get what they need to make good decisions. These aren't perfect rules, but they're the rules that stand between a system that works for patients and a system that works only for companies.‚Źé‚ŹéIf you're going to remove certification criteria, tell me specifically which ones and why. Tell me why patients and providers don't need to know how an AI system was trained. Tell me why it's okay for companies to hide that information. Tell me why the mistake we made with medical records is worth repeating with something even more important. And tell me what happens when a patient asks their doctor whether a system is safe and the doctor says, "I have no idea, the company won't say." That's not innovation. That's just negligence with better marketing.‚Źé‚ŹéMy daughter is healthy now. Her new cardiologist has all her records, and she's doing well. But I think about her every time I hear about these rules, and I think about other people's daughters and sons and parents who are going to face the same problems if we weaken the rules that are supposed to protect them. That's why I'm writing this. That's why it matters.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-02-02T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-02T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ7‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒurgent‚ôĒNebraska‚ôĒconstruction worker‚ôĒ55‚ôĒLast year my daughter had to switch cardiologists after we moved, and I watched her spend three weeks calling back and forth trying to get her old records transferred‚ÄĒrecords she needed before her first appointment with the new doctor.
