CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0094‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒKevin Baker‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe commenter opposes removing the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion, arguing that transparency requirements for AI clinical decision support tools‚ÄĒincluding model cards and training data disclosures‚ÄĒare essential safeguards t...‚ôĒ**Docket Comment on Proposed Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability Deregulation**‚Źé‚ŹéThe situation with my wife's rheumatoid arthritis care has made me think hard about what happens when health systems don't talk to each other, and I need to be direct: removing the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion is a mistake that will hurt patients like Janet, not help them.‚Źé‚ŹéLast April, I spent an entire afternoon on the phone between Ann Arbor and Traverse City trying to get her lab results moved. The rheumatology office kept saying the primary care system "couldn't receive" the files. I suspect they didn't want to spend the time, but the real problem wasn't the technology‚ÄĒit was that nobody was being held accountable for whether information actually got where it needed to go. When I finally got the records, I learned the primary care doctor had already ordered duplicate tests. That cost money we didn't have, and Janet got stuck with extra needle sticks.‚Źé‚ŹéNow I'm reading that the proposed rule would strip out the requirement for AI model cards and transparency disclosures in clinical decision support tools. I understand the argument about regulatory burden. Developers say certification requirements slow innovation. But I keep thinking about what that means in practice. If a rheumatologist's software is recommending medications or dosing adjustments based on an AI model nobody's examined, how do we know it's safe? How do we know it wasn't trained on data that doesn't represent patients like Janet, who has multiple comorbidities and takes other medications? The transparency requirement isn't bureaucratic theater. It's supposed to make sure the people using these tools‚ÄĒand the patients affected by them‚ÄĒactually know what they're getting.‚Źé‚Źé**What the Current Certification Criterion Requires**‚Źé‚ŹéThe existing Decision Support Interventions criterion requires disclosure of:‚Źé‚Źé- Intended use and target population‚Źé- Known risks and limitations‚Źé- Training data characteristics‚Źé- External validation evidence‚Źé‚ŹéThis isn't abstract. When a clinical tool tells a doctor to adjust a treatment plan, the doctor should be able to know whether the AI was tested on a population similar to the patient in front of them. If the model was trained mostly on younger patients without the comorbidities Janet has, that's something the clinician needs to know before following its recommendation. Right now, the certification process makes sure that information gets documented and available. Without it, we're back to a black box.‚Źé‚Źé**The Privacy Concern is Real**‚Źé‚ŹéI also worry about what easier data portability means if we're reducing oversight at the same time. Yes, Janet should be able to get her records transferred without spending hours on the phone. That part makes sense. But "easier to move" shouldn't mean "easier for systems to share without proper safeguards." If we're going to make it simpler to move health information across providers, we need stronger accountability about who's looking at it, not weaker. Right now, audit logs and access controls are part of what keeps that in check. Removing certification standards feels like we're loosening one hand while trying to tighten the other.‚Źé‚Źé**Why This Matters for Real Patients**‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not against innovation. I want better tools. But innovation that puts patients at risk isn't progress. When a hospital system implements a clinical decision support tool that hasn't been tested for safety or transparency, who bears the risk if something goes wrong? The patient does. Janet does. The doctor probably does. The software company? They're protected by liability clauses. There's no incentive to be careful if the regulatory requirement that was supposed to enforce care gets stripped away.‚Źé‚ŹéThe model card and transparency disclosure requirement isn't perfect, but it exists for a reason. It came from lessons learned about bias in healthcare AI, about tools that worked for some populations and failed for others. I've read the studies. In 2019, researchers found that a widely used algorithm for managing patient care was systematically biased against Black patients. That happened because nobody required the developers to disclose what population the model was actually trained on. If Janet had been using that system, it might have steered her care in the wrong direction and nobody would have known why.‚Źé‚Źé**What I'm Asking**‚Źé‚ŹéKeep the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion. Require AI model cards. Require disclosure of intended use, target population, known risks, and training data characteristics. Make external validation evidence part of the standard. This isn't about slowing down good innovation. It's about making sure the innovation that gets implemented in hospitals and clinics where real people are being treated is actually safe.‚Źé‚ŹéI understand the frustration with regulatory burden. But the burden of a patient getting harmed because a tool wasn't properly validated is a lot heavier. My wife shouldn't have to wonder whether the recommendations her doctor is following came from an AI system that was even designed to work for someone like her. That's not asking too much. That's asking for the minimum amount of accountability that makes sense.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-29T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-29T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒconcerned‚ôĒMichigan‚ôĒretail worker‚ôĒ63‚ôĒMy wife Janet was diagnosed with rheumatoid arthritis three years ago, and we've had to coordinate care between her rheumatologist in Ann Arbor and her primary care doctor here in Traverse City‚ÄĒtwo separate health systems that seem deliberately incompatible. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0095‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒEdward Torres‚ôĒCareQuest Institute for Oral Health‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒCareQuest Institute for Oral Health opposes the removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) transparency and model card requirements under 45 CFR 170.315(b)(6), arguing that these certification criteria are essential for detecting inequitable ...‚ôĒCareQuest Institute for Oral Health‚Źé1325 Franklin Street, Suite 200‚ŹéOakland, California 94612‚Źé‚ŹéJune 16, 2025‚Źé‚ŹéOffice of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology‚ŹéU.S. Department of Health and Human Services‚Źé200 Independence Avenue, S.W.‚ŹéWashington, D.C. 20201‚Źé‚Źé**RE: Comments on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity (RFI)**‚Źé‚Źé---‚Źé‚ŹéCareQuest Institute for Oral Health submits these comments regarding the proposed removal and revision of certification criteria under 45 CFR 170.315(b)(6), specifically the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) transparency requirements. Our organization represents the interests of safety-net dental providers, oral health researchers, and the low-income populations they serve across 47 states. We urge the Office of the National Coordinator to retain the current model card and transparency disclosure requirements for AI-assisted clinical decision support systems.‚Źé‚Źé**THE DATA FRAGMENTATION PROBLEM THAT ACTUALLY EXISTS**‚Źé‚ŹéLast year, our research team conducted a retrospective study of oral health outcomes across low-income communities in the Portland metro area. The project was straightforward in concept: identify treatment patterns across three major clinic networks serving uninsured and Medicaid-eligible patients, looking for disparities in access to restorative care versus extractions. The execution was a different story. We spent eight months negotiating data access agreements with three separate clinic networks, each operating on different electronic health record systems, each with different interpretations of what they could release and under what conditions. Two of the three networks had implemented AI-driven clinical decision support tools for treatment planning, but we had almost no visibility into how those systems worked, what data trained them, or whether they were producing systematically different recommendations based on patient demographics.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen we finally obtained access to the underlying data, we discovered something we would have missed entirely without the documentation that current certification requirements mandate. The three clinics had implemented decision support systems from two different vendors. One system recommended extraction over restoration for patients over 60 at roughly twice the rate of the other system, even when clinical indicators were identical. We could only flag this discrepancy because the certification documentation required under 45 CFR 170.315(b)(6) included model cards that specified the training data characteristics, intended use populations, and known limitations. Without those transparency requirements, we would have attributed the pattern to clinical judgment variation. Instead, we documented a potentially serious equity issue: the system recommending higher extraction rates had been trained primarily on data from academic dental centers, not safety-net clinics, and the model card explicitly noted this limitation.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not a theoretical concern. This is what happens when you strip back the documentation requirements that regulators put in place specifically to catch these problems.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY REMOVING DSI CERTIFICATION CRITERIA CREATES A VISIBILITY PROBLEM FOR VULNERABLE POPULATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed deregulatory actions would remove or substantially weaken the certification criterion requiring AI model cards, transparency disclosures, intended use statements, target population documentation, known risks, and external validation characteristics (45 CFR 170.315(b)(6)). The stated rationale is to reduce burden on health IT developers and accelerate innovation. The unstated consequence is that safety-net providers and health equity researchers lose the only systematic mechanism we have to detect when clinical decision support systems are producing disparate outcomes across patient populations.‚Źé‚ŹéHere is the operational reality: small dental practices and federally qualified health centers do not have data science teams. They do not have the resources to conduct independent validation studies of commercial decision support tools. When a vendor says their system improves clinical efficiency, most safety-net providers take that at face value because they lack the expertise and institutional capacity to verify it. They rely on regulatory certification to mean that someone, somewhere, has checked whether the system works equitably across different populations. Removing that requirement does not eliminate the risk; it just makes the risk invisible until harm accumulates.‚Źé‚ŹéThe certification criterion as currently written requires:‚Źé‚Źé- A description of the intended use and intended user of the Decision Support Intervention‚Źé- A description of the target population for which the DSI was designed‚Źé- A complete description of the training data, including data source, size, and demographic composition‚Źé- Documentation of known risks and limitations, with particular attention to populations or clinical scenarios where performance may degrade‚Źé- Results of external validation testing on data not used for model development‚Źé‚ŹéThese are not burdensome requirements. They are basic transparency measures that allow health IT purchasers and health equity researchers to assess whether a tool is safe for their specific patient population. The burden argument assumes that transparency documentation is expensive and time-consuming. In practice, vendors already maintain this information internally. Requiring it to be disclosed does not create new work; it requires existing documentation to be shared with regulators and purchasers.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT THE CURRENT STANDARDS ACTUALLY DO**‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criterion implements requirements grounded in 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1) through 170.315(b)(5), which establish broader standards for clinical decision support. The model card requirement specifically implements the transparency principles outlined in the FDA's 2021 guidance on modifications to AI/ML-based software as a medical device (FDA-2021-D-0900). These are not novel or experimental requirements. They reflect consensus recommendations from the National Academy of Medicine, the American Medical Association, and health equity research organizations about what constitutes responsible deployment of AI in clinical settings.‚Źé‚ŹéThe concern about innovation burden does not hold up to scrutiny when you look at actual implementation. The Health Level 7 Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources (FHIR) standard includes a Decision Support Service module (FHIR Implementation Guide for CDS Hooks Release 1.0) that already accommodates model card data. Vendors integrating their systems with FHIR-compliant EHRs already structure their documentation in ways compatible with these transparency requirements. The certification criterion does not require new technical infrastructure; it requires vendors to document what they already know about their systems.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC CONCERN: DISPARITIES IN AI TRAINING DATA**‚Źé‚ŹéOne concrete example from our research illustrates why this matters. A decision support system used by 23 safety-net dental clinics in our study was trained on data from 340 patients with an average age of 42 years and 78% insured status. The system was then deployed in clinics where the average patient age was 56 and 91% were uninsured or Medicaid-eligible. The model card disclosed this mismatch explicitly. The vendor also noted in the known limitations section that the system's accuracy on treatment planning decisions for patients over 65 was "not well-characterized." This was not a reason to reject the system, but it was a reason to use it differently: clinicians flagged recommendations for patients over 65 for manual review rather than accepting them automatically.‚Źé‚ŹéWithout the model card requirement, those clinicians would have had no way to know about the age mismatch in training data. The system would have functioned exactly the same way in their workflows, but their decision-making process would have been different. Some proportion of recommendations for elderly patients would have been accepted without scrutiny, and we would not know how many of those decisions were suboptimal because the underlying model was not trained on data from that population. The model card requirement does not prevent deployment of imperfect tools; it enables clinicians to use them appropriately given their limitations.‚Źé‚Źé**RECOMMENDATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéCareQuest Institute respectfully recommends that the Office of the National Coordinator retain 45 CFR 170.315(b)(6) in its current form, with no weakening of the transparency and documentation requirements. If there are specific compliance barriers that vendors have identified, we recommend a collaborative process to streamline documentation without eliminating transparency.‚Źé‚ŹéWe further recommend that any future modifications to DSI certification criteria include explicit requirements for:‚Źé‚Źé- Demographic stratification in external validation testing, with separate performance metrics for age, race/ethnicity, insurance status, and clinical complexity‚Źé- Documentation of disparities identified during development or post-deployment monitoring‚Źé- Clear guidance on when vendor disclaimers about limited evidence in specific populations should trigger mandatory clinician review of recommendations for those populations‚Źé‚ŹéThese are not radical proposals. They reflect the direction that health IT governance is moving across health systems that have invested in equity infrastructure. Requiring them now prevents fragmentation later, when some vendors disclose disparities and others do not, creating uneven accountability across the market.‚Źé‚ŹéThe deregulatory framework assumes that removing certification requirements will reduce costs and accelerate adoption. That may be true in the short term for vendors. For safety-net providers and the vulnerable populations they serve, it means losing the only systematic way to detect whether the tools we are adopting work equitably. We have lived through eight months of data fragmentation trying to answer questions that model card disclosures would have answered in weeks.‚Źé‚ŹéCareQuest Institute is not opposed to innovation in clinical decision support. We are opposed to innovation that proceeds blind to its own effects on equity. The current DSI certification criterion is the minimum transparency standard necessary to ensure that we can see those effects before they accumulate into disparities that harm patients.‚Źé‚ŹéRespectfully submitted,‚Źé‚Źé**Edward Torres**‚ŹéResearch Director‚ŹéCareQuest Institute for Oral Health‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-23T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-23T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ4‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHospital and health system procurement teams need transparent AI model information to make evidence-based decisions about which tools to adopt and how to implement them safely in their local contexts.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒangry‚ôĒOregon‚ôĒresearch director‚ôĒ36‚ôĒLast year, we conducted a retrospective study on oral health outcomes across low-income communities in the Portland metro area, and the data fragmentation nearly killed the project‚ÄĒwe spent eight months just negotiating access agreements with three separate clinic networks. But here's what infuriates me: when we finally got the data, we discovered significant disparities in treatment patterns that nobody was tracking systematically, disparities that would have remained invisible if regulations hadn't required those clinics to maintain certain documentation standards. Now I'm watching those same safeguards get stripped back, and I know exactly which vulnerable populations will lose visibility first.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1481
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0096‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒThomas Torres‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe commenter argues that requiring doctors to understand and be transparent about AI tools they use is a common-sense safety measure that should not be dismissed simply because formal studies proving its benefit don't yet exist, drawing on person...‚ôĒMy daughter switched doctors last spring when our insurance changed, and it took three months just to get her records transferred. The old office kept saying they were busy, so she had to repeat all her tests and explain everything again to the new doctor. That's exactly why I think doctors need to know what tools they're using and what those tools are actually supposed to do. The agency says there's no proof that requiring transparency about AI tools helps patients, but that's not the same as saying it doesn't help. Nobody's published a study yet, sure, but that shouldn't mean we get rid of something that just makes common sense for safety. When doctors don't understand how an AI tool works or what it's designed for, patients end up getting worse care because nobody knows what went wrong.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-02-04T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-04T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe agency's claim that 'no publicly available evidence' supports transparency requirements conflates absence of evidence with evidence of absence; lack of published studies does not mean the criterion provides no value.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒlow‚ôĒsupportive‚ôĒNew Hampshire‚ôĒelementary school teacher‚ôĒ71‚ôĒLast spring, my daughter had to switch doctors because our insurance changed, and it took three months just to get her medical records transferred‚ÄĒthe old office kept saying they were busy. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0097‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒMelissa Wright‚ôĒPolicy Center for Maternal Mental Health‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health opposes removing Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements for health IT systems, arguing that transparency mandates requiring model documentation, validation evidence, and disclos...‚ôĒ**COMMENT ON HEALTH DATA, TECHNOLOGY, AND INTEROPERABILITY: ASTP/ONC DEREGULATORY ACTIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéPolicy Center for Maternal Mental Health‚Źé‚ŹéThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health submits this comment on the proposed deregulatory framework regarding health IT certification criteria, specifically addressing the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements and related transparency standards. Our organization works with maternal health providers, health systems, and patient advocates across rural and underserved communities. Over the past eighteen months, we have documented how the absence of AI transparency in clinical decision support systems directly threatens the safety and autonomy of pregnant and postpartum patients, particularly those already marginalized by insurance denials and algorithmic bias.‚Źé‚ŹéWe want to be direct: removing the current DSI certification criterion requiring model cards, transparency disclosures, and external validation would be a mistake. Not because we oppose innovation or regulatory efficiency in general, but because the specific harms we have witnessed are preventable with existing transparency requirements that impose minimal burden on responsible developers.‚Źé‚Źé**THE REAL PROBLEM WE OBSERVED**‚Źé‚ŹéLast spring, I was consulting with a rural health clinic in the Arkansas Delta when they discovered something deeply troubling. A young mother had been denied postpartum depression treatment by her insurance company. The insurer had obtained her mental health records through a data broker‚ÄĒwithout her knowledge or explicit consent‚ÄĒand fed them into a proprietary risk assessment algorithm that classified her depression as a pre-existing condition. The algorithm's reasoning was opaque. The clinic director couldn't explain to the patient what had happened or why. The clinic had no way to know what data the algorithm had used, how it was trained, or whether it had been validated in populations like the one they served. The mother, terrified and ashamed, delayed treatment for three months.‚Źé‚ŹéThis wasn't a regulatory violation at the time. The algorithm vendor had no obligation to disclose how their model worked. The clinic had no legal standing to demand transparency. The patient had no recourse. What strikes me still is that this preventable harm occurred not because innovation was suppressed, but because transparency requirements were absent.‚Źé‚ŹéSince then, we have been tracking similar incidents. We spoke with a women's health network in Mississippi that discovered their EHR's maternal risk flagging system was trained almost exclusively on hospital populations and systematically overestimated risk in community clinic patients. The vendor knew this from internal validation studies but had no requirement to disclose it to users. We interviewed three different health systems that had implemented AI-assisted depression screening tools without knowing whether the underlying models had been validated in postpartum populations specifically, as opposed to general depression screening cohorts. The vendors could not or would not provide this information.‚Źé‚ŹéNone of these are edge cases. They represent the downstream consequences of the current certification framework, which exists precisely to prevent this kind of opacity.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THE DSI CRITERION MATTERS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current requirement for Decision Support Interventions certification, as codified in the Office of the National Coordinator's 2015 Edition Health IT Certification Criteria (45 CFR 170.315(a)(1) et seq.), mandates that developers disclose: intended use, target population, known risks, characteristics of training data, and evidence of external validation. This is not a novel or onerous requirement. It is standard practice in responsible AI development across healthcare. It is not innovation suppression. It is innovation accountability.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat the DSI criterion actually requires is transparency. The model card requirement (modeled on the approach described in Gebru et al.'s work on model cards for model reporting, 2021) asks developers to document what their system does, who it was tested on, and what it fails at. That is not a burden. That is basic professional responsibility.‚Źé‚ŹéWe have reviewed the proposed rule's cost-benefit analysis, and we think it underestimates the costs of removing this requirement. The analysis assumes that removing DSI certification will reduce developer compliance costs and accelerate market entry. We do not dispute that it will do both. But it will do so by externalizing the costs of opacity onto patients, clinicians, and health systems that must now evaluate and use these systems blind.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT CLINICIANS NEED TO MAKE DECISIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéOur member clinics‚ÄĒOB/GYNs, midwives, maternal health social workers, primary care physicians serving pregnant and postpartum populations‚ÄĒtell us consistently that they want to use AI-assisted decision support. They want tools that flag high-risk pregnancies, that help them identify postpartum depression earlier, that reduce disparities by systematically checking for bias. But they cannot responsibly use a tool when they do not know how it works, what it was trained on, or whether it has been validated in their patient population.‚Źé‚ŹéI keep coming back to a conversation with a maternal-fetal medicine specialist in rural Tennessee. She said, "I'll use whatever tool helps me catch preeclampsia earlier. But I need to know if this model was trained in white women and then applied to my Black patients. I need to know if it was trained on singleton pregnancies and then applied to my multiples. I need to know what it gets wrong, because I need to know when to trust it and when to override it." That is not an unreasonable ask. That is the foundation of informed clinical decision-making.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI criterion provides exactly this information. It requires developers to specify: (1) the clinical context in which the system is intended to operate; (2) the population characteristics of the training and validation data; (3) known performance gaps, including any identified disparities; and (4) external validation evidence. A clinician reviewing this documentation can assess whether the tool is appropriate for her patients and her clinical setting. Without it, she is using a black box.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule states that removing DSI certification will "promote innovation" and "reduce unnecessary burden on developers." We agree that excessive certification requirements can suppress innovation. But the DSI criterion is not excessive. It is foundational. Removing it does not promote innovation. It promotes the deployment of opaque systems without accountability.‚Źé‚Źé**THE REGULATORY PRECEDENT**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Food and Drug Administration's recent guidance on clinical decision support software (FDA Guidance, "Proposed Regulatory Framework for Modifications to Artificial Intelligence/Machine Learning (AI/ML)-Based Software as a Medical Device," January 2021) makes clear that some level of transparency and validation is necessary for safe deployment. The FDA does not require FDA approval for certain types of clinical decision support, but it does require that developers maintain documentation of performance, intended use, and validation evidence. The ONC's DSI criterion is less stringent than FDA expectations, not more.‚Źé‚ŹéSimilarly, the European Union's AI Act (2024) requires transparency documentation for high-risk AI systems, including those used in healthcare. The EU does not view this as anti-competitive or innovation-suppressing. It views it as the minimum standard for responsible deployment.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI criterion is aligned with international best practice. Removing it would place the United States behind the EU and Japan in AI governance for healthcare.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT REMOVING THE CRITERION WOULD MEAN IN PRACTICE**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the DSI certification requirement is removed, here is what happens. Developers will have no obligation to document how their systems were trained, what populations they were validated on, or what they fail at. Health systems will acquire these systems without standard transparency documentation. Clinicians will use them without knowing their limitations. Patients will be affected by algorithmic decisions they cannot understand or challenge.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not theoretical. We have already seen it happen in maternal health. A major health system in the South acquired an AI-based maternal risk assessment tool. The vendor's marketing materials promised to "identify high-risk pregnancies." The system was deployed across 15 clinics. Six months in, a clinician noticed that the system was flagging an unusually high percentage of Black patients as high-risk, compared to white patients with similar clinical profiles. She asked the vendor for validation data broken down by race. The vendor declined to provide it, citing proprietary concerns. The health system had no regulatory leverage to demand it. The system remained in use, with known or suspected bias, because there was no certification requirement that would have mandated transparency during acquisition.‚Źé‚ŹéIf DSI certification is removed, this scenario becomes the norm rather than the exception.‚Źé‚Źé**THE BURDEN ARGUMENT IS OVERSTATED**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule claims that DSI certification imposes significant compliance burden on developers. We have spoken with several health IT vendors about this. Their honest answer is that the burden is real but modest. Preparing a model card and validation documentation takes time, but it is time developers should be spending anyway if they are responsible. The burden is not in meeting the requirement. The burden is in being transparent about systems that were not developed with transparency in mind.‚Źé‚ŹéOne vendor told us directly: "If we have to document our model card, it means we have to actually understand our model well enough to document it. That's not a compliance cost. That's a quality cost. We should be paying it."‚Źé‚ŹéThe compliance cost argument also assumes that all developers are currently meeting the DSI requirement. They are not. Many developers have worked around it by claiming that their system is not a clinical decision support system but rather an informational tool. This semantic distinction allows them to avoid certification altogether. Removing the requirement does not reduce burden on responsible developers. It just eliminates the incentive for irresponsible ones to become transparent.‚Źé‚Źé**THE SPECIFIC LANGUAGE WE NEED TO PRESERVE**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the proposed rule proceeds, we urge the agency to preserve the following specific requirements within the DSI certification criterion:‚Źé‚Źé1. Documentation of intended use and target population. The system must specify what clinical decision(s) it supports and what patient population it was designed for.‚Źé‚Źé2. Disclosure of training data characteristics. The vendor must disclose the source, size, and demographic composition of the data used to train the model.‚Źé‚Źé3. Evidence of external validation. The system must have been validated on data separate from its training set, and this validation evidence must be documented.‚Źé‚Źé4. Disclosure of known performance gaps. The vendor must disclose any identified disparities in system performance across demographic groups or clinical subpopulations.‚Źé‚Źé5. Information on modification and update practices. If the model is updated or modified after initial deployment, documentation of these changes must be available to users.‚Źé‚ŹéThese are not novel requirements. They are standard practice in medical device regulation. They are what responsible vendors already do. Preserving them does not suppress innovation. It ensures that when innovation is deployed, it is deployed responsibly.‚Źé‚Źé**THE EQUITY IMPACT**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be explicit about what we are most concerned about. The patients most likely to be harmed by opaque AI systems are the ones already at highest risk: pregnant and postpartum people in rural areas, people of color, people in low-income communities, people with mental health histories, people with substance use histories.‚Źé‚ŹéThese are the populations most likely to be underrepresented in AI training data. These are the populations most likely to experience bias in algorithmic systems. These are also the populations least likely to have the resources or knowledge to challenge algorithmic decisions or seek alternative care.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen we remove transparency requirements, we are not removing burden equally. We are removing accountability disproportionately for the most vulnerable patients.‚Źé‚ŹéThe maternal health disparities crisis in the United States is not incidental. Maternal mortality for Black women is nearly three times higher than for white women. Maternal morbidity is even more disparate. If we deploy opaque AI systems into this context, we risk automating and scaling existing bias.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criterion is not perfect. But it is a check on this risk. Removing it without replacement would be a significant step backward.‚Źé‚Źé**ALTERNATIVE APPROACHES WE WOULD CONSIDER**‚Źé‚ŹéWe are not opposed to regulatory efficiency. If the agency believes that the current DSI certification process is inefficient or overly prescriptive, there are ways to streamline it without eliminating it entirely. Some possibilities:‚Źé‚Źé- Streamline the certification process itself by reducing documentation requirements for lower-risk systems (e.g., systems that provide informational support but do not directly influence clinical decisions).‚Źé- Establish a tiered certification framework where systems with higher clinical impact or broader populations are subject to more rigorous transparency requirements.‚Źé- Allow vendors to provide validation evidence from independent third parties rather than requiring all evidence to be generated by the vendor itself.‚Źé- Create standardized model card templates that reduce the time burden of documentation without reducing information quality.‚Źé‚ŹéThese approaches would preserve accountability while potentially reducing compliance burden. We would support regulatory reform that takes one of these paths.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE HAVE HEARD FROM CLINICIANS**‚Źé‚ŹéWe conducted a survey of 87 maternal health providers across six states in late 2024. We asked whether they would feel comfortable using AI-assisted clinical decision support without transparency documentation. Seventy-two percent said no. When asked what documentation they would need to feel comfortable, the most common responses were: (1) information about what populations the system was trained on (cited by 68 percent), (2) information about known performance gaps (cited by 64 percent), and (3) information about external validation (cited by 61 percent).‚Źé‚ŹéThese are exactly what the DSI criterion requires. Clinicians are asking for what the regulation already mandates. Removing the mandate does not serve clinicians. It serves vendors who do not want to be transparent.‚Źé‚Źé**THE BURDEN ON HEALTH SYSTEMS**‚Źé‚ŹéWe have also heard from health system IT leadership. When they acquire new clinical decision support systems, they currently have the option to require DSI certification as part of their procurement process. This gives them a standard by which to evaluate systems. If certification is eliminated, health systems will need to develop their own evaluation criteria or rely on vendor claims. This actually increases burden on health systems, because they now have to do individually what was previously done through a common standard.‚Źé‚ŹéA health system CIO in Arkansas told us: "If I can't require a model card, I have to hire a consultant to audit every system I acquire. That's more expensive and less reliable than a certification standard. You're not reducing burden. You're just shifting it."‚Źé‚Źé**INTERNATIONAL COMPETITIVENESS**‚Źé‚ŹéThere is an argument that removing US regulatory requirements makes US health IT vendors more competitive internationally. But this argument assumes that other countries will not adopt stronger AI governance standards. The evidence suggests the opposite.‚Źé‚ŹéThe EU AI Act requires transparency for high-risk systems. The UK's AI Bill of Rights includes transparency principles. Japan's Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare has published guidance on AI governance in healthcare. If the United States removes its transparency requirements, US vendors may have short-term competitive advantage in the US market, but they will face higher barriers to entry in international markets where transparency is mandated.‚Źé‚ŹéThe long-term competitive advantage lies in developing systems that are transparent by design, not in lobbying to remove transparency requirements at home.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE ARE NOT ASKING FOR**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be clear about what our concern is not. We are not asking for expanded certification requirements. We are not asking for pre-market approval of all AI systems. We are not asking for FDA-level review of every clinical decision support tool. We are not asking for anything that would prevent innovation or market entry.‚Źé‚ŹéWe are asking for transparency. We are asking that vendors document what their systems do and what they have been tested on. We are asking that clinicians and health systems have access to this documentation. We are asking that patients can understand how algorithmic decisions affect them.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not radical. This is baseline.‚Źé‚Źé**THE SPECIFIC CASE THAT BROUGHT US HERE**‚Źé‚ŹéLet me return to the case that prompted this work. The young mother who was denied postpartum depression treatment based on an algorithmic decision: what would have changed if the vendor had been required to maintain DSI certification?‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, the health system would have had a standard by which to evaluate the vendor's system before acquisition. They could have asked for the model card. They could have seen that the system was trained on insurance claims data, which are known to be biased against mental health diagnoses in certain populations. They might have chosen not to implement the system, or implemented it with strong oversight.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, the vendor would have been required to disclose if the system's performance differed across demographic groups. This particular algorithm performed worse in rural populations. If this was documented and disclosed, the clinic could have known to be especially skeptical of its recommendations for their patient population.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, when the patient tried to understand what had happened, the clinic would have had documentation to point to. They could not tell the patient the vendor's proprietary algorithm details, but they could explain what the system was designed to do and what it was tested on. This is not full transparency, but it is better than the current situation, where the patient has no information at all.‚Źé‚ŹéThe patient still would not have gotten her treatment approved by insurance. But at least the system would have been transparent about its limitations and biases. At least there would have been accountability.‚Źé‚Źé**OUR RECOMMENDATION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health urges the Department of Health and Human Services to preserve the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion in its current form, or if modifications are deemed necessary, to preserve the core transparency and validation requirements within that criterion.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the agency believes that streamlining is necessary, we would support targeted modifications that reduce documentation burden without reducing information quality. But we cannot support wholesale elimination of a requirement that exists specifically to prevent the harms we have documented.‚Źé‚ŹéThe maternal health system in the United States is fragile. Maternal mortality is rising. Maternal mental health is a crisis. Maternal health disparities are widening. In this context, deploying opaque algorithmic systems is not forward-thinking. It is reckless.‚Źé‚ŹéWe have the tools to do this better. The DSI certification criterion is one of those tools. It is not perfect, but it works. Removing it would make a difficult situation worse.‚Źé‚ŹéWe remain available to discuss these concerns with the agency and to provide additional documentation of the cases and patterns we have observed. We also welcome the opportunity to work with vendors, health systems, and other stakeholders on streamlined approaches that preserve accountability while reducing unnecessary burden.‚Źé‚ŹéThe future of maternal health depends on systems that are trustworthy and transparent. That future is not served by removing the requirements that make transparency possible.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-21T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-21T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ10‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒangry‚ôĒArkansas‚ôĒdirector of government affairs‚ôĒ58‚ôĒLast spring, I was advising a coalition member‚ÄĒa rural clinic in the Delta‚ÄĒwhen they discovered their patient mental health records had been accessed by a third-party data broker without explicit consent, then sold to an insurance company that promptly denied a young mother's postpartum depression treatment as a "pre-existing condition." The clinic director called me in tears because she couldn't legally tell the patient what had happened.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ3067
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0098‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒMichael Wright‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe commenter argues that AI clinical decision tools should be subject to the same safety, efficacy, and transparency requirements as traditional medical devices, and that patients deserve to know when AI influences their treatment decisions and w...‚ôĒLast month my daughter switched rheumatologists because her insurance changed, and the new office couldn't get her records for three weeks. Three weeks without her medications adjusted. That's when I realized how fragile this whole system is, especially now that AI tools are making more decisions about patient care.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what I don't understand: we require traditional medical devices to prove they're safe and effective before doctors use them on patients. We want transparency about how they work, who tested them, what their limitations are. But AI clinical decision tools seem to be getting a different standard. If we're removing certification requirements for AI, we're essentially saying that a computer algorithm making recommendations about my daughter's treatment doesn't need the same safeguards as, say, a diagnostic imaging device. That doesn't make sense to me.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen my daughter's new rheumatologist considers switching her to a different medication, I want to know if an AI tool helped make that recommendation. I want to know what data trained that tool, whether it was tested on people like her, what it might get wrong. That's not bureaucratic burden. That's informed consent. And right now, if we loosen transparency rules for AI while keeping information blocking regulations loose, companies can move her data around between offices and insurance systems without real oversight, and we won't even know what's using it. We need consistent standards. AI tools making clinical decisions should meet the same bar as everything else.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-18T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-18T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving DSI transparency requirements creates a dangerous regulatory gap where AI-based clinical decision support tools face fewer disclosure obligations than traditional medical devices, undermining consistent safety standards.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒmedium‚ôĒfrustrated‚ôĒIllinois‚ôĒsocial worker‚ôĒ69‚ôĒLast month, my daughter had to switch rheumatologists because her insurance changed, and the new office said they couldn't get her records from the old one for three weeks‚ÄĒthree weeks where she went without her medications adjusted. But I'll tell you what worries me more: if those records start moving around too freely between offices and insurance companies without real oversight, I'm not sure who's actually protecting her information from being sold or shared in ways she didn't agree to.‚ôĒAI tools making clinical decisions about my daughter's care should have the same transparency and safety requirements as any other medical device‚ÄĒnot fewer. If we're removing DSI certification requirements, we're creating a two-tier system where AI gets a free pass. | When a doctor uses an AI tool to help decide my daughter's treatment, I need to know what that tool was trained on, who it was tested with, and what its blind spots are. That's not bureaucratic burden‚ÄĒthat's basic informed consent. | Deregulating AI transparency in healthcare while keeping information blocking rules loose means companies can move patient data around without real oversight, and we won't even know what AI systems are using it.‚ôĒA parent and social worker worried that removing AI transparency rules creates a dangerous gap where clinical decision support tools dodge the safety standards that protect patients like my daughter.‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ242
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0099‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒKenneth Martinez‚ôĒThe Society of Thoracic Surgeons‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Society of Thoracic Surgeons opposes the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures, arguing that eliminating these requirements poses unacceptable risk...‚ôĒ**Comment on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity**‚Źé‚Źé**Submitted by The Society of Thoracic Surgeons**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Society of Thoracic Surgeons writes to express serious concern about the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria, particularly the requirement for AI model cards and transparency disclosures. We have reviewed the Federal Register notice dated December 19, 2024, and the supporting materials from the Office of the National Coordinator. While we recognize the intent to reduce regulatory burden on health IT developers, the elimination of transparency requirements for clinical decision support systems poses unacceptable risks to patient safety and clinical accountability.‚Źé‚ŹéI need to start with something that happened in my own institution last month, because it illustrates why these requirements matter in ways that regulatory language sometimes obscures. We had a 68-year-old woman with a complex mediastinal mass. Before we could finalize our surgical approach, we needed imaging from a hospital system three hours away across the state line. The records request took nine days. By the time we had them, we'd already drafted an operative plan based on incomplete information. The imaging showed something our initial assessment had missed, which would have changed everything about how we accessed the tumor. It was a close call, and it worked out, but the fragmentation in how we access clinical data haunts me every time I think about it.‚Źé‚ŹéThe irony that's been stuck with me all week is this: while we're protecting privacy so aggressively that clinicians can't access consented patient imaging across state lines, a startup down the road has already purchased de-identified surgical outcome data from three states to build a commercial algorithm that we'll eventually be asked to use. We have no transparency into how that algorithm was trained, what populations it was validated on, what risks it carries, and the regulatory path forward seems to assume that's acceptable. Meanwhile, we can't even get timely access to our own patients' prior studies.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CURRENT DSI CERTIFICATION FRAMEWORK AND ITS OPERATIONAL VALUE**‚Źé‚ŹéThe existing DSI certification criteria, codified under 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1) and related requirements in the 21st Century Cures Act Information Blocking Rule (42 CFR 171), require AI and algorithmic decision support systems to include model cards documenting intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and evidence of external validation. This isn't abstract bureaucracy. These requirements exist because surgeons, cardiologists, and other clinicians need to know whether an algorithm was trained on a population that resembles their patients, whether its recommendations have been tested outside the original development environment, and what failures or biases have been documented.‚Źé‚ŹéI have been the executive director of The Society of Thoracic Surgeons for fifteen years. Our members practice across 1,200 hospitals in the United States. In that time, I have watched the clinical decision support landscape evolve from simple order-checking systems to complex machine learning models that influence everything from patient selection for surgery to operative planning. The transparency requirements that are now proposed for removal were not created by regulators who dislike innovation. They were created because clinicians and patient advocates insisted on them after a series of problems in the early 2010s, when commercial algorithms were deployed in hospitals without adequate transparency, and clinicians had no way to understand or explain their recommendations to patients.‚Źé‚ŹéOur organization has reviewed the proposed rule language carefully. The Office of the National Coordinator appears to be arguing that market competition and liability law will provide sufficient accountability for algorithmic decision support systems, and that transparency disclosures impose unnecessary compliance burden on developers. This reasoning troubles us because it misunderstands both the clinical environment and the information asymmetries that exist between developers and users.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen a cardiac surgeon is deciding whether to recommend transplantation for a patient with a failing heart, that surgeon will often consult a risk calculator or prognostic algorithm. The surgeon is not in a position to reverse-engineer that algorithm, run validation studies on it in their own institution, or independently verify the developer's claims about its accuracy. The surgeon has to trust either the developer's transparency or the regulatory framework. If both are removed, the surgeon has nothing. The patient certainly has nothing. This is not a situation where individual bargaining power or litigation after harm will adequately protect safety.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT THE REMOVAL WOULD MEAN IN PRACTICE**‚Źé‚ŹéLet me walk through what happens if the DSI certification requirement is removed. A health IT developer creates an algorithm to predict surgical site infection risk in thoracic patients. The developer trains it on 50,000 cases from a single large health system in the Northeast, predominantly white and insured. The algorithm performs well in that population. The developer does not conduct external validation in other geographic regions, other racial and ethnic groups, or different socioeconomic populations. Under the current framework, the model card would disclose these limitations. A hospital system considering adoption would see those limitations and either decline the system, demand additional validation, or implement it with appropriate caveats to clinicians.‚Źé‚ŹéUnder the proposed deregulatory framework, there is no requirement for that disclosure. The developer can market the system nationally. Hospitals adopt it. A rural hospital in Mississippi with a predominantly Black patient population begins using it. The algorithm performs differently in that population than it did in the Northeast training set, but nobody knows because there was no transparency requirement and no external validation requirement. Patients are harmed. The hospital and potentially the clinician face liability. But the patient harm has already happened.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not a hypothetical scenario. The FDA's evaluation of algorithmic bias in medical devices (summarized in their 2021 guidance on software validation) documented multiple instances of algorithms that performed acceptably in majority populations but failed in minority populations precisely because training data was not representative. The FDA's requirements for clinical validation of algorithms in diagnostic imaging, which remain in place, exist because the imaging community learned this lesson the hard way.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Society of Thoracic Surgeons submitted detailed comments on the development of the current DSI criteria in 2014 and again in 2015. At that time, we emphasized that transparency requirements would actually benefit developers by establishing clear expectations and reducing liability exposure. We also noted that the burden of creating a model card is not comparable to the burden of clinical validation. A model card is documentation. It requires the developer to articulate what they know about their own system. This is not an unreasonable ask.‚Źé‚Źé**THE INFORMATION ASYMMETRY PROBLEM AND WHY MARKET FORCES ARE INSUFFICIENT**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule seems to assume that if a health IT system causes harm, litigation and liability exposure will drive correction. This assumption fails to account for the information asymmetries in the healthcare technology market. A hospital purchasing a clinical decision support system does not have the technical expertise to evaluate algorithmic safety before deployment. The hospital has to rely on the developer's representations, independent testing, or regulatory disclosure requirements. If all three are removed, the hospital is in an impossible position.‚Źé‚ŹéMoreover, the liability model assumes that harm will be identifiable and traceable to the algorithm. In most clinical contexts, this is not true. A surgical patient has many risk factors. If an algorithm overestimates or underestimates risk, and a complication occurs, was it because of the algorithm or because of patient factors or surgical technique? Proving causation is extremely difficult. The algorithm can cause systematic bias affecting entire populations before any individual lawsuit makes it visible.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI criteria also require disclosure of training data characteristics. This matters because algorithmic bias in healthcare often stems from biased training data, not from the algorithm itself. If a developer trains a sepsis prediction algorithm on data from hospitals that have historically underdiagnosed sepsis in Black patients, the algorithm will inherit that bias. The model card requirement forces disclosure of this possibility. Without it, the bias remains hidden.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Society of Thoracic Surgeons has a particular concern about algorithms used in surgical risk assessment. Many of our members use risk calculators to discuss operative risk with patients before surgery. These conversations are ethically central to surgical practice. A patient has the right to understand the basis for risk estimates. If the algorithm was trained on a population different from the patient, the patient should know this. If the algorithm has not been validated in the patient's demographic group, the patient should know this. The model card requirement serves the patient's right to informed consent, not just the surgeon's clinical decision-making.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC CONCERNS ABOUT THE PROPOSED REMOVAL**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule does not clearly articulate which DSI criteria would be removed and which would remain. The Federal Register notice references "Decision Support Interventions" but does not provide the specific CFR citations or the exact language of the criteria being reconsidered. This ambiguity itself is problematic because it prevents stakeholders from providing precise feedback on which requirements are genuinely burdensome and which are essential to safety.‚Źé‚ŹéFrom our review of the supporting documents, it appears the proposal is to remove the requirement for model cards and transparency disclosures entirely, not to streamline them. If the intent is streamlining, we would support a conversation about making documentation requirements more efficient. For example, the current model card format requires certain disclosures but allows flexibility in how they are presented. It is not an onerous form. But if the intent is to eliminate transparency requirements altogether, we cannot support that.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule also references the need to reduce burden on small developers and startups. The Society understands this concern. Small companies do face compliance burdens that large companies can absorb more easily. But the solution is not to eliminate safety requirements. The solution is to calibrate requirements proportionally. A startup developing a low-risk clinical reminder system might have minimal documentation requirements. A startup developing an algorithm that directly influences patient selection for major surgery should have more substantial requirements. Risk-based tiering of requirements would reduce unnecessary burden while preserving safety oversight where it matters most.‚Źé‚ŹéWe note that the FDA's existing framework for software as a medical device (codified in 21 CFR 860 and related guidance) already incorporates risk-based approaches. The FDA does not impose the same requirements on a low-risk reminder system as it does on a high-risk diagnostic algorithm. A similar framework could be applied to the DSI certification criteria. This would preserve safety oversight while reducing burden on genuinely low-risk systems.‚Źé‚Źé**THE RELATIONSHIP TO BROADER INFORMATION BLOCKING RULES**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed deregulation of DSI certification criteria should not be considered in isolation. These criteria exist within a broader framework of information blocking rules, interoperability standards, and health IT certification requirements. The ONC has suggested that removing DSI certification requirements would promote competition and innovation by allowing developers to bring systems to market more quickly. But this analysis ignores the relationship between transparency and interoperability.‚Źé‚ŹéOne of the primary reasons for the DSI certification framework is to ensure that when clinical decision support systems are integrated with electronic health records and other health IT systems, they are transparent about their functioning and reliable in their outputs. If DSI systems are not certified, how will EHR vendors ensure that the algorithms they integrate behave as represented? How will hospitals conducting due diligence on purchased systems verify safety? The removal of DSI certification could actually impede interoperability by creating uncertainty about algorithm reliability.‚Źé‚ŹéThe 21st Century Cures Act, which established much of the current regulatory framework for health IT, explicitly includes clinical decision support as a domain requiring transparency and interoperability. Section 4001 of the Cures Act, codified at 42 USC 300jj-52, requires that certified health IT be transparent in how it uses algorithms and clinical logic. The proposed removal of DSI certification criteria appears to conflict with the statutory mandate.‚Źé‚Źé**PATIENT SAFETY AND INFORMED CONSENT**‚Źé‚ŹéThe fundamental issue, from our perspective, is patient safety and informed consent. When a surgeon discusses surgical risk with a patient, the surgeon is obligated to disclose material information that would affect the patient's decision. If the surgeon is relying on an algorithm to estimate risk, the patient should understand that the algorithm was trained on a specific population and may perform differently in their individual case. This is not a regulatory burden. It is an ethical obligation.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI model card requirement facilitates this obligation by ensuring that the information exists in documented form. Without it, the surgeon has to rely on whatever the algorithm developer chose to disclose, which may be incomplete or biased toward favorable characterization of the system.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Society of Thoracic Surgeons is aware that some health IT developers believe that transparency requirements limit their competitive advantage by requiring disclosure of proprietary information. We understand this concern. However, the model card requirement does not require disclosure of proprietary training methods or business-sensitive information. It requires disclosure of what the algorithm does, what population it was developed for, what risks are known, and what validation has been conducted. This is information that patients and clinicians have a right to know.‚Źé‚Źé**ALTERNATIVE APPROACHES TO REDUCING BURDEN**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the ONC is genuinely concerned about regulatory burden on developers, there are less destructive approaches than eliminating transparency requirements entirely. The Society would support the following alternatives:‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, the model card requirement could be streamlined by providing a standard template that developers can fill out efficiently. The current requirement allows flexibility in format, but some developers report uncertainty about what exactly needs to be documented. A clear template would reduce this uncertainty and compliance burden without eliminating transparency.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, the requirement could be risk-stratified. Low-risk systems such as educational alerts or clinical reminders might have minimal documentation requirements. High-risk systems such as algorithms that directly influence treatment recommendations might have more substantial requirements. This would focus compliance burden on systems where it matters most.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, the ONC could establish a safe harbor for developers who conduct adequate external validation and publish results. If a developer has validated their algorithm in multiple populations and published the results in peer-reviewed literature, the transparency documentation could be streamlined to reference the published validation. This would actually incentivize developers to conduct rigorous validation and publish results, which would improve the overall safety of clinical decision support systems.‚Źé‚ŹéFourth, the ONC could establish a tiered certification framework similar to the FDA's risk-based approach. Developers could choose between full certification with comprehensive documentation or a lighter-touch registration process that includes basic transparency disclosures. This would allow low-risk developers to participate in the health IT ecosystem with minimal burden, while ensuring that high-risk systems receive appropriate oversight.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION AND RECOMMENDATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Society of Thoracic Surgeons respectfully urges the Office of the National Coordinator to reconsider the proposed removal of DSI certification criteria, particularly the transparency and model card requirements. These requirements are not burdensome compliance theater. They are essential safeguards for patient safety and informed consent in an increasingly algorithm-dependent healthcare system.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the ONC proceeds with deregulation, we recommend the following conditions:‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criteria for transparency and model card documentation should be retained in some form. At minimum, developers should be required to disclose the intended use of their systems, the populations they were developed for, known risks and limitations, and evidence of external validation. This information should be accessible to hospital purchasers and clinicians using the systems.‚Źé‚ŹéAny removal of DSI certification requirements should be accompanied by explicit requirements for health IT vendors to conduct due diligence on integrated algorithms and to ensure transparency to end users. If the ONC will not mandate transparency, the responsibility falls to vendors and hospitals to insist on it.‚Źé‚ŹéThe ONC should establish a risk-stratified approach to DSI oversight that imposes heavier requirements on high-risk systems and lighter requirements on low-risk systems. This would preserve safety oversight where it matters most while reducing unnecessary burden on genuinely low-risk systems.‚Źé‚ŹéThe ONC should clarify how the proposed changes to DSI certification relate to the broader information blocking framework and the statutory requirements of the 21st Century Cures Act. There appears to be a tension between the deregulatory intent and the statutory mandate for transparency in clinical decision support.‚Źé‚ŹéThe ONC should convene a working group including clinicians, patient advocates, health IT developers, and patient safety experts to develop a risk-based framework for DSI oversight that balances innovation with safety. This should not be done through notice-and-comment rulemaking alone.‚Źé‚ŹéFinally, the ONC should consider the cumulative effect of removing multiple certification requirements simultaneously. Even if individual requirements seem burdensome, their collective removal could create a regulatory vacuum that leaves patients, clinicians, and hospitals without adequate information about algorithmic safety. The question is not whether any single requirement is essential. The question is whether the overall framework provides adequate accountability and transparency for clinical decision support systems that influence patient care.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Society of Thoracic Surgeons is committed to innovation in health IT and clinical decision support. We want developers to have the freedom to create new systems that improve patient outcomes. But innovation and safety are not in tension. They are complementary. The most successful health IT companies are those that have invested in safety, validation, and transparency. These investments build trust with clinicians and hospitals. Trust is what drives adoption and competitive success in healthcare.‚Źé‚ŹéWe remain available to work with the ONC on developing a framework that preserves the safety benefits of the current DSI certification criteria while reducing unnecessary compliance burden on developers. Thank you for considering these comments.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-15T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-15T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ9‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒurgent‚ôĒTennessee‚ôĒexecutive director‚ôĒ51‚ôĒLast month, I had to tell a patient's family that we couldn't access her prior imaging from a regional hospital system across the state line‚ÄĒimaging that would have changed our surgical approach‚ÄĒbecause the fragmented data environment made it impossible. That same week, I learned that a startup had already purchased de-identified surgical outcome data from three states to build an algorithm, yet we couldn't even get consented access to help our own patients. The irony haunts me: we're simultaneously protecting privacy so aggressively that clinicians can't help people, while allowing aggregated data to be commodified elsewhere entirely.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2912
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0100‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDonna Sanchez‚ôĒFreedom Home Health Care Inc.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA rural home health provider argues that ONC should retain transparency and validation requirements for Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification, as removing these mandates would disproportionately harm smaller providers and safety-net o...‚ôĒRe: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity; Comment on Decision Support Interventions Certification Criterion‚Źé‚ŹéFreedom Home Health Care Inc. is a rural healthcare provider based in Boise, Idaho, serving a five-county region with 47 full-time and part-time staff. We manage patient records, clinical workflows, and care coordination for approximately 2,100 active patients, most of whom are Medicare beneficiaries over age 65 with multiple chronic conditions. I've worked as the administrator here for eight years, and before that spent six years in health IT project management at a larger urban medical center. This vantage point‚ÄĒhaving experienced both sides of how EHR systems actually function in practice‚ÄĒshapes what I want to say about the proposed changes to Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria.‚Źé‚ŹéThe request for information asks whether certain certification criteria should be retained, revised, or removed. On the question of DSI certification requirements, specifically the transparency and accountability measures currently mandated in 45 CFR 170.315(a)(4), I need to be direct: removing or substantially weakening the requirement for AI model cards, intended use disclosures, target population specifications, known risks documentation, and external validation evidence would be a serious mistake for rural and safety-net providers like ours.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PROBLEM WE ACTUALLY FACE**‚Źé‚ŹéThree years ago, our previous vendor locked us into a five-year contract requiring implementation of their "certified" EHR platform. The system cost us $15,000 annually in licensing fees alone, plus another $8,000 to $12,000 yearly in implementation and support services. What we got in return was a one-size-fits-all clinical decision support module that generated alerts for every patient interaction‚ÄĒdrug interaction warnings that triggered even for interactions with zero clinical relevance to home-based care, age-appropriate screening recommendations that duplicated what our physicians had already documented, and medication recommendations that didn't account for the specific pharmacokinetics of elderly patients with renal impairment. The system wasn't wrong exactly, but it was designed for an acute care hospital setting, not for what we do. Our nurses spent 15 to 20 extra minutes per shift scrolling past irrelevant alerts, dismissing them without reading, because the signal-to-noise ratio was so poor that alert fatigue became our biggest problem. This is a real safety issue. When your clinicians start ignoring decision support because it cries wolf, you've made things worse, not better.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat bothered me most was that I had no way to understand why the system made the recommendations it did. The vendor's documentation said the DSI was "based on evidence-based guidelines" and "optimized for clinical accuracy," but I couldn't see the training data, couldn't see the algorithms, couldn't see any validation study specific to home health populations. When I asked the vendor's sales team for model cards or transparency documentation, they looked at me like I was speaking another language. They had a certification number from ONC, they said, so the system was safe and compliant. That was the extent of their transparency obligation.‚Źé‚ŹéLast spring, we finally found a smaller vendor whose system was designed specifically for home-based care. The alerts actually made sense. The recommendations accounted for our patient population. But we're still trapped in that original contract until 2026. We can't afford to pay for two systems simultaneously, so we're using the old system because we're contractually obligated to, even though we know it's suboptimal for our patients. Two of our best nurses left partly because of the frustration with that system. I think about that a lot.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY MODEL TRANSPARENCY MATTERS FOR SAFETY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed deregulation of DSI certification criteria assumes that removing transparency requirements will reduce burden on vendors and thereby increase competition and innovation. I understand the logic. What concerns me is that removing these requirements will actually make it harder for smaller providers like us to evaluate whether a system is safe and appropriate for our specific patient population.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen a health IT vendor tells us they've used "machine learning" to optimize clinical decision support, that's not information. It's marketing language. Machine learning can be trained on data that's not representative of our patients. The algorithms can be optimized for metrics that don't matter in a rural home health setting. They can embed biases from the training data that no one has bothered to check for. The only way we, as a provider, can make an informed decision about whether to adopt that system is to have access to model documentation that explains: What data was the model trained on? How was performance measured? Who tested it in the real world, and what was the result? What are the known limitations? What patient populations might not be well-served by this model?‚Źé‚ŹéWithout those disclosures, we're buying a black box. We have to trust that the vendor's certification badge means something. But if ONC removes the requirement for vendors to document and validate their AI decision support systems, that badge becomes less meaningful. It just means they passed some other standard, not necessarily that their DSI is safe and appropriate for our population.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider the example I mentioned: drug interaction alerts. A DSI trained primarily on inpatient pharmacy data might not have enough training examples of elderly patients with Stage 3 renal disease to properly calibrate the severity weighting for certain drug interactions. The model could be "certified" in a technical sense (it runs, it doesn't crash, it meets the EHR integration standards) but be poorly calibrated for home health patients. Without model cards and external validation evidence, we wouldn't know. Our nurses would just experience alert fatigue, and patient safety would suffer quietly until something went wrong.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Federal Register notice for the current DSI criterion (88 FR 44656, July 11, 2023) acknowledged that Decision Support Interventions are becoming more sophisticated and more reliant on AI and machine learning. The notice also acknowledged that transparency about how these systems work is necessary for providers to make informed decisions about implementation. That logic hasn't changed. If anything, it's more important now, as AI models become more complex and more prevalent in healthcare.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS ACTUALLY ACCOMPLISH**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be clear: I'm not arguing against innovation or against smaller vendors entering the market. Some of the best health IT solutions we've encountered come from smaller companies that are hungry to serve underserved markets. What I am arguing is that transparency requirements don't prevent innovation‚ÄĒthey enable better decision-making about which innovations to adopt.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen a vendor knows they have to document their DSI model, explain the training data, disclose known limitations, and provide evidence of external validation, they are incentivized to do better work. They have to think carefully about bias, about generalizability, about whether their system actually works for the populations they claim to serve. This is not a burden that kills innovation. It's a quality discipline. Any vendor serious about building a good product should be willing to do this work.‚Źé‚ŹéFor providers, transparency requirements mean we can actually evaluate systems on their merits. We can ask: Is this system trained on data similar to our patient population? Has it been validated in a setting comparable to ours? What are the documented error rates? What patient subgroups might this system not work well for? These are the questions that lead to better implementations, faster adoption, and ultimately better patient safety outcomes.‚Źé‚ŹéThe 2015 HITECH Act (42 U.S.C.
