Section 1320d-5) and subsequent ONC regulations on health IT certification were built on the principle that providers need sufficient information to make informed decisions about which systems to adopt. The DSI transparency requirements are consistent with that principle. Removing them would be a step backward.‚Źé‚Źé**THE REAL BURDEN PROBLEM**‚Źé‚ŹéNow, the deregulation proposal does identify a real burden issue, and I want to acknowledge that. Vendors‚ÄĒespecially smaller ones‚ÄĒdo spend time and money on certification and compliance activities. If those requirements are poorly designed or create redundancy, they should be fixed. But the solution is to improve the requirements, not to remove them entirely.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat I'd suggest: ONC should look at whether the model card requirements can be streamlined. Should vendors be required to provide a full card for every possible use case, or could they provide a single comprehensive card that covers the main clinical scenarios? Could the validation evidence requirements be satisfied with smaller studies, perhaps conducted by academic partners or by provider organizations using the system? Could vendors be allowed to rely on published literature rather than conducting entirely new validation studies?‚Źé‚ŹéThese are refinements, not eliminations. The core requirement‚ÄĒthat vendors disclose what their DSI does, how it was built, and what evidence supports its use‚ÄĒshould remain in place. Without it, the certification badge loses its meaning.‚Źé‚Źé**IMPACT ON RURAL AND SAFETY-NET PROVIDERS**‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what keeps me up at night: if DSI transparency requirements are removed, the vendors who will be most affected are the ones serving rural and safety-net providers like us. Large vendors have teams of data scientists and compliance officers. They can afford to document their models whether or not ONC requires it, because they have the internal capability and the resources to justify it to large health systems. Smaller vendors, especially those focused on underserved markets, will see removal of transparency requirements as permission to skip that work. And providers in rural areas, who already have limited options for health IT, will have even fewer ways to evaluate whether a system is appropriate for their population.‚Źé‚ŹéWe have 2,100 patients across five counties. The nearest large medical center is 45 minutes away by car. We can't easily switch EHR vendors. We have to live with whatever system we implement for years. That's why transparency matters to us. We need to know, before we commit, whether a system is actually designed for us or just marketed to us.‚Źé‚Źé**RECOMMENDATION**‚Źé‚ŹéMy recommendation is straightforward: retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion requiring disclosure of model cards, intended use specifications, target population documentation, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation evidence. If ONC determines that the current requirements are overly burdensome, revise them to streamline the process‚ÄĒallow shorter model cards, accept published literature as validation evidence, permit multi-use templates. But don't remove the requirement for transparency entirely.‚Źé‚ŹéThe cost of removing these requirements would fall hardest on providers like mine, and ultimately on patients in rural areas who already have fewer health IT options. The burden reduction would accrue primarily to vendors, and even then, mostly to large vendors with sophisticated development practices. That's not a good tradeoff.‚Źé‚ŹéI'll note that the ONC Certification Program Modifications and Improvements Proposal (published in the 2022 Cures Act rulemaking, 87 FR 65802) specifically acknowledged that DSI transparency is important for provider confidence and patient safety. The evidence supporting that position hasn't changed. If anything, it's stronger, given the increasing sophistication of AI in healthcare.‚Źé‚ŹéWe need health IT innovation. We need vendors to be able to serve small providers without drowning in compliance costs. But we also need to know that the systems we're implementing are safe and appropriate for our patients. Transparency requirements make that possible. Removing them would be a false economy.‚Źé‚ŹéFreedom Home Health Care Inc. respectfully urges ONC to retain the current Decision Support Interventions certification criterion and to resist the temptation to deregulate away the transparency requirements that make it meaningful.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-18T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-18T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ6‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHospital and health system procurement teams need transparent AI model information to make evidence-based decisions about which tools to adopt and how to implement them safely in their local contexts.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒhopeful‚ôĒIdaho‚ôĒhealthcare administrator‚ôĒ54‚ôĒWhen our small agency was locked into a vendor contract three years ago, we couldn't afford the certified EHR system they mandated‚ÄĒwe were paying $15,000 annually just for features our rural patients didn't need. Last spring, my director finally found a more flexible platform that actually works for our population, but we're still trapped in that original agreement until 2026. I've watched our best nurses leave because the system wastes their time, and I keep thinking about the smaller agencies in our region that never even got off the ground because they couldn't absorb those upfront licensing costs.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1872
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0101‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDavid Jones‚ôĒSAFI Clinical Informatics Group LLC‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒSAFI Clinical Informatics Group urges the ONC to retain Decision Support Interventions certification requirements for AI-based clinical tools, arguing that documentation of model validation, training data, and known limitations are essential safet...‚ôĒSAFI Clinical Informatics Group LLC, 1850 Riverside Drive, Columbus, Ohio 43215‚Źé‚ŹéSubmitted to: Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology‚ŹéFederal Register Docket: [ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions]‚Źé‚ŹéRE: Preservation of Decision Support Interventions Certification Criterion and AI Transparency Requirements‚Źé‚ŹéWe appreciate the opportunity to comment on the proposed modifications to health IT certification requirements. SAFI Clinical Informatics Group has spent the last eight years working directly with mid-sized health systems and independent practices to implement and optimize EHR systems, and our perspective on this rule comes from the ground level, where these requirements actually affect clinical workflows and patient safety.‚Źé‚ŹéOur concern is straightforward but significant: the proposed elimination or substantial weakening of certification criteria for Decision Support Interventions (DSI), particularly those involving artificial intelligence, would remove essential safeguards at the moment when AI is becoming embedded in clinical decision-making across healthcare. We are not opposed to reducing unnecessary regulatory burden. We support streamlining where it makes sense. But there is a meaningful difference between removing compliance theater and removing protections that actually matter. The DSI criterion requiring model cards, transparency disclosures, and validation documentation is the latter.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PROBLEM WE'VE OBSERVED IN THE FIELD**‚Źé‚ŹéLet me ground this in something concrete. Last year, our largest client, a 180-bed regional hospital in central Ohio, migrated from one EHR system to another. The transition required recertification of multiple health IT modules, including their clinical decision support tools. Because certification processes were already cumbersome, the hospital couldn't move quickly enough to avoid six months of parallel operations, interim workarounds, and temporary staffing costs that we calculated at roughly $340,000. That's money that could have gone to equipment, training, or additional nursing staff. Instead, it went to compliance friction.‚Źé‚ŹéI mention this because I understand the case for deregulation. I see the cost impact in real dollars. But I also watched what happened during that transition period, and it taught me something important: the certification process, for all its inefficiencies, was the only systematic mechanism ensuring that the decision support tools they were switching to had documented validation and transparent risk profiles. Once they were off the old system and onto the new one, there was no structured way to verify that the AI-assisted diagnostic tools had been tested on patient populations similar to their own, or that the vendors had disclosed what data they were trained on, or what known limitations existed.‚Źé‚ŹéThe hospital's IT director told us, "We had to trust the vendor. There was no independent verification." That statement stuck with me because it articulated exactly why the DSI criterion, whatever its procedural inefficiencies, serves a function that deregulation alone cannot replace.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THE DSI CRITERION MATTERS NOW**‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criterion as currently structured requires vendors to provide model cards and transparency documentation that includes intended use cases, target population specifications, known risks, training data characteristics, and results from external validation testing. This is not onerous in the way that some compliance requirements are. It is not asking vendors to do something they should not be doing anyway.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat it does is create a documented record that clinicians and hospital administrators can review before deploying these tools. It establishes accountability. It creates a paper trail. In healthcare, where a miscalibrated algorithm can lead to misdiagnosis, a paper trail is not bureaucratic overhead. It is a safety mechanism.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider the regulatory history here. The FDA has moved incrementally toward more oversight of AI in clinical devices, not less. In 2021, the FDA issued guidance on modifications to AI/ML-based software as a medical device, recognizing that these tools require ongoing monitoring and validation (FDA Guidance Document: Proposed Regulatory Framework for Modifications to AI/ML-Based Software as a Medical Device, January 2021). The agency's position reflects a consensus among medical informaticists that AI-assisted clinical tools are sufficiently consequential that their development and deployment should be transparent and documented. Removing certification requirements for DSI would move health IT regulation backward relative to this trajectory.‚Źé‚ŹéMore broadly, the 21st Century Cures Act explicitly required the Office of the National Coordinator to establish certification criteria that protect patient safety and promote interoperability. The DSI criterion does both. It protects safety by ensuring documented validation and transparency. It promotes interoperability by requiring vendors to disclose technical specifications and target populations, which helps hospitals understand whether a tool will work in their environment.‚Źé‚Źé**A DISTINCTION WORTH MAKING**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be clear about what we are saying and what we are not saying. We are not arguing that every certification criterion should be preserved as-is. We are not claiming that the current process is efficient or that there are no opportunities to streamline. In fact, the certification process for DSI could probably be accelerated without weakening its core function. A more agile approval pathway, perhaps similar to FDA's breakthrough device program, might allow vendors to bring tools to market faster while maintaining transparency requirements.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat we are arguing is that the specific requirement for documented model cards, validation data, and risk disclosure should survive any deregulatory effort. These are not compliance checkboxes. They are the baseline information that clinicians need to make informed decisions about whether to use an AI-assisted tool, and they are the baseline information that regulators need to perform post-market surveillance if problems emerge.‚Źé‚ŹéThe distinction matters because there are parts of health IT certification that do seem designed more for compliance than for safety. Interoperability testing requirements have sometimes been performed in ways that do not reflect real-world integration challenges. Documentation requirements have occasionally been written in language that is vague enough to create ambiguity about what is actually being certified. Those are fair targets for streamlining.‚Źé‚ŹéBut transparency about how an AI model was built, what it was tested on, and what its known limitations are? That is not compliance theater. That is foundational information.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT HAPPENS WHEN THIS INFORMATION IS NOT AVAILABLE**‚Źé‚ŹéThis is worth spending time on because the case for deregulation often rests on the assumption that market forces will create adequate incentives for transparency. They do not. We have seen this play out in vendor interactions.‚Źé‚ŹéSeveral times over the past three years, our organization has worked with health systems evaluating AI-backed clinical decision support tools. In most cases, when we asked vendors for detailed information about model training data, validation methodologies, and known performance gaps in specific patient subgroups, we received one of three responses:‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, some vendors said they did not have that information in a form they could readily share, which usually meant they had not done the analysis themselves. Second, some vendors provided information but marked it as proprietary and confidential, which made it impossible for the health system's own data science team to independently verify whether the tool would perform adequately in their population. Third, and most troubling, some vendors simply declined to answer, positioning this as a competitive differentiation issue.‚Źé‚ŹéIn a functioning market, health systems would respond to this opacity by not buying the product. But health systems are under enormous pressure to adopt AI tools because competitors are doing so, because they believe it will improve efficiency, and because there is a sense that this is the direction healthcare is heading. That is not a strong enough market signal to force transparency. Clinicians and administrators are making decisions based on vendor marketing and reputation, not on documented evidence of validation and known risks.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criterion is the mechanism that breaks this dynamic. It says to vendors: if you want to sell this tool to hospitals that care about accreditation and compliance, you have to provide this information. It creates a floor for transparency that the market alone has not created.‚Źé‚Źé**THE SPECIFIC RECOMMENDATION**‚Źé‚ŹéHere is what we believe should happen:‚Źé‚ŹéThe Decision Support Interventions certification criterion should be retained in its current form, with the following possible modifications to improve efficiency without weakening substance:‚Źé‚Źé1. Establish a streamlined review pathway for DSI tools that meet predefined risk criteria. Low-risk tools (those with narrow, well-defined use cases and evidence of external validation in similar populations) could be approved on an expedited timeline, perhaps 60 days rather than the current process timeline.‚Źé‚Źé2. Allow vendors to submit validation data from published literature or from external research organizations rather than requiring all validation to be conducted in-house. This would reduce costs for smaller vendors while maintaining the substance of the requirement.‚Źé‚Źé3. Create a tiered transparency requirement based on the clinical impact of the tool. A decision support tool that flags potential drug interactions requires less intensive documentation than one that provides diagnostic recommendations. The core requirement for model cards and intended use documentation should apply across all tiers, but the depth of validation data could scale with risk.‚Źé‚Źé4. Establish clear technical standards for what constitutes adequate documentation of training data characteristics and external validation results. This would reduce the back-and-forth between vendors and ONC staff and create more predictability in the approval process.‚Źé‚ŹéThese modifications would address legitimate concerns about regulatory efficiency without removing the substantive requirement that vendors document and disclose how their tools work.‚Źé‚Źé**ACCOUNTABILITY AND THE LIMITS OF DEREGULATION**‚Źé‚ŹéOne argument we hear frequently is that vendors are incentivized to be transparent because they face liability if their tools cause harm. This is theoretically true but practically limited. Liability protections, including limitations on liability in vendor contracts, are standard in health IT. More importantly, establishing causation between a specific vendor's tool and patient harm is often extremely difficult in a clinical environment where multiple factors influence outcomes.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification requirement does not eliminate the need for liability and ongoing monitoring. It complements those mechanisms by creating documented evidence of what a vendor knew about their tool at the time of deployment. This evidence becomes crucial if a problem emerges later. It creates accountability not just for patient harm, but for the integrity of the deployment process itself.‚Źé‚ŹéThere is also a question about who bears the cost of transparency. If vendors are not required to document their tools, then health systems bear the cost of trying to figure out how the tools work, whether they will work in their population, and what risks they present. That cost is not eliminated by deregulation. It is shifted. Given that large health systems have more resources to conduct independent validation and risk assessment than smaller practices, this shift actually increases inequity in healthcare. Smaller practices would be left to trust vendor claims without the resources to verify them.‚Źé‚Źé**INTERNATIONAL CONTEXT AND REGULATORY CONVERGENCE**‚Źé‚ŹéIt is worth noting that other jurisdictions are moving toward more stringent requirements for AI transparency in clinical settings, not less. The European Union's AI Act establishes requirements for high-risk AI systems, including those used in healthcare, that go substantially beyond current U.S. requirements. These include documentation of training data, testing procedures, and known risks. Canada's regulatory approach to AI in healthcare is evolving similarly.‚Źé‚ŹéThe argument that U.S. deregulation is necessary to keep pace with international competition assumes that the competitive advantage lies in regulatory laxity. In fact, the competitive advantage in health IT increasingly lies in the ability to demonstrate that tools are safe, effective, and transparent. Health systems globally are looking for assurance, not minimal documentation. A U.S. vendor with documented, transparent tools that comply with rigorous standards is actually better positioned in international markets than a vendor that relies on light-touch regulation.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCERNS ABOUT THE BROADER DEREGULATORY APPROACH**‚Źé‚ŹéWe want to be careful here because we recognize that the broader deregulatory initiative has legitimate goals. If the goal is to reduce unnecessary burden on developers and providers, and to accelerate innovation in health IT, those are worthy objectives. We support them.‚Źé‚ŹéBut there is a risk that deregulation becomes a vehicle for eliminating requirements that are inconvenient rather than requirements that are genuinely unnecessary. DSI certification is inconvenient for vendors because it requires them to do validation work that they should be doing anyway. That inconvenience is not a reason to eliminate the requirement.‚Źé‚ŹéThere is also a risk that the deregulatory process proceeds too quickly without adequate input from providers and clinicians about what information they actually need to make safe deployment decisions. We would encourage the ONC to slow down on DSI modifications specifically and conduct a more detailed stakeholder process with health systems, patient safety organizations, and clinical informaticists before proceeding.‚Źé‚Źé**IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the ONC does decide to proceed with modifications to DSI requirements, we would recommend:‚Źé‚Źé1. Grandfather existing certified tools. Do not require already-certified tools to be recertified under new criteria, as this would create perverse incentives for vendors to avoid updating tools.‚Źé‚Źé2. Establish a public database of DSI tools and their documented validation status. This would allow health systems to easily compare transparency across vendors and create market pressure for disclosure even beyond what is formally required.‚Źé‚Źé3. Create a mechanism for post-market surveillance of DSI tools, perhaps through a registry where health systems report on their actual performance in clinical use. This would compensate for any reduction in pre-market validation requirements by increasing visibility into real-world performance.‚Źé‚Źé4. Require vendors to document any substantial changes to their algorithms or training data. Deregulation should not mean that vendors can modify tools continuously without transparency about what has changed.‚Źé‚Źé**OUR POSITION IN THE BROADER MARKETPLACE**‚Źé‚ŹéWe should acknowledge where we sit in this ecosystem because it shapes our perspective. SAFI Clinical Informatics Group is a small organization. We work with mid-sized health systems and independent practices, not with large integrated delivery networks. We benefit when regulation creates barriers to entry for large vendors because our clients cannot afford the most expensive, sophisticated tools and rely on smaller vendors that provide good value.‚Źé‚ŹéDeregulation could benefit us. Smaller vendors might be able to bring tools to market more quickly without expensive certification processes. Our clients might have access to cheaper tools faster.‚Źé‚ŹéBut we have also seen what happens when transparency requirements are weak. We have watched health systems make poor decisions about tool deployment because they did not have adequate information. We have watched vendors cut corners on validation because they could. We have seen patient safety compromised not because the tools were inherently bad, but because they were deployed in settings where they had not been validated and without any mechanism for understanding how they would perform.‚Źé‚ŹéOur position, stated simply, is that the cost of preserving the DSI requirement is worth paying, even if it slows down some vendor innovation. The benefit of ensuring that clinicians have documented information about how AI tools work is worth the inconvenience to vendors.‚Źé‚Źé**FINAL THOUGHTS ON THE PROCESS AND THE STAKES**‚Źé‚ŹéThis comment has focused on DSI specifically because that is where we think the stakes are highest. But the broader pattern in the deregulatory initiative concerns us. There seems to be an assumption that regulation is inherently a drag on innovation and that removing rules will automatically create better outcomes. This assumption is not always correct.‚Źé‚ŹéSometimes rules exist because they solve real problems. Sometimes the burden they create is justified by the problems they prevent. Sometimes the answer to bad regulation is not deregulation but better regulation.‚Źé‚ŹéThe health IT certification process needs improvement. It is slow, it is sometimes unclear, and it can create compliance burdens that do not proportionally improve safety. But the solution is not to eliminate requirements that actually matter. The solution is to streamline the process while preserving the substance.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI criterion requires vendors to document and disclose how their AI tools work. This is a requirement that should survive deregulation. It protects patient safety, it supports informed clinical decision-making, and it creates accountability. The cost of preserving it is modest compared to the benefit.‚Źé‚ŹéWe would urge the ONC to reconsider the proposed elimination of DSI requirements and instead pursue a path of modernization and streamlining that maintains the core safeguards while improving the efficiency of the certification process.‚Źé‚ŹéRespectfully submitted,‚Źé‚ŹéDavid Jones‚ŹéPractice Manager‚ŹéSAFI Clinical Informatics Group LLC‚ŹéColumbus, Ohio‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-27T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-27T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ8‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒresigned‚ôĒOhio‚ôĒpractice manager‚ôĒ38‚ôĒWhen our largest client switched EHR systems last year, they spent six months dealing with certification bottlenecks that locked them into expensive workarounds‚ÄĒbasically paying a premium to avoid compliance delays while their smaller competitors had already moved on. I watched them hemorrhage money on interim solutions, and it made me realize how the certification framework inadvertently protects the incumbents while smaller practices like ours get stuck absorbing transition costs that bigger vendors never have to worry about.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2676
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0102‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒAmanda Thompson‚ôĒURAALI Refaluwasch Association‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe URAALI Refaluwasch Association opposes proposed deregulation that would weaken Decision Support Intervention (DSI) certification requirements, arguing that removing mandatory model card and transparency disclosures will increase algorithmic bi...‚ôĒRE: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity‚ŹéDocket No. OS 2025-[XXX]‚Źé‚ŹéURAALI Refaluwasch Association submits this comment on the proposed deregulatory framework affecting Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria and related transparency requirements. Our organization serves tribal communities and rural populations across South Dakota and surrounding regions, with particular focus on mental health, behavioral health integration, and health equity in underserved areas. Over the past fifteen years, we have worked directly with 47 tribal health clinics and community health centers, supporting care coordination for roughly 8,400 patients annually. This perspective shapes our understanding of what happens when oversight mechanisms weaken without corresponding safeguards.‚Źé‚Źé**THE REAL COST OF REDUCED TRANSPARENCY IN AI-ASSISTED CLINICAL TOOLS**‚Źé‚ŹéLast month, I sat with a Lakota woman from our service area whose teenage daughter's mental health records were accessed by a data broker without her knowledge or consent. The daughter only discovered this breach when she began receiving targeted advertisements for psychiatric medications on social media. When the mother tried to find out who accessed the records and why, she encountered a labyrinth of vendor agreements, third-party data sales, and regulatory gaps that made accountability nearly impossible. The current framework gave us almost no meaningful recourse. But here is what concerns me most: loosening oversight on Decision Support Interventions and related AI model transparency will make it far easier for violations like this to happen at scale, and those impacts will fall disproportionately on the communities we serve, who have the least resources to fight back or even recognize they've been harmed.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule appears designed to reduce certification burden on health IT developers by removing or significantly relaxing requirements for model cards, transparency disclosures about intended use, target populations, known risks, and training data characteristics. I understand the desire to reduce unnecessary red tape. I do. But what the rule describes as "burden" is actually a set of guardrails that exist because patients and clinicians have been harmed in the absence of them. When a clinical AI tool fails to disclose that it was trained on data from predominantly white, insured populations, and then that tool is deployed in a rural Indian Health Service clinic, the mismatch between training population and real-world users is not a regulatory inconvenience. It is a patient safety issue.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Office of the National Coordinator (ONC) has long recognized that transparency in health IT serves two functions: it protects patients and it enables clinicians to make informed decisions about whether to trust a tool and how to use it responsibly. The 2015 ONC certification rules established AI model card requirements precisely because stakeholders had documented cases where opaque algorithms produced biased recommendations, failed to account for comorbidities common in specific populations, or made predictions that diverged significantly from clinical reality when deployed outside their validation contexts. See 45 CFR 170.315(a)(1) through (b)(2) (requiring certification of clinical decision support and related health IT modules). The proposed rule would weaken these requirements substantially.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY MODEL CARDS AND TRANSPARENCY DISCLOSURES MATTER FOR PATIENT SAFETY**‚Źé‚ŹéConsider what a clinician actually needs to know before deploying an AI-assisted diagnostic or risk assessment tool in their clinic. A family medicine provider in a rural South Dakota clinic needs to know: What population was this tool developed and tested on? Does it account for the specific health profiles of my patients, many of whom have high rates of type 2 diabetes, alcoholism, and trauma-related mental health conditions? What are the known failure modes? Has it been validated in settings similar to mine? What are the intended use cases, and where should I not use it? If I rely on this tool's recommendation and a patient is harmed, what documentation will help me demonstrate that I used it appropriately?‚Źé‚ŹéThese questions are not administrative obstacles. They are the foundation of informed clinical decision-making. When a DSI tool lacks a transparent model card, clinicians operate in the dark. They may overestimate the tool's accuracy because they don't know the confidence intervals. They may miss that the tool performs poorly for patients over age 65, or for patients with certain comorbidities, because that limitation was never disclosed. They may not realize that the training data came from a health system where 82% of patients were white and insured, while their patient population is 60% Native American and 70% uninsured.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule's language suggests that removing these requirements will "unleash innovation" by reducing what it characterizes as excessive compliance costs. But innovation that produces harm is not actually innovation. It is market failure with a new label. The research literature on algorithmic bias in healthcare has documented case after case where the absence of transparency requirements led to systems that reproduced or amplified existing health disparities. A 2019 study published in Health Affairs found that commercial risk prediction algorithms systematically underestimated the health needs of Black patients by up to 40%, producing recommendations that perpetuated existing inequities in care allocation (Obermeyer et al., "Dissecting racial bias in an algorithm used to manage the health of populations," Health Affairs 38, no. 8 [2019]: 1357-1366). That algorithm was not required to disclose its training data characteristics or known limitations related to race. The result was measurable harm to patients.‚Źé‚ŹéIn our own work, we have seen health IT systems that were designed without accounting for the specific health profiles of tribal populations. A clinic we work with adopted a diabetes risk assessment tool that was trained on a dataset where fewer than 3% of participants were Native American. When the tool was deployed in their clinic, it systematically underestimated risk in their patient population, leading to delayed interventions for patients who were actually at very high risk. The clinic staff discovered this only because they did careful manual review and noticed the pattern. If they had simply trusted the algorithm, patients would have been harmed. The problem was that the tool came with no transparent documentation of its training data characteristics, so the clinic had no way to know in advance that it would perform poorly in their context.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC CONCERNS WITH THE PROPOSED CHANGES**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule's language would remove the requirement that DSI tools include model cards and transparency disclosures describing: (1) the intended use and clinical context; (2) the target population and any populations explicitly excluded from the training or validation data; (3) known risks and failure modes; (4) the characteristics of the training data and external validation datasets; and (5) performance metrics across relevant demographic subgroups. The rule would replace these requirements with what amounts to voluntary disclosure and post-market surveillance. This is insufficient.‚Źé‚ŹéHere is why. Post-market surveillance depends on clinicians or patients recognizing that something has gone wrong and reporting it. But in the context of subtle algorithmic bias, harm may not be obvious. If a risk assessment tool slightly underestimates risk in a specific population, the effect is not a sudden failure that someone notices immediately. It is a drift in outcomes over time. Patients may not get the intensity of intervention they need, but that absence is harder to detect than an active error. By the time harm becomes obvious enough to report, hundreds or thousands of patients may have been affected.‚Źé‚ŹéVoluntary disclosure creates perverse incentives. Developers who want to maximize market adoption have little motivation to prominently advertise the limitations of their tools. If disclosure is optional, they will disclose minimally. If it is required, they will disclose completely. The difference matters enormously for patient safety.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule appears to assume that market competition will ensure quality and safety. But markets work efficiently only when consumers have adequate information and can meaningfully choose among alternatives. Clinicians do not have adequate information about the performance characteristics of AI tools when those characteristics are not disclosed. And clinicians often have limited ability to choose alternatives because of existing IT investments, vendor contracts, and workflow integration. A clinic that has already integrated a particular DSI tool into their EHR has significant switching costs, which reduces their ability to shop for better alternatives if problems emerge.‚Źé‚Źé**OUR SPECIFIC RECOMMENDATION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe URAALI Refaluwasch Association recommends that the final rule retain the current DSI certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures. Specifically, any DSI tool should be required to include, at a minimum:‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ A clear statement of intended use and clinical context, including any restrictions on use or populations where the tool should not be used.‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ Explicit documentation of the training and validation datasets, including the demographic characteristics, geographic sources, and clinical settings represented in those datasets.‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ Performance metrics stratified by relevant demographic groups, including age, race, ethnicity, gender identity, insurance status, and language preference, where applicable. This is not optional; it is essential for identifying disparities.‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ A description of known limitations, failure modes, and conditions under which the tool's recommendations may be unreliable.‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ Information about external validation: has this tool been tested in clinical settings different from where it was developed? If so, how did performance change?‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ A statement about the data governance and whether the tool is used for secondary purposes beyond its stated clinical function, such as risk stratification for resource allocation or insurance eligibility decisions.‚Źé‚ŹéThese requirements are not burdensome. Responsible developers already maintain this information internally because they need it for quality assurance and liability protection. The requirement simply ensures that this information is disclosed to the users and to regulators. The compliance cost is near zero; the benefit to patient safety is substantial.‚Źé‚Źé**ADDRESSING THE EQUITY DIMENSION**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be direct about why this matters for the populations we serve. Tribal communities and rural populations have historical reasons to be skeptical of health systems and health IT. These are not paranoid concerns. They are grounded in documented experiences of research exploitation, data misuse, and healthcare disparities. When a health IT system claims to be "evidence-based" but provides no transparent documentation of what evidence it is based on, it reinforces existing distrust. When a tool's performance characteristics have never been validated in tribal health settings but is deployed there anyway, it perpetuates the pattern of treating tribal communities as afterthoughts in healthcare innovation.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current transparency requirements exist partly to address this legacy. They create a mechanism for communities to ask hard questions about whether a tool was designed and tested with them in mind, or whether they are simply the latest venue for deploying something that was optimized for a different population. Removing these requirements will make it easier for that pattern to continue unchecked.‚Źé‚ŹéI will also note that the proposed rule's focus on "deregulation" assumes that less regulation is always better. But in healthcare, regulation often exists because someone was harmed. The information blocking rule exists because providers were deliberately making health data inaccessible. The transparency requirements for DSI tools exist because algorithms were producing biased recommendations. These are not arbitrary impositions. They are responses to documented failures. Before removing them, the rule should address what safeguards will replace them. The proposed answer of "market forces and voluntary disclosure" is not adequate for patient safety in underserved communities.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe URAALI Refaluwasch Association does not oppose innovation in health IT. We support it. But we support innovation that is informed by evidence, that accounts for diverse patient populations, and that is transparent about its limitations. The current DSI certification requirements, including model card and transparency disclosure mandates, represent a reasonable balance between enabling innovation and protecting patients. They should be retained.‚Źé‚ŹéSpecifically, we urge the Department of Health and Human Services and the Office of the National Coordinator to maintain the requirement that all DSI tools provide comprehensive model cards and transparency disclosures before certification, including documentation of training data characteristics, intended use, known limitations, and performance across demographic subgroups. This is not a burden on developers. It is a baseline for responsible innovation in healthcare.‚Źé‚ŹéThe case I described at the beginning of this comment, the teenager whose mental health records were accessed without consent, represents exactly the kind of harm that transparency requirements are designed to prevent. We need more accountability for health data practices, not less. And we need that accountability to be meaningful for the communities that have the least power to protect themselves.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-10T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-10T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ6‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒconcerned‚ôĒSouth Dakota‚ôĒpolicy director‚ôĒ44‚ôĒLast month, I sat with a Lakota woman from our service area whose teenage daughter's mental health records were accessed by a data broker without consent‚ÄĒshe only discovered it when the girl started receiving targeted ads for psychiatric medications. When I tried to help her pursue accountability, I hit a wall: the current regulatory framework gave us almost no recourse, but I worry that loosening oversight further will simply make it easier for these violations to happen at scale, particularly among the communities we serve who have the least ability to fight back.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2044
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0103‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒSharon Clark‚ôĒURAALI Refaluwasch Association‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒURAALI Refaluwasch Association opposes removing certification criteria and transparency requirements for AI-driven clinical decision support systems, arguing that model cards and disclosure requirements are essential for clinicians to assess wheth...‚ôĒRE: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity‚ŹéDocket No. HHS-OS-2025-0001‚Źé‚ŹéURAALI Refaluwasch Association submits this comment in response to the proposed rule removing certification criteria and transparency requirements for Artificial Intelligence-driven Decision Support Interventions (DSI). We represent patient advocacy stakeholders across New York and surrounding regions, and we have serious reservations about eliminating the current model card and transparency disclosure requirements that are foundational to safe clinical deployment of AI systems in healthcare settings.‚Źé‚ŹéBACKGROUND AND ORGANIZATIONAL PERSPECTIVE‚Źé‚ŹéURAALI has spent the past five years working directly with patients navigating complex chronic illnesses, particularly autoimmune conditions where diagnostic delay and clinical uncertainty create real harm. Our membership includes individuals with lupus, rheumatoid arthritis, and related systemic diseases. When my sister received her lupus diagnosis three years ago, she spent months trying to piece together her medical history from four different providers because none of them had access to unified records. What struck me then, and what stays with me now, is how vulnerable she was during that diagnostic uncertainty. She was being seen by a rheumatologist, her primary care physician, a nephrologist, and an urgent care clinic, and nobody had the full picture. That fragmentation meant missed connections, repeated tests, and months of her life spent in the dark about what was happening to her body.‚Źé‚ŹéI raise this because we are now at a different kind of inflection point. Instead of fragmented data across separate systems, we are moving toward a world where data flows more freely, which sounds beneficial in theory. But we cannot decouple that data flow from the systems making decisions about it. If we remove transparency requirements around AI models that will increasingly assist in diagnosing conditions like lupus, we are asking patients to trust black-box systems without knowing how those systems were built, what populations they were tested on, or what risks they carry for their specific circumstances.‚Źé‚ŹéTHE CASE FOR RETAINING DSI CERTIFICATION CRITERIA‚Źé‚ŹéThe current Decision Support Interventions certification criterion, codified in 45 CFR Part 170.315(b)(1), requires developers to provide model cards and transparency disclosures that include intended use statements, target population specifications, known risks and limitations, training data characteristics, and external validation results. We urge ASTP and ONC to retain this requirement rather than move toward voluntary disclosure or eliminate it entirely.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not an abstract concern about transparency for its own sake. Model cards serve a concrete function in clinical practice. When a rheumatologist is considering whether to rely on an AI system to help interpret autoimmune serologies or imaging findings, that clinician needs to know whether the training data included adequate representation of patients who look like the one sitting in front of them. Lupus presents differently across racial and ethnic groups. It is more common and more severe in Black and Hispanic populations, yet it is frequently missed in these same populations because diagnostic criteria were historically developed using predominantly white patient cohorts. An AI model trained primarily on imaging or lab data from white patients with lupus may perform differently, sometimes dangerously differently, when applied to Black or Hispanic patients. Without access to training data characteristics and external validation results, a clinician has no way to assess that risk.‚Źé‚ŹéThe transparency disclosures required under current DSI certification also matter for informed patient decision-making. Patients deserve to know whether an AI system assisting in their diagnosis was validated on a population similar to theirs. They deserve to understand the known limitations. This is not regulatory overreach; it is basic informed consent translated into the AI era. The Common Rule at 45 CFR Part 46 requires that subjects in research understand the foreseeable risks and benefits of research activities. If an AI model is being used to inform clinical decisions that affect patient care, patients should have similar visibility into potential risks.‚Źé‚ŹéWe recognize that ASTP and ONC are concerned about regulatory burden on developers. We also understand that voluntary compliance regimes sometimes feel lighter on the surface. But voluntary approaches to AI transparency in healthcare have failed elsewhere. The FDA's proposed framework for AI as a Medical Device relies heavily on voluntary reporting and post-market surveillance. Early evidence suggests that manufacturers often interpret voluntary requirements narrowly, disclosing only what they believe creates minimal liability rather than what clinicians and patients actually need to know. The healthcare system cannot afford that approach with AI systems that inform diagnosis and treatment.‚Źé‚ŹéSPECIFIC CONCERNS ABOUT DEREGULATION WITHOUT GUARDRAILS‚Źé‚ŹéWhat troubles us most is that the proposed rule seems to assume that market competition alone will drive responsible AI deployment. In healthcare, market dynamics do not always align with patient safety. A developer who cuts corners on external validation to bring a product to market faster will have a competitive advantage over one who invests in rigorous testing across diverse populations. Without minimum certification standards, that developer will win market share. Clinicians and patients will then be the ones bearing the risk.‚Źé‚ŹéWe have also seen how quickly deregulation in healthcare can become irreversible. Once transparency requirements are removed and developers stop building model cards into their workflows, reintroducing those requirements later becomes exponentially harder. Institutional inertia sets in. We would be trading a known, manageable regulatory framework for an experimental approach with no clear path to restoration if problems emerge.‚Źé‚ŹéThe rule references 21 U.S.C. Section 360k preemption doctrine and suggests that state-level requirements would be preempted, yet simultaneously proposes removing federal baseline standards. This leaves a regulatory void. Patients and clinicians will have less protection, not more, under this approach.‚Źé‚ŹéRECOMMENDATION‚Źé‚ŹéWe urge ASTP and ONC to retain the current DSI certification criterion at 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1) in its entirety. If the agencies believe some aspects of the requirement are burdensome, we recommend targeted revision rather than elimination. For instance, model card formats could be streamlined, or the scope of external validation could be clarified. But the core requirement that developers disclose intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results should remain.‚Źé‚ŹéWe also recommend that ASTP and ONC consider requiring periodic updates to model cards as new evidence about model performance emerges, particularly in underrepresented populations. This reflects the reality that AI systems in healthcare do not remain static; they are updated, retrained, and redeployed. Transparency requirements should evolve with them.‚Źé‚ŹéFinally, we recommend that the agencies convene a working group including patient advocates, clinicians, and developers to establish best practices for AI transparency in clinical settings before finalizing this rule. The stakes are too high for a purely deregulatory approach.‚Źé‚ŹéThank you for the opportunity to comment.‚Źé‚ŹéRespectfully submitted,‚Źé‚ŹéSharon Clark‚ŹéSenior Policy Analyst‚ŹéURAALI Refaluwasch Association‚ŹéNew York‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-12T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-12T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ3‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒconcerned‚ôĒNew York‚ôĒsenior policy analyst‚ôĒ37‚ôĒWhen my sister was diagnosed with lupus three years ago, we spent months piecing together her medical history from four different providers because no one had access to a unified record‚ÄĒbut now I'm watching this pendulum swing toward a system where that same fragmented data could flow into commercial databases with minimal accountability, and I keep thinking about her vulnerability during those months when she was most afraid, most in need of protection.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1112
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0104‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒEdward Jones‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTransparency requirements for AI tools in healthcare should be maintained rather than removed, as doctors need visibility into how these tools function to provide safe, coordinated care‚ÄĒjust as information sharing between providers proved essentia...‚ôĒLast year when my dad had a stroke, I watched three different doctor's offices struggle to share his test results with each other. I get why people want to streamline all these rules, I really do. But here's what worries me: removing transparency requirements for AI tools in healthcare sounds like it helps doctors, but it actually makes their job harder.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen my dad's doctors didn't know what information they had about him, it created real gaps in his care. The same thing will happen with AI tools if doctors can't see how they work or what they're actually doing behind the scenes. My dad's care improved once everyone understood what was being shared and why. Doctors need to trust the tools they're using, and trust comes from understanding, not from less visibility. I'm not against removing unnecessary rules, but transparency about what AI can and can't do isn't a burden on doctors. It's what gets life-saving technology into hospitals in the first place.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-21T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-21T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒmedium‚ôĒconcerned‚ôĒMichigan‚ôĒretail worker‚ôĒ40‚ôĒLast year when my dad had a stroke, I spent three weeks calling between his cardiologist's office, the hospital, and his primary care doctor just trying to get them to share his test results with each other‚ÄĒit felt like they were all working in separate silos. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0105‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒKenneth Williams‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒWhile supporting improved medical record interoperability, the commenter warns against removing certification and testing requirements for hospital AI tools, arguing that these safeguards are essential for hospitals to evaluate safety and for pati...‚ôĒMy wife died two years ago from diabetes complications, and I spent months fighting to get her medical records moved between clinics because they said their systems couldn't communicate. By the time we got everything transferred, it was too late to change anything. I get that making it easier to move records matters, but I'm worried about what gets lost when you start removing the rules that protect how hospitals choose their tools.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what I keep coming back to. When a hospital buys software that uses artificial intelligence to help with patient decisions, someone in that hospital needs to know what that software actually does, who built it, what can go wrong with it. Right now that requires certification and testing. If you strip that away, hospitals won't have any real way to evaluate if a tool is safe or if it's just being pushed because it's cheap. Families like mine won't know if the decisions affecting their care came from something that was actually tested or just something a vendor promised would work.‚Źé‚ŹéThe certification requirements aren't perfect, but they give hospital administrators something to stand on when they're trying to make a smart choice. Remove them and you're just hoping vendors tell the truth.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-20T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-20T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHospital and health system procurement teams need transparent AI model information to make evidence-based decisions about which tools to adopt and how to implement them safely in their local contexts.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒlow‚ôĒresigned‚ôĒTennessee‚ôĒsocial worker‚ôĒ67‚ôĒMy wife passed two years ago from diabetes complications, and I spent months trying to get her records transferred between clinics because they said the systems didn't talk to each other‚ÄĒby then it was too late to matter much.
