CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0106‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDavid Scott‚ôĒPolicy Center for Maternal Mental Health‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health opposes removing Decision Support Interventions certification requirements that mandate transparency about AI system training data, validation, and known risks, arguing that such transparency is essenti...‚ôĒ# Public Comment on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions‚Źé‚ŹéThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health submits this comment regarding the proposed modifications to certification criteria and standards requirements for health information technology, specifically addressing the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements and transparency standards.‚Źé‚ŹéOur organization works with maternal mental health advocates, clinicians, and families across rural and urban communities. Over the past two years, we have documented recurring patterns in how gaps in health data transparency affect maternal mental health outcomes. When my daughter was discharged from the hospital after a postpartum psychosis episode, her discharge happened on a Friday afternoon. Her psychiatrist couldn't access her obstetric records without filing separate authorization requests through channels that took weeks. The hospital's electronic health record system and the OB clinic's system didn't communicate. What should have been a straightforward clinical handoff became a gap where critical information sat in separate databases. Her medications during pregnancy, her labor complications, her blood pressure readings from the third trimester, her pre-pregnancy psychiatric history‚ÄĒnone of it was available to the psychiatrist making decisions about her postpartum treatment plan. That delay cost time we couldn't afford to lose. We were fortunate. I understood the system enough to push back, to call the OB office, to insist on records being sent. I could advocate. Most families cannot.‚Źé‚ŹéThe question before regulators now involves loosening oversight of how artificial intelligence systems make recommendations in clinical settings. Specifically, the proposal would remove certification requirements that currently mandate developers of Decision Support Interventions disclose their model cards, training data characteristics, intended use populations, known risks, and external validation results. The current framework, established under 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1), requires this transparency. The proposed change would eliminate it.‚Źé‚ŹéWe want to be clear about what we are not arguing. We do not oppose innovation in health technology. We recognize that AI systems can help clinicians make better decisions faster. We understand the regulatory burden argument. What we are asking is that before regulators remove guardrails that require transparency, they think seriously about who bears the risk when those guardrails disappear.‚Źé‚ŹéThe maternal mental health population is particularly vulnerable to harms from opaque AI systems. Postpartum psychosis, postpartum depression, and perinatal anxiety disorders affect roughly 1 in 5 women. The conditions are serious. They require rapid diagnosis and precise medication management. They intersect with obstetric care, psychiatric care, and primary care. The records are fragmented. The clinicians involved rarely speak to each other. A clinical decision support system that recommends medication adjustments, screening protocols, or risk stratification in this context has real consequences. If that system was trained predominantly on data from academic medical centers and excludes rural populations, we need to know that. If it was validated only in English-speaking populations, we need to know that. If the developers know the system performs poorly for women on Medicaid, we need to know that. The current DSI certification requirement forces this disclosure. The proposed change removes that requirement.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Federal Register notice for this proposal (89 FR 80452, published October 2, 2024) frames the DSI removal as reducing unnecessary burden on developers. We understand this argument on its face. Requiring model cards and validation documentation does cost money and time. But the cost of removing transparency is borne by someone else. It is borne by the clinician who doesn't know whether the system she is using was tested on a population that looks like her patients. It is borne by the patient who receives a recommendation without understanding how that recommendation was generated, what populations it was tested on, or what the known failure modes are.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current regulation exists because earlier versions of health IT certification did not require this level of transparency, and clinicians and patients had no way to evaluate the systems they were using. The 21st Century Cures Act Implementation Final Rule, issued in 2020 (85 FR 25642), added the DSI requirements specifically to address this gap. The rule recognized that decision support systems are not neutral tools. They encode assumptions about which populations matter, which outcomes matter, and which risks are acceptable. Making those assumptions visible is not a burden. It is a requirement for informed clinical decision-making.‚Źé‚ŹéWe have been tracking implementation of the current DSI requirements since 2023. We reviewed publicly available model cards for three commercial decision support systems used in obstetric and psychiatric settings. Two of the three systems disclosed their training data characteristics. One system disclosed that its training dataset included fewer than 200 patients from rural settings, despite the system being marketed for use in rural hospitals. One system disclosed that external validation had not been completed in non-English-speaking populations. One system disclosed known risks related to medication interactions but did not disclose risks related to pregnancy and lactation, despite being used in postpartum psychiatric contexts. These disclosures matter. A clinician reading them can make an informed choice about whether to use the system, how much to trust its recommendations, and what additional clinical judgment is needed. Without the requirement to disclose, that clinician has no way to know.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal argues that market forces and professional judgment will ensure appropriate use of AI systems even without transparency requirements. This argument assumes that clinicians have time to independently validate every health IT tool they use, that they have access to technical expertise to review model architecture and training methodologies, and that they have leverage to demand transparency from vendors. In large academic medical centers, some of this may be true. In rural clinics, federally qualified health centers, and community mental health agencies that serve low-income populations, none of it is true. We work with maternal mental health clinics in rural Arizona, rural Colorado, and rural New Mexico. These clinics operate with minimal IT staff. They use whatever health IT systems their electronic health record vendor includes. They do not have the capacity to conduct independent technical audits of decision support systems. They rely on regulatory frameworks to ensure that the tools they use have been tested, validated, and disclosed in a way that allows clinical judgment.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal also argues that removing DSI certification requirements will increase innovation by reducing barriers to market entry for smaller developers. This argument has merit. Certification processes do create costs. But there is a difference between streamlining a process and removing transparency requirements entirely. The certification burden could be reduced by simplifying the documentation format, extending timelines, or creating tiered requirements based on system risk level. The transparency requirement itself‚ÄĒthat developers disclose what their systems are, how they were built, and what they were tested on‚ÄĒis not something that should be removed. It is something that should be standardized and simplified. The 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1) requirement does not mandate a specific model card format. It allows flexibility in how developers document their systems. What it does require is that the documentation exists and is accessible.‚Źé‚ŹéOur organization has surveyed 47 maternal mental health clinicians across 12 states about their experience with decision support systems. When asked whether they would use a system if they could not access information about its training data and validation, 39 clinicians said they would not. When asked whether they would be more likely to use a system if it included accessible documentation about known limitations and risk factors, 44 clinicians said they would. These are not abstract preferences. These are clinicians making decisions about which tools to integrate into their workflow. Transparency increases adoption among clinicians who are thoughtful about implementation. It does not decrease it.‚Źé‚ŹéThe specific concern we raise is this. The proposal removes DSI certification requirements without replacing them with any alternative mechanism for transparency. There is no proposed requirement that developers voluntarily disclose model cards. There is no proposed framework for post-market surveillance of decision support systems. There is no proposed requirement that vendors document how their systems are used in clinical settings or what outcomes result from their recommendations. The removal is clean and complete. Once the requirement is gone, there is no expectation of transparency. A developer could build a system, train it on a narrow population, validate it in an academic setting, and deploy it nationwide without any obligation to disclose these facts to clinicians or patients.‚Źé‚ŹéWe want to propose an alternative. Retain the current DSI certification criterion. Do not remove it. But simplify it. The current regulation requires developers to submit model cards for certification review. This could be modified to allow developers to submit model cards directly to a public repository instead, with certification based on whether the model card meets a standardized template rather than on subjective evaluation of its contents. The template could be simplified from what currently exists. The submission could be streamlined. But the requirement that developers disclose what their systems are, how they were built, what they were tested on, and what known risks exist should remain. This balances the innovation argument with the transparency argument. It reduces burden without removing accountability.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Federal Register notice invites comment on whether DSI certification requirements should be eliminated, modified, or retained. We are advocating for retention with modification. The specific modifications we recommend are as follows: first, simplify the model card template to focus on four core elements: intended use and target population, training data characteristics, external validation results, and known limitations and risks. Second, allow developers to submit model cards to a public registry rather than to a certification authority, with certification based on template compliance rather than substantive review. Third, require that model cards be updated annually or within 30 days of any material change to the system. Fourth, require that clinicians receive access to the model card before implementing a decision support system. Fifth, establish a post-market surveillance mechanism through which clinicians can report outcomes and adverse events related to decision support systems, with results made available to developers and regulators.‚Źé‚ŹéThese modifications would reduce the certification burden while preserving the transparency requirement. They would allow smaller developers to enter the market without conducting expensive certification processes. They would preserve the ability of clinicians and patients to understand how decision support systems work and what risks they carry. They would create a feedback loop through which outcomes data could inform future system development.‚Źé‚ŹéThe maternal mental health population deserves this level of transparency. The conditions are serious. The medications are complex. The populations affected are diverse in terms of race, ethnicity, language, insurance status, and geography. An AI system that makes recommendations in this context without transparent documentation of how it was built and what it was tested on is a system that could cause harm. We have seen what happens when data systems operate without transparency. We have seen the delays that result from fragmented records. We have seen the gaps in communication between clinicians. We have seen the outcomes when critical information is not accessible. We do not want to see a future in which decision support systems operate with the same opacity that characterizes our current health data infrastructure.‚Źé‚ŹéWe recognize that this comment raises concerns about a single certification requirement in a larger deregulatory proposal. We are not opposing the overall direction of the proposal. We support removal of redundant standards. We support streamlining of certification processes. We support innovation in health technology. What we are asking is that transparency requirements be preserved and simplified rather than removed. The cost of this preservation is minimal. The cost of removal is borne by clinicians and patients who cannot see inside the systems that make recommendations about their care.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health respectfully urges the Office of the Secretary and the Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology to retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion under 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1), with the modifications outlined above. We are available to discuss this recommendation further and would welcome the opportunity to present the perspectives of maternal mental health clinicians and families as this proposal moves forward.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-19T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-19T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ6‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒangry‚ôĒArizona‚ôĒcommunity organizer‚ôĒ64‚ôĒWhen my daughter was discharged after a postpartum psychosis episode two years ago, her psychiatrist couldn't access her OB records without filing separate authorization requests‚ÄĒdelays that meant missing critical context about her medications during pregnancy. Now I'm watching regulators consider loosening the very guardrails that forced that conversation to happen, and I keep thinking about women in rural counties or without insurance literacy who won't have advocates like me pushing back when their data gets traded around without them knowing.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2004
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0107‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDavid Rodriguez‚ôĒPolicy Center for Maternal Mental Health‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health opposes weakening Decision Support Interventions certification criteria that require AI transparency disclosures, arguing that documented algorithmic bias in depression screening tools and the unique vu...‚ôĒ**COMMENT TO THE OFFICE OF THE NATIONAL COORDINATOR FOR HEALTH INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY**‚Źé‚Źé**Re: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health submits this comment in response to the proposed deregulatory actions affecting Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria and related transparency requirements. Our organization represents the interests of postpartum women and their families, particularly in rural and underserved communities where fragmented mental health care remains a persistent barrier to timely diagnosis and treatment.‚Źé‚Źé**BACKGROUND AND ORGANIZATIONAL PERSPECTIVE**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health operates across rural Georgia, where we work directly with maternal health providers, perinatal psychiatrists, and women navigating the postpartum period. Last year, we conducted a survey of 200 postpartum women across rural Georgia to understand how mental health information flows between providers. What we found was sobering but also illuminating. Women in our sample frequently saw multiple providers during the perinatal period: their obstetrician-gynecologist, a primary care physician, sometimes a psychiatrist, and occasionally a midwife or nurse practitioner. Yet these providers almost never had access to each other's mental health documentation in real time. In at least 14 cases within our survey cohort, this fragmentation directly delayed diagnosis of peripartum depression. One woman we interviewed saw her OB-GYN at 6 weeks postpartum, reported symptoms of depression, and was referred to a psychiatrist. The psychiatrist's office called requesting records from the OB-GYN, but the records transfer took four weeks. By the time the psychiatrist had the information, the woman was in crisis.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen we began aggregating this data to build a case for better information-sharing protocols, we confronted a different problem entirely. Who actually owns access to mental health records once they're digitized and aggregated? What safeguards prevent a woman's perinatal depression diagnosis from ending up in the hands of an insurer looking to deny coverage or reassign her to higher-risk job categories? What happens if an employer discovers that an employee sought psychiatric care during pregnancy? These questions matter tremendously, and they sit at the center of the current rulemaking.‚Źé‚ŹéWe are not opposed to innovation in health information technology. We are not arguing against deregulation in principle. But we have serious concerns about the proposal to remove or significantly weaken the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion that requires AI model cards, transparency disclosures, and documentation of intended use, target population, known risks, and training data characteristics. This specific criterion exists because maternal mental health is a domain where algorithmic error can cause real harm, and where the stakes of opacity are especially high.‚Źé‚Źé**THE SPECIFIC PROBLEM: DSI TRANSPARENCY AND MATERNAL MENTAL HEALTH**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification criterion, outlined in 45 CFR 170.315(a)(6), requires that developers of clinical decision support tools provide transparency documentation including intended use, target population, known limitations, training data characteristics, and external validation evidence. The proposal under review suggests removing or significantly weakening this requirement as part of broader deregulatory efforts.‚Źé‚ŹéWe want to be clear about what we are asking for. We are not asking ONC to prevent the use of AI and machine learning in maternal mental health. We are asking that any AI system used to assist clinicians in identifying or managing peripartum depression be required to disclose what it was trained on, who it was validated in, what its known failure modes are, and what it is actually designed to do. This is not a high bar. This is a minimum bar.‚Źé‚ŹéHere is why this matters for maternal mental health specifically. Peripartum depression presents differently in different populations. The symptom presentation in rural low-income women may differ from that in suburban higher-income women. The timing of symptom onset varies. Risk factors are not uniform. If a clinical decision support tool is trained primarily on data from academic medical centers in urban areas, it may fail to identify depression in rural women who present with different symptom clusters or who have different social risk factors. Without transparency about training data, a clinician in rural Georgia has no way to know whether the tool in front of them is actually valid in their population. The tool might be excellent. Or it might be biased. There is no way to know.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not a theoretical concern. A 2023 study published in JAMA Psychiatry examining algorithmic bias in depression screening tools found that models trained on predominantly white, higher-income samples showed significant performance degradation when applied to low-income and Black women (JAMA Psychiatry, 80(9), 2023). The researchers found that without explicit documentation of training cohort characteristics, clinicians had no way to identify or correct for this bias. The tools worked fine in the populations they were trained on. In other populations, they missed cases or generated false positives. The clinicians using these tools had no transparency into what had happened.‚Źé‚ŹéWe are not asking for perfection. We are asking for honesty about what a tool can and cannot do. We are asking that certification criteria require developers to disclose known limitations, validation scope, and training data characteristics. This allows clinicians to make informed decisions about when and how to use a tool. This allows hospital systems and health IT vendors to understand the actual scope of what they are purchasing. This allows patient advocacy groups and regulatory bodies to identify potential equity gaps.‚Źé‚Źé**INFORMATION BLOCKING AND MENTAL HEALTH PRIVACY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed deregulatory actions also address information blocking regulations under 45 CFR 171.102. Some commenters have argued that information blocking restrictions should be loosened to allow more flexibility in how and when providers share data. We want to highlight a specific concern: mental health data occupies a special status in federal law, and loosening information blocking rules without explicit safeguards for mental health information could create serious patient privacy risks.‚Źé‚ŹéUnder 42 CFR Part 2, substance use disorder treatment records are protected from disclosure except under narrow circumstances. Mental health records generally are not subject to Part 2, but they are subject to HIPAA, and many states have additional privacy protections for mental health information (Georgia Code 34-26-2, for example, requires explicit patient authorization for most mental health record disclosures). The policy question is not whether providers should share data. They should. The question is whether the rules should require transparency about what data is being shared, with whom, and under what conditions.‚Źé‚ŹéOur concern is this: if information blocking regulations are loosened without explicit carve-outs for mental health data, we could see scenarios where mental health information flows to insurers, employers, or other third parties without the woman's knowledge or explicit consent. A woman seeking perinatal psychiatric care should not discover years later that her diagnosis was shared with her employer or used to deny her a future insurance policy. This is not a hypothetical risk. It is a documented concern in the mental health advocacy community.‚Źé‚ŹéWe are not asking ONC to block information sharing. We are asking that any deregulatory changes to information blocking rules explicitly preserve state and federal privacy protections for mental health data, and that certification criteria continue to require disclosure of what data is being shared and under what conditions.‚Źé‚Źé**CERTIFICATION CRITERIA AND CLINICAL ACCOUNTABILITY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current certification framework under 45 CFR 170.315 requires health IT developers to meet specific technical and functional standards. The DSI transparency criterion is one element of this broader framework. The proposal suggests streamlining certification requirements to reduce burden on developers.‚Źé‚ŹéWe understand the burden argument. Compliance is expensive. Documentation takes time. Developers want to innovate faster. But certification criteria exist for a reason. They create a floor below which health IT products should not fall. They establish minimum standards for safety, security, and transparency. They allow hospitals and providers to purchase products with reasonable confidence that those products meet baseline requirements. They allow regulators to identify problems when they arise.‚Źé‚ŹéThe specific burden we are concerned about is not the burden on developers. It is the burden on patients and clinicians if certification criteria are weakened to the point where safety and transparency cannot be assured. In maternal mental health, this is not abstract. A woman experiencing peripartum depression needs her clinician to have access to accurate, timely information. She needs any AI tool assisting her clinician to be transparent about what it can and cannot do. She needs to know that her mental health data is being protected, not shared with parties who might discriminate against her.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE ARE ASKING FOR**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health is asking ONC to retain the current DSI certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures. Specifically, we are asking that any final rule maintain the requirement that Decision Support Interventions certification include:‚Źé‚Źé1. Documentation of intended use and target population. This allows clinicians to understand whether the tool is actually validated for their patient population.‚Źé‚Źé2. Known limitations and failure modes. This allows clinicians to know when not to rely on the tool or to use it with additional caution.‚Źé‚Źé3. Training data characteristics. This allows clinicians and health systems to identify potential equity gaps and to understand whether the tool was trained on populations similar to the ones they serve.‚Źé‚Źé4. External validation evidence. This allows clinicians to have confidence that the tool actually works as claimed, not just in the developer's internal testing environment.‚Źé‚ŹéThese requirements are not onerous. They are standard practice in clinical research and medical device development. The FDA requires similar documentation for software as a medical device (21 CFR 860). The European Union requires similar transparency under the proposed AI Act. These are not radical demands. They are baseline standards for responsible AI development in healthcare.‚Źé‚ŹéWe are also asking that any changes to information blocking regulations preserve explicit protections for mental health data, and that certification criteria continue to require disclosure of data sharing practices.‚Źé‚Źé**IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéWe recognize that implementation matters. If ONC retains DSI transparency requirements, the agency should also consider how compliance will be assessed and enforced. Will developers be required to submit model cards as part of the certification process? Will ONC maintain a public registry of certified tools with their associated documentation? Will there be mechanisms for clinicians or patient advocates to report when tools fail to perform as documented in their target population?‚Źé‚ŹéThese are practical questions that affect whether a certification criterion is actually meaningful. A requirement that sounds good but is not enforced is not a requirement at all. We are asking ONC to think carefully about implementation, not just the substantive standard.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health supports innovation in health IT. We support efforts to reduce unnecessary regulatory burden on developers. But we cannot support weakening the specific transparency requirements that apply to clinical decision support tools used in maternal mental health. These requirements exist because the stakes are high, because equity gaps are real, and because women deserve to know that the tools assisting their care are safe, validated, and transparent.‚Źé‚ŹéThe women in our survey who experienced delayed diagnosis of peripartum depression did not fail to receive care because health IT was too heavily regulated. They received delayed care because information was fragmented and providers did not have access to what they needed. The deregulatory actions under consideration do not address this core problem. What they do risk is weakening the safeguards that ensure the tools meant to solve this problem are actually safe and equitable.‚Źé‚ŹéWe urge ONC to retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion requiring model cards, transparency disclosures, and documentation of intended use, target population, known risks, and training data characteristics. This is a modest requirement that serves an important function. Removing it would be a mistake.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-21T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-21T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ6‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒurgent‚ôĒGeorgia‚ôĒdirector of government affairs‚ôĒ44‚ôĒLast year, our organization conducted a survey of 200 postpartum women across rural Georgia, and we discovered that fragmented mental health records‚ÄĒscattered between OB-GYNs, psychiatrists, and primary care physicians‚ÄĒhad directly delayed diagnosis in at least 14 cases of peripartum depression. But when we began aggregating that data to build a case for better information-sharing protocols, we immediately confronted questions we couldn't answer: who actually owns access to these records, and what safeguards exist if a woman's mental health data ends up in the hands of insurers or employers looking to deny coverage or reassign her to higher-risk job categories?‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1947
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0108‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒGeorge Flores‚ôĒHealthcare Leadership Council‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Healthcare Leadership Council opposes removal of Decision Support Interventions certification requirements for AI model cards and transparency disclosures, arguing that these requirements are essential for patient safety and health equity by e...‚ôĒRE: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity‚Źé‚ŹéHealthcare Leadership Council appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory framework. Our organization represents health systems, medical groups, and community health providers across New Mexico and the Southwest, and we work directly with patients navigating fragmented care systems. I want to speak to something specific that emerged from our work last year, because it shaped how we think about what should stay in place and what might actually benefit from revision.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen I was helping that Navajo elder consolidate her medical records across three hospital systems, the chaos wasn't just inconvenient. Her primary care doctor had prescribed one diabetes medication while an endocrinologist at a different system had prescribed another, based on incomplete information about her other conditions. The records eventually did get consolidated, but it took six weeks and written requests to three separate billing departments. What struck me wasn't just the inefficiency. It was that nobody could see what the other was doing, and there was no accountability for why. No transparency about what data existed, who had access to it, or what assumptions the systems were making about her care needs.‚Źé‚ŹéThis experience is why we have concerns about the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions certification criteria, particularly the requirement for AI model cards and transparency disclosures. We understand the impulse to reduce burden on developers. But certification criteria exist for a reason in healthcare, and that reason is patient safety and clinical accountability. When you strip away the requirement for developers to document intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results, you don't just reduce paperwork. You remove the mechanism by which providers and patients can actually understand what a tool is supposed to do and whether it's doing it correctly.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CURRENT DSI CERTIFICATION FRAMEWORK AND WHAT IT PROTECTS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe existing Decision Support Interventions certification criterion under 45 CFR 170.315(b)(7) requires developers to provide model cards and transparency documentation as a condition of certification. This isn't arbitrary compliance theater. These requirements exist because AI tools in healthcare operate differently than traditional clinical software. An EHR module does what it's programmed to do. An AI decision support tool learns patterns from training data, and those patterns may not hold for every population or clinical context. A model trained primarily on data from patients in urban hospital systems may perform differently for rural or underserved populations. A tool trained to predict hospital readmission using certain socioeconomic variables might be making assumptions about resource access that don't apply to the patient sitting in front of a provider right now.‚Źé‚ŹéThe model card requirement is the mechanism by which this complexity gets surfaced. It requires developers to document what data they trained on, what populations they validated the tool with, what known limitations exist, and what the tool is actually intended to do. When a provider sits down to use a clinical decision support tool, they need to know whether it was validated in a population similar to their patient. They need to know if the tool's recommendations might be biased by the historical patterns in its training data. They need to know what happens when the patient in front of them doesn't match the tool's assumptions about diagnosis or risk.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not theoretical. The healthcare literature is full of examples of AI tools that performed well in development environments but failed in real clinical settings because the underlying populations or data distributions were different. A tool for predicting sepsis that was trained on data from a teaching hospital may not work the same way in a community hospital with different patient acuity patterns and different laboratory testing practices. A tool for assessing risk of adverse events that was trained on predominantly male populations may systematically underestimate risk in women, because sex differences in symptom presentation and disease progression are real. Without transparency about training data and validation, providers have no way to know whether the tool is reliable for their specific patients.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat concerns us is that the proposed deregulation would remove these requirements entirely. The rationale in the proposed rule is that developers should be free to innovate without the burden of certification, and that market forces and tort liability will naturally incentivize safety. But in healthcare, market forces alone have never been sufficient to protect patient safety. That's why we have FDA oversight of medical devices. That's why we have HIPAA standards. That's why we have clinical practice guidelines and accreditation standards. Those mechanisms exist because the consequences of failure in healthcare are not just financial. They are measured in patient harm.‚Źé‚ŹéThe certification requirement for AI model cards serves a specific function that market forces cannot replicate. It creates an expectation of transparency that is baked into the development process from the beginning, not retrofitted after a problem occurs. When a developer knows they must document their training data, their validation methodology, and their known limitations, they are more likely to actually conduct that validation rigorously. They are more likely to test their tool in diverse populations. They are more likely to identify and document bias. These are not burdens imposed after the fact. They are good development practices that improve the final product.‚Źé‚Źé**INFORMATION BLOCKING AND THE RELATIONSHIP TO CLINICAL DECISION SUPPORT**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule seems to treat certification requirements and information blocking rules as separate problems, but they are actually connected. The information blocking regulations under 45 CFR 171 require providers and health IT developers to share health information without unnecessary restrictions, with specific exceptions for genuine safety concerns. One of those exceptions is for information that could reasonably be expected to interfere with clinical decision-making or clinical care (45 CFR 171.102(b)(2)(viii)). This exception exists precisely because clinical decision-making tools can malfunction or produce unreliable outputs, and providers need to be able to restrict access to information when there is genuine evidence that access would cause harm.‚Źé‚ŹéIf we remove the certification requirements that ensure decision support tools are reliable, safe, and validated, we simultaneously weaken the justification for the information blocking exception. A provider cannot credibly claim that sharing information would interfere with clinical decision-making if there is no documented, validated basis for that claim. The certification requirement and the information blocking exception work together. The certification requirement gives providers the evidentiary basis they need to make informed decisions about when information blocking is appropriate. Without it, providers are either forced to accept all information sharing requests regardless of safety concerns, or they are forced to defend information blocking decisions without clear documentation of the risks they are trying to prevent.‚Źé‚ŹéThis matters for patient safety in a very concrete way. Take the case of the Navajo elder again. Once her records were consolidated, the providers could see that her diabetes medications were contradictory. But what if, instead of human review, her care had been managed by an AI decision support tool that had been trained on data that didn't include information about medication interactions in her specific population? What if the tool had been recommending a treatment plan based on incomplete information? And what if, under a deregulated framework, there was no way for her providers to know that the tool was making recommendations based on limited validation data?‚Źé‚ŹéThe information blocking exception exists so that providers can say no to requests that would compromise patient safety. But that exception only makes sense if there is a documented basis for believing that the tool is unreliable. If we remove certification requirements, we remove the basis for defending those exceptions. We end up in a situation where providers either comply with all information sharing requests regardless of safety concerns, or they make blocking decisions without clear documentation or accountability.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE KNOW FROM REAL-WORLD IMPLEMENTATION**‚Źé‚ŹéOur organization has been working with health systems across the Southwest to improve care coordination, and we have direct experience with what happens when transparency requirements are missing. Last year, we helped one health system implement a tool for predicting which patients at risk for chronic kidney disease should be referred to nephrology. The tool was developed by a vendor and had never been certified. When we asked the vendor for documentation about the tool's training data and validation methodology, they declined to provide it, claiming proprietary protection.‚Źé‚ŹéWe implemented the tool anyway, because it seemed like it would save time and improve referral decisions. Within three months, we noticed something odd. The tool was recommending nephrology referrals for patients with early-stage kidney disease at rates substantially higher than clinical guidelines would suggest. When we finally pushed the vendor to explain their methodology, it turned out the tool had been trained primarily on data from patients already enrolled in a nephrology program, not from the general population. This created selection bias in the training data. The tool was not predicting who should be referred to nephrology. It was predicting who, given historical patterns in that particular health system, had already been referred. It was essentially telling us to do what we had already been doing, not what evidence suggested we should be doing.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the tool had been required to document its training data and validation methodology as part of a certification process, this problem would have been caught before implementation. The vendor would have had to disclose the selection bias in their training data. Clinicians would have understood that the tool's recommendations reflected historical patterns, not evidence-based guidelines. We would have either rejected the tool or implemented it with clear understanding of its limitations. Instead, we spent three months using a tool we didn't understand, making clinical decisions based on outputs we couldn't interpret.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is what happens when transparency requirements are removed. Vendors have no incentive to document limitations. Providers have no basis for evaluating reliability. Patients end up with care decisions that are influenced by tools nobody really understands. The deregulatory rationale assumes that vendors will voluntarily provide transparency because it is good business practice, or that providers will demand it through procurement processes. But procurement processes in healthcare are complex and often driven more by cost and interoperability features than by detailed analysis of AI validation methodology. Most providers do not have the data science expertise to evaluate whether a tool's training data is representative of their population. They rely on certification and regulatory standards to tell them whether a tool is safe.‚Źé‚Źé**THE EQUITY DIMENSION**‚Źé‚ŹéThis gets worse when you consider health equity. Our organization works extensively in rural and underserved communities. These are exactly the populations most likely to be harmed by AI tools that were not validated in diverse populations. If a decision support tool was trained primarily on data from urban academic medical centers, it may not perform the same way in rural clinics with different patient populations and different care patterns. If it was trained on data from predominantly insured populations, it may not work well for uninsured or underinsured patients whose health patterns are different. If it was trained on predominantly English-speaking populations, it may not work for patients with limited English proficiency whose medical records contain more sparse or differently structured information.‚Źé‚ŹéUnder the current certification framework, a provider can at least ask whether a tool was validated in their specific population. They can look at the model card and see whether the training and validation data included rural patients, Medicaid beneficiaries, or non-English speakers. Under a deregulated framework, that information may not exist at all. Vendors will have no obligation to validate in diverse populations. Providers will have no way to know whether a tool is reliable for their patients. And the populations most likely to be harmed are the ones least likely to have the resources to detect or challenge problems after the fact.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is particularly concerning because AI bias in healthcare is not a theoretical problem. It is well documented in the literature. A study published in Science in 2019 examining a widely used algorithm for assessing risk in hospitalized patients found that the tool systematically underestimated risk for Black patients, because it used healthcare spending as a proxy for health status, and Black patients face documented barriers to accessing healthcare. Another study in Radiology found that an AI tool for detecting tuberculosis performed differently across racial and ethnic groups because training data was not representative of all populations. These are not edge cases. They are examples of what happens when tools are deployed without sufficient attention to whether they work the same way across different populations.‚Źé‚ŹéThe certification requirement for AI model cards exists to prevent exactly this problem. It requires developers to explicitly consider population diversity in their validation methodology. It creates an accountability mechanism. If a tool is certified without evidence of validation in diverse populations, that is a gap that regulators and providers can identify. If certification requirements are removed, that gap becomes invisible. Vendors have no obligation to validate in diverse populations. Providers have no way to know whether a tool is biased. And patients in underserved communities become unwitting participants in uncontrolled experiments with tools that may not work for them.‚Źé‚Źé**ADDRESSING THE BURDEN ARGUMENT**‚Źé‚ŹéWe understand that the deregulatory proposal is motivated by concerns about burden on developers. Creating model cards and conducting external validation takes time and resources. For small vendors, this can be costly. But the solution to high compliance costs is not to eliminate safety requirements. It is to make the requirements more efficient and better tailored to actual risk. A small vendor developing a low-risk tool for administrative functions should not face the same certification burden as a vendor developing a high-risk tool for clinical decision-making that could directly affect patient care decisions.‚Źé‚ŹéIn fact, the FDA has already developed a framework for risk-based regulation of software. Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) guidance, issued under 21 CFR Part 11, distinguishes between different risk categories and applies different levels of scrutiny accordingly. A tool that provides information to support clinical decision-making faces different regulatory requirements than a tool that makes autonomous clinical decisions. A tool used in a specialist setting faces different requirements than a tool used in primary care. This kind of risk-based differentiation is entirely possible within a certification framework.‚Źé‚ŹéThe issue is not that certification requirements exist. The issue is that they may not be optimally designed. If the AI model card requirement is creating unnecessary burden, the solution is to streamline it, not eliminate it. Require model cards only for tools that directly support clinical decision-making, not for administrative tools. Allow vendors to provide abbreviated model cards for low-risk tools. Create templates and standardized formats that make documentation faster and easier. Work with vendors to understand what information is actually useful for clinical decision-making and what is just compliance theater. But do not eliminate the requirement entirely.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is especially important because the certification framework is one of the few mechanisms available to ensure that tools work the same way across diverse populations. If you remove the certification requirement, you are not just reducing burden on developers. You are removing the mechanism that requires developers to think about equity and validation in diverse populations. You are removing the mechanism that allows providers and patients to know whether a tool is reliable for their specific context. That is a real loss, and the burden reduction does not justify it.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC RECOMMENDATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéWe recommend that the final rule retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures. If the concern is burden, we recommend that the criterion be revised to be more risk-based and streamlined, but not eliminated. Specifically, we recommend that the final rule:‚Źé‚ŹéRequire model cards and transparency disclosures only for decision support tools that directly support clinical decision-making (not administrative tools). Allow vendors to provide documentation in a standardized template format that reduces development time. Require explicit documentation of the populations included in validation testing, with particular attention to whether validation included representation from racial and ethnic minorities, rural populations, and underserved communities. Require documentation of any known limitations or biases identified during development and validation. Allow vendors to provide information about external validation conducted by independent researchers, not just internal validation. Create a public registry where model cards are available to providers and patients, so that transparency information is actually used in real-world decision-making, not just archived for compliance purposes.‚Źé‚ŹéThese revisions would preserve the safety and equity benefits of the certification requirement while reducing unnecessary burden. They would also make the certification process more useful for providers and patients, because the information provided would be tailored to questions that actually matter for clinical decision-making.‚Źé‚Źé**CONNECTING THIS TO THE BROADER DEREGULATORY FRAMEWORK**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Health Leadership Council does not oppose all deregulation. In fact, we support efforts to reduce unnecessary burden where regulation is not providing meaningful benefit. But decision support transparency is not unnecessary burden. It is a mechanism that directly affects patient safety and health equity. It is one of the few tools available to ensure that AI tools in healthcare work reliably across diverse populations and clinical contexts.‚Źé‚ŹéThe broader deregulatory proposal seems to assume that innovation and safety are in direct conflict, and that reducing regulation will automatically lead to better, cheaper, more innovative tools. But in healthcare, innovation that is not safe is not actually innovation. It is harm. The tools that are most valuable are the ones that providers trust, that patients understand, that work reliably across different populations and care settings. Those tools are more likely to be developed under a framework that requires transparency and validation, not less likely.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat we have seen in our work is that providers want to use AI tools, but they want to understand them. They want to know what the tool is designed to do, whether it has been validated in populations similar to their patients, and what the known limitations are. When that information is available, providers can make informed decisions about whether and how to use the tool. When it is not available, providers either avoid the tool or use it blind, without understanding whether it is reliable for their specific context.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not regulation preventing innovation. This is regulation enabling innovation by creating the conditions under which providers and patients can confidently use new tools. The vendors developing the most innovative and useful tools are often the ones most willing to provide transparency documentation, because they have nothing to hide. It is vendors with unreliable or biased tools that resist transparency requirements.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Healthcare Leadership Council urges the Department of Health and Human Services to retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures in the final rule. We recognize the value of reducing unnecessary regulatory burden, but we believe this particular requirement provides real and measurable benefit for patient safety and health equity. If the concern is burden, we recommend that the criterion be streamlined and made more risk-based, but not eliminated. The stakes are too high, and the evidence too clear, to remove this safeguard.‚Źé‚ŹéHealthcare systems across the Southwest are working to improve care coordination and reduce health disparities. Those efforts depend on tools that are reliable, transparent, and validated in the populations we serve. We cannot do that work if developers have no obligation to document whether their tools actually work for our patients. We cannot build trust in new technologies if there is no mechanism to ensure that those technologies have been properly validated. The certification requirement is not perfect, but it is far better than the alternative, which is a marketplace where vendors can deploy tools without any documented evidence that they are safe or effective.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-26T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-26T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ10‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒurgent‚ôĒNew Mexico‚ôĒcommunity organizer‚ôĒ63‚ôĒLast spring, I was helping a Navajo elder navigate her medical records after she'd been seeing specialists across three different hospital systems, and we discovered her diabetes management plan contradicted itself‚ÄĒone doctor didn't know what the other had prescribed. When I finally got her information consolidated, it took six weeks and written requests to three separate billing departments. I kept thinking: if we're going to break down these walls between providers, we need ironclad guarantees that her information won't end up in the hands of insurance companies trying to deny her coverage, or worse, sold to pharmaceutical marketers targeting her specific zip code.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ3296
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0109‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒLisa Lee‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒPatients should have equal transparency and accountability requirements for AI-based diagnostic tools as for traditional medical devices, including disclosure of the AI's design, limitations, training data, and involvement in care decisions‚ÄĒnot a ...‚ôĒWhen my mom needed a second opinion on her heart condition last year, I spent three weeks calling between her cardiologist and the hospital trying to get her test results. They kept saying everything was "in the system" but nobody could actually send us the files. It felt like her own medical information was locked away, and I kept thinking there had to be a better way for families like ours to access what we need when we need it.‚Źé‚ŹéNow I'm concerned that while we're working to fix those access problems, we're also loosening rules on AI tools that help doctors diagnose and treat patients. I don't understand why a newer technology like an AI decision support tool would have fewer transparency requirements than a traditional medical device. Both should have to show their work, right? Tell us what they're designed for, who they work best with, what problems they might have. Patients deserve to know when AI is involved in our care decisions, what data trained that AI, and what its limitations are. That's not a burden on companies‚ÄĒthat's basic accountability. We need consistent safeguards across all medical tools, not a two-tier system where the newer, less-understood technology gets a free pass.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-17T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-17T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving DSI transparency requirements creates a dangerous regulatory gap where AI-based clinical decision support tools face fewer disclosure obligations than traditional medical devices, undermining consistent safety standards.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒmedium‚ôĒhopeful‚ôĒColorado‚ôĒcustomer service representative‚ôĒ45‚ôĒLast year when my mom was diagnosed with her heart condition, I spent three weeks calling between her cardiologist's office and the hospital trying to get copies of her test results so we could get a second opinion‚ÄĒthey kept saying the files were "in the system" but nobody could actually send them to us.
