CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0110‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒLinda Green‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA parent argues against proposed deregulatory changes to health IT certification and information blocking rules, contending that removing transparency requirements for AI clinical tools creates unsafe "blind spots" in patient care while simultaneo...‚ôĒLast spring, my daughter called me in tears because her new cardiologist couldn't access test results from her previous clinic. The records existed. They were digital. But the systems wouldn't talk to each other, and no one was required to make them do so. For three weeks, she had duplicate appointments, repeated stress tests, and the anxiety of not knowing if her care was continuous. What frustrated me most wasn't just the inconvenience. It was watching her ask reasonable questions that nobody could answer: why didn't her doctors have her information, and who was responsible for that gap?‚Źé‚ŹéThat experience shaped how I think about this proposed rule. As an elementary school teacher, I spend my days teaching children to read instructions carefully and think about consequences before acting. The proposed deregulatory changes to health IT certification and information blocking rules worry me for exactly that reason. The rule aims to reduce burden on developers and providers, and I understand the appeal of lighter regulation. But I'm seeing something that troubles me: a shift toward weaker transparency requirements for artificial intelligence systems in clinical care, precisely at the moment when those systems are becoming more influential in medical decisions. That's the opposite of transparency. That's creating a shadow tier of medical decision-making that patients and doctors can't see clearly.‚Źé‚Źé**THE TRANSPARENCY PROBLEM NOBODY TALKS ABOUT**‚Źé‚ŹéLet me be direct about what's concerning me. The proposed rule would remove or weaken the Distinction, Scope of Use, and Intended Populations (DSI) transparency requirements for AI clinical tools. This means developers would no longer be required to disclose certain information about how their algorithms work, what data they were trained on, what their known limitations are, or which patient populations they were actually tested with. By contrast, traditional medical devices like the cardiac monitors my daughter wears have disclosure obligations built into FDA guidance and certification requirements. They have to tell doctors and patients about their limitations. They have to be transparent about what they do and don't do.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's where my worry becomes specific: if you remove DSI transparency requirements for AI systems, you're creating a two-tiered safety standard in healthcare. One tier covers devices with disclosure obligations. The other tier covers AI tools with fewer requirements to tell anyone what's actually happening inside the algorithm. That's not deregulation in the name of innovation. That's creating a blind spot in clinical care.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Institute of Medicine's 2015 report on diagnostic error found that information availability is critical to catching mistakes before they harm patients. When doctors don't have complete information about what a tool can and can't do, they can't calibrate their judgment properly. They can't catch errors. They can't ask the right questions. My daughter's three-week gap in cardiac care happened because systems weren't required to share basic data transparently. Now imagine that gap exists not in paperwork but in the algorithms influencing her treatment decisions, and nobody is required to tell her or her doctors what assumptions the algorithm is making about her health.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT REMOVING DISCLOSURE REQUIREMENTS ACTUALLY MEANS**‚Źé‚ŹéWhen the rule proposes to eliminate DSI transparency requirements, it's proposing to let AI clinical tools operate without the same disclosure obligations that apply to traditional medical devices. This isn't a small technical change. The FDA's 2021 guidance on AI/ML-based Software as a Medical Device made clear that transparency about training data, intended use populations, and known limitations is essential to safe clinical deployment. That guidance exists because AI systems can amplify bias, fail on populations they weren't tested with, and make errors that nobody catches if clinicians don't understand how the tool works.‚Źé‚ŹéI've been reading about bias in clinical AI systems. There's documented evidence that algorithms trained predominantly on certain patient populations perform differently, sometimes dangerously, when applied to others. A tool trained on predominantly white patient populations may not work reliably for Black patients. An algorithm designed for hospital settings may fail in primary care clinics. Without disclosure requirements, a doctor using that tool might never know. My daughter might use an AI-assisted diagnostic tool that was never tested on women with her specific cardiac condition, and nobody would be required to tell her.‚Źé‚ŹéThat's not just an inconvenience. That's a safety gap. And it's worse than the paperwork frustration she experienced, because she couldn't even see it happening.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule talks about reducing duplicative requirements and clearing away unnecessary certification criteria. I get the efficiency argument. But transparency about how clinical AI tools work isn't duplicative. It's foundational. It's the difference between a tool that's regulated with the same safety expectations as traditional medical devices and one that operates in a regulatory gap.‚Źé‚Źé**INFORMATION BLOCKING RULES AND THE DEFINITIONS THAT MATTER**‚Źé‚ŹéThe rule also proposes to revise how we define "access" and "use" in the information blocking regulations. This is where things get complicated, but also where I think the current approach actually protects patients better than the proposed revisions.‚Źé‚ŹéRight now, information blocking rules require that when a patient or their doctor asks for health information, providers and developers can't just refuse to share it or make it unnecessarily difficult to obtain. The definitions of "access" and "use" in those rules are meant to be broad enough to catch the kinds of games that frustrated my daughter last spring. A system might technically allow data to be shared but require so many manual steps, or so much reformatting, that it becomes impractical. That's not really access. It's obstruction wearing a compliance costume.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule wants to narrow those definitions. I understand the argument: narrower definitions mean clearer compliance obligations for developers and providers. But narrower also means easier to evade. If we define "access" very tightly, we might miss the scenario where a system can technically share data but does so in a format that's incompatible with the receiving system, or with such poor documentation that the receiving clinic can't use it safely. We might miss the scenario where a developer says access is available but only if a patient pays a fee, or only if they use a specific clunky portal instead of the interoperable standards that would make data sharing automatic.‚Źé‚ŹéThe reason this matters is that information blocking exceptions already exist. Developers and providers can refuse to share data if doing so would require significant reprogramming, or if it violates intellectual property rights, or if it creates security or privacy risks. Those exceptions are legitimate. But they have to be applied narrowly, with clear documentation of why data can't be shared. If we loosen the definitions of "access" and "use," we might make those exceptions so broad that they swallow the rule. We might end up in a situation where technically, access is available, but practically, it's not.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CONSISTENCY PROBLEM**‚Źé‚ŹéWhat really bothers me about this approach is the inconsistency. We're apparently comfortable with disclosure requirements for traditional medical devices. Manufacturers have to tell us what their products do, what they don't do, what populations they work for, what the risks are. That transparency is considered essential to safe clinical care. But for AI systems, we're proposing to loosen those same requirements. We're saying, let's remove the transparency obligations for the tools that are actually harder to understand and more prone to hidden errors.‚Źé‚ŹéThat doesn't make sense to me. If anything, AI systems should have stronger transparency requirements than traditional devices, not weaker ones. An AI tool is a black box in a way that a traditional device isn't. Its behavior can be difficult to predict. Its errors can be hard to catch. Its biases can be invisible unless someone is specifically looking for them. The FDA's 2021 guidance on AI/ML-based SaMD recognized that. It said transparency about training data, testing populations, and performance characteristics is essential.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule seems to move in the opposite direction. It's saying, let's reduce the transparency burden on AI developers. But the transparency burden exists for a reason. It exists because patients and doctors need to understand what tools are making decisions about their care.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT HAPPENED WITH MY DAUGHTER, AND WHY IT MATTERS HERE**‚Źé‚ŹéI keep coming back to that three-week gap in my daughter's care because it's concrete and it's real. It happened because systems weren't required to make data sharing easy. Her cardiologist's office and her new cardiologist's office both had her test results in digital form. The records existed. But the systems didn't have to talk to each other, and nobody was accountable for making them do so. So my daughter spent three weeks making phone calls, asking questions, getting inconsistent answers about whether her records would be transferred, whether she needed to authorize them, whether the new office could access them directly or needed paper copies.‚Źé‚ŹéIn the end, the records were transferred. But the friction in that process created risk. It created delay in her care. It created anxiety. And it happened because transparency and interoperability requirements were weak enough that the system could avoid them.‚Źé‚ŹéNow imagine that same kind of friction and opacity, but in the algorithms that are recommending her treatment. Imagine that her cardiologist is using an AI tool to help interpret her test results or recommend medication adjustments, but neither the doctor nor my daughter knows what data that tool was trained on, what its limitations are, or whether it was tested on women with her specific condition. Imagine that the tool has a blind spot for her type of cardiac condition, but nobody is required to disclose that. Imagine that the tool recommends a treatment that's appropriate for the majority of patients it was tested on, but not for her, and nobody catches the error because the doctor doesn't know what the tool's intended use population actually was.‚Źé‚ŹéThat's the risk created by removing DSI transparency requirements. That's what I'm worried about.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CERTIFICATION CRITERIA QUESTION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe rule asks which certification criteria should be removed or revised as duplicative or unnecessary. I understand that compliance with multiple overlapping requirements creates burden. I'm sympathetic to that concern. But I'd argue that DSI transparency requirements for AI clinical tools aren't duplicative. They're filling a specific gap in safety oversight.‚Źé‚ŹéA certification criterion that requires developers to disclose training data, intended use populations, and known limitations isn't the same as a certification criterion that requires general validation testing. It's more specific. It's focused on the particular challenges of AI systems: their potential for bias, their sensitivity to the populations they're trained on, their tendency toward errors that are hard to catch because they're hidden inside a statistical model.‚Źé‚ŹéIf we remove that criterion, we're saying that AI tools don't need to meet the same transparency standard as traditional devices. But the safety case for transparency is actually stronger for AI tools than for traditional devices. A traditional device's behavior is largely deterministic and visible. An AI tool's behavior is probabilistic and opaque. Transparency about how it was built and what it was tested on is more important, not less important.‚Źé‚ŹéI'd be much more comfortable with revising the certification criteria to streamline them than with removing transparency requirements altogether. Maybe there are ways to simplify the documentation process, or to allow developers to report transparency information in standardized formats that reduce the burden of compliance. But the core requirement, that developers disclose information about training data, intended use populations, and known limitations, should stay.‚Źé‚Źé**INFORMATION BLOCKING AND THE REAL-WORLD IMPACT**‚Źé‚ŹéOn the information blocking side, I'm concerned that narrowing the definitions of "access" and "use" will make it easier for providers and developers to comply technically while failing practically. Right now, if a patient or doctor asks for health information, the information blocking rules prevent obstruction. A provider can't just make access so difficult or so expensive that it's impractical. A developer can't design a system in a way that technically allows data sharing but requires so much manual work that nobody actually does it.‚Źé‚ŹéThat matters. I saw it matter in my daughter's case. Her previous clinic didn't actively refuse to share her records. They just made the process complicated enough that it took three weeks. They said the records had to be printed and mailed, not sent electronically. They said the authorization had to be obtained in a specific form, not by email. They said the new clinic had to use a specific fax number, not their regular one. Each of these requirements was technically defensible. But together, they created a barrier to access that the information blocking rules are meant to prevent.‚Źé‚ŹéIf we narrow the definitions of "access" and "use," we might make room for more of that kind of obstruction. We might create a situation where providers can comply with the letter of the rule while violating its spirit.‚Źé‚ŹéThe rule proposes to clarify these definitions, and clarity has value. But the clarification should go in the direction of protecting access, not limiting it. The definitions should be clear that "access" means practical, timely access, not just theoretical access. They should be clear that "use" includes the ability to actually use the data for clinical care, not just the ability to receive it in some form.‚Źé‚Źé**THE STAKES FOR PATIENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéWhat's at stake here is whether patients and doctors have the information they need to make safe clinical decisions. That's not a technical question. It's a fundamental question about the kind of healthcare system we want to have.‚Źé‚ŹéI teach elementary school, and I teach my students that information matters. When you make a decision, you need to understand what you're deciding about. You need to know the facts. You need to know the limitations of what you know. That principle applies to clinical care. When my daughter's cardiologist recommends a treatment, my daughter has a right to understand what that recommendation is based on. If it's based partly on an AI tool, she has a right to know how that tool works, what it was trained on, and what its limitations are.‚Źé‚ŹéThat's not just good policy. It's basic respect for patients as people who deserve to understand the care they're receiving.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule moves away from that principle. It reduces transparency requirements for AI systems at the same time that AI is becoming more influential in clinical care. It narrows the definitions of access and use in information blocking rules, potentially making it easier for providers and developers to obstruct data sharing in practical terms while complying technically.‚Źé‚ŹéI understand the desire to reduce regulatory burden. Innovation and efficiency matter. But they shouldn't come at the cost of transparency and safety. They shouldn't come at the cost of patients' ability to understand the care they're receiving and the decisions being made about their health.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT I'M ASKING FOR**‚Źé‚ŹéMy specific recommendations are two. First, keep the DSI transparency requirements for AI clinical tools. Don't remove them. Don't weaken them. If anything, strengthen them. If developers are going to build tools that influence clinical decisions, they should be required to disclose how those tools work, what data they were trained on, what populations they were tested with, and what their known limitations are. That's not burdensome. It's essential.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, be careful about narrowing the definitions of "access" and "use" in information blocking rules. These definitions should protect practical access to health information, not just theoretical access. They should prevent obstruction, not facilitate it. If there are ways to clarify them that increase compliance without reducing protection, that would be valuable. But the default should be toward protecting access, not limiting it.‚Źé‚ŹéThese aren't radical recommendations. They're conservative in the best sense of the word. They're asking us to hold onto the safety protections that already exist for traditional medical devices and to extend them to AI systems that arguably need them more.‚Źé‚ŹéMy daughter eventually got her cardiac records transferred. Her new cardiologist has access to her test results now. But that three-week gap happened because the system allowed it. Systems weren't required to make data sharing easy. The medical devices she uses have disclosure requirements. The AI tools that might influence her care don't, under the proposed rule.‚Źé‚ŹéThat inconsistency is what troubles me. That's what I'm asking you to fix.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-02-05T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-05T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ9‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving DSI transparency requirements creates a dangerous regulatory gap where AI-based clinical decision support tools face fewer disclosure obligations than traditional medical devices, undermining consistent safety standards.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒconcerned‚ôĒAlabama‚ôĒelementary school teacher‚ôĒ64‚ôĒLast spring, my daughter had to switch cardiologists when our insurance changed, and I watched her spend three weeks calling back and forth between offices trying to get her previous test results transferred‚ÄĒrecords that were already digital, just sitting in another system. It frustrated me to see how much time and anxiety that caused her, but it also made me think about what would happen if there were even fewer rules protecting how those files move around, especially when I see how casually some of these younger doctors' offices seem to handle patient data.‚ôĒRemoving DSI transparency requirements creates a two-tiered safety standard where AI clinical tools have fewer disclosure obligations than traditional medical devices, leaving patients vulnerable to hidden risks and biased algorithms they don't know are influencing their care. | When AI systems make recommendations about patient treatment without requiring disclosure of training data, known limitations, or intended use populations, doctors and patients lose the information they need to catch errors‚ÄĒjust like my daughter lost three weeks of care continuity because systems weren't required to share basic data transparently.‚ôĒAs a parent and educator who values transparency and accountability, I'm concerned that weakening AI disclosure rules in healthcare creates exactly the kind of information gaps that already frustrate patients‚ÄĒexcept now the stakes are medical decisions, not just paperwork.‚ôĒFDA 2021 guidance on AI/ML-based Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) transparency requirements | Institute of Medicine report on diagnostic error and information availability in clinical settings | Journal of Medical Internet Research study on patient trust and algorithmic disclosure in clinical decision support (2022‚Äď2023 timeframe) | ONC Health IT Certification Program audit findings on information blocking exceptions and patient harm‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2707
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0111‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒBrian Brown‚ôĒTexas Medical Association‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Texas Medical Association opposes removing Decision Support Interventions certification requirements for AI clinical tools, arguing that transparency standards requiring vendors to disclose model validation, limitations, and training data char...‚ôĒBrian Brown, Research Director‚ŹéTexas Medical Association‚ŹéWritten Testimony for the Federal Register‚Źé‚ŹéRe: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity‚Źé‚ŹéThe Texas Medical Association appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory framework and offers this response based on direct experience with how artificial intelligence systems are currently being deployed in clinical settings across our member network. We represent roughly 50,000 physicians and health professionals in Texas, and through our research division, we have visibility into how clinical decision support tools are being implemented, evaluated, and‚ÄĒfrankly‚ÄĒsometimes misused when transparency requirements are unclear or absent.‚Źé‚ŹéLast month, while reviewing claims and utilization data from one of our member clinics in the Dallas area, I encountered a situation that made the stakes of this comment concrete. A patient's genetic markers were cross-referenced with their insurance claims history without explicit informed consent. The data came through a third-party analytics vendor operating in what I can only describe as a regulatory gray zone. The clinic had signed agreements with the vendor, but those agreements didn't explicitly authorize genetic data integration with financial information. When I flagged this to our legal team, they spent two weeks trying to determine whether this practice would be prohibited under current rules or simply require patient notification. They couldn't reach a clear answer. The vendor, meanwhile, kept processing data while everyone waited for clarification. That uncertainty is not a bug in the system. It is a feature for companies that profit from ambiguity.‚Źé‚ŹéThe reason I mention this incident is that it shaped how we approached the question of whether to recommend retention of the Decision Support Interventions certification criteria. The answer, after considerable internal deliberation, is yes. We believe the current DSI certification requirements should remain in place, and we want to explain why this matters for patient safety and clinical practice in ways that go beyond abstract privacy concerns.‚Źé‚ŹéThe existing certification criteria for DSI systems require vendors to provide model cards that disclose intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and results from external validation testing. This sounds technical, but it is actually a basic transparency standard that accomplishes something important: it forces vendors to be honest about what their systems can and cannot do. A model card is not a guarantee of perfection. It is a document that says, "Here is what we tested. Here is what we found. Here are the populations where we are confident and where we are not." Without this requirement, vendors can market tools with vague claims about accuracy or applicability, and clinicians are left guessing whether a recommendation applies to their specific patient.‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be clear about something that might not be obvious from the outside. Most vendors are not acting in bad faith. The companies we work with are genuinely trying to improve care. But the incentives in healthcare technology are structured in a way that rewards speed over clarity. If a vendor can get to market faster by skipping detailed validation in underrepresented populations, or by avoiding transparent documentation of algorithmic limitations, that vendor has a competitive advantage. The certification requirement does not eliminate this pressure, but it does create an enforceable baseline. Once you remove that baseline, you are betting that market forces alone will push vendors toward transparency. That is a bet against decades of experience in healthcare IT adoption.‚Źé‚ŹéThe clinical reality is that physicians are trained to be skeptical of new tools, but we are also under tremendous time pressure. In a busy clinic, a physician might use an AI system dozens of times per day. If that system is poorly validated, or validated only in populations that do not match the clinician's patient panel, problems emerge slowly. A tool that works well for middle-aged, privately insured patients with straightforward diagnoses might perform differently for elderly patients on multiple medications or for populations with higher rates of comorbidities. Without transparent documentation of validation scope, clinicians cannot even ask the right questions. They do not know what they do not know. That is the gap the DSI certification criteria are designed to close.‚Źé‚ŹéLet me give you a concrete example from our member feedback. One of our Texas cardiologists began using a diagnostic decision support tool last year that was marketed as appropriate for acute chest pain evaluation. The tool had been developed using a dataset of roughly 100,000 patient encounters. What the vendor's marketing materials did not clearly state, and what the cardiologist only discovered by contacting the vendor directly, was that the validation cohort was 89 percent male and drew heavily from a single academic medical center. When the cardiologist began using the tool in a community setting with a more diverse patient population, the recommendation patterns felt off. Some of the high-risk alerts were triggering for patients whose presentations seemed lower risk based on clinical judgment. The cardiologist was suspicious enough to investigate, but how many clinicians have the time or inclination to do that? If a certification criterion required transparent disclosure of demographic composition and geographic origin of training and validation data, this cardiologist would have known from the outset that the tool's recommendations required careful interpretation in her specific setting.‚Źé‚ŹéThe deregulatory proposal suggests that removing certification requirements will reduce burden on developers and accelerate innovation. We do not dispute that compliance with certification standards requires work. But the burden reduction comes at a cost, and that cost is borne primarily by clinicians and patients, not by developers. Developers will still need to validate their tools. They will still need to understand their systems' performance characteristics. Removing the certification requirement does not eliminate the work. It simply transfers the liability and the uncertainty to the healthcare system. Under the current framework, a vendor has a responsibility to document what they have tested and what they have found. Under the proposed framework, that responsibility becomes implicit and unenforceable. A clinician who uses an insufficiently validated tool bears the responsibility if something goes wrong, even if the tool's limitations were not transparent.‚Źé‚ŹéThe argument for deregulation in this space often rests on the assumption that competitive pressure will naturally incentivize transparency. We have heard this argument before in other healthcare IT contexts, and it has not held up well. When EHR systems were first being adopted, vendors competed on features and cost, not on usability or interoperability. The market did not naturally gravitate toward solutions that made data sharing easier or that prioritized clinician workflow. Regulation was required to push the industry in that direction. The Meaningful Use program, for all its imperfections, established baseline expectations that shaped vendor behavior over time. We believe something similar is necessary for AI-assisted clinical decision support. The certification criteria are not perfect, but they establish a floor below which vendors cannot go without losing certification. That floor matters.‚Źé‚ŹéThere is also a secondary argument about what happens when vendors begin collecting real-world performance data after deployment. Under the current framework, vendors are accountable for disclosing what they tested before bringing a tool to market. They are expected to be transparent about the limitations of their validation studies. Once a tool is deployed, real-world performance should ideally be monitored and reported back to the vendor and to clinicians. But without clear certification requirements, there is no mechanism to ensure this happens. A vendor could deploy a tool, discover through post-market surveillance that it performs poorly in a particular subpopulation, and choose not to disclose this finding if there is no regulatory obligation to do so. This is not theoretical. It happens. The FDA has documented multiple cases of AI tools that performed differently in clinical practice than they did in validation studies, and many of these discrepancies were discovered by clinicians or by independent researchers, not by the vendors themselves.‚Źé‚ŹéThe certification requirement for external validation is particularly important. Internal validation conducted by a vendor on their own data is valuable, but it is not sufficient. External validation means an independent group, typically at a different healthcare system, tests the tool in their own patient population and reports the results. This is where real limitations become apparent. A tool that performs well in the vendor's development environment might perform differently when deployed in a hospital with different EHR systems, different patient demographics, or different clinical workflows. External validation catches these gaps. It is also the point at which vendors often discover that their tool does not work as well as expected in certain subpopulations. If you remove the requirement for external validation as a condition of certification, you remove one of the only mechanisms that forces this information into the open.‚Źé‚ŹéOur concern is not that innovation will stop without certification requirements. Vendors will continue to develop tools. Clinicians will continue to adopt them. The question is whether we will have any systematic way to know whether those tools are safe and appropriate for the populations using them. The certification criteria create a record. They create accountability. They create a baseline expectation that vendors must meet to sell their products into the healthcare system. Remove that requirement, and you are relying on individual clinicians to evaluate tools independently. Some will do this well. Many will not have the time, expertise, or incentive to do so. Patients will bear the consequences of that information asymmetry.‚Źé‚ŹéWe also want to flag something that has emerged from our research over the past eighteen months. There is a growing practice of using AI systems to identify which patients might benefit from particular treatments or interventions, and then marketing those recommendations to patients directly or to payers without transparent disclosure of how the system works or what it was trained on. This is different from clinical decision support, where a tool is recommending something to a clinician. In this case, the AI system is essentially making a clinical determination and then marketing the result. The patient or the payer may not even know that an algorithmic recommendation was involved. If we remove certification requirements for decision support tools, the regulatory gap for these kinds of applications only widens. At minimum, we would urge the agency to maintain transparency requirements for any AI system that generates clinical recommendations that affect patient care or coverage decisions.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Texas Medical Association has not taken a blanket anti-innovation position. We recognize that AI tools can improve clinical outcomes when they are well-designed, appropriately validated, and transparently presented to clinicians. We have members who are excited about the potential of these technologies. But that potential is only realized if clinicians can trust the tools they are using. Trust requires transparency. The DSI certification criteria are one mechanism for ensuring that transparency. They are not the only mechanism, and we are open to discussing whether the specific requirements should be modified or streamlined. But removing them entirely would be a mistake.‚Źé‚ŹéWe have also observed, and this is something that did not make it into our initial written comments, that there is significant variation in how certification criteria are currently being applied across different vendor types. Large vendors with established compliance infrastructure can navigate certification requirements relatively easily. Smaller vendors and academic institutions developing tools often find the process more burdensome. If the goal is to reduce burden while maintaining transparency, there might be a better approach than simply eliminating certification. The agency could consider creating a tiered certification system where vendors can choose different levels of documentation and validation rigor based on the scope and risk profile of their tools. A low-risk tool might require less extensive documentation. A high-risk tool that affects major treatment decisions would require more. This approach would maintain the transparency baseline while reducing unnecessary burden on vendors developing less critical applications.‚Źé‚ŹéThe specific language of the current certification criterion requires that vendors provide model cards disclosing intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. We believe this language is appropriate and should be retained. The only modification we would suggest is clarifying what "known risks" means in this context. Does it mean risks identified during validation? Does it include risks identified in post-market surveillance? We would recommend that the criterion specify that vendors must disclose risks identified through any credible source, including academic literature, clinician reports, and post-market surveillance. This would create an ongoing obligation rather than a one-time documentation requirement.‚Źé‚ŹéWe also want to note that maintaining certification requirements for DSI systems is consistent with how the FDA regulates software as a medical device. The FDA requires that developers of clinical decision support software provide documentation of intended use, validation scope, and known limitations. The ONC certification criteria are not more stringent than FDA requirements in most respects. They are actually less stringent in some ways. Maintaining them would not put the healthcare IT industry at a competitive disadvantage relative to other regulated medical device markets. It would simply keep the standards aligned with established practice in healthcare regulation.‚Źé‚ŹéFinally, we want to acknowledge that there is legitimate debate about whether the specific validation standards are appropriate or whether they should be modified based on emerging evidence about how AI systems perform in clinical practice. We are not suggesting that the current standards are perfect or that they should never be revisited. What we are saying is that the answer to imperfect standards is better standards, not no standards. The agency has the opportunity to engage with stakeholders over the next several years to understand how certification requirements are affecting vendors and clinicians, and to refine the approach based on that feedback. We would welcome the opportunity to participate in that process. But removing the requirements preemptively, before we have good data on whether they are actually creating the burden that advocates claim they are, would be premature.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Texas Medical Association therefore recommends that the agency retain the current Decision Support Interventions certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures. We further recommend that the agency consider clarifying the definition of "known risks" to include risks identified through post-market surveillance and academic literature, and that the agency engage with vendors and clinicians to understand whether the current validation standards are appropriately calibrated. We believe that maintaining transparency requirements for clinical decision support tools is essential to protecting patient safety and ensuring that physicians can make informed decisions about which tools to use in their practice.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-31T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-31T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ8‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒfrustrated‚ôĒIndiana‚ôĒresearch director‚ôĒ56‚ôĒLast month, I reviewed a dataset from one of our member clinics where a patient's genetic markers were cross-referenced with their insurance claims without explicit consent‚ÄĒthe information came through a third-party analytics vendor operating in a gray zone. When I flagged it to our legal team, they couldn't determine whether this would be prohibited or require notification under the new framework, and that uncertainty itself felt like a concession to the vendors who profit from these ambiguities.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2403
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0112‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒAshley Lewis‚ôĒSouth Carolina Primary Health Care Association‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe South Carolina Primary Health Care Association opposes removing Decision Support Interventions certification requirements for AI model transparency and validation disclosures, arguing that without mandatory documentation of training data, inte...‚ôĒSouth Carolina Primary Health Care Association‚ŹéComments on HHS/ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions Notice‚Źé‚ŹéThe South Carolina Primary Health Care Association represents 41 federally qualified health centers and safety-net providers across the state, serving over 380,000 patients annually, including 67,000 uninsured individuals and many with limited English proficiency. We appreciate the opportunity to comment on the proposed modifications to certification criteria and information blocking regulations under the Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability initiative.‚Źé‚ŹéWe support the Administration's goal of reducing unnecessary regulatory burden on health IT developers and providers. Our members operate under severe resource constraints, and administrative complexity does add cost. However, we must raise a serious concern about the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements, specifically the requirement for AI model cards and transparency disclosures covering intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation. This particular criterion should be retained, and I want to explain why based on what we actually see in our clinics.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PROBLEM WE'RE WITNESSING**‚Źé‚ŹéThree months ago, one of our affiliated providers in Charleston integrated an AI-assisted clinical decision tool for diabetes management without full transparency about its development. The tool recommended against insulin therapy for a 62-year-old patient with Type 2 diabetes and declining kidney function. The clinician, trusting the system without understanding its limitations, delayed appropriate treatment for six weeks. The patient's condition worsened. When we later requested information about the model's training data and validation across different patient populations, the vendor could not provide it. We had no way to know the tool had been trained primarily on data from non-Hispanic white populations and had not been externally validated in our patient population, which is 58% African American and includes significant numbers of patients with comorbidities the training data underrepresented.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not a hypothetical concern. The 21st Century Cures Act Information Blocking Rule (42 CFR 171.102) requires transparency, but it does not mandate that vendors disclose how their AI systems were built or what they were tested on. The ONC's 2021 guidance on AI governance recommended model cards and transparency documentation (Federal Register Vol. 86, No. 10), but certification requirements made compliance meaningful. Without certification criteria requiring these disclosures, vendors have no regulatory incentive to provide them, and our clinicians have no systematic way to evaluate whether an AI tool is safe for their specific patient population.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THIS MATTERS FOR SAFETY AND EQUITY**‚Źé‚ŹéOur organization works with patients who face documented health disparities. African American patients in our network experience higher rates of diabetes complications and mortality. Latino patients often have limited access to interpretation services and may not fully understand how their data is being used in algorithmic decision-making. Undocumented patients, like Maria (the woman I mentioned earlier), have heightened privacy concerns and are more likely to disengage from care if they don't understand or trust the systems involved in their treatment.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen vendors are not required to disclose model card information, transparency disclosures about intended use, or external validation results, our clinicians cannot make informed decisions about whether to use these tools. The burden shifts entirely to us. We become responsible for reverse-engineering vendor claims and testing tools ourselves, which is impossible for safety-net providers operating without data science staff. The proposed deregulation would make this worse, not better.‚Źé‚Źé**OUR RECOMMENDATION**‚Źé‚ŹéWe ask that the DSI certification criterion requiring AI model cards, transparency disclosures, intended use documentation, target population specification, known risks identification, training data characteristics, and external validation be retained in any final rule. This is a narrow, specific requirement that directly supports patient safety and informed clinical decision-making. It does not require vendors to open-source their models or reveal proprietary algorithms. It requires only that they document what they built, what it was tested on, and what it works for.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving this criterion in the name of deregulation would place the burden of AI safety and equity on health centers that lack the resources to bear it. Our patients deserve better.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-21T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-21T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒurgent‚ôĒMaryland‚ôĒcommunity organizer‚ôĒ65‚ôĒLast month, I was helping Maria, a 58-year-old undocumented woman in our clinic network, understand her diabetes treatment options when her insurance company suddenly had access to her full mental health records without her knowledge‚ÄĒrecords that included notes about her immigration status concerns. She became terrified to seek any mental health support after that, and I watched her disengage from care entirely. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0114‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒSarah Wilson‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA Louisiana veteran argues that safety and transparency requirements for AI tools used in medical decision-making should not be weakened, citing concerns about health information security and the need to understand how AI systems are trained and w...‚ôĒMy name is Sarah Wilson and I'm a veteran in Louisiana. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0115‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒSandra Taylor‚ôĒSouth Carolina Primary Health Care Association‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe South Carolina Primary Health Care Association opposes removing Decision Support Intervention (DSI) certification requirements, arguing that transparency documentation regarding AI model training data, intended use, and external validation is ...‚ôĒRe: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity (RFI)‚Źé‚ŹéSouth Carolina Primary Health Care Association respectfully submits this comment on the proposed deregulatory framework. We represent 43 federally qualified health centers and primary care networks serving over 380,000 patients across South Carolina, with particular focus on rural and underserved populations. Our membership includes rural health clinics, community health centers, and integrated primary care networks where artificial intelligence and decision support tools are increasingly embedded into clinical workflows.‚Źé‚ŹéWe write with specific concern about the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria that currently require AI model cards, transparency disclosures, and external validation documentation. Our position is not ideological opposition to deregulation. We understand the genuine burden that certification requirements place on developers and the real cost pressures affecting innovation. What troubles us is the gap between the stated goal of "unleashing prosperity" and what happens on the ground when we lose the transparency mechanisms that allow us to use these tools responsibly in vulnerable patient populations.‚Źé‚Źé**The Certification Requirement Serves a Real Clinical Function**‚Źé‚ŹéOver the past eighteen months, our organization has worked with four health IT vendors implementing AI-assisted diagnostic support tools in our primary care network. This is not theoretical. Three of those vendors provided detailed model cards, training data characteristics, known limitations, and external validation results. One did not. The difference in our ability to deploy these tools safely was substantial. When we had the transparency data, our clinical leadership could actually make informed decisions about which patient populations the tool was appropriate for, what conditions it performed well on, and what it performed poorly on. We could read the training data summary and immediately recognize that one vendor's model was trained predominantly on urban, insured populations and therefore we needed additional validation before using it for our rural Medicaid population.‚Źé‚ŹéThe vendor that refused to provide this documentation? We ultimately did not implement their tool. Not because of regulatory burden on us as a user, but because we had no way to answer basic questions our clinicians asked. What populations was this trained on? What does "accuracy of 92 percent" actually mean in our clinical context? Are there known disparities in performance across demographic groups? These are not bureaucratic gotchas. These are the questions any responsible healthcare organization should ask before letting an algorithm influence clinical decisions that affect real patients.‚Źé‚ŹéI keep returning to something that happened in our clinic network in rural Orangeburg County last year. One of our physicians used a diagnostic support tool that none of us knew had not been externally validated. The tool flagged a patient's presenting symptoms as low-risk for a particular condition. The physician followed the tool's output. The patient was actually presenting with an atypical presentation of that condition. The outcome was not catastrophic because the physician happened to do a follow-up visit, but it could have been. When we pulled the documentation on that tool afterward, we discovered the vendor had no external validation data and the tool had been trained on a dataset that explicitly excluded patients with certain comorbidities that were present in our patient population. This is exactly the scenario that DSI certification criteria exist to prevent.‚Źé‚Źé**The Transparency Gap Disproportionately Affects Our Patients**‚Źé‚ŹéOur concern is not abstract. The population we serve is the population most likely to distrust healthcare systems. We work with patients who have experienced medical discrimination, who have reason to be skeptical of technologies they don't understand, and who are already less likely to engage with care if they feel their autonomy is being eroded. We have spent fifteen years building trust with these communities. When a patient asks us whether we're using an algorithm to make decisions about their care, we owe them an honest answer. We owe them the ability to understand what that algorithm is doing. We owe them information about whether that algorithm has been tested on people like them.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification requirement does not prevent AI use. It requires documentation of intended use, target population, known risks, and training data characteristics. This is minimal transparency, not prohibition. Under the proposed deregulation, vendors would be able to market tools with no requirement to disclose these basic facts. Smaller developers might benefit from reduced burden, but larger organizations like ours would face an asymmetric information problem. We would have incentive to demand this information anyway, but we would have no leverage to obtain it if no regulation requires it.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider the specific language in 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1), which establishes the current DSI certification criterion. The requirement is straightforward: vendors must provide "a description of the intended use of the decision support intervention" and "characteristics of the training data, including whether the model was trained on a representative sample of the population to which it will be applied." This is not onerous documentation. We have reviewed dozens of these model cards. A thorough one is typically five to eight pages. The burden is real but manageable. The benefit to clinical users is substantial.‚Źé‚ŹéWe recognize that the proposed rule operates within a particular regulatory philosophy. The preamble suggests that market forces and voluntary disclosure will drive appropriate transparency. In theory, this is reasonable. In practice, we have seen vendors resist transparency documentation even when it is currently required. The incentive structure does not favor voluntary disclosure when a competitor will not disclose. We will face pressure to adopt tools from vendors who do not provide model cards because those vendors can undercut on price. We will not adopt them, and we will face competitive disadvantage because we are unwilling to operate without transparency. That is not innovation. That is a race to the bottom.‚Źé‚Źé**Specific Implementation Issues and Recommendations**‚Źé‚ŹéOur concern extends to the interaction between DSI certification removal and the information blocking regulations established under 42 U.S.C. ¬ß 300jj-52(a). The current framework allows us to refuse information blocking liability when we decline to integrate a tool that does not meet certification standards. If certification criteria are removed, this safe harbor disappears. We would be forced to make a binary choice: integrate tools without knowing their characteristics, or risk information blocking liability. This is not deregulation. This is shifting regulatory burden from developers to users.‚Źé‚ŹéWe would urge the agency to consider a middle path. Rather than removing DSI certification entirely, consider whether the certification criterion could be streamlined while retaining the core transparency requirements. The specific elements that matter are clear: intended use, target population, known risks, and training data characteristics. These could be certified through a lighter-weight process, perhaps through third-party attestation rather than full FDA-style review. The burden would be reduced. The transparency would remain.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the agency proceeds with removal, we would ask for explicit protection for healthcare providers who decline to implement non-certified tools. Add language to 45 CFR 171.202 clarifying that information blocking liability does not apply when a provider refuses to integrate a decision support tool on the basis that the vendor did not provide transparency documentation regarding training data, intended use, or external validation. This would preserve the incentive for vendors to provide documentation even if certification is not mandated.‚Źé‚ŹéThe stakes are not abstract regulatory compliance. They are whether our physicians can make informed decisions about the tools they use. They are whether our patients in rural South Carolina have any mechanism to understand how algorithms are influencing their care. They are whether smaller healthcare organizations like ours can compete without being forced to adopt tools we cannot evaluate.‚Źé‚ŹéWe support innovation. We want better tools. But we want them deployed responsibly. The DSI certification criterion is a modest requirement that enables that responsibility. Its removal would create a genuine gap in our ability to serve our population safely.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-16T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-16T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ4‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒfrustrated‚ôĒTennessee‚ôĒresearch director‚ôĒ57‚ôĒLast year, I discovered that one of our clinic networks had been sharing patient demographic data with a third-party vendor without explicit consent‚ÄĒburied in a terms-of-service update nobody reads. When we tried to tighten our protocols, we found the regulatory framework was so fragmented across state lines that compliance became nearly impossible, and we ended up spending more on legal review than on actual patient outreach. Now we're watching these guardrails loosen further, and I keep thinking about the patients in our rural and underserved programs who already distrust the healthcare system‚ÄĒthey're the ones most likely to opt out of care entirely if they lose confidence in our discretion.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1300
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0116‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒMichelle Garcia‚ôĒSAFI Clinical Informatics Group LLC‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒSAFI Clinical Informatics Group argues that Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements should be retained because they provide essential transparency about AI-assisted clinical tools' training data, intended populations, and l...‚ôĒ**SAFI CLINICAL INFORMATICS GROUP LLC**‚Źé‚ŹéRE: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity (RFI)‚Źé‚ŹéSAFI Clinical Informatics Group LLC submits this comment regarding the proposed modifications to health IT certification requirements, specifically as they relate to Decision Support Interventions (DSI) and the certification criteria for AI-assisted clinical decision tools. Our organization has spent the last eighteen months implementing a certified electronic health record system across multiple clinical settings, and this experience has given us a front-row perspective on both the benefits and genuine limitations of the current certification framework.‚Źé‚Źé**OUR CONTEXT AND EXPERIENCE**‚Źé‚ŹéSAFI Clinical Informatics Group works with mid-sized healthcare delivery networks and specialty clinics in North Carolina and the broader Southeast. We serve in a regulatory affairs capacity, which means we sit at the intersection of vendor relationships, compliance obligations, and clinical operations. That positioning has been instructive. When we began our EHR implementation in 2022, we had high expectations that a certified system would meet our population's specific needs. Our patient cohort includes a significant proportion of individuals with multiple chronic conditions and limited English proficiency, with distinct documentation and care pathway requirements that differ from the general population the EHR was designed to serve.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat we discovered was that the certified EHR, while meeting the minimum standards required for certification, lacked the flexibility to customize clinical workflows and decision support logic for our population. We ended up running parallel spreadsheets for nearly a year to track outcomes, medication interactions, and care coordination metrics that the certified system simply couldn't be configured to address. This wasn't the vendor's fault in any simple sense. The certification process doesn't require‚ÄĒand in fact may discourage‚ÄĒthe kind of iterative customization that real-world clinical operations demand. The vendor had already invested in achieving certification; they weren't eager to modify core modules that might require recertification.‚Źé‚ŹéWe eventually resolved this through workarounds that cost us roughly $180,000 in staff time and external consulting. That's a real number from our actual budget. It's also the kind of cost that smaller practices simply cannot absorb, which means they either accept the limitations of their certified system or they fall out of compliance trying to build local solutions. This is the background against which we offer our perspective on the proposed deregulation.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CASE FOR RETAINING DSI CERTIFICATION REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current Decision Support Interventions criterion requires vendors to provide model cards and transparency disclosures that document intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. We understand the impulse to remove this requirement as part of a broader deregulatory agenda. Certification is slow. It's expensive. It does create barriers to rapid innovation. But the DSI criterion is not where the problem lies, and removing it would create genuine patient safety risks that far outweigh any compliance burden reduction.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's why this matters to us specifically. Last year, one of our network's affiliated clinics began using a decision support tool that recommended antibiotic prescribing protocols based on patient symptomatology. The tool was not subject to the DSI certification requirement because it was considered a general-purpose clinical decision aid, not a certified component. What we discovered when we dug into the documentation was that the tool had been trained primarily on data from academic medical centers with predominantly White patient populations. Our clinic serves a Latinx community with distinct patterns of antimicrobial resistance, different baseline health profiles, and different medication tolerability profiles. The tool's recommendations were systematically misaligned with our population's actual clinical needs. We caught this because our medical director manually validated the tool's recommendations against our local outcomes data. But that's exactly the kind of validation work that the current DSI criterion exists to document upfront, not catch retrospectively.‚Źé‚ŹéWithout the transparency requirements embedded in the DSI criterion, there is no systematic mechanism to prevent this mismatch. A vendor could deploy a decision support tool across dozens of health systems with no documented disclosure of the populations it was actually designed for, the validation studies it was based on, or the known limitations of its training data. The burden of discovering misalignment then falls entirely on individual providers, which means the providers with the least resources and the most vulnerable populations bear the highest risk.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI criterion is also not a barrier to innovation in the way that other certification requirements might be. Model cards and transparency documentation don't prevent innovation; they document what's actually being deployed. They make the innovation visible and testable. Removing them doesn't accelerate innovation. It obscures it.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT THE DSI CRITERION ACTUALLY DOES**‚Źé‚ŹéLet's be specific about what the current 45 CFR 170.315(a)(4) DSI criterion requires:‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ Vendors must disclose the intended use of the decision support tool, including the specific clinical scenarios and patient populations for which it was designed.‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ They must provide information about the training data, including its source, size, temporal characteristics, and demographic composition.‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ They must document known risks and limitations, including any populations for which the tool's performance has not been adequately validated.‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ They must provide evidence of external validation on datasets that reflect the diversity of real-world clinical populations.‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ They must maintain an audit trail showing how the model was updated and when.‚Źé‚ŹéThese are not burdensome requirements. They are documentation requirements. A vendor that has actually done the work to develop a robust decision support tool already has this information. What the requirement does is make that information visible to the providers who will use the tool and to the patients whose care will be affected by it. It's an accountability mechanism, not a development barrier.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider the National Academies' 2022 report on algorithmic bias in healthcare (National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine, "Algorithmic Bias in Health Care" 2022). The report identified transparency requirements as one of the most effective mechanisms for preventing harm from biased algorithms. The researchers noted that vendors with high-quality training data and rigorous validation were generally comfortable disclosing this information, while vendors with limited validation or poor performance on minority populations often resisted transparency requirements. That pattern is exactly what we should expect.‚Źé‚Źé**THE INNOVATION ARGUMENT IS MISPLACED**‚Źé‚ŹéThe deregulatory proposal frames certification requirements as barriers to innovation. But our actual experience with rapid innovation in health IT has not been blocked by documentation requirements. What blocks innovation is the need to maintain backward compatibility with existing systems, the challenges of health data interoperability, the liability concerns around making changes to deployed systems, and the basic complexity of integrating new tools into clinical workflows. None of these barriers would be removed by eliminating DSI certification requirements.‚Źé‚ŹéIf anything, the pressure to innovate faster without documentation accountability creates perverse incentives. A vendor can deploy a decision support tool more quickly if it doesn't have to document its limitations. But that speed is only valuable to the vendor and to the early adopters who gain competitive advantage. The broader healthcare system‚ÄĒand especially vulnerable populations‚ÄĒlose when deployment outpaces understanding of what's actually being deployed.‚Źé‚ŹéWe're not opposed to reducing burden where burden doesn't serve a purpose. But the burden we actually experienced in implementing our EHR wasn't primarily driven by certification requirements. It was driven by the difficulty of integrating multiple vendors' tools, the limitations of data standards for representing our specific population's needs, and the fact that the vendor had already optimized their product for a general market rather than the specific use case we needed.‚Źé‚Źé**A TARGETED APPROACH TO BURDEN REDUCTION**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the goal is genuine burden reduction without compromising patient safety, we'd suggest a more targeted approach. The current certification process has real inefficiencies. Recertification timelines are often lengthy. The process for updating certified tools to incorporate new validation evidence is cumbersome. There are genuine opportunities to streamline certification without eliminating the transparency requirements that make AI-assisted decision support accountable.‚Źé‚ŹéFor instance, consider a tiered certification approach. Decision support tools that are deployed in lower-risk clinical contexts (for example, tools that provide informational support but don't directly influence prescribing or treatment decisions) could be subject to lighter-touch documentation requirements. Tools that directly influence high-risk clinical decisions (antibiotic selection, dosing decisions, triage recommendations) would maintain the current transparency requirements. This approach would reduce burden for innovation in lower-risk areas while maintaining accountability where it matters most.‚Źé‚ŹéAnother approach would be to streamline the recertification process for tools that are updated with new validation evidence. If a vendor wants to update a decision support tool to improve its performance on underrepresented populations, that should be encouraged, not delayed by lengthy recertification processes. The current system sometimes creates an incentive to leave tools as-is rather than improve them.‚Źé‚Źé**INFORMED CLINICAL DECISION-MAKING DEPENDS ON TRANSPARENCY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe core purpose of the DSI criterion is to ensure that clinicians have the information they need to make informed decisions about whether and how to use decision support tools in their practice. This is not a regulatory luxury. It's fundamental to the informed consent principle that underlies all clinical practice.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen a clinician uses a decision support tool, they're making an implicit claim that the tool is appropriate for their patient and their clinical context. That claim is only justified if the clinician actually knows what the tool was designed for, what populations it was validated on, and what its known limitations are. Without that information, the clinician is using the tool blindly, which is not meaningfully different from random decision-making.‚Źé‚ŹéWe've seen this play out in practice. One of our network's clinics began using a sepsis prediction tool that was trained on ICU data from a large academic medical center. The tool was being used in our clinic's emergency department, which serves a much less acutely ill population overall. The tool's predictions were systematically overestimating risk in our population. Clinicians who didn't understand the tool's training context were unnecessarily escalating care. Without access to the tool's documentation of its training data and validation results, there was no way for our medical director to identify this mismatch quickly. With that documentation, it took about an hour to understand the problem.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is what the DSI criterion enables: the informed, accountable use of AI-assisted decision support in clinical practice.‚Źé‚Źé**ACCOUNTABILITY AND LIABILITY**‚Źé‚ŹéThere's also a liability dimension here that the deregulatory proposal doesn't adequately address. If a decision support tool causes harm because it was misaligned with a patient population, who bears responsibility? If the tool was certified with transparency requirements, and the vendor provided the documentation, and a provider chose to use it anyway without reviewing the documentation, the liability picture is relatively clear. If the tool was never required to disclose its limitations, the liability picture becomes murkier. The provider might reasonably argue that they had no way to know the tool was inappropriate for their population. The vendor might argue that they disclosed everything they were required to disclose (which would be nothing). The patient is harmed either way.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving the DSI certification requirement doesn't eliminate this risk. It just shifts it around without reducing it. And it likely shifts it toward providers and patients who are least equipped to bear it.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéSAFI Clinical Informatics Group supports streamlining health IT certification where streamlining serves the goal of enabling innovation without compromising patient safety. But the DSI criterion is not an obstacle to innovation. It's a transparency mechanism that makes AI-assisted decision support accountable. Removing it would create genuine risks to patient safety, particularly for vulnerable populations who are already at higher risk of harm from biased or misaligned clinical decision tools.‚Źé‚ŹéWe recommend retaining the current DSI certification requirements under 45 CFR 170.315(a)(4). We also recommend considering targeted reforms to the recertification process to make it easier for vendors to update tools with new validation evidence, and a tiered approach that calibrates certification burden to clinical risk.‚Źé‚ŹéThe goal of regulation should not be to eliminate burden, but to eliminate burden that doesn't serve a purpose. The DSI criterion serves a clear purpose: ensuring that clinicians and patients have the information they need to use AI-assisted decision support safely and appropriately. That's worth the compliance cost.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-19T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-19T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ6‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒsupportive‚ôĒNorth Carolina‚ôĒVP of regulatory affairs‚ôĒ54‚ôĒMy team spent eighteen months implementing a certified EHR system that couldn't be customized to track our specific patient population's needs, and we ultimately had to work around it with parallel spreadsheets‚ÄĒa costly workaround that wouldn't have been necessary if vendors could innovate faster without the certification bottleneck slowing everything down.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2016
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0117‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDavid Smith‚ôĒClinovations Government + Health‚ôĒ‚ôĒGovernment Agency‚ôĒ‚ôĒClinovations Government + Health opposes eliminating Decision Support Interventions certification requirements, arguing that model card and transparency disclosure mandates serve critical functions in ensuring clinicians understand AI tool perform...‚ôĒClinovations Government + Health respectfully submits this comment regarding the proposed deregulatory actions under the Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability initiative. The organization does not oppose innovation or burden reduction in health IT certification. However, the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements warrants careful reconsideration, particularly the elimination of model card and transparency disclosure mandates. State health departments and clinical delivery systems have invested substantially in AI-assisted decision support tools over the past five years, and the current certification framework has provided necessary accountability structures that should not be dismantled without understanding their operational role in practice.‚Źé‚Źé**THE VERMONT EXPERIENCE WITH COMPLIANCE INFRASTRUCTURE**‚Źé‚ŹéIn 2015, Vermont's Department of Health aligned its disease surveillance system with newly established federal reporting standards. The effort took eighteen months and consumed resources that state officials had allocated for other priorities. Three full-time positions went unfilled that fiscal year because the compliance infrastructure build-out consumed the available budget. Staff members worked extended hours to implement the new data pipeline, rebuild legacy systems, and validate that all outputs met federal specifications. In hindsight, some of that investment proved worthwhile. The standardized reporting framework did improve data quality and allowed for better interstate comparison of disease trends. But here is what matters now: when federal requirements change direction, the sunk costs remain sunk, and the opportunity costs become visible in ways they were not before.‚Źé‚ŹéThat Vermont experience shapes how Clinovations Government + Health views this proposal. The organization has worked with state agencies and health systems implementing AI-assisted clinical decision support tools. These tools are now embedded in workflows at seventeen hospitals and thirty-two primary care practices across three states. They assist with sepsis screening, medication interaction flagging, and evidence-based protocol recommendations. Clinicians using these tools have expressed confidence in them, but that confidence rests partly on the understanding that the tools have undergone third-party validation and that their training data characteristics, intended use parameters, and known limitations have been documented and disclosed. The proposed rule would remove that certification requirement.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT THE CURRENT DSI CRITERION ACTUALLY DOES**‚Źé‚ŹéThe existing Decision Support Interventions certification requirement mandates that AI models intended for clinical decision support include a model card documenting: (1) the intended use case, (2) the target population for which the model was validated, (3) known performance limitations and failure modes, (4) characteristics of the training data, and (5) results of external validation testing. This is not an onerous requirement. It is a transparency requirement. The model card is a document, typically fifteen to thirty pages, that describes what the tool does, what it was tested on, what it does not do well, and what clinicians should know before using it.‚Źé‚ŹéWithout this requirement, clinicians and health system administrators have no standardized mechanism for understanding an AI tool's actual scope and limitations. Marketing materials from vendors often describe what tools are designed to do, not what they actually do across diverse patient populations. The model card creates a common language between vendors and users. It allows a clinician in Vermont to understand whether an AI tool validated on a predominantly white, insured population in Massachusetts will perform similarly for the clinician's patient population, which has higher rates of Medicaid coverage and greater racial diversity.‚Źé‚ŹéClinovations Government + Health has reviewed model cards from twelve AI decision support tools currently in use at partner health systems. The variation in model performance across different demographic groups is substantial. One sepsis detection tool performed with 87 percent sensitivity in the overall validation cohort but only 76 percent sensitivity in patients over age seventy-five and 71 percent sensitivity in patients with chronic kidney disease. Without the model card requirement, that hospital system would have had no systematic way to discover that the tool performed worse on its oldest and sickest patients. The clinicians would have relied on vendor marketing claims and their own subjective impression of the tool's usefulness, which is not a reliable basis for clinical decision-making.‚Źé‚Źé**PATIENT SAFETY AND INFORMED DECISION-MAKING**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed deregulation frames certification requirements as barriers to innovation. This framing misses the actual function these requirements serve in clinical practice. Certification is not about preventing AI tools from being used. It is about ensuring that clinicians and health system administrators have accurate information about what those tools do and do not do. Removing transparency requirements does not accelerate innovation. It obscures the limits of existing innovations and makes it harder for clinicians to make informed decisions about when and how to use decision support tools.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider a concrete scenario. A primary care practice in rural Vermont implements an AI tool designed to identify patients at high risk for heart failure readmission. The vendor markets the tool as achieving 91 percent sensitivity in identifying high-risk patients. The practice adopts it. After three months, the practice realizes that the tool is flagging almost all of its patients as high-risk, making the alerts clinically useless. The practice then discovers, by reading a published paper about the tool, that the model was trained primarily on data from urban hospitals with more comprehensive outpatient monitoring infrastructure. In rural settings with less intensive follow-up, the tool's performance characteristics are different. This could have been known upfront if the model card requirement had been in place and enforced. Instead, the practice wasted time and resources implementing a tool that was not fit for its clinical context.‚Źé‚ŹéThat scenario is not hypothetical.
