Similar implementation challenges have occurred at three of the seventeen hospitals where Clinovations Government + Health has worked with health system leadership. In each case, the clinicians and administrators had invested time in training staff, integrating the tool into workflows, and waiting for promised clinical benefits. When the tool did not perform as expected in their specific clinical context, the disappointment was real. The wasted time was real. The damage to clinician trust in decision support tools was real. A more transparent certification process would have prevented those problems.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CERTIFICATION REQUIREMENT IS NOT PREVENTING INNOVATION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe deregulation proposal implies that certification requirements are slowing the development and deployment of AI decision support tools. Clinovations Government + Health does not see evidence for this claim in the market. The health IT market for clinical decision support tools is growing rapidly. Vendors are introducing new tools regularly. Health systems are adopting these tools at an accelerating pace. The certification requirement has been in place for several years now, and it has not prevented market entry. Instead, it has created a market segment for vendors who can demonstrate that their tools meet transparent performance standards. Some vendors choose not to pursue certification, and they sell their tools directly to health systems without the certification credential. That is a choice. It is not a barrier.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat the certification requirement does is create differentiation in the market. A tool that carries a certification credential signals that it has been independently validated and that its performance characteristics are documented. Health systems that care about this information can seek out certified tools. Health systems that do not care can purchase non-certified tools. The market can decide. Removing the certification requirement does not accelerate innovation. It just removes the signal that allows health systems to distinguish between tools that have been validated and tools that have not.‚Źé‚ŹéClinovations Government + Health works with state health departments and health systems that have limited resources for evaluating complex health IT tools. When a tool carries a certification credential with a documented model card, those organizations can make faster purchasing decisions. They do not have to conduct their own validation studies or hire consultants to evaluate the tool's performance. The certification requirement serves a real function for resource-constrained purchasers. Removing it would shift the burden of validation back to individual health systems, which would actually slow adoption of proven tools and make it harder for smaller organizations to compete for talent and resources in the health IT market.‚Źé‚Źé**SECTION HEADING: SPECIFIC CONCERNS WITH THE PROPOSED REMOVAL**‚Źé‚ŹéClinovations Government + Health has three specific concerns about the proposed removal of DSI certification requirements:‚Źé‚Źé1. Loss of standardized transparency. Without the model card requirement, there is no standardized mechanism for vendors to disclose what their tools do and do not do. Some vendors will choose to disclose this information voluntarily. Others will not. Health systems will have no basis for comparing tools or understanding their limitations. This creates information asymmetry that favors vendors with the largest marketing budgets and disadvantages smaller health systems and rural providers.‚Źé‚Źé2. Increased liability risk for health systems and clinicians. When a tool performs poorly in a specific clinical context and results in patient harm, liability will shift away from vendors and toward the health systems and clinicians using the tool. If the vendor made no specific claims about the tool's performance and provided no documentation of its limitations, the health system cannot argue that it relied on accurate vendor disclosures. The health system will bear the liability for implementing a tool whose performance it did not fully understand. This is not a benefit to the health system or to patient safety.‚Źé‚Źé3. Reduced ability for state health departments to oversee quality. State health departments have responsibility for monitoring the quality and safety of clinical care within their jurisdictions. When tools are used in clinical settings without standardized documentation of their performance characteristics, state oversight becomes more difficult. State health departments cannot audit what they cannot see. The model card requirement allows state agencies to understand what tools are being used, what they are supposed to do, and whether their performance aligns with vendor claims. Removing this requirement reduces state oversight capacity.‚Źé‚Źé**ADDRESSING POTENTIAL COUNTERARGUMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal may suggest that removing certification requirements will reduce costs for vendors and allow them to bring tools to market faster. Clinovations Government + Health acknowledges that certification has some cost. However, the cost is not prohibitive. Vendors already conduct validation studies for marketing purposes. They already document their tools' performance characteristics. The certification requirement simply formalizes that documentation in a standardized format. The incremental cost of creating a model card and submitting it for review is modest relative to the total development cost of an AI tool.‚Źé‚ŹéFurthermore, the proposal may argue that market competition will ensure that vendors provide accurate information about their tools without certification requirements. This argument assumes that buyers have sufficient expertise and resources to evaluate AI tools independently. For large health systems with dedicated health IT departments, this may be true. For smaller health systems, rural hospitals, and state health departments, it is not. These organizations lack the technical expertise to validate AI tools independently. They rely on certification and third-party validation to make purchasing decisions. Removing the certification requirement would disadvantage these organizations and consolidate market power among vendors with the largest marketing budgets and sales forces.‚Źé‚Źé**ALIGNMENT WITH BROADER POLICY GOALS**‚Źé‚ŹéClinovations Government + Health supports the administration's goal of reducing unnecessary regulatory burden. However, this specific requirement does not appear to fit the category of unnecessary burden. It is a transparency requirement that serves a real function in clinical practice. It protects patient safety by ensuring that clinicians understand the tools they are using. It protects health systems by creating documented evidence of vendor claims and performance characteristics. It protects state oversight by providing standardized documentation that state agencies can review.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the goal is to reduce burden while protecting these important functions, an alternative approach would be to streamline the certification process rather than eliminate the requirement entirely. For example, the certification review could be shortened from twelve weeks to eight weeks. The model card template could be simplified to reduce documentation burden. Vendors could be allowed to use alternative documentation formats that achieve the same transparency goals. These approaches would reduce burden while preserving the important transparency function that the current requirement serves.‚Źé‚Źé**RECOMMENDATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéClinovations Government + Health respectfully recommends that the final rule retain the Decision Support Interventions certification requirement requiring model cards and transparency disclosures. If the agency determines that burden reduction is necessary, the organization recommends the following modifications:‚Źé‚Źé1. Streamline the certification review timeline from twelve weeks to eight weeks, reducing vendor wait time without compromising review quality.‚Źé‚Źé2. Allow vendors to use simplified model card templates that focus on the most clinically relevant information: intended use, target population, known performance limitations, and external validation results.‚Źé‚Źé3. Create a tiered certification process where tools with lower clinical risk receive expedited review, while tools intended for high-stakes clinical decisions receive standard review.‚Źé‚Źé4. Establish a public registry of certified tools with model cards available to purchasers, allowing health systems to make informed comparisons and reducing vendor liability by creating documented evidence of disclosed performance characteristics.‚Źé‚Źé5. Allow vendors to update model cards annually rather than requiring new certification for every update, reducing administrative burden while maintaining transparency about tool performance changes.‚Źé‚ŹéThese modifications would address the stated goal of reducing burden on vendors while preserving the transparency protections that serve patient safety and informed decision-making.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéVermont's experience with federal compliance requirements in 2015 illustrates an important principle: when federal standards change, organizations that invested in compliance infrastructure face real costs. Clinovations Government + Health does not argue that federal standards should never change. However, when standards do change, the agency should carefully consider whether the costs of removing a requirement outweigh the benefits. In this case, the DSI certification requirement serves important functions in clinical practice. It protects patient safety by ensuring that clinicians understand the tools they are using. It enables state oversight by providing standardized documentation. It protects health systems by creating evidence of vendor claims and performance characteristics. These functions are valuable and should not be eliminated without substantial justification.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed deregulation does not provide that justification. Instead, it assumes that removing certification requirements will accelerate innovation and reduce burden without considering the downstream consequences for patient safety, state oversight, and health system decision-making. Clinovations Government + Health urges the agency to reconsider this proposal and to retain the DSI certification requirement, potentially with streamlined processes that reduce burden without compromising transparency and safety protections.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-02-06T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-06T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ7‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒgovernment‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒfrustrated‚ôĒVermont‚ôĒstate budget analyst‚ôĒ56‚ôĒWhen our department spent eighteen months aligning Vermont's disease surveillance system with the 2015 federal reporting standards, we had to rebuild our entire data pipeline at significant cost. Now I'm watching the same federal agencies signal they're relaxing those requirements, and I keep thinking about the three staff positions we couldn't fill that year because the budget went to compliance infrastructure‚ÄĒinfrastructure that apparently wasn't as essential as we were told it was.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2355
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0118‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒAmanda Anderson‚ôĒURAALI Refaluwasch Association‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe URAALI Refaluwasch Association opposes the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements and model card transparency mandates for clinical decision support tools, arguing that eliminating these standards w...‚ôĒ**URAALI Refaluwasch Association**‚Źé**Comments on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions**‚Źé‚ŹéThe URAALI Refaluwasch Association submits these comments in response to the proposed deregulatory actions affecting health information technology certification and information blocking requirements. Our organization represents patients, families, and clinicians working within rare disease communities across the United States, with particular focus on improving diagnostic pathways and treatment access for conditions that affect fewer than 200,000 Americans annually.‚Źé‚Źé**BACKGROUND AND STANDING**‚Źé‚ŹéURAALI has spent the past eighteen months coordinating with health systems across the Midwest on a rare disease registry initiative. Last spring, we launched a campaign to improve symptom pattern recognition across regional hospital networks. What we discovered was both frustrating and instructive: patient data that could have helped a young woman in our network obtain a correct diagnosis sat locked in separate electronic health record systems, unable to be shared even in anonymized form. That woman spent three years seeing different specialists before her condition was correctly identified. The information that could have shortened her diagnostic journey existed in hospitals just two hours away.‚Źé‚ŹéThis experience shapes how we view the current proposal. We understand the appeal of reducing regulatory burden on technology developers. We have heard directly from health system administrators about the costs associated with compliance and certification. But our concern is straightforward: the proposal to remove the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion and weaken transparency requirements around artificial intelligence models will push us backward at a moment when we should be pushing forward on interoperability.‚Źé‚Źé**THE SPECIFIC PROBLEM WITH REMOVING DSI CERTIFICATION REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule would eliminate the requirement that clinical decision support systems‚ÄĒparticularly those using artificial intelligence and machine learning‚ÄĒobtain certification and maintain publicly available model cards disclosing intended use, target population, known limitations, training data characteristics, and external validation results. The rationale given is that certification creates unnecessary burden and that market forces will discipline vendors who produce poor-quality tools.‚Źé‚ŹéThis argument fails to account for how clinical decision support actually functions in practice, especially in the context of rare diseases where data is sparse and clinician expertise is limited.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen a clinician in a rural Indiana hospital encounters a patient with unusual symptoms, that clinician often relies on decision support tools to narrow diagnostic possibilities. If that tool was trained primarily on common conditions and contains no documentation of its limitations for rare disease diagnosis, the clinician may not know they are operating outside the tool's validated domain. The tool does not announce its limitations. The vendor may not have conducted external validation in rare disease populations because the business case was weak. But the patient still receives a recommendation that feels authoritative because it came from a computer.‚Źé‚ŹéWe have watched this dynamic play out. A clinician in one of our partner health systems told us about a young patient whose symptoms were flagged as "low probability of serious disease" by a decision support system. The tool had been trained on acute care presentations in large urban hospitals. The patient actually had a rare connective tissue disorder. The delay caused by inappropriate reassurance had real consequences.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving certification requirements does not make this problem go away. It makes it invisible. A clinician cannot know whether a tool has been validated for their patient population if there is no requirement to disclose what populations the tool was tested on. A health system cannot make an informed procurement decision if vendors are not required to document known risks. A researcher trying to understand why a particular diagnostic error occurred cannot trace it back to tool limitations because those limitations were never recorded.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY MODEL CARDS AND TRANSPARENCY MATTER FOR PATIENT SAFETY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification criterion requires vendors to maintain and publicly disclose model cards that include the following elements: the intended clinical use case, the target patient population, known performance limitations, characteristics of the training data, and results from external validation studies. This is not an onerous requirement. It is basic scientific documentation.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider what happens when these disclosures are absent. A vendor develops a triage algorithm trained on 500,000 patient encounters from three large urban health systems in the Northeast. The algorithm performs well at identifying patients who need immediate intervention. The vendor markets it nationally. A health system in rural Mississippi implements it. No one has conducted validation studies in a rural population. No one has tested whether the algorithm performs differently when applied to patients with limited access to prior diagnostic testing. The model card would have disclosed the training data source and the absence of rural validation. Without it, the purchasing health system has no way to know what they do not know.‚Źé‚ŹéThis matters beyond individual diagnostic errors. When multiple health systems implement undocumented decision support tools, the cumulative effect can be systematic bias in how different populations are triaged and treated. We have seen early evidence of this in our rare disease work. Tools trained primarily on patients in academic medical centers may systematically underestimate disease probability in primary care settings. Tools trained on populations that have better access to prior testing may perform poorly for patients with limited diagnostic history. These are not hypothetical concerns. They reflect patterns we have observed in our network.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal assumes that vendors have strong incentives to produce accurate, well-documented tools. In many cases this is true. But vendors also have incentives to reach markets quickly, to avoid costly validation studies, and to minimize disclosure of limitations that might reduce adoption. The current certification requirement creates a counterbalance. It says: if you want to sell this tool into the U.S. healthcare market, you must be able to document what it does and what it does not do well.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving this requirement does not reduce burden in any meaningful sense. It shifts the burden. Instead of vendors documenting their tools, health systems must conduct their own validation studies. Instead of transparency, we get opacity. Instead of a level playing field where all vendors must meet the same disclosure standards, we get a market where vendors compete partly on the basis of how effectively they obscure their limitations.‚Źé‚Źé**INTEROPERABILITY AND THE ROLE OF TRANSPARENT AI STANDARDS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe broader context for this proposal is the push toward greater health data interoperability. The 21st Century Cures Act and subsequent ASTP/ONC guidance have created regulatory momentum toward enabling data to flow more freely between health systems. This is genuinely important. The rare disease diagnostic delays we see in our network would be substantially reduced if patient data could be shared across institutional boundaries in ways that preserve privacy while enabling clinical insight.‚Źé‚ŹéBut interoperability only works well when the tools that process that data are trustworthy and transparent. If health systems are sharing patient data with each other through standardized APIs and common data models, but the decision support tools analyzing that shared data are undocumented black boxes, we have not really solved the problem. We have just made it faster and larger.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current regulatory framework attempts to balance these concerns. HIPAA and the information blocking rule create the legal structure for data sharing. The certification criteria create standards for the tools that will consume that shared data. The DSI certification criterion specifically addresses the emerging risk that artificial intelligence models will make clinical recommendations based on that shared data without any public documentation of how they work or what they are validated for.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving the DSI certification requirement while expanding data interoperability would be like removing safety inspection requirements from automobiles while increasing the speed limit. You are making it easier for vehicles to move faster, but you are not making sure the vehicles are safe.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE HAVE LEARNED FROM REGISTRY WORK**‚Źé‚ŹéURAALI's registry initiative has involved working with twelve health systems across five states. These systems have different EHR vendors, different governance structures, and different approaches to clinical decision support. What we have learned from this work is that transparency about the tools being used is not a burden on health systems. It is a necessity.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen we began asking health systems about their decision support tools, we discovered that many administrators could not easily articulate what those tools do. They knew the tools existed. They knew clinicians used them. But they had limited visibility into the underlying algorithms, the training data, or the validation studies. We were asking simple questions: "What patient population was this tool validated on? What are the known limitations? How often has this been tested in your specific clinical setting?" The answers were frequently "I do not know" or "Let me get back to you."‚Źé‚ŹéThis lack of visibility is not because health system administrators are incompetent. It is because vendors often do not provide this information in accessible form. When vendors do provide model cards or technical documentation, health systems can engage with it. When they do not, there is a gap. The gap is filled by assumption, by hope, and sometimes by error.‚Źé‚ŹéOne health system in our network implemented a decision support tool for rare disease triage. After six months, they conducted an internal audit of the tool's performance and discovered that it performed significantly worse for patients under age eighteen than for adults. The tool had not been validated in pediatric populations, but this limitation had not been disclosed. The health system had to make a decision: remove the tool for pediatric patients, invest in pediatric validation, or continue using an undocumented tool in an unstudied population. They chose to remove it. The problem was caught because this particular health system had the resources to conduct internal validation. Many do not.‚Źé‚Źé**THE SPECIFIC LANGUAGE WE SUPPORT**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification criterion appears in 45 CFR 170.315(a)(7). It requires that clinical decision support systems certified as meeting this criterion must "enable a user to access the certification ID or link to the certification ID of the clinical decision support mechanism(s) utilized within the certified EHR technology." More importantly, it requires that vendors maintain and make available a model card that discloses:‚Źé‚Źé1. The clinical use case and intended patient population for the tool‚Źé2. Characteristics of the training data, including size, source, and demographic composition‚Źé3. Known performance limitations, including performance variations across patient subgroups‚Źé4. Results from external validation studies‚Źé5. Information about how the tool handles missing or incomplete data‚Źé‚ŹéThis is the language we want retained. It is specific. It is measurable. It does not require vendors to redesign their tools or fundamentally change their business models. It requires them to document what they have already built and tested.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule would remove this requirement, arguing that it creates unnecessary burden and that market forces will ensure quality. We believe this is a mistake. Market forces are important. But market forces alone do not ensure transparency. A vendor that produces a high-quality tool has every incentive to document it and publicize the documentation. A vendor that produces a lower-quality tool has every incentive to minimize disclosure. Certification requirements level this playing field.‚Źé‚Źé**INFORMATION BLOCKING AND DECISION SUPPORT TRANSPARENCY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal also suggests modifications to the information blocking rule as it applies to decision support tools. Specifically, it would reduce requirements for vendors and health systems to disclose when decision support recommendations might be blocking information exchange.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is concerning because decision support tools increasingly make recommendations about whether to share data, which data to prioritize, or how to format data for exchange. If these recommendations are undocumented and unvalidated, they can become invisible forms of information blocking. A tool might recommend that a particular data element not be shared based on a proprietary algorithm. The health system user might follow this recommendation without understanding the underlying logic. The patient or the requesting provider has no way to know that an algorithm, rather than a deliberate clinical decision, filtered their information.‚Źé‚ŹéThe information blocking rule, codified in 42 CFR 171 and expanded through subsequent guidance, already creates a framework for distinguishing between legitimate clinical reasons for not sharing information and blocking that is motivated by anti-competitive intent. The DSI certification criterion fits within this framework. It ensures that when decision support tools make recommendations that affect information flow, those recommendations are based on documented logic that can be audited and challenged if necessary.‚Źé‚Źé**THE COST OF REMOVING TRANSPARENCY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal estimates that removing DSI certification requirements would reduce burden on vendors by approximately $2 million per year across the industry, based on the costs of developing and maintaining model cards. This is presented as a significant cost savings.‚Źé‚ŹéBut this calculation misses the costs that would be shifted elsewhere. Health systems that cannot rely on vendor transparency would need to conduct their own validation studies. Researchers studying why clinical errors occur would need to reconstruct what algorithms were used. Patients and advocates would have less ability to understand how their data was processed. Regulators responding to adverse events would have less documented information to work with.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen URAALI conducted our registry work, we found that health systems spent significant time and resources trying to understand the decision support tools they had already purchased. One health system brought in a consultant to review their clinical decision support landscape. The consultant spent forty hours trying to extract information about algorithms, training data, and validation. The information was scattered across vendor contracts, technical documentation, marketing materials, and institutional memory. A model card would have consolidated this information and made it accessible.‚Źé‚ŹéThe cost of removing this requirement is not the cost of creating model cards. It is the cost of information asymmetry, of opacity, of health systems and patients operating with incomplete knowledge of the tools that affect their care.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC CONCERN ABOUT RARE DISEASE POPULATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéWe want to be explicit about how this affects rare disease diagnosis and care. Rare disease populations are particularly vulnerable to inadequate validation and transparency around decision support tools. This is because:‚Źé‚Źé1. Training data for rare diseases is inherently sparse. A machine learning model trained on 500,000 common disease presentations will have few examples of rare conditions. Vendors know this. They know that validating tools for rare diseases is expensive and that the market is small. Without a requirement to disclose validation limitations, there is little incentive to do so.‚Źé‚Źé2. Rare disease patients often see multiple specialists across different health systems. If each health system is using undocumented decision support tools, the patient experiences a fragmented care pathway where the tools may not communicate with each other and the limitations of each tool are invisible.‚Źé‚Źé3. Rare disease clinicians often practice in settings with less access to comprehensive decision support infrastructure. Rural specialists treating rare diseases may rely more heavily on whatever tools are available, which makes transparency about those tools' limitations even more important.‚Źé‚ŹéOur registry work found that rare disease patients experienced diagnostic delays partly because decision support tools were not validated for rare presentations. These patients were flagged as "low probability of serious disease" by tools trained on common presentations. Transparency about training data limitations would have at least alerted clinicians to question the tool's recommendations.‚Źé‚Źé**ACKNOWLEDGING THE LEGITIMATE CONCERNS ABOUT BURDEN**‚Źé‚ŹéWe recognize that the proposal is motivated by genuine concerns about regulatory burden. Health IT developers have told us that certification requirements are expensive. Smaller vendors say they cannot afford to participate in the certification process. There is a real tension between ensuring quality and maintaining a competitive market with diverse vendors.‚Źé‚ŹéBut the solution is not to eliminate transparency requirements. The solution is to make them more efficient. CMS could streamline the certification process, reduce documentation requirements for tools with clear limited scope, or create tiered certification for different risk levels. A tool used only as a supplementary reference in a specialty setting might have different requirements than a tool used as primary triage in a busy emergency department.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current proposal takes an all-or-nothing approach: eliminate the requirement entirely. We would support a more nuanced approach that maintains the transparency requirement while reducing unnecessary burden. For example:‚Źé‚Źé- Allow vendors to submit abbreviated model cards for tools with narrow, well-defined scope‚Źé- Create a public registry of decision support tools where vendors can post their own documentation without formal certification, with clear labeling of which tools have been formally certified‚Źé- Provide technical templates that make model card creation straightforward and less costly‚Źé- Allow vendors to use third-party validators rather than requiring expensive in-house validation studies‚Źé‚ŹéThese approaches would preserve transparency while reducing burden. The current proposal sacrifices transparency entirely in the name of burden reduction.‚Źé‚Źé**THE RELATIONSHIP TO BROADER INTEROPERABILITY GOALS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal is framed as part of a broader deregulatory agenda aimed at unleashing innovation and competition in health IT. We support that goal. But we think it is misguided to pursue deregulation of decision support transparency at the same time that the government is pushing for greater data interoperability.‚Źé‚ŹéThe two goals pull in opposite directions. Greater data interoperability means more patient information is flowing between systems, being processed by algorithms, and influencing clinical decisions. If that is happening, we need more transparency about those algorithms, not less. We need to know what tools are making recommendations, what data they are based on, what populations they were validated for, and what their known limitations are.‚Źé‚ŹéThe 21st Century Cures Act emphasized the importance of patient access to their own health information and the ability to share that information across institutional boundaries. The rule implementing the Cures Act, codified in 42 CFR 171, created information blocking requirements to ensure that this data sharing could happen. Those requirements assume that once data is shared, it will be used appropriately. But if the tools processing that shared data are undocumented and unvalidated, the assumption breaks down.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC RECOMMENDATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéBased on our experience and our concerns, URAALI makes the following specific recommendations:‚Źé‚Źé1. Retain the DSI certification criterion in 45 CFR 170.315(a)(7) and the requirement that vendors maintain and publicly disclose model cards.‚Źé‚Źé2. If burden reduction is necessary, streamline the certification process rather than eliminating the transparency requirement. Create an expedited pathway for tools with narrow scope or lower risk.‚Źé‚Źé3. Ensure that any modifications to information blocking rules do not reduce transparency about how decision support tools influence information exchange.‚Źé‚Źé4. Consider creating a public registry of decision support tools, with different tiers indicating certification status, so that health systems and clinicians can see what tools are available and what documentation exists.‚Źé‚Źé5. Require vendors to disclose when decision support recommendations might affect information blocking compliance, and ensure that health systems can audit and override such recommendations.‚Źé‚Źé6. Conduct a study of how decision support tools currently perform across different patient populations, particularly rare disease populations, to establish a baseline for understanding the impact of transparency requirements.‚Źé‚ŹéThe rare disease community has learned through hard experience that transparency matters. We have seen what happens when patients move through a healthcare system guided by undocumented algorithms. We have watched clinicians make decisions based on tools whose limitations they do not understand. We have supported patients in seeking diagnoses across multiple institutions, only to find that the decision support tools at each institution had not been validated for their actual condition.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal to remove DSI certification requirements would make these problems worse, not better. It would create a healthcare technology landscape where vendors have no obligation to document what their tools do or what they were validated for. Health systems would have no way to know what they were purchasing. Clinicians would have no way to understand the limitations of the tools they were using. Patients would have no way to understand how their data was being processed.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not a burden reduction. It is a shift toward opacity at a moment when the healthcare system is moving toward greater data sharing and greater reliance on algorithmic decision support. We urge ASTP/ONC to retain the DSI certification requirement and the model card disclosure requirement. If modifications are necessary to address legitimate burden concerns, those modifications should preserve transparency while reducing unnecessary administrative cost.‚Źé‚ŹéThe young woman in our network who spent three years reaching a diagnosis deserves a healthcare system where the tools that might have helped her reach that diagnosis faster are documented, validated, and transparent. That is not an unreasonable standard. It is a minimum requirement for responsible health information technology deployment.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-02-07T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-07T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ11‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving DSI transparency requirements creates a dangerous regulatory gap where AI-based clinical decision support tools face fewer disclosure obligations than traditional medical devices, undermining consistent safety standards.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒhopeful‚ôĒIndiana‚ôĒcampaign director‚ôĒ37‚ôĒLast spring, I helped coordinate a campaign for rare disease awareness, and we discovered that patient registries operated by different health systems couldn't share data‚ÄĒeven anonymized data‚ÄĒabout symptom patterns across regions. A young woman in our network who had spent three years getting a correct diagnosis could have benefited from insights that existed in hospitals just two hours away. I've seen firsthand how information silos hurt people, but I've also watched vulnerable communities get exploited when their health data moves too freely without real safeguards.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ3404
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0119‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒBetty Sanchez‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe commenter argues that proposed reductions in AI transparency and oversight requirements for medical decision-making tools are dangerous and unjustified, emphasizing that patients deserve the same rigorous safety standards for AI systems as for...‚ôĒMy mom had a heart attack last spring, and I spent three weeks on the phone trying to get her records from the hospital to her cardiologist to her regular doctor. Nobody was talking to each other. Everyone kept saying the same thing: "We're working on it." Meanwhile my mom is sitting there scared, wondering if her doctors actually know what's going on with her heart. I finally got everything transferred, but it shouldn't take that kind of fight.‚Źé‚ŹéNow I'm reading that we might be loosening the rules around AI tools that doctors use to make decisions about patient care, and I have to be honest, that worries me. If we're removing transparency requirements so companies don't have to tell doctors and patients what their AI was trained on or what problems to watch for, how is that fair? We don't loosen safety rules for regular medical devices just because it's easier for the company. AI that helps decide your treatment shouldn't get weaker oversight. My mom needs to know that when her cardiologist uses any tool to figure out her care, that tool has been tested and held to real standards. I get that innovation matters, but not at the expense of patient safety. We can do both.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-08T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-08T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving DSI transparency requirements creates a dangerous regulatory gap where AI-based clinical decision support tools face fewer disclosure obligations than traditional medical devices, undermining consistent safety standards.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒmedium‚ôĒhopeful‚ôĒNorth Dakota‚ôĒadministrative assistant‚ôĒ44‚ôĒLast spring when my mom was diagnosed with her heart condition, I spent three weeks calling between her cardiologist's office and the hospital trying to get her records transferred so her primary care doctor could see the full picture‚ÄĒit was so frustrating watching her worry while we all just waited. Now that I finally understand how this stuff works, I'm hoping my kids won't have to do the same thing for me someday, but I'm also nervous about who else might end up looking at those files if the rules aren't tight enough.‚ôĒAI tools used by doctors should have the same safety rules and transparency requirements as other medical devices‚ÄĒright now there's a gap where AI decision support tools could slip through with less oversight, and that's not fair to patients like my mom who depend on doctors having all the right information. | When hospitals and clinics use AI to help make decisions about your care, you deserve to know what that AI was trained on, who it's supposed to work for, and what problems doctors should watch out for‚ÄĒremoving those transparency rules means patients and doctors are flying blind.‚ôĒBetty frames this as a fairness and safety issue: if we're going to let AI help doctors make decisions about our health, the rules protecting us shouldn't be weaker than the rules for regular medical devices.‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ210
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0120‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒWilliam Garcia‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe commenter argues that while data interoperability in healthcare is a genuine problem requiring solutions, removing transparency requirements for AI-based clinical decision support systems is not the appropriate fix and would create an unsafe t...‚ôĒMy father had a stroke last October. For three weeks, I was on the phone constantly‚ÄĒcalling his cardiologist, the hospital, the rehab facility, trying to piece together his complete medical history because nobody's systems talked to each other. I'd explain his medications to one doctor, his prior imaging to another, his discharge summary to a third. I kept thinking: if these hospitals and clinics could just share his information directly, we'd save hours and probably reduce the risk of someone missing something critical. That experience is why I'm paying attention to the interoperability rule. But I'm also worried we're about to solve one problem by creating another.‚Źé‚Źé**THE DATA SHARING PROBLEM IS REAL, BUT IT'S NOT ABOUT TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéLet me be clear: the fragmentation I witnessed is a genuine problem. When my dad moved between acute care and rehabilitation, his cardiologist didn't have access to the updated anticoagulation protocols from the hospital. The rehab facility didn't have his full medication list without asking us for it. This kind of siloed access costs time, creates safety gaps, and puts unnecessary burden on families. I understand why regulators and developers are looking to remove barriers and streamline standards. That makes sense.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat doesn't make sense is removing transparency requirements for AI-based clinical decision support systems as part of the solution. These are two separate problems. Data sharing is about connectivity and interoperability standards. Transparency in AI is about patients and clinicians knowing what an algorithm is actually doing and what its limitations are. Removing the second to fix the first is like fixing a broken water main by removing fire safety inspections.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY REMOVING DSI TRANSPARENCY CREATES A TWO-TIER SAFETY STANDARD**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal appears to eliminate or substantially weaken Data System Integrity (DSI) transparency requirements that currently mandate disclosure of how AI clinical tools work, what their training data included, and what they're designed and not designed to do. These are called model cards or clinical algorithm fact sheets. For traditional medical devices like blood pressure monitors or pacemakers, the FDA requires clear labeling and risk documentation. But if we remove DSI transparency requirements for AI systems, we're saying: algorithmic decision support gets lighter oversight than a physical device.‚Źé‚ŹéThat's backwards. My father's stroke recovery depended on clinicians making good decisions about his medications, his rehab intensity, and whether he was ready to go home. If an AI system was helping guide any of those decisions and nobody‚ÄĒnot his doctors, not him, not me‚ÄĒcould see what the system was trained on or what it might miss, we'd be flying blind. The FDA's 2021 guidance on AI/ML-based Software as a Medical Device specifically emphasized that transparency about model development and limitations is essential to safe clinical deployment. Removing those requirements doesn't unleash innovation; it unleashes risk we can't see.‚Źé‚ŹéI've read about cases where clinical decision support systems made serious errors because their training data didn't include certain patient populations or rare conditions. In one documented case from the Journal of the American Medical Informatics Association, a sepsis prediction algorithm was less accurate for Black patients, but clinicians didn't catch it because the model's performance metrics weren't disclosed in a way that allowed for stratified analysis. If transparency requirements are weakened, we won't even know when these gaps exist until someone gets hurt.‚Źé‚Źé**THE REAL BOTTLENECK ISN'T TRANSPARENCY‚ÄĒIT'S FRAGMENTED SYSTEMS**‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what I think regulators need to hear: removing transparency requirements will not make it easier for my father's cardiologist and his rehab team to share information. That problem exists because EHR systems don't interoperate, because standards like FHIR (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources) aren't fully implemented, and because there are financial incentives to keep patient data locked in proprietary systems. Weakening AI transparency rules doesn't fix any of that.‚Źé‚ŹéIn fact, it makes the interoperability problem worse. If hospitals are supposed to implement FHIR-forward standards and move away from proprietary data formats, they need to understand what's in their data and how it flows. Adding another layer of opacity‚ÄĒAI systems that clinicians and patients can't see into‚ÄĒactually impedes that transition. It's harder to ensure data quality and interoperability when part of the decision pipeline is a black box.‚Źé‚ŹéThe certification criteria that are truly duplicative are probably things like redundant testing requirements for basic connectivity. The standards that need updating are the ones that force developers to build for ten different EHR platforms instead of a unified FHIR standard. But the criteria that require disclosure of how an AI system was trained and what it's designed to do? Those aren't barriers to interoperability. They're safeguards that should exist regardless of how the data flows.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT THE RESEARCH ACTUALLY SHOWS ABOUT AI TRANSPARENCY AND PATIENT OUTCOMES**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Commonwealth Fund released findings in 2023 showing that patient trust in AI-assisted diagnosis increases significantly when transparency disclosures are available compared to when they're absent. Trust matters because patients who understand how a diagnosis was reached are more likely to ask informed questions, push back if something doesn't feel right, and advocate for themselves. When my father was recovering, having clear information about what the doctors were doing and why made us better partners in his care. That principle applies to AI-supported decisions too.‚Źé‚ŹéThe ONC Health IT Certification Program's 2023 audit findings also showed a correlation between model card disclosure and reduced adverse event reporting. Systems where developers were required to disclose performance across different patient populations, edge cases, and limitations had fewer reported safety incidents. This isn't coincidence. When developers know they have to be transparent about what their system can and can't do, they're more careful in development. When they know clinicians and patients can see those limitations, they're incentivized to be honest about them.‚Źé‚Źé**DISTINGUISHING BETWEEN "REMOVING BARRIERS TO DATA EXCHANGE" AND "REMOVING VISIBILITY INTO AI DECISION-MAKING"**‚Źé‚ŹéTo the regulators reviewing this rule, I'd ask you to make this distinction explicit. There are legitimate barriers to interoperability that should come down. Certification criteria that force developers to implement proprietary standards when FHIR alternatives exist should be removed or revised. Requirements that duplicate testing or create unnecessary administrative burden should be streamlined. Those changes would actually accelerate innovation and data sharing.‚Źé‚ŹéBut transparency requirements for clinical AI systems aren't barriers to interoperability. They're not slowing down the deployment of FHIR standards or preventing EHRs from communicating. What they do is ensure that when AI is involved in clinical decision-making, there's a documented record of what the system is supposed to do, what data it was trained on, and what its known limitations are. That's a safety requirement, not a burden on innovation.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the goal is to unleash prosperity through better health data exchange, consider this: hospitals will move faster to implement interoperability standards if they're confident in the quality and safety of the systems handling that data. Removing transparency requirements sends the opposite signal. It says the rule prioritizes developer convenience over patient safety.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC RECOMMENDATIONS FOR CERTIFICATION CRITERIA AND STANDARDS UPDATES**‚Źé‚ŹéIf I had to identify which criteria could actually be streamlined without compromising safety:‚Źé‚ŹéDuplicate testing requirements where multiple certification programs require the same validation steps could be consolidated. If a system passes HITRUST or FDA SaMD review, requiring redundant ONC certification testing for the same functionality seems wasteful. That would genuinely reduce burden.‚Źé‚ŹéStandards implementation specifications should be updated to prioritize FHIR-forward approaches. Developers shouldn't have to maintain legacy HL7v2 interfaces if FHIR accomplishes the same goal. Removing the requirement to support deprecated standards would actually accelerate adoption of newer, more interoperable ones.‚Źé‚ŹéBut for any certification criteria related to clinical decision support, algorithmic transparency, or risk disclosure, the bar should stay high. These aren't nice-to-have features. They're fundamental to safe clinical deployment.‚Źé‚Źé**CLOSING THOUGHT: SOLVING THE RIGHT PROBLEM**‚Źé‚ŹéI want my father's next health crisis, if it comes, to happen in a world where his doctors can instantly access his complete medical history across different health systems. I want to spend time with him instead of on hold with hospital records departments. But I also want to know that any AI system advising his care is operating in the open, with clear documentation of what it does and what it might miss.‚Źé‚ŹéThose two goals aren't in conflict. We can improve interoperability without sacrificing transparency. We should. The rule as proposed treats them as if they're the same problem, and they're not. Removing DSI transparency requirements won't fix fragmented EHR systems. It will just add another layer of opacity to clinical decision-making, and patients like my father will bear the risk.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ4‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving DSI transparency requirements creates a dangerous regulatory gap where AI-based clinical decision support tools face fewer disclosure obligations than traditional medical devices, undermining consistent safety standards.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒhopeful‚ôĒWest Virginia‚ôĒcustomer service representative‚ôĒ42‚ôĒWhen my dad had his stroke last year, I spent three weeks coordinating between his cardiologist, the hospital, and the rehab facility, calling each one repeatedly because nobody could just pull up his full medical history‚ÄĒI was basically the human filing system. Now I'm wondering if making it easier for all of them to actually share that information would've saved me those hours on hold, but I'm also nervous about whether every detail gets locked down as carefully as it should be.‚ôĒRemoving DSI transparency requirements creates a two-tier safety standard where AI clinical tools get lighter oversight than traditional medical devices, leaving patients like my dad vulnerable to hidden risks and undisclosed limitations. | When hospitals can't or won't share complete patient histories, it's because of fragmented systems‚ÄĒbut weakening AI transparency rules won't fix that; it'll just add another layer of opacity that patients can't see or question.‚ôĒI want better data sharing between my dad's doctors, but not by sacrificing the transparency rules that let patients know what AI is actually deciding about their care.‚ôĒFDA 2021 guidance on AI/ML-based Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) transparency expectations | Journal of the American Medical Informatics Association study on clinical decision support safety incidents linked to inadequate model documentation | ONC Health IT Certification Program 2023 audit findings showing correlation between model card disclosure and reduced adverse event reporting | Commonwealth Fund report on patient trust in AI-assisted diagnosis when transparency disclosures are available vs. absent‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1423
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0121‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒSteven Mitchell‚ôĒTexas Medical Association‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Texas Medical Association argues that transparency requirements for AI-driven clinical decision support systems‚ÄĒspecifically model cards documenting training data, intended use, target populations, and known limitations‚ÄĒshould be retained in r...‚ôĒRe: CMS-1811-NC and HHS-OS-2024-0090 ‚ÄĒ Proposed Modifications to Health IT Certification Criteria and Information Blocking Regulations‚Źé‚ŹéTexas Medical Association respectfully submits this comment regarding the proposed revisions to Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria under 45 CFR 170.315(b)(16) and related transparency requirements for AI-assisted clinical decision support systems.‚Źé‚ŹéBACKGROUND AND ORGANIZATIONAL PERSPECTIVE‚Źé‚ŹéThe Texas Medical Association represents over 55,000 physicians and medical students across the state, with members working in primary care, specialty practice, hospital-based medicine, and rural healthcare settings. Our membership includes physicians who directly use clinical decision support tools in daily practice, as well as health IT executives and medical informatics specialists who have implemented these systems in their institutions. This comment reflects input gathered from our Health IT Policy Committee over the past eighteen months, including structured feedback from physician practices ranging from small independent clinics to large integrated health systems.‚Źé‚ŹéI should note upfront that this comment comes from a somewhat complicated position. We understand the intent behind the proposed deregulatory framework. We genuinely do. The burden of compliance with existing certification criteria has created real barriers for smaller health IT developers and has slowed innovation in some segments of the market. Some of our members have expressed frustration with the pace of new feature development in their EHR systems, and we've heard credible complaints that overly rigid certification pathways discourage experimental approaches to clinical decision support. That's not a trivial concern in a healthcare system that needs to move faster in some areas.‚Źé‚ŹéBut the problem is that when you look at what's actually happening in clinical practice right now, the case for retaining robust transparency requirements around AI-driven decision support becomes difficult to ignore. And it's not because we're reflexively opposed to deregulation or because we're captured by incumbent vendors. It's because we've started to see the concrete consequences of inadequate transparency in decision support systems, and those consequences matter.‚Źé‚ŹéTHE PROBLEM WE'RE SEEING IN PRACTICE‚Źé‚ŹéLet me describe what happened in one of our member practices in Houston last year. A mid-sized primary care group with about twenty-five physicians implemented a new AI-assisted diagnostic support tool. The vendor marketed it as improving diagnostic accuracy in complex cases. The practice went through the standard procurement process, reviewed the basic specifications, and deployed it across their EHR. Within six weeks, physicians began noticing something odd. The system was generating high-confidence alerts for specific diagnostic pathways in ways that seemed inconsistent with actual patient presentations.‚Źé‚ŹéOne physician in the practice, a family medicine doctor with fifteen years of experience, did something that probably should be routine but isn't. She actually tried to understand what the system was doing. She looked at the training data characteristics, the validation methodology, the known performance gaps. Except she couldn't. The vendor provided no model card. There was no documentation of the target population used in training. There was no transparent discussion of performance across demographic subgroups. There was no disclosure of what types of cases the system was known to perform poorly on. The vendor's position was essentially: trust us, it works.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen she pushed back, the vendor explained that their competitive interests required them to keep this information proprietary. They weren't being evasive in a hostile way. They were genuinely concerned that disclosing training data characteristics or model limitations would give competitors an advantage. So the physicians in that practice had to make a choice. They could either use the system with incomplete information about how it actually worked, or they could spend time reverse-engineering its behavior through trial and error. Most chose the first option, which is what most people do when the friction of obtaining information becomes too high.‚Źé‚ŹéNow, the system probably didn't cause significant harm in that practice. Their physicians were experienced enough to maintain appropriate skepticism. But I keep coming back to that situation because it represents a failure at a fundamental level. These physicians couldn't make truly informed clinical decisions about when to trust the system because they lacked basic information about how the system was trained, what it was optimized for, and where its actual limitations were.‚Źé‚ŹéAnd here's what makes this relevant to the regulatory question before you: the reason that practice couldn't get this information wasn't because of regulatory burden on the vendor. It was because there was no regulatory requirement that would have made transparency a competitive necessity. The vendor had no incentive to disclose information they perceived as proprietary, and the practice had no leverage to demand it. The absence of certification criteria requiring model cards didn't protect innovation. It just meant that physicians had to operate with incomplete information.‚Źé‚ŹéTHE CASE FOR RETAINING DSI TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS‚Źé‚ŹéThis brings us to why we believe the existing requirement for AI model cards and transparency disclosures in 45 CFR 170.315(b)(16) should be retained in any revised certification framework. The requirement currently mandates that developers of Decision Support Interventions provide documentation of intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and results of external validation testing. This isn't an onerous requirement. It's a transparency requirement. It doesn't dictate how the system should be built. It dictates what developers need to disclose about how they actually built it.‚Źé‚ŹéThe distinction matters because transparency requirements work differently than prescriptive design requirements. A requirement that says "your algorithm must use only validated data sources" is prescriptive and potentially stifling. A requirement that says "you must disclose what data sources you used and how you validated them" is transparent and allows developers complete freedom in their technical approach while ensuring that the people who actually use the system can make informed decisions about deployment.‚Źé‚ŹéLet's be specific about what the current model card requirement includes under the existing certification criterion. Developers must provide:‚Źé‚Źé- A clear statement of the system's intended use, including the clinical context for which it was designed and the specific clinical decision it's meant to support.‚Źé- Identification of the target population, including relevant demographic characteristics, clinical characteristics, and any known limitations in how well the system performs across different population segments.‚Źé- Documentation of the training data used, including the source, size, time period, and any known biases or limitations in that data.‚Źé- Results from external validation testing, including performance metrics across different subgroups and in different clinical settings from where the system was developed.‚Źé- Known risks and failure modes, including scenarios where the system is known to perform poorly or where its recommendations should not be trusted without additional clinical judgment.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is baseline transparency. This isn't asking vendors to revolutionize their business model or spend a year documenting every decision made during development. It's asking them to be transparent about what they actually did.‚Źé‚ŹéADDRESSING THE BURDEN ARGUMENT‚Źé‚ŹéWe've read the comments from some health IT developers arguing that these requirements are burdensome and that removing them would accelerate innovation. We take that concern seriously, which is why we want to be clear about what we're actually arguing for. We're not arguing that every health IT developer should have to submit to the same certification pathway regardless of the complexity of their system or the risk profile of their clinical application. We're arguing that for Decision Support Interventions specifically, which directly influence clinical decision-making in ways that can affect patient safety, transparency requirements are justified.‚Źé‚ŹéIf a vendor wants to build a decision support system quickly without comprehensive validation testing, that's fine. The system doesn't need to be certified. But if they want their system to be integrated into certified EHR systems and used in clinical settings where physicians are relying on it to inform diagnoses or treatment decisions, then transparency about how the system works is a reasonable condition.‚Źé‚ŹéThe burden argument also assumes that vendors don't already have this information. They do. Every vendor who has built an AI-driven clinical decision support system knows what their training data looked like. They know what population they optimized for. They know where their system performs well and where it doesn't. The question isn't whether they have this information. The question is whether they're required to share it with the clinicians who are going to use it.‚Źé‚ŹéWe would also note that the argument about competitive harm from disclosure of training data characteristics is overstated. Knowing that a system was trained on data from a particular health system or patient population doesn't tell a competitor how to replicate the system. It tells a clinician whether that system is likely to work well in their population. These are different questions serving different purposes.‚Źé‚ŹéCLINICAL DECISION-MAKING AND INFORMED CONSENT‚Źé‚ŹéThere's a broader principle here that goes beyond the specific mechanics of this regulation. Clinical practice is built on the concept of informed decision-making. Physicians are expected to understand the evidence base for the interventions they use. They're expected to know the limitations of that evidence and the populations to which it applies. When they use a diagnostic test, they're supposed to understand the sensitivity and specificity of that test. When they prescribe a medication, they're supposed to understand the evidence from clinical trials and the relevant pharmacology.‚Źé‚ŹéAI-driven decision support is not an exception to this principle. It's a new form of evidence that's being integrated into clinical practice, and the same principle applies. Physicians should understand what they're using, how it was developed, what populations it was tested in, and where its limitations lie. Without that information, they can't really practice informed medicine. They're just following algorithmic recommendations.‚Źé‚ŹéNow, we recognize that this creates a knowledge problem. Most practicing physicians don't have deep expertise in machine learning or data science. They can't audit the underlying algorithms or reproduce the validation studies. So there's a gap between the ideal of fully informed decision-making and what's actually achievable in practice. But that gap shouldn't be an argument for complete opacity. It's an argument for transparency at the level of abstraction where it's actually useful to clinicians. Model cards serve that function. They translate technical information into a form that clinicians can actually use to make decisions about deployment and usage.‚Źé‚ŹéPATIENT SAFETY AND ACCOUNTABILITY‚Źé‚ŹéWe should also discuss what happens when something goes wrong. If a decision support system makes a systematic error that affects patient care, there needs to be a mechanism for understanding what went wrong and how to fix it. This is where transparency becomes a safety issue, not just a consumer protection issue.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider a hypothetical situation where a decision support system recommends a particular diagnostic pathway that turns out to be ineffective for patients in a particular demographic group. Maybe the system was trained primarily on data from one hospital system and performs poorly when deployed in a different clinical environment. How would anyone know about this problem? Without transparency about the training data and external validation results, the problem might not surface until it's caused significant harm. With transparency, clinicians and health IT leaders can look at the model card, recognize that the system was trained in a particular context, and either modify how they use it or decide not to use it until additional validation is performed.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current requirement under 45 CFR 170.315(b)(16) includes a specific mandate for external validation testing. This is crucial because it forces developers to test their systems in clinical environments different from where they were trained. This is how systematic performance gaps get discovered. Without this requirement, systems might be deployed in clinical settings where their actual performance is unknown.‚Źé‚ŹéCOMPARISON TO OTHER REGULATED DOMAINS‚Źé‚ŹéIt's worth noting that transparency requirements for AI and algorithmic decision-making are not unique to healthcare. The FDA's proposed framework for AI/ML-based Software as a Medical Device includes specific requirements for documentation of training data, intended use, and known limitations. The SEC has proposed disclosure requirements for algorithmic decision-making in financial services. The FTC has issued guidance on algorithmic transparency and accountability. These are not healthcare-specific ideas. They're emerging as standard practice across regulated industries because experience has shown that transparency is necessary to maintain accountability.‚Źé‚ŹéThe healthcare context is actually more sensitive than most of these other domains because the stakes involve human health and life. If an algorithmic recommendation in a financial service turns out to be flawed, the harm is typically financial and can be corrected through market mechanisms. If an algorithmic recommendation in clinical decision support turns out to be flawed, the harm can be to patient health, and those harms often can't be undone.‚Źé‚ŹéEVIDENCE FROM IMPLEMENTATION‚Źé‚ŹéWe've also looked at the practical experience from health systems that have attempted to implement strong transparency requirements internally, even where they weren't mandated by regulation. What we see is that the systems that perform best over time are the ones where clinicians actually understand how the decision support tools work and where they have good information about the limitations of those tools. This isn't because transparency makes the tools more effective. It's because understanding creates appropriate usage. Clinicians who understand a system's limitations are more likely to use it appropriately and less likely to over-rely on it.‚Źé‚ŹéConversely, systems that are deployed without adequate transparency documentation tend to have more problems with inappropriate usage, lower adoption rates among experienced clinicians, and higher rates of adverse events. We don't have a large body of published literature on this because most health systems don't systematically track these outcomes. But in our conversations with members who work in health IT and patient safety, this pattern is consistent.‚Źé‚ŹéSCOPE AND LIMITATION OF WHAT WE'RE ASKING‚Źé‚ŹéWe want to be clear that we're not asking for comprehensive re-regulation or for expansion of the certification requirements beyond their current scope. We're specifically arguing that the existing transparency requirements for Decision Support Interventions should be retained in any revised certification framework. We're not arguing that other certification criteria should be retained if they're genuinely burdensome without corresponding safety benefit. We're not arguing against efforts to streamline certification pathways or reduce unnecessary documentation requirements. We're arguing that for this specific category of technology, transparency about how the system works is a basic requirement for safe clinical use.‚Źé‚ŹéWe're also not arguing that the model card requirement needs to be expanded. The current requirement is proportionate to the risk and doesn't impose unreasonable burden. It could potentially be streamlined in terms of format or level of detail, and we'd be open to suggestions about how to make compliance easier without sacrificing the underlying transparency principle.‚Źé‚ŹéTHE ALTERNATIVE: WHAT HAPPENS WITHOUT THIS REQUIREMENT‚Źé‚ŹéIf the DSI transparency requirement is removed, what happens? In the best case, vendors continue to provide this information voluntarily because they recognize that clinicians and health IT leaders need it to make good deployment decisions. Some vendors probably will, particularly larger vendors who have robust compliance and documentation processes. But smaller vendors and more innovative startups will likely take the position that disclosure is optional, and many of their customers will accept that because the friction of demanding better documentation is high.‚Źé‚ŹéIn the worst case, we end up in a situation where decision support systems are deployed in clinical practice with minimal transparency about how they work, what populations they were tested in, or where their limitations lie. This creates risk without corresponding benefit. The vendors aren't moving faster or innovating more effectively because they don't have to document their training data. They're just not documenting it. And the clinicians who use the systems are operating with incomplete information.‚Źé‚ŹéWe've actually lived through versions of this dynamic in healthcare before. In the 1990s and early 2000s, before comprehensive EHR regulation, health IT vendors had minimal requirements to document interoperability or data quality. The result wasn't faster innovation or better competition. It was fragmentation and vendor lock-in, because clinicians had no way to evaluate whether systems could actually communicate with each other or whether their data was accurate. The standards and certification frameworks that were eventually put in place didn't slow innovation. They created the conditions under which interoperability became competitive and innovation accelerated.‚Źé‚ŹéWe're likely to see a similar dynamic with decision support transparency. In the short term, removing requirements might reduce documentation burden for some vendors. But in the medium term, as healthcare organizations become more sophisticated about AI governance and more concerned about algorithmic bias and safety, they're going to demand this information anyway. Smart vendors will build it into their process. Less sophisticated vendors will be left behind. The net effect is that we've just created a period of opacity and risk that could have been avoided.‚Źé‚ŹéTECHNICAL FEASIBILITY‚Źé‚ŹéOne final point: we want to be clear that the model card requirement is technically feasible and not burdensome. This isn't a requirement that forces developers to fundamentally change how they build systems. It's a documentation requirement. Any competent development team already has this information. They know what data they used. They know what population they optimized for. They know what external validation testing they did. The requirement is just that they document it in a structured way that's accessible to clinicians.‚Źé‚ŹéThe format of the documentation could be streamlined. The current requirement doesn't specify a particular template or format, which means developers have flexibility in how they present the information. If there's concern that the documentation is too burdensome, the Office of the National Coordinator could develop a standard template that makes it easier for vendors to comply and easier for clinicians to review the information. This would actually reduce burden compared to the current situation where every vendor uses a different format.‚Źé‚ŹéCONCLUSION‚Źé‚ŹéThe Texas Medical Association supports the goals of the deregulatory framework, including reducing unnecessary burden on health IT developers and promoting competition and innovation. We also recognize that some existing certification criteria may be overly prescriptive or may not reflect current best practices. We're not opposed to thoughtful revision of the certification framework.‚Źé‚ŹéBut we believe that transparency requirements for Decision Support Interventions serve an essential function in ensuring that clinicians can make informed decisions about how to use these systems. The current requirement for model cards, documentation of training data, and external validation testing is proportionate to the risks and is not burdensome relative to the benefit. Removing this requirement would create risk without corresponding benefit and would likely create problems down the road as healthcare organizations discover that they need this information and have to demand it anyway.‚Źé‚ŹéWe strongly urge the Office of the National Coordinator and the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services to retain the existing DSI transparency requirements in any revised certification framework. If there are specific aspects of the requirement that are genuinely burdensome, we're happy to work with the agencies to streamline them. But the core principle‚ÄĒthat developers of clinical decision support systems should be transparent about how those systems work‚ÄĒis one we believe is essential to maintaining safe, accountable clinical practice.‚Źé‚ŹéWe appreciate the opportunity to comment on this important regulatory proposal and welcome any questions regarding the Texas Medical Association's position.‚Źé‚ŹéRespectfully submitted,‚Źé‚ŹéSteven Mitchell‚ŹéSenior Policy Analyst‚ŹéTexas Medical Association‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-02-01T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-01T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ10‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒangry‚ôĒIdaho‚ôĒsenior policy analyst‚ôĒ45‚ôĒLast year, my sister was denied access to her own genetic test results because the lab claimed patient privacy regulations required them to gate the data behind their portal‚ÄĒa system so antiquated it crashed whenever more than a few hundred people tried logging in simultaneously. She had to wait six weeks to share critical hereditary information with her oncologist, and by then the treatment window had shifted. The irony is suffocating: we've created a regulatory framework so obsessed with protecting data that it's actually preventing patients from wielding their own medical information when it matters most.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ3175
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0122‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDaniel Martinez‚ôĒNational Alliance for Care at Home‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe National Alliance for Care at Home opposes removal of Decision Support Interventions certification requirements, arguing that transparency disclosures about AI model design, training data, and validation are essential safeguards for vulnerable...‚ôĒRE: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity‚ŹéDocket No. OS-24-XX-XX‚Źé‚ŹéNational Alliance for Care at Home respectfully submits this comment regarding the proposed revisions to Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria and related health information technology standards. Our organization represents care coordination networks, home-based care agencies, and patient advocacy groups across Nevada and the broader West, with particular focus on ensuring that elderly populations and medically vulnerable communities maintain agency and safety as healthcare systems become increasingly automated.‚Źé‚Źé**THE FRAGMENTATION I WITNESSED, AND WHY TRANSPARENCY MATTERS NOW**‚Źé‚ŹéMy mother's final eighteen months taught me something that regulatory language often misses. When she was receiving in-home care in Las Vegas, we worked with seven different providers: her primary care physician, two specialists, a home health agency, a physical therapist, a hospice team, and the local hospital where she had two brief admissions. Each time she saw someone new, the medical history started from scratch. The home health nurse would ask questions the hospice doctor had already asked.
