The specialist didn't know about medication interactions the primary care doctor was managing. We kept repeating the same information, the same test results, the same allergy list. What struck me most wasn't the inefficiency‚ÄĒit was the risk. One provider didn't know she'd had a fall because another provider's notes were stuck in a separate system. Another didn't see the blood pressure readings from her home monitor because we had no way to share them electronically.‚Źé‚ŹéI tell you this because it explains why I'm concerned about the proposed removal of the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion. I understand the regulatory impulse toward deregulation and market competition. But what I witnessed wasn't a problem of too much regulation‚ÄĒit was a problem of too little transparency about how healthcare systems make decisions that affect people's lives.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI criterion, established under 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1), requires AI model cards and transparency disclosures. These aren't bureaucratic obstacles. They're documents that specify the intended use of an algorithm, the target population it was designed for, the known limitations and risks, what data it was trained on, and whether it's been validated outside the original training environment. When a clinical decision support tool recommends a medication, dosage, or diagnostic pathway, the physician using it should know whether that recommendation was tested on patients like theirs. They should know if the training data came from a single hospital system or diverse populations. They should understand what the tool is designed to do and what it's not designed for.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY REMOVAL CREATES REAL SAFETY GAPS**‚Źé‚ŹéConsider what happens in practice. A home health agency in rural Nevada uses an AI-assisted tool to flag patients at high risk for hospital readmission. The tool recommends increased monitoring for certain vital signs based on patterns it learned from a dataset of 50,000 urban Medicare patients. But our rural patients have different comorbidity profiles, different access to transportation for preventive care, different medication availability through mail-order pharmacies. If the tool's model card doesn't disclose the source population and the external validation evidence, how does the clinical team know whether this tool's predictions apply to their patients? They don't. They're operating blind.‚Źé‚ŹéThis isn't hypothetical. The FDA's guidance on clinical decision support (referenced in 21 CFR 801.109) distinguishes between tools that simply inform clinical judgment and tools that drive clinical decisions. The distinction matters because the stakes are different. When an algorithm influences treatment decisions for elderly patients on fixed incomes, we need to know who it was built for and how well it works for different groups. The Center for Medicare and Medicaid Services has acknowledged this in their proposed modifications to the Information Blocking Rule (88 FR 78552, November 15, 2023), recognizing that transparency in algorithm design affects care quality and patient safety.‚Źé‚ŹéThe transparency disclosures required under the current DSI criterion aren't onerous. Model cards typically include: (1) intended use and clinical context, (2) target population characteristics, (3) training data sources and size, (4) known performance variations across demographic groups, (5) external validation results, and (6) documented limitations. These elements exist to prevent exactly the situation my mother faced‚ÄĒwhen systems don't communicate and nobody knows what assumptions are baked into the recommendations being followed.‚Źé‚Źé**OUR SPECIFIC RECOMMENDATION**‚Źé‚ŹéNational Alliance for Care at Home recommends that the DSI certification criterion remain in place, specifically the requirements in 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1) regarding AI model cards and transparency disclosures. We base this on three operational realities:‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ Home-based care teams often work with limited IT infrastructure and must rely on clear documentation to understand whether tools are appropriate for their patient populations. Rural clinics and small agencies cannot hire data scientists to reverse-engineer algorithm behavior. Transparency requirements level this playing field.‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ Patient populations receiving home-based care include disproportionate numbers of older adults, individuals with multiple chronic conditions, and immigrants with limited English proficiency. These groups have historically been underrepresented in algorithm training data. Model card disclosures help identify when this is the case, preventing harm from algorithmic bias.‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ Accountability becomes impossible without documentation. If an algorithm recommends a treatment plan that harms a patient, the clinical team and the patient need to understand what inputs drove that recommendation and whether it was validated for that patient's circumstances.‚Źé‚ŹéWe are not arguing against innovation or market competition. We are arguing that innovation in clinical decision support should be transparent innovation. A developer who has built a robust, well-validated tool should welcome model card disclosure because it demonstrates confidence in the work. A tool that performs well across diverse populations and has been externally validated is a competitive advantage, not a liability.‚Źé‚ŹéThe deregulatory actions proposed in this notice should focus on reducing documentation burden, streamlining certification timelines, and removing outdated standards. But removing requirements for transparency in AI-assisted clinical decision-making moves in the wrong direction. It prioritizes speed to market over patient safety for exactly the populations we serve.‚Źé‚ŹéWe appreciate the opportunity to comment and welcome dialogue on implementation approaches that maintain safety while reducing unnecessary burden.‚Źé‚ŹéRespectfully submitted,‚Źé‚ŹéNational Alliance for Care at Home‚ŹéOn behalf of affiliated members and partner organizations‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-26T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-26T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ3‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒurgent‚ôĒNevada‚ôĒcampaign director‚ôĒ65‚ôĒMy mother spent her final eighteen months in her home in Las Vegas with a caregiver we found through a local agency, and I watched firsthand how fragmented data systems forced us to repeat her medical history to seven different providers‚ÄĒeach visit risked gaps that could have been dangerous. Now I'm terrified we're rushing toward a system where that same data gets pooled without real guardrails, and the communities we've built our advocacy around‚ÄĒelderly people on fixed incomes, immigrants who already distrust institutions‚ÄĒwill have no meaningful way to control who sees their most intimate health information.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1024
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0123‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDorothy Scott‚ôĒCareQuest Institute for Oral Health‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒCareQuest Institute for Oral Health opposes the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria requiring transparency disclosures for AI-assisted clinical tools, arguing that such disclosures are essential for clin...‚ôĒRe: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity‚ŹéRIN 0945-ZA03‚ŹéDocket ID: HHS-OS-2024-0012‚Źé‚ŹéCareQuest Institute for Oral Health respectfully submits this comment regarding the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria and associated transparency requirements for artificial intelligence-assisted clinical tools in health information technology systems.‚Źé‚Źé**ORGANIZATIONAL BACKGROUND AND CLINICAL CONTEXT**‚Źé‚ŹéCareQuest Institute for Oral Health is a national non-profit organization dedicated to expanding access to quality oral health care for vulnerable populations. Our organization operates through direct clinical partnerships with community health centers, Federally Qualified Health Centers, and rural health networks across 15 states, including three rural oral health clinics in Oklahoma. We serve as executive leadership and strategic advisor to these clinical operations, and we have direct oversight of patient care quality, data governance, and health IT implementation. Our operational footprint gives us real visibility into how health information technology actually functions in resource-constrained clinical environments where patients are most vulnerable.‚Źé‚ŹéThe oral health care system in rural America operates under conditions of extreme resource scarcity. Our three Oklahoma clinics collectively serve approximately 8,400 Medicaid patients annually. These patients typically have limited access to dental care, no established relationships with multiple providers, and fragmented medical and dental records. Last month, I reviewed our intake and clinical encounter data from these three clinics and identified a pattern that concerns me deeply. Nearly 40% of our Medicaid patients who came through our doors had never been screened for oral cancer by any provider in our network, despite oral cancer being one of the few cancers with significantly improved outcomes when detected early through systematic screening. This wasn't because our clinical staff lacked capacity or training. It was because our health IT systems couldn't reliably track who had been seen where, what interventions had been performed, and what clinical findings had been documented.‚Źé‚ŹéOne specific case illustrated the real-world consequence of this fragmentation. A patient came to our Oklahoma City clinic in March 2024. During her initial comprehensive exam, our clinician noted that she had an existing lesion in her buccal mucosa that had been flagged as suspicious in a clinical note dated eighteen months earlier at a different provider's office across county lines. That earlier provider had documented the finding but had no mechanism to ensure follow-up communication or tracking. The patient had moved, changed insurance, and lost continuity. When we discovered this gap, we spent two weeks tracking down records, confirming the finding, and accelerating her referral to oral surgery. She required a biopsy. The lesion was dysplastic. Had we not caught that gap in the patient's second year without follow-up, the clinical trajectory would likely have been very different.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not an anecdote about a single patient. This is a description of how fragmented health information technology fails vulnerable populations systematically. It is also the context in which I approach the proposed deregulation of DSI certification criteria.‚Źé‚Źé**THE RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS AND PATIENT SAFETY IN AI-ASSISTED CLINICAL TOOLS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal under consideration would remove or substantially weaken certification criteria requiring AI-assisted decision support systems to disclose model cards, intended use statements, target population specifications, known limitations, training data characteristics, and external validation results. The stated rationale is to reduce regulatory burden on health IT developers and accelerate market innovation.‚Źé‚ŹéI understand that rationale. Regulatory burden is real. Compliance costs are real. Time-to-market delays impose real consequences on developers seeking to bring new tools to market. These are legitimate concerns, and I do not dismiss them. What I am asking is whether the specific benefit of removing DSI transparency requirements actually justifies the specific risk being imposed on patient safety in clinical environments like ours.‚Źé‚ŹéLet me be direct about what I think is being underestimated in this analysis. When a health IT vendor develops an AI-assisted clinical decision support tool and brings it to market, that tool makes recommendations to clinicians about patient care. In many cases, especially in resource-constrained settings, clinicians act on those recommendations with limited ability to independently verify them. A clinician in a rural clinic with one dentist, one hygienist, and a part-time records manager does not have the capacity to conduct an independent validation study of an AI algorithm before using it in patient care. That clinician is making a trust-based decision. The question is: what information does that clinician need to make that decision responsibly?‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification criterion requires that decision support tools disclose: (1) the specific clinical context for which the tool was designed and validated; (2) the population characteristics of the training dataset; (3) known performance limitations and failure modes; (4) external validation results from independent testing; and (5) any known disparities in performance across demographic groups (45 CFR 170.315(b)(1), finalized in the 2015 Edition Health IT Certification Criteria).‚Źé‚ŹéThese are not abstract compliance requirements. They are information requirements designed to answer a specific clinical question: "Is this tool safe and effective for my patient population in my clinical context?"‚Źé‚ŹéConsider what happens when a tool is designed and validated on a dataset that is predominantly non-Hispanic white, urban, privately insured patients, but is then deployed in a rural clinic serving 85% Medicaid patients, 40% Hispanic, 35% African American. If that tool has not been externally validated on a population matching the deployed setting, the clinician has no reliable information about whether the tool's recommendations will be accurate for her patients. The tool might have a 92% sensitivity for detecting certain findings in the population it was trained on, but a 67% sensitivity in her patient population due to differences in presentation, imaging quality, or other factors. If the clinician does not know this, she is making care decisions based on incomplete information.‚Źé‚ŹéThe certification criterion does not require that a tool perform equally well across all populations. It requires disclosure. It requires that the clinician know what the tool's limitations are. That is fundamentally different from banning the tool or imposing impossible performance standards.‚Źé‚Źé**REGULATORY AUTHORITY AND THE INFORMATION BLOCKING RULE FRAMEWORK**‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criteria exist within the broader framework established by the 21st Century Cures Act, specifically the information blocking provisions codified in 42 CFR Part 171. The Cures Act established that certified health IT must meet certain standards of transparency and interoperability, with exceptions carved out for legitimate regulatory purposes. The DSI criteria were established as part of the 2015 Edition Health IT Certification Criteria and have been refined through subsequent regulatory cycles.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology (ONC) has authority to establish and modify certification criteria under 42 U.S.C. Section 300jj-52. That authority is broad. However, it is not unlimited. The Cures Act itself directs that certification criteria be designed to promote interoperability, reduce information blocking, and ensure that certified health IT is safe and effective for the populations it is deployed to serve.‚Źé‚ŹéThe specific language in 42 CFR 171.100 defines information blocking as practices that are likely to interfere with, prevent, or materially discourage access, exchange, or use of electronic health information. The regulatory scheme recognizes that transparency itself is a form of protection against information blocking. When a vendor is required to disclose what a tool does, how it was trained, and what its limitations are, that disclosure mechanism creates a form of accountability. The clinician and the organization deploying the tool have information they need to make responsible decisions about implementation.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving that disclosure requirement does not eliminate the underlying risk. It simply transfers the burden of discovery to the clinician and the patient. In our experience, that transfer of burden falls disproportionately on the most vulnerable populations and the most resource-constrained settings.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT THE DATA ACTUALLY SHOWS ABOUT DSI IMPLEMENTATION IN PRACTICE**‚Źé‚ŹéThere is a gap between what the regulations require and what actually happens in clinical deployment. Over the past eighteen months, CareQuest has been working with our partner clinics to implement health IT systems that include AI-assisted decision support components. We have observed how clinicians actually interact with these tools. We have also observed how frequently the transparency information that is supposed to be available in certification documentation actually reaches the clinician making the care decision.‚Źé‚ŹéIn practice, what we have found is this: The DSI certification criterion exists. The disclosure requirements are written into 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1). But the actual flow of that information from vendor to health IT implementation team to clinical leadership to point-of-care clinician is inconsistent and often incomplete. A vendor provides a model card in technical documentation. That documentation sits in a compliance file. The health IT director reviews it. But does the clinician actually see it? Does the clinician understand what it means?‚Źé‚ŹéWe have also found that when transparency information is absent or unclear, clinical teams tend toward one of two responses. Either they implement the tool with excessive caution, using it only for the most straightforward cases and missing opportunities where it could improve care. Or they over-trust the tool, treating its recommendations as more reliable than they actually are and implementing them without adequate clinical scrutiny. Neither response is optimal.‚Źé‚ŹéIn our Oklahoma clinics, we recently implemented an AI-assisted screening tool designed to flag findings in intraoral photographs that might warrant additional clinical evaluation. The vendor provided a model card that disclosed that the tool was trained primarily on images from urban dental schools and had not been externally validated on images from community health center settings. That disclosure was critical. It told us: "This tool might have different performance characteristics in your setting." We implemented the tool as an alert mechanism for our clinicians to review, not as a primary diagnostic tool. We trained our staff on its limitations. We built in a clinical verification step. The tool has been useful, but only because we had the information we needed to implement it responsibly.‚Źé‚ŹéIf that transparency requirement had not existed, the vendor could have provided us with minimal information about the tool's training data and validation. We would have implemented it differently, and likely with greater risk.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC CONCERNS ABOUT PROPOSED REMOVAL OF TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal would remove or significantly weaken the DSI certification criterion. The stated rationale emphasizes burden reduction and market acceleration. But I want to be specific about what I think would be lost.‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, the removal would eliminate a standardized mechanism for capturing and disclosing information about training data characteristics. This matters because training data characteristics are not neutral. An AI model trained on a dataset that overrepresents certain demographic groups or certain clinical presentations will have performance characteristics that reflect that training data. A model trained on 80% non-Hispanic white patients will likely perform differently on Hispanic and African American patients. This is not speculation. It is documented in the literature. The 2021 study by Obermeyer et al. in Science demonstrated systematic racial bias in a widely used clinical risk algorithm because the training data did not adequately represent minority populations. More recent work by Rajkomar et al. at Google Health has shown similar patterns in AI-assisted diagnostic tools. These are not edge cases. They are normal characteristics of how machine learning systems behave.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI transparency requirement does not solve this problem. But it makes it visible. A clinician who knows that a tool was trained on a dataset that is predominantly one demographic group can ask: "How was the tool validated in our patient population?" If the answer is "it wasn't," the clinician can take appropriate precautions. If that information is not disclosed, the clinician does not have the option to ask the question.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, the removal would eliminate standardized disclosure of known limitations and failure modes. Every AI system has limitations. Every model has cases where it performs poorly. A tool designed to detect early signs of oral cancer in photographs might perform well on clear, well-lit images but poorly on images with motion artifact or suboptimal lighting. A tool designed to assist with treatment planning might have lower performance when presented with complex multi-tooth cases. These limitations are not failures of the regulation. They are realities of the technology. The question is whether the clinician knows about them.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, the removal would eliminate requirements for external validation. External validation is the gold standard for assessing whether a model trained on one dataset will actually perform as expected when deployed on new data from a different setting. A tool validated only on data from the vendor's development environment might perform very differently in actual clinical practice. This is not a hypothetical concern. It is standard practice in clinical validation studies to test tools on independent data. The DSI criterion requires it. Removing that requirement means vendors could bring tools to market with validation evidence limited to internal testing.‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be clear about what I am not saying. I am not saying that AI-assisted clinical tools should be banned. I am not saying that the burden on vendors is not real or that regulatory streamlining has no value. What I am saying is that the specific transparency requirements in the DSI criterion serve a specific function, and that function is important for patient safety in resource-constrained settings where clinicians cannot independently validate tools before deploying them.‚Źé‚Źé**IMPLEMENTATION EXPERIENCE AND WHAT WORKS IN PRACTICE**‚Źé‚ŹéCareQuest's experience implementing health IT systems in rural and safety-net settings has taught us something about what makes implementation successful and what creates risk. The organizations that implement AI-assisted tools most successfully are those that have clear information about what the tools are designed to do, what populations they were validated on, and what their limitations are. That information allows clinical leadership to make informed decisions about implementation, training, and appropriate use.‚Źé‚ŹéConversely, the situations where we have seen problems are typically those where transparency is incomplete. A vendor provides minimal documentation. Clinical leadership makes assumptions about the tool's scope and limitations. The tool gets deployed in a way that exceeds its validated use case. Clinicians encounter situations where the tool's recommendations seem wrong, and they lose confidence in the system.‚Źé‚ŹéWe have also observed that transparency requirements, when properly implemented, do not actually slow adoption of good tools. A well-designed tool with clear documentation of its capabilities and limitations tends to gain clinical acceptance faster than a tool with ambiguous or minimal documentation. Clinicians trust tools they understand. They are skeptical of tools that seem to be a "black box."‚Źé‚ŹéThe cost of the transparency requirement is real. Vendors must develop model cards. They must conduct external validation studies. These activities require resources and time. But the cost of removing the requirement is also real. It is borne by clinicians who must make care decisions without adequate information, by patients who receive care based on tools whose performance characteristics they cannot assess, and by health systems that implement tools that may not be appropriate for their populations.‚Źé‚Źé**ALTERNATIVE APPROACHES TO REGULATORY STREAMLINING**‚Źé‚ŹéI recognize that the goal of this rulemaking is to reduce unnecessary burden while maintaining safety. There may be ways to achieve that goal without eliminating transparency requirements entirely.‚Źé‚ŹéOne approach would be to streamline the process for vendors to comply with the transparency requirement, rather than eliminating the requirement itself. For example:‚Źé- Establish a standardized template for model cards that reduces the documentation burden while ensuring that critical information is disclosed.‚Źé- Allow vendors to disclose certain categories of information (such as training data characteristics) in a simplified format that does not require extensive statistical analysis.‚Źé- Create a tiered approach where tools intended for lower-risk use cases have lighter documentation requirements than tools intended for high-risk applications.‚Źé- Establish performance benchmarks for external validation that reflect the actual complexity of conducting such studies in real-world settings.‚Źé‚ŹéAnother approach would be to maintain the transparency requirement but provide clearer guidance about what information must be disclosed and in what format. This would reduce ambiguity and allow vendors to comply more efficiently.‚Źé‚ŹéA third approach would be to transition from a pre-market certification model to a post-market surveillance model where transparency requirements are maintained but compliance timelines are extended, allowing vendors to gather external validation data after initial deployment rather than before.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat I am asking the agency to consider is whether removing transparency requirements entirely is the only way to achieve the stated goal of reducing burden. My experience suggests it is not.‚Źé‚Źé**THE SPECIFIC STAKES FOR ORAL HEALTH CARE AND VULNERABLE POPULATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéOral health care is the setting where AI-assisted decision support tools are likely to proliferate rapidly in the next 3-5 years. The technology is advancing quickly. Vendors are developing tools for screening, diagnosis, treatment planning, and quality assessment. These tools will be deployed in safety-net clinics, community health centers, and rural practices. Many of those settings have limited resources for independent technology assessment and clinical validation.‚Źé‚ŹéOral cancer, specifically, is a condition where early detection has profound implications for patient outcomes. Five-year survival rates for oral cancer detected at stage 1 exceed 80%. Survival rates for stage 4 disease are below 20%. The difference between early and late detection is often the difference between survival and death. If an AI-assisted screening tool is trained on a population that does not represent the populations being served, and if the clinician does not know that limitation, the result could be systematic underdetection in certain populations.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not abstract. It is a real risk in real clinical settings where real patients are being cared for.‚Źé‚ŹéThe populations most dependent on community health centers and rural clinics for oral health care are predominantly Medicaid-eligible, and they are disproportionately people of color. These are the exact populations most likely to be underrepresented in the training datasets for AI tools developed by larger vendors. These are also the populations least able to advocate for themselves if tools perform poorly.‚Źé‚Źé**RECOMMENDATION**‚Źé‚ŹéCareQuest Institute for Oral Health recommends that the Health and Human Services Office of the Secretary, in coordination with the Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology, retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion requiring AI model transparency disclosures, including disclosure of intended use, target population, known limitations, training data characteristics, and external validation results.‚Źé‚ŹéWe further recommend that the agency consider streamlining the compliance process for this criterion rather than eliminating it, and that the agency provide clear guidance to vendors about the specific information that must be disclosed and the format in which it should be provided.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the agency determines that modification of the DSI criterion is necessary, we recommend that any modification preserve the core requirement for disclosure of training data characteristics and external validation results, as these are the elements most critical to ensuring that clinicians have information adequate to make responsible decisions about implementation in their specific clinical settings.‚Źé‚ŹéThe burden of regulatory compliance is real. But the burden of clinical uncertainty is also real, and it falls most heavily on the patients and clinicians in the settings least able to absorb it. We respectfully ask that the agency weigh these burdens carefully before proceeding with removal of transparency requirements.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-21T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-21T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ10‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒurgent‚ôĒOklahoma‚ôĒexecutive director‚ôĒ37‚ôĒLast month, I reviewed our intake data from three rural Oklahoma clinics and found that nearly 40% of our Medicaid patients had never been screened for oral cancer‚ÄĒnot because we lack the clinical capacity, but because fragmented records mean we can't reliably track who's been seen where. When I finally connected with a patient's previous provider across county lines, I discovered she'd already had a suspicious lesion flagged two years ago that somehow never made it to her current care team. That gap nearly cost her a diagnosis.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ3181
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0124‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒGeorge Scott‚ôĒiPill inc‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒiPill Inc. opposes eliminating AI decision support transparency requirements in health IT certification, arguing that while certification burden is real and should be streamlined, removing transparency standards would enable cost-cutting competito...‚ôĒCOMMENT ON PROPOSED DEREGULATORY ACTIONS FOR HEALTH IT CERTIFICATION‚Źé‚ŹéSubmitted on behalf of iPill Inc.‚Źé‚ŹéGeorge Scott‚ŹéCompliance Officer‚ŹéiPill Inc.‚ŹéArizona‚Źé‚Źé**BACKGROUND AND ORGANIZATIONAL STAKE**‚Źé‚ŹéiPill Inc. operates as a health IT vendor serving healthcare providers across multiple states, with particular focus on rural and underserved markets. My role managing compliance and certification matters means I spend considerable time navigating the current certification framework. Last year alone, we invested $340,000 to recertify a single decision support module that fewer than 10 percent of our clients were actively using. That cost had to pass through to customers, and it directly contributed to losing a long-standing client relationship in rural Nevada who switched to a competitor with deeper pockets to absorb certification expenses. I watched that happen, and I understand the frustration with regulatory burden. But I also know what happened next, and it matters for how we think about what to keep and what to change.‚Źé‚ŹéThat same competitor's platform, while cheaper upfront, provided minimal transparency about how their AI-driven clinical decision support actually worked. Our client never got clear documentation about the model's training data, intended use cases, or known limitations. When the system flagged a medication interaction incorrectly for one of their patients, there was no framework for understanding why, and no accountability pathway. Within four months, they switched back. The cost savings evaporated, replaced by clinical risk they couldn't tolerate.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CASE FOR RETAINING DECISION SUPPORT TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule would eliminate or significantly reduce certification requirements for Decision Support Interventions, including the requirement for AI model cards and transparency disclosures (45 CFR 170.315(a)(1) and related guidance from ONC's 2015 Edition Health IT Certification Companion Guide). I want to be direct: removing these requirements is a mistake, even if I understand the impulse to reduce burden.‚Źé‚ŹéHere is what the current criterion actually requires:‚Źé‚Źé- Documentation of intended use and target population‚Źé- Identification of known risks and limitations‚Źé- Characteristics of training data used‚Źé- Evidence of external validation‚Źé- Transparency about how the system generates recommendations‚Źé‚ŹéNone of this is theoretical. When a cardiologist receives a recommendation to escalate treatment based on risk stratification, that clinician needs to know whether the model was trained on a diverse population or primarily on data from large academic centers. When a primary care physician gets a decision support alert, they need to understand the evidence base behind it. The burden here is not compliance theater. It is patient safety (42 U.S.C. 1395jj, establishing quality and safety as core to Medicare participation). It is informed consent. It is accountability.‚Źé‚ŹéI have walked through dozens of implementations, and I can tell you that the transparency requirement does create real work. Vendors have to invest in documentation. We have to maintain external validation studies. Yes, that costs money. But the alternative is a market where smaller vendors can undercut larger ones by simply providing less information, which pushes the price of safety onto healthcare systems and ultimately onto patients who have no way to evaluate these tools independently.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT SHOULD CHANGE INSTEAD**‚Źé‚ŹéI am not arguing that certification burden is imaginary. It is real, and some of it is poorly designed. But the solution is not to remove transparency requirements for AI-driven clinical decision support. The solution is to streamline the certification process itself while keeping the substantive requirements intact.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider a tiered approach (similar to models used in FDA's real-world performance monitoring framework, 21 CFR 860.7):‚Źé‚Źé1. Vendors should be permitted to submit model cards and validation documentation once, with annual updates rather than full recertification unless material changes occur.‚Źé2. ONC should establish template requirements for transparency disclosures, reducing the time vendors spend on documentation format rather than content quality.‚Źé3. For low-risk decision support tools (those that provide information only, without direct clinical recommendations), reduced documentation requirements would be appropriate.‚Źé‚Źé4. For higher-risk tools (those that recommend specific treatments or interventions), current transparency standards should remain in place.‚Źé‚ŹéThis approach reduces compliance costs without sacrificing the transparency that clinicians and patients actually need. It acknowledges that certification burden exists while protecting the safeguards that matter most.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current system is not perfect, but removing the transparency requirements will not create a better market. It will create a cheaper market, and those are not always the same thing.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-18T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-18T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHospital and health system procurement teams need transparent AI model information to make evidence-based decisions about which tools to adopt and how to implement them safely in their local contexts.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒurgent‚ôĒArizona‚ôĒcompliance officer‚ôĒ44‚ôĒLast quarter, we spent $340,000 on recertification for a single module that our clients weren't even using‚ÄĒmeanwhile, a competitor half our size undercut us because they could absorb those costs into a larger parent company's compliance budget. When our smallest client in rural Nevada had to choose between renewing our contract or switching to that competitor's platform, the certification costs we had to pass through made the decision for them, and I watched years of relationship-building evaporate over regulatory inefficiency that benefited nobody.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ720
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0125‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒMargaret Thompson‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe commenter supports improved health information interoperability but warns against weakening AI transparency and validation requirements in healthcare, arguing that accountability and rigorous testing of AI systems used in clinical decision-mak...‚ôĒMy dad had a heart attack two years ago, and when we tried to move his records from Mercy Hospital to the VA, the systems couldn't share the information. He had to get retested for everything. The doctors caught what they needed to, but I kept thinking about how dangerous that gap could have been if he'd been sicker or if the delay had cost us time we didn't have.‚Źé‚ŹéI get that making systems talk to each other matters. But I'm worried about what happens when we strip away the requirements that make sure hospitals and doctors understand how the AI tools they're using actually work. You can't trust a black box with someone's health. If a hospital is going to use an AI system to help decide my family's care, someone needs to make sure that system was properly tested and validated. That's not red tape, that's accountability. Before we remove transparency requirements or testing standards, I need to know there's still real oversight. When something goes wrong, someone has to answer for it. That's the only way I'm going to feel like my family is safe.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-31T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-31T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒmedium‚ôĒconcerned‚ôĒPennsylvania‚ôĒfirefighter‚ôĒ41‚ôĒWhen my dad had his heart attack two years ago, I couldn't get his records transferred from Mercy to the VA hospital fast enough‚ÄĒwe literally had to have him re-tested because the systems wouldn't talk to each other. I was grateful the doctors caught everything in time, but I remember thinking how dangerous that gap could be for someone sicker or older. Now I'm worried that making it easier to move records around means less oversight of who's actually looking at them, and I don't want my family's medical history floating around without protection.‚ôĒDoctors and patients deserve to know exactly how AI systems work before they're used to make decisions about our health‚ÄĒthat's just basic accountability and safety, not red tape. | Removing transparency requirements like model cards means hospitals could use AI systems without understanding their limitations, which puts vulnerable patients at risk just like my dad was at risk when his records got lost between systems. | When you strip away oversight of how AI was tested and validated, you're essentially asking patients to trust a black box instead of trusting their doctors to make informed decisions.‚ôĒAs someone who's seen firsthand how medical system failures can endanger lives, I need to know that AI tools being used on my family have been properly tested and that someone is actually accountable if something goes wrong.‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ189
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0126‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒPatricia Williams‚ôĒNational Alliance for Care at Home‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe National Alliance for Care at Home opposes removing the Decision Support Intervention certification requirement, arguing that eliminating transparency and validation standards for AI clinical tools would shift risk to vulnerable patients and h...‚ôĒ**COMMENT TO DEPARTMENT OF HEALTH AND HUMAN SERVICES, OFFICE OF THE NATIONAL COORDINATOR FOR HEALTH INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY**‚Źé‚Źé**Re: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity; RIN 0945-ZA28**‚Źé‚ŹéNational Alliance for Care at Home submits the following comment in response to the proposed rule regarding decision support interventions and AI model certification requirements in health information technology.‚Źé‚Źé**BACKGROUND AND ORGANIZATIONAL STANDING**‚Źé‚ŹéThe National Alliance for Care at Home represents community organizations, care coordinators, and patient advocates across 15 states working directly with older adults and disabled individuals navigating complex healthcare decisions. Our members serve populations with limited health literacy, fragmented care relationships, and significant vulnerability to information asymmetry. We have seen firsthand how opacity in clinical decision-making tools affects real people's health outcomes and their willingness to participate in their own care.‚Źé‚ŹéLast year, our Seattle office worked with Maria, a 76-year-old stroke survivor with no family nearby. After her hospitalization, Maria was referred to an outpatient physical therapy provider for rehabilitation. Three months into her treatment, she discovered that her PT clinic was sharing her location data, appointment history, and functional status information with a third-party vendor without her knowledge or explicit consent. When we helped Maria request information about who had access to her health data and for what purpose, the clinic could not provide clear answers. The PT staff themselves seemed uncertain about what was being shared. Maria's response was to stop reporting her symptoms honestly during sessions. She downplayed her pain levels and fatigue because she felt violated and worried about where her information was going. Her therapist, unaware of this self-censorship, incorrectly concluded that her recovery had plateaued and adjusted her treatment plan accordingly. That misjudgment delayed her progress by weeks.‚Źé‚ŹéThis experience shapes our position on the proposed deregulation of decision support interventions and AI model transparency requirements. We are not opposed to innovation in clinical decision tools. We are opposed to removing the guardrails that would allow similar opacity to occur at scale across healthcare systems.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CURRENT CERTIFICATION REQUIREMENT AND WHAT IT DOES**‚Źé‚ŹéUnder 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1), health IT developers must submit Decision Support Interventions (DSI) for certification and provide model cards documenting intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. This is not a burdensome requirement. It is a documentation requirement. It asks that developers disclose what their AI tool is designed to do, who it is designed for, what its limitations are, and whether it has been tested outside the development environment.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule would remove this certification criterion entirely, moving DSI to a voluntary disclosure framework with no standardized format, no requirement for validation, and no obligation for developers to identify populations for whom their tool might perform poorly.‚Źé‚ŹéWe understand the argument: certification takes time, costs money, slows product release. Developers want to move faster. But the framing of this as "unleashing prosperity" misses what actually gets unleashed when you remove transparency requirements from tools that influence clinical decisions. What gets unleashed is the ability to deploy AI systems without knowing whether they work, for whom they work, and what harms they might cause.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THIS MATTERS FOR VULNERABLE POPULATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe patients we serve are not abstract beneficiaries of "market innovation." They are people like Maria. They are also people who cannot easily challenge a clinician's AI-assisted decision. They are people who depend on their providers to act in their interest because they lack the health literacy or social capital to demand accountability.‚Źé‚ŹéResearch published in JAMA Health Forum (2023) examined algorithmic bias in clinical decision support tools and found that tools trained predominantly on data from majority populations showed significantly reduced accuracy for older adults and people with multiple chronic conditions. That was with certification requirements in place. Without transparency about training data and validation populations, that bias will be invisible until patients experience it.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider a hypothetical decision support tool designed to help clinicians identify patients at high risk for hospital readmission. If that tool was trained on data from urban academic medical centers serving mostly insured patients, it may systematically underestimate risk for rural patients, patients with transportation barriers, or patients without stable housing. A clinician using that tool would have no way of knowing about this limitation because the developer would have no obligation to disclose the training population or external validation results. The clinician might deprioritize discharge planning for a patient flagged as "low risk" by the tool, when in fact the tool's accuracy for that patient's demographic group is unknown.‚Źé‚ŹéThe patients harmed by this scenario are not theoretical. They are the ones we work with every day.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT THE CERTIFICATION REQUIREMENT ACTUALLY REQUIRES**‚Źé‚ŹéWe want to be precise about what is being proposed for removal. The certification criterion requires:‚Źé‚Źé1. Documentation of intended use and clinical context for which the DSI is designed‚Źé2. Identification of the target population and any known limitations by population subgroup‚Źé3. Description of the training data characteristics, including data sources, time period, and population demographics‚Źé4. Evidence of external validation on data not used in model development‚Źé5. Disclosure of known risks and performance limitations‚Źé‚ŹéNone of this prevents a tool from being used. None of this requires perfection. A developer can certify a tool that works well in some populations and poorly in others, as long as they disclose that. A developer can certify a tool that has significant limitations, as long as they document what those limitations are. What a developer cannot do is certify a tool without acknowledging what they actually know about its performance.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not a burden. This is accountability.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Office of the National Coordinator's own guidance on AI governance (ONC AI Governance Playbook, 2024) recommends that healthcare organizations using AI tools conduct validation studies, document performance across demographic groups, and monitor for bias in real-world use. The certification requirement simply ensures that developers, not just adopting health systems, are responsible for basic transparency.‚Źé‚Źé**REMOVING CERTIFICATION WOULD CREATE REAL PROBLEMS**‚Źé‚ŹéWe recognize that some developers argue that the certification process delays product release and that removing it would accelerate innovation. We are skeptical of this framing for three reasons.‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, the certification process for DSI tools under the current rule is not lengthy. Developers submit model cards and validation evidence. ONC reviews for completeness. The median review time is approximately 90 days. This is not the bottleneck to healthcare innovation. The bottleneck is clinical adoption, which depends on trust and evidence of performance. Developers who can demonstrate that their tools work across diverse populations will have a competitive advantage in the market. Developers who cannot or will not provide this evidence will be at a disadvantage. This is how markets are supposed to work.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, removing the certification requirement would not actually reduce burden. It would shift burden from developers to health systems. Every hospital, clinic, and care coordination program would need to conduct its own validation studies of every AI tool it considers adopting. This is vastly more expensive and duplicative than requiring developers to validate once and disclose the results. A small primary care practice in rural Washington cannot afford to hire data scientists to validate a decision support tool. Under the current rule, they can rely on the developer's certification. Under the proposed rule, they would have to choose between adopting tools blind or conducting expensive validation themselves. The net effect is increased burden on small providers and reduced access to validated tools.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, moving to a "voluntary disclosure" framework does not actually increase competition or innovation. It increases the risk of adverse selection. Developers with good validation results would want to disclose them to differentiate their products. Developers with poor or incomplete validation results would have incentive to disclose nothing. Clinicians would have no way to distinguish between a developer who has chosen not to validate and a developer who validated and found poor results. This asymmetry of information is the opposite of what competitive markets need to function.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT CERTIFICATION ACTUALLY PROTECTS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe certification requirement protects three things: patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability.‚Źé‚ŹéPatient safety is straightforward. A decision support tool that has not been validated on diverse populations and is deployed without documentation of its limitations can harm patients when it fails in ways the clinician did not anticipate. We have seen this in other healthcare contexts. The COMPAS algorithm used in criminal justice to assess recidivism risk was deployed without adequate validation and was later found to have significant racial bias. The algorithm was not inherently malicious. It was the product of poor validation and inadequate transparency about its limitations. When similar tools are deployed in clinical settings without validation, the harm is direct and immediate.‚Źé‚ŹéInformed clinical decision-making requires that clinicians understand the tools they are using. Under the current rule, a clinician can request the model card and understand what the tool is designed to do, what populations it has been validated on, and what its known limitations are. This information allows the clinician to use the tool appropriately. A clinician who knows that a readmission risk tool was validated only in urban academic medical centers can adjust her interpretation of the tool's output for a rural patient. A clinician who has no information about the tool's validation has no basis for such adjustment.‚Źé‚ŹéAccountability is perhaps most important for vulnerable populations. When a tool causes harm, the question of who is responsible matters. If a developer deployed a tool without validation and without documentation of its limitations, the developer should be responsible for harm caused by the tool's failure. If the developer can claim they never promised the tool would work for a particular population because there is no requirement to document anything, accountability becomes impossible. Patients harmed by the tool have no recourse.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PROPOSED ALTERNATIVE IS NOT ADEQUATE**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule suggests that "market forces" and "reputation" will encourage developers to validate tools and disclose limitations voluntarily. This is optimistic and, frankly, not grounded in the history of healthcare innovation.‚Źé‚ŹéWe have seen repeatedly in healthcare that voluntary disclosure frameworks fail to protect patients. The FDA's 510(k) pathway for medical devices allowed companies to bring devices to market based on substantial equivalence to existing devices, with minimal evidence of safety and effectiveness. The result was that some devices harmed patients before the FDA intervened. The Theranos blood-testing scandal involved a company that made claims about its technology without adequate validation and without transparent disclosure of what the technology could actually do. The company's downfall came only after journalists and regulators forced disclosure, not because of market reputation effects.‚Źé‚ŹéIn healthcare, reputation effects are weak because patients do not choose their decision support tools. Clinicians choose them, and clinicians may not be aware when a tool performs poorly. A tool that systematically under-estimates risk for certain populations may appear to work fine because the patients it fails to identify do not return to the clinic to report that they were misclassified. The harm is invisible.‚Źé‚ŹéVoluntary disclosure is also subject to strategic non-disclosure. A developer with poor validation results has incentive to disclose nothing and claim that validation is proprietary or ongoing. A clinician reviewing the developer's voluntary disclosure will not know whether the absence of information reflects absence of validation or strategic non-disclosure. This uncertainty is bad for patients.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC CONCERNS ABOUT THE PROPOSED APPROACH**‚Źé‚ŹéWe have three specific concerns about how the proposed rule would operate in practice.‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, the proposed rule suggests that developers would provide "voluntary" documentation of AI tool performance. But there is no mechanism to ensure that this documentation is accurate, complete, or updated as the tool is deployed in clinical settings. Under the current rule, ONC reviews model cards for completeness and accuracy. Under the proposed rule, there is no review process. A developer could claim that their tool has been validated on "diverse populations" without defining what diverse means, where the data came from, or what the results actually showed. Clinicians would have no basis for evaluating these claims.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, the proposed rule does not address the problem of performance monitoring in real-world use. A tool may perform well in the development environment but differently when deployed in actual clinical settings with real patients and real clinicians. The current rule does not explicitly address post-deployment monitoring, but it creates a foundation for it by requiring baseline documentation of validation and performance. The proposed rule removes even that foundation. Clinicians would have no standard expectation that developers are monitoring tool performance over time or reporting changes in accuracy or bias.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, the proposed rule does not address the problem of tool modifications and updates. A developer may start with a validated tool but then modify it to add new features, change the underlying model, or adapt it for new clinical contexts. Each modification could change the tool's performance characteristics. Under the current rule, significant modifications would require recertification. Under the proposed rule, there would be no mechanism to identify when a modification is significant enough to require new validation. A clinician using a tool would have no way of knowing whether the version they are using is the version that was validated or a later version with unknown performance characteristics.‚Źé‚Źé**THE ALTERNATIVE: RETAIN THE REQUIREMENT WITH MODEST REFORMS**‚Źé‚ŹéWe are not arguing that the current certification process is perfect. There are legitimate questions about how long certification should take, what evidence should be required, and how to balance the need for transparency with the reality that perfect validation is impossible.‚Źé‚ŹéBut the answer to these questions is not to eliminate the requirement. The answer is to reform it thoughtfully.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current rule requires external validation, which is appropriate. However, there could be clarity about what constitutes adequate external validation. A tool validated on 1000 patients in one hospital system is less convincing than a tool validated across multiple health systems and diverse populations. ONC could publish guidance clarifying what evidence thresholds are expected for certification, allowing developers to understand the requirements in advance.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current rule requires documentation of training data characteristics. This is appropriate. However, there could be flexibility about what training data is used. A tool trained on proprietary data that a developer cannot fully disclose could still be certified if the developer provides sufficient information about the data source, time period, and population characteristics that clinicians can assess relevance to their own patients.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current rule requires documentation of known risks. This is appropriate. However, there could be clarity about what "known" means. A developer does not need to identify every possible limitation but should identify limitations that were discovered during development and validation or that are inherent to the tool's design.‚Źé‚ŹéThese are modest reforms that would reduce burden while maintaining the core protection of transparency. ONC could implement them through guidance or through a modest rule revision. The goal would be to speed up certification without eliminating it.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéWe are writing this comment because we have seen what happens when clinical tools are deployed without adequate understanding of their limitations. We have seen patients harmed when their clinicians relied on tools that performed poorly for their particular characteristics. We have seen patients lose trust in their care when they discovered that information about their health was being shared in ways they did not understand.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule would make these problems worse. It would allow developers to deploy AI tools without validation, without transparency about training data and populations, and without accountability for performance failures. This is not innovation. This is shifting risk from developers to patients.‚Źé‚ŹéWe understand that certification takes time and costs money. But the alternative, which is deploying unvalidated tools in clinical settings where they influence decisions about patient care, is worse. The costs of that approach would be borne by patients in the form of misdiagnosis, delayed treatment, and erosion of trust in healthcare.‚Źé‚ŹéWe urge the Office of the National Coordinator to retain the current Decision Support Intervention certification requirement. We welcome discussion about how to streamline the requirement or make it more efficient. But we cannot support its elimination.‚Źé‚ŹéThe patients we serve deserve better.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-21T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-21T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ8‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒfrustrated‚ôĒWashington‚ôĒcommunity organizer‚ôĒ58‚ôĒLast year, I helped Maria‚ÄĒa 76-year-old client with no family nearby‚ÄĒnavigate her Medicare options after a stroke, and we discovered her physical therapy provider was sharing her location data with third parties without her knowledge or explicit consent. When I tried to find out who had access and why, I hit a wall of corporate opacity that left her feeling utterly violated. She stopped reporting her symptoms honestly to her PT out of fear, which actually set back her recovery.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2699
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0127‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒGeorge Gonzalez‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒGeorge Gonzalez opposes removing transparency requirements for AI systems in healthcare, arguing that patients and doctors need clear information about how AI makes medical decisions and what errors it might produce, especially given existing diff...‚ôĒMy name is George Gonzalez and I live in Washington. My daughter had to call twice just to get them to send a CD, and even then half the information was missing. I was so angry about that.‚Źé‚ŹéNow you're talking about removing rules that make AI systems show how they work and what they might get wrong. I don't understand why you'd do this. If a computer is making decisions about my health, I deserve to know how it was trained and what it might mess up. Doctors need to know if the AI is giving them good advice or bad advice.‚Źé‚ŹéWe already can't get our own medical records when we need them. Loosening transparency rules on AI will just make things worse. At least with my old doctor I could ask questions. With some invisible computer system, who do I even call? Nobody has to tell me anything.‚Źé‚ŹéThis doesn't make sense. You're stripping away the only protection we have to actually understand what's happening with our care. We deserve better than another runaround.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-05T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-05T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒlow‚ôĒangry‚ôĒWashington‚ôĒcustomer service representative‚ôĒ78‚ôĒLast month my doctor retired and I had to switch to a new clinic across town, but they said they couldn't get my heart records from the old office for three weeks.
