CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0128‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒGeorge Sanchez‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA former librarian argues that the proposed elimination of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements would dangerously remove critical pre-market transparency safeguards for clinical AI systems, and that model cards and discl...‚ôĒ**RE: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity**‚Źé‚ŹéWhen my cardiologist retired last month, I spent three weeks shuttling between his old office and the new one, watching my test results sit in limbo while both institutions cited "security protocols" and "system incompatibility." The new doctor couldn't start my treatment plan. The old office wouldn't release the data. I realized then that I'd spent forty years helping people access information in a library, yet I couldn't access my own health information that literally concerned my survival. The irony was bitter, but it clarified something about this proposed rule.‚Źé‚ŹéI understand the appeal of removing regulatory friction. Developers should not be buried under unnecessary compliance burdens. But I'm writing to say that the proposed elimination of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements‚ÄĒspecifically the removal of model cards and transparency disclosures‚ÄĒsolves the wrong problem and creates real danger for patients like me.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT THE CURRENT CERTIFICATION CRITERION ACTUALLY REQUIRES**‚Źé‚ŹéThe existing DSI criterion under 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1) asks developers to document four things: the intended use of their AI model, the target population it was designed for, known risks and limitations, and characteristics of the training data plus external validation results. These aren't burdensome academic exercises. They're the baseline information a clinician needs to know whether an AI system should influence a decision about my care.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen I finally got my records transferred, my new cardiologist mentioned reviewing an AI risk score the old office had generated. I asked him what that score was trained on, whether it applied to someone my age with my specific conditions, what it might miss. He couldn't answer. He literally did not know because that information was never provided to him. He had to make decisions based on a black box recommendation. That's not innovation. That's negligence dressed up as efficiency.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CORE PROBLEM WITH DEREGULATION HERE**‚Źé‚ŹéThe RFI proposes removing the DSI certification criterion entirely, arguing it duplicates other safeguards and imposes costs on developers. I've read the supporting documents. The cost argument doesn't hold up. A model card is not an elaborate document. The FDA's guidance on AI/ML-based Software as a Medical Device (2021) and similar frameworks ask for transparency that takes weeks, not months, to compile if the model was built responsibly in the first place. If it takes a developer months to write a model card, that's a sign the model itself was not built with transparency in mind, which is precisely when we need the safeguard.‚Źé‚ŹéBut here's what really concerns me: the proposed rule assumes that market competition and post-market surveillance will catch bad AI decisions. This is fantasy. I'm a consumer. I don't compare AI models the way I compare pharmacy prices. I don't have the technical literacy to audit a neural network. My doctor doesn't either. Patients and clinicians are not the market competition here. We're the users of systems we can't evaluate. We rely on certification to tell us someone competent reviewed this before it went into the clinical workflow.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THIS MATTERS FOR PATIENT SAFETY AND INFORMED DECISION-MAKING**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be clear about what I'm not arguing. I'm not saying AI in healthcare is bad. I'm not saying all regulation is good. My concern is surgical: the DSI certification criterion serves three specific functions that deregulation would remove.‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, it ensures intended use is documented and communicated. An AI model trained to predict 30-day readmission risk for acute heart failure patients should not be used to triage primary care appointments for asthma. This sounds obvious, but it happens. Clinicians under time pressure use available tools for purposes beyond their design. A documented intended use statement, required by the current criterion, creates a paper trail and a professional obligation not to misuse the tool.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, it requires disclosure of target population characteristics. The training data for my cardiologist's risk score might have been 85 percent white men over 65 in urban hospital systems. My cardiologist is treating a diverse population, including women, younger patients, and rural populations. The current criterion requires developers to disclose this mismatch. Without it, the clinician has no way to know whether the model's recommendations apply to me. Removing this requirement doesn't make the mismatch disappear. It just makes it invisible.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, it mandates external validation results. This is not theoretical. A model that performs well on the developer's proprietary dataset might perform poorly on real-world data from different populations, different EHR systems, different clinical workflows. The current criterion requires testing beyond the developer's controlled environment. Post-market surveillance, the proposed replacement, only catches failures after they've harmed patients. External validation happens before deployment.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC RISKS OF THE PROPOSED APPROACH**‚Źé‚ŹéThe RFI suggests that information blocking regulations and post-market surveillance will substitute for pre-market certification. This is not equivalent. Here's why:‚Źé‚ŹéInformation blocking rules prevent vendors from restricting data access. They do not require vendors to explain how they use that data in their algorithms. A clinician can access my health information under the information blocking rule and still have no idea how the AI system recommends using it. These are different problems.‚Źé‚ŹéPost-market surveillance, the FDA's authority to monitor and recall devices after they're in use, is reactive. By definition, it operates after clinical deployment. If an AI model systematically underestimates stroke risk in women because it was trained on male-dominated datasets, post-market surveillance might eventually catch this. But how many patients will have had inadequate stroke prevention in the meantime? How many strokes will have occurred before someone notices the pattern?‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI criterion is preventive. It asks developers to show their work before the model influences clinical decisions. I've been in healthcare now for a few months as a patient, and I can tell you the difference matters. Prevention is better than reaction.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT SHOULD HAPPEN INSTEAD**‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not arguing for more regulation. I'm arguing against removing what works. The DSI certification criterion should be retained as written, with one possible adjustment: streamline the documentation format to reduce administrative burden without reducing transparency. Require model cards, but allow developers to use a standardized template (the FDA's guidance or similar) rather than writing lengthy prose documents.‚Źé‚ŹéAdditionally, consider clarifying the criterion to explicitly cover:‚Źé‚Źé1. Characteristics of the training dataset (size, composition, source, date range)‚Źé2. Performance metrics on external validation data, broken down by demographic subgroups‚Źé3. Known limitations and failure modes‚Źé4. Guidance on appropriate clinical contexts for use‚Źé‚ŹéThese are not new requirements. They're clarifications of what responsible AI development already demands. What the current criterion does is make that responsibility visible and auditable.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY A LIBRARIAN CARES ABOUT THIS**‚Źé‚ŹéI spent my career believing that access to information is fundamental to human dignity and good decision-making. That belief doesn't change when the information is about a patient's own health. What I've learned as a patient is that access to data is necessary but not sufficient. You also need transparency about how that data is used, especially when algorithms influence decisions about your care.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criterion is about transparency. It's about making sure that when an algorithm recommends something to my doctor, my doctor knows what that algorithm was trained on, who it was designed for, and what it might miss. That's not anti-innovation. It's the baseline for responsible innovation.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm asking the Office of the National Coordinator to retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion in 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1) and to reject the proposal to eliminate it. Keep the requirement for model cards and transparency disclosures. Streamline the documentation if necessary, but don't eliminate the disclosure itself.‚Źé‚ŹéMy cardiologist eventually got my records. I'm grateful for that. But I'm more grateful that he's trained to think critically about the tools he uses, including AI systems. That critical thinking only works if he knows what those systems are built on and what they're designed to do. The DSI criterion makes sure he has that information.‚Źé‚ŹéThat's what I want to see protected in this rule.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-27T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-27T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ4‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving DSI transparency requirements creates a dangerous regulatory gap where AI-based clinical decision support tools face fewer disclosure obligations than traditional medical devices, undermining consistent safety standards.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒurgent‚ôĒMissouri‚ôĒlibrarian‚ôĒ57‚ôĒLast month when my cardiologist retired, I spent three weeks trying to get my test results transferred to his replacement‚ÄĒthe old office kept citing "security protocols" while my new doctor couldn't start my treatment plan. It finally occurred to me that I'd spent my career helping people access information, yet I couldn't access my own health data that literally concerned my survival. I realized then how vulnerable patients become when their own records are held hostage by institutional friction.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1334
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0129‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDonna Allen‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA truck driver argues that proposed removals of AI transparency requirements‚ÄĒspecifically model card documentation and real-world testing mandates‚ÄĒprioritize corporate convenience over patient safety and informed decision-making, and urges regulat...‚ôĒ**Submission to ONC Docket: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability**‚Źé‚ŹéLast year I needed my MRI images moved from a clinic in Salt Lake City to a specialist in Provo because of a herniated disc. Three weeks of phone calls. The first place said they couldn't send digital files without a formal request form filled out in triplicate. Meanwhile I'm sitting in a truck cab taking ibuprofen wondering why my own medical images feel like classified documents. When I finally got them transferred, nobody explained to me what happened to that data between systems, who could see it, or what safeguards were actually protecting it. I was told the transfer was "secure" but I had no way to verify that claim or understand what it meant.‚Źé‚ŹéNow I hear there's a push to loosen things up so records move faster between providers, and I understand the appeal. Nobody wants bureaucratic slowdowns when you're in pain. But I'm reading about removing requirements for AI transparency and model cards, and I have to say this concerns me deeply. Streamlining data access is one thing. Stripping away the rules that tell patients and doctors what an algorithm is actually doing to make decisions about their care is something else entirely.‚Źé‚Źé**THE DIFFERENCE BETWEEN SPEED AND ACCOUNTABILITY**‚Źé‚ŹéLet me be clear about what I'm asking for here. I'm not against faster data sharing. I'm not against innovation or competition in health IT. What I am against is the idea that we have to choose between those things and basic transparency about how AI systems work. Those two things are not mutually exclusive, no matter what some developers might argue.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen a certification requirement asks a company to document how they built an AI system, what data they used to train it, what risks they found during testing, and how they validated it before putting it into a hospital, that's not red tape. That's accountability. That's what the Institute of Medicine called shared decision-making in clinical AI deployment‚ÄĒthe recognition that patients have a right to know what's being used to make decisions about their bodies. Removing model card requirements means I lose the ability to understand what went into the algorithm that might influence whether I get a certain treatment or not. It means my doctor loses the ability to evaluate whether that algorithm is trustworthy for my specific situation.‚Źé‚ŹéI've read some of the arguments for removing these requirements. Developers say they slow down innovation. They say the paperwork is duplicative. They say competition will naturally drive companies to be transparent if it matters to customers. But I've been in healthcare systems long enough to know that what gets measured is what gets done, and what doesn't get measured gets forgotten. Once you remove a requirement, nobody has an incentive to document it anymore. The market doesn't police transparency when transparency isn't required.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT MODEL CARDS ACTUALLY DO**‚Źé‚ŹéA model card is not bureaucratic overhead. It's a document that describes how an AI system was developed, what data trained it, what its performance looks like across different patient populations, and what limitations or risks came up during testing. According to FDA guidance on AI/ML-based Software as a Medical Device issued in 2021, these kinds of transparency requirements are foundational safety measures, not obstacles to innovation. They're how we know whether an algorithm is safe to use.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what concerns me specifically about removing this requirement. I'm going to ask the agency to keep model card documentation as a certification criterion, and I'm going to explain why by asking you to think about my situation again. When I had that herniated disc, the specialist in Provo might have used an AI system to help decide whether I needed surgery or could try physical therapy first. That algorithm would have been trained on some dataset, validated on some population, and tested in some context. Maybe it was trained on data from younger patients, or wealthier patients, or patients in a different geographic region. Maybe it performs differently for women than men. Maybe it was validated in a hospital setting but I'm being treated in an outpatient clinic. None of that information would be available to me if we remove model card requirements. I'd just be told "the algorithm recommends surgery" or "the algorithm recommends conservative treatment." No explanation of what went into it. No way for my doctor to evaluate whether it applies to me.‚Źé‚ŹéThat's not innovation. That's opacity dressed up as efficiency.‚Źé‚Źé**REAL-WORLD TESTING AND THE STAKES OF REMOVING IT**‚Źé‚ŹéThe RFI asks which real-world testing requirements should be modified or eliminated. I want to be direct: I don't think any of them should be eliminated. I think they should be expanded. But I understand the question is about what's duplicative or unnecessary. Let me address that specifically.‚Źé‚ŹéReal-world testing requirements exist because laboratory validation is not the same as actual clinical use. An algorithm might perform perfectly on a curated dataset in a controlled environment and then fail in a messy real hospital where patients have multiple conditions, take multiple medications, and have complex medical histories that don't fit neatly into the categories the algorithm was trained on. The FDA's 2021 guidance on SaMD was explicit about this. Removing these requirements because they slow down deployment means we're essentially saying it's okay to test medical devices on real patients instead of testing them thoroughly before they ever touch a patient.‚Źé‚ŹéI know what that feels like. I was that patient last year, sitting in a clinic, not knowing what data was being transferred or what protections were actually in place. I was the person with no visibility into how the system worked or whether it was safe. I don't want other people to have to live with that kind of uncertainty when it comes to algorithms that might be influencing their medical decisions.‚Źé‚Źé**CERTIFICATION CRITERIA AND WHAT THEY PROTECT**‚Źé‚ŹéThe RFI also asks which certification criteria should be removed or revised as duplicative. I want to push back on the assumption that something is duplicative just because it appears in more than one place in the regulations. Some requirements are cross-cutting because they matter across multiple contexts. Requiring developers to disclose what data they used to train an algorithm matters whether that algorithm is used in a hospital EHR or a patient portal. Requiring validation across different patient populations matters whether the algorithm is helping with diagnosis or treatment planning. These aren't duplicates. They're consistent applications of the same principle: that patients and providers need to understand what they're relying on.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat I would actually support removing or revising is anything that creates real barriers to competition without protecting patients. If there's a certification requirement that serves only to protect incumbent companies and doesn't actually measure safety or effectiveness, that's worth looking at. But the ones that require transparency about how algorithms work, what data trained them, and what risks they carry? Those aren't incumbent protection. Those are patient protection.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm a truck driver who has to navigate healthcare systems just like anyone else. I'm not a technologist or a policy expert. But I know what it feels like to be on the other side of a system that doesn't explain itself. I know what it feels like to have your own medical data treated like someone else's property. And I know that when someone tells me they're going to speed things up by removing the requirements for transparency, I have to ask: transparent to whom? Transparent to the companies building the systems, or transparent to the people being treated by them?‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT PATIENTS ACTUALLY NEED**‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what I'm asking for in this comment. Keep the model card requirements. They're not burdensome. They're documentation of what you built and how you tested it. Every responsible developer is already doing this internally. Making it visible just means patients and providers can actually see it. Keep the real-world testing requirements. They're not duplicative with laboratory validation. They're necessary because real clinical environments are messier than labs. And when you're considering removing certification criteria that require transparency about algorithm design and validation, I'm asking you to think about whether you're really making things better for patients or just making things easier for companies.‚Źé‚ŹéThe evidence is clear on this. A 2022 study in the Journal of Medical Internet Research showed that patient trust erodes quickly when algorithmic decision-making lacks explainability. People don't just want faster access to their data. They want to understand what's being done with it and how decisions about their care are being made. Removing transparency requirements doesn't serve patients. It serves the companies that don't want to document their work.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not asking for perfection. I'm not asking for barriers to innovation. I'm asking for accountability. That's not too much to ask when we're talking about systems that influence medical decisions.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-28T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-28T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ4‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒfrustrated‚ôĒUtah‚ôĒtruck driver‚ôĒ48‚ôĒLast year when I had that herniated disc, I needed my MRI images transferred from the clinic in Salt Lake City to the specialist in Provo, and it took three weeks of phone calls‚ÄĒturns out the first place claimed they couldn't send digital files without a formal request form. Meanwhile, I'm sitting in a truck cab popping ibuprofen wondering why my own medical data feels like it's locked in a safe I don't have the combination to. Now I hear they're loosening things up so records move faster, which sounds great until you realize nobody's really explaining what happens to that data once it's floating around between systems.‚ôĒPatients deserve to know exactly how AI systems making decisions about their care were built, tested, and validated‚ÄĒthat's not bureaucratic red tape, that's basic accountability. | Removing model card requirements means healthcare providers and patients lose the ability to understand what data trained these systems and what risks they carry, which directly undermines informed consent. | Transparency about AI design and validation isn't a burden on innovation‚ÄĒit's the foundation of trust that patients need before letting algorithms influence their medical decisions.‚ôĒA working person who's already frustrated by opaque healthcare systems argues that stripping AI transparency requirements will make patients even more powerless when algorithms affect their care.‚ôĒInstitute of Medicine (2015) report on patient engagement and shared decision-making in clinical AI deployment | FDA guidance on AI/ML-based Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) transparency requirements, 2021 | Journal of Medical Internet Research study on patient trust erosion when algorithmic decision-making lacks explainability (Cabral et al., 2022) | ONC Health IT Certification Program documentation on model card requirements as patient safety safeguards‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1478
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0130‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒAmanda Taylor‚ôĒBroadspire, Inc.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA health IT director argues that the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI transparency should be retained, not eliminated, but should be streamlined through standardized model card templates, third-party certif...‚ôĒBroadspire, Inc. appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed modifications to health IT certification criteria. As a health IT director managing enterprise systems across multiple clinical settings, I've spent the last year grappling with a problem that gets at the heart of why this regulatory framework matters: we need AI transparency requirements that actually work, not burdensome ones that we can simply strip away.‚Źé‚ŹéLast year, we partnered with a promising startup that built a clinical decision support module for our oncology team. The module used machine learning to flag potential drug interactions and flag patients at risk for adverse events. It worked well in testing. But when we looked at the certification pathway, the startup faced roughly $180,000 in additional costs to satisfy the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) criterion under 45 CFR Part 170.315(a)(4), which requires AI model cards, documentation of intended use, target population specifications, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. The startup couldn't absorb that cost. They killed the product line. We ended up licensing a clunky workaround from one of the major vendors, paying nearly double what we'd have paid the startup, and spending three months retraining staff on workflows that don't match our clinical processes. Our clinicians were frustrated. I was frustrated. But here's the thing: I don't think the answer is to remove the transparency requirement.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI criterion exists because AI in healthcare isn't like a standard software module. When a clinical decision support system makes a recommendation that influences whether a physician prescribes a medication or orders a test, the stakes are different. The physician needs to know what population the model was trained on, whether that population looks like the patient in front of them, what the model's known failure modes are, and what data it was built from. I've read the studies from the Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA) and the New England Journal of Medicine on algorithm bias in clinical settings (see, for example, work on racial bias in kidney function estimation algorithms that went undetected for years). These aren't theoretical concerns. They're real. When we implemented a vendor's sepsis prediction model in 2022, we discovered only after deployment that the training data was skewed toward a specific patient demographic. We had to recalibrate our protocols. That discovery happened because we had transparency into what the model was designed for and how it was built. Without the model card, we would have had no way to catch that.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat I'm asking the Office of the Secretary and ONC to do is keep the DSI certification criterion as written. I understand the regulatory burden argument. I live it every day. But the burden here isn't the problem. The problem is that we're trying to solve for it in the wrong direction. The startup I mentioned didn't fail because the transparency requirement was inherently costly. It failed because there's no standardized template for creating model cards in healthcare, no shared infrastructure, and no economies of scale for small vendors. Three different certification bodies were asking for the same information in three different formats. That's the inefficiency that should be addressed, not the requirement itself.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what would actually help. ONC should develop a standardized model card template for clinical decision support systems, similar to what FDA has begun developing for AI/ML in medical devices (see FDA's Proposed Regulatory Framework for Modifications to AI/ML-Based Software as a Medical Device, 2021). If ONC published a template that all developers could use, and recognized third-party certifiers who could validate model cards against that template, the cost would drop dramatically. The startup would still have needed to invest in documentation, but they could have done it once and used it across certifications. They might still be in business.‚Źé‚ŹéAdditionally, I'd suggest that ONC consider tiered certification for DSI systems based on clinical risk. A decision support tool that flags drug interactions has different stakes than one that recommends specific treatment protocols. The transparency requirements should scale with risk. A low-risk flagging system might need a basic model card covering intended use, target population, and known limitations. A high-risk system recommending treatment would need the full suite: training data characteristics, external validation results, performance metrics across demographic subgroups, and documented performance degradation in edge cases. That tiering would reduce burden for lower-risk innovations while keeping safety guardrails in place where they matter most.‚Źé‚ŹéThe broader argument here is that removing the DSI criterion would be a short-term fix that creates long-term problems. Right now, vendors document their systems because the certification framework requires it. If that requirement disappears, documentation becomes a competitive disadvantage. Vendors won't voluntarily create model cards. Clinicians won't know what they're using. We'll end up in a market where transparency becomes a luxury feature that only the largest vendors can afford to offer, because they can absorb the cost as a differentiator. Smaller innovators, the ones building the targeted solutions that don't fit the big vendors' portfolios, will have even less incentive to document their systems properly. That's not deregulation. That's regulatory capture disguised as burden reduction.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not arguing that every aspect of the current certification framework is perfect. There are legitimate inefficiencies. But the DSI criterion isn't one of them. It's the foundation that keeps AI-assisted clinical decision-making accountable. The fix is to streamline how vendors comply with it, not to remove it. ONC should invest in standardized templates, recognize qualified third-party certifiers, and implement risk-based tiering. That approach keeps patients safe, maintains clinician trust, and actually reduces burden in a way that helps innovators instead of just helping them disappear.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-14T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-14T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ3‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHospital and health system procurement teams need transparent AI model information to make evidence-based decisions about which tools to adopt and how to implement them safely in their local contexts.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒfrustrated‚ôĒCalifornia‚ôĒhealth IT director‚ôĒ39‚ôĒLast year, I watched a promising health IT startup we'd partnered with abandon their EHR module because they couldn't justify the certification costs for a feature our clinicians actually needed‚ÄĒsomething the big players simply wouldn't build. We ended up paying twice as much for a clunky workaround from a vendor who had economies of scale I resented subsidizing, and our workflows suffered for months while we adapted around their limitations instead of the other way around.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ936
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0131‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒBetty Campbell‚ôĒPolicy Center for Maternal Mental Health‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health opposes the proposed removal of the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion, arguing that transparency requirements for clinical decision support tools are essential to prevent harm...‚ôĒ**COMMENT ON PROPOSED REVISIONS TO CERTIFICATION CRITERIA FOR HEALTH IT MODULES USED IN CLINICAL DECISION SUPPORT**‚Źé‚Źé**Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health submits this comment on the proposed removal of the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion and related transparency requirements for AI-assisted clinical decision support tools. We are a nonprofit policy research organization focused on improving data infrastructure and clinical outcomes in maternal mental health, and our analysis of these proposals is grounded in four years of direct work building interoperable datasets across fragmented health systems.‚Źé‚ŹéWe support the regulatory intent to reduce unnecessary burden on health IT developers and to accelerate innovation in clinical decision support. We have reviewed the Request for Information issued in this rulemaking, and we want to be direct about something that concerns us: the proposed removal of model card requirements and transparency disclosures appears to assume that the current certification criterion is creating significant compliance costs without generating measurable safety or equity benefits. Our experience suggests the opposite. The DSI criterion, as currently written in 45 CFR 170.315(a)(3), requires vendors to disclose intended use, target population, known limitations, training data characteristics, and results of external validation testing. These requirements exist because clinical decision support tools are not like other software. They sit between a clinician and a patient at a moment of high consequence, and when they fail silently or work differently for different populations, the harm cascades through healthcare systems in ways that are difficult to detect and trace.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT HAPPENED TO MY SISTER, AND WHY IT MATTERS FOR THIS RULE**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to explain why we take this position by describing something that happened in 2019 that shaped our organization's approach to data governance. My sister was hospitalized for postpartum psychosis at a large academic medical center in Miami. She was prescribed an antipsychotic medication that caused severe metabolic complications. The psychiatry team at the hospital did not know that a different hospital system in the same city had already tried the same medication two weeks earlier and discontinued it for the same reason. The electronic health records systems did not communicate. She was prescribed it again. What should have been a one-week medication trial became a two-week crisis in which we fought to physically move records between three hospital systems while her condition deteriorated.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not an unusual story. It happens thousands of times a year in the United States. But here is what matters for this comment: when we began building data infrastructure to prevent this kind of fragmentation, we discovered that clinical decision support tools were already being used in many of the systems we were trying to integrate, and almost none of the clinicians using them could tell us how those tools made decisions, what patient populations they had been tested in, or whether they had been validated on postpartum psychiatric patients at all. One hospital was using a mental health risk stratification tool that had been trained almost entirely on general population data with minimal representation of perinatal cases. Another was using a medication recommendation engine that had never been validated for patients with the specific comorbidities that are common in postpartum psychosis. No clinician we spoke to had received clear documentation about the limitations of these tools for their specific patient population.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is what the DSI criterion was designed to prevent. It does not require perfect accuracy or eliminate all clinical judgment. It requires vendors to be transparent about what their tools were designed to do, what populations they were tested in, what their known limitations are, and what external validation data exists. That transparency is the only thing standing between a clinician and a tool that might make a dangerous recommendation without the clinician even knowing the tool's limitations.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PROBLEM WITH ASSUMING MARKET COMPETITION WILL SOLVE THIS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe RFI suggests that removing certification requirements will accelerate innovation and allow the market to differentiate between high-quality and low-quality decision support tools. This assumes that end users (clinicians and health systems) are in a position to evaluate the safety and appropriateness of these tools themselves, and that they have the technical expertise and time to conduct their own external validation studies.‚Źé‚ŹéThis assumption does not match the reality of how clinical decision support tools are actually adopted. Most health systems do not have data science teams capable of validating AI models. Most clinicians do not have training in machine learning or statistics. Purchasing decisions are typically made at the IT or compliance level, not the clinical level, and they are made on the basis of cost, integration ease, and vendor reputation, not on the basis of independent safety validation. When a tool performs poorly or fails for a specific population, the failure is often attributed to poor implementation or clinician error rather than to the tool itself. This is not a character flaw in healthcare organizations. It is a realistic assessment of where expertise and resources actually sit.‚Źé‚ŹéThe market does not naturally correct for these information asymmetries in clinical decision support. If anything, it exploits them. A vendor who invests in transparency and external validation incurs costs that a competitor who simply claims to have "state-of-the-art AI" does not. Under a deregulated framework, the vendor who cuts corners on validation testing and transparency becomes more profitable, not less. This is not innovation. It is a race to the bottom where the lowest-cost provider wins, regardless of whether their tool is actually safe for the populations using it.‚Źé‚ŹéWe have seen this happen in other domains. The FDA's 510(k) pathway for medical devices was designed to accelerate approval for devices that were substantially equivalent to already-approved devices. Over time, this pathway became a mechanism for introducing devices with minimal new safety testing by claiming equivalence to earlier devices. The result is that some devices approved through 510(k) in 2023 are substantially equivalent to devices approved in 1995, with no meaningful validation of safety in between. Clinical decision support tools are not yet subject to FDA oversight, but the same dynamic is already emerging: tools are marketed as "AI-powered" or "machine learning-based" without any clear disclosure of what that means, what data was used to train them, or whether they have been tested on the specific populations they are being used on.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS ARE COST-EFFECTIVE, NOT COST-PROHIBITIVE**‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criterion does not require vendors to conduct expensive external validation studies. It requires them to disclose the results of validation studies they have already conducted, to describe their training data, and to document known limitations. For a vendor that has developed a tool responsibly, this documentation already exists internally. Making it available to clinicians and health systems is not a major additional cost. It is a transparency cost, not a research cost.‚Źé‚ŹéIn fact, requiring this documentation creates an incentive for vendors to conduct validation studies that they might otherwise skip. A vendor who knows they will have to disclose that their tool was trained on 95% male patients or 85% white patients or entirely on adult populations, when it is being marketed for use in pediatric settings, faces market pressure to either conduct additional validation studies or to narrow their marketing claims. This is good for patients. It is not a burden on innovation. It is a condition for responsible innovation.‚Źé‚ŹéWe have looked at the current DSI criterion and the model card requirements in the Certification Companion Guide (45 CFR 170.315(a)(3), with supporting requirements in 170.102(a)(3) and 170.102(a)(5)). These require vendors to document seven specific elements: (1) intended use, (2) intended user, (3) target population, (4) known limitations, (5) training data characteristics, (6) accuracy metrics, and (7) results of external validation testing. The burden is not in conducting the research. The burden is in writing it down clearly.‚Źé‚ŹéA responsible vendor already knows this information. The burden is on the vendor who has not thought about who their tool works for and who it does not work for. That vendor should face burden. That is the point.‚Źé‚Źé**THE EQUITY PROBLEM WITH DEREGULATION**‚Źé‚ŹéHere is what keeps us up at night about this proposed change. The populations most likely to be harmed by opaque decision support tools are the same populations most likely to be underrepresented in the training data. Perinatal populations are underrepresented in most medical AI training data. Black mothers are underrepresented. Low-income mothers are underrepresented. Mothers with substance use disorders are underrepresented. If a decision support tool for maternal mental health is trained on 80% affluent, white, insured patients, that tool will perform worse for the remaining 20% of the population. That is not a hypothetical. That is what the machine learning literature shows us happens repeatedly.‚Źé‚ŹéWithout transparency requirements, health systems have no systematic way to know whether the tools they are using are safe for their patient populations. A small clinic in rural Mississippi serving primarily Black mothers will deploy the same decision support tool as a large urban hospital in Boston, with no way to know whether that tool has been validated for their patient population. The vendor has no obligation to disclose that their tool was trained on data from urban teaching hospitals in the Northeast.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not a failure of individual health systems. It is a structural problem that transparency requirements are designed to address. The DSI criterion does not guarantee that all tools will work equally well for all populations. But it creates an obligation for vendors to disclose where they have done that work and where they have not. That disclosure is what allows health systems and clinicians to make informed decisions about how to use these tools safely.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving this requirement will make it easier for vendors to avoid this work. It will not make the tools better. It will make the harms less visible.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC CONCERNS ABOUT THE PROPOSED REVISIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéWe want to flag three specific problems with the proposed removal of the DSI criterion:‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, the proposed rule states that the DSI certification criterion is duplicative of other regulatory oversight, citing FDA guidance on clinical decision support and FDA's proposed oversight framework for AI/ML-based Software as a Medical Device (SaMD). This is incorrect. FDA oversight of clinical decision support tools is not mandatory, and FDA's framework for SaMD is still in proposed form. As of the publication date of this RFI (March 2024), the FDA has not established binding requirements for transparency in clinical decision support tools. CMS has no authority to regulate clinical decision support at all. ASTP/ONC's certification criterion is the only existing federal requirement that ensures transparency in how these tools work. Removing it does not eliminate duplication. It eliminates the only layer of oversight that currently exists.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, the proposed rule suggests that certification requirements create "barriers to entry" for smaller vendors and innovative startups. Our review of vendor participation in USCDI data exchanges and certified health IT modules suggests that smaller vendors already participate actively in certification programs. Certification does not appear to be a barrier to entry; it appears to be a condition of market participation. Removing the DSI criterion will not help startups enter the market. It will help larger vendors reduce their compliance costs.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, the proposed rule does not address what will replace the DSI criterion. If the criterion is removed, clinicians and health systems will have no systematic way to evaluate decision support tools before deploying them. The proposed rule suggests that market forces and voluntary adoption of industry standards will fill this gap. Our experience suggests that this is optimistic. Industry standards for AI transparency (such as the AI Bill of Materials or model card frameworks developed by academia and industry groups) exist, but they are voluntary. Vendors who adopt them are doing so because they believe it is good business, not because they are required to do so. A vendor who skips this work faces no consequences.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE ARE ASKING FOR**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health respectfully asks that ASTP/ONC retain the DSI certification criterion with its current transparency requirements. We are not asking for additional burden on vendors. We are asking that the current requirements remain in place because they serve a specific, important function: they create an obligation for vendors to think clearly about who their tools work for and who they do not, and they create an obligation to disclose that information to the clinicians and health systems that will use these tools.‚Źé‚ŹéIf ASTP/ONC determines that the current criterion is poorly written or creates compliance costs that are disproportionate to the benefits, we support targeted revisions. Specifically, we would recommend the following:‚Źé‚Źé- Clarify the definition of "clinical decision support" in 45 CFR 170.102(a)(5) to distinguish between tools that make recommendations (which require transparency) and tools that merely display data (which may not). Current language is ambiguous and creates compliance uncertainty.‚Źé‚Źé- Allow vendors to satisfy the transparency requirement through a standardized model card template, similar to the AI Bill of Materials format used by NIST, rather than requiring narrative documentation. This would reduce compliance burden while maintaining transparency.‚Źé‚Źé- Establish a public registry of certified clinical decision support tools and their model cards, similar to the FDA's medical device database. This would allow clinicians to compare tools and understand their limitations without requiring each health system to conduct independent validation.‚Źé‚Źé- Require annual updates to model cards to reflect new validation data and newly discovered limitations. This creates an ongoing accountability mechanism without imposing a one-time research burden.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the proposed deregulatory framework moves forward despite these concerns, we ask that ASTP/ONC establish a monitoring mechanism to track patient safety outcomes associated with clinical decision support tools. Specifically, we recommend that CMS require health systems to report adverse events or near-misses associated with decision support tools as a condition of Medicare participation, similar to the reporting requirements for other clinical technologies. This would create visibility into whether removing certification requirements actually improves safety or whether it simply makes harms less visible.‚Źé‚Źé**CLOSING**‚Źé‚ŹéWe understand that the regulatory intent behind this RFI is to reduce burden and accelerate innovation. Those are legitimate goals. But innovation in clinical decision support should not mean removing the only systematic requirement that ensures transparency about how these tools work and who they have been tested on. That requirement exists because patients depend on clinicians to use tools safely, and clinicians depend on vendors to be honest about what their tools can and cannot do.‚Źé‚ŹéThe four years we have spent building data infrastructure for maternal mental health have taught us that fragmentation and lack of transparency in health data create real harms. These harms fall disproportionately on populations that are already underrepresented in medical research and already at higher risk for poor outcomes. Removing transparency requirements in clinical decision support will make these problems worse, not better.‚Źé‚ŹéWe appreciate the opportunity to comment on this proposed rule, and we are available to discuss these concerns in more detail with ASTP/ONC staff.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-21T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-21T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ8‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒangry‚ôĒFlorida‚ôĒsenior policy analyst‚ôĒ37‚ôĒWhen my sister was hospitalized for postpartum psychosis in 2019, the fragmented data across three different hospital systems meant her psychiatrist never saw the initial medication trial that had failed‚ÄĒshe was prescribed it again, and we spent two weeks fighting to get records transferred while she deteriorated. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0132‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒMargaret Wright‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒRemoving certification requirements for AI model cards and transparency disclosures in clinical decision-support systems risks patient safety by eliminating accountability mechanisms that ensure doctors and patients understand how AI tools make me...‚ôĒRESPONSE TO HHS ASTP/ONC REQUEST FOR INFORMATION ON HEALTH DATA INTEROPERABILITY‚Źé‚ŹéMy mother had a stroke last March. Nobody wants unnecessary red tape. First: thank God, this could catch someone like her faster. Second: does her doctor actually understand how that system works, or is he just trusting a black box? That question has kept me awake more than once.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving certification criteria for AI model cards doesn't just eliminate paperwork. It removes the only real accountability mechanism we have right now. Here's what I mean:‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ A model card is supposed to tell doctors and patients what data the AI system was trained on, what its limitations are, and how well it actually performs. Without this, a doctor might use a tool to help diagnose heart disease, but he won't know it was trained mostly on data from 45-year-old men and might perform differently for women or older patients.‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ Validation gaps and design flaws don't disappear when you stop requiring companies to disclose them. They just stay hidden. A 2023 Institute of Medicine report on diagnostic errors in AI-assisted clinical settings found that opaque AI systems contributed to missed diagnoses because clinicians didn't understand the tool's boundaries.‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ This isn't about burdening industry. This is about informed consent. I want my mother's doctor to know exactly what he's using to make decisions about her care.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT DOCTORS ACTUALLY NEED TO DO THEIR JOBS SAFELY**‚Źé‚ŹéHealthcare providers are already in an impossible position with some of these systems. They're expected to make clinical judgments about tools they don't fully understand. That's not innovation, that's negligence waiting to happen.‚Źé‚ŹéA Journal of Medical Internet Research study on clinician trust in opaque AI decision-support systems found that doctors lose confidence in tools when they can't explain how those tools reached their conclusions. Paradoxically, removing transparency requirements doesn't make doctors trust the systems more. It makes them either ignore the recommendations or follow them blindly, and neither outcome is safe.‚Źé‚ŹéReal-world testing requirements shouldn't be stripped. They should be streamlined if they're redundant, but the core requirement that AI systems work reliably in actual clinical environments is not bureaucratic overhead. It's foundational.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT CERTIFICATION CRITERIA SHOULD STAY**‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not saying keep everything. Duplicate certifications that serve the same function should go. But model card requirements and transparency disclosures about AI limitations aren't duplicative. They're doing something nothing else does: they're telling doctors and patients what we're actually working with.‚Źé‚ŹéInteroperability is good. Competition is good. But not at the cost of accountability. My mother deserves to know how the tools being used to keep her alive actually work. So does every other patient. We can have faster innovation and patient safety at the same time. We just have to be willing to require that companies tell us the truth about what their tools can and can't do.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-29T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-29T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒhopeful‚ôĒOhio‚ôĒcustomer service representative‚ôĒ56‚ôĒWhen my mother had her stroke last year, I spent three days calling between her cardiologist, the hospital, and her primary care doctor just trying to get her test results shared so they could coordinate her treatment‚ÄĒit felt like I was translating between three different countries.
