CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0134‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒBarbara Robinson‚ôĒHEP, LLC‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA primary care network leader argues that while healthcare IT regulatory burden should be reduced, removing the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion would be a dangerous mistake, as algorithm transparency requirements are e...‚ôĒHEP, LLC represents a network of primary care and specialty clinics across Alaska, serving roughly 85,000 patients across 14 locations. Over the past decade, I've watched our organization navigate three separate electronic health record implementations, each one consuming weeks of staff retraining and millions in direct costs. The current proposed changes to certification criteria raise genuine concerns about patient safety that I need to address directly, particularly regarding the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) criterion.‚Źé‚ŹéThe question isn't whether we should reduce regulatory burden. It is. I'm genuinely frustrated by certification processes that don't meaningfully improve care but do lock us into vendor relationships and make switching systems prohibitively expensive. When our board began exploring the proposed deregulatory framework last month, I initially thought some of these changes might finally give us real flexibility in selecting systems based on clinical workflow fit rather than which vendors have invested in compliance infrastructure. But the DSI criterion is different, and removing it would be a mistake we'd regret within months of implementation.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what I keep coming back to: AI-assisted clinical decision support is not like other health IT features. It's not infrastructure, and it's not interoperability plumbing. It's a tool that sits between patient data and clinical judgment, and when it fails silently or systematically, the consequences aren't a workflow delay or a data synchronization error. They're patient harm. The current DSI certification requirement under 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1) mandates that developers provide model cards documenting intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. This isn't bureaucratic overhead. This is the bare minimum transparency needed for clinicians to understand what they're actually using.‚Źé‚ŹéAt HEP, we recently implemented a sepsis prediction algorithm integrated into our EHR. Before deployment, we demanded the vendor provide exactly the documentation that the current DSI criterion requires. What we found changed how we used the tool. The model had been trained predominantly on data from urban academic medical centers and performed less reliably in our patient population, which includes older adults and patients with higher rates of chronic kidney disease. The vendor's external validation data showed a 7 percent false negative rate in the training population but hadn't tested performance in our demographic. We didn't ban the tool. We implemented it with specific safeguards: alerts that flag cases where the patient characteristics diverged from the training cohort, mandatory review protocols for low-confidence predictions, and staff training that emphasized the algorithm's limitations. Without that documentation, we would have deployed it broadly, and we would have missed cases. I'm confident of that.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed deregulation would allow vendors to market decision support tools without providing this basic transparency. The argument is that market competition and customer demand will drive quality. I understand that logic, but it doesn't work in healthcare. Hospitals and large health systems have procurement teams and IT governance, but most primary care practices don't. Smaller providers like our rural clinics lack the resources to demand independent algorithm audits or conduct their own validation studies. They'll deploy whatever their EHR vendor includes, trust that it works, and hope nothing goes wrong. The regulatory requirement doesn't prevent innovation. It prevents bad actors from hiding poor performance behind vendor proprietary claims. Removing it shifts all the risk onto clinicians and patients who have no way to evaluate the tool's actual performance in their setting.‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be clear about what I'm not saying. I'm not arguing for more certification burden generally. If ONC wants to streamline the process for reviewing model cards, standardize the format of vendor documentation, or reduce redundant testing requirements, those are reasonable efficiency improvements. The American Medical Association's policy on clinical decision support systems, adopted in 2017, calls for exactly this kind of transparency without requiring clinicians to become machine learning experts. What I'm saying is that removing the DSI criterion entirely treats algorithm transparency as optional, and that's wrong.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider what happens in our clinic network with a respiratory illness prediction tool that flags patients for early intervention. The algorithm was trained on insured populations in temperate climates and tested in those same populations. In Alaska, we have different seasonal patterns, different comorbidity distributions, and different healthcare access patterns. A model card would tell us this immediately. We could adjust our protocols, validate performance locally before full rollout, or decide the tool isn't appropriate for our setting. Without it, we're flying blind. The 21st Century Cures Act emphasized the importance of information blocking prevention and interoperability, but it also embedded requirements for health IT certification (42 U.S.C. 300jj-52) specifically to ensure that technology used in clinical care meets baseline safety standards. DSI certification is part of that mandate, not a bug in the system.‚Źé‚ŹéThe vendors arguing for deregulation will say that documentation requirements slow innovation. In our experience, they don't. The companies we work with that provide robust algorithm documentation typically innovate faster, not slower, because they understand their tools' limitations and can improve them systematically. The vendors that resist transparency are the ones trying to hide performance issues or cut corners on validation. Those aren't the competitors we want to encourage.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what I'd recommend: retain the DSI certification criterion requiring model cards, but streamline the approval process. Establish a standard template for algorithm documentation so vendors aren't submitting 50-page narratives. Allow third-party validation instead of requiring everything to come through ONC. Set a 60-day review window for straightforward submissions. These changes would reduce burden while keeping patient safety intact. We've learned from experience that the margin between good decision support and dangerous decision support is often narrow, and that margin is where transparency lives.‚Źé‚ŹéHEP, LLC's experience across multiple EHR implementations and multiple clinical decision support tools shows that vendor lock-in and regulatory burden are real problems that deserve real solutions. But the solution isn't to eliminate transparency requirements for algorithms that influence patient care. It's to make those requirements more efficient while keeping them in place.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-23T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-23T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ3‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒhopeful‚ôĒAlaska‚ôĒchief medical officer‚ôĒ40‚ôĒWhen we implemented our third EHR system in eight years at our clinic network, I watched talented staff spend weeks relearning workflows instead of seeing patients, and our smaller competitors simply couldn't absorb those transition costs‚ÄĒI found myself genuinely excited when our board discussed how streamlined certification requirements might finally let us choose systems based on clinical fit rather than vendor lock-in and regulatory burden.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ991
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0135‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒLinda Moore‚ôĒAgilian Government Solutions‚ôĒ‚ôĒGovernment Agency‚ôĒ‚ôĒAgilian Government Solutions urges the Department to retain the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures, arguing that removing transparency standards for clinical decision-...‚ôĒAgilian Government Solutions submits this comment in response to the Proposed Rule on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions (88 FR 80752, November 20, 2023). As a state government technology solutions firm working directly with health departments and state Medicaid agencies across the upper Midwest, AGS has spent the last three years managing the practical consequences of fragmented health IT standards and inconsistent data reporting requirements. We write to urge the Department to retain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures, and to reconsider the broader deregulatory approach outlined in this proposal.‚Źé‚Źé**The Real Cost of Deregulation in Public Health Infrastructure**‚Źé‚ŹéMinnesota's experience with hepatitis C case tracking illustrates why removing AI transparency standards will create genuine harm, not just administrative inconvenience. Last spring, when our state's disease surveillance office attempted to reconcile hepatitis C reporting with CMS requirements, we discovered three separate state systems pulling case data independently, with no shared definitions for "confirmed case," "probable case," or "follow-up required." None of these systems could communicate with each other. Federal timelines demanded we fix it by summer. The outbreak cluster in Hennepin County two years prior had nearly escaped detection because one system's data was two weeks behind another's, and nobody knew it until after the fact.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not a hypothetical problem. This is what happens when technical standards fragment and each agency or vendor optimizes for their own immediate needs rather than system-wide reliability. When we finally unified that reporting infrastructure last August, we did it by implementing the very standards this rule proposes to weaken: clear data definitions, transparent algorithm specifications, and validated handoff procedures between systems. The model card requirement for AI-assisted clinical decision support exists for the same reason. It forces vendors to document what their algorithms actually do, what populations they were tested on, and what failure modes they have. Without that documentation, state health departments have no way to know whether a clinical decision support tool trained primarily on urban, insured populations will work reliably in rural Minnesota or among our Medicaid population with higher rates of comorbidity.‚Źé‚Źé**Why Decision Support Interventions Are Different From Other Health IT**‚Źé‚ŹéThe rule proposes removing the AI model card and transparency disclosure requirement from the DSI certification criterion. The stated rationale is to reduce burden on developers. But the DSI criterion is fundamentally different from many other IT standards because it involves algorithms making recommendations about patient care. A broken data exchange standard is a headache. A broken clinical decision algorithm can change treatment decisions.‚Źé‚ŹéAgilian works with state health departments that deploy these tools daily. A hepatitis C treatment recommendation system guides whether a patient gets offered direct-acting antivirals. An opioid risk assessment tool influences prescribing decisions. A sepsis alert algorithm determines how quickly a nurse responds to a patient's lab values. These are not administrative conveniences. They are clinical tools that affect whether patients receive appropriate care.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI criterion requires disclosure of:‚Źé- Intended use and clinical context‚Źé- Target population and characteristics of the training data‚Źé- Known risks, limitations, and failure modes‚Źé- Results from external validation studies‚Źé- Model architecture and input variables‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not excessive. This is the minimum information a hospital's medical director should know before deciding whether to implement a tool in their emergency department. This is what state Medicaid agencies need to understand before approving a tool for coverage decisions. And this is what regulators need to enforce provider safety and hold vendors accountable when things go wrong.‚Źé‚Źé**The Accountability Gap That Transparency Disclosures Prevent**‚Źé‚ŹéWhen Minnesota's disease surveillance office discovered the Hennepin County outbreak cluster was nearly missed, we asked the vendors whose systems had failed to explain themselves. Their response was instructive: "We built this system for a different use case. Nobody told us it would be used for statewide outbreak detection." That became a teachable moment, but it should not have required an outbreak to surface. If the vendor had been required to document intended use, target population, and external validation, the mismatch would have been obvious before deployment.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI criterion creates accountability. If a vendor makes claims about an algorithm's performance, the model card makes those claims verifiable. If external validation studies show an algorithm performs worse in certain populations, that limitation becomes part of the public record. If an algorithm fails in ways nobody anticipated, the model card provides a baseline to determine whether the failure was foreseeable or truly novel.‚Źé‚ŹéRemove that requirement, and vendors have no incentive to conduct external validation. They can make vague claims about "improvement in clinical outcomes" and deploy tools without knowing whether those claims hold up in the real world. State health departments and hospital systems end up bearing the entire risk of discovering problems after deployment. This is not deregulation in service of innovation. This is regulatory abandonment that shifts risk from vendors (who understand their products) to end-users (who do not).‚Źé‚Źé**Practical Implementation Challenges in State Health Systems**‚Źé‚ŹéAgilian's work across state Medicaid agencies and public health departments reveals a consistent pattern: when IT standards weaken, the burden does not disappear. It shifts. When vendors are not required to document their algorithms, end-users must invest their own resources to understand what they have purchased. This is not a reduction in overall burden. It is a transfer of burden from vendors (who have technical expertise) to government agencies (who do not).‚Źé‚ŹéConsider a concrete example. A Medicaid agency in a neighboring state is evaluating a prior authorization decision support tool. Under the current DSI requirement, the vendor provides a model card showing that the tool was trained on claims data from a large urban health system and validated on a separate dataset. The model card notes that performance is lower for patients over 65 with multiple comorbidities. The Medicaid agency can read this, understand the limitations, and decide whether to implement the tool or request modifications. The vendor bears the cost of generating this transparency.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the DSI requirement is removed, the vendor has no obligation to provide this information. The Medicaid agency must now conduct its own validation studies, hire data scientists to reverse-engineer the algorithm's decision rules, or accept the tool without understanding its limitations. This is not burden reduction. This is cost externalization. The vendor saves the cost of transparency. The government agency absorbs the cost of uncertainty.‚Źé‚Źé**The Specific Problem With Removing Model Card Requirements**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal frames the DSI model card requirement as a "certification criterion" that adds unnecessary compliance burden. But certification is not the same as documentation. A certification criterion establishes a standard that health IT must meet. A model card is the evidence that the standard has been met. Removing the requirement does not make the problem go away. It makes the problem invisible.‚Źé‚ŹéState agencies cannot manage what they cannot see. When Minnesota consolidated disease surveillance reporting, we did not remove data standards because they created administrative burden. We implemented more rigorous standards because the burden of not having them (missing outbreak clusters) was unacceptable. The same logic applies to clinical decision support algorithms.‚Źé‚ŹéThe model card requirement is proportionate to the risk. An algorithm that recommends whether to approve a medication has high clinical consequence. Requiring transparency about how that algorithm works, what data it was trained on, and what populations it has been validated for is reasonable. An IT vendor that cannot document these basics should not be trusted with clinical decision-making responsibility. If a vendor believes documentation creates unreasonable burden, that vendor should not be in the clinical decision support market.‚Źé‚Źé**What Transparency Actually Enables**‚Źé‚ŹéAgilian has worked with hospital systems implementing sepsis alert algorithms, and the model card requirement has made that implementation dramatically more effective. When the hospital's chief medical officer could read the model card and see that the algorithm was developed and validated in ICU populations, she could make an informed decision about whether to deploy it in the emergency department, which has different patient acuity and different treatment protocols. When the model card disclosed that the algorithm had higher false-positive rates in patients on dialysis, the hospital could implement a workflow rule to flag those alerts for manual review rather than trusting the algorithm blindly.‚Źé‚ŹéThe hospital did not experience this as a burden. They experienced it as essential information. The vendor did not experience this as crushing compliance cost. The vendor already knew how their algorithm worked. Documenting it in a structured format took technical effort, but not unreasonable effort.‚Źé‚ŹéThe transparency requirement also enables accountability for patient safety. When a hospital implements an algorithm and sees that it is recommending treatment changes that do not align with clinical evidence, the medical director can reference the model card to determine whether this is a limitation of the algorithm or a misuse of the tool. When a patient is harmed by a recommendation from a clinical decision support tool, the model card becomes part of the safety investigation. It allows regulators to determine whether the vendor's claims about the algorithm's performance were accurate, whether the algorithm was being used as intended, and whether the harm was foreseeable or preventable.‚Źé‚ŹéWithout the model card requirement, these investigations become impossible. A vendor can claim an algorithm performs well without evidence. An end-user can deploy an algorithm without understanding its limitations. When something goes wrong, there is no baseline to determine responsibility. This is not a recipe for innovation. This is a recipe for preventable harm.‚Źé‚Źé**Alignment With Existing Patient Safety Standards**‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI model card requirement aligns with patient safety standards already embedded in other areas of healthcare regulation. FDA guidance on clinical decision support software (issued in 2019) recommends transparency about algorithm development, validation, and known limitations. The AHRQ standards for implementing clinical decision support in primary care include similar transparency requirements. The VA's requirements for internal algorithm development include mandatory documentation of training data, validation results, and known failure modes.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criterion is not an outlier. It is consistent with how healthcare regulators approach algorithms that affect patient care. Removing this requirement would make health IT regulation less stringent than FDA guidance on clinical decision support, less stringent than AHRQ best practices, and less stringent than the VA's internal standards. This is not harmonization with the regulatory environment. This is deharmonization.‚Źé‚Źé**State Government Capacity and the Real Burden of Deregulation**‚Źé‚ŹéThe broader deregulatory proposal assumes that state health departments and Medicaid agencies have the technical capacity to conduct independent validation of health IT systems. This assumption is inconsistent with the reality of state government staffing and budgets. Minnesota's health department has one data scientist dedicated to health IT oversight. One. Our Medicaid agency has two staff members responsible for IT vendor evaluation across 1.2 million covered lives.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen federal standards weaken, state agencies cannot simply hire expertise to fill the gap. State budget cycles are rigid. If the federal government deregulates in January, the state cannot reallocate budget until July. If a vendor deploys an algorithm in March that turns out to have serious limitations, the state's two IT evaluators cannot conduct validation studies quickly enough to prevent harm. The state must take the algorithm offline, deal with provider complaints, and eventually hire consultants at premium rates to figure out what went wrong.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not burden reduction. This is risk transfer with no corresponding resource transfer. The federal government reduces vendor burden. The state absorbs the consequences. Patients bear the actual risk if an algorithm fails.‚Źé‚Źé**THE SPECIFIC CASE FOR RETAINING DSI TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéAgilian urges the Department to retain the current DSI certification criterion requiring AI model cards and the following transparency disclosures:‚Źé‚Źé- Intended use, clinical context, and target population for which the algorithm was designed and validated‚Źé- Characteristics of the training data, including population demographics, clinical setting, and data collection period‚Źé- Known limitations, failure modes, and populations where performance has not been validated‚Źé- Results from external validation studies, including performance metrics across different patient populations‚Źé- Model architecture, key input variables, and decision logic (at a level of specificity sufficient for clinical and regulatory review)‚Źé- Recommendations for appropriate implementation, monitoring, and human oversight‚Źé‚ŹéThese requirements are not excessive. They are the minimum information necessary for informed adoption of a clinical tool. They are consistent with FDA guidance, AHRQ best practices, and existing VA standards. They enable accountability for patient safety. And they shift burden from end-users (who lack technical capacity) to vendors (who have technical expertise and financial incentive to develop good algorithms).‚Źé‚ŹéThe deregulatory proposal assumes that removing standards will accelerate innovation. But innovation without accountability is recklessness. Minnesota's experience with disease surveillance fragmentation demonstrates that deregulation without standards does not produce better outcomes. It produces fragmentation, duplication, and preventable failures.‚Źé‚Źé**Broader Concerns About the Deregulatory Approach**‚Źé‚ŹéBeyond the specific DSI issue, Agilian is concerned about the overall direction of this proposal. The rule proposes removing multiple certification criteria and standards with the stated goal of reducing burden on health IT vendors. But health IT vendors are not the primary stakeholders in healthcare. Patients are. Clinicians are. State health departments and Medicaid agencies responsible for ensuring safe, effective care are.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen you remove standards without replacing them with something better, you do not reduce burden on those stakeholders. You increase uncertainty. Hospitals and health departments must decide whether to implement tools that have no transparent documentation of their capabilities. Clinicians must use algorithms without knowing their limitations. Patients receive care recommendations that may or may not be appropriate for their specific circumstances.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal assumes that market competition will ensure quality. But health IT markets are not perfectly competitive. A large vendor with an established product can survive in the market even if their algorithm has known limitations and undisclosed training data biases. A smaller vendor with a better algorithm cannot enter the market if they cannot afford to conduct external validation studies and generate model cards. So deregulation does not necessarily produce better algorithms. It produces a market dominated by vendors with sufficient scale to survive even when their products are opaque.‚Źé‚Źé**Precedent From the EHR Certification Program**‚Źé‚ŹéThe health IT certification program has a track record. The meaningful use rule and the subsequent ONC Health IT Certification Program created standards that, despite significant compliance burden, have produced measurable benefits. Hospitals using certified EHRs can exchange data more reliably. Providers can implement clinical decision support with confidence that tools meet baseline safety standards. State health departments can deploy health IT infrastructure knowing that vendors have met established criteria.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving standards from the certification program is not analogous to removing certification requirements altogether. It is analogous to removing safety standards from the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA). You can argue that seatbelt requirements add cost to vehicle manufacturing. You can argue that airbag testing is burdensome. But the reason we have these standards is that we learned through preventable deaths what happens when manufacturers are not required to meet safety baselines.‚Źé‚ŹéHealth IT is not as immediately consequential as vehicle safety, but the logic is the same. Clinical decision support algorithms influence treatment decisions. When those algorithms have known biases, undisclosed limitations, or unvalidated performance claims, patients are harmed. Transparency requirements exist because we learned through preventable failures what happens when vendors are not required to document their products' capabilities.‚Źé‚Źé**Specific Implementation Recommendations**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the Department proceeds with broader deregulation, Agilian urges the following modifications to protect patient safety while still reducing unnecessary burden:‚Źé‚Źé1. Retain the DSI model card requirement in its current form. Do not weaken transparency standards for algorithms that influence clinical decision-making.‚Źé‚Źé2. For other certification criteria, consider a tiered approach where lower-risk IT (data exchange standards, administrative tools) has lighter documentation requirements, while higher-risk IT (algorithms influencing clinical decisions, security and privacy controls) maintains current standards.‚Źé‚Źé3. Provide transition support to states and health systems. If standards change, allow a reasonable implementation period (at least 18 months) before enforcement. This allows state agencies and hospitals time to evaluate existing tools and plan transitions.‚Źé‚Źé4. Establish a public registry of algorithm model cards. Require vendors to submit model cards for any algorithm used in clinical decision-making, regardless of certification status. This creates transparency without requiring government enforcement.‚Źé‚Źé5. Enhance FDA oversight of clinical decision support tools. If health IT certification standards are weakened, ensure that FDA guidance on clinical decision support (issued in 2019) is updated and more actively enforced.‚Źé‚Źé6. Require state Medicaid agencies to establish minimum transparency standards for any health IT tool used in coverage or prior authorization decisions. If federal standards weaken, states should strengthen their own requirements.‚Źé‚Źé**Conclusion**‚Źé‚ŹéAgilian's work with state health departments has shown repeatedly that deregulation without standards produces fragmentation, not innovation. Minnesota's disease surveillance consolidation required stronger standards, not weaker ones. Clinical implementation of decision support algorithms requires transparency, not opacity. Patient safety depends on accountability, not blind trust in vendor claims.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI model card requirement is not excessive burden. It is essential information. Removing it will transfer risk from vendors to state agencies, hospitals, and ultimately patients. If the Department is committed to reducing unnecessary burden, there are other areas of the certification program where standards might be relaxed without compromising patient safety.‚Źé‚ŹéBut clinical decision support algorithms are not one of those areas. These tools influence whether patients receive appropriate treatment. The requirement that vendors document what their algorithms do, what populations they have been validated for, and what their known limitations are is proportionate to the risk. It should be retained.‚Źé‚ŹéAgilian appreciates the opportunity to comment on this important proposal. State government partners depend on health IT standards that balance innovation with safety. The DSI transparency requirements are a reasonable, evidence-based approach to that balance. We urge the Department to retain them.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-17T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-17T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ9‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒgovernment‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒurgent‚ôĒMinnesota‚ôĒstate budget analyst‚ôĒ49‚ôĒWhen we reconciled Minnesota's disease surveillance data with CMS reporting requirements last spring, we discovered our hepatitis C case tracking was pulling from three different state systems that couldn't talk to each other‚ÄĒand federal timelines demanded we fix it by summer. If reporting standards fragment further, we're looking at either hiring staff we don't have the budget for or risking the kind of gaps that nearly let us miss an outbreak cluster in Hennepin County two years ago.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2981
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0136‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒMargaret Jackson‚ôĒDoximity, Inc.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒDoximity argues that the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model documentation and external validation should be retained because it ensures clinical decision support systems are transparent and safe, rather...‚ôĒRe: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity‚Źé‚ŹéDoximity respectfully submits this comment on the proposed deregulatory framework. Our organization develops health IT solutions used by over 80 percent of U.S. physicians and operates at the intersection of clinical workflow, data exchange, and regulatory compliance. What I want to address directly is the proposal to eliminate the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion, which currently requires vendors to document AI model cards including intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. We believe this criterion should remain in place, and I want to explain why based on what we've learned building these systems at scale.‚Źé‚Źé**THE COST TRAP AND WHAT IT ACTUALLY REVEALS**‚Źé‚ŹéI understand the frustration behind deregulation efforts. The compliance burden is real. Last year, I watched a competitor in our space spend nearly $400,000 and six months of engineering capacity to recertify a telemedicine platform. That's time they couldn't spend on actual innovation. I get why people see certification requirements as friction that favors incumbents like us. We can absorb those costs. Smaller teams can't. The logic seems clean: remove the rules, level the playing field, let innovation flourish.‚Źé‚ŹéBut here's what I've learned watching this industry evolve: the DSI criterion isn't arbitrary bureaucratic overhead. It's a guardrail that emerged because of a real problem. When we deploy decision support systems into clinical environments, we're not just selling software. We're placing tools in front of physicians who make decisions that affect patient outcomes. That's different from other software categories. A design tool or a messaging platform can have bugs and rough edges. A clinical decision support system that doesn't transparently disclose what population it was trained on, or what its failure modes are, creates genuine patient safety risk. The certification requirement for model cards isn't protecting incumbents. It's protecting patients and clinicians from making decisions based on systems they don't understand.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT TRANSPARENCY ACTUALLY MEANS IN PRACTICE**‚Źé‚ŹéOur organization has spent the last three years building and refining AI-assisted clinical tools. We've gone through this process multiple times now, and I want to be honest about what it costs versus what it prevents. The DSI criterion requires us to document intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation. That's straightforward documentation work. It's not a major engineering lift. What it does is force us, as developers, to actually understand and articulate what our models do and don't do well.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen we built a decision support tool for sepsis identification, we had to document that it performed differently in pediatric populations than adult populations. Our training data came primarily from adult ICUs. We had to disclose that. We had to run external validation studies to understand the performance gap. That wasn't a regulatory checkbox. That work actually changed what we built. We now have a separate pathway for pediatric cases with different alert thresholds. Without the requirement to articulate and validate our model's behavior across populations, we would have shipped a single tool that worked great for adults and poorly for children. The clinician wouldn't have known that. The model card requirement forced transparency that led to better clinical outcomes.‚Źé‚ŹéThe transparency burden that the deregulation proposal wants to eliminate isn't just about compliance theater. It's about ensuring that the humans using these tools understand their boundaries. When a physician sees a clinical decision support recommendation, they're making a judgment call about whether to follow it. That judgment is only as good as their understanding of what the system is. Is it trained on patients like the one in front of them? Does it perform well on edge cases? What are its failure modes? These questions matter enormously. The model card requirement ensures those answers are documented and available.‚Źé‚Źé**MARKET COMPETITION AND WHAT ACTUALLY MATTERS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe deregulation logic assumes that removing certification requirements will increase competition and innovation. I've heard that argument, and it sounds right in theory. But in practice, the certification criterion for DSI doesn't create a barrier to entry for competitors who are building legitimate clinical decision support tools. It creates a requirement that they document and validate their work. That's different from a barrier to entry. A barrier to entry would be a rule that says only certain companies can build decision support systems, or a rule that requires expensive FDA approval for every update. That's not what the DSI criterion does.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat actually happens when you remove transparency requirements is you increase the gap between large companies and smaller ones, not the other way around. A large company like ours can absorb liability risk from deploying poorly validated systems. We have insurance, legal resources, and brand value that protects us if something goes wrong. A smaller vendor who deploys a decision support system without external validation, without documented intended use, without disclosed risks, is taking on enormous liability. When something goes wrong, they're the ones who get sued. They're the ones who face regulatory action. The certification requirement, paradoxically, protects smaller vendors by creating a safe harbor. It says: if you meet this standard, you've done your due diligence. Smaller companies need that clarity more than we do.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CLINICAL CONTEXT THAT REGULATORS UNDERSTAND**‚Źé‚ŹéI should note that this perspective isn't unique to Doximity. The American Medical Association, in its guidance on clinical decision support systems, recommends that developers disclose exactly what the DSI criterion requires: intended use, target population, validation evidence, and known performance limitations. The FDA's 2021 proposed framework for AI/ML in healthcare uses similar transparency requirements. The NIH's standards for AI model governance include model cards as a foundational component. These aren't arbitrary regulatory inventions. They reflect what clinicians and patient safety experts have learned from deploying these systems at scale.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat concerns me about the deregulation proposal is that it seems to treat all certification criteria equally, as if they're all equally burdensome and equally arbitrary. Some are. I'd agree that certain documentation requirements for interoperability standards, for example, have accumulated redundancies over time. But the DSI criterion isn't one of those. It's lean, focused, and directly connected to patient safety. It requires documentation that any responsible developer should be doing anyway.‚Źé‚Źé**THE REAL COST OF REMOVING GUARDRAILS**‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what I worry will happen if the DSI criterion is removed. Smaller vendors will face pressure to deploy decision support systems without the validation and documentation they need. They'll compete on cost and speed, not on transparency and safety. Larger vendors, us included, will have an incentive to deploy with minimal validation because our competitors are doing the same. Clinicians will be using tools they don't understand, making decisions based on recommendations from systems with unknown limitations. The liability will shift from vendors to hospitals and health systems and ultimately to patients.‚Źé‚ŹéWe've seen this pattern before in other industries. When regulatory requirements for transparency and validation get removed, the initial effect is often cheaper, faster deployment. The longer-term effect is that the market gradually shifts toward companies willing to cut corners on validation and transparency, because that's where the margin is. Eventually, something breaks. A system fails catastrophically. Then you get crisis regulation, which is much more restrictive than the balanced requirement you had before.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not arguing for keeping all certification requirements. Some are outdated. Some create friction without clear safety benefit. The DSI criterion isn't one of those. It's a focused requirement that ensures decision support systems are documented, validated, and transparent about their limitations. That's worth keeping.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE'RE ACTUALLY PROPOSING**‚Źé‚ŹéOur recommendation is straightforward: retain the DSI certification criterion in its current form. The documentation requirements are: (1) statement of intended use; (2) description of target population; (3) documentation of known risks and performance limitations; (4) summary of training data characteristics; and (5) results of external validation studies. These requirements are proportionate to the clinical risk involved. They're not burdensome relative to the safety value they create.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the agency is concerned that the documentation standards themselves need updating, we're open to that conversation. The model card format could be streamlined. The validation requirements could be clarified to allow different types of evidence depending on the clinical use case. But the core requirement, that decision support systems be documented and externally validated before deployment, should remain.‚Źé‚ŹéThe irony is that keeping this requirement actually supports the deregulation goal in a meaningful way. By maintaining a clear, focused certification criterion for clinical decision support, you reduce uncertainty for developers. You create a predictable compliance path. You protect smaller vendors by establishing a safe harbor. You ensure that competition happens on the basis of clinical value and innovation, not on the basis of who's willing to deploy the least validated system. That's the kind of deregulation that actually works.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéDoximity is supportive of efforts to reduce unnecessary regulatory burden on health IT developers. But that effort should be focused and evidence-based. It should eliminate rules that don't serve a clear purpose. It should streamline processes that are redundant or outdated. It shouldn't eliminate safeguards that exist because we've learned, through experience, that they matter.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criterion exists because clinical decision support systems carry patient safety implications that other types of software don't. Removing that criterion won't reduce burden in a meaningful way. It will shift risk to clinicians and patients. I'd urge the agency to preserve this requirement while considering streamlining other aspects of the certification framework that truly create unnecessary friction.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-12T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-12T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ5‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒhopeful‚ôĒWyoming‚ôĒVP of regulatory affairs‚ôĒ43‚ôĒLast year, I watched our smaller competitor in telemedicine struggle through a recertification cycle that cost them nearly $400K and consumed six months of their engineering roadmap‚ÄĒtime they desperately needed to innovate on their actual product. Meanwhile, we absorbed those compliance costs as a line item. It crystallized for me how the current certification framework inadvertently protects incumbents like us while suffocating the scrappy teams that might actually solve problems differently.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1581
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0137‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒKimberly Thomas‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA parent of a child with epilepsy opposes removing transparency and testing requirements for AI systems in healthcare, arguing that patients and doctors need to understand how AI tools work, what data they're trained on, and their error rates to m...‚ôĒPATIENT SAFETY CANNOT BE THE PRICE OF CONVENIENCE‚Źé‚ŹéMy daughter has epilepsy. Last spring we switched neurologists because her old clinic wasn't managing her care well, and the new practice told us they couldn't access her seizure records for three weeks. Three weeks. We paid $150 to get copies faxed over, and I was so angry I could barely think straight. But now I'm reading that the government wants to make it easier for health data to move around, and part of that involves removing transparency requirements for AI systems in healthcare. I'm furious about this in a completely different way.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what concerns me: if we strip away the rules that require companies to tell us how their AI tools actually work, what they've been trained on, and where they might fail, we're basically saying it's okay to use black-box technology on patients without real oversight. My daughter's seizure management depends on accurate information. If a doctor is using an AI system to help decide on her medication and we don't know whether that system has been tested on kids her age, or what its error rate is, or what data it was built from, how can we make informed decisions about her care? How can her doctor?‚Źé‚ŹéI already had to fight tooth and nail just to get copies of basic medical records. The argument I keep hearing is that removing these safeguards will make things faster and cheaper for tech companies. But faster and cheaper for whom? Not for patients like us. We're the ones who live with the consequences when something goes wrong.‚Źé‚ŹéREMOVING TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS IS THE WRONG MOVE‚Źé‚ŹéThe certification criteria and testing requirements exist because patient safety matters. Yes, they create some burden on developers. But that burden exists for a reason. When it comes to AI in healthcare, "we'll figure it out later" is not acceptable. My daughter's life is not a beta test. I urge you to keep the transparency rules in place. Companies should have to tell us how their tools work and prove they're reliable. That's not deregulation.
