I was furious that information about her own health was trapped like that, but now I'm hearing they might make it even easier for that data to move around without asking us first, and honestly, that scares me more than the filing fees did.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements for AI systems in healthcare means doctors and patients won't know how reliable these tools actually are or what they might get wrong, which is dangerous when someone's health is on the line. | If we make it easier for AI systems to be used without disclosing their limitations and training data, we're basically saying patient safety doesn't matter as much as making things convenient for tech companies. | I already had to fight to get my daughter's basic medical records‚ÄĒnow you want to let AI systems operate in the dark without even telling us how they work or what they've been tested on?‚ôĒDeregulating AI transparency in healthcare trades away patient safety and informed decision-making for corporate convenience, and we can't afford that gamble with our kids' lives.‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ352
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0138‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDonald Mitchell‚ôĒTexas Medical Association‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Texas Medical Association opposes removing the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion, arguing that requirements for AI model cards, transparency disclosures, and external validation are essential safeguards that enable c...‚ôĒRe: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity‚Źé‚ŹéThe Texas Medical Association appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory actions under consideration by the Assistant Secretary for Technology Policy and the Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology. Our organization represents over 55,000 physicians across Texas, and our members work daily with the clinical decision support systems that would be affected by changes to certification requirements. We write specifically to address the proposal to remove or substantially revise the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion, which currently requires AI model cards, transparency disclosures, and validation documentation.‚Źé‚ŹéOur interest in this matter is not academic. Last month, our research team encountered a real problem that illustrates why the current DSI requirements exist in the first place. We were analyzing patient outcomes data across 12 rural oncology centers in Texas to identify treatment disparities when our Institutional Review Board flagged our data-sharing agreement as potentially noncompliant with evolving oversight standards. We had to halt the project midstream. My team of four spent two weeks determining whether we needed fresh informed consent from patients whose data had already been de-identified under the prior IRB approval. The research was designed to catch health inequities before they calcify in practice patterns, and instead we were paralyzed by uncertainty. That experience shaped how we think about certification standards for AI tools. When oversight gets unclear, research stops. When clinical decision support operates without transparency requirements, clinicians can't make informed choices about whether to trust the tool. Both situations harm patients.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THE DSI CRITERION MATTERS IN PRACTICE**‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification requirement exists because clinical decision support systems are not neutral tools. They embed assumptions about populations, outcomes, and appropriate care pathways. A model trained primarily on data from academic medical centers may perform differently when deployed in a rural setting. A system developed to identify candidates for aggressive oncology treatment may have different predictive accuracy in patients over 75 than in younger cohorts. These differences are not theoretical concerns. They affect whether a 68-year-old patient in rural East Texas gets recommended for a treatment regimen that might extend her life by months or cause months of toxicity with minimal benefit.‚Źé‚ŹéThe model card requirement under the current DSI criterion serves a specific function: it forces developers to document what they know and don't know about their system's performance across different populations and use contexts. When a clinician in Tyler, Texas opens a clinical decision support tool that recommends a particular chemotherapy regimen, she can see in the model card that the system was trained on 80 percent academic center data, that its positive predictive value drops from 0.89 to 0.76 in patients over 70, and that external validation in rural settings is pending. That transparency allows her to weigh the recommendation against her clinical judgment and her specific patient's circumstances.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving this requirement would not simply reduce administrative burden. It would create information asymmetry between the developers and the clinicians using the tools. The developers would know the limitations. The clinicians would not. This is particularly problematic in rural and underserved settings where clinicians may have less access to health informatics expertise and where the population characteristics often differ from the populations used in algorithm development.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CERTIFICATION CRITERION AS A BASELINE FOR ACCOUNTABILITY**‚Źé‚ŹéWe recognize that the proposed deregulation is framed around reducing burden and promoting innovation. We agree that excessive regulation can slow beneficial innovation. But the DSI criterion is not a burden on innovation. It is a baseline for accountability. The requirement to produce a model card does not prevent a developer from building and deploying a clinical decision support system. It requires them to be transparent about what they built and what they know about its performance.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider the parallel in pharmaceutical regulation. The FDA does not prevent companies from developing new drugs. It requires them to document safety and efficacy data before those drugs can be prescribed to patients. Physicians can then make informed decisions about when to use those drugs. The documentation requirement is not a barrier to innovation. It is a condition for responsible deployment. The DSI criterion functions similarly in the AI context.‚Źé‚ŹéWe also want to be clear about what we are not arguing. We are not proposing that all clinical decision support systems require FDA approval. Many tools operate in lower-risk domains where transparency requirements are less critical. A scheduling optimization tool that helps a clinic allocate appointment slots does not need the same level of documentation as a tool that recommends treatment decisions for cancer patients. The current DSI criterion already contains proportionality. It applies to systems that make recommendations or provide clinical judgment support, not to all health IT tools. That proportionality should be preserved.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE HAVE LEARNED FROM IMPLEMENTATION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Texas Medical Association has been involved in discussions about clinical decision support standards and certification since the early iterations of the criteria. We have watched how health systems actually implement these tools. The picture is more complex than debates about regulatory burden sometimes suggest.‚Źé‚ŹéIn 2022, we surveyed 200 of our member practices about their use of clinical decision support systems. Thirty-eight percent reported using at least one AI-based decision support tool in their electronic health record or as a standalone system. When we asked what would increase their confidence in using these tools more broadly, the most common response (64 percent) was "better documentation of how the system was developed and what it's designed to do." The second most common response (52 percent) was "evidence that the system has been tested in settings like ours." These are exactly what the DSI criterion's model card and external validation requirements provide.‚Źé‚ŹéWe also asked practices what barriers prevented them from adopting additional decision support tools. Regulatory burden did not rank high. Interoperability challenges ranked first. Cost ranked second. Uncertainty about the tool's performance in their specific patient population ranked third. The certification requirement was mentioned by only 14 percent of respondents as a barrier. This does not suggest that the DSI criterion is driving clinicians away from beneficial tools.‚Źé‚ŹéWe did hear concerns about the cost of external validation, particularly for smaller vendors. This is a fair point. It costs money to conduct validation studies in new settings. Some of that cost is passed to healthcare systems. But the alternative is deployment without validation, which means clinicians cannot know whether a tool trained on one population will perform similarly in their population. For a system recommending treatment decisions, that uncertainty is not acceptable.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC PROVISIONS WE BELIEVE SHOULD BE RETAINED**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the ASTP/ONC moves forward with revisions to the DSI criterion, we recommend retaining these specific elements:‚Źé‚Źé1. A requirement that vendors document the intended use of the system, including the clinical context, patient population, and specific decision or recommendation that the system is designed to support. This documentation should be accessible to clinicians who are considering whether to deploy the tool.‚Źé‚Źé2. A requirement that vendors document known limitations of the system, including populations or clinical scenarios where performance may differ from the primary development population. This is not a request for perfection. It is a request for honesty about what the developer knows.‚Źé‚Źé3. A requirement for external validation in at least one setting different from the development setting before widespread deployment. This validation does not need to be conducted by the FDA or a government agency. It can be conducted by academic medical centers, health systems, or independent validators. But some independent assessment of performance should be required.‚Źé‚Źé4. A requirement that the model card and relevant documentation be made available to health systems and clinicians deploying the system. A model card is only useful if clinicians can see it. The documentation should be written in language that a practicing clinician can understand, not in technical jargon that requires a PhD in machine learning to parse.‚Źé‚ŹéThese provisions are not burdensome. They are the baseline expectations that would apply to any medical device or therapeutic intervention. A clinician would never prescribe a medication without knowing what it is designed to treat, what the known side effects are, and whether it has been studied in populations similar to her patients. She should not deploy a clinical decision support system without knowing these things either.‚Źé‚Źé**ADDRESSING THE INNOVATION ARGUMENT**‚Źé‚ŹéWe understand that some argue these requirements slow innovation and give larger vendors an advantage because they have more resources to conduct validation studies. This concern is not entirely baseless, but it is overstated.‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, the validation requirement does not mandate any specific type of study. A vendor can conduct a prospective validation study with a health system partner. They can conduct a retrospective analysis using de-identified data from multiple settings. They can work with an academic medical center to publish results in a peer-reviewed journal. They can conduct a smaller pilot study in a rural setting if that is relevant to their target market. The requirement is for evidence of performance in a new setting, not for a specific type of evidence.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, smaller and newer vendors can partner with health systems and academic centers to conduct validation. Several health systems in Texas have innovation programs specifically designed to support evaluation of new health IT tools. These partnerships can reduce the burden on vendors while ensuring that evidence is generated. The certification requirement can actually incentivize these partnerships because vendors know that evidence of performance will help them market their tools.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, and most importantly, the current certification requirement has not prevented innovation in clinical decision support. The number of vendors offering clinical decision support systems has grown substantially since the DSI criterion was adopted. The tools have become more sophisticated. The market has expanded. We are not aware of evidence that the certification requirement is actually holding back beneficial innovation.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat we are concerned about is a different kind of innovation: innovation in how to deploy tools without transparent documentation of their limitations. That is the kind of innovation that harms patients.‚Źé‚Źé**IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS FOR RETENTION**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the certification criterion is retained in its current form or modified, we recommend the ASTP/ONC consider these implementation improvements:‚Źé‚ŹéThe current model card template is somewhat technical. It would be helpful if the ONC provided guidance on translating the model card into clinical language that a practicing physician can understand. A cardiologist should be able to read the model card for a clinical decision support system and know whether it is appropriate for her patient population without needing to consult a data scientist.‚Źé‚ŹéThe external validation requirement could be made more flexible for lower-risk applications. A system that recommends screening tests might have a lower validation burden than a system that recommends specific treatments. The criterion could be tiered based on clinical risk.‚Źé‚ŹéThe ONC could establish a registry or clearinghouse where model cards and validation evidence are made publicly available. This would reduce duplicative work if multiple health systems want to evaluate the same tool. It would also increase transparency across the market.‚Źé‚ŹéThere could be a pathway for vendors to submit evidence of validation for review and certification, rather than requiring each health system to independently evaluate every tool. This would reduce burden on health systems while maintaining the requirement for evidence.‚Źé‚Źé**BROADER CONTEXT ON HEALTH IT CERTIFICATION**‚Źé‚ŹéWe recognize that this comment focuses narrowly on the DSI criterion, but it is worth noting that the broader question of what health IT systems should be certified and how is important for the field. The certification process has problems. There are inconsistencies in how different criteria are applied. There is a perception, whether or not it is justified, that larger vendors have advantages in navigating the certification process. There are legitimate questions about whether all current certification criteria are proportionate to the actual risks they are designed to address.‚Źé‚ŹéBut the solution to these problems is not to remove certification requirements wholesale. The solution is to improve the certification process, to ensure that requirements are proportionate to risks, and to make the process more accessible to smaller vendors. Removing the DSI criterion would not solve these problems. It would simply mean that clinicians would have less information about the tools they are using.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT IS AT STAKE**‚Źé‚ŹéThe decision about whether to retain the DSI certification criterion comes down to a fundamental question about how we want health IT to develop. Do we want a market where vendors are incentivized to be transparent about their tools' strengths and limitations? Or do we want a market where vendors can deploy tools with minimal disclosure, and clinicians have to guess about whether the tool is appropriate for their patients?‚Źé‚ŹéThe current evidence on algorithmic bias in healthcare suggests this is not a hypothetical concern. Studies have documented that machine learning models trained on historical data can perpetuate and amplify existing disparities in care. A system trained on data reflecting historical patterns of care disparities can learn to recommend treatments in ways that reproduce those disparities. Without transparency about the data used to train a system and evidence of performance across different populations, clinicians cannot identify and correct these biases.‚Źé‚ŹéWe are not arguing that the DSI criterion is perfect or that it could not be improved. But we are arguing that removing it would be a mistake. The criterion serves a real function. It requires transparency about tools that affect patient care. That transparency helps clinicians make better decisions about when and how to use these tools.‚Źé‚ŹéIn our research work in rural oncology, we learned that clarity about data limitations and known risks is not a burden. It is a prerequisite for responsible research. The same principle applies to clinical practice. Clinicians deserve to know what they are deploying and how it was developed. The DSI criterion ensures they have that information.‚Źé‚ŹéWe urge the ASTP/ONC to retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion in its current form or, if modifications are made, to preserve the core requirements for model card documentation, transparency about intended use and known limitations, external validation, and accessibility of documentation to clinicians. These requirements are not barriers to responsible innovation. They are the baseline for responsible deployment of AI-based tools in clinical settings.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-18T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-18T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ7‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe agency's claim that 'no publicly available evidence' supports transparency requirements conflates absence of evidence with evidence of absence; lack of published studies does not mean the criterion provides no value.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒurgent‚ôĒMaine‚ôĒresearch director‚ôĒ31‚ôĒLast month, I was analyzing aggregated patient outcomes data for a study on treatment disparities in rural oncology centers when our IRB suddenly flagged our data-sharing agreement as potentially noncompliant with evolving oversight standards‚ÄĒwe had to halt three months of research midstream. My team of four spent two weeks untangling whether the new guidance meant we needed fresh consent from patients we'd already de-identified, and frankly, watching us scramble made me realize how quickly these shifts can paralyze the exact research infrastructure designed to catch health inequities before they calcify.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2382
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0139‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒEdward Jackson‚ôĒDental Standards Institute‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Dental Standards Institute opposes removing the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion, arguing that while compliance costs are real, eliminating transparency requirements for AI clinical tools entirely‚ÄĒrather than modify...‚ôĒ**COMMENT ON ASTP/ONC DEREGULATORY ACTIONS: PROPOSED REMOVAL OF DECISION SUPPORT INTERVENTIONS CERTIFICATION CRITERION**‚Źé‚Źé**Submitted by: Edward Jackson, Senior Policy Analyst, Dental Standards Institute**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Dental Standards Institute appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed modifications to health IT certification criteria under the ASTP/ONC deregulatory framework. We have a specific concern about the proposed removal of the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion, particularly the transparency and accountability requirements embedded in model cards and disclosure mandates. While we recognize the intent to reduce compliance burden, we believe this particular change would create measurable risks to patient safety and clinical decision-making quality that outweigh the regulatory relief benefits.‚Źé‚ŹéOur position is not based on abstract principle. Last year, our organization worked with a coalition analyzing dental access disparities across rural counties in Texas, Oklahoma, and New Mexico. Three of our partner clinics initially couldn't participate in a regional data-sharing initiative because they lacked infrastructure to meet existing privacy standards. This was frustrating because those same safeguards were designed to protect vulnerable patient populations. Once we got them connected through proper channels, their ability to identify and reach underserved patients improved dramatically. But during that process, I watched firsthand how easily those same data channels could expose vulnerabilities in communities that already distrust the healthcare system. That experience shaped how we think about balancing innovation with accountability.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CURRENT DSI CRITERION AND WHAT IT REQUIRES**‚Źé‚ŹéUnder the existing certification framework codified at 45 CFR 170.315(a)(9), health IT systems that include clinical decision support functionality must meet specific transparency requirements. The criterion mandates that systems provide users with clear documentation of intended use, target population characteristics, known limitations and risks, training data composition, and external validation results. These aren't abstract reporting requirements. They're documentation that allows a clinician to understand whether an AI model was designed and tested for their specific patient population.‚Źé‚ŹéThe model card requirement (often called "transparency disclosure" in practical terms) creates an accountability mechanism. When a dental practice in rural Oklahoma uses an AI-assisted diagnostic tool, the clinician needs to know if that tool was trained predominantly on images from urban teaching hospitals with different patient demographics. They need to know if external validation was conducted on populations similar to theirs. They need to know what the known error rates are, particularly for edge cases. This isn't regulatory theater. This is foundational information for informed clinical decision-making.‚Źé‚ŹéWe understand the argument for deregulation. Compliance costs are real. The burden of maintaining detailed model cards, updating them as systems evolve, and ensuring they meet certification standards takes staff time and resources. For smaller health IT vendors, this can represent a meaningful expense. But the proposed solution of simply removing the DSI criterion entirely would eliminate the requirement to provide this information at all, not just reduce the compliance burden. That's a different calculation.‚Źé‚Źé**OUR EXPERIENCE WITH AI-ASSISTED TOOLS IN CLINICAL SETTINGS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe coalition work I mentioned involved implementing AI-assisted caries detection tools in three community health clinics. The tools were legitimate innovations. They could flag early-stage cavities on radiographs with reasonable accuracy, potentially improving detection rates in populations where access to specialist radiologists was limited. But clinicians had legitimate questions about how these tools performed: What was the training dataset? Were minority populations adequately represented? What was the false negative rate? What happened when the image quality was poor?‚Źé‚ŹéWhen we pushed the vendor for answers, we got pushback. Not hostile pushback, but the vendor essentially said: we don't have all that information readily available, we'd have to reconstruct it, and frankly, our competitive advantage depends on some of that remaining proprietary. We understood their concern. But from the clinician's perspective, recommending this tool to their patients without clear understanding of its limitations felt like a liability they weren't comfortable accepting.‚Źé‚ŹéEventually, the vendor did provide most of the documentation we requested. It wasn't formatted as a formal "model card," but it contained the essential information: the tool was trained on 40,000 radiographs, approximately 22 percent from patients with darker pigmentation, external validation showed 91 percent sensitivity and 87 percent specificity overall but only 83 percent sensitivity in the validation cohort with more severe periodontitis. Those numbers mattered. They changed how the clinicians understood the tool's appropriate use cases.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the DSI criterion is removed entirely, there's no requirement for vendors to provide this information. They can argue it's proprietary. They can decline to share validation data. Clinicians lose leverage to demand transparency. That's the real consequence of this change, separate from the compliance burden question.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THIS MATTERS FOR PATIENT SAFETY AND EQUITY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed deregulation is framed around innovation and market competition. The reasoning goes: if we require vendors to maintain detailed model cards and transparency disclosures, we slow down innovation cycles, increase costs, and advantage large established companies that can absorb compliance expenses. Smaller vendors and startups face steeper barriers. That logic isn't wrong. But it misses something important about health care markets.‚Źé‚ŹéHealth care isn't a normal competitive market. Patients don't shop for AI diagnostic tools. Clinicians often adopt them because they're bundled with broader EHR systems or because they reduce documentation burden, not because they've carefully evaluated their accuracy. The information asymmetry is extreme. In that context, regulatory requirements for transparency aren't just about reducing burden; they're about creating the conditions where competition can actually work as intended.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen the FDA regulates diagnostic devices under 21 CFR 510k or PMA pathways, the agency doesn't require those details to reduce vendor burden. The agency requires them because clinicians and patients need that information to make informed decisions. The DSI criterion operates in a similar space. It's not burdensome for its own sake; it's burdensome because it requires vendors to actually understand their own systems well enough to describe them.‚Źé‚ŹéThere's also a specific equity concern that troubles me. The rural clinics we worked with serve populations with higher rates of untreated dental disease, lower incomes, and lower health literacy. These are exactly the populations where AI tools could be most beneficial if used appropriately, but also where inappropriate use could cause the most harm. A tool trained predominantly on affluent urban populations might perform differently in these contexts. Without transparent documentation of training data composition and validation results, clinicians in these settings have no way to assess appropriateness. Removing the DSI requirement essentially leaves vulnerable populations with less information about the tools being used on them.‚Źé‚Źé**THE SPECIFIC LANGUAGE OF THE DSI CRITERION AND WHAT'S AT STAKE**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current criterion at 45 CFR 170.315(a)(9) requires that decision support interventions meet the following standards:‚Źé‚Źé1. Documented intended use describing the clinical purpose, target population, and clinical workflow integration‚Źé2. Transparency documentation including known limitations, performance characteristics, and data characteristics used in development‚Źé3. External validation evidence or clear disclosure if external validation has not been completed‚Źé4. Documentation of any performance differences across subpopulations‚Źé‚ŹéThis isn't an exhaustive list of what vendors must do. It's a basic floor for transparency. Critically, the standard doesn't prohibit proprietary information. Vendors can keep their algorithms secret. They can protect their training methodologies. But they have to describe what their system does, who it's for, what it's good at, and what it's not good at.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal to remove this criterion would eliminate all of these requirements. Vendors would have no obligation to maintain any of this documentation. They could refuse to answer clinician questions about training data, validation methodology, or known limitations. The market would function with complete information asymmetry.‚Źé‚ŹéI keep coming back to a specific example from our coalition work. One vendor offered a tool for predicting patient no-show rates, ostensibly to help clinics optimize scheduling. The tool seemed useful until we asked about the training data. It turned out the algorithm was trained on a dataset that was 78 percent white, from a region with different socioeconomic demographics than our partner clinics. The no-show prediction wasn't actually predicting clinical need; it was predicting insurance status and demographic characteristics. Without the requirement to document training data, the clinic would have adopted this tool and potentially discriminated against patients without realizing it. The transparency requirement caught this problem. Without it, the problem wouldn't have surfaced until harm occurred.‚Źé‚Źé**DISTINCTION BETWEEN BURDEN REDUCTION AND ELIMINATION OF ACCOUNTABILITY**‚Źé‚ŹéWe want to acknowledge something that doesn't get said often enough in regulatory comments: the burden is real. Maintaining current certification standards does cost vendors time and money. For smaller companies, those costs are proportionally larger. If the goal is to streamline compliance without eliminating accountability, there are options that don't require removing the DSI criterion entirely.‚Źé‚ŹéThe criterion could be modified to:‚Źé- Allow vendors to submit model card information on a less frequent schedule (e.g., annually rather than with every update, unless material changes occur)‚Źé- Standardize the format for transparency documentation to reduce custom compliance work‚Źé- Allow vendors to mark certain information as proprietary while still disclosing performance characteristics and known limitations‚Źé- Create a tiered approach where vendors can meet a basic transparency threshold for faster certification‚Źé- Establish a safe harbor for good-faith compliance efforts that fall short of perfect documentation‚Źé‚ŹéThese modifications would reduce burden while preserving the essential accountability mechanism. Removing the criterion entirely goes beyond burden reduction; it eliminates the requirement entirely. That's a different policy choice.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT THE LITERATURE SHOWS ABOUT AI TRANSPARENCY IN HEALTH CARE**‚Źé‚ŹéThere's growing evidence that transparency about AI systems matters for clinical adoption and patient safety. A 2023 study published in the Journal of the American Medical Informatics Association found that clinicians were significantly more likely to trust and appropriately use AI diagnostic tools when they had access to information about training data composition, validation results, and known limitations. Conversely, when that information was unavailable, clinicians either avoided using the tools or used them without appropriate skepticism.‚Źé‚ŹéThe NIH's guidance on responsible AI in health care (published in their Health Information Technology Strategic Plan) emphasizes that transparency about model development, validation, and performance characteristics is essential for safe clinical integration. The FDA's recent guidance on clinical decision support software (issued in January 2024 as a draft) similarly emphasizes that providers need clear information about intended use, target populations, and performance characteristics to make appropriate clinical decisions.‚Źé‚ŹéNone of this research suggests that vendors should be required to disclose proprietary algorithms or competitive secrets. But the research is consistent that some level of transparency about what a system does, who it's designed for, and how well it actually performs is necessary for safe use. Removing the DSI criterion would eliminate that transparency requirement entirely.‚Źé‚Źé**IMPLEMENTATION CHALLENGES WE'VE OBSERVED**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be honest about something that doesn't always get discussed in formal comments. Even with the current DSI criterion in place, compliance is uneven. Some vendors take transparency seriously and provide detailed, useful model cards. Others provide bare-minimum documentation that technically satisfies the criterion but doesn't actually convey meaningful information to clinicians. The certification process doesn't have strong mechanisms to distinguish between these approaches.‚Źé‚ŹéThat's a real problem, and it's separate from the question of whether the requirement should exist. It suggests that if the criterion is retained, the certification and oversight process needs to be more rigorous about actually evaluating the quality and usefulness of transparency documentation. But that's an argument for better implementation, not for elimination.‚Źé‚ŹéWe've also seen vendors struggle with the documentation requirement for older systems where the original training data is no longer available or where the development process wasn't well-documented at the time. In those cases, vendors face a choice: conduct new external validation to fill information gaps, or claim they can't meet the requirement. Some vendors have chosen the former; others have simply refused to participate in the certification process. This creates a market where older, undocumented systems remain in use outside the certification framework, which is arguably worse than the current situation.‚Źé‚ŹéThese implementation challenges suggest the criterion might benefit from clarification or modification. But they don't support elimination.‚Źé‚Źé**THE COMPETITIVE IMPACT ARGUMENT DESERVES SCRUTINY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe regulatory impact analysis accompanying the proposed changes suggests that removing the DSI criterion would level the playing field for smaller vendors and startups, allowing them to compete more effectively with large established health IT companies that can absorb compliance costs. This argument has some validity on its surface. But it's worth examining more carefully.‚Źé‚ŹéLarge vendors can absorb compliance costs because they have large revenue bases. Removing a regulatory requirement doesn't actually help smaller competitors catch up; it just reduces costs across the board. The real advantage small companies have is agility and innovation capacity, not lower regulatory burdens. If anything, removing transparency requirements might advantage larger companies more, because they have the resources to build and maintain sophisticated AI systems that smaller competitors can't easily audit or validate.‚Źé‚ŹéThe competitive argument also assumes that transparency requirements primarily benefit large, established players. But that's backwards. Transparency requirements are most important when there's information asymmetry between vendors and users. Established vendors with long track records and existing relationships can rely on reputation. New vendors actually benefit from being able to transparently document their systems and demonstrate quality. Removing transparency requirements makes it harder for new vendors to establish credibility.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE'RE ASKING FOR**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Dental Standards Institute requests that the DSI certification criterion, specifically the requirements for transparency documentation, model cards, and disclosure of training data characteristics and validation results, be retained in the final rule. We understand this comes with compliance costs. If the agency determines that burden reduction is appropriate, we suggest modification rather than elimination.‚Źé‚ŹéSpecifically, we recommend:‚Źé‚Źé- Retain the core transparency requirements but allow vendors to document them in standardized formats that reduce custom compliance work‚Źé- Establish a reasonable timeline for updates (e.g., annual updates with exception reporting for material changes)‚Źé- Allow vendors to designate certain information as confidential business information while still disclosing performance characteristics and known limitations‚Źé- Clarify expectations for external validation so vendors understand what level of evidence is necessary‚Źé- Create a process for vendors to request extensions or alternative compliance approaches for legacy systems‚Źé‚ŹéWe also recommend that ONC strengthen the certification review process to actually evaluate the quality and usefulness of transparency documentation, rather than simply checking that it exists. The criterion is only valuable if it results in information that clinicians can actually use.‚Źé‚Źé**BROADER CONTEXT: TRUST IN HEALTH CARE SYSTEMS**‚Źé‚ŹéThis comment focuses on a specific regulatory requirement, but it sits within a larger context. The rural communities we work with in Texas have historical reasons to distrust health care institutions. Many of these communities experienced medical racism and exploitation. When we ask them to adopt new technologies, including AI-assisted tools, that trust is fragile. It depends partly on their ability to understand what these tools do and whether they work appropriately for their populations.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criterion is a mechanism for building that trust. It requires vendors to be transparent about what their systems do and how well they do it. Removing that requirement sends a message that transparency isn't required, that vendors can decline to answer questions about their systems, that clinicians and patients need to accept tools on faith. That message will be heard especially clearly in communities that have historically been asked to accept medical interventions without understanding or consent.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not suggesting the regulation is perfect or that it couldn't be improved. But its elimination would make it harder to build the trust necessary for responsible AI adoption in health care.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Dental Standards Institute supports regulatory modernization and burden reduction where it's appropriate. But the proposed removal of the DSI certification criterion goes beyond burden reduction; it eliminates accountability for transparency. We believe this change would create measurable risks to patient safety, clinical decision-making quality, and health equity that outweigh the compliance benefits.‚Źé‚ŹéWe ask that ONC retain this criterion, potentially with modifications to reduce burden, and strengthen the certification review process to ensure that transparency documentation is actually useful to clinicians. The goal should be innovation that's accountable, not innovation without oversight.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-01T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-01T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ8‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒsupportive‚ôĒTexas‚ôĒsenior policy analyst‚ôĒ31‚ôĒLast year, I worked with a coalition analyzing dental access disparities in rural counties, and we discovered that three of our partner clinics couldn't participate in a regional data-sharing initiative because they lacked the infrastructure to meet existing privacy standards‚ÄĒironically, the very safeguards that were supposed to protect their patients. When we finally got them connected, their ability to identify and reach underserved populations improved dramatically, but I watched firsthand how easily those same data channels could expose vulnerabilities in communities that already distrust the healthcare system.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2681
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0140‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒRobert Young‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA nurse and patient advocate argues against removing Decision Support Interventions certification requirements and weakening information blocking safeguards, contending that transparency documentation for AI tools and privacy protections are essen...‚ôĒ**Comment on ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions to Unleash Prosperity**‚Źé‚ŹéMy daughter got diagnosed with lupus last year. For two months she couldn't start treatment because her rheumatologist couldn't see the complete picture of her lab work. The tests were scattered across three different electronic systems, and nobody had a process to pull them together without her manually requesting each one, then emailing files back and forth like we were still in 2005. When I was working as a nurse, I saw the frustration this caused constantly. Patients sitting in waiting rooms while staff made phone calls trying to track down results that should have been available in seconds. But here's what keeps me up at night now: I also watched what happens when privacy guardrails get removed. I saw colleagues dealing with insurance companies that had access to diagnoses they shouldn't have. A woman I worked with got denied a mortgage because an underwriter found out about her depression treatment. Another colleague had a past psychiatric hospitalization used against her in a custody dispute. These aren't hypothetical problems. They're what happens when the systems that are supposed to protect patient information get hollowed out.‚Źé‚ŹéSo when I read about removing the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion and weakening the transparency requirements around AI in healthcare, I felt both sides of this pulling at me. I want my daughter to get faster care. I want the barriers down. But I need to know that opening those records doesn't turn people's medical history into a weapon against them.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PROBLEM WITH REMOVING DSI CERTIFICATION REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current certification criterion for Decision Support Interventions requires health IT developers to provide model cards and transparency disclosures. This means they have to document the intended use of their AI tools, identify the target population, disclose known risks, describe how the training data was selected, and show that the model was validated externally. This isn't bureaucratic theater. It's the difference between a clinician knowing what a tool can and can't do and flying blind.‚Źé‚ŹéI worked in a hospital where we implemented a new diagnostic support system. The vendor provided documentation about what patient populations it had been tested on, what its failure modes were, and what conditions it performed poorly on. Our team could read that documentation and make informed decisions about when to use it and when to override it. A radiologist caught a case where the AI had been trained primarily on adult imaging but we were using it for pediatric patients. The documentation made that visible. Without those transparency requirements, that mismatch would have been invisible until something went wrong.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat concerns me is that the proposed deregulation treats these disclosures as pure burden rather than as safety infrastructure. Removing the requirement to document intended use, target population, known risks, and training data characteristics doesn't make the AI better or safer. It just makes it more opaque. The clinicians using these tools don't get smarter. The systems don't work better. What changes is that nobody's accountable when something fails.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is where my experience as a nurse and my current experience as a patient's parent align. When my daughter's rheumatologist made decisions about her treatment, I wanted that doctor to have complete information and to understand the limitations of every source of that information. The same principle applies to AI-assisted decision-making. If a clinical decision support tool is recommending a treatment plan, the clinician needs to understand what patient populations that tool was actually designed for, what the known failure modes are, and whether the training data included people who look like the patient in front of them.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY TRANSPARENCY ABOUT AI MATTERS FOR PATIENT SAFETY**‚Źé‚ŹéThere's a reason the FDA requires detailed labeling for medications. It's not to slow down the approval process or burden manufacturers. It's because doctors need to know what a drug is designed to do, who it's been tested on, what the side effects are, and what populations might respond differently. Health IT with AI components should work the same way. The model card requirement isn't different from drug labeling in principle. It's just applied to software instead of pharmaceuticals.‚Źé‚ŹéI've been reading about how some of these AI tools in healthcare are being trained on datasets that don't represent the full diversity of patients who'll eventually use them. One study looked at an algorithm used to identify patients for kidney disease screening, and it turned out the algorithm had been trained primarily on data from white patients and performed much less accurately for Black patients (Obermeyer et al., "Dissecting Racial Bias in an Algorithm Used to Manage the Health of Populations," Science, 2019). The algorithm didn't know it had this limitation. The clinicians using it didn't know it had this limitation. It was invisible until someone dug into the training data. If the certification criterion for decision support tools requires transparency about training data characteristics, these kinds of disparities get documented and visible. Remove that requirement, and you're betting that clinicians will somehow discover these problems on their own, which they won't.‚Źé‚ŹéThe argument for removing the DSI criterion seems to rest on the idea that the market will police itself, that bad tools will fail because clinicians won't use them. That's not how incentives work in healthcare. Vendors have every reason to overstate what their tools can do. Hospitals have every reason to believe that tools with shiny dashboards and impressive marketing will solve staffing shortages and speed up diagnoses. Individual clinicians are busy and often don't have time to independently validate every piece of software they're told to use. The certification criterion creates a floor. It says you have to tell us what this tool is designed for, what it's been tested on, what it's bad at, and what data went into training it. That's not onerous. It's the minimum.‚Źé‚Źé**WHERE PRIVACY AND INTEROPERABILITY COLLIDE**‚Źé‚ŹéNow I need to address the other side of what's in this proposed rule. Removing information blocking safeguards sounds like it would make healthcare faster and more efficient. In some ways, it would. If my daughter's records could move seamlessly between her rheumatologist, her primary care doctor, and her specialist without her having to request them manually, that's genuinely good. That's the goal. But information blocking rules exist because when you make it easy for records to move, you also make it easy for them to move to places they shouldn't go.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current rules around information blocking have exceptions. They allow providers to block access to information if blocking is necessary to protect patient privacy or security, or if the request is for commercial purposes unrelated to treatment. These exceptions aren't perfect. I know people who've fought with their doctors' offices because the staff didn't understand the exceptions and blocked access unnecessarily. But the exceptions exist for a reason. Without them, you get a system where a patient's entire medical history flows to any entity that claims a legitimate purpose, and the definition of legitimate gets broader and broader.‚Źé‚ŹéI mentioned the woman who got denied a mortgage because of her psychiatric history. How did that underwriter get access to that information? There are various ways it could have happened, but one increasingly common path is through health data brokers. These companies buy or license patient data from healthcare systems, sometimes with consent and sometimes in ways where consent gets buried in a terms-of-service document nobody reads. They then sell that data to insurance companies, employers, and yes, sometimes to financial institutions. If information blocking safeguards get weakened, the volume of data flowing into these markets increases. The opportunities for misuse multiply.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not saying we should keep information blocking rules exactly as they are. They do create friction. But the solution isn't to remove them entirely. It's to make them smarter, not weaker. You could streamline the process for legitimate interoperability while maintaining the exceptions that protect against misuse. You could require transparency about where data is flowing. You could create real penalties for entities that use patient information for purposes outside the scope of their original request.‚Źé‚Źé**THE NEED FOR ACCOUNTABILITY IN ALGORITHM DEPLOYMENT**‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what really troubles me about removing the decision support interventions certification criterion specifically. It's not just about transparency in the abstract. It's about accountability. When a health IT vendor provides a model card that documents intended use, target population, known risks, and training data characteristics, there's a paper trail. If that tool is used in a way it wasn't designed for, if it fails in a population it wasn't tested on, if it causes harm, there's evidence of what the vendor knew and what they disclosed. That evidence matters for liability. It matters for learning what went wrong. It matters for preventing the same failure from happening again.‚Źé‚ŹéWithout that documentation requirement, vendors have no incentive to investigate their tools' limitations. Why spend money on external validation of your algorithm if you don't have to disclose what you found? Why test it on diverse patient populations if you don't have to report the results? Why worry about failure modes if nobody's asking you to document them? The certification criterion creates an incentive to actually do the work of making sure your tool is safe and effective. Remove it, and you've removed the incentive.‚Źé‚ŹéThe 21st Century Cures Act (42 U.S.C. 300jj et seq.) was supposed to break down silos and make health information more accessible. That's a good goal. But the Act also includes provisions protecting against information blocking precisely because policymakers understood that accessibility without safeguards creates risks. The current regulations implementing those provisions aren't perfect, but they represent an attempt to balance two legitimate goals: getting information to the people who need it for patient care, and keeping information out of the hands of people who would misuse it.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat I'm asking is that the deregulatory actions proposed in this rule maintain that balance. Streamline the DSI certification process if it's genuinely burdensome. But don't eliminate the requirement for vendors to disclose what their tools are designed for, what populations they've been tested on, what their limitations are, and how they were trained. And don't weaken the information blocking exceptions so far that patient data flows freely to any entity that can claim a tangential connection to healthcare.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT HAPPENED WHEN SAFEGUARDS WERE REMOVED**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be concrete about this because it matters. When I was working in nursing, we saw what happened when oversight got lax. A hospital system near us implemented a new electronic health record system that was supposed to speed up documentation. The vendor promised it would reduce administrative burden and let clinicians spend more time with patients. They removed a lot of the checks and balances from the previous system because they were considered obstacles to efficiency. For about eight months, everything seemed fine. Then someone noticed that a clinician with access to the system had been accessing records for patients they weren't treating. The person had been documenting substance abuse issues and psychiatric histories in notes that then got flagged to employers and insurance companies. By the time anyone noticed, the damage was done. The hospital had to notify hundreds of patients, settle lawsuits, and ultimately replace the entire system.‚Źé‚ŹéThe problem wasn't that the EHR was bad technology. The problem was that the safeguards that had been in place to prevent unauthorized access got removed in the name of efficiency. Once they were gone, there was nothing to catch misuse until it had already happened at scale.‚Źé‚ŹéI think about this case a lot when I hear arguments about removing information blocking safeguards or weakening transparency requirements for AI tools. The people making those arguments usually aren't wrong that the safeguards create friction. They do. But friction sometimes exists for a reason. It's the thing that slows down harm.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT NEEDS TO HAPPEN INSTEAD**‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not asking for the rule to be withdrawn. I think there are genuine inefficiencies in how health IT is regulated and how information flows in healthcare. But I'm asking that the deregulatory actions maintain the core requirements that keep systems accountable and patients protected.‚Źé‚ŹéFor decision support interventions specifically, keep the certification criterion that requires model cards and transparency disclosures. The documentation burden on vendors is real but not excessive. Most responsible vendors are already doing this work. Those who aren't doing it should have to. The alternative is a system where AI tools are deployed in clinical settings with no documentation of what they're designed for, what they've been tested on, what their limitations are, or how they were trained. That's not innovation. That's gambling with patient safety.‚Źé‚ŹéFor information blocking, keep the exceptions that protect against commercial misuse and privacy violations. Streamline the process for legitimate clinical interoperability if you want to. But don't remove the guardrails. The goal should be making it faster and easier for information to flow for patient care while making it harder for information to flow for profit. That's not impossible. It just requires specificity about what counts as a legitimate use and enforcement when entities overstep.‚Źé‚ŹéI think about my daughter and what she went through. Two months without treatment because her records couldn't talk to each other. That's a failure of interoperability that needs to be fixed. But I also think about the woman who couldn't get a mortgage because a health data broker sold her psychiatric history to a financial institution. That's a failure of privacy protection that also needs to be prevented. These aren't incompatible goals. You can have fast, efficient information sharing for clinical care and strong protections against misuse. You just have to be intentional about building both into the system.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule has a chance to move toward that balance. It can reduce burden on vendors and providers without eliminating the transparency and accountability mechanisms that keep patients safe. The decision about whether to maintain the DSI certification criterion is one place to start. Keep it. Improve it if necessary. But don't remove it.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-02-02T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-02T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ7‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHospital and health system procurement teams need transparent AI model information to make evidence-based decisions about which tools to adopt and how to implement them safely in their local contexts.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒsupportive‚ôĒMaryland‚ôĒretired nurse‚ôĒ52‚ôĒWhen my daughter was diagnosed with lupus last year, she had to request her lab work manually from three different systems before her rheumatologist could even see the full picture‚ÄĒit took nearly two months. If those barriers came down, she could have started treatment weeks earlier. But I've also seen what happens in healthcare when privacy falls away; I watched colleagues struggle with insurance denials based on information that shouldn't have been accessible.
