CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0141‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒAshley Brown‚ôĒCareAtlas Inc.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒCareAtlas supports maintaining current Decision Support Interventions certification requirements for AI model transparency and validation data, arguing that such disclosures are essential for patient safety, clinician decision-making, and reducing...‚ôĒCareAtlas Inc. appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed modifications to health IT certification criteria. Our organization manages clinical operations for a multi-specialty practice in Portland, Oregon, with approximately 280 providers and administrative staff supporting patient care across three locations. We have direct experience with the consequences of vendor lock-in and the real costs of switching health information systems, which informs our position on the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion.‚Źé‚ŹéThree years ago, CareAtlas upgraded to a new EHR platform. The decision itself was straightforward enough, but the compliance pathway was not. Our vendor required a five-year service agreement as a condition of implementation, which effectively locked us into their system regardless of whether it met our operational needs. The recertification process for switching would have cost approximately $200,000 and consumed four to six months of IT and clinical staff time. We were not unique in facing this constraint. When our clinicians encountered workflow inefficiencies in the new system's decision support features, our options were limited: adapt our processes to the software or absorb extraordinary switching costs. We adapted.‚Źé‚ŹéThis experience shapes our view on DSI certification requirements. We support maintaining the current certification criterion that requires AI model cards and transparency disclosures, including intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. Here is why this matters operationally.‚Źé‚Źé**Patient Safety and Clinical Decision-Making**‚Źé‚ŹéDecision support tools increasingly influence clinical workflows. When a clinician receives a recommendation from an AI-assisted system, they need to know what population the model was trained on, what it was designed to detect or predict, and what its failure modes are. A model trained primarily on data from academic medical centers may perform differently in a community practice setting. A tool built to identify sepsis in ICU patients may not work as intended for post-operative ward patients. Without model cards, clinicians cannot make informed judgments about when to trust a recommendation and when to apply skepticism. The 21st Century Cures Act (42 U.S.C. 300jj et seq.) already requires transparency in health information systems. The DSI criterion operationalizes that mandate by ensuring vendors provide actionable information rather than opaque outputs. This is not regulatory burden; it is clinical necessity.‚Źé‚Źé**Vendor Accountability and Competition**‚Źé‚ŹéThe transparency requirement also creates market incentives for developers to build better tools. When model performance data is public, competitors can benchmark against each other. Customers can compare options. Poor-performing systems face scrutiny. This is how markets work. Removing these disclosures would entrench the vendors with the largest installed bases, because switching costs would increase further. A provider evaluating a new decision support tool without transparency data faces higher risk; that risk translates into higher switching costs, which favors incumbents. We have seen this dynamic play out in our own vendor relationships. The vendors with the most restrictive documentation practices are those with the deepest institutional entrenchment.‚Źé‚Źé**Implementation Recommendations**‚Źé‚ŹéCareAtlas recommends the following approach to balance innovation with accountability:‚Źé‚Źé- Retain the core DSI certification criterion requiring model cards and external validation summaries as currently defined in 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1).‚Źé- Allow vendors flexibility in the format and delivery mechanism for disclosures, provided the required information is accessible to purchasing organizations and clinicians.‚Źé- Establish a public registry of certified decision support tools with summary performance data, similar to existing frameworks under the FDA's Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) guidance (FDA-2019-D-5974).‚Źé- Require vendors to report on model performance across demographic subgroups to identify potential disparities, consistent with recommendations from the National Academies report on algorithmic fairness in health care (2022).‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI criterion is not preventing innovation. It is ensuring that innovation happens with adequate information for clinical decision-making. Removing it would shift risk from vendors to providers and patients. CareAtlas has no interest in defending vendor lock-in, but we also have no interest in trading it for vendor opacity. Transparency requirements actually reduce long-term switching costs by giving providers the information they need to evaluate alternatives.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-12T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-12T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒresigned‚ôĒOregon‚ôĒpractice manager‚ôĒ57‚ôĒWhen we upgraded our EHR system three years ago, we were locked into a five-year service agreement with our vendor because switching would have meant recertifying everything from scratch‚ÄĒa process that would have cost us north of $200,000 and taken months we couldn't afford to lose. I watched our clinicians frustrate over features that didn't fit our workflow, but we were essentially held hostage by compliance requirements that favored vendor lock-in over our actual operational needs.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ662
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0142‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒSusan Baker‚ôĒTexas Medical Association‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Texas Medical Association opposes eliminating the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion, arguing that while certification adds cost and timeline to AI tool development, removing transparency requirements for model documentatio...‚ôĒThe Texas Medical Association appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory actions outlined by ASTP/ONC. Our organization represents over 55,000 physicians and medical students across Texas, and we have a direct stake in how health information systems function in clinical practice. We want to address what we see as a critical gap in the proposed rule: the proposal to eliminate or substantially weaken the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion and its associated transparency requirements for AI-assisted clinical tools.‚Źé‚ŹéWe understand the impulse behind deregulation. Certification requirements do add cost and timeline to product development. But last spring, I watched a volunteer coordinator's mother spend three weeks fighting to get her medical records transferred between providers so she could pursue an experimental arthritis treatment. The insurance company claimed they couldn't access her full medical history from her previous provider, not because the records didn't exist but because they weren't in a format the new system could read or because nobody could locate them across the fragmented network. The irony isn't lost on us now: we're discussing whether to remove safeguards around data sharing and AI decision-making at exactly the moment when the stakes for getting this wrong are highest. That woman eventually got her records. But what happens when an AI system makes a coverage determination or clinical recommendation based on incomplete or biased training data, and nobody can explain why? What happens when a patient can't access the model card, can't understand what population the algorithm was designed for, and can't know what risks the developers identified during validation?‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification criterion, established under 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1), requires developers to provide model cards documenting intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. This isn't bureaucratic theater. It's the minimum transparency needed for clinicians to make informed decisions about when and how to use these tools. The Health Care Payment Learning and Action Network identified in its 2023 white paper on AI governance that lack of transparency around decision support systems creates liability exposure for providers and erodes trust in clinical recommendations. When CMS began reviewing AI-assisted prior authorization tools in late 2024, the agency found that roughly 40 percent of the tools it examined lacked adequate documentation of their training datasets or validation methodologies. Forty percent. That's not a niche problem. It's a systemic issue that will only get worse if we remove the requirement to document how these systems work.‚Źé‚ŹéWe're not arguing against innovation. Texas physicians use clinical decision support tools every day. EHRs integrate evidence-based alerts into workflows. Diagnostic support systems help identify rare conditions. These tools are valuable. But there's a difference between streamlining approval processes and removing transparency requirements altogether. The distinction matters because what's being proposed isn't just removing red tape; it's removing the paper trail. Under the current rule, when a physician encounters an AI-assisted recommendation, they can theoretically access documentation about what population the tool was validated on, what data it was trained on, and what known limitations the developer identified. That documentation doesn't guarantee good outcomes. It doesn't prevent bias or errors. But it does create a foundation for accountability. It lets clinicians understand whether the recommendation applies to their patient. It lets patients ask questions about how a decision affecting their care was made.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider what happened with algorithmic bias in healthcare more broadly. The 2019 study in Science examining a widely used risk-prediction algorithm found that the tool systematically underestimated health needs for Black patients because it used healthcare costs as a proxy for medical complexity (Obermeyer et al., Science 366, no. 6464). The algorithm wasn't deliberately designed to be discriminatory. But it was deployed without adequate documentation of its training data or known limitations. If the DSI certification requirement had been in place, developers would have been required to disclose that the algorithm was trained on cost data that reflected existing healthcare disparities. Clinicians and health systems would have had the information they needed to apply the tool more carefully or not at all. We lost that opportunity with that algorithm. We shouldn't lose it going forward.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Texas Medical Association recognizes that certification adds cost. A typical AI tool review under the current DSI criterion takes 60 to 90 days and costs developers between $150,000 and $300,000. That's real money. Smaller vendors feel this burden acutely. But the solution isn't to eliminate transparency requirements; it's to streamline them. ASTP/ONC could simplify the model card template, create a pre-certification pathway for lower-risk tools, or allow developers to update certifications more easily as algorithms evolve. These are the kinds of reforms that reduce burden without sacrificing accountability. The current proposal does both simultaneously, and that's the problem.‚Źé‚ŹéWe're also concerned about what happens to liability and accountability if certification requirements disappear. Under 42 U.S.C. 1395cc(a)(1)(T), providers are responsible for the clinical decisions made in their facilities, including decisions assisted by AI systems. If a physician relies on a decision support tool that recommends denying a treatment, and that recommendation is based on training data or validation methodologies the provider can't access, who bears responsibility when something goes wrong? The physician? The health system? The vendor? The current regulatory framework distributes this responsibility in ways that make sense: vendors must document what they built, providers must understand what they're using, and regulators can review both. If we remove that requirement, we're essentially saying vendors can build and deploy clinical decision systems with no obligation to explain how they work. That's not deregulation. That's abdication.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Texas Medical Association also wants to note that this isn't a hypothetical concern. We've already seen what happens when AI tools are deployed in healthcare without adequate transparency. The prior authorization systems that began using algorithmic decision-making in 2022 and 2023 generated substantial pushback from physicians precisely because the tools made recommendations that clinicians couldn't understand or challenge. Doctors reported that algorithms denied coverage for treatments based on criteria that seemed arbitrary because those criteria weren't disclosed. Some of these systems were making decisions based on diagnosis codes that didn't match the patient's actual condition. The lack of transparency didn't make the prior authorization process faster or better; it made it more frustrating and less trustworthy. Removing the DSI certification criterion would create a similar dynamic in clinical decision support.‚Źé‚ŹéWe recommend that ASTP/ONC retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion but streamline its implementation in the following ways. First, create a tiered certification pathway where lower-risk tools (those with narrow use cases, homogeneous target populations, or limited clinical impact) face reduced documentation requirements. Second, standardize the model card template so developers aren't spending time on format compliance rather than substantive disclosure. Third, allow developers to update certifications incrementally as algorithms evolve, rather than requiring full re-certification. Fourth, establish a safe harbor for vendors who provide transparent documentation, limiting their liability exposure for good-faith implementation of certified tools. These changes would reduce burden on developers while preserving the transparency that clinicians and patients need.‚Źé‚ŹéThe fundamental point is this: deregulation should reduce unnecessary burden, not eliminate accountability. The DSI certification criterion serves a legitimate regulatory purpose. It ensures that when clinicians use AI-assisted decision support, they have the information they need to use it responsibly. Removing that requirement doesn't unleash innovation; it just removes the requirement that innovators explain what they've built. We can do better. We can create a regulatory pathway that's faster and less costly for developers while keeping the transparency that healthcare requires.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-24T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-24T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ4‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒfrustrated‚ôĒVirginia‚ôĒcampaign director‚ôĒ42‚ôĒLast spring, I was managing a statewide campaign for a primary candidate when our volunteer coordinator's mother was denied coverage for an experimental arthritis treatment‚ÄĒnot because it was deemed ineffective, but because the insurance company claimed they couldn't access her complete medical history from her previous provider. We spent weeks fighting just to get copies of records that should have been portable. Now I'm watching this play out differently on the other side: more data sharing without guardrails feels like we're trading one broken system for another, especially knowing it'll hit hardest on the patients least equipped to advocate for themselves.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1250
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0143‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDaniel Johnson‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe commenter argues that proposed rollbacks to Decision Support Intervention (DSI) certification requirements‚ÄĒspecifically the removal of AI model card documentation standards‚ÄĒpose serious patient safety risks and should be retained, as these min...‚ôĒWhen my wife was diagnosed with stage-two lymphoma last year, we spent three months piecing together her oncology records from four different hospital systems. Faxes, phone calls, repeating the same medical history to each specialist while she was undergoing active treatment. Had those records been accessible to us digitally, we could have spared her the stress of that administrative gauntlet during chemotherapy. She was exhausted enough without having to advocate for her own medical information. Now I'm reading that some of the protections that forced those systems to share might be rolled back, and I'm genuinely uncertain whether the next patient will have it easier or harder.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm a librarian, so access to information is something I think about professionally. But this is different. This is about how we handle information that shapes whether people live or die, and I think the current proposal on Decision Support Interventions and AI model transparency needs serious reconsideration before any rollback happens.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal to remove certification requirements for Decision Support Interventions (DSI) concerns me for reasons that go beyond abstract principle. These are clinical tools. They're being used right now to help doctors make treatment decisions. When a system recommends a particular chemotherapy regimen or flags a potential drug interaction, that recommendation carries weight. A doctor might not question it as thoroughly as they should, especially in a busy clinic where they're seeing thirty patients a day. I've watched my wife's oncologists work. They're excellent, but they're also human. They're tired. They're managing multiple complex cases. If an AI system tells them something, they're going to listen to it more carefully than they listen to a random salesman.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat the current certification criterion requires is not excessive. It asks for AI model cards. Model cards are documentation that includes the intended use of the system, the target population it was trained on, known risks and limitations, characteristics of the training data, and whether the model was validated externally. These are not burdensome requirements. They're the baseline of what any responsible developer should be doing anyway. They're what I would want to know if that system were influencing my wife's treatment plan.‚Źé‚ŹéThe problem with removing this requirement is that it creates a two-tier system. Developers who are careful and responsible will continue to provide this transparency because their customers, particularly large health systems and hospital networks, will demand it. But smaller vendors, or vendors who cut corners, will have no obligation to do it. And hospitals operating under budget pressure might choose the cheaper option without full transparency. Patients and their families won't know what information the system was trained on. They won't know if it was tested on populations similar to them. They won't know the documented limitations.‚Źé‚ŹéI keep thinking about a specific moment during my wife's treatment. One of her oncologists mentioned that a particular drug combination had shown promise in recent trials, but the hospital's IT system had flagged a potential interaction with one of her other medications. The doctor investigated further, called the pharmacist, and they determined the interaction was manageable with adjusted dosing. But what if that system had no documented limitations? What if nobody actually knew whether it had been tested on patients with her specific combination of comorbidities? What if the doctor had just accepted the flag without investigation? We got lucky because the team was thorough. But luck shouldn't be the basis for patient safety.‚Źé‚ŹéThe argument I'm hearing is that removing these requirements reduces burden on developers and spurs innovation. I understand that argument. I don't think it's frivolous. But innovation in healthcare isn't like innovation in consumer software. If a social media algorithm makes a bad recommendation, I can ignore it. If a healthcare AI system makes a bad recommendation and a doctor relies on it, somebody might get hurt. The burden of certification is the price of operating in a system where people's lives depend on the information being reasonably trustworthy.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what troubles me most: the proposal frames information blocking safeguards and certification requirements as obstacles to progress. But they're not obstacles. They're guardrails. They're the difference between a market where vendors compete on quality and a market where vendors compete on cost while cutting corners on transparency. The companies that have invested in responsible AI development are already doing these things. They're already creating model cards. They're already doing external validation. Removing the requirement doesn't help them. It only helps the companies that would otherwise have to do the work.‚Źé‚ŹéThe certification criterion should be retained precisely because it's minimal. It's not asking developers to redesign their systems. It's not asking them to open-source their algorithms or hand over proprietary training data. It's asking them to document what they built, what it's supposed to do, who it was tested on, and what its limitations are. That's the bare minimum for a clinical tool.‚Źé‚ŹéI've read the preamble to the proposed rule. I understand the administration's philosophy about deregulation and market-driven innovation. But healthcare is not a normal market. People cannot simply choose not to participate. They cannot shop around easily when they're sick. They cannot become experts in AI systems overnight. They depend on their doctors to be informed about the tools being used to help them, and they depend on regulators to ensure that basic transparency exists.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat I want to see is the retention of the DSI certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures. This means keeping the requirement that developers document intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. It's not onerous. It's not anti-competitive. It's basic accountability.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the administration is committed to reducing burden on developers, there are other places to look. There are administrative requirements that could be streamlined. There are reporting timelines that could be adjusted. But the substance of what the certification criterion requires should stay in place. The requirement for transparency in clinical AI systems is not a barrier to innovation. It's a foundation for responsible innovation.‚Źé‚ŹéMy wife is in remission now. We're grateful. But I think about the family that comes after us, the one that doesn't get lucky because a system with undocumented limitations influenced a critical treatment decision. That's what keeps me writing this comment. The certification criterion matters. It matters because my wife mattered, and every patient who comes after her matters just as much.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-12T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-12T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ3‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒfrustrated‚ôĒConnecticut‚ôĒlibrarian‚ôĒ68‚ôĒWhen my wife was diagnosed with stage-two lymphoma last year, we spent three months piecing together her oncology records from four different hospital systems‚ÄĒfaxes, phone calls, repeating the same medical history to each specialist‚ÄĒwhile she was undergoing active treatment. Had those records been accessible to us digitally, we could have spared her the stress of that administrative gauntlet during chemotherapy, but now I'm reading that some of those protections that *forced* them to share might be rolled back, and I'm genuinely uncertain whether the next patient will have it easier or harder.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1068
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0144‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒJoseph Smith‚ôĒURAALI Refaluwasch Association‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe URAALI Refaluwasch Association, representing 4.2 million patients with rare and complex conditions, urges the Office of the National Coordinator to retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion requiring AI model cards and ...‚ôĒJune 18, 2025‚Źé‚ŹéOffice of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology‚ŹéU.S. Department of Health and Human Services‚Źé200 Independence Avenue, S.W.‚ŹéWashington, D.C. 20201‚Źé‚ŹéRE: Request for Information on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity (Federal Register Notice, June 2025)‚Źé‚ŹéURAALI Refaluwasch Association respectfully submits this comment on the proposed deregulatory actions affecting Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria and the broader certification and standards framework for health information technology.‚Źé‚Źé**ORGANIZATIONAL BACKGROUND AND STAKEHOLDER INTEREST**‚Źé‚ŹéThe URAALI Refaluwasch Association represents over 280 patient advocacy organizations across the United States, with a combined membership exceeding 4.2 million individuals living with rare autoimmune, genetic, and complex multi-system conditions. Our members depend on accurate clinical decision support and reliable access to their genetic and diagnostic data to identify appropriate specialists, navigate treatment options, and advocate for themselves within healthcare systems that often lack familiarity with their conditions. We serve as a coordinating body for patient voices in health policy discussions, and we have been actively engaged in health information technology standards development through participation in the HL7 Patient Care Committee since 2019.‚Źé‚ŹéOur interest in this rulemaking is not abstract. Three years ago, one of our board members spent eighteen months fighting with insurance companies and healthcare providers to access her sister's genetic screening data. The data existed. The rheumatologist had ordered it. But the data was locked behind proprietary systems that the specialist himself could not access without permission from the insurance company, which in turn required authorization from the original testing facility. The genetic mutation was eventually identified, but only after months of delay, wrong diagnoses, and a cascade of failed medication trials that left the patient significantly worse off. That experience illustrated both the genuine danger of data silos in clinical care and the equally genuine danger of allowing medical information to flow without proper safeguards.‚Źé‚ŹéWe are writing to urge the Office of the National Coordinator to retain the current Decision Support Interventions certification criterion that requires AI model cards and transparency disclosures, including documentation of intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. We understand the regulatory burden argument. We also understand that over-regulation can stifle innovation and that some certification requirements may impose costs that ultimately harm patient access to beneficial technologies. But the DSI certification framework addresses a different kind of burden: the burden that falls on patients when clinical decision support systems fail, when they encode bias, when they recommend inappropriate treatment pathways for rare conditions, or when they obscure the reasoning behind their suggestions in ways that prevent clinicians and patients from catching errors.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PROBLEM WE ACTUALLY FACE IN PRACTICE**‚Źé‚ŹéLet me be direct about something that does not always surface in regulatory discussions. Patient advocacy groups are not uniformly opposed to deregulation. We are opposed to specific deregulations that will harm our constituents. We also recognize that some regulatory requirements create perverse incentives. The question is not whether to regulate but where the regulatory line should sit.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification criterion exists because clinical decision support systems make recommendations that directly influence patient treatment. Unlike general business software, where a recommendation algorithm might suggest which product to purchase, clinical decision support operates in a domain where errors are measured in patient harm. Over the past five years, our member organizations have documented more than 300 cases where clinical decision support systems recommended treatment pathways inappropriate for rare genetic conditions, primarily because the systems were trained on datasets that lacked sufficient representation of rare populations. In one case, a rare form of hereditary angioedema was misclassified by a DSI system as anaphylaxis, which led to entirely different treatment recommendations. The error was caught by the patient's specialist, but only because the specialist was familiar with the condition. In many cases across the United States, there is no specialist readily available, and the patient is treated by a primary care physician who relies on the DSI system and has no independent way to verify whether the recommendation is appropriate.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current certification requirement for model cards and external validation documentation serves a specific function in this context. It forces developers to be transparent about what population groups the system was trained on, what conditions it has been tested for, and what the known limitations are. That transparency allows clinicians and patients to ask the right questions. It allows rare disease specialists to flag when a system is likely to fail for their population. It creates accountability. When a DSI system is deployed without model card documentation, there is no clear record of what it was designed for, and when it fails, there is no baseline against which to measure that failure.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY TRANSPARENCY MATTERS FOR PATIENT SAFETY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe concern here is not theoretical. Consider the current landscape of AI-assisted clinical decision support. The FDA has taken a relatively hands-off approach to many DSI systems, classifying many of them as not subject to premarket review. The ONC certification process is one of the few remaining checkpoints where transparency requirements apply. If we remove those requirements, we are not simply reducing regulatory burden. We are removing one of the few mechanisms that ensures patients and clinicians can understand what the system is supposed to do and what it might get wrong.‚Źé‚ŹéA peer-reviewed study published in JAMA Internal Medicine in 2023 evaluated 47 commercially available clinical decision support systems and found that only 18 percent provided sufficient documentation of training data composition to assess whether the system was appropriate for use in populations with rare genetic conditions. When researchers contacted the developers to request additional information, 31 companies declined to provide it, citing proprietary concerns. That is the market behavior we can expect if transparency requirements are eliminated. Developers will have no incentive to document limitations, because doing so might reduce sales.‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be clear about something else. We are not arguing that all certification requirements should remain in place. Some requirements create compliance costs that likely exceed their safety benefit. The requirement that all DSI systems undergo annual recertification, for example, seems designed more to generate revenue for certification bodies than to meaningfully assess ongoing safety. If you are looking for places to reduce burden, that is a reasonable target. But the model card and transparency requirement is different. It is asking for documentation that costs money to produce but also costs money to not produce, in the form of patient harm.‚Źé‚Źé**IMPLEMENTATION CHALLENGES IN THE CURRENT SYSTEM**‚Źé‚ŹéThere is another dimension to this problem that does not always get discussed in regulatory forums. The current DSI certification requirement exists, but it is unevenly applied. Some developers submit detailed model cards. Others submit boilerplate documentation that barely meets the regulatory minimum. Some developers have invested in external validation studies. Others have not. The certification process has limited teeth. An organization could in theory submit a model card that says "intended use: general clinical support" and "known limitations: none identified," and the certification process might still approve it.‚Źé‚ŹéSo in one sense, the current certification requirement is not as strong as it appears on paper. In another sense, it is exactly because the requirement is not perfectly enforced that we should not remove it. The requirement provides a baseline. It creates an expectation that developers should document what they are doing. When enforcement is weak, the solution is to strengthen enforcement, not to eliminate the requirement.‚Źé‚ŹéOur organization has worked with ONC staff on several occasions to discuss how model card requirements could be streamlined without losing their safety function. It is possible to reduce burden while maintaining transparency. For example, model cards could be required only for DSI systems that are used in clinical settings serving patients with rare conditions, rather than applying universally. The documentation requirements could be simplified to focus on the most salient risk factors rather than requiring comprehensive disclosure of every variable in the training dataset. The annual recertification requirement could be replaced with a three-year cycle, reducing frequency without eliminating oversight.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC CONCERNS ABOUT PROPOSED CHANGES**‚Źé‚ŹéThe RFI asks whether the DSI certification criterion should be modified or removed entirely. We have reviewed the draft guidance documents that have circulated in the health IT community, and we understand that some stakeholders are advocating for complete removal of the criterion. We are concerned about that approach for the following reasons:‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, removing the requirement would eliminate the one certification-level mechanism that explicitly addresses algorithmic bias and population representation. The current criterion requires developers to disclose the demographic composition of training datasets and to validate systems across different population subgroups. If that requirement is removed, there is no remaining certification-level safeguard against systems that perform differently for patients with rare conditions, patients from underrepresented racial and ethnic backgrounds, or patients with comorbid conditions. The FDA regulates some DSI systems through the premarket pathway, but many fall outside FDA jurisdiction. ONC certification is the backstop.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, the DSI criterion is one of the few certification requirements that directly serves patient safety rather than interoperability goals. Most other ONC certification criteria focus on data exchange, standards compliance, and system functionality. The DSI criterion is about ensuring that the advice the system gives is safe. That is a different regulatory function. Lumping it together with interoperability requirements and treating deregulation as a uniform good misses the distinction between burden reduction and risk creation.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, there are documented cases where transparency about DSI system limitations has led to corrective action. In 2022, a major health system identified a bias in a widely used sepsis prediction algorithm after reviewing the model card submitted as part of the ONC certification process. The model card revealed that the training dataset had underrepresented patients on dialysis, which meant the algorithm performed poorly for that population. The health system initiated a corrective action plan to either retrain the algorithm or restrict its use in that population. That corrective action would likely not have happened without the transparency requirement. The bias would have persisted silently until a patient was harmed.‚Źé‚Źé**MARKET FAILURES THAT JUSTIFY RETENTION OF THE CRITERION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe argument for deregulation typically rests on the assumption that market competition will drive quality and safety. In the health IT market for clinical decision support, that assumption does not hold. Several market failures are relevant here:‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, clinicians and patients typically do not have choice in which DSI system they use. The system is selected by the health system, the EHR vendor, or the hospital administrator. The end-user (the clinician and patient) has limited ability to switch to a competitor if the system performs poorly. Market competition therefore does not drive quality in the way it might in other industries.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, the failure modes of DSI systems are often invisible. A system that recommends suboptimal treatment may not generate obvious errors that prompt a change. A clinician may not realize that the system is performing poorly for a particular population because the adverse events are not clearly attributable to the system. This is sometimes called the "latent failure" problem in patient safety literature. It means that market feedback mechanisms do not work well. A system could perform poorly for years without generating the kind of obvious failure that would prompt a switch to a competitor.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, there are asymmetries in information. Developers know the limitations of their systems. Health systems and clinicians often do not. Patients almost never do. Without a transparency requirement, there is no mechanism to bridge that information gap. The developer has no incentive to disclose limitations, because doing so might reduce adoption. The purchaser does not know to ask about limitations they are not aware of. The result is a market for "lemons" in the Akerlof sense.‚Źé‚ŹéThese market failures are well-documented in the health IT literature. A 2021 review published in the American Journal of Managed Care examined the evidence on DSI system implementation and found that the majority of studies examining real-world performance reported performance degradation compared to validation study results. That is, systems that looked good in controlled testing performed worse when deployed in actual clinical settings. The authors attributed this partly to differences between validation populations and actual patient populations. The current ONC certification requirement for external validation and population documentation is designed to flag exactly this kind of mismatch.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE MEAN BY "TRANSPARENCY DISCLOSURES"**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification criterion requires submission of model cards that include intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. This is not asking for publication of proprietary algorithms. It is asking for documentation that allows clinicians and patients to understand what the system is supposed to do and what it might get wrong. The documentation is submitted as part of the certification process. It becomes part of the certified product's documentation.‚Źé‚ŹéIn practice, what does this mean? A model card might say something like the following: "This sepsis prediction algorithm was trained on a dataset of 87,000 patient encounters at three urban academic medical centers, of which 11 percent were patients on dialysis, 8 percent were patients over age 85, and 4 percent were patients with stage 4 chronic kidney disease. The algorithm was validated on a separate dataset of 22,000 encounters at two rural hospitals and one federally qualified health center. Validation performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, negative predictive value) are reported separately for the overall population and for subgroups defined by age, race, ethnicity, dialysis status, and comorbid conditions. Known limitations include: reduced performance in patients with sepsis secondary to fungal infection (not well-represented in training data); slower response time in settings with poor data connectivity; and inability to incorporate narrative information from nursing notes."‚Źé‚ŹéThat is the kind of transparency we are talking about. It is not burdensome. It is not proprietary. It is exactly what a clinician should be able to know about a tool they are using to make treatment recommendations.‚Źé‚Źé**ADDRESSING THE BURDEN ARGUMENT**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to acknowledge that developing and submitting model card documentation does impose costs on developers. In our conversations with health IT companies, representatives have indicated that compliance with the current DSI criterion requires investment in external validation studies, documentation development, and certification submission processes. For smaller companies, these costs may be substantial relative to revenue. We understand that concern.‚Źé‚ŹéBut we also want to point out something that does not always surface in these discussions. The costs of not documenting are also substantial. When a DSI system fails and harms a patient, the liability and reputational costs to the developer are much higher than the upfront cost of validation and documentation. A single liability case can cost millions of dollars. Documentation and transparency actually reduce developer risk by creating a clear record of what the system was designed for and what its known limitations are. If a system fails outside its intended use, that documentation provides a defense. If a system fails within its intended use but in a population that was not adequately represented in validation, that documentation also provides clarity about what happened and why.‚Źé‚ŹéFrom a patient safety standpoint, there is also the question of what kind of burden matters most. The burden of compliance on a developer is measurable in dollars and staff time. The burden of a patient harmed by a biased or unreliable clinical decision support system is measured in health outcomes. We need to be honest about which burden is more important.‚Źé‚ŹéThat said, we are not opposed to streamlining the process. A few specific suggestions:‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ Develop a tiered approach where model card requirements vary based on the clinical use case and patient population. Systems used in high-volume primary care settings serving general populations could have lighter documentation requirements than systems used in specialty care for rare conditions.‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ Create a standardized model card template that reduces documentation burden while maintaining transparency. The FDA's work on AI documentation standards provides a useful foundation here.‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ Allow developers to submit validation studies conducted by independent third parties rather than requiring in-house validation, which might reduce costs while maintaining rigor.‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ Establish a transition period of 18 months for currently certified systems to submit updated model cards if the requirements are modified, rather than requiring immediate recertification.‚Źé‚Źé**THE ROLE OF EXTERNAL VALIDATION**‚Źé‚ŹéOne specific element of the current DSI criterion that we want to highlight is the external validation requirement. The current standard (45 CFR 170.315(b)(1)) requires that DSI systems undergo validation testing on datasets that are separate from the training dataset and that represent the intended use population. This is a fundamental patient safety principle. A system might perform perfectly on the data it was trained on simply because it has memorized patterns in that specific dataset. External validation tests whether the system generalizes to new data.‚Źé‚ŹéIn our experience working with health systems, external validation is often where population representation issues surface. A system trained primarily on data from urban academic medical centers may perform differently when validated on data from rural hospitals or community health centers. A system trained on predominantly white populations may perform differently when validated on patients from other racial and ethnic backgrounds. These differences might not be obvious without explicit validation testing.‚Źé‚ŹéThere are legitimate questions about what external validation should require. Does it need to be a massive study? No. Does it need to be conducted by an independent third party? The current regulations do not require that, though it would be ideal. But some form of external validation, with documented results, is important. Removing that requirement would mean removing one of the few mechanisms that ensures systems are tested on populations beyond those used for training.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION AND RECOMMENDATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe URAALI Refaluwasch Association urges the Office of the National Coordinator to retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion with its current requirements for model cards, transparency disclosures, and external validation. We recognize that some certification requirements impose burden without proportionate benefit, and we support targeted deregulation in areas where that is the case. The DSI criterion is not one of those areas.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the ONC is considering modifications to the criterion, we respectfully recommend the following approach:‚Źé‚Źé1. Retain the core requirement for model cards and transparency documentation, but streamline the specific requirements to focus on the most clinically significant risk factors.‚Źé‚Źé2. Implement a tiered system where requirements scale based on the clinical use case and population representation.‚Źé‚Źé3. Allow for alternative forms of external validation, including studies conducted by independent third parties, to reduce developer burden while maintaining rigor.‚Źé‚Źé4. Create a clear appeals or variance process where developers can request exemptions from specific documentation requirements if they can demonstrate that the requirement is not applicable to their particular system.‚Źé‚Źé5. Strengthen enforcement of the current requirement so that the benefits of transparency are realized in practice, rather than eliminating the requirement due to weak enforcement.‚Źé‚ŹéWe have seen what happens when medical information flows without proper safeguards, and we have also seen what happens when information is locked behind proprietary systems without transparency or accountability. The path forward is not to swing to either extreme but to maintain a balanced approach that supports innovation while protecting patient safety. The DSI certification criterion, properly implemented and enforced, serves that balance.‚Źé‚ŹéThank you for the opportunity to comment on this important rulemaking.‚Źé‚ŹéRespectfully submitted,‚Źé‚ŹéJoseph Smith‚ŹéDirector of Government Affairs‚ŹéURAALI Refaluwasch Association‚ŹéLittle Rock, Arkansas‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-13T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-13T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ10‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒhopeful‚ôĒArkansas‚ôĒdirector of government affairs‚ôĒ48‚ôĒMy sister was diagnosed with a rare autoimmune condition three years ago, and we spent eighteen months fighting with her insurance company to access her own genetic screening data so we could find a specialist who'd actually seen her specific mutation‚ÄĒturns out the data was locked behind proprietary systems that even her rheumatologist couldn't access. That experience showed me how frustrating the current data silos are, but it also made me realize how vulnerable people become when their medical information flows too freely without proper guardrails, which is exactly what keeps me up at night about getting this balance wrong.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ3247
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0145‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒRobert Mitchell‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒHealthcare organizations should be required to maintain transparency about AI systems used in medical decision-making, as patients deserve access to documentation about how these tools were developed and tested‚ÄĒjust as they deserve access to their...‚ôĒ**Patient Right to Know: AI Transparency in Healthcare**‚Źé‚ŹéLast year my wife needed her mammogram results sent to a specialist in Salt Lake City. The clinic in Provo had the images right there on the patient portal, but it still took three weeks to get them transferred electronically. That experience taught me something I already knew from my work as a librarian: when organizations control information, they control what patients can do with it.‚Źé‚ŹéNow I'm reading about removing transparency requirements for AI systems used in healthcare. I understand the desire to reduce burden on developers, but patients need to know how these tools were built and tested before we trust them with medical decisions. When hospitals use AI to help diagnose conditions or decide on treatment, we deserve the same access to basic documentation that we deserve for our medical records. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0146‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒSarah Thompson‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe commenter, drawing on personal experience with delayed medical records transfer, argues that transparency requirements for AI tools in healthcare‚ÄĒincluding model cards and risk disclosures‚ÄĒare essential for ensuring hospitals can make informed...‚ôĒLast year when my cardiologist changed and my insurance switched, it took three weeks to get my records moved over. That's what transparency rules do.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving the requirement for AI model cards and risk disclosures would make it harder for hospital procurement teams to know if a tool is safe for patients like me with complicated medical situations. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0147‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒRebecca Scott‚ôĒAd Hoc LLC‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒAd Hoc LLC opposes removing AI transparency requirements from health IT certification standards, arguing that model card documentation and external validation requirements are essential for patient safety and clinician informed decision-making rat...‚ôĒRe: RFI on Proposed Modifications to Health IT Certification Requirements and Information Blocking Regulations‚Źé‚ŹéAd Hoc LLC submits this comment in response to the proposed deregulatory actions affecting health IT certification standards, particularly those governing decision support interventions and AI transparency requirements. Our organization has spent the better part of a decade working with mid-sized providers and health systems to implement and maintain certified EHR technology, and what I want to stress upfront is this: there is real regulatory bloat in certain areas, but the proposed removals go too far in directions that will actually harm patient safety and clinical accountability.‚Źé‚ŹéBACKGROUND AND ORGANIZATIONAL PERSPECTIVE‚Źé‚ŹéAd Hoc LLC operates as a health IT consulting firm specializing in EHR infrastructure optimization and certification compliance for organizations with between 50 and 500 clinical providers. We've worked directly with 31 health systems and independent practices across Wyoming, Montana, Colorado, and Utah over the past 12 years. Our team includes former EHR implementation leads, compliance officers, and clinical informaticists. When I say we understand the compliance burden, I mean we've sat through the audits, watched staff spend weeks compiling evidence, and yes, we've had clients spend significant money retrofitting systems to meet overlapping requirements that made no practical sense. But we've also seen what happens when transparency requirements disappear, and it's not pretty.‚Źé‚ŹéLast year, one of our clients, a 140-bed rural hospital system in northwestern Wyoming, went through a complete EHR refresh. They spent roughly $180,000 retrofitting their clinical decision support infrastructure specifically to meet the 2015 Edition certification criteria for AI model transparency. That included documenting intended use cases, target patient populations, known performance limitations, training data characteristics, and the results of external validation studies. The hospital's IT director was frustrated, and honestly, she had cause to be. Three different EHR vendors had told her their systems had already validated these models internally, so why was she paying again for external documentation?‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what happened next, though. Six months after go-live, their oncology department discovered that the chemotherapy drug interaction checker, which the vendor had trained primarily on adult patients receiving standard-of-care regimens, was missing flagged interactions in a subset of geriatric patients on polypharmacy regimens. The external validation documentation they'd been forced to maintain actually helped them catch the problem faster and understand its scope. They caught it before any patient harm occurred. Without the model card requirements, without the transparency about training data limitations, they might have kept using that tool longer.‚Źé‚ŹéThat's the core of what I want to say in this comment. The certification criteria for Decision Support Interventions, specifically the requirement for AI model cards and transparency disclosures under 45 CFR 170.315(a)(1), should not be removed or significantly weakened. I understand the desire to reduce redundant burden. I've lived that frustration. But this particular requirement is not redundant. It's foundational to safe AI deployment in clinical settings.‚Źé‚ŹéCURRENT CERTIFICATION CRITERIA ARE NOT REDUNDANT WITH VENDOR VALIDATION‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule suggests that vendor internal validation processes and existing FDA oversight are sufficient to ensure AI safety and transparency in clinical decision support. This misunderstands what vendor validation actually covers and what the certification criteria actually require.‚Źé‚ŹéFDA clearance for clinical decision support devices typically focuses on whether a tool does what the manufacturer claims it does within the intended use statement. FDA review is important, but it's not comprehensive in the way that health IT certification criteria need to be. Consider these specific differences:‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ FDA review occurs at a point in time, based on data available at submission. It does not track ongoing performance degradation or drift in real-world deployment. Certification criteria, by contrast, require ongoing transparency that allows deploying organizations to monitor actual performance.‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ Vendor internal validation is proprietary. FDA submissions contain some detail, but clinicians at deploying sites rarely see the full training data characteristics, the specific populations excluded from validation, or the edge cases where the tool underperforms. Certification criteria require this disclosure happen at the point of purchase and implementation.‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ FDA clearance addresses whether a tool is safe and effective for its intended use. It does not address whether the intended use statement itself is appropriate for the population actually using it in clinical practice. Hospital A might deploy a tool cleared for general medical patients into a specialty practice serving a completely different population. Without the transparency requirements, nobody catches that mismatch.‚Źé‚ŹéWe've seen this play out. A client in Colorado deployed a sepsis risk prediction tool that carried FDA clearance. The vendor's intended use statement was for "acutely ill hospitalized patients." The hospital's ED director started using it routinely in their urgent care clinic to decide admission versus discharge for patients with respiratory symptoms. The model performed poorly in that population because it had been trained on inpatient data with different acuity distributions. Nobody had forced a conversation about whether the intended use matched actual use. The certification criteria would have required that conversation happen explicitly.‚Źé‚ŹéThe model card requirement under 45 CFR 170.315(a)(1) requires documentation of:‚Źé‚Źé- Intended use and target population‚Źé- Known performance limitations and failure modes‚Źé- Characteristics of training data and any identified biases‚Źé- Results of external validation studies‚Źé- Clinician-facing information about confidence and uncertainty‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not duplicative of FDA review. It's a different layer of transparency that happens at the point of clinical implementation, not at the point of regulatory clearance.‚Źé‚ŹéTHE COST ARGUMENT NEEDS ACTUAL DATA‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule frames compliance costs as a burden that stifles innovation and market entry. I want to push back on this with specificity. Yes, our clients spent money on these requirements. But the rule makes claims about what vendors will do if burden is reduced, and those claims need evidence.‚Źé‚ŹéThe assertion seems to be that smaller vendors are kept out of the market by certification requirements. I haven't seen data supporting that. In our experience, smaller vendors don't enter the health IT market because they lack the capital to build interoperable systems, the regulatory expertise to navigate FDA pathways, and the sales infrastructure to reach hospital purchasing departments. Certification requirements are a cost, but they're not the binding constraint. The binding constraint is usually the $3 million to $8 million upfront investment needed to build a HIPAA-compliant, FHIR-capable system that integrates with existing EHR infrastructure.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat I'm concerned about is the opposite scenario: that removing transparency requirements creates a race to the bottom where vendors compete on price and features rather than on safety and clarity. A vendor who can skip the external validation studies and model documentation saves maybe $50,000 to $150,000 per product. A vendor who has to do that work spends $200,000 to $500,000. In a competitive market with thin margins, which approach wins? The cheaper one, even if the cheaper one is less transparent.‚Źé‚ŹéOur experience suggests that compliance costs are not actually what's holding back innovation in clinical decision support. What's holding it back is that EHR vendors already have decision support tools bundled into their platforms, and hospital IT departments are reluctant to add third-party tools because of integration complexity. The real barrier to market entry is integration and interoperability, not certification. Removing certification requirements won't change that dynamic.‚Źé‚ŹéPATIENT SAFETY AND CLINICIAN INFORMED DECISION-MAKING‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what keeps me up at night about this proposal. AI tools in clinical settings are making real decisions that affect patient care. When a system flags a patient as high-risk for readmission and triggers a social work intervention, that's good. When a system fails to flag a patient and that person gets readmitted, that's a problem. When clinicians don't understand why the system made a particular recommendation, they either over-trust it or ignore it entirely.‚Źé‚ŹéThe literature on this is pretty clear. Studies on clinical decision support adoption show that clinicians are more likely to use a tool appropriately when they understand its limitations (Ash et al., 2004, Journal of the American Medical Association). When they don't understand how a tool works or what its failure modes are, they either trust it blindly, which increases the risk of cascading errors, or they ignore it entirely, which wastes the investment.‚Źé‚ŹéThe model card requirement ensures that clinicians have baseline information about what they're trusting. It's not perfect transparency. No documentation is. But it's a floor. It says that at minimum, when you deploy this tool, you will know what population it was designed for, what populations it might fail in, and what the external validation data showed about its performance.‚Źé‚ŹéWithout that requirement, we're in a situation where vendors can say "trust us, it works" and that's the end of the conversation. That's not clinician autonomy. That's vendor dependence.‚Źé‚ŹéRECOMMENDATIONS FOR TARGETED BURDEN REDUCTION‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be clear that I'm not arguing against all deregulation here. There are genuinely redundant or outdated requirements in the certification criteria. The 2015 Edition has been around for a decade. Some requirements have been superseded by other standards or by vendor internal practices. But the answer is not to throw out the DSI transparency requirements wholesale.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what I would recommend instead:‚Źé‚Źé1. Streamline the external validation requirement by allowing vendors to submit published peer-reviewed studies demonstrating performance in the relevant population, rather than requiring original studies for every implementation. This reduces cost while maintaining the substance of the requirement.‚Źé‚Źé2. Create a standardized template for model cards so that vendors don't have to reinvent documentation for each tool. The format doesn't have to be complex. It can be a three-page document. But it should be consistent so that clinicians can compare tools and understand them quickly.‚Źé‚Źé3. Allow vendors to mark certain fields as proprietary if they contain genuinely confidential information (like exact training data sources or algorithmic details), while requiring disclosure of everything that affects clinical decision-making (intended use, target population, known limitations, validation results).‚Źé‚Źé4. Require certification bodies to publish guidance on what constitutes adequate external validation, so vendors know what they're being evaluated against. Right now, there's some ambiguity about how rigorous the external validation has to be, and that creates uncertainty.‚Źé‚Źé5. Consider a compliance pathway where vendors can submit for one-time certification of a decision support model, and then that certification remains valid across multiple EHR implementations unless the vendor materially changes the model or its deployment parameters.‚Źé‚ŹéThese changes would reduce compliance costs significantly without removing the transparency requirements that actually matter for patient safety.‚Źé‚ŹéTHE INFORMATION BLOCKING CONCERN‚Źé‚ŹéI want to note one more thing about the broader deregulatory proposal. The RFI mentions potentially weakening information blocking regulations. I understand the argument that information blocking rules create compliance burdens. But in our experience, the information blocking rules have actually worked. They've forced EHR vendors to be more transparent about the data they're hoarding and more willing to share data through standard APIs.‚Źé‚ŹéBefore the information blocking rules, it was common for vendors to say "we can't share your data with another system because of integration complexity." After the rules, suddenly integration became possible. The burden was real, but it served a purpose. I'm concerned that weakening these rules without a clear understanding of what they've accomplished would be a mistake.‚Źé‚ŹéCONCLUSION‚Źé‚ŹéAd Hoc LLC supports targeted deregulation of health IT certification criteria where the requirements are truly redundant or outdated. But the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion requiring AI model transparency is neither redundant nor outdated. It's foundational to safe and accountable use of AI in clinical settings. Removing it would shift the burden of ensuring transparency from vendors to deploying organizations, and smaller hospitals without the resources to do their own validation would bear that cost.‚Źé‚ŹéThe $180,000 our client spent on DSI compliance looks different when you understand what they caught with it. That's not bureaucratic waste. That's patient safety. That's clinician autonomy. That's accountability.‚Źé‚ŹéWe recommend retaining the DSI certification criteria while implementing the streamlined compliance approaches outlined above. This balances the genuine burden reduction goals of the proposed rule with the patient safety imperative that should underlie all health IT regulation.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-02-03T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-03T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ6‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒangry‚ôĒWyoming‚ôĒhealth IT director‚ôĒ43‚ôĒLast year, we spent $180,000 retrofitting our EHR infrastructure to meet certification standards that three different vendors told us were redundant with their own internal validation processes‚ÄĒmoney that could have gone toward actually improving patient outcomes instead of satisfying bureaucratic checkbox requirements that big vendors like Epic and Cerner wrote into the system themselves.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2010
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0148‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒAnthony Wright‚ôĒThe Pharmacy Health Information Technology Collaborative‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Pharmacy Health Information Technology Collaborative urges retention of clinical decision support AI certification requirements mandating transparent model cards disclosing intended use, target population, training data characteristics, and va...‚ôĒThe Pharmacy Health Information Technology Collaborative appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory actions outlined in the ASTP/ONC notice. Our organization represents researchers and practitioners across three state hospital systems in West Virginia, and over the past eighteen months, we have confronted directly the consequences of fragmentary data governance in clinical settings. We write to urge retention of the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures, specifically those elements addressing intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results.‚Źé‚ŹéOur concern is neither abstract nor ideological. In 2023 and 2024, The Pharmacy Health Information Technology Collaborative built a cross-institutional database designed to track medication errors and adverse drug events across hospital systems in West Virginia, Kentucky, and Ohio. The goal was straightforward: identify patterns in prescribing failures, dosing errors, and contraindication oversights that occur when patients move between facilities. We invested eighteen months in this work. We obtained institutional review board approval. We negotiated data-sharing agreements with each participating health system. Then, as regulatory pressure on interoperability eased, proprietary electronic health record (EHR) vendors deliberately fragmented our data access. We literally cannot stitch together patient records across facilities now. The vendors know there is no enforcement mechanism to compel interoperability standards anymore, and they have acted accordingly.‚Źé‚ŹéThis experience informs our position on AI certification criteria. The proposed deregulatory action would remove or substantially weaken the requirement that developers of clinical decision support systems provide model cards disclosing intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results (45 CFR 170.315(b)(1), as currently written). The stated rationale is burden reduction and innovation acceleration. We understand that argument. We also believe it miscalculates the actual cost of transparency failure in clinical AI systems.‚Źé‚Źé**The Real Vulnerability: Opacity in Clinical Decision Support**‚Źé‚ŹéMedication error tracking depends on accurate, consistent clinical decision-making data. When AI systems assist in prescribing decisions, those systems operate as part of the medication use process. If a clinical decision support tool recommends a dosing interval without transparent disclosure of the population on which it was trained, the clinician using that tool operates with incomplete information. If the training data excluded elderly patients or patients with renal impairment, and those populations are now using the system, the risks are not theoretical. They are patient safety risks.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider the evidence base for this concern. A 2023 study in JAMA Internal Medicine found that machine learning models trained on predominantly white populations performed substantially worse when applied to Black and Latino patients across multiple clinical domains (Gianfrancesco et al., 2023). The model cards that would have disclosed training data composition were not available to the clinicians or health systems deploying these tools. The harm occurred silently, without accountability or intervention.‚Źé‚ŹéSimilarly, research published in the American Journal of Health-System Pharmacy documented cases where clinical decision support systems designed for acute care settings were deployed in ambulatory and long-term care contexts without modification (Hsieh et al., 2022). The intended-use disclosures would have prevented these misapplications. Instead, clinicians assumed the tools were validated across settings when they were not.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criterion requiring model cards is not a regulatory luxury. It is a basic transparency requirement that enables clinicians to make informed decisions about whether and how to use AI-assisted decision support. Removing this requirement does not reduce burden on developers in any meaningful sense. It transfers that burden to clinicians and health systems, who must now conduct their own validation studies to determine whether a tool is safe for their patient population.‚Źé‚Źé**Why This Matters for Interoperability and Data Quality**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Pharmacy Health Information Technology Collaborative's experience with data fragmentation across facilities demonstrates why transparency in clinical AI is inseparable from broader interoperability goals. When medication decisions are made by opaque AI systems, the reasoning behind those decisions is not captured or shared across systems. When a patient moves from one facility to another, the receiving clinician sees the decision but not the logic. If that decision was influenced by an AI system trained on a narrow population or with known risk factors, the receiving facility has no way to know.‚Źé‚ŹéThis creates a secondary problem: data quality deterioration. When medication error tracking relies on retrospective chart review, as ours did, clinicians must interpret prescribing decisions without understanding the decision-making process. Was a particular dosing choice intentional or an error? Without visibility into the decision support system's inputs and training data, that question becomes unanswerable. Our database contains dozens of cases where we cannot determine whether an unusual prescribing pattern represents clinical judgment, system error, or AI system recommendation.‚Źé‚ŹéThe 21st Century Cures Act (42 U.S.C. 300jj et seq.) mandates information blocking prevention and health data interoperability. The proposed deregulation would weaken one of the few mechanisms through which information blocking prevention can actually be enforced in the context of clinical AI. If a vendor's decision support system makes recommendations that differ across facilities without transparent disclosure of why, how would a health system or researcher identify the problem? The answer is they cannot, unless the model card requirement remains in place.‚Źé‚Źé**Specific Recommendations**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Pharmacy Health Information Technology Collaborative recommends the following:‚Źé‚Źé1. Retain the DSI certification criterion requiring developers to submit model cards disclosing (a) the intended use of the system, (b) the target population for which the system was validated, (c) known performance limitations and risks, (d) characteristics of the training data including demographic composition and temporal span, and (e) results of external validation studies conducted on populations outside the development cohort.‚Źé‚Źé2. Require that model cards be made available to purchasing health systems and, upon request, to clinicians using the system. This is not a public disclosure requirement but rather a transparency requirement between vendor and customer.‚Źé‚Źé3. Establish a straightforward appeal process through which health systems can request clarification or challenge vendors' model card disclosures if evidence suggests misrepresentation of intended use or target population.‚Źé‚Źé4. Link the DSI certification criterion to the information blocking prevention rule (42 CFR 171.102) such that vendors cannot be held liable for information blocking claims if they provide required model card disclosures.‚Źé‚ŹéThese recommendations do not impose substantial new burdens on vendors. The Pharmacy Health Information Technology Collaborative has reviewed the current model card requirements (45 CFR 170.315(b)(1) and associated documentation standards). The required disclosures are information that responsible developers should already possess. The requirement to submit and maintain these disclosures is a documentation and communication burden, not a research or engineering burden. Vendors already track training data composition, validation cohorts, and performance metrics. They already document intended use. The DSI certification criterion simply requires that this information be disclosed in a standardized format.‚Źé‚Źé**Interoperability Without Transparency Is Not Interoperability**‚Źé‚ŹéThe broader deregulatory action proposes to streamline certification requirements in order to accelerate health IT innovation and market competition. The Pharmacy Health Information Technology Collaborative supports innovation and competition. We also know from direct experience that innovation without transparency creates fragmentation, not integration. The EHR vendors that fragmented our medication error database acted rationally within a regulatory environment that allowed them to do so. They competed by locking in customers, not by improving data quality or interoperability.‚Źé‚ŹéClinical AI systems are tools that influence prescribing, dosing, and medication selection decisions. Those decisions affect patient safety. The DSI certification criterion requiring model cards is a minimal transparency requirement that enables clinicians and health systems to understand what they are using and whether it is appropriate for their patient population. Removing this requirement in the name of deregulation would leave clinicians and researchers unable to determine whether AI-assisted decisions are safe, valid, and appropriate for the populations they serve.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Pharmacy Health Information Technology Collaborative stands ready to work with ONC and CMS to implement a streamlined model card submission process that reduces unnecessary complexity while preserving the transparency that clinical AI systems require.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-02-02T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-02T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ4‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety
