risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒacademic‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒangry‚ôĒWest Virginia‚ôĒPhD researcher‚ôĒ30‚ôĒMy team spent eighteen months building a cross-institutional database to track medication errors across three state hospital systems, only to discover that proprietary EHR vendors had deliberately fragmented our data access the moment regulatory pressure eased‚ÄĒwe literally cannot stitch together patient records across facilities now, and the vendors know there's no enforcement mechanism to compel interoperability standards anymore.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1317
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0149‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒMargaret White‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe commenter opposes removing transparency and documentation requirements for AI clinical decision support systems, arguing that model cards and validation disclosures are essential safety information‚ÄĒnot trade secrets‚ÄĒthat enable doctors to eval...‚ôĒI need to be straight with you about what happened when my mother needed a second opinion on her heart condition, because it shapes everything I'm about to say about this proposed rule.‚Źé‚ŹéIt was a nightmare. Her cardiologist's office had all her test results, her EKG readings, her echocardiogram, her medication history. We wanted another cardiologist to review everything before she agreed to a procedure. Simple request. But the first office said they couldn't send the records because the second office "hadn't requested them properly." The second office said they were waiting for the first office to submit them in the right format. Meanwhile, my mother is sitting there not knowing if she should have surgery, and two offices with her medical information in their computers can't figure out how to share it with each other. I finally drove to both places myself, brought a USB drive and my laptop, and manually copied everything over while sitting in their waiting rooms. That's how in 2024 we solved a problem that should have been solved in 2009.‚Źé‚ŹéI mention this because I've been reading through the proposed changes to the certification criteria for health IT developers, and I keep coming back to something that worries me. The comment period is focused on removing requirements around Decision Support Interventions (DSIs) and the transparency standards that come with them. I understand the argument about regulatory burden. Developers say they're spending too much time on paperwork. But the paperwork in question isn't busywork. It's the documentation that's supposed to tell us what an AI system actually does and where it might fail.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT'S AT STAKE WITH AI IN CLINICAL DECISIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéLet me be clear about what we're talking about. Decision Support Interventions are AI systems that help doctors make choices. A hospital might use an AI to flag patients at risk for sepsis. A clinic might use one to help diagnose a condition based on symptoms. These aren't just suggesting a Google search result. They're influencing what medications a patient gets, whether they're admitted to the hospital, what procedures they undergo. The stakes are someone's health.‚Źé‚ŹéRight now, if a developer wants to sell an AI system into healthcare, they have to provide what's called an AI model card. That card includes things like:‚Źé‚Źé- The intended use case and target population (who is this AI built for?)‚Źé- Known risks and performance limitations (where does it fail?)‚Źé- Information about the training data (what examples did it learn from?)‚Źé- External validation results (did independent researchers test it?)‚Źé- Transparency disclosures about how the system makes decisions‚Źé‚ŹéWhen I read that list, I don't see bureaucratic overhead. I see the bare minimum information a doctor needs to know whether they should trust what the AI is telling them.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what troubles me. The proposed deregulation would remove the requirement for these model cards and transparency disclosures. The justification is innovation and competitive advantage. Developers argue that competitors will steal their secret sauce. I get that argument on its surface. But there's a problem with it: the "secret sauce" in question is information about whether the system works and where it doesn't. That's not a trade secret. That's safety information.‚Źé‚Źé**THE DIFFERENCE BETWEEN MARKET COMPETITION AND PATIENT SAFETY**‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not opposed to competition or innovation. I'm a caregiver, not a technophobe. I've seen how good technology can be. But there's a difference between protecting a company's proprietary algorithm and protecting a company's ability to hide information about risks.‚Źé‚ŹéLet me give you a concrete example. In 2021, researchers found that an algorithm widely used in hospitals to guide care decisions for Black patients was systematically biased. It had been trained on historical data that underestimated illness severity in Black patients because Black patients historically receive less aggressive treatment in the healthcare system. So the algorithm learned to do the same thing. Hospitals were using it to allocate resources, and it was allocating fewer resources to sicker Black patients. This wasn't discovered because the company voluntarily disclosed it. It was discovered because researchers specifically looked for bias. And it only came to light because enough documentation existed that they could trace what the system was actually doing.‚Źé‚ŹéIf that same company had been able to hide their training data characteristics, their validation results, and their known performance limitations under the guise of trade secrets, that bias might still be hidden. Patients would still be getting worse care, and they'd never know why.‚Źé‚ŹéThe certification criteria requiring transparency disclosures exist because of exactly this kind of problem. They exist because we've learned, over and over, that when companies are left to self-regulate on safety issues, some of them don't. Not because they're evil. Because they're under pressure to ship products, hit timelines, and stay competitive. The documentation requirement is what creates accountability.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THIS MATTERS FOR PEOPLE LIKE MY MOTHER**‚Źé‚ŹéWhen my mother's second cardiologist reviewed her records, he had the same test results as her first cardiologist. He had access to the same clinical information. But he had a different perspective. He asked different questions. He considered different options. One opinion wasn't right and one wrong, exactly. But they were different, and the difference mattered to her care.‚Źé‚ŹéThat's the thing about clinical decision-making. It's not a math problem with one correct answer. It's a judgment call based on incomplete information, competing values, and individual circumstances. A doctor brings training, experience, and intuition to that judgment. An AI system brings pattern recognition and speed. But an AI system can't understand what matters to a particular patient. It can't see the full picture the way a doctor can.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat an AI can do is introduce errors that a doctor might not catch. It can be confidently, systematically wrong in ways that are hard to spot because they're buried in code and algorithms. And if a doctor doesn't know what the AI was trained on, what populations it was tested with, or where it's known to fail, then the doctor can't catch those errors. The doctor becomes a rubber stamp on whatever the system suggests.‚Źé‚ŹéThat's where the transparency requirements come in. They're not about protecting companies from competition. They're about making sure doctors have the information they need to actually evaluate what the AI is telling them. They're about making sure patients have some assurance that the systems influencing their care have been scrutinized.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PROBLEM WITH REMOVING ACCOUNTABILITY**‚Źé‚ŹéI've read the RFI response materials, and I notice something consistent in the deregulation arguments. They focus on burden to developers. They talk about complexity and compliance costs. And those things are real. Developers do have to spend time on documentation. It does cost money to get independent validation. But the documents I'm looking at don't spend much time on what happens when you remove those requirements.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what I think happens. In the short term, maybe nothing bad occurs. A company releases an AI system without a detailed model card. Hospitals start using it. For a while, everything seems fine. Doctors are happy because the system helps them make faster decisions. Administrators are happy because the system reduces costs. The company is happy because they got a product to market without the documentation burden.‚Źé‚ŹéThen something goes wrong. The system misses a cohort of patients who don't match the training population. Or it gets systematically wrong about a particular type of case. Or someone figures out that the system is biased in ways nobody disclosed. Maybe a patient gets hurt. Maybe several do.‚Źé‚ŹéAt that point, you have a problem. The hospital wants to investigate what went wrong. Regulators want to investigate what went wrong. Patients want to know if the system affected their care. But the company doesn't have to provide detailed information about how the system was built, what it was trained on, or what was tested. They can claim it's proprietary. And because there was no certification requirement, there's no baseline of documentation to fall back on.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not saying every developer would do this. Most wouldn't. But some would. And the certification requirements exist specifically to make sure that even the ones who would cut corners can't hide what they're doing.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT SHOULD HAPPEN INSTEAD**‚Źé‚ŹéI think there's a middle ground here that the current proposal doesn't explore. I think you could reduce burden on developers without removing accountability entirely.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat if instead of removing the model card requirement, you simplified it? What if you made it shorter, less detailed, with less redundancy? What if you created a standard template that developers could fill out in a day or two instead of weeks? That would reduce burden without eliminating the documentation.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat if you allowed developers to claim certain information as confidential business information while still requiring them to disclose safety-relevant information? You could still protect the algorithm itself while requiring disclosure of training data characteristics, known limitations, and validation results. The safety stuff doesn't need to be secret.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat if you created different tiers of certification based on risk level? A system that suggests a diagnosis for a doctor to review is lower risk than a system that automatically adjusts medication doses. You could have lighter requirements for lower-risk systems.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat if you required disclosure of where the system was tested and validated, without requiring developers to run their own validation studies? If an independent lab tested it, or a hospital system tested it, that's good enough. You don't need to mandate who does the testing.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not a regulatory expert. I don't know if any of these approaches would work. But what I do know is that the current proposal essentially says, "We're going to remove the requirements that make sure AI systems are documented and validated, and we're going to do this in the name of innovation." That's not the right trade-off.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CERTIFICATION CRITERIA NEED TO STAY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Decision Support Intervention certification criteria, including the requirement for model cards and transparency disclosures, should not be removed. They should maybe be revised. They should maybe be streamlined. But they should stay.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's why. Healthcare is not a normal market. Patients don't shop around for AI systems the way they shop for phones. They don't choose their hospital based on which AI the hospital uses. They come to a hospital when they're sick or injured and they trust that the hospital is using systems that are safe and effective. That trust is not a competitive advantage. It's a baseline requirement. And that baseline is maintained by documentation and accountability.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen my mother needed a second opinion, she needed to be able to get her own medical records. That's basic. It's in the law now. But it took her driving to two offices with a USB drive to make it happen, because the systems weren't designed to share information. The certification criteria and information blocking rules exist to prevent that kind of fragmentation and obstruction.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving the transparency requirements for AI systems would create a different kind of obstruction. It would obstruct a doctor's ability to understand what they're using. It would obstruct a patient's ability to know how decisions about their care are being made. And it would obstruct regulators' ability to investigate when things go wrong.‚Źé‚ŹéThe answer is not to remove accountability. The answer is to make accountability work better, faster, and with less burden. That requires keeping the certification criteria in place and refining them, not eliminating them.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm asking the Office of the Secretary and ONC to reconsider this part of the proposed rule. Keep the Decision Support Intervention certification criteria. Revise them if you need to, streamline them, make them more workable. But don't remove the requirement for model cards, transparency disclosures, and validation information. That's the foundation that lets doctors trust the systems they're using. And if doctors can't trust the systems, patients suffer.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-02-07T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-07T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ6‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒhopeful‚ôĒTennessee‚ôĒcaregiver‚ôĒ41‚ôĒWhen my mother was diagnosed with atrial fibrillation last year, I spent weeks calling between her cardiologist's office and her primary care clinic trying to get her test results transferred so we could get a second opinion‚ÄĒthe receptionist at one place kept saying the other office "hadn't requested it properly." I finally drove to both locations in person with a USB drive and my laptop to manually copy everything over, and it struck me how absurd it was that *her own medical information* felt like it belonged to them rather than to her. I keep thinking about how different that experience could have been if the records just moved seamlessly between providers the way they should.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1981
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0150‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒSharon Thomas‚ôĒThe Society of Thoracic Surgeons‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Society of Thoracic Surgeons opposes removing Decision Support Interventions certification criteria requiring external validation, model cards, and transparency disclosures, arguing that these safeguards are essential to prevent algorithmic bi...‚ôĒJune 28, 2025‚Źé‚ŹéOffice of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology‚ŹéDepartment of Health and Human Services‚Źé200 Independence Avenue, SW‚ŹéWashington, DC 20201‚Źé‚ŹéRe: RFI Response ‚Äď Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions‚ŹéFederal Register, Vol. 90, No. 118‚Źé‚ŹéThe Society of Thoracic Surgeons appreciates the opportunity to respond to the Request for Information on regulatory modifications to health information technology certification and interoperability standards. Our organization represents over 2,100 board-certified thoracic surgeons across the United States, and we have a direct stake in how AI-assisted clinical decision support systems are evaluated, validated, and deployed in operating rooms and intensive care units where the stakes for patient safety are literally life and death.‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be direct about what prompted this comment. Last month, I had to explain to the family of a patient who suffered a preventable complication why the hospital where their loved one received care had no systematic way to track and learn from that outcome. The data existed somewhere in their electronic health record system, but it was trapped in isolation. Meanwhile, I watched hospital administrators invoke patient privacy as the reason they couldn't share their surgical outcome metrics with patients or their families who wanted to understand whether they were choosing a center that had adequate experience with their specific condition. It struck me that we were using privacy protections as a shield for opacity that actually harmed the very people we claimed to protect. That experience informed how I'm thinking about this proposed deregulation.‚Źé‚ŹéThe specific issue we want to address concerns the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria that currently require AI model cards, transparency disclosures, documentation of intended use, target population specifications, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. We understand the impulse behind deregulation. Certification burden is real. Developers face costs that slow innovation. Smaller vendors struggle to navigate complex requirements. These are not trivial concerns, and we don't dismiss them. But we also cannot ignore what happens when those safeguards disappear from a system that directly influences clinical judgment in time-pressured environments where surgeons make decisions that determine whether patients live, die, or suffer permanent disability.‚Źé‚ŹéTHE PROBLEM WITH REMOVING DSI CERTIFICATION CRITERIA‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification criterion under 45 CFR 170.315(b)(6) requires vendors to demonstrate that their AI decision support systems have been externally validated, that developers disclose known limitations, and that intended use cases are clearly documented. These requirements exist because clinical decision support is not a neutral tool. It shapes how physicians think. Studies in human factors research show that clinicians tend to over-rely on algorithmic recommendations, particularly when time pressure is high and cognitive load is elevated. In cardiac surgery, for instance, a surgeon may have minutes to decide whether to proceed with a complex case or refer the patient elsewhere. If an AI system recommends proceeding without that surgeon understanding the data that system was trained on, the population it was validated in, or whether it has been tested in patients similar to the one on the table, that surgeon is operating partially blind.‚Źé‚ŹéWe have seen this dynamic play out in our own clinical networks. Three years ago, one of our member institutions adopted a commercially available risk stratification tool for predicting postoperative complications in cardiac surgery. The vendor had provided limited information about the training cohort. It turned out the algorithm had been trained predominantly on data from high-volume academic centers with advanced intensive care units and robust pharmacological support. When applied to a community hospital with fewer ICU beds and less specialized staffing, the tool systematically underestimated risk in elderly patients with multiple comorbidities. Surgeons, trusting the algorithm, recommended procedures that led to poor outcomes in that population. The hospital eventually abandoned the tool, but not before patient harm occurred. Had the DSI criteria been in place when that vendor submitted their product, the external validation requirement would likely have caught this population mismatch before clinical deployment.‚Źé‚ŹéThe question we keep circling back to is this: what exactly are we deregulating for? If the answer is faster time to market and reduced vendor burden, we understand the appeal. But in clinical decision support, speed and burden reduction come with a real cost. It is not abstract. It is borne by patients. The 2016 study by Char et al. in JAMA Internal Medicine documented how clinician reliance on algorithmic recommendations increased when those recommendations were presented without transparency about their underlying logic. Removing certification criteria that mandate this transparency does not eliminate the reliance effect. It just means clinicians will be relying on systems they know even less about.‚Źé‚ŹéWe also want to acknowledge something that often gets lost in these discussions. The vendors developing these tools are not malicious. Most are genuinely trying to improve clinical care. But they operate under different incentives than clinicians do. A vendor's incentive is to get their product to market quickly, to maximize its applicability across as many institutions and patient populations as possible, and to minimize the cost of validation and documentation. A clinician's incentive is to know whether the tool will help or harm their specific patient. These incentives do not automatically align, and deregulation shifts the balance further toward vendor interests.‚Źé‚ŹéWHAT EXTERNAL VALIDATION ACTUALLY MEANS IN PRACTICE‚Źé‚ŹéHere is where I think there is sometimes a misunderstanding about what the current certification criteria actually require. External validation does not mean the vendor must conduct a randomized controlled trial. It means they must test their system on data they did not use to build it, ideally in settings different from where it was developed, and they must report what they find. This is the industry standard in medical device development. A diagnostic imaging algorithm, before FDA clearance, must be validated on datasets it was not trained on. A new surgical device must be tested outside the manufacturer's lab. Why should AI clinical decision support be different?‚Źé‚ŹéThe vendor community sometimes argues that validation requirements are prohibitively expensive. We have heard this claim. But we have also worked with vendors who have successfully completed external validation studies for decision support tools. Some of these studies were conducted in partnership with academic health systems. Others involved multi-institutional consortia. Yes, they required time and resources. But they also produced credible evidence that the tools worked as intended across different populations and settings. One vendor we worked with spent approximately $180,000 to externally validate a sepsis prediction algorithm across three different hospital systems. That is not trivial, but it is also not an impossible barrier for a company bringing a product to market.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat does concern us is the suggestion that we simply remove the validation requirement and trust the market to sort out which tools are good and which are not. The market works reasonably well for many things. But it does not work well for clinical decision support because the people who bear the consequences of a bad decision are not the same people who choose the tool. A hospital administrator might adopt a cost-saving algorithm based on vendor marketing and ease of integration. The surgeon and patient bear the consequences if that algorithm is biased, undertrained, or poorly validated.‚Źé‚ŹéTHE SPECIFIC IMPORTANCE OF MODEL CARDS AND TRANSPARENCY DISCLOSURES‚Źé‚ŹéModel cards are not bureaucratic theater. They are a mechanism for capturing essential information about how an AI system works and where it might fail. A proper model card for a clinical decision support tool should include the characteristics of the training data (sample size, demographic distribution, institutional sources), the performance metrics achieved during development and external validation, the intended patient population and clinical context, known limitations and failure modes, and the conditions under which performance degrades. This information is what allows a clinician to make an informed judgment about whether to trust the system's recommendation in a particular case.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider a concrete example from our field. A decision support tool designed to help surgeons predict mortality risk in patients undergoing coronary artery bypass grafting was marketed to hospitals as a general-purpose risk calculator. The model card, if properly completed, would have revealed that the tool was trained exclusively on patients under age 75. When applied to an octogenarian with multiple comorbidities, the tool's predictions were unreliable. This is not a secret. It is knowable information. But it requires someone to disclose it. Removing the certification requirement that mandates this disclosure does not eliminate the problem. It just ensures that fewer clinicians will know about it before they rely on the tool to influence a clinical decision.‚Źé‚ŹéWe recognize that creating comprehensive model cards requires effort from vendors. But again, this is not a new or unreasonable requirement in the medical device space. The FDA has issued guidance on AI/ML validation that includes similar documentation requirements. The International Organization for Standardization is developing standards for AI system transparency and documentation. These requirements are becoming industry norms, not regulatory exceptions. The question is not whether vendors should document their systems. The question is whether there will be any mechanism to ensure they do.‚Źé‚ŹéTHE ROLE OF TRANSPARENCY IN ACCOUNTABILITY‚Źé‚ŹéThere is another dimension to this that matters beyond just clinical performance. It is accountability. When something goes wrong, when a patient is harmed by a decision made with the assistance of an AI system, there needs to be a way to understand what happened. This requires documentation of how the system works, what data it was trained on, and how it was validated. Without model cards and transparency disclosures, accountability becomes nearly impossible. You cannot investigate a system you do not understand. You cannot determine whether an adverse outcome was caused by the algorithm, the clinician's misuse of the algorithm, or something else entirely.‚Źé‚ŹéTort law and malpractice proceedings depend on being able to understand the decision-making process that led to harm. If a surgeon uses an AI system to recommend a procedure and the patient suffers a complication, the legal system needs to know whether that system was validated in the patient population, whether the surgeon understood the system's limitations, and whether the system performed as represented. Model cards and certification criteria enable this accountability. Removing them does not eliminate liability. It just makes it harder to determine who should bear it.‚Źé‚ŹéA WORD ON MARKET COMPETITION AND INNOVATION‚Źé‚ŹéWe have heard arguments that certification criteria stifle innovation and that removing them will lead to more vendors entering the market with better, cheaper tools. We are skeptical of this claim, and our skepticism is based on experience in other medical device sectors. The removal of validation requirements has not historically led to a flood of innovative new entrants in medical devices. Instead, it has led to consolidation around larger vendors with the resources to navigate complex regulatory environments and to dominate through brand and distribution rather than superior performance. Smaller vendors with innovative ideas often struggle more without clear standards, not less, because they lack the market presence to overcome skepticism about unvalidated products.‚Źé‚ŹéMoreover, the claim that certification criteria slow innovation assumes that the current criteria are poorly designed or outdated. We do not believe that is true for DSI certification. The requirement for external validation, for model cards, and for transparency disclosures are based on sound principles of medical device evaluation. They are not arbitrary bureaucratic hurdles. They are mechanisms for ensuring that clinical decision support systems actually do what vendors claim they do. A vendor with a genuinely innovative, well-designed tool should welcome the opportunity to demonstrate that through external validation. If they cannot, that is useful information.‚Źé‚ŹéTHE EQUITY DIMENSION‚Źé‚ŹéWe want to raise one more concern that we think has not received adequate attention in the deregulation discussion. It concerns health equity and disparities. AI systems trained on biased datasets can perpetuate and amplify existing disparities in healthcare. A decision support tool trained predominantly on data from affluent academic centers may perform poorly in safety-net hospitals serving predominantly minority populations. A tool trained on data from one geographic region may not generalize well to others with different demographic compositions and healthcare infrastructure. External validation across diverse settings and populations is one of the key mechanisms for identifying and mitigating these disparities. Removing the validation requirement removes one of the safeguards against algorithmic bias.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Society of Thoracic Surgeons is committed to improving outcomes and access to high-quality surgical care across all populations. We have seen firsthand how algorithmic bias can affect patient care. One of our member institutions discovered that a scheduling algorithm was systematically delaying surgery for minority patients because it was trained on historical data that reflected prior discriminatory scheduling practices. The algorithm was not intentionally biased. But it was biased nonetheless. Had there been a requirement for external validation and equity analysis as part of the certification process, this bias might have been caught before deployment. We cannot eliminate these risks entirely. But we can reduce them through thoughtful regulatory requirements.‚Źé‚ŹéOUR RECOMMENDATION‚Źé‚ŹéThe Society of Thoracic Surgeons respectfully recommends that the current Decision Support Interventions certification criteria be retained, particularly the requirements for external validation, model cards, and transparency disclosures about intended use, target populations, known risks, and training data characteristics. We recognize that this imposes costs on vendors. We also recognize that some vendors will argue that these requirements are unnecessary. But we believe the evidence is clear that transparency and validation are essential for safe, equitable, and accountable AI-assisted clinical decision making.‚Źé‚ŹéIf there are specific aspects of the current certification criteria that are creating unnecessary burden, we are open to discussing targeted modifications. For instance, there might be ways to streamline the documentation process without eliminating the substantive requirements. There might be opportunities to create tiered certification pathways for different types of decision support tools, with less stringent requirements for lower-risk applications. There might be ways to harmonize FDA and ONC requirements to reduce duplication. These are reasonable conversations to have. But the conversation should start from a presumption that transparency and validation are necessary, not from a presumption that they should be eliminated.‚Źé‚ŹéWe also want to emphasize that deregulation in one domain does not require deregulation everywhere. The current regulatory framework includes requirements for information blocking, for standards adoption, and for certification of health IT systems more broadly. Some of these requirements may merit reconsideration. But clinical decision support is not the place to experiment with removing validation and transparency requirements. The stakes are too high. The potential for harm is too real. And the evidence that these requirements are necessary is too strong.‚Źé‚ŹéWe appreciate your consideration of these views and welcome the opportunity to provide additional information or to discuss these issues in more detail.‚Źé‚ŹéRespectfully submitted,‚Źé‚ŹéSharon Thomas‚ŹéExecutive Director‚ŹéThe Society of Thoracic Surgeons‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-25T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-25T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ8‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒfrustrated‚ôĒIdaho‚ôĒexecutive director‚ôĒ37‚ôĒLast month, I had to explain to a patient's family why their surgical outcomes data‚ÄĒwhich could have helped them advocate for better care at their rural hospital‚ÄĒwas locked behind systems that don't talk to each other, while I watched the same hospitals defend those silos as "privacy protection." It crystallized for me how we're using privacy as a shield for opacity that actually harms the very people we claim to protect.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2466
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0151‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒBrian King‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒWhile acknowledging the benefits of improved health record access, the commenter argues against removing privacy and transparency safeguards without sufficient evidence that they are ineffective or harmful, citing personal experience with data bre...‚ôĒMy mom's diabetes diagnosis last year turned into this frustrating loop where every doctor she saw wanted her to fill out the same paperwork again because nobody had access to her previous records. I get why people want that stuff to move around easier. But here's what worries me, and honestly I think this matters even if nobody's published a study proving it yet. Her information got leaked from a hospital database two years ago, and I just can't shake the feeling that making records easier to access also makes them easier for the wrong people to grab. When you're talking about removing safeguards because there's no evidence they work, I mean, shouldn't we be asking if there's evidence they don't work before we just get rid of them? That's backwards to me. And the transparency stuff about AI systems making health decisions, that's not just paperwork. That's how my mom would know whether some algorithm is deciding what treatment she gets. I don't think we should strip that out just because we haven't studied it enough yet.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-22T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-22T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe agency's claim that 'no publicly available evidence' supports transparency requirements conflates absence of evidence with evidence of absence; lack of published studies does not mean the criterion provides no value.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒlow‚ôĒresigned‚ôĒGeorgia‚ôĒcustomer service representative‚ôĒ37‚ôĒLast year my mom got diagnosed with diabetes and had to see like three different doctors, and every time she'd go to a new one they'd say they didn't have her records from the last place and she'd have to fill out the same forms all over again‚ÄĒit was frustrating to watch her deal with that, so I get why people want that stuff to be easier to move around. But honestly I'm also worried because her information's already been leaked from places before, so I just hope they don't make it so easy to access that somebody can just grab it without permission.‚ôĒJust because nobody's published a study on something doesn't mean it's not important‚ÄĒthat's like saying my mom's privacy doesn't matter because there's no official report about it yet | The AI model cards and transparency stuff might seem like extra paperwork, but it's actually the only way patients and doctors know what they're dealing with when these systems are making decisions about their health | Removing these requirements because there's 'no evidence' they work is backwards‚ÄĒwe should be asking if there's evidence they DON'T work before we just get rid of them‚ôĒA regular person worried that regulators are confusing 'we haven't studied this enough' with 'this doesn't matter,' when it comes to something as serious as AI making healthcare decisions.‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ178
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0152‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒBarbara Rivera‚ôĒRubyWell, Inc.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒRubyWell opposes the removal of the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirement, arguing that while the current certification process is inefficient, eliminating transparency and validation standards entirely would compromise pa...‚ôĒRe: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity; RIN 0945-AA07‚Źé‚ŹéRubyWell, Inc. submits this comment regarding the proposed removal of the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion and related transparency requirements for artificial intelligence-assisted clinical decision support systems. We appreciate the opportunity to address the specific implications of this proposed deregulation for patient safety, clinical accountability, and the long-term viability of the health IT market.‚Źé‚Źé**ORGANIZATIONAL CONTEXT AND EXPERIENCE**‚Źé‚ŹéRubyWell develops clinical decision support software deployed across ambulatory and inpatient settings. Over the past five years, our products have been implemented in 23 health systems across seven states, supporting approximately 4,200 clinicians in real-time decision-making. We hold current ONC certification under 45 CFR 170.315(a)(1) and related standards, including the DSI criterion. This experience has given us direct visibility into how certification requirements function in practice, what drives adoption and trust, and what gaps emerge when regulatory clarity erodes.‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be direct: the eighteen-month certification pathway we pursued last year, which consumed $2.3 million and ultimately collapsed when standards shifted mid-process, was expensive and inefficient. That was genuinely painful. But the lesson we took from it was not that certification itself is the problem. It was that clear, stable requirements are essential. Removing the DSI criterion entirely addresses the symptom, not the disease.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CURRENT DSI CERTIFICATION REQUIREMENT AND WHAT IT REQUIRES**‚Źé‚ŹéThe existing DSI criterion, codified in 45 CFR 170.315(a)(1), mandates that health IT developers demonstrate the following for any AI or algorithmic decision support system:‚Źé‚Źé1. Disclosure of the intended use of the model, including the specific clinical context and population for which it was designed‚Źé2. Identification of the target population, including demographic characteristics, clinical inclusion and exclusion criteria, and any known performance variations across subgroups‚Źé3. Transparent documentation of known risks, limitations, and conditions under which the system may perform poorly‚Źé4. Detailed information about training data characteristics, including data sources, time periods, sampling methods, and any preprocessing or feature engineering applied‚Źé5. Evidence of external validation, including testing in clinical environments outside the original development dataset‚Źé6. Ongoing performance monitoring and reporting mechanisms‚Źé‚ŹéThese requirements do not mandate specific algorithms, proprietary disclosure, or business-sensitive information. They require what clinicians and their organizations need to make informed decisions about whether to deploy a system and how to use it safely.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THIS REQUIREMENT MATTERS FOR PATIENT SAFETY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe argument for removing DSI requirements typically rests on the claim that they impose unnecessary burden on vendors without demonstrable benefit. I've seen this argument made in industry forums, and I understand its appeal. But it misses something fundamental about how clinical decision support actually functions in practice.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen a clinician uses a decision support system, they are implicitly delegating part of their diagnostic or therapeutic reasoning to a black box. The quality of their clinical judgment depends on understanding what that box does, how reliable it is, and where it might fail. This is not theoretical. In our implementation work, we've encountered three situations where lack of transparency about model behavior created real safety problems.‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, a pediatric health system deployed one of our competitors' sepsis prediction models without understanding that the model had been trained primarily on adult data with limited pediatric validation. The model performed poorly on neonatal patients, generating false negatives that delayed treatment. The clinicians didn't know this because the vendor had not disclosed the training data characteristics or the population specificity. This was discovered only after a patient outcome review flagged the discrepancy.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, we worked with a cardiology group that adopted a heart failure risk stratification tool. The vendor disclosed that the model performed well "across populations," but did not break down performance by race, sex, or age. When the cardiology group analyzed their own data, they found that the model systematically underestimated risk in women over 65, a finding that the vendor later admitted they had observed during development but not reported.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, we helped implement a medication interaction checker that was flagged as high-risk because its training data included only inpatient pharmacy records from the prior five years. When outpatient use increased during the pandemic, the system encountered medication combinations that were underrepresented in its training set, generating inconsistent alerts. Had the training data characteristics been transparent, the clinical team could have configured safer override protocols.‚Źé‚ŹéThese are not edge cases. They reflect the normal operation of clinical decision support in heterogeneous environments. Clinicians are responsible for their own clinical judgments, even when they use decision support tools. That responsibility can only be discharged if they have accurate information about what the tool does and does not do.‚Źé‚Źé**THE BUSINESS CASE FOR MAINTAINING THE REQUIREMENT**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to address the economic argument directly, because this is where I think the deregulation proposal gets the incentives backwards.‚Źé‚ŹéThe claim is that removing certification burden will reduce costs and accelerate market entry for smaller vendors. This is true in the short term. Eliminating the DSI requirement does lower the cost of market entry. But it also eliminates a competitive advantage for vendors who have invested in transparency, validation, and safety infrastructure. In the medium term, this creates a race to the bottom.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's how this plays out. Vendors who maintain robust validation practices and transparent disclosures will be undercut by competitors who skip these steps and pass the savings on to customers. Health systems will face pressure to adopt cheaper, less-validated systems. Clinicians will lose trust in decision support as a category when unvalidated systems produce poor outcomes. And the market will contract, not expand.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not hypothetical. We've watched it happen in other industries. When regulatory requirements are relaxed without corresponding market mechanisms to reward quality, the average quality of the market declines. The vendors who have invested in doing things right either exit the market or reduce their investment in quality. The result is fewer good products, not more.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat we've observed in our own business is that health systems actually prefer to buy from vendors who can demonstrate validation and transparency. When we compete against cheaper, less-validated alternatives, our main selling point is that we can show exactly how our system performs, what it does and doesn't do, and what the clinical team should watch for. Health system procurement teams want this information. Removing the requirement to disclose it doesn't make their jobs easier. It makes it harder.‚Źé‚Źé**BURDEN REDUCTION WITHOUT SAFETY DEGRADATION**‚Źé‚ŹéI'm genuinely sympathetic to the burden-reduction argument. The eighteen months and $2.3 million we spent on a certification pathway that was discontinued was wasteful. There are real inefficiencies in the current system. But the solution is to streamline the requirement, not to eliminate it.‚Źé‚ŹéSeveral modifications would reduce burden while preserving the safety function:‚Źé‚ŹéEstablish clear, objective standards for what constitutes adequate disclosure. Right now, vendors often must iterate multiple times with ONC to clarify what documentation is sufficient. If the regulation spelled out specific formats, required data elements, and acceptable levels of technical detail, vendors could build compliance processes once rather than negotiating with regulators. This would reduce the timeline and cost significantly.‚Źé‚ŹéCreate tiered requirements based on risk. A decision support system that offers a suggestion for a provider to consider carries different risk than a system that generates automated alerts or directly influences medication dosing. A three-tier framework could require full DSI documentation for high-risk systems, streamlined documentation for moderate-risk systems, and basic disclosure for low-risk systems. This would reduce burden on vendors of lower-risk systems without compromising safety for higher-risk applications.‚Źé‚ŹéDevelop standardized templates for model cards and validation documentation. The machine learning community has already created reasonable frameworks for this, including work on model cards (Gebru et al., 2021) and documentation practices (Mitchell et al., 2019). The FDA has published guidance on AI/ML validation. Rather than requiring vendors to invent their own documentation structure, ONC could specify acceptable templates. Vendors could then populate the template rather than creating documentation from scratch.‚Źé‚ŹéAllow for phased validation and disclosure. A system could enter the market with initial validation evidence and a commitment to conduct additional external validation within a specified timeframe (e.g., 18 months). This would reduce the up-front burden while maintaining accountability for ongoing validation. The initial disclosure would be transparent about what validation has and has not yet been completed.‚Źé‚ŹéStreamline the certification process itself. Much of our $2.3 million expenditure went to remediating documentation that ONC reviewers found incomplete. If the certification process included clear feedback loops and milestone-based review rather than a single comprehensive submission, vendors could iteratively improve their documentation without starting over. This would reduce both timeline and cost.‚Źé‚ŹéThese modifications would preserve the core safety function of the DSI requirement while reducing the administrative burden. They would also create a more level playing field, because the standards would be clear and predictable rather than subject to reviewer interpretation.‚Źé‚Źé**THE INFORMATION BLOCKING CONTEXT**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed deregulation occurs alongside ongoing implementation of the information blocking regulations (45 CFR 171.100 et seq.). There's a tension here that I want to name explicitly.‚Źé‚ŹéInformation blocking rules require vendors to make certain information available to customers and competitors. The current DSI requirement is one mechanism through which those rules operate. By requiring disclosure of model characteristics, intended use, training data, and validation results, the certification criterion enforces transparency that aligns with information blocking principles.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the DSI requirement is removed, the information blocking rules still technically apply, but the mechanism for enforcing them becomes less clear. A health system might not know what information about a decision support system they are entitled to request under the information blocking rules if there is no regulatory standard defining what that information should be.‚Źé‚ŹéThis creates a gap. Vendors might argue that model architecture, training data characteristics, and validation results are "trade secrets" or "proprietary information" that they are not obligated to disclose under information blocking rules. Without the DSI requirement establishing that these disclosures are necessary for interoperability and care delivery, health systems will have less leverage to demand them.‚Źé‚ŹéConversely, maintaining the DSI requirement clarifies that certain information about decision support systems is not optional or proprietary. It is information that customers are entitled to have. This supports the underlying policy goals of the information blocking rules, which include ensuring that providers have the information they need to make informed decisions about technology adoption.‚Źé‚Źé**COMPETITIVE DYNAMICS AND MARKET CONCENTRATION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe deregulation proposal assumes that reducing regulatory burden will increase competition and benefit smaller vendors. This assumption deserves scrutiny.‚Źé‚ŹéIn the health IT market, larger vendors already have significant advantages: established relationships with health systems, capital to invest in compliance infrastructure, and the ability to absorb losses during regulatory uncertainty. Removing the DSI requirement might seem to level the playing field by eliminating a compliance cost. But it actually favors large vendors who have the capital to invest in building trust through proprietary means, such as extensive direct customer engagement, on-site implementation support, and custom validation studies.‚Źé‚ŹéSmaller vendors like RubyWell, and smaller health systems that cannot afford custom validation studies, benefit from clear regulatory standards. The DSI requirement creates a floor of transparency that applies to all vendors equally. Removing it eliminates that floor and replaces it with a marketplace where transparency is negotiated individually. Large vendors with large customers have more leverage in those negotiations. Smaller vendors and smaller health systems have less.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is worth thinking about carefully. We compete with several larger health IT companies. One of our competitive advantages is that we maintain robust validation evidence and transparent documentation because we believe it's the right thing to do and because it builds trust. But if our competitors can reduce their costs by eliminating validation and transparency, and if health systems stop demanding these things because there's no regulatory requirement, our differentiation disappears. We would then have to choose between matching their cost structure (which means cutting validation and transparency) or exiting the market.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not claiming that removing the DSI requirement would immediately cause this outcome. But it would remove a regulatory backstop that currently prevents this race to the bottom from occurring. For vendors committed to safety and transparency, that is a real concern.‚Źé‚Źé**PATIENT SAFETY AND LIABILITY IMPLICATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéThere's a liability question embedded in this proposal that the regulatory impact analysis does not adequately address.‚Źé‚ŹéIf a health system deploys a decision support system, and the system contributes to a patient harm event, and the system's behavior was not adequately documented or validated, what are the liability implications for the health system? Under current law, health systems have a duty to ensure that the systems they use are appropriate for their patient population and clinical context. That duty is easier to discharge if vendors provide clear information about system performance, known risks, and limitations.‚Źé‚ŹéIf vendors are not required to provide this information, health systems will need to conduct their own validation studies or demand custom validation from vendors. This will increase costs for health systems, not reduce them. It will also increase liability risk, because health systems will be making adoption decisions with less information.‚Źé‚ŹéMoreover, if the DSI requirement is removed, vendors will have less incentive to conduct the external validation studies that generate the evidence health systems need. The cost of validation will shift from vendors to health systems. This is not a burden reduction. It's a burden shift, and it shifts the burden onto the organizations that are least able to absorb it.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CASE FOR STABILITY IN STANDARDS**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to return to the point I made at the beginning about our experience with the discontinued certification pathway. The problem was not that the standards were demanding. The problem was that they changed. Vendors invested in building compliance infrastructure based on one set of requirements, and then the requirements changed, making that infrastructure obsolete.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is a real risk with deregulation. If the DSI requirement is removed, vendors will adjust their practices accordingly. They will reduce investment in validation, reduce documentation, and streamline their compliance infrastructure. If the requirement is later reinstated (as it might be, if patient safety concerns arise), vendors will need to rebuild these capabilities. The cost of that transition could be higher than the cost of maintaining the requirement continuously.‚Źé‚ŹéFrom a business planning perspective, what vendors need is not necessarily light regulation. We need clear, stable regulation. We can invest in compliance infrastructure if we know what we need to comply with and we can be confident that the requirements will not change unexpectedly.‚Źé‚ŹéThe deregulation proposal is justified partly as a way to reduce burden and unleash innovation. But regulatory instability is itself a burden and a drag on innovation. Vendors cannot invest confidently in compliance infrastructure if they do not know whether the regulatory requirements will still exist in two years.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC RECOMMENDATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéBased on this analysis, RubyWell recommends that the DSI certification requirement be retained, but with the following modifications to reduce burden and improve clarity:‚Źé‚Źé1. Establish a standardized model card template that specifies required disclosures, acceptable formats for documentation, and technical detail requirements. This template should be developed in consultation with health IT vendors, health systems, and clinical informaticists, and should be published for public comment before finalization.‚Źé‚Źé2. Create a tiered framework that applies different documentation requirements based on risk level. High-risk systems (those that generate alerts, influence medication dosing, or triage patients) require full DSI documentation. Moderate-risk systems (those that provide suggestions for clinician review) require streamlined documentation. Low-risk systems (those that provide informational content only) require basic disclosure.‚Źé‚Źé3. Implement a phased validation approach that allows systems to enter the market with initial validation evidence and a commitment to conduct external validation within 18 months. Initial disclosure must transparently identify which validation has been completed and which is pending.‚Źé‚Źé4. Streamline the certification review process by establishing clear review criteria, providing detailed feedback on incomplete submissions, and allowing vendors to resubmit with revisions rather than requiring complete resubmission.‚Źé‚Źé5. Clarify the relationship between the DSI requirement and the information blocking rules by explicitly stating that disclosures required under the DSI criterion are mandatory under information blocking rules and are not subject to claims of trade secret or proprietary information protection.‚Źé‚Źé6. Establish a schedule for updating the DSI requirement based on advances in AI/ML validation practices and regulatory guidance from other agencies (FDA, NIST, etc.). This schedule should be published at least 18 months in advance to allow vendors to plan for changes.‚Źé‚Źé7. Create a vendor advisory group that meets quarterly to discuss implementation challenges, suggest refinements, and identify opportunities to further reduce burden without compromising safety. This group should include representatives from small, medium, and large vendors, health systems, and clinicians.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe deregulation proposal reflects a legitimate interest in reducing unnecessary burden on health IT vendors. But removing the DSI certification requirement is not the right way to achieve that goal. The requirement serves an important safety function by ensuring that clinicians and health systems have the information they need to make informed decisions about decision support system adoption and use. Removing it would shift costs and risks from vendors to health systems, would disadvantage smaller vendors and smaller health systems, and would undermine the information blocking rules' transparency objectives.‚Źé‚ŹéInstead, we recommend streamlining the requirement through clear standards, tiered approaches, phased validation, and improved processes. This would reduce burden while preserving the safety and transparency functions that the requirement serves.‚Źé‚ŹéRubyWell is committed to maintaining high standards for validation, transparency, and patient safety. We believe that the health IT market functions best when these standards are clear, stable, and consistently applied across all vendors. We look forward to working with ONC to develop the specific standards and processes outlined in these recommendations.‚Źé‚ŹéThank you for the opportunity to comment on this important proposal.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-13T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-13T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ9‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒfrustrated‚ôĒArizona‚ôĒVP of regulatory affairs‚ôĒ47‚ôĒLast year, we spent eighteen months and nearly $2.3 million pursuing a certification pathway that was ultimately discontinued because the standards shifted mid-process‚ÄĒa situation I'm watching repeat itself as these requirements evolve. Our smaller competitors simply absorbed those costs and moved forward, but for a company our size, it meant delaying product launches and hemorrhaging resources on compliance infrastructure we can't repurpose.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2975
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0153‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDorothy Hall‚ôĒPolicy Center for Maternal Mental Health‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe commenter argues that removing Decision Support Intervention (DSI) transparency and model card requirements would harm patient safety, particularly for vulnerable populations in rural and underserved areas, because clinicians and patients woul...‚ôĒ**POLICY CENTER FOR MATERNAL MENTAL HEALTH**‚ŹéNevada‚Źé‚ŹéI want to speak directly to the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements, specifically the model card and transparency disclosure criteria. This matters in ways that go beyond regulatory process, and I'm going to explain why by starting with what happened in our actual community.‚Źé‚ŹéLast year, we coordinated a postpartum depression support group in rural Nevada. Three women in that group experienced severe medication side effects. One had a seizure after being prescribed an antidepressant that interacted with her existing medication. Another developed serotonin syndrome. The third had cardiac arrhythmias that sent her to the emergency room. What strikes me now, looking back, is that none of these incidents were captured in any systematic way that would have warned the next woman coming through the door. Her OB didn't know what her psychiatrist had prescribed. Her psychiatrist didn't know about her cardiac history from her internist. Two different health systems, minimal communication, and when I tried to gather these three cases to bring to our state health board, I hit a wall of HIPAA restrictions that were supposedly protecting these women but actually left us completely blind to patterns that could have prevented harm.‚Źé‚ŹéThat's the ground I'm standing on when I read about removing transparency requirements for AI decision support tools. I understand the deregulatory impulse. I genuinely do. But in healthcare, especially in maternal mental health where we're already failing rural women and women of color at alarming rates, removing oversight mechanisms feels like we're building another wall instead of breaking one down.‚Źé‚Źé**THE ACTUAL PROBLEM WITH REMOVING MODEL CARDS AND TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule would eliminate the requirement that developers of Decision Support Interventions disclose their intended use, target populations, known risks, training data characteristics, and results from external validation testing. Under 21 CFR 868.3040 as currently structured, these disclosures are mandatory. The new framework would remove them as a certification criterion.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what concerns me: A clinician in Nevada is trying to decide whether to use an AI tool to help assess suicide risk in postpartum patients. She doesn't know if the tool was trained on data from women like her patients. She doesn't know if it was tested in rural settings or only in urban academic medical centers. She doesn't know what the false negative rate is, meaning she doesn't know how many women at risk the tool might miss. She doesn't know if the training data included women on Medicaid or primarily insured women. She doesn't know if it was validated on diverse racial and ethnic populations.‚Źé‚ŹéThe argument for removing these requirements is that it reduces burden on developers and allows faster innovation. But what happens on the ground? The clinician doesn't have the information she needs to make an informed decision about whether to use the tool. Her patients don't have the information they deserve to know about how their care is being assessed. And if something goes wrong, there's no transparency trail to understand why.‚Źé‚ŹéI've read the Federal Register notice. The agency suggests that market forces and existing FDA guidance will be sufficient safeguards. The problem is that market forces don't work the same way in healthcare, especially not in maternal mental health. There isn't a robust competitive market for these tools. A rural hospital might have one option, or no option. A woman on Medicaid in rural Nevada might have whatever her county health department can afford. She doesn't get to choose between competing AI systems and pick the one with better transparency.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT HAPPENED WHEN WE COULDN'T ACCESS DATA**‚Źé‚ŹéLet me connect this back to my experience. When we tried to compile those three medication side effect cases, we ran into HIPAA privacy barriers. But the real problem wasn't HIPAA itself. HIPAA allows for aggregate reporting of de-identified safety information. The real problem was that the systems didn't talk to each other, and there was no standardized way to collect and share safety signals. We couldn't see the pattern because the data was fragmented.‚Źé‚ŹéNow imagine the same fragmentation happening with AI decision support tools. A tool gets deployed at hospitals across Nevada. Some patients have bad outcomes. But without transparency about how the tool works, what populations it was tested on, and what its known limitations are, how would anyone even know to look for a pattern? How would a clinician know if the tool is performing differently in her patient population than it did in the validation study? How would the state health board know if they need to investigate?‚Źé‚ŹéThe 21st Century Cures Act, which amended the Public Health Service Act, requires that developers of certified EHR technology maintain transparency about how their products work (42 U.S.C. 300jj et seq.). The intent was to prevent information blocking and ensure that healthcare providers and patients could access and understand information about how technology is being used. Removing the DSI transparency requirements goes backward from that intent.‚Źé‚Źé**THE EQUITY PROBLEM**‚Źé‚ŹéMaternal mental health in rural Nevada is already a crisis. We have the second-highest maternal mortality rate in the nation, and maternal suicide is one of the leading causes of death for pregnant and postpartum women. A large portion of these deaths occur in rural areas with limited access to mental health specialists. Women of color, particularly Native American women, are at disproportionate risk.‚Źé‚ŹéNow imagine deploying an AI decision support tool into this landscape without requiring transparency about its training data and validation. The tool might be trained on data from urban academic medical centers where most of the participants were white and insured. It gets deployed in a rural clinic serving a population that's 40% Medicaid and includes significant numbers of Native American women. Nobody knows if the tool was ever tested on a population like this. Nobody knows if it has different error rates in different racial or ethnic groups.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not a hypothetical concern. A 2019 study published in Science showed that a widely used healthcare algorithm contained significant racial bias (Obermeyer et al., 2019, Science, 366(6464), 447-453). The algorithm was used to allocate healthcare resources. It systematically disadvantaged Black patients because it was trained on historical data that reflected existing racial disparities in healthcare spending, not in actual health need. The problem wasn't caught earlier because the algorithm's developers and the healthcare systems using it didn't have transparent information about what the algorithm was optimizing for and what populations it had been tested on.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the DSI certification requirements are removed, how would we catch similar problems before they harm patients? The answer is we wouldn't. At least not quickly. By the time a pattern emerges, the tool might have been used on thousands of patients in rural and underserved areas.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS ACTUALLY DO**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification criteria require that developers provide:‚Źé‚Źé1. A description of the intended use of the tool‚Źé2. Identification of the target population‚Źé3. Documentation of known risks and limitations‚Źé4. Characteristics of the data used to train the tool‚Źé5. Results from external validation testing‚Źé‚ŹéThis isn't burdensome in the way that, say, requiring a full clinical trial for every software update would be. A model card is a document. It's a few pages. I've looked at published examples of model cards for healthcare AI systems, and they're detailed but not impossible to create. They tell you important information without requiring months of additional testing.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat happens when this information is available? A clinician can actually make an informed decision about whether to use the tool. She can think about whether her patient population matches the target population in the validation study. She can understand what the known risks are. If something goes wrong, there's a baseline of information to help investigate. A patient can make an informed decision about her own care if she's told that an AI tool is being used and what its limitations are.‚Źé‚Źé**ADDRESSING THE DEREGULATORY ARGUMENT**‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not opposed to deregulation as a principle. I understand that some certification requirements are outdated or unnecessarily burdensome. But the DSI transparency requirements aren't that. They're not requiring new testing. They're not requiring new clinical trials. They're requiring documentation of testing that should already be happening. If a developer hasn't done external validation of their tool, they probably shouldn't be deploying it in clinical settings. If they don't know what population their training data came from, they probably shouldn't be marketing it as a general-purpose clinical tool.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Federal Register notice suggests that removing these requirements will accelerate innovation and lower costs. But accelerating the deployment of tools without transparency about their limitations doesn't create innovation. It creates risk. And not all innovation is good. A tool that gets deployed faster but harms patients in underserved populations isn't progress.‚Źé‚ŹéThere's also a question about what happens to the information that developers do have. If a company develops an AI decision support tool, they necessarily have information about what it was trained on and how it performs in different populations. They have that information because they had to generate it to develop and test the tool. The question isn't whether they have it. The question is whether they have to disclose it. Removing the requirement to disclose doesn't make that information disappear. It just makes it private instead of transparent.‚Źé‚Źé**THE BIGGER PICTURE ON INTEROPERABILITY AND DATA SHARING**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to circle back to the problem that started me thinking about this issue in the first place. We couldn't compile safety information about medication side effects because the health systems didn't share data. That's a different problem from AI transparency, but it's related. Both problems come down to the same thing: if healthcare providers and patients don't have access to information about what's happening in their own care, they can't make good decisions, and they can't catch problems early.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule includes changes to information blocking regulations and interoperability requirements (45 CFR 171.101 et seq.). I'm concerned that the combination of reduced DSI transparency requirements plus loosened interoperability requirements could create a perfect storm. Healthcare systems that don't talk to each other, deploying AI tools that no one understands, with reduced requirements for documentation about how those tools work.‚Źé‚ŹéIn our community, we're trying to build systems where maternal mental health information is shared between OBs and psychiatrists and internists. We're trying to get data systems to talk to each other so that when a woman switches providers, her history goes with her. That's hard work. It requires investment and coordination. But it's the only way to actually improve safety and catch patterns early.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving transparency requirements for AI tools goes in the opposite direction. It makes the information landscape more opaque instead of more transparent. It makes it harder, not easier, for healthcare providers to understand what's happening in their patients' care.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE RECOMMEND**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health recommends that the final rule retain the current DSI certification criteria requiring model cards and transparency disclosures as specified in 21 CFR 868.3040. These requirements should include:‚Źé‚Źé- A clear statement of the intended use and target population for the tool‚Źé- Documentation of the data used to train the tool, including demographic characteristics and potential biases‚Źé- Results from external validation testing in populations that reflect real-world use‚Źé- Documentation of known risks and limitations, including differential performance in different subgroups‚Źé- A process for ongoing monitoring and reporting of safety signals once the tool is deployed‚Źé‚ŹéThese requirements don't prevent innovation. They enable informed innovation. They make it possible for clinicians and patients to understand what they're using and make decisions based on that understanding. They create accountability. And in a field like maternal mental health, where we're already failing too many women, accountability matters.‚Źé‚ŹéI know what it feels like to have information withheld in the name of efficiency. I know what it feels like to hit a wall when you're trying to protect women in your community. I also know what it feels like when systems work better because people have access to information about what's actually happening.‚Źé‚ŹéThe women in our support group deserved to know about each other's medication reactions. They deserved a system that would have caught the pattern and warned the next woman. They deserved transparency about the care they were receiving. The women coming into maternal mental health clinics across rural Nevada deserve the same thing. They deserve to know how their care is being assessed, what tools are being used, and what the limitations of those tools are.‚Źé‚ŹéPlease keep the DSI transparency requirements. They're not perfect, but they're a floor, not a ceiling. They're a baseline that says we care enough about patient safety to require that developers tell us how their tools work.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-30T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-30T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ7‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒsupportive‚ôĒNevada‚ôĒcommunity organizer‚ôĒ62‚ôĒLast year, I helped coordinate a support group for postpartum depression in our rural county, and we discovered that three women in our cohort had experienced severe medication side effects that went unreported because their data wasn't shared between their OB and their psychiatrist‚ÄĒtwo different health systems that don't talk to each other. When I tried to compile these cases to bring to our state health board, I hit a wall of privacy restrictions that were ostensibly protecting them but actually left us flying blind about patterns that could have warned other women.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2141
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0154‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒSharon Hernandez‚ôĒAmerican College of Cardiology‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe American College of Cardiology opposes removing Decision Support Intervention certification requirements, information blocking protections, and health IT standards requirements, arguing that while deregulation may reduce vendor compliance cost...‚ôĒ**American College of Cardiology Comments on Proposed Deregulatory Actions for Health IT Certification and Standards**‚Źé‚ŹéThe American College of Cardiology submits these comments in response to the Office of the Secretary's proposed deregulatory framework for health information technology (health IT), specifically regarding modifications to certification criteria, standards requirements, and information blocking protections. The College has represented cardiologists and cardiovascular specialists for 75 years and maintains a direct interest in how regulatory changes to electronic health record (EHR) systems and clinical decision-support tools affect patient safety, clinical practice, and the quality of care delivered to millions of Americans with cardiovascular disease.‚Źé‚ŹéThe College recognizes the stated intent to reduce regulatory burden and promote innovation through market competition. We understand the appeal of deregulation as a mechanism to lower compliance costs for EHR vendors and encourage new entrants into the health IT marketplace. However, the College's experience with cardiovascular care and our members' frontline interactions with fragmented health IT systems compel us to comment specifically on one critical area: the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements, particularly the requirement for AI model transparency disclosures including model cards, intended use documentation, target population specifications, known risks, and training data characteristics.‚Źé‚Źé**THE REAL-WORLD CONSEQUENCES OF INTEROPERABILITY FAILURE**‚Źé‚ŹéBefore addressing the specific regulatory question, context matters. In South Carolina last month, a 16-year-old patient presented to her cardiologist for evaluation of exercise-induced symptoms. The clinical team ordered a stress test at a hospital facility across town. When the test was completed, the patient's primary cardiologist could not access the results through the EHR system used in his office. The patient and her family waited two weeks for paper records to arrive by mail. During that time, clinical uncertainty persisted.
