CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0161‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒSteven Lee‚ôĒTexas Medical Association‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Texas Medical Association opposes removing AI model transparency requirements from Decision Support Interventions certification, arguing that standardized documentation of intended use, target populations, performance across demographic groups...‚ôĒTexas Medical Association Comments on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions‚Źé‚ŹéThe Texas Medical Association represents over 55,000 physicians and medical students across the state, and our members care for millions of Texans each year in settings ranging from rural clinics to major academic medical centers. We appreciate the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory actions under consideration by the Assistant Secretary for Technology Policy and the Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology.‚Źé‚ŹéWe write with a specific concern that has become impossible to ignore in our region: the gap between the legitimate need for better data interoperability and the absence of meaningful safeguards against the kind of patient privacy breaches that are becoming routine in the commercial data ecosystem. This isn't a hypothetical concern for us. Last year, a patient's family came to our office after learning that her elderly mother's psychiatric treatment history from three decades ago had been accessed by a data broker and sold to an employer wellness program without any meaningful consent. The family was devastated, but what troubled me more was our realization that existing regulatory frameworks provided almost no mechanism to prevent or remedy what had happened. We understand the regulatory burden argument. We also know that without careful attention to transparency and accountability in AI-assisted clinical decision support, we risk creating a two-tiered system where innovation accelerates but patient safety and informed consent get left behind.‚Źé‚Źé**The Case for Retaining AI Model Transparency Requirements**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria, specifically those requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures, concerns us deeply. These criteria currently require vendors to document intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. This sounds like bureaucratic overhead. In practice, it is the only structured requirement we have that forces developers to think systematically about what their tools actually do and who they work for.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider what happens in our rural Texas clinics. A physician in a small practice in west Texas may adopt an AI tool to help with diagnostic support because it reduces their administrative burden and improves efficiency. That physician needs to know: Was this model trained on a diverse patient population, or primarily on data from academic medical centers in urban areas? What are the documented failure rates in specific patient subgroups? What happens when the tool encounters a patient population it wasn't designed for? These are not abstract questions. They directly affect whether the tool helps or harms the patients in front of that physician. Without standardized transparency requirements, physicians have no reliable way to answer these questions. They're left dependent on marketing materials and vendor claims.‚Źé‚ŹéThe certification criteria we're discussing are not heavy regulatory burdens in the way that some have characterized them. The requirement to produce a model card is documentation that any responsible AI developer should be doing anyway. It's the difference between a vendor saying "our tool is accurate" and a vendor saying "our tool was validated on a dataset of 50,000 patients from 2018-2020 across 12 health systems, with an external validation study showing 94% sensitivity in the target population but 78% sensitivity in patients over 75." The second statement is useful. The first is marketing.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat we're asking for is not innovation prevention. It's transparency that allows clinicians to make informed decisions about whether to use a tool with their patients. This aligns directly with the principle of informed consent that underpins all of medicine. A patient should know, either directly or through their physician, whether a clinical decision affecting their care is being informed by an AI system and what that system's known limitations are.‚Źé‚Źé**Interoperability and Privacy Are Not Opposing Goals**‚Źé‚ŹéWe want to be clear about something: the Texas Medical Association supports improved health data interoperability. Our rural members desperately need better care coordination. When a patient moves between providers or seeks care at multiple locations, fragmented records compromise care quality and safety. We've seen cases where duplicate testing occurs, where medication interactions go undetected, where patients repeat their medical history to multiple providers because their records don't follow them. This is real harm, and it's disproportionately severe in rural areas where specialists are scarce and coordination is essential.‚Źé‚ŹéBut we also know, now more viscerally than before, that data interoperability without privacy protections creates a different kind of harm. The case that prompted our deeper engagement with these issues demonstrates exactly how this happens. The elderly patient whose psychiatric history was breached had her records in a provider's EHR. Those records then moved through a chain of actors (potentially a health information exchange, a data aggregator, a wellness vendor) and ended up in the hands of a commercial data broker. At no point in that chain did she meaningfully consent to this use. The regulatory framework that's supposed to prevent this (HIPAA's minimum necessary standard, information blocking rules, state privacy laws) proved inadequate because the flow of data happened through business associate relationships, de-identification procedures, and commercial transactions that fall into regulatory gaps.‚Źé‚ŹéThe solution is not to abandon interoperability. It's to make sure that when we expand interoperability, we do so with clear rules about how data can be used, who can access it, and what transparency is owed to patients. This is where the DSI certification criteria become relevant again. When AI systems are making recommendations based on patient data, and when that data is flowing through complex networks of vendors and third parties, the only protection patients have is transparency. They need to know that the system making recommendations about their care has been tested, documented, and validated. They need to know what the system's limitations are. They need to know what data it was trained on.‚Źé‚Źé**Specific Recommendations**‚Źé‚ŹéWe recommend that the ASTP/ONC maintain the current Decision Support Interventions certification criteria requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures. Specifically:‚Źé‚Źé1. Keep the requirement that vendors document intended use, target population, and known risks in a standardized format. This should include documentation of performance across demographic subgroups, not just overall accuracy metrics.‚Źé‚Źé2. Maintain the requirement for transparency about training data characteristics. Developers should disclose whether their training data came from diverse populations and health care settings, and what limitations this creates for use in different contexts.‚Źé‚Źé3. Preserve the external validation requirement. Tools should be tested on datasets independent of their training data, and results should be made available to clinicians in a transparent format.‚Źé‚Źé4. As interoperability expands under TEFCA and related initiatives (as described in 45 CFR 171.102), pair data access improvements with enhanced patient consent and transparency mechanisms. Patients should have clear information about how their data is being used when it flows to third parties.‚Źé‚Źé5. Work with ONC and CMS to develop standards for how DSI transparency disclosures should be presented to clinicians in clinical workflows. The documentation should be usable, not just compliant.‚Źé‚ŹéThe burden argument in favor of removing these criteria assumes that they are heavy and unnecessary. Our experience suggests otherwise. These requirements represent the minimum standard we need to ensure that as we expand data access and deploy AI in clinical settings, we're doing so with appropriate accountability. They're not preventing innovation. They're ensuring that innovation happens responsibly.‚Źé‚ŹéWe understand the desire to reduce regulatory burden and spur competition and innovation in health IT. These are legitimate goals. But they should not come at the cost of patient safety and informed consent. The case we described at the beginning of these comments shows what happens when the balance tips too far toward deregulation without adequate safeguards. A patient's most sensitive information was sold without her knowledge or consent, and the regulatory framework provided no meaningful recourse.‚Źé‚ŹéWe urge the ASTP/ONC to maintain the current DSI certification criteria, and to use this moment to strengthen rather than weaken the transparency requirements that protect patients as data flows through increasingly complex health IT ecosystems.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-02-05T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-05T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ4‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒfrustrated‚ôĒUtah‚ôĒexecutive director‚ôĒ39‚ôĒLast year, a patient's family came to our office after their elderly mother's detailed medical history‚ÄĒincluding her psychiatric treatment decades ago‚ÄĒwas accessed by a data broker and sold to her employer's wellness program without meaningful consent. The breach devastated her, but what frustrated me more was realizing our existing frameworks gave us almost no recourse to prevent similar incidents, even as we genuinely need better data interoperability to improve care coordination for underserved rural communities in our region.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1328
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0162‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒJames Rivera‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA Virginia caregiver argues that proposed deregulation removing AI model transparency requirements from clinical decision support systems would endanger patients by allowing hospitals to deploy algorithms without disclosing how they work, what dat...‚ôĒMy name is James Rivera. I'm a caregiver in Virginia, and I spend most of my days managing medical appointments, coordinating between specialists, and trying to keep my elderly client's care from falling apart across three different hospital systems. Last month I watched her sit in her living room for three weeks not knowing whether her pathology results were good or bad news. The oncologist's office kept saying the records were "still being compiled." When I finally got copies, I learned the hospital had been slow-walking the release intentionally, trying to keep her locked into their network for follow-up imaging and treatment planning. That's when I started paying attention to what these rules actually do in practice.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm writing because the proposed deregulation on Decision Support Interventions and AI model transparency troubles me deeply. I understand the argument about burden and innovation. I really do. But I've seen what happens when systems prioritize speed and flexibility over accountability, and I'm asking you not to gut the safeguards that protect people like my client from being treated as data assets instead of patients.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PROBLEM I'M SEEING**‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what concerns me most: when a hospital system withholds a patient's own results, there's no transparency, no accountability, no way for a patient or a caregiver to know whether the delay was legitimate or strategic. Add AI-assisted clinical decision-making into that environment, and you're multiplying the problem. If an algorithm recommends a treatment pathway, and that recommendation gets built into a hospital's workflow, and nobody outside the system can see how that algorithm was trained, what data it was built on, or what populations it was tested in, then patients can't know whether they're getting the best care or the most profitable care.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not anti-technology. I'm pro-transparency. My client needs better coordination across her care team. I would give anything to be able to pull her records from all three specialists into one place and see the whole picture. But that coordination tool is useless if the data that flows through it gets sold to an insurance company or used to deny her coverage down the road because an algorithm flagged her as high-risk based on training data that didn't include people like her.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Decision Support Interventions certification criterion that's on the table right now requires AI model cards. That means transparency about intended use, target population, known risks, and training data characteristics. It requires external validation. That's not red tape. That's the minimum bar for a system that's going to influence decisions about a person's health. Removing it would be a mistake.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT THE RULE ACTUALLY REQUIRES**‚Źé‚ŹéLet me be clear about what I'm talking about. The certification criterion for DSI systems currently mandates that developers disclose:‚Źé‚Źé- The intended use case and clinical context‚Źé- The target population the model was designed for‚Źé- Known limitations and risks identified through testing‚Źé- Characteristics of the training data, including potential sources of bias‚Źé- External validation results from independent testing‚Źé‚ŹéThese aren't new requirements. They've been in place through ONC's 2015 Edition certification criteria and carried forward into the 2024 roadmap. The proposal on the table right now suggests removing or substantially weakening these requirements as part of the broader deregulation push (see 45 CFR Part 170.315(b)(1) and related guidance in the Federal Register notice published March 2025).‚Źé‚ŹéI've read through the rationale. The concern is that model cards and external validation add cost and delay to product development. Smaller vendors can't afford the compliance infrastructure. Clinicians need faster access to innovative tools. I get it. But here's what I don't get: why patient safety has to be the thing we trade away to make that happen.‚Źé‚Źé**MY ACTUAL EXPERIENCE WITH THIS**‚Źé‚ŹéLet me give you a concrete example of why this matters to me personally. My client was diagnosed with stage 3 breast cancer two years ago. Her oncologist recommended a specific chemotherapy regimen based partly on her pathology results and partly on what the hospital's tumor board decided. I never knew whether that tumor board had access to all her imaging from the other hospital system where she'd had a mammogram six months earlier. I never knew whether the recommendation was based on the full picture or just the subset of data available in that one system.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat I did know was this: when I asked for copies of the pathology results to take to a second opinion oncologist, the hospital sat on the request for three weeks. When I finally got them, the records showed that the pathology slides had been available to the tumor board for two weeks before the recommendation was made. So the delay wasn't about compiling records. It was about leverage.‚Źé‚ŹéNow imagine if that hospital was also using an AI system to help predict treatment response or recommend drug combinations. And imagine that AI system was trained on a dataset that didn't include many patients like my client, who was in her seventies with some comorbidities. The algorithm might flag her as higher-risk than she actually is. The oncologist might deprioritize her for certain clinical trials. The insurance company might use the flagged risk status to deny coverage for a drug combination that was actually appropriate for her.‚Źé‚ŹéWithout transparency into how that algorithm was built and tested, neither I nor my client could ever know whether we were being treated unfairly. And the hospital would have zero accountability for deploying a biased system.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THE MODEL CARD REQUIREMENT ACTUALLY WORKS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe model card requirement isn't theoretical. It's been used in tech companies for years. Google, Microsoft, and academic medical centers have all published model cards for their AI systems. They include things like:‚Źé‚Źé- The clinical problem the model is designed to solve‚Źé- The population it was validated on (age range, gender distribution, comorbidities, etc.)‚Źé- Performance metrics broken down by subgroup (to catch where the model performs worse)‚Źé- Known limitations and when clinicians should not rely on the model‚Źé- The provenance of the training data‚Źé‚ŹéThis information doesn't prevent a bad algorithm from being deployed. But it lets clinicians and patients know what they're working with. It lets independent researchers audit the system. It creates accountability.‚Źé‚ŹéThe 2024 ONC Certification Program Update explicitly reaffirmed the DSI model card requirement as part of the ongoing certification framework. The rationale was straightforward: clinicians need to make informed decisions about whether and how to use AI-assisted recommendations. They can't do that if they don't know what the algorithm is actually doing.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving that requirement would mean developers could deploy AI systems into clinical workflows with zero transparency about how they work, what data they were trained on, or what populations they've been tested in. That's not innovation. That's negligence dressed up as deregulation.‚Źé‚Źé**THE TENSION I FEEL ABOUT THIS**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be honest here: I'm not opposed to loosening some rules. I think the information blocking regulations are important, but I also think they can be enforced in ways that create perverse incentives. I've seen hospitals use the "technical infeasibility" exception to avoid integrating with competing systems. I've seen EHR vendors charge exorbitant fees for data export because they claim it's technically complex, even though every tech company in the world manages to export customer data without much fuss.‚Źé‚ŹéAnd I get that compliance costs are real. A small health IT startup can't afford a team of regulatory lawyers and compliance officers. If we make the certification process too burdensome, only the big vendors will survive, and we'll end up with less competition and less innovation, not more.‚Źé‚ŹéBut the answer to that problem isn't to remove accountability. It's to make accountability smarter and cheaper. You could streamline the model card process. You could create a standardized template that doesn't require hiring consultants. You could allow developers to use third-party validators instead of requiring in-house validation capacity. You could lower the bar for what counts as "external validation" so that academic partnerships or crowdsourced testing could substitute for expensive proprietary studies.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat you shouldn't do is say that clinicians don't need to know anything about how the AI systems influencing their decisions were built.‚Źé‚Źé**THE SPECIFIC CONCERN ABOUT PATIENT SAFETY**‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what keeps me up at night about this proposal: the combination of information blocking deregulation and DSI transparency removal.‚Źé‚ŹéInformation blocking rules say that providers can't deliberately withhold patient data. But there are exceptions, including "clinical reasoning" (providers can withhold their own clinical notes) and "infeasibility" (providers can withhold data if it's technically difficult to produce). These exceptions are necessary in some contexts. But they also create opportunities for bad actors to keep patients and caregivers in the dark about how decisions are being made.‚Źé‚ŹéIf you remove the transparency requirement for AI systems at the same time you're loosening information blocking enforcement, you've created a situation where a hospital can deploy an algorithm that influences treatment decisions, withhold information about how that algorithm works under the "clinical reasoning" exception, and face no accountability for whether the algorithm is any good.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not saying this is the explicit intent of the proposal. I'm saying it's the logical consequence of the combination of moves, and I'm asking you to think about it before you finalize the rule.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Federal Register notice from March 2025 mentions that the deregulation is intended to reduce burden while maintaining patient safety. But patient safety isn't maintained by removing transparency. It's maintained by making sure the people providing care can actually understand what they're doing and why.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT I'M ASKING FOR**‚Źé‚ŹéI have a specific request. Keep the Decision Support Interventions model card requirement. Don't water it down. Don't make it optional. Don't let vendors self-certify without independent review.‚Źé‚ŹéYou can do this and still address the burden concerns:‚Źé‚Źé- Streamline the model card template. Make it a one-page form instead of a twenty-page document.‚Źé- Allow academic medical centers, teaching hospitals, and professional societies to serve as external validators, not just for-profit testing companies.‚Źé- Create a tiered system where low-risk algorithms (e.g., those that provide suggestions but don't directly influence treatment decisions) have lighter validation requirements than high-risk algorithms.‚Źé- Publish the model cards publicly so that clinicians can compare systems and make informed choices about which tools to adopt.‚Źé‚ŹéThese changes would reduce burden on developers without sacrificing transparency. A startup could work with a university hospital to validate their algorithm. They could publish a one-page model card. They could get certified and launch their product. That's not bureaucracy. That's accountability.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat would be wrong is saying that we don't need to know anything about how these systems work because innovation is more important than understanding.‚Źé‚Źé**THINKING ABOUT THE BIGGER PICTURE**‚Źé‚ŹéI keep coming back to my client and the three weeks she spent not knowing her pathology results. That delay wasn't driven by regulation. It was driven by a hospital system trying to maximize its control over her care pathway. The rules about information blocking are supposed to prevent that kind of thing. But they only work if there's also transparency about what's happening in the background.‚Źé‚ŹéThe same logic applies to AI. You can have rules that say providers can't deliberately withhold data. But if the systems making decisions about what data matters and what doesn't are completely opaque, those rules don't actually protect patients. You've just moved the problem from the foreground to the background.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not asking for perfection. I'm asking for the minimum level of transparency that lets clinicians and patients know what they're dealing with. That's what the model card requirement provides.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm also asking you to think about who benefits from removing that requirement and who bears the cost. The benefits go to vendors who want to deploy systems faster and cheaper. The costs fall on patients and clinicians who have to use those systems without knowing whether they're any good or whether they're introducing bias.‚Źé‚ŹéThat's not a trade I'm willing to make. And I don't think you should ask patients to make it either.‚Źé‚Źé**MY CLOSING THOUGHT**‚Źé‚ŹéI know this comment is long. I know I'm asking you to keep a rule that creates some compliance burden for vendors. But I've watched what happens when systems prioritize efficiency over accountability. I've sat in a living room watching my client's anxiety spiral because a hospital was slow-walking her results. I've thought about what could happen if an AI system recommended against her getting a treatment she actually needed, and nobody would tell us why.‚Źé‚ŹéThe model card requirement is a small thing. It's a one-page form that says what a system is designed to do, who it was tested on, and what it can't do well. It's not a barrier to innovation. It's a baseline for responsible deployment.‚Źé‚ŹéKeep it. Please.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-01T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-01T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ7‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe agency's claim that 'no publicly available evidence' supports transparency requirements conflates absence of evidence with evidence of absence; lack of published studies does not mean the criterion provides no value.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒangry‚ôĒVirginia‚ôĒcaregiver‚ôĒ42‚ôĒLast month, I discovered my elderly client's oncologist had withheld her pathology results for three weeks because they were "still compiling the records," while I sat in her living room watching her anxiety spiral‚ÄĒonly to find out later the hospital system was dragging their feet to keep her locked into their network. If that barrier came down, I could actually help coordinate her care across the three different specialists she sees, but I'm furious at the idea that loosening these rules might mean her genetic information gets sold to some insurance company or pharmaceutical firm without real safeguards.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2138
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0163‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒSusan Hall‚ôĒVerve, LLC‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒVerve, LLC supports retaining the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion requiring AI model transparency disclosures, but proposes a tiered approach that distinguishes between major changes (requiring full documentation) and minor ...‚ôĒ**VERVE, LLC COMMENT ON PROPOSED DEREGULATORY ACTIONS FOR HEALTH IT CERTIFICATION**‚Źé‚ŹéVerve, LLC is a health information technology vendor based in Maryland specializing in clinical decision support systems for mid-sized health systems and regional provider networks. We employ 34 people and have been actively involved in the Health IT Certification Program since 2018. We understand the genuine frustration that drives these deregulatory proposals. The certification process is expensive, time-consuming, and we've felt that burden directly. Last year alone, we spent $340,000 preparing recertification documentation for decision support features we had already validated through our own rigorous internal testing and external validation studies. During that same period, we watched competitors with substantially larger market share and resources grandfather in nearly identical functionality without undergoing similar scrutiny. That disparity is real, and it's frustrating. That said, we need to be careful about what we fix and how we fix it.‚Źé‚ŹéWe want to specifically address the proposed removal of the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion, which currently requires vendors to provide AI model cards and transparency disclosures including intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. We recognize this requirement adds cost. We also believe it is worth keeping, with some refinement. Our position comes not from a desire to protect our competitors or preserve regulatory theater, but from what we've learned deploying these systems in actual clinical settings.‚Źé‚Źé**Why the DSI Criterion Matters in Practice**‚Źé‚ŹéWhen we implemented our first clinical decision support tool at a 250-bed hospital in suburban Baltimore in 2019, the hospital's medical staff had legitimate questions about how our sepsis prediction model worked. The model flagged certain patient populations at higher risk than others, and clinicians wanted to understand why before they trusted it with patient care decisions. We provided them with our internal validation data showing the model was trained on 47,000 patient records from similar health systems, that it had been validated against a holdout test set with a 0.89 AUC, and that we knew it performed less accurately in pediatric populations under age 5, which we documented clearly. That transparency allowed the hospital's clinical leadership to make an informed decision about where to deploy the tool and which patient populations to monitor more carefully. One of their ICU physicians told us later that having that documentation actually increased clinician adoption because people trusted the tool more, not less, once they understood its limitations.‚Źé‚ŹéThe model card requirement isn't about regulatory theater. It's about informed clinical decision-making. Clinicians need to know what population a model was developed on, what it was designed to predict, and where it's likely to fail. That's not bureaucratic overhead. That's patient safety information. When providers are using AI-assisted clinical tools, they're making decisions that affect patient care. Those decisions should be informed. A provider using a sepsis prediction model that was trained primarily on adult populations should know that before they use it in a pediatric ICU, not discover it after implementation.‚Źé‚Źé**The Real Problem Isn't the Certification Criterion Itself**‚Źé‚ŹéWe think the actual problem is that the certification process doesn't distinguish well between incremental updates and fundamental changes. When we recertified last year, we had to resubmit model cards and validation documentation for a model that had three minor algorithmic adjustments from the previous version. Those adjustments improved performance on a specific patient subgroup but didn't change the fundamental purpose or scope of the tool. That's where we spent unnecessary money. The solution isn't to remove the DSI criterion. The solution is to create a tiered approach to model card updates that distinguishes between:‚Źé‚Źé1. New decision support tools or major changes to training data, intended use, or target population (full model card and external validation required)‚Źé2. Minor algorithmic refinements or performance improvements that don't change scope or intended use (updated model card with change summary and internal validation only)‚Źé3. Implementation-only changes where the underlying model is unchanged (documentation of implementation configuration, no model card update)‚Źé‚ŹéThis approach would cut our recertification burden substantially while keeping meaningful transparency requirements in place. We estimate it would have reduced our $340,000 recertification cost by roughly 60 percent, which is significant for a company our size.‚Źé‚Źé**The Competitive Fairness Piece**‚Źé‚ŹéWe need to acknowledge something directly. Part of our frustration with the current process comes from competitive asymmetry. Large vendors with established market positions can absorb certification costs more easily than we can. If you're a 500-person company with a dozen decision support tools already on the market, the marginal cost of recertifying one more tool is different than it is for us. We've been in a position where we're spending proportionally more of our development budget on compliance than our larger competitors. That's a real problem, but removing the transparency requirements isn't the right solution. The right solution is making the process more efficient and proportional to risk, not eliminating safeguards that actually matter for patient care.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat would help us more than deregulation is a clearer pathway for vendors our size to engage with the certification process in a way that doesn't require us to hire a full-time compliance team just to document what we already know about our own tools. We've talked to other mid-sized vendors. That's a common refrain.‚Źé‚Źé**Where We'd Support Streamlining**‚Źé‚ŹéWe do support streamlining other aspects of the certification process. The clinical quality measures certification, for instance, requires documentation that doesn't seem connected to actual patient safety. We support modernizing the standards requirements to align with industry adoption of FHIR (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources) rather than legacy HL7v2 standards. The 21st Century Cures Act Section 4006 already directed ONC to consider standards adoption and market prevalence, and that guidance should probably be applied more systematically across the certification program (45 CFR 170.102(b)).‚Źé‚ŹéWe also think there's room to reduce documentation burden for vendors working in mature areas where safety profiles are well understood. If a vendor is building a clinical decision support tool for a use case where there are already multiple certified tools with similar scope and similar performance characteristics, the external validation requirements might be reduced if the new tool meets a specified performance threshold. That would encourage innovation without sacrificing safety.‚Źé‚Źé**Patient Safety and Informed Consent**‚Źé‚ŹéThe deeper issue is that health IT certification exists for a reason. The FDA regulates software as a medical device when it's being used to make clinical decisions or diagnose conditions. The ONC certification program serves a similar function for health IT. When a provider selects a clinical decision support tool, they're making a decision that affects how they treat patients. That decision should be informed by accurate information about what the tool does and what it doesn't do.‚Źé‚ŹéWe've seen situations where providers implemented decision support tools without fully understanding their limitations, and then encountered unexpected performance issues in specific patient populations. One regional hospital network we work with deployed a tool for predicting hospital readmission without fully understanding that it had been trained on data from urban academic medical centers and performed significantly worse in rural populations. The hospital serves a rural area. They discovered this months into implementation, after they'd already made staffing and resource decisions based on the tool's recommendations. The transparency requirements exist because of situations like that.‚Źé‚Źé**Our Specific Recommendation**‚Źé‚ŹéRetain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion with the following modifications:‚Źé‚Źé- Require full model cards with intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation for new decision support tools or tools with major changes to training data, intended use, or scope.‚Źé- Allow streamlined documentation (updated model card with change summary and internal validation data) for minor algorithmic refinements that don't change the fundamental purpose or intended patient population.‚Źé- Establish performance benchmarks for specific use cases; if a new tool meets the benchmark for a mature use case category, allow reduced external validation requirements if the vendor documents why.‚Źé- Create a compliance cost waiver or subsidy program for vendors under a specified revenue threshold to reduce the proportional burden on smaller companies.‚Źé‚ŹéThese changes would reduce our compliance costs meaningfully while preserving the transparency that actually matters for patient safety and informed clinical decision-making. That's worth the investment.‚Źé‚ŹéVerve is committed to innovation in clinical decision support. We're also committed to making sure that when clinicians use our tools, they understand what those tools do and what they don't. That's not regulatory burden. That's responsible practice.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-31T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-31T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ4‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHospital and health system procurement teams need transparent AI model information to make evidence-based decisions about which tools to adopt and how to implement them safely in their local contexts.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒangry‚ôĒMaryland‚ôĒcompliance officer‚ôĒ42‚ôĒWhen our smaller health IT vendor went through recertification last year, we spent $340,000 on compliance documentation for features we'd already validated‚ÄĒwhile watching a major competitor grandfather in nearly identical functionality without a single audit. That's money that could have gone to our development team instead of regulatory theater, and it's exactly why I'm watching this closely.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1411
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0164‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒSarah Flores‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒMaintaining transparency requirements for AI model cards in clinical decision support systems is essential to prevent algorithmic bias and ensure patients and providers understand how AI recommendations are generated, particularly for vulnerable p...‚ôĒ**PRESERVING TRANSPARENCY IN AI-ASSISTED CLINICAL CARE**‚Źé‚ŹéI've spent 28 years as a social worker watching patients navigate medical systems that don't talk to each other. What happened to Mrs. Chen last year still bothers me. When she switched cardiologists, her new doctor couldn't pull ejection-fraction data from the hospital three miles away for 21 days. She had repeat testing ordered. Insurance denied coverage on the duplicate exams. She paid $1,200 out of pocket for measurements that already existed somewhere in the system. Then she stopped going to appointments because the bills frightened her more than the heart condition.‚Źé‚ŹéI thought the 2015 information blocking rules fixed problems like this. They did, partly. But I'm concerned about what I'm hearing regarding potential rollback of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria, specifically the requirement that AI model cards disclose intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. This matters because it's not academic.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY MODEL TRANSPARENCY ISN'T OPTIONAL**‚Źé‚ŹéThe cardiologist Mrs. Chen saw relies on decision support software to flag high-risk patients. That AI was trained on data from predominantly white, male populations over age 55. Mrs. Chen is 68, female, and Asian-American. When that software recommended against certain interventions because her profile didn't match the training cohort, nobody told her doctor that. Nobody told her. The system just worked silently, and she got different treatment based on opaque algorithmic reasoning.‚Źé‚ŹéIf we remove the DSI certification requirement for model cards and transparency disclosures (as referenced in 21 CFR Part 11 and the proposed ONC changes to 45 CFR 170.315(b)(2)), we lose the ability to catch these failures. Providers won't know what their AI is trained on. Patients won't know either. And vulnerable people, the ones I work with who already distrust the system, will be the ones harmed first.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT NEEDS TO HAPPEN**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification criterion requires:‚Źé- Disclosure of intended use and target population‚Źé- Documentation of known risks and performance limitations‚Źé- Training data characteristics that inform clinician interpretation‚Źé- Evidence of external validation beyond development cohorts‚Źé‚ŹéThis isn't regulatory burden for its own sake. It's the difference between a doctor understanding why her software flagged a patient and just trusting a black box. Mrs. Chen deserved to know if that algorithm was built for someone like her. Patients deserve that information. Clinicians deserve it. Removing this requirement doesn't unleash innovation; it unleashes harm on people who can't afford mistakes.‚Źé‚ŹéI‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-31T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-31T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒangry‚ôĒMaryland‚ôĒsocial worker‚ôĒ66‚ôĒWhen my client Mrs. Chen tried to switch cardiologists last year, her new doctor's office couldn't access her ejection-fraction measurements from the previous hospital for three weeks‚ÄĒthree weeks of duplicated testing and mounting anxiety for a woman already terrified of her diagnosis. I watched her pay out of pocket for tests insurance wouldn't cover twice, and I thought we'd finally fixed this mess with new rules. Now I'm hearing those protections might get rolled back, and I'm furious on behalf of every vulnerable person I've worked with who can't afford to be somebody's data hostage.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ409
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0165‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒRichard Robinson‚ôĒNational Alliance for Care at Home‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe National Alliance for Care at Home urges retention of AI transparency requirements in health IT certification, arguing that removing Decision Support Interventions (DSI) model card documentation would shift safety burden from developers to car...‚ôĒ**NATIONAL ALLIANCE FOR CARE AT HOME**‚Źé**Comment on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity**‚Źé‚ŹéThe National Alliance for Care at Home represents home and community-based care providers, care coordination networks, and patient advocacy organizations across 38 states. Our members manage care for over 2.3 million older adults and people with disabilities annually, coordinating services across fragmented systems where AI-assisted clinical decision support has become increasingly common but alarmingly opaque. We write to urge the Office of the Secretary and the American Health Standards Technology Panel to retain the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures as currently specified in 45 CFR 170.315(b)(6).‚Źé‚ŹéI understand the pressure to reduce burden on health IT developers. I do. We hear from our IT partners constantly about certification costs, and I recognize that meaningful regulation creates real friction in product development cycles. But I've also sat in rooms with care coordinators who couldn't explain to family members why an AI system recommended against a particular treatment option, and I've listened to pharmacists describe the moment they discovered medication interactions that should have been caught by algorithms nobody had ever actually validated. The case I opened this memo with is not an outlier. It's a structural problem that will only accelerate if we remove the guardrails that currently exist.‚Źé‚Źé**THE SPECIFIC PROBLEM WITH REMOVING DSI TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéWhat troubles me most about the proposed deregulation is that it conflates two separate things: the burden of compliance and the necessity of transparency. These are not the same. A developer can provide a model card describing intended use, target population, known limitations, and training data characteristics without adding significant cost to the certification process. What removal would do is eliminate the only mechanism we currently have to ensure that care teams using these systems actually understand what they're using.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider what happens in practice. A home health agency in Winston-Salem adopts an AI system that recommends medication dosing adjustments for seniors on multiple drugs. The system was trained primarily on data from younger adult populations because that's where the training data was available. Nobody told the care coordinator this. When an 82-year-old patient with renal impairment suffers a serious adverse event, the agency's leadership has no documentation of the system's limitations to review. They can't even determine whether the recommendation fell within the system's validated scope. This isn't theoretical. This is what we're watching happen at scale right now across decision support tools in primary care, medication management, and hospital discharge planning.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI criterion under 45 CFR 170.315(b)(6) requires that certified technology include model cards documenting:‚Źé‚Źé- Intended use and use case constraints‚Źé- Target population characteristics and any population-specific limitations‚Źé- Known performance limitations and risks‚Źé- Training data characteristics and composition‚Źé- External validation study results and methodology‚Źé‚ŹéThese requirements exist for a reason. They're not bureaucratic overhead. They're the difference between a care team that knows what it's using and one flying blind.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THIS MATTERS FOR PATIENT SAFETY IN DISTRIBUTED CARE SETTINGS**‚Źé‚ŹéOur members operate in a fundamentally different care environment than hospital-based IT administrators. We're managing care coordination across multiple agencies, each with different systems, different staff training levels, and different oversight structures. When a home health nurse in rural North Carolina is deciding whether to escalate a patient concern based on an AI recommendation, she's making that decision with incomplete information about the system. She doesn't have IT security specialists on staff. She doesn't have data scientists to audit model performance. She has the system's output and her clinical judgment.‚Źé‚ŹéTransparency disclosures level that playing field slightly. If the model card says the system was validated in urban settings with predominantly white populations, and she's working with rural Appalachian patients with different comorbidity patterns, she can adjust her reliance on that recommendation accordingly. Without that information, she can't. She has to trust the system or ignore it entirely, with no middle ground for informed clinical decision-making.‚Źé‚ŹéThe FDA has recognized this exact principle in its approach to clinical decision support software. The 2019 FDA guidance on clinical decision support software (published March 2019, updated to reflect 21 CFR 880.1200 and related sections) explicitly distinguishes between systems that require transparency disclosures and those that don't, based on the clinical risk and the care setting. Home and community-based care is precisely the setting where that distinction matters most, because the care team is more distributed, less specialized, and more dependent on the system's reliability.‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be clear about what I'm not saying. I'm not arguing that DSI certification is perfect or that the current requirements couldn't be streamlined. I'm saying that removing transparency requirements entirely is not streamlining. It's elimination. There's a difference.‚Źé‚Źé**THE UNEXAMINED COSTS OF REMOVING ACCOUNTABILITY**‚Źé‚ŹéWhat concerns me most about this proposal is that it treats accountability as a burden rather than a feature. When we remove the requirement for developers to produce model cards and document limitations, we're not just reducing paperwork. We're removing the paper trail that allows us to answer basic questions after something goes wrong.‚Źé‚ŹéLast year, one of our member agencies in Charlotte discovered that an algorithm recommending hospital readmission risk had a documented failure rate of 34 percent in patients over 85 years old. The system's developer eventually provided this information, but only after the agency requested it specifically, and only because someone on their team happened to be technically sophisticated enough to know to ask. Most of our members don't have that capability. They would have continued using a system they didn't understand, with a performance failure rate they didn't know about, because nobody was required to tell them.‚Źé‚ŹéUnder the current DSI criterion, that information would be in the model card. Required. Documented. Available to every care team using the system. The developer still makes the product. The developer still profits. The developer just has to be honest about what the product does and doesn't do.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving this requirement doesn't reduce developer burden in any meaningful way. It shifts burden onto care teams and patients, who now have to discover limitations through adverse events instead of reading documentation. That's not deregulation in service of innovation. That's deregulation in service of opacity.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT THE EVIDENCE ACTUALLY SHOWS ABOUT MODEL TRANSPARENCY**‚Źé‚ŹéI realize I should address the argument that transparency requirements slow innovation. The data here is mixed, and I think the regulatory community has oversimplified the relationship between documentation and development speed.‚Źé‚ŹéResearch on clinical decision support adoption across health systems (Rajkomar et al., Nature Medicine, 2018, and related work on implementation science) consistently shows that care teams adopt and appropriately use AI systems when they understand those systems. Conversely, when transparency is low and clinicians don't understand what the system does, adoption is either too high (blind trust) or too low (rejection), with neither being clinically optimal. The transparency requirement doesn't slow innovation. It actually enables appropriate adoption.‚Źé‚ŹéThe burden argument needs scrutiny too. A model card documenting intended use, target population, known limitations, and training data characteristics is not an elaborate document. It's a structured summary of information the developer should already know. If a developer can't document what their system does and doesn't do, that's a signal that the system isn't ready for clinical use, not that the requirement is too strict. The burden being described here is often the burden of admitting limitations, not the burden of documenting them.‚Źé‚ŹéI've also reviewed the comment submissions from health IT developers to prior certification rulemakings (Federal Register, Docket CMS-2015-0001, 81 FR 39496). The developers who complained most about DSI transparency requirements were not small innovators with limited resources. They were large companies with mature development processes who didn't want to disclose performance limitations they'd already identified but hadn't addressed. That's not a legitimate regulatory burden. That's an attempt to hide information.‚Źé‚Źé**A SPECIFIC PROPOSAL THAT DOESN'T REQUIRE REMOVING THE CRITERION**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the concern is genuinely about reducing certification burden on developers, there are ways to streamline the DSI criterion without eliminating transparency requirements entirely.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current 45 CFR 170.315(b)(6) could be modified to:‚Źé‚Źé- Establish a standardized model card template that developers can populate rather than writing narrative documentation, reducing compliance variation and certification review time‚Źé- Allow developers to reference published validation studies rather than requiring new external validation for every product release, as long as material changes to training data or model architecture are documented‚Źé- Clarify that "known limitations" refers to limitations documented in development and validation work, not hypothetical limitations the developer speculates about, reducing uncertainty about what must be disclosed‚Źé- Create a streamlined certification pathway for updates to existing certified systems that don't involve model retraining or material changes to intended use‚Źé- Establish a reasonable timeline for certification review of model card submissions (30 days, for example) to prevent indefinite delays in the certification process‚Źé‚ŹéThese modifications would reduce burden substantially while preserving the core transparency function. A developer would still have to document what their system does and doesn't do. They just wouldn't have to do it in different ways for different certifiers, and they wouldn't have to repeat validation work that's already been published.‚Źé‚ŹéI mention this because I want to be constructive rather than just oppositional. I understand that some aspects of the current DSI criterion could be more efficient. I'm arguing that efficiency improvements should preserve transparency, not eliminate it.‚Źé‚Źé**THE SILENCE ON EQUITY IMPLICATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéI notice that the proposed rule doesn't explicitly address how removing DSI transparency requirements might affect algorithmic equity and disparities in care. This concerns me substantially.‚Źé‚ŹéMany of the AI systems currently used in decision support were trained on data that overrepresents certain populations. Algorithms for clinical risk prediction, medication dosing, and hospital readmission have documented performance differences across racial and ethnic groups. The Medicare disparities documentation (CMS Office of Minority Health data releases, 2020-2023) shows that these differences translate to real disparities in care recommendations and outcomes.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI transparency requirement is one of the few mechanisms we have to identify and address these disparities. If a care team knows that a system's training data was 72 percent white and 18 percent Black, they can adjust their interpretation of recommendations for Black patients accordingly. If they don't know the training data composition, they can't. Removing the requirement to disclose this information doesn't make the disparities go away. It just hides them.‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be fair to the Office of the Secretary here. I don't think the deregulatory intent includes an intent to worsen disparities. But I think the proposal hasn't grappled with the fact that transparency requirements are often the only mechanism available to identify and mitigate algorithmic bias in care settings. Removing them without a replacement mechanism would leave care teams essentially blind to equity concerns.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT OUR MEMBERS ARE TELLING US ABOUT CURRENT PRACTICE**‚Źé‚ŹéI should probably ground this in what we're actually hearing from the field, because the abstract arguments matter less than the operational reality.‚Źé‚ŹéOur members manage care coordination across multiple AI systems. A typical large home health agency in our network uses:‚Źé‚Źé- Medication management systems with AI-assisted drug interaction checking‚Źé- Hospital readmission risk prediction tools‚Źé- Care coordination algorithms that prioritize which patients need outreach‚Źé- Wound care systems with AI-assisted assessment‚Źé- Staffing optimization systems that recommend visit frequency and duration‚Źé‚ŹéFor exactly one of those systems could the care coordinator explain what data the algorithm was trained on and what populations it was validated in. One. For the medication management system, the documentation mentions "general adult populations" without specifying age range, comorbidity distribution, or renal function considerations. For the readmission risk tool, the developer's documentation is completely silent on how the model was trained. The care coordinator just uses it.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not unusual. This is standard across our membership. Care teams are using AI systems in decision-making capacity without basic information about what those systems do and don't do. The current DSI requirement would fix this. Removing it would cement it.‚Źé‚Źé**ON THE INFORMATION BLOCKING IMPLICATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéI should also note that removing DSI transparency requirements creates an awkward gap in the information blocking regulations as currently structured. Under 45 CFR 171.102 and related sections, there's an expectation that health IT developers provide information necessary for care teams to use systems safely. That expectation is somewhat toothless without corresponding transparency requirements in certification.‚Źé‚ŹéIf a care team needs to understand an AI system's limitations in order to use it safely and appropriately, and the system is not required to disclose those limitations in certification, then the information blocking regulations become a potential mechanism for forcing disclosure after the fact. That's not efficient for anyone. It's better to require disclosure upfront in certification than to require it later through information blocking disputes.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule doesn't address this interaction explicitly, which is an oversight in my view. The deregulatory approach to DSI certification should be coordinated with the information blocking framework, not just layered on top of it without coordination.‚Źé‚Źé**REAL-WORLD CONSEQUENCE: THE CASE FOR RETENTION**‚Źé‚ŹéLet me return to the case I mentioned at the start, because it illustrates why this matters practically.‚Źé‚ŹéThe 78-year-old patient whose medication list was being shared without her knowledge was using a home health agency that relies on an AI-assisted care coordination system. The system recommended a medication interaction check with her primary care physician based on a new prescription. The system flagged the interaction. But the care coordinator never saw the alert because she hadn't been trained on how to interpret the system's output format, and nobody had told her which alerts were high-priority versus routine. The pharmacy caught the interaction instead.‚Źé‚ŹéIf that care coordinator had known that the system was trained on data from urban settings with predominantly younger populations, she might have been more skeptical of its recommendations for a rural, elderly patient. She might have checked interactions manually as a standard practice instead of relying on the system's judgment. If she'd known the system's performance limitations, she would have understood that not seeing an alert didn't mean the system had already checked for interactions. She would have checked anyway.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI requirement wouldn't have prevented the medication interaction itself. But it would have created the documentation and transparency necessary for the care team to use the system appropriately and catch the problem internally instead of relying on the pharmacy to do it.‚Źé‚ŹéThat's what I mean by transparency enabling safety. It's not about preventing all problems. It's about creating the information infrastructure for care teams to use technology appropriately within their own knowledge and capability constraints.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe National Alliance for Care at Home respectfully urges the Office of the Secretary and the American Health Standards Technology Panel to retain the DSI certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures. If streamlining is needed, we recommend exploring the specific modifications I outlined above: standardized templates, referenced validation studies, clarified limitation standards, streamlined updates, and reasonable timelines.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving transparency requirements entirely would shift burden from developers to care teams and patients, would eliminate one of the few mechanisms we have to identify and address algorithmic disparities, and would create an information vacuum in decision support systems that are increasingly central to clinical care.‚Źé‚ŹéInnovation doesn't require opacity. Market competition doesn't require hiding product limitations. Removing these requirements doesn't unleash prosperity. It unleashes risk.‚Źé‚ŹéWe're ready to work with the agency on solutions that reduce genuine burden while preserving patient safety and care team transparency.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-17T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-17T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ8‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving DSI transparency requirements creates a dangerous regulatory gap where AI-based clinical decision support tools face fewer disclosure obligations than traditional medical devices, undermining consistent safety standards.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒurgent‚ôĒNorth Carolina‚ôĒdirector of government affairs‚ôĒ54‚ôĒLast month, I sat with a 78-year-old constituent whose daughter discovered her mother's medication list was being shared with four different agencies without her knowledge‚ÄĒshe found out only because the pharmacy flagged a dangerous interaction that should have been caught earlier. When I asked the daughter why she hadn't been informed, the care coordinator simply said the consent forms "covered it." CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0166‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒCarol Brown‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe commenter opposes weakening transparency requirements for AI tools in healthcare, arguing that clear explanations of AI capabilities and limitations are essential for safe clinical use and patient trust, and that prioritizing data transfer spe...‚ôĒI've spent the last year dealing with the fallout of institutions prioritizing speed over safeguards, so I need to be direct about what worries me most in this rule. If a doctor has clear explanations of what an AI system can and can't do, they're more likely to trust it and use it properly instead of guessing. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0167‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒGeorge Wilson‚ôĒbeHuman Corp.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒbeHuman Corp urges the Office of the National Coordinator to retain Decision Support Interventions certification requirements for AI model transparency and disclosure, arguing that these safeguards are essential informed-consent mechanisms for cli...‚ôĒGeorge Wilson, on behalf of beHuman Corp.‚ŹéCompliance Officer, Health IT Division‚ŹéWilmington, Delaware‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed deregulation of health information technology certification requirements presents a critical juncture for patient safety and clinical accountability. beHuman Corp respectfully urges the Office of the National Coordinator to retain the existing Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion, particularly the requirements for AI model cards and transparency disclosures that document intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. While we understand the impulse to reduce compliance burden‚ÄĒand we have felt that burden acutely‚ÄĒremoving these safeguards would shift unmanageable risk onto clinicians and patients who lack the technical expertise to evaluate AI-assisted clinical decisions on their own.‚Źé‚ŹéLast year, our division spent eighteen months and approximately $400,000 recertifying a single EHR module when we restructured our architecture. That was painful. I watched our small team absorb costs that larger vendors distribute across quarterly budgets measured in the billions. I get why the deregulatory impulse exists. Smaller vendors face genuine barriers to entry and innovation when certification pathways remain static or overly prescriptive. But here is what concerns me: the DSI criterion is not a bureaucratic obstacle. It is a disclosure mechanism. When a clinician receives a recommendation from our system about patient dosing, antibiotic selection, or discharge planning, that clinician needs to know what population the model was trained on, what data it saw, what blind spots exist, and whether external validators have tested it. This is not process theater. This is informed consent translated into the clinical workflow. The 21st Century Cures Act (42 U.S.C. 300jj) established information blocking prohibitions precisely because locked-in data and opaque systems harm patients. Removing transparency requirements on AI decision support inverts that logic.‚Źé‚ŹéThe FDA's 2021 guidance on Clinical Decision Support Software (available in the Federal Register, Vol. 86, No. 3) draws a careful line between general informational tools and systems that actively guide clinical decisions. Our experience deploying AI-assisted modules across three regional health systems in Delaware and Pennsylvania shows clinicians do rely on these recommendations, sometimes without adequate skepticism. In one instance, a model we built flagged high-risk sepsis patients with 89 percent sensitivity but had been trained predominantly on data from academic medical centers. When deployed in a smaller rural hospital with different case-mix and staffing, clinicians initially over-interpreted the recommendations because they had no way to assess whether the model's performance held in their context. We had documentation of the model's training characteristics and external validation results, which let us quickly recalibrate their confidence intervals. Without that transparency requirement, that hospital would have operated blind.‚Źé‚ŹéThe certification criterion requiring AI model cards reflects principles outlined in the FDA's 2019 framework for software modifications and AI/ML-based software validation (FDA-2019-D-6585). It mirrors the transparency expectations in NIST's AI Risk Management Framework. These are not redundant bureaucratic overlays. They are industry-standard practice for systems where errors have clinical consequences. Removing them does not reduce burden on vendors; it transfers that burden to clinicians who must now guess at model reliability, and to patients whose safety depends on that guesswork.‚Źé‚ŹéWe support streamlining redundant or duplicative certification pathways. We support tiered approaches that distinguish between low-risk informational tools and high-risk clinical decision systems. We support faster approval cycles for truly innovative features that existing criteria cannot accommodate. But the DSI transparency requirement should remain mandatory. It costs vendors time and honesty, not money. It requires documentation that responsible developers should maintain anyway. And it preserves the clinical and legal accountability that healthcare demands. The alternative is a marketplace where vendors cut corners on validation, clinicians lose visibility into model limitations, and patient safety becomes a cost externality. That is not deregulation. That is abdication.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-21T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-21T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒresigned‚ôĒDelaware‚ôĒcompliance officer‚ôĒ50‚ôĒLast year, our small health IT division spent eighteen months and nearly $400,000 getting a single module recertified when we pivoted our EHR architecture‚ÄĒa disproportionate hit for a company our size compared to what the majors absorb in their quarterly budgets. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0168‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒAshley Williams‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒRemoving transparency and accountability requirements for AI tools in healthcare will worsen existing problems with medical record management and patient safety, as doctors need to understand these systems before using them on patients and patient...‚ôĒMy daughter had to switch doctors last year because our insurance changed, and when we asked for my medical records to transfer over, it took three weeks. Some of the test results were just gone from their system. Nobody could explain what happened to them. I'm worried that if you remove the requirements for hospitals and clinics to explain how their AI tools work and what data they're using, it's only going to get worse. Right now at least there's supposed to be some checking and transparency. If companies can hide how their systems work:‚Źé‚Źé1. Doctors won't know if the AI is safe before they use it on patients‚Źé2. We won't know who's looking at our records or what they're doing with them‚Źé3. When records go missing like mine did, there's nobody to hold accountable‚Źé‚ŹéI just think hospitals need to understand their tools before putting them in front of people like me. Removing those safeguards won't help patients. It'll just make it easier for things to slip through the cracks.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-20T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-20T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHospital and health system procurement teams need transparent AI model information to make evidence-based decisions about which tools to adopt and how to implement them safely in their local contexts.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒlow‚ôĒconcerned‚ôĒTexas‚ôĒadministrative assistant‚ôĒ78‚ôĒMy daughter had to switch doctors last year because our insurance changed, and when we asked for my medical records to send over, it took three weeks and they said some of the test results were just gone from their system.
