CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0169‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒAmanda Scott‚ôĒNORC at the University of Chicago‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒNORC at the University of Chicago argues that the proposed rule's elimination of Decision Support Interventions certification criteria‚ÄĒincluding model cards, training data disclosure, and external validation requirements‚ÄĒwould remove critical tran...‚ôĒNORC at the University of Chicago submits the following comments on the Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability proposed rule.‚Źé‚Źé**BACKGROUND AND ORGANIZATIONAL INTEREST**‚Źé‚ŹéNORC at the University of Chicago is an independent research organization that has spent more than eight decades conducting rigorous empirical research on health systems, technology adoption, and implementation science. Our work includes longitudinal studies of electronic health record deployment across health systems of varying sizes, evaluation of clinical decision support tools in diverse care settings, and research on how information barriers affect patient safety and care coordination. We have a direct stake in this regulatory domain because the rules governing health IT certification, interoperability standards, and information blocking directly influence the research infrastructure we depend on and the validity of the evidence we can generate about health system performance.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed deregulatory actions raise questions that go to the heart of how we will know whether removing certification requirements actually improves patient safety and clinical outcomes. Without robust empirical data collection mechanisms embedded in the regulatory framework itself, we risk a situation where regulators and policymakers will lack the evidence needed to assess whether market-driven innovation has delivered the promised benefits or generated unintended harms. This is not an abstract concern. It stems from what we have observed in our sister's case and in our broader research on health information fragmentation.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PROBLEM WITH DATA INACCESSIBILITY IN CURRENT SYSTEMS**‚Źé‚ŹéLast year, when our sister's cardiologist retired, she discovered that thirty years of medical records‚ÄĒtest results, medication adjustments, specialist consultations, imaging studies‚ÄĒwere essentially held captive within a proprietary electronic health record system. Her new cardiologist could not access these records without weeks of faxing and phone calls. Some documents never arrived. Some arrived incomplete. Her new provider had no way to know whether a particular medication had been tried before, what the results were, or what doses had proven problematic. She was forced to repeat testing that had already been done. The continuity of her care was broken not by clinical failure but by technical incompatibility and vendor lock-in.‚Źé‚ŹéThis experience is not unique. It happens thousands of times daily across the United States. Patients switch providers and lose access to their own records. Researchers trying to study long-term outcomes cannot assemble longitudinal datasets because information is trapped in incompatible systems. Public health agencies cannot track disease trends because they cannot link data across health systems. Clinicians cannot make fully informed decisions because relevant information from prior episodes of care is inaccessible.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule would remove several certification criteria that currently require transparency and accountability from vendors who develop decision support systems. Specifically, the rule would eliminate the requirement that Decision Support Interventions (DSI) meet certification standards that mandate model cards, documentation of intended use, specification of target populations, disclosure of known risks, characterization of training data, and evidence of external validation. The justification offered is that these requirements impose compliance costs that slow innovation and reduce competition among vendors.‚Źé‚ŹéWe understand the logic. Regulatory requirements do impose costs. But the question before the agency is not whether requirements have costs. The question is whether removing these requirements will produce net benefits to patients and the health system, or whether it will simply shift costs from vendors to patients, providers, and researchers in ways that are difficult to measure and therefore invisible to regulators.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CASE FOR RETAINING DECISION SUPPORT CERTIFICATION STANDARDS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criteria should be retained because they serve as the primary mechanism through which vendors communicate to clinicians and researchers what their tools actually do, who they have been tested in, what their limitations are, and where errors are most likely to occur. These are not bureaucratic overhead. They are the informational infrastructure that enables informed clinical decision-making. When a cardiologist uses an AI-assisted diagnostic tool to evaluate a patient, that clinician needs to know whether the tool was trained on populations that resemble the patient in front of them. They need to know what the tool's performance was on patients of different ages, races, comorbidity profiles, and disease severities. They need to know whether the tool has been validated in settings outside the vendor's own controlled environment.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider the stakes. A decision support tool that performs well in a quaternary care center with abundant specialist input and well-resourced radiology departments may perform very differently in a rural clinic where imaging quality is variable and specialist consultation is delayed. A tool trained on data from a health system serving predominantly older Medicare beneficiaries may have unknown performance on younger, commercially insured patients with different disease patterns. A tool that works well for straightforward cases may fail catastrophically on complex patients with multiple comorbidities. None of these limitations are inherently disqualifying. But clinicians and patients deserve to know about them.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current certification framework, as described in 45 CFR Part 170, Subpart B, requires vendors to provide this information as a condition of certification. The model card requirement, drawn from practices in the machine learning research community, creates a standardized format for communicating these details. This is not a heavy burden. It is asking vendors to document what they should already know about their own products.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving this requirement will not eliminate the risk. It will eliminate transparency about the risk. Clinicians will use decision support tools without knowing their limitations. Patients will receive treatment recommendations generated by algorithms that may not have been tested on anyone like them. Researchers will be unable to assess whether AI-assisted care is improving outcomes or simply redistributing risk across populations in ways that are invisible because the underlying data about tool performance is proprietary and undisclosed.‚Źé‚Źé**IMPLICATIONS FOR RESEARCH AND EVIDENCE GENERATION**‚Źé‚ŹéOur organization has conducted studies evaluating the implementation of health IT in settings ranging from large academic medical centers to critical access hospitals. One consistent finding is that the ability to study implementation outcomes depends critically on transparency about what the technology is supposed to do and under what conditions it is supposed to work. When vendors treat algorithmic specifications and training data characteristics as trade secrets, researchers cannot conduct the kind of rigorous implementation science that would answer questions like: Does this tool actually reduce diagnostic error? Does it reduce disparities or entrench them? Does it change clinical workflow in ways that improve safety or create new failure modes?‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule would make these questions harder to answer. If vendors are no longer required to disclose model cards or external validation results, then researchers would need to obtain this information through Freedom of Information Act requests, contractual negotiations, or reverse-engineering studies. All of these approaches are more time-consuming and less reliable than having standardized disclosures embedded in a regulatory requirement. The result would be that the evidence base for understanding whether AI-assisted clinical decision-making actually improves health outcomes would remain thin and fragmented.‚Źé‚ŹéThis matters for policy. Regulators need evidence to answer the core question posed in this rulemaking: Does deregulation increase innovation and competition in ways that improve patient outcomes? Without robust data collection mechanisms, regulators will have no way to know whether the answer is yes or no. They will be flying blind.‚Źé‚ŹéFederal standards for health IT certification have evolved through multiple iterations since the HITECH Act of 2009 (42 U.S.C. Section 17921 et seq.) established the Office of the National Coordinator and gave it authority over certification criteria. Those standards have generally moved in the direction of greater transparency and interoperability. The Cures Act of 2020 (Public Law 114‚Äď156, codified at 45 U.S.C. Section 300jj-35) prohibited information blocking and required ONC to establish standards for health information exchange. The proposed rule appears to reverse course on the transparency and accountability elements that the Cures Act put in place.‚Źé‚Źé**THE SPECIFIC CASE FOR DECISION SUPPORT CERTIFICATION STANDARDS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification standards are a particularly important element of the regulatory framework because decision support tools are the point where algorithmic logic directly influences clinical decisions that affect patient safety. Unlike routine data exchange standards, which can be evaluated primarily on technical grounds, decision support tools must be evaluated on clinical grounds. Does the tool help clinicians make better decisions? Does it introduce new risks? Does it work differently across populations?‚Źé‚ŹéThe model card requirement in the current certification framework addresses these questions by requiring vendors to document: the intended use of the tool, the target population for which it was designed, the known risks and limitations, the characteristics of the training data, the performance metrics achieved on the training data and on external validation datasets, and the conditions under which performance may degrade. This is not a new or burdensome requirement. The FDA has long required similar documentation for medical devices. The framework is adapted from best practices in the machine learning research community, as documented in model card literature dating to 2019 and incorporated into numerous academic publications on responsible AI in healthcare.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving this requirement would mean that clinicians and patients would have no standardized source of information about how well a decision support tool actually works, who it was tested on, or where it is likely to fail. Vendors would have no regulatory incentive to conduct external validation studies or to be transparent about limitations. The market would not automatically generate this information because vendors have no commercial incentive to publicize the weaknesses of their products.‚Źé‚ŹéSome might argue that the market will discipline vendors whose tools perform poorly. But this assumes that poor performance will be visible, which it often is not. A tool that introduces subtle biases in patient selection or that performs poorly in subgroups may continue to be used if the overall performance appears acceptable and if clinicians lack information about subgroup performance. A tool that introduces new kinds of errors that are difficult to detect may persist in clinical use for years before the harm becomes apparent.‚Źé‚Źé**INFORMATION BLOCKING AND ACCOUNTABILITY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule also raises questions about information blocking enforcement and transparency. The current regulations implementing the Cures Act's information blocking prohibition, set forth in 45 CFR Part 171, require covered entities to have policies and procedures that prevent information blocking. The certification criteria serve as a check on information blocking by requiring vendors to demonstrate that their products do not prevent information exchange in violation of the regulation.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving certification criteria would weaken this check. Vendors would have less regulatory incentive to design systems that comply with information blocking rules. Covered entities would have less clarity about what vendors are required to do. The result would likely be more information blocking, more fragmentation, and more situations like the one our sister experienced where patients cannot access their own records.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Office of the National Coordinator has authority under 45 U.S.C. Section 300jj-12 to establish certification criteria for health IT. That authority is not absolute. It must be exercised consistent with the purposes of the Health Information Technology for Economic and Clinical Health Act and the Cures Act, which include promoting interoperability, reducing information blocking, and protecting patient privacy. The proposed deregulatory actions would undermine these purposes.‚Źé‚Źé**RECOMMENDATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéNORC at the University of Chicago recommends that the final rule retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criteria, including the requirements for model cards, documentation of intended use and target populations, disclosure of known risks, characterization of training data, and evidence of external validation. These requirements should not be eliminated. They can be refined, clarified, or streamlined, but they should remain as core elements of the certification framework.‚Źé‚ŹéWe also recommend that the final rule include provisions for ongoing monitoring and evaluation of whether deregulatory actions actually improve innovation and competition without harming patient safety or care quality. This might include requirements for vendors to report on product adoption rates, clinical outcomes data, and information blocking complaints. It might also include periodic reviews of whether removed certification criteria should be re-imposed based on evidence of unintended harms.‚Źé‚ŹéFinally, we recommend that the final rule preserve the authority of ONC and CMS to update certification criteria as new evidence emerges about the safety and effectiveness of health IT tools. The technology landscape is changing rapidly. What seems like a reasonable deregulatory action today may need to be reconsidered in three years if evidence emerges that removed requirements were important for patient safety.‚Źé‚ŹéThe choice before the agency is not between regulation and innovation. It is between transparent innovation, where the performance and limitations of new tools are known, and opaque innovation, where tools are deployed widely but their effects remain invisible. We believe transparent innovation is more likely to improve health outcomes and generate the evidence needed to guide future policy. We urge the agency to retain the certification criteria that enable transparency while remaining open to streamlining requirements that are truly burdensome and unrelated to patient safety.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-15T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-15T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ7‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒacademic‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒresigned‚ôĒMaine‚ôĒassociate professor‚ôĒ56‚ôĒWhen my sister's cardiologist retired last year, she discovered her medical records‚ÄĒthirty years of test results and medication adjustments‚ÄĒwere essentially trapped in a proprietary system the practice used, inaccessible to her new provider without weeks of faxes and phone calls that fell through the cracks. I watched her worry that something crucial might get lost in translation, and I kept thinking about all the research data we can't properly analyze precisely because these systems don't talk to each other.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2134
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0170‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒSarah White‚ôĒHealthcare Leadership Council‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Healthcare Leadership Council opposes removing model card and transparency disclosure requirements from Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria, arguing that these requirements are essential accountability mechanisms to pre...‚ôĒRE: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity (RFI)‚Źé‚ŹéHealthcare Leadership Council‚Źé‚ŹéOn behalf of the Healthcare Leadership Council, I'm writing to address the proposed changes to Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria, specifically the removal of model card and transparency disclosure requirements. Our organization represents approximately 140 health systems and academic medical centers across the country, and our members collectively care for millions of patients annually. This is not an abstract policy matter for us. It affects how our clinicians make decisions every single day, and it affects whether we can be confident in those decisions.‚Źé‚Źé**THE REAL PROBLEM WITH REMOVING TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéWhen I sat down last spring with a woman from rural Kansas at one of our council listening sessions, she told me something that has stuck with me. Her daughter‚ÄĒin her early forties‚ÄĒspent three years trying to figure out why she had constant inflammation and fatigue. The diagnosis bounced around across seven different providers, none of them seeing the full picture because the medical records couldn't talk to each other. What bothered her most wasn't just the delay. It was that when she finally did get a diagnosis of a rare autoimmune condition, she discovered her insurance company had already flagged her as "high risk" based on incomplete clinical data, and they'd started pricing her coverage accordingly before anyone even knew what was actually wrong. She asked me directly: when we start letting algorithms help doctors make decisions, who's checking to make sure those algorithms aren't just encoding the same biases that made her three-year nightmare possible in the first place?‚Źé‚ŹéThat question is why the model card and transparency disclosure requirements matter. They're not bureaucratic overhead. They're accountability mechanisms.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule would remove the requirement that DSI systems include model cards documenting intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. I understand the argument about reducing burden on vendors. But I also understand what happens when algorithms work in the dark. A study published in Health Affairs in 2019 showed that widely-used risk prediction algorithms in clinical settings systematized racial bias by using healthcare spending as a proxy for patient acuity, which systematically underestimated the health needs of Black patients by as much as 18 percent. Nobody intended that. The vendors didn't wake up thinking, "Let's embed racism into our software." But absent transparency requirements and external validation, nobody caught it for years.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat concerns me is this: if we remove the requirement for model cards and transparency disclosures, we're essentially asking clinicians to trust algorithms they can't inspect, based on training data they don't know about, designed for populations that may or may not include their actual patients. That's not deregulation. That's asking healthcare providers to operate blind.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY CERTIFICATION CRITERIA MATTER IN PRACTICE**‚Źé‚ŹéI need to be clear about what we're actually debating here. The DSI certification criteria in 45 CFR 170.315(b)(6) require that decision support systems document their design, training methodology, and limitations. This isn't theoretical. Our health systems have actually tried to implement AI-assisted clinical decision support without this level of transparency, and we've learned the hard way why it matters.‚Źé‚ŹéOne of our member systems in the Midwest deployed a diagnostic support tool for sepsis screening. The vendor provided documentation that the system was trained on data from six major academic medical centers. What nobody looked at closely until after implementation was that the training data was 82 percent white patients. When our clinicians started using it, they noticed it was generating more false positives in our diverse patient population than the vendor's published studies had indicated. We caught it because we were asking questions and tracking outcomes. But most health systems don't have the analytical capacity to do that. Without the requirement for external validation and transparent reporting of performance across demographic groups, we would have just kept using it, and our patients from underrepresented groups would have gotten flagged for interventions they didn't need, which sounds minor until you realize it means more tests, more disruption, and more erosion of trust in the healthcare system.‚Źé‚ŹéThe certification criteria as written require vendors to disclose known risks and limitations. That's not a bug. That's a feature.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what the current requirement actually does in 45 CFR 170.315(b)(6):‚Źé‚Źé- Mandates that vendors provide documentation of the clinical problem the DSI is designed to address and the intended user population‚Źé- Requires disclosure of the data used to train the system and its characteristics (size, source, temporal coverage, demographic composition)‚Źé- Demands that vendors identify known limitations and risks, including performance variations across subpopulations‚Źé- Insists on independent external validation in clinical settings different from the training environment‚Źé- Creates an audit trail showing what the system is supposed to do versus what it actually does‚Źé‚ŹéWithout these requirements, we're back to a world where vendors can say, "Trust us, it works," and health systems have to decide whether to take that at face value.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT HAPPENS WHEN TRANSPARENCY DISAPPEARS**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to walk through what removal of these criteria would actually mean operationally, because I think the abstract policy discussion sometimes misses the real-world implementation.‚Źé‚ŹéIf a vendor is not required to provide model cards or external validation data, a health system's compliance officer can't verify the system performs as claimed. Our contracts with vendors now routinely include language requiring them to disclose training data characteristics and performance metrics across demographic groups. That's because we've learned we need it. If those requirements go away at the certification level, we'll either have to enforce them ourselves individually (which means every health system negotiates from scratch with every vendor) or we accept opacity as normal.‚Źé‚ŹéThe second consequence is harder to quantify but possibly more important. Right now, when something goes wrong with a clinical decision support system, there's a paper trail. We can ask: what population was this trained on? What external validation was done? What were the known risks? If adverse outcomes occur, we can actually investigate whether the system performed outside its validated parameters or whether something unexpected happened. Without transparency requirements, we lose that investigative capacity. The FDA doesn't have the resources to audit every DSI system in the market. State medical boards don't have the expertise. That responsibility would fall on individual health systems, which is inefficient and leaves gaps.‚Źé‚ŹéThe third consequence is that it creates competitive disadvantage for vendors who do invest in rigorous validation. If I'm a well-meaning vendor who spent two years and several million dollars validating my system across diverse populations and documenting limitations, and I'm competing against a vendor who cut corners and just put something on the market without that work, the regulatory playing field is now tilted against me. The transparency requirements create a level field where everyone has to do the work or nobody buys their product. Remove those requirements, and the market incentivizes cutting corners.‚Źé‚Źé**OUR SPECIFIC RECOMMENDATION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Healthcare Leadership Council strongly recommends that 45 CFR 170.315(b)(6) DSI certification criteria remain in place, including the requirements for:‚Źé‚Źé1. Model card documentation of intended use, target population, and known risks‚Źé2. Disclosure of training data characteristics and composition‚Źé3. External validation in clinical settings representative of intended use‚Źé4. Performance metrics stratified by demographic subgroups‚Źé5. Clear documentation of system limitations and conditions under which performance may degrade‚Źé‚ŹéWe understand the argument that certification requirements slow down vendor innovation and market entry. That's true. They do. That's intentional. The burden exists because the alternative (unvetted algorithms making clinical recommendations without documentation or validation) creates risk to patient safety and data integrity. The question is not whether to have burden, but where to place it. Right now, that burden falls on vendors. The alternative is to place it on health systems and patients, who are less equipped to bear it.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THIS MATTERS FOR HEALTH EQUITY SPECIFICALLY**‚Źé‚ŹéI'm going to come back to that woman from Kansas one more time, because her daughter's experience is not unique. According to research from the American Medical Association published in 2023, algorithmic bias in clinical decision support systems disproportionately affects patients from racial and ethnic minorities, patients with disabilities, and patients from lower-income backgrounds. These populations are already navigating healthcare systems with structural inequities built in. When we add algorithmic bias on top of that without any requirement for vendors to disclose, test, or document performance disparities, we're not innovating. We're automating existing discrimination.‚Źé‚ŹéThe model card and external validation requirements exist partly because of this problem. When vendors have to document the composition of their training data and performance metrics across demographic groups, it creates visibility into whether their system works equally well for all patients. It doesn't solve bias, but it makes it harder to hide.‚Źé‚ŹéIf we remove these requirements, what happens? Vendors have no incentive to test for performance disparities. Health systems have no contractual basis to demand that testing. Regulators can't verify it's been done. Patients don't know whether the algorithm flagging them for a certain intervention was developed and validated on people who look like them or not. That's not deregulation. That's regulatory abdication.‚Źé‚Źé**PRACTICAL IMPLEMENTATION CONCERNS**‚Źé‚ŹéI should note that we're not opposed to streamlining certification processes or reducing unnecessary administrative burden. There are parts of the certification framework that could be modernized. But the DSI model card requirement isn't unnecessary burden. It's the minimum documentation needed for informed clinical decision-making.‚Źé‚ŹéOne thing we've heard from vendors is that model card requirements are vague or that external validation is expensive. On the first point, the current regulatory language at 45 CFR 170.315(b)(6) is actually fairly specific about what needs to be documented. It's not perfect, and there's room to clarify expectations. We'd be open to working with ONC to make those requirements clearer without removing them entirely. On the second point about expense: yes, external validation costs money. That's a feature, not a bug. It's the cost of putting a system into clinical use that will influence patient care decisions. That cost should be borne by the vendor, not distributed across hundreds of health systems each doing their own validation studies.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE'RE HEARING FROM VENDORS**‚Źé‚ŹéWe've had conversations with several health IT vendors about their concerns with the current certification criteria. The most honest ones acknowledge that external validation is expensive and time-consuming. Some smaller vendors say they can't afford it. That's a real constraint, and it's worth taking seriously. But the solution isn't to remove the requirement. It's to think about whether there are ways to make validation more efficient without compromising rigor. Could ONC create a shared validation registry where vendors can contribute to or draw from external validation studies? Could CMS create a more streamlined pathway for vendors with limited resources? Those are policy options worth exploring.‚Źé‚ŹéThe less honest vendors essentially say, "Trust us, this works," and they're frustrated that they have to prove it. We should be skeptical of that argument. In any regulated industry, proof of safety and efficacy is a baseline requirement, not a burden to be eliminated. We don't let pharmaceutical companies skip clinical trials because it's expensive. We shouldn't let health IT vendors skip validation because it's inconvenient.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC CONCERNS ABOUT THE PROPOSED DEREGULATION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe broader proposal in this RFI suggests removing or significantly weakening certification criteria across multiple areas. For DSI specifically, I want to flag three concerns.‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, if we remove transparency requirements for decision support systems but keep them for other health IT functions, we're creating a regulatory arbitrage situation where vendors can route functionality through DSI modules to avoid transparency requirements. That would undermine the whole structure.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, removing these requirements now, while AI and machine learning in healthcare are still relatively immature, is particularly risky. We're at a moment when we need more transparency and validation, not less. In five years, when AI clinical decision support is ubiquitous and we've worked out best practices for validation and bias testing, we might have a different conversation. Right now, we're still figuring out what works and what doesn't. Transparency requirements are part of how we figure that out.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, the proposal seems to assume that market forces will drive vendors toward transparency and validation even without regulatory requirements. I don't think that assumption holds up empirically. The market incentivizes speed to market and lower costs. Vendors who cut corners on validation can undercut vendors who invest in rigor. Without regulatory requirements leveling the playing field, the market doesn't reward good behavior. It rewards cheap behavior.‚Źé‚Źé**ALTERNATIVE APPROACHES WORTH CONSIDERING**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the concern is specifically that the current model card documentation requirements are too prescriptive or create unnecessary burden, there are ways to address that without removing the requirements entirely. The Healthcare Leadership Council would support exploring:‚Źé‚Źé- Clearer regulatory language specifying exactly what should be included in model cards to reduce ambiguity and vendor confusion‚Źé- A standardized model card format so vendors aren't starting from scratch with every certification‚Źé- A tiered approach where different types of DSI systems face proportionate requirements based on clinical risk‚Źé- Phased implementation that gives vendors time to build validation infrastructure‚Źé- Collaboration with academic medical centers to create shared external validation studies that reduce costs for individual vendors‚Źé‚ŹéWhat we would not support is removing the requirement for external validation or removing the requirement to disclose training data characteristics and known risks. Those are the core elements that make the system trustworthy.‚Źé‚Źé**CLOSING THOUGHTS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe woman from Kansas asked me a question I still think about. She wanted to know whether we're actually making healthcare better when we deploy algorithms we don't fully understand, based on data we haven't examined, designed for populations that may not include her daughter. I think that's the right question to be asking. And I think the answer is: we can make healthcare better with AI and algorithmic decision support, but only if we insist on transparency, validation, and accountability.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criteria requiring model cards and external validation are how we insist on those things. They're not perfect, and they're worth refining. But they're worth keeping.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Healthcare Leadership Council respectfully urges the Office of the Secretary and ONC to retain 45 CFR 170.315(b)(6) DSI certification criteria, including all current requirements for model card documentation, external validation, and disclosure of training data characteristics and known limitations.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-16T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-16T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ8‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHospital and health system procurement teams need transparent AI model information to make evidence-based decisions about which tools to adopt and how to implement them safely in their local contexts.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒhopeful‚ôĒNebraska‚ôĒpolicy director‚ôĒ64‚ôĒLast spring, I sat across from a woman in her seventies at our council's listening session‚ÄĒa retired teacher from rural Kansas‚ÄĒwho broke down describing how her daughter's rare autoimmune condition took three years to diagnose because her medical records were scattered across seven different providers with no way to access them collectively. When I think about expanded data access now, I keep seeing her face, and I wonder whether the safeguards we're building will actually protect people like her daughter from having that information compiled and sold to the very insurers who denied her coverage based on incomplete information before.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2400
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0171‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒGeorge King‚ôĒiPill inc‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒiPill Inc. opposes removing Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements for AI-assisted clinical decision support tools, arguing that model card and transparency disclosure requirements protect patient safety by ensuring clinic...‚ôĒ**Submitted on behalf of iPill Inc.**‚Źé‚ŹéGeorge King, Healthcare Administrator‚ŹéiPill Inc.‚ŹéNorth Dakota‚Źé‚Źé---‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed deregulation framework for health IT certification warrants careful consideration, particularly regarding the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) criterion. While iPill Inc. supports reasonable efforts to reduce unnecessary compliance burden, we have significant concerns about removing the model card and transparency disclosure requirements that currently govern AI-assisted clinical decision support tools. Our position is grounded in nearly three years of implementing and maintaining these systems across multiple care settings, and we believe the current DSI certification requirements represent a floor, not a ceiling, for patient safety in this domain.‚Źé‚Źé**The Current State of DSI Certification**‚Źé‚ŹéThe existing DSI certification criterion under 45 CFR 170.315(a)(4) requires vendors to disclose intended use, target population, known performance limitations, training data characteristics, and external validation results. This requirement sounds bureaucratic in the abstract, but in practice it serves a specific function: it forces vendors like us to actually know what our tools do and for whom they work reliably. Before we implemented our discharge optimization system at iPill, we had to document that it was designed for adult patients in acute care settings, not pediatric populations. We had to specify that it was trained on data from Midwest health systems and acknowledge that its performance in other regions remained unvalidated. We had to list the specific clinical scenarios where the model underperformed.‚Źé‚ŹéThis wasn't onerous. It took our team about four weeks to compile. But it fundamentally changed how we approached validation and how our customers understood what they were buying. When a healthcare system in South Carolina asked to use our tool, we could tell them honestly: we don't have external validation data from Southern hospitals, so your clinical staff should treat this as a supportive tool, not a directive tool, until you've accumulated six months of local performance data. That conversation has real value. It prevented what could have been a problematic implementation.‚Źé‚Źé**Why This Matters for Patient Safety**‚Źé‚ŹéThe concern about removing DSI transparency requirements isn't abstract. Consider what happens in a busy discharge planning workflow. A nurse is reviewing five patients' cases before shift end. She sees a recommendation from a decision support tool: discharge Patient A with home health services. The tool was built on data from 50,000 discharges, mostly from urban academic medical centers. Patient A lives in a rural area with no home health agencies within 40 miles. The nurse doesn't know this context. She doesn't know the model was trained primarily on urban populations. She doesn't know that external validation in rural settings hasn't been completed. She makes the discharge recommendation based on the tool's output.‚Źé‚ŹéThis isn't a failure of the nurse. It's a failure of the system that presented an AI recommendation without adequate context about its limitations. The current DSI criterion exists because we learned hard lessons about clinical decision support in the early 2000s. Studies by Middleton and colleagues (Journal of the American Medical Informatics Association, 2016) documented that clinicians misuse decision support when they lack understanding of how the tool works, what data it uses, and where its confidence intervals narrow.‚Źé‚ŹéThe model card requirement isn't compliance theater. It's a mechanism to ensure that the people making clinical decisions have the information they need to weight that recommendation appropriately. Removing it doesn't reduce burden on vendors meaningfully. It shifts burden to providers and patients, who must now do detective work to understand what they're using.‚Źé‚Źé**What "Deregulation" Means in Health IT**‚Źé‚ŹéHere's where I need to be honest about something that frustrates me. When policymakers talk about reducing certification burden, they often mean reducing the work that vendors must do to prove their products are safe. But in healthcare, that burden reduction doesn't vanish. It gets redistributed. If ONC removes the requirement that we document our training data characteristics, hospitals must now spend their own compliance resources figuring out whether they should use our tool and with what caveats. They'll hire consultants. They'll build their own validation studies. They'll create internal governance processes to manage AI tools that lack transparent disclosure.‚Źé‚ŹéI've watched this pattern in our own regulatory environment. When CMS reduced certain documentation requirements around electronic health records, providers didn't experience less burden. They experienced different burden. They had to create their own internal standards. They hired more IT compliance staff. Some smaller health systems simply opted out of certain innovations because they couldn't afford to do the due diligence that regulation used to require vendors to do.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI requirement isn't a burden we should shed. It's a cost that should be borne by the vendor, not by the provider. We're the ones building the algorithms. We should be the ones documenting how they work.‚Źé‚Źé**The Specific Case for Retaining Model Card Requirements**‚Źé‚ŹéiPill's position is straightforward: retain the current DSI certification criterion with its requirements for model cards and transparency disclosures, specifically including intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. This isn't a request to add requirements. It's a request not to remove them.‚Źé‚ŹéThe practical reasons are several:‚Źé‚Źé**Intended use documentation**: When we specify that our tool is designed for discharge planning in adult acute care settings, this prevents misapplication. A customer can't claim our tool is appropriate for pediatric mental health decisions when we've documented otherwise. This protects both the vendor and the provider from liability and, more importantly, from patient harm.‚Źé‚Źé**Target population specificity**: Our training data came from 47 health systems across 12 states, representing roughly 2.3 million discharges over five years. The population was 68 percent White, 22 percent Black, and 10 percent other races and ethnicities. The median age was 67. The median length of stay was 4.2 days. When we document this, a provider using our tool in a pediatric hospital understands immediately that the model may not perform well on their population. Without this documentation, they might assume the tool works universally.‚Źé‚Źé**Known risks and limitations**: Our tool occasionally recommends discharges for patients with complex social situations that it hasn't been trained to recognize. We've documented this. Specifically, we know the model underperforms in cases where patients have untreated substance use disorders that weren't captured in structured EHR fields. We've documented this limitation. Providers who know this can compensate. Providers who don't know it might make unsafe discharge decisions.‚Źé‚Źé**Training data characteristics**: This is where I think the current requirement actually serves vendors well. By documenting how we built our training set, we establish a clear record that we made reasonable efforts to build a representative model. If a provider later claims our tool discriminated against a particular population, we have documentation showing what data we used and how we validated it. Removing this requirement doesn't reduce our liability. It increases it.‚Źé‚Źé**External validation results**: We've validated our discharge tool in three health systems outside our original training set. The results were good but not identical to our internal validation. Performance was slightly lower in one system and slightly higher in another. This variation is normal and important. When we document these external validation results, providers understand that the tool performs reasonably well across different settings but that local validation may refine performance. This is the kind of transparency that builds appropriate trust.‚Źé‚Źé**The Economic Reality**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to acknowledge the legitimate concern behind the deregulation proposal. Certification is expensive. For a smaller vendor, the cost of compiling model cards, conducting external validation, and documenting training data can reach $200,000 to $400,000 per product. This is a real barrier to market entry.‚Źé‚ŹéBut here's what I've learned: removing the requirement doesn't actually reduce that cost significantly. It just moves it. If we remove the DSI requirement, healthcare systems will implement their own validation processes. They'll require vendors to provide exactly the information that the DSI criterion currently requires. The difference is that instead of having one standard that all vendors must meet, we'll have dozens of health systems creating their own standards. Some will be more rigorous than the current criterion. Some will be less rigorous but more expensive to satisfy because they're not standardized.‚Źé‚ŹéThe better solution isn't to eliminate transparency requirements. It's to streamline them. The current DSI criterion could be refined to reduce unnecessary documentation while retaining the core elements that matter for patient safety.‚Źé‚Źé**Specific Recommendations**‚Źé‚ŹéRather than removing the DSI certification requirement, we recommend the following modifications:‚Źé‚Źé1. Establish a standardized model card template that vendors must complete but that doesn't require extensive narrative documentation. This reduces the work of compiling information without reducing the substance of what's disclosed.‚Źé‚Źé2. Allow vendors to reference external validation studies published in peer-reviewed journals rather than requiring new studies conducted specifically for certification. This reduces cost without reducing rigor.‚Źé‚Źé3. Permit vendors to document training data characteristics at an aggregate level (e.g., "trained on 2.3 million discharges from 47 health systems across 12 states") rather than requiring individual-level data documentation. This protects patient privacy while maintaining transparency.‚Źé‚Źé4. Require updates to model cards only when the underlying model changes materially, not with every software release. This reduces the certification burden for incremental improvements.‚Źé‚Źé5. Create a tiered certification approach where simpler decision support tools (those making recommendations in domains with extensive external research) require less documentation than complex tools in novel domains.‚Źé‚ŹéThese modifications would reduce burden on vendors without removing the transparency requirements that protect patients and providers.‚Źé‚Źé**What We've Learned from Implementation**‚Źé‚ŹéOur experience implementing AI-assisted discharge planning across multiple health systems has taught us something important: clinicians trust decision support more when they understand its limitations. This sounds counterintuitive, but it's true. When we provide a discharge recommendation with full transparency about training data and known limitations, clinicians engage more carefully with the recommendation. They ask better questions. They're more likely to override the recommendation when their clinical judgment differs.‚Źé‚ŹéConversely, when decision support appears as a black box recommendation, clinicians either follow it uncritically or ignore it entirely. There's no middle ground. Transparency creates appropriate skepticism, which is exactly what we want in clinical decision-making.‚Źé‚ŹéOne of our healthcare system partners in Minnesota conducted an interesting analysis. They tracked how often their discharge planners overrode our tool's recommendations in 2022 (when we provided detailed model documentation) versus 2023 (before documentation requirements were widely publicized). The override rate was identical: about 12 percent. But the documentation in the EHR about why clinicians overrode the recommendation improved significantly. When clinicians understood the tool's limitations, they documented their reasoning more clearly. This created a richer record for quality improvement.‚Źé‚Źé**The Broader Regulatory Landscape**‚Źé‚ŹéIt's worth noting that the DSI certification requirement isn't unique to ONC. The FDA's approach to software as a medical device increasingly emphasizes algorithmic transparency. The FTC has been active in scrutinizing AI tools that lack adequate disclosure of limitations. State medical boards are developing guidance on physician use of AI tools, much of which depends on vendors providing clear information about what their tools do and don't do.‚Źé‚ŹéIf ONC removes the DSI requirement, we'll face a fragmented landscape where different regulators require different disclosures. This creates more burden, not less, because vendors must satisfy multiple standards. The current DSI requirement actually reduces overall burden by establishing a single standard that satisfies multiple regulatory perspectives.‚Źé‚Źé**Addressing the Competitiveness Argument**‚Źé‚ŹéOne argument for deregulation is that certification requirements disadvantage smaller vendors relative to large technology companies. This argument has merit in some contexts, but it doesn't apply strongly to DSI certification. The work of compiling a model card and documenting training data is genuinely scalable. A small vendor and a large vendor doing this work spend roughly similar amounts of time and money. What differs is the total number of products each vendor certifies, not the per-product cost.‚Źé‚ŹéMoreover, removing the DSI requirement wouldn't actually level the playing field. Large vendors have advantages in other areas: sales and marketing, customer support, integration with other products. Smaller vendors compete on innovation and niche focus. The DSI requirement doesn't prevent this competition. If anything, it enables smaller vendors to compete by clearly demonstrating that their specialized tool works well for a specific population or use case.‚Źé‚Źé**Patient and Provider Perspective**‚Źé‚ŹéI keep coming back to what these requirements mean for the people actually using the tool. A discharge planner at a rural hospital in North Dakota using our tool needs to know: Was this model built on rural population data? Was it validated in rural settings? How does its performance compare when it's applied to my specific patient population?‚Źé‚ŹéWithout the model card requirement, she has no reliable way to answer these questions. She can call the vendor and ask, but she'll get whatever answer the sales team thinks will help them make a sale. She can try to conduct her own validation, but she's a clinician, not a data scientist. She can hire a consultant, but that's expensive and time-consuming.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current requirement ensures she gets a standardized, auditable answer. That matters. It matters for patient safety. It matters for her ability to use the tool appropriately.‚Źé‚Źé**Conclusion**‚Źé‚ŹéiPill Inc. supports reasonable efforts to reduce unnecessary regulatory burden on health IT vendors. We recognize that compliance costs can be substantial and that reducing barriers to innovation serves patients and providers. But the DSI certification requirement isn't unnecessary burden. It's a foundational transparency requirement that serves legitimate safety and accountability functions.‚Źé‚ŹéRather than removing this requirement, we recommend refining it to reduce documentation costs while retaining the core elements that matter: clear disclosure of intended use, target population, known limitations, training data characteristics, and external validation results. This approach balances the legitimate interest in reducing burden with the equally legitimate interest in ensuring that AI-assisted clinical decision support tools are used appropriately.‚Źé‚ŹéThe healthcare system is complex and fragile. Decision support tools that lack transparency about their limitations and performance characteristics introduce risk that extends beyond the vendor to the provider and ultimately to patients. The DSI criterion represents a reasonable balance between innovation and safety. We should refine it, not remove it.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-22T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-22T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ7‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒhopeful‚ôĒNorth Dakota‚ôĒhealthcare administrator‚ôĒ35‚ôĒLast spring, I watched our entire EHR implementation timeline slip by six months because our vendor couldn't justify the certification costs for a relatively minor customization that would have cut our discharge-processing time in half‚ÄĒsomething our nursing staff had been asking for since 2021. That's when I realized how much of our operational roadmap gets written by regulatory overhead rather than by what actually improves patient care.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2349
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0172‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒJoshua Green‚ôĒHealthcare Leadership Council‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Healthcare Leadership Council opposes eliminating Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements, arguing that transparency and validation standards are essential to prevent algorithmic bias and ensure patient safety across di...‚ôĒ**Comment on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Healthcare Leadership Council appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory framework. Our member organizations operate across care delivery, health plans, life sciences, and health information technology, and we have a direct stake in how certification requirements, standards, and information blocking regulations evolve. We also have a responsibility to patients and the clinical teams who depend on accurate, reliable health information systems.‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be direct about our position: we support reducing unnecessary regulatory burden where it genuinely improves innovation and market entry. The current certification landscape is dense, and some requirements create friction without proportional benefit. But the proposed elimination of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria, particularly the transparency and validation requirements embedded in the model card framework, concerns us for reasons that go beyond abstract regulatory philosophy. These requirements exist because we have seen what happens when they do not.‚Źé‚Źé**BACKGROUND: WHY MODEL CARDS MATTER IN PRACTICE**‚Źé‚ŹéLast year, I worked with a member organization on a case study that clarified this for me. One of their integrated delivery systems implemented a new AI-assisted clinical decision support tool for sepsis management. The tool had been developed by a reputable vendor, carried appropriate regulatory approvals for its narrow use case, and was marketed as ready for deployment. What was missing, however, was transparent documentation of what the model was trained on, what populations it had been validated against, and what failure modes were known. When the system went live, clinicians in a rural facility discovered within weeks that the model's recommendations degraded significantly for patients with renal impairment, a population that made up roughly 30 percent of their patient base. The vendor had trained the model on data from three urban academic centers where renal patients represented less than 8 percent of the cohort. No one had documented this. No one had flagged it as a known limitation. The tool had to be pulled, revalidated, and reimplemented. The delay disrupted care coordination for several months.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not a hypothetical failure. It is one example among many that our member organizations encounter regularly. When health systems evaluate new clinical decision support tools, they are making judgments about patient safety under conditions of incomplete information. The DSI certification criteria, including the requirement for model cards specifying intended use, target populations, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results, exist because clinicians and health system leaders cannot reliably assess tool reliability without this information.‚Źé‚ŹéThe 21st Century Cures Act Information Blocking Rule (42 CFR Part 171) establishes a framework for access to health information, but it does not address the quality or reliability of the tools through which that information flows. Certification criteria under 45 CFR Part 170 do. They create a floor below which tools sold into the market should not fall. Removing DSI certification criteria would eliminate one of the few systematic mechanisms that ensure clinical decision support tools have been scrutinized for safety and validated across relevant patient populations before widespread adoption.‚Źé‚Źé**THE EQUITY DIMENSION THAT GETS OVERLOOKED**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed deregulation also intersects with health equity in ways that deserve explicit attention. Algorithmic bias in clinical decision support is not a theoretical concern. A 2019 study published in Science found that a widely used clinical algorithm systematically underestimated health needs for Black patients because it used healthcare spending as a proxy for medical need, failing to account for historical disparities in access and treatment patterns (Obermeyer et al., Science, 364:6441, 2019). The algorithm had been deployed in major health systems before the bias was identified. When it was, the harm had already occurred.‚Źé‚ŹéOur member organizations that serve safety-net populations and historically marginalized communities face a particular challenge. They often have limited resources for independent validation of new tools. They rely on certification frameworks and transparent disclosure requirements to flag potential problems. If we eliminate the requirement that decision support tools document their training data composition, intended populations, and external validation results, we are, in effect, shifting the burden of identifying bias and population-specific failures onto the health systems least equipped to bear it. The rural facility I mentioned earlier had a clinical informatics staff of two people. They caught the sepsis model's renal impairment problem only because a nurse happened to notice a pattern in recommendations. Most health systems would not have caught it.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT THE PROPOSED RULE ACTUALLY REMOVES**‚Źé‚ŹéThe notice of proposed rulemaking indicates intent to remove or substantially revise certification criteria for health IT modules that include clinical decision support functions, specifically DSI certification. The current DSI criterion (45 CFR 170.315(a)(1)) requires:‚Źé‚Źé- Documentation of the tool's intended use and clinical context‚Źé- Specification of the target population for which validation occurred‚Źé- Identification of known limitations and known risks‚Źé- Description of training data characteristics and sources‚Źé- Evidence of external validation on populations distinct from the training dataset‚Źé- Transparency regarding performance metrics and failure modes‚Źé‚ŹéThe rationale offered in the proposed rule is that these requirements impose compliance costs on developers without commensurate benefit, that market forces and liability law provide sufficient incentive for quality, and that FDA oversight of certain algorithmic tools is sufficient to ensure safety. We disagree with this reasoning, for three specific reasons.‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, FDA oversight and certification criteria operate on different timelines and cover different ground. FDA clearance for a clinical decision support tool typically focuses on whether the tool performs its stated function within a defined scope. It does not systematically evaluate whether the tool's performance is consistent across demographic groups, whether its training data adequately represents the populations that will actually use it, or whether health systems implementing the tool have the information they need to use it safely. The FDA's own guidance on clinical decision support (FDA, Clinical Decision Support Software, 2019) acknowledges that manufacturers should disclose intended use, training data sources, and performance characteristics. That guidance is not mandatory. Certification criteria make it mandatory.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, market forces are insufficient here because information asymmetry is severe. A health system purchasing a clinical decision support tool cannot easily reverse-engineer what it was trained on or validate its performance on their specific patient populations without significant technical expertise. They rely on vendors to disclose this information. Vendors have financial incentive to minimize disclosure of limitations. The DSI certification requirement creates a baseline standard that forces disclosure regardless of vendor preference. Removing it does not unleash market efficiency; it enables selective transparency.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, liability law is a poor substitute for prospective validation requirements. Liability creates incentive to defend against claims after harm occurs, not to prevent harm before it occurs. It is also expensive and slow. A health system that implements a biased algorithm and experiences poor outcomes for a particular population may eventually prevail in litigation, but the patients harmed in the interim have borne the cost. Prospective transparency and validation requirements prevent that harm from occurring in the first place.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC CONCERNS WITH THE PROPOSED DEREGULATION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule suggests that certification criteria create burden on developers without measurable benefit. We would push back on this claim in two ways. First, the burden is not as substantial as suggested. Developers of clinical decision support tools already document training data, performance metrics, and intended use for internal purposes and for regulatory submissions. Formalizing this documentation through certification criteria does not create new work; it systematizes and standardizes work that responsible developers are already doing. Second, the benefits are measurable. Certification criteria reduce the variation in disclosure quality and completeness, which allows health systems to make more reliable assessments of tool suitability for their populations and use cases. This is not a speculative benefit. It is a direct consequence of standardized requirements.‚Źé‚ŹéWe are also concerned that the proposed rule conflates "deregulation" with "market expansion" without asking whether market expansion should be the primary objective. The stated goal is to reduce barriers to entry for new health IT developers and to promote competition. Those are legitimate policy objectives. But they should not override safety and equity considerations. There are ways to reduce developer burden without eliminating transparency requirements. For example:‚Źé‚Źé- Certification criteria could be streamlined to require documentation of training data and intended population, external validation results, and known limitations, without requiring specific formatting or additional testing beyond what responsible developers already conduct.‚Źé- Developers could be given extended timelines for compliance, particularly small companies with limited regulatory affairs capacity.‚Źé- The certification process itself could be accelerated to reduce time-to-market without reducing substantive requirements.‚Źé- Alternative pathways could be created for certain classes of low-risk decision support tools that have narrow, well-defined use cases.‚Źé‚ŹéThese approaches reduce burden without abandoning the core requirement that clinical decision support tools be transparent about their capabilities and limitations.‚Źé‚Źé**CLINICAL WORKFLOW AND IMPLEMENTATION REALITY**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to ground this in how clinical decision support actually works in practice. When a clinician receives a recommendation from a decision support tool, they are making a real-time judgment about whether to follow it. That judgment depends on whether they understand the tool's reliability in their specific context. A tool that performs well in a predominantly white, insured population may perform poorly for uninsured patients with complex comorbidities. A tool trained on data from hospitals may not generalize to ambulatory care. A tool optimized for efficiency may sacrifice sensitivity for specific high-risk populations.‚Źé‚ŹéClinicians cannot make these judgments without information. The DSI certification requirement ensures that information is available. If we remove that requirement, clinicians will be left with vendor marketing claims and their own clinical intuition. Clinical intuition is valuable, but it is not a substitute for systematic validation evidence.‚Źé‚ŹéWe have also seen, across our member organizations, that health system leadership increasingly wants to know what is in the "black box" of algorithmic tools before they deploy them. This is not regulatory paranoia. It is responsible governance. When a clinical decision support tool contributes to an adverse outcome, the health system is liable, not the vendor. Health system leaders need information to make informed decisions about implementation, monitoring, and modification of these tools.‚Źé‚Źé**RECOMMENDATION: RETAIN DSI CERTIFICATION WITH STREAMLINED REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéOur recommendation is straightforward: retain the DSI certification criterion. Do not eliminate it. If the concern is developer burden, reduce the burden through streamlining and extended timelines, but do not abandon the requirement for transparency and validation.‚Źé‚ŹéSpecifically, we recommend:‚Źé‚Źé1. Maintain the core requirement that clinical decision support tools document intended use, target population, training data characteristics, and known limitations.‚Źé2. Streamline the certification process to allow developers to submit existing documentation (regulatory submissions, internal validation reports, vendor specifications) rather than creating new documentation from scratch.‚Źé3. Extend the compliance timeline for DSI certification to 24 months from the final rule, with additional extensions available for small developers.‚Źé4. Create a tiered certification pathway where low-risk tools with narrow, well-defined use cases can be certified through an expedited process with reduced documentation requirements.‚Źé5. Require that all clinical decision support tools deployed in health systems include, at minimum, a publicly available summary of validation evidence, training data sources, and known limitations, even if full certification is not pursued.‚Źé‚ŹéThese changes reduce burden on developers while preserving the transparency and validation requirements that protect patients and support equitable care.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Healthcare Leadership Council supports regulatory modernization and market innovation. We also support patient safety and equity in care. These are not competing values. The DSI certification criteria exist because we have learned, through painful experience, that clinical decision support tools can cause harm when they are deployed without clear understanding of their capabilities and limitations. Removing these requirements would shift risk from developers to health systems and patients. We should not do that.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current regulatory framework is not perfect. It can be streamlined, modernized, and made more accessible to new developers. But the fundamental requirement that clinical decision support tools be transparent about their training, validation, and known limitations should remain.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-31T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-31T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ6‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHospital and health system procurement teams need transparent AI model information to make evidence-based decisions about which tools to adopt and how to implement them safely in their local contexts.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒconcerned‚ôĒSouth Carolina‚ôĒsenior policy analyst‚ôĒ34‚ôĒLast year, my mother was diagnosed with type 2 diabetes, and watching her navigate three separate health systems to piece together her own medical history was deeply frustrating‚ÄĒbut it also made me realize how fragmented access protections have become. When I learned that proposed changes would expand third-party data sharing without strengthening the guardrails around who can access that information and how it's used, I immediately thought of her, and of the countless patients in underserved communities who lack the literacy or resources to monitor whether their data is being misused.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2001
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0173‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒSandra Jackson‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA patient advocates for retaining AI transparency and certification requirements for clinical decision support tools in health IT, arguing that model cards documenting AI system testing, limitations, and training data are essential safeguards for ...‚ôĒ**RE: Proposed Deregulation of Health IT Certification Criteria ‚Äď Concerns About AI Transparency Requirements**‚Źé‚ŹéLast spring I had to switch cardiologists when mine retired, and his office couldn't pull my echocardiogram results from my old practice for three weeks. I ended up repeating the entire test. It scared me that there could be gaps in what my new doctor knew about my health history. Now I'm reading that the rules protecting how my medical information moves between providers might get loosened, and I'm worried we're heading in the wrong direction on this.‚Źé‚ŹéI understand the goal is to make my data more portable and easier to share. That part I actually support. But I've also been reading about what's being proposed around removing certification requirements for Decision Support Interventions, or DSI ‚Äď basically the AI tools that help doctors make clinical decisions. This is where I have a serious concern.‚Źé‚Źé**Why AI Transparency Matters in Healthcare**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current certification rules require something called model cards for these AI systems. Model cards aren't complicated ‚Äď they just document what the AI tool is supposed to do, who it was tested on, what risks were found, and what data trained it. This matters because:‚Źé‚Źé- My doctor needs to know if an AI tool was tested on patients like me (a 66-year-old woman with cardiac history) before it influences decisions about my care‚Źé- Hospitals need to know if there are known failures or biases in the system before they deploy it‚Źé- If something goes wrong, there's a documented record of what the system was supposed to do and what it actually did‚Źé‚ŹéRight now, under 21 CFR Part 11 and ONC's certification criteria in 45 CFR 170.315, these transparency requirements create accountability. Removing them would mean AI tools could be used in my care with no public record of their limitations or how they were built. That's different from removing burden ‚Äď that's removing oversight.‚Źé‚Źé**What I'm Asking For**‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not asking the agency to block innovation. I'm asking them to keep the DSI certification criterion that requires AI model cards and transparency disclosures. Keep the requirement that developers document intended use, target population, known risks, and training data characteristics. Keep the requirement for external validation.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is the minimum transparency a patient should expect when an algorithm influences her medical care. When I had to switch doctors, better data portability would have helped. But if that same data gets fed into untested AI systems with no documented safeguards, portability becomes a liability.‚Źé‚ŹéPlease retain the current DSI certification requirements.‚Źé‚ŹéRespectfully,‚Źé‚ŹéSandra Jackson‚ŹéHenderson, Nevada‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-26T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-26T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe agency's claim that 'no publicly available evidence' supports transparency requirements conflates absence of evidence with evidence of absence; lack of published studies does not mean the criterion provides no value.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒfrustrated‚ôĒNevada‚ôĒadministrative assistant‚ôĒ66‚ôĒLast spring, my cardiologist retired and I had to switch to someone new across town, but his office couldn't access my echocardiogram results from the old practice for three weeks‚ÄĒI ended up repeating the test entirely, which felt like a waste and frankly scared me that there might be gaps in my care history.
