CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0174‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒWilliam Anderson‚ôĒThe Pharmacy Health Information Technology Collaborative‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Pharmacy Health Information Technology Collaborative opposes the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements for AI-assisted clinical decision support systems, arguing that transparency disclosures about...‚ôĒ**The Pharmacy Health Information Technology Collaborative's Comments on ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Proposal: Preserving Safety Standards in Decision Support Systems**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Pharmacy Health Information Technology Collaborative submits these comments regarding the proposed deregulatory actions outlined in the ASTP/ONC notice published in the Federal Register. Our organization brings together academic researchers, practicing pharmacists, hospital systems, and health IT vendors across the upper Midwest. We are writing to address a specific concern about the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria, particularly the requirements for AI model cards and transparency disclosures that document intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results.‚Źé‚ŹéLast year, a colleague at our institution‚ÄĒa clinical pharmacologist with twenty years of experience‚ÄĒnearly missed a serious drug interaction because medication histories were fragmented across three incompatible hospital EHR systems in our region. The patient had moved between Kaiser Permanente, a regional critical access hospital, and a VA clinic, each running different pharmacy software. Only by chance, during a follow-up phone conversation about a separate concern, did the pharmacist learn that the patient was taking a CYP3A4 inhibitor that would have caused dangerous accumulation of a newly prescribed statin. The systems were technically required to be interoperable under the 21st Century Cures Act and its implementing regulations (45 CFR 171), but the vendor platforms had not achieved meaningful exchange of medication data. This incident, while it ended safely, made clear to us the gap between regulatory requirements on paper and what actually happens in clinical practice when vendors prioritize proprietary lock-in over genuine data sharing.‚Źé‚ŹéWe understand the impulse behind the proposed deregulation. The Cures Act Information Blocking Rule (42 U.S.C. 300jj-52) was intended to reduce friction, and there are legitimate concerns about whether certification requirements slow innovation or burden smaller vendors. The Collaborative has reviewed the ASTP/ONC rationale, and we are not opposed to streamlining redundant standards or reducing duplicative testing burdens. However, we are deeply concerned about the specific proposal to remove DSI certification criteria requiring transparency about AI-assisted clinical decision support systems. This is not a burden we believe should be eliminated.‚Źé‚Źé**Why DSI Transparency Requirements Serve Patient Safety, Not Just Regulatory Theater**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification criterion, established in 45 CFR 170.315(a)(10), requires vendors to disclose:‚Źé‚Źé- The intended use and clinical context for which the model was designed‚Źé- The target population and any known limitations in performance across demographic subgroups‚Źé- Known risks, failure modes, and conditions under which the model may produce unreliable outputs‚Źé- Characteristics of the training data, including size, source, and potential biases‚Źé- Results from external validation studies conducted on data not used in model development‚Źé‚ŹéThese are not arbitrary compliance checkboxes. Each element addresses a distinct safety hazard that has emerged in the pharmacy and clinical informatics literature over the past five years.‚Źé‚ŹéExternal validation is particularly important. A 2023 systematic review published in the Journal of the American Medical Informatics Association (JAMIA, volume 30, issue 8) examined 47 AI-assisted clinical decision support systems implemented in real hospital environments. The authors found that 31 of the 47 systems performed significantly worse on external validation data than on the developer's internal test sets. Some of this degradation was modest (3-5% reduction in sensitivity), but 12 systems showed performance drops greater than 15%. In one case, a sepsis detection algorithm trained on academic medical center data showed 23% lower sensitivity when applied to a rural hospital population with different patient demographics and clinical workflows. Without transparency about training data characteristics and external validation results, clinicians would have no way to know whether a system was reliable in their specific setting.‚Źé‚ŹéThis concern is not theoretical. In 2022, the FDA issued a warning letter to a vendor whose medication interaction screening tool, once approved for certification, was found to have high false-negative rates in geriatric populations because the training data consisted primarily of adults under age 65 (FDA warning letter reference, available in FDA's Regulatory Actions database). The vendor had not disclosed this demographic limitation. An elderly patient in a rural clinic missed a contraindicated drug combination that the system failed to flag.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Collaborative has also spoken with pharmacy directors at regional health systems. In a survey of 28 hospital pharmacies conducted in 2024, we asked whether they would change their clinical protocols if they learned that a decision support system had not been validated on their patient population or had been trained on data from a single health system. 24 of 28 respondents said they would either disable the system or add manual verification steps. But they could not make this judgment without transparency about the model's training and validation. One director told us: "We trust our vendor, but I can't trust something I can't see."‚Źé‚Źé**The Market-Consolidation Risk We Are Watching**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Collaborative's concern goes beyond individual patient safety incidents. We are observing a troubling pattern in the pharmacy IT vendor market: as regulatory oversight loosens in other areas, vendors are racing to lock in hospital contracts with proprietary AI systems that are difficult to audit or replace. Three large vendors now control approximately 62% of the inpatient pharmacy management system market (KLAS Research, 2024). When these vendors implement AI-assisted decision support without transparency requirements, hospitals have limited ability to evaluate whether the systems are appropriate for their patient populations or to switch to competitors if performance is inadequate.‚Źé‚ŹéWithout DSI certification criteria, we anticipate several outcomes:‚Źé‚Źé- Vendors will implement more aggressive, opaque AI features without external validation, relying on terms-of-service disclaimers to shift liability to hospitals‚Źé- Hospitals will face pressure to adopt unvalidated systems because competitors have already implemented similar tools‚Źé- Smaller, regional EHR vendors will lack the resources to conduct external validation studies and will fall further behind, increasing consolidation‚Źé- Rural and safety-net hospitals will have even less ability to understand or challenge the algorithms affecting their patients‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not speculation. One vendor has already informed several of our member institutions that they plan to integrate a proprietary medication interaction AI module into their next platform release and will not provide model cards or external validation data, citing competitive sensitivity. The vendor's position is that hospitals can "trust the system" because it is "FDA-cleared" (though FDA clearance applies only to the vendor's quality management processes, not to the performance of individual AI models). Without DSI certification requirements, hospitals will have no formal mechanism to demand transparency.‚Źé‚Źé**Specific Recommendation: Retain DSI Certification Criteria with Modest Streamlining**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Collaborative respectfully requests that the final rule retain the DSI certification criteria in 45 CFR 170.315(a)(10), with the following clarifications to address any genuine compliance burden:‚Źé‚Źé1. **Streamline Documentation Format.** Rather than prescribing a specific "model card" template, allow vendors to provide transparency information in any structured format that includes the required elements (intended use, target population, known risks, training data, external validation results). This flexibility would reduce burden while preserving substance.‚Źé‚Źé2. **Scale External Validation to Risk.** Systems identified as lower-risk (e.g., informational alerts with no direct patient impact) could rely on internal validation conducted on held-out test sets, provided the vendor discloses the methodology. Higher-risk systems (e.g., those recommending drug contraindications) would require external validation on data from at least one independent healthcare organization.‚Źé‚Źé3. **Establish a Public Registry.** ONC should maintain a searchable repository of DSI disclosures so that hospitals can compare systems and researchers can track performance claims across vendors. This transparency would reward vendors who conduct rigorous validation and create competitive pressure for quality.‚Źé‚Źé4. **Provide Transition Period.** Vendors with systems already in use would have 18 months to provide required disclosures for existing systems, reducing immediate compliance burden.‚Źé‚ŹéThese modifications would preserve the safety value of DSI certification while reducing unnecessary friction. They also align with emerging standards in other regulated sectors. The FDA's proposed framework for AI/ML-based software modifications (published in the January 2021 Software as a Medical Device action plan) contemplates exactly this approach: transparency about performance and limitations, scaled to risk level, with periodic updates rather than one-time certification.‚Źé‚Źé**Closing Observation**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Collaborative recognizes that not every regulatory requirement generates proportional value. We have supported other elements of the proposed deregulation where the burden-to-benefit ratio appears unfavorable. However, DSI certification is different. The cost to vendors of providing transparent documentation about their AI systems is relatively modest compared to the development investment already made. The benefit to patient safety, clinical decision-making, and market efficiency is substantial and well-documented in peer-reviewed literature.‚Źé‚ŹéWe stand ready to work with ONC and CMS on implementation details, including the specific documentation formats and validation standards that would be most feasible for vendors while maintaining clinical utility for hospitals.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-02-04T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-04T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ4‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe agency's claim that 'no publicly available evidence' supports transparency requirements conflates absence of evidence with evidence of absence; lack of published studies does not mean the criterion provides no value.‚ôĒacademic‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒconcerned‚ôĒSouth Dakota‚ôĒassociate professor‚ôĒ61‚ôĒLast spring, I watched a colleague in our pharmacology department struggle to access patient medication histories across three different hospital systems in our region‚ÄĒeach using incompatible software platforms‚ÄĒwhich nearly resulted in a dangerous drug interaction we caught only by chance during a follow-up conversation. That experience crystallized something I've been concerned about: as oversight loosens, I see pharmacy IT vendors racing to lock in contracts with proprietary systems rather than building bridges between them, and I'm genuinely uncertain whether we're trading safety for speed.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1442
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0175‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒMelissa Moore‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe commenter opposes deregulation of AI tools in healthcare unless accompanied by mandatory transparency requirements, arguing that doctors must understand how these "black box" systems work before using them on patients to ensure accountability ...‚ôĒMy daughter did some research after I had to pay $300 for duplicate bloodwork because my old clinic wouldn't share my records with my new doctor. Turns out this happens all the time, and clinics basically have no real reason to make it easy for patients to get their own information. I get that deregulation might help some businesses move faster, but I'm worried this rule is going the wrong direction on something that actually matters for patient safety.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what concerns me. If doctors are going to start using AI tools to help with diagnosis or treatment decisions, they need to understand what those tools are actually doing. Right now a lot of these systems are basically black boxes, and I've heard clinics sometimes can't even explain why the system flagged something or made a recommendation. When I had my duplicate bloodwork situation, at least I knew a person ordered those tests, even if they couldn't access my old records. But if an AI system tells a doctor something and nobody really understands how it got there, how do patients like me hold anyone accountable when something goes wrong? We need doctors to have real information about how these tools work, not just trust them because they're automated.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm asking that any rules loosening certification requirements for AI in healthcare require transparency about how these systems work. Doctors need to understand their tools before using them on patients. This isn't about slowing down innovation. It's about making sure innovation doesn't mean patients get treated by systems nobody comprehends.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-10T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-10T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒmedium‚ôĒresigned‚ôĒNew Jersey‚ôĒwarehouse worker‚ôĒ45‚ôĒLast year when I switched doctors, my new one couldn't get my test results from the old clinic for three weeks, and I ended up getting duplicate bloodwork done‚ÄĒcost me $300 out of pocket even with insurance. I figured it was just how things work, but my daughter looked into it and said the clinics were basically gatekeeping my own medical records. If they actually had to share that stuff easier, maybe I wouldn't have wasted money, but I guess I'm worried some other company could just buy up all that health data without me knowing about it.‚ôĒDoctors need to actually understand what AI tools are doing before they use them on patients‚ÄĒmodel cards and transparency requirements are the only way to make sure that happens instead of just trusting a black box. | When I had to get duplicate bloodwork because records couldn't transfer, it cost me real money and real time; if AI systems are going to be used in healthcare, at least we need to know how they work so clinics can't just hide behind 'the algorithm said so' when something goes wrong.‚ôĒKeeping transparency requirements for AI in healthcare protects patients like me from being treated by systems nobody‚ÄĒnot even the doctors‚ÄĒactually understands.‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ259
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0176‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒMichael Lopez‚ôĒAinsley Advisory Group‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒAinsley Advisory Group urges the retention and strengthening of the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion requiring AI model transparency disclosures (model cards), arguing that such requirements are essential for hospital procure...‚ôĒRe: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity‚Źé‚ŹéAinsley Advisory Group submits this comment on behalf of our organization and the regional healthcare providers we serve. We are a health systems advisory firm based in Columbus, Ohio, and we work directly with hospital CFOs, chief information officers, and compliance officers across the Midwest on health information technology implementation and policy matters. Our perspective is grounded in the real costs and operational consequences that emerge when certification frameworks are either too rigid or too permissive, and we want to speak to one specific criterion that we believe should remain in place despite the broader deregulatory intent of this proposal.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CONTEXT WE'RE OPERATING IN**‚Źé‚ŹéLast spring, we spent eighteen months and approximately $2.3 million implementing what our vendor called a "fully certified, seamlessly integrated" electronic health record system. The vendor is a smaller, regional player, and they promised integration with our lab infrastructure would be straightforward. It wasn't. We've been stuck in a costly manual workaround for months now, and switching systems would trigger another full certification cycle that we cannot absorb financially or operationally. This isn't a complaint about the vendor alone. It's a window into why certification standards matter.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen health IT vendors make claims about their products, hospitals like ours have to trust that those claims have been tested against something real. The certification process is supposed to be that something real. But the certification criteria themselves vary in how much they actually protect the buyer and, more importantly, the patient. Some criteria are genuinely useful. Others create compliance theater without safety benefit. And some, we believe, are being targeted for removal when they should stay in place.‚Źé‚Źé**DECISION SUPPORT INTERVENTIONS AND AI TRANSPARENCY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe criterion we want to focus on is the one governing Decision Support Interventions, or DSI. Specifically, we're talking about the requirement that AI models used in clinical decision support be accompanied by model cards and transparency disclosures that include intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. The proposed rule appears to be considering whether this criterion should be reduced or eliminated.‚Źé‚ŹéWe believe it should not be. In fact, we think the transparency requirements should be strengthened, not weakened.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's why this matters beyond regulatory compliance language. When we evaluate health IT systems for our clients, we're increasingly seeing AI components embedded in workflows where clinicians may not even know they're making decisions informed by a machine learning model. A lab ordering system might use AI to suggest which tests to order. A diagnostic support tool might use AI to flag abnormal results. A workflow system might use AI to predict patient no-shows or readmission risk. These aren't theoretical features. They're in the systems being deployed right now in hospitals across Ohio and the surrounding region.‚Źé‚ŹéThe problem is that most of the time, clinicians don't know what data these models were trained on, what populations they were validated against, or what risks are baked into their outputs. I'll be direct: this concerns me. It concerns the CFOs we work with, and it concerns the compliance officers and clinical leadership we advise.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THE MODEL CARD REQUIREMENT EXISTS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe model card requirement comes from research in machine learning transparency and from lessons learned in other industries where algorithmic decision-making has produced unexpected harms. A model card is essentially a data sheet for an AI model. It documents what the model does, what it was trained on, what populations it was tested with, what its known limitations are, and what it was designed to do versus what it shouldn't be used for.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not an abstract concern. In 2019, researchers at UC Berkeley and Obermeyer Health published work showing that a widely used clinical risk prediction algorithm had been trained on healthcare spending as a proxy for health risk. Because spending patterns are shaped by structural racism and unequal access to care, the model systematically underestimated risk for Black patients (Obermeyer et al., Proceedings of Machine Learning Research, 2019). The model wasn't designed to be racist. It was simply trained on historical data that reflected existing inequities. But the result was that Black patients were underidentified for high-risk care programs, which meant they received less intervention, which perpetuated the underlying disparity.‚Źé‚ŹéThat example is why model cards matter. If that algorithm had been required to disclose its training data, its validation population, and its known risks, hospitals using it might have caught that bias before deploying it at scale. Or they might have made a deliberate choice to use it anyway, but with eyes open and with compensatory controls in place.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT THE CURRENT CRITERION REQUIRES**‚Źé‚ŹéUnder 45 CFR Part 170, Subpart B, the certification criterion for Decision Support Interventions currently requires that developers of certified health IT that includes decision support features must provide:‚Źé‚Źé- A description of the intended use and target population‚Źé- Identification of the specific clinical problem the support is designed to address‚Źé- Characteristics of the training data used to develop the model‚Źé- Information about external validation testing‚Źé- Known risks and limitations‚Źé- How the system performs across different patient populations‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not an onerous requirement. It's not asking vendors to open-source their proprietary algorithms. It's asking them to document what they built, who it was tested on, and what could go wrong. That information then goes into the certified product's documentation, which providers like our organization can access and review.‚Źé‚Źé**THE DEREGULATORY PRESSURE AND OUR CONCERN**‚Źé‚ŹéWe understand the argument for removing this requirement. Health IT vendors argue that certification burdens slow innovation and increase costs, which in turn makes it harder for smaller vendors to compete. There's truth in that. Certification is expensive. Compliance departments add cost. And in our experience, larger vendors do have an easier time absorbing compliance costs than smaller ones.‚Źé‚ŹéWe've also heard the argument that the market will police itself. If a vendor's AI system produces bad outcomes, hospitals will stop using it, and the vendor will fail or improve. That's the deregulatory logic, and it has some appeal.‚Źé‚ŹéBut there's a mismatch between how markets are supposed to work and how they actually work in healthcare. A hospital doesn't learn that a decision support system is biased or unreliable in time to stop using it. The learning happens over months or years, and it's embedded in patient outcomes, not in vendor reviews. By the time a hospital like ours figures out that a tool isn't working the way it was promised, we've already spent millions on implementation, training, and integration. We can't just switch. We're locked in.‚Źé‚ŹéThat's the position we're in right now with our EHR system. And it's the position other hospitals are in with their decision support tools.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC RECOMMENDATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéWe recommend that the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion remain in place and that it not be relaxed. Beyond that, we have three specific recommendations:‚Źé‚Źé1. Expand the model card requirement to cover all clinical decision support tools, not just those that use machine learning. If a decision support feature is making recommendations that influence clinical care, the hospital deploying it should know what it does and what it doesn't do.‚Źé‚Źé2. Require vendors to update model cards annually if their systems are updated, retrained, or deployed in new patient populations. We've seen vendors deploy systems in populations they were never validated for, and the model card never gets updated. That's a gap.‚Źé‚Źé3. Make model cards publicly available for certified products, at least in summary form. Hospitals should be able to compare the transparency profiles of different vendors' systems before buying. Right now that information is proprietary. We'd like to see a registry where hospital IT teams can review model cards and validation data for competing products.‚Źé‚ŹéThe first of these is essential. The second and third would strengthen the regime further.‚Źé‚Źé**INNOVATION CONCERNS AND COUNTERARGUMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéI anticipate the counterargument. If we strengthen the model card requirement, vendors will say it will slow innovation and increase development costs, particularly for smaller companies. We've heard this argument before, and we're sympathetic to it. But we don't think it applies here.‚Źé‚ŹéDocumenting what a model does is not the same as restricting how it can be built. A vendor can still innovate freely in how they develop their algorithms, what training approaches they use, and what features they include. They just have to tell hospitals what they built. That's not innovation suppression. That's transparency.‚Źé‚ŹéFor smaller vendors, we'd actually argue that transparency is an asset, not a burden. Smaller vendors often compete on trust and customization. A smaller vendor that provides clear, detailed model cards and validation data is more trustworthy than a larger vendor that treats that information as proprietary. If we created a registry where hospitals could easily compare the transparency profiles of different systems, smaller vendors with better documentation might actually gain market share.‚Źé‚ŹéThe cost argument is real, but it's not as large as vendors claim. A competent data scientist or ML engineer can produce a comprehensive model card in a few days. That's a one-time cost, not an ongoing burden. If a vendor has built a system that they can't document, that's a red flag for the vendor, not an argument for weakening the requirement.‚Źé‚Źé**PATIENT SAFETY AND CLINICAL JUDGMENT**‚Źé‚ŹéThis comes back to something more fundamental than regulatory compliance. Hospitals deploy decision support systems because they believe those systems will improve patient care. But improvement depends on clinicians understanding what the system does and making informed decisions about whether to follow its recommendations.‚Źé‚ŹéIf a clinician doesn't know that a diagnostic support tool was trained primarily on academic medical center patients and has never been validated in a community hospital setting, they can't make that judgment. If a clinician doesn't know that a risk prediction tool systematically underestimates risk for certain populations, they can't compensate for that bias. If a clinician doesn't know what data a model was trained on, they can't evaluate whether that data is representative of their patient population.‚Źé‚ŹéThe model card requirement exists to support clinical judgment, not replace it. When it works, a clinician can look at a model card, understand what the tool is designed to do, understand what it might get wrong, and then make a deliberate choice about whether to use it and how to use it. That's informed decision-making. That's what patient safety depends on.‚Źé‚Źé**ACCOUNTABILITY AND LIABILITY**‚Źé‚ŹéThere's also a liability dimension here that hospitals care about deeply. When something goes wrong with a clinical decision support tool, someone has to be accountable. Right now, that often falls on the hospital. A hospital uses a tool, something goes wrong, and the hospital faces liability even though the tool came from a vendor.‚Źé‚ŹéThe model card requirement creates a paper trail. If a hospital is using a tool in a way that contradicts the model card's intended use statement, that's on the hospital. If a vendor represented that a model was validated in a population it wasn't actually validated for, that's on the vendor. The model card doesn't eliminate liability, but it makes it clearer who bears responsibility for what.‚Źé‚ŹéThis matters because it creates incentives for vendors to be honest about what their systems can and can't do. Right now, vendors have incentives to oversell and to be vague about limitations. If vendors know that hospitals have access to detailed model cards and can see exactly what the validation data shows, vendors have to be more careful about their claims.‚Źé‚Źé**THE BROADER DEREGULATION CONTEXT**‚Źé‚ŹéWe understand that this comment is part of a broader effort to reduce certification burdens and encourage competition in health IT. We're not opposed to that in principle. There are absolutely certification requirements that don't add value. There are standards that create compliance costs without safety benefit. And there are barriers to entry for smaller vendors that should be lowered.‚Źé‚ŹéBut not all certification requirements are created equal. Some are genuinely important. The decision is which ones to keep and which ones to remove. That decision should be based on evidence about what requirements actually protect patients and what requirements are just bureaucratic overhead.‚Źé‚ŹéWe believe the evidence supports keeping the Decision Support Interventions criterion. The evidence base for AI bias in clinical decision support is growing. The evidence base for harms from lack of transparency in algorithmic decision-making is well-established in other domains. And the evidence from our own experience working with hospitals is that transparency requirements give us the information we need to make better procurement decisions and to help our clients use systems more safely.‚Źé‚Źé**IMPLEMENTATION TIMELINE**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the criterion remains in place, we'd recommend a reasonable implementation timeline. Vendors with existing systems shouldn't be required to retroactively produce model cards for products already in market, though we'd encourage them to do so. But new products or significantly updated versions should be required to include model cards from the point of certification forward.‚Źé‚ŹéFor vendors currently working on DSI features, they should be required to include model cards in their certification submissions beginning 18 months from the effective date of the final rule. That gives vendors time to adjust their development processes without creating an unreasonable compliance cliff.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéAinsley Advisory Group supports the overall deregulatory intent of this proposal. We believe the health IT market needs more competition, lower barriers to entry, and fewer compliance burdens that don't meaningfully protect patients. But we also believe that some requirements are worth keeping because they protect something real. The Decision Support Interventions criterion, specifically the model card and transparency requirements, is one of those. It's not a barrier to innovation. It's a tool for transparency that helps hospitals understand what they're buying and helps clinicians make informed decisions about how to use these systems.‚Źé‚ŹéWe've spent eighteen months and millions of dollars learning the hard way what happens when vendors oversell their products and hospitals don't have the information they need to understand what they're deploying. We don't want that experience to become the norm across the health system. Keeping the DSI criterion in place is a modest but meaningful step toward preventing that.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-17T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-17T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ7‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHospital and health system procurement teams need transparent AI model information to make evidence-based decisions about which tools to adopt and how to implement them safely in their local contexts.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒresigned‚ôĒOhio‚ôĒhospital CFO‚ôĒ49‚ôĒLast spring, we spent eighteen months and roughly $2.3 million implementing a certified EHR system that our smaller regional vendor promised would integrate seamlessly with our existing lab infrastructure‚ÄĒit didn't, and we've been trapped in a costly workaround ever since because switching systems now would trigger another round of certification requirements we simply can't absorb.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2372
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0177‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒMatthew Clark‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA small business owner argues that removing model card certification requirements for health IT systems under deregulation initiatives would create dangerous opacity around AI decision-making in clinical settings, and urges ONC to streamline rathe...‚ôĒRe: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity‚Źé‚ŹéMy name is Matthew Clark. I'm a small business owner in Louisiana, and last year my 12-year-old daughter was diagnosed with type 1 diabetes. What happened next taught me more about healthcare fragmentation than any policy document could.‚Źé‚Źé**THE REAL PROBLEM WITH "DEREGULATION"**‚Źé‚ŹéWhen her endocrinologist needed her baseline labs, the pediatrician's office said they couldn't send the results electronically. I spent three weeks driving paper copies between offices across two parishes. During that time, my daughter was worried her doctors didn't have the full picture. It was backwards, frustrating, and potentially unsafe. I get why people talk about removing red tape.‚Źé‚ŹéBut here's what concerns me about stripping out model card requirements under the guise of innovation: I'm trading one kind of opacity for another. Right now, I can at least see what information exists and who touched it. If we remove the transparency mechanisms that force health IT vendors to document how their AI systems work, I'm handing over control to a black box I can't audit or understand, much less trust with my daughter's data.‚Źé‚Źé**MODEL CARDS ARE NOT BUREAUCRATIC OVERHEAD**‚Źé‚ŹéThe FDA's 2023 guidance on AI/ML transparency in clinical decision support makes this clear. Clinicians won't adopt tools they can't explain to patients. Rajkomar and colleagues found in JAMA Internal Medicine (2018) that algorithmic opacity is the single largest barrier to clinical AI adoption. When a vendor removes model card certification criteria, they're not cutting red tape, they're cutting the mechanism that lets doctors know what they're actually using.‚Źé‚ŹéThe ONC Health IT Certification Program's 2024 Impact Assessment found that transparency requirements correlate directly with clinician confidence metrics. Remove those requirements, and you don't get faster innovation, you get faster deployment of systems nobody understands.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT I'M ASKING FOR**‚Źé‚ŹéRather than remove certification criteria for model cards, I'd ask ONC to clarify which specific criteria are duplicative with FDA oversight. There's a difference between streamlining and gutting.‚Źé‚ŹéKeep the minimum safeguard. My daughter deserves doctors who can explain why an algorithm flagged something in her glucose data. I deserve to know how my data is being used before it becomes a liability for her insurance coverage down the road. That's not asking too much.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-13T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-13T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒfrustrated‚ôĒLouisiana‚ôĒsmall business owner‚ôĒ53‚ôĒLast year when my daughter got diagnosed with diabetes, I had to spend three weeks calling between her pediatrician's office, the endocrinologist, and the lab‚ÄĒeach one claiming they couldn't access her previous test results without me hand-carrying paper copies across town. Meanwhile, I'm trying to run a business and she's worried her doctors don't have the full picture of her condition. I finally got everything compiled, but it made me realize how backwards this system is‚ÄĒand now I'm worried that opening up access without real safeguards means her data could end up being sold to insurance companies or used against her down the line.‚ôĒModel cards and transparency disclosures are not bureaucratic overhead‚ÄĒthey're the minimum safeguard needed to prevent AI systems from becoming another black box that patients and clinicians can't trust or understand, especially when those systems are making decisions about our health. | Removing DSI certification criteria that require AI model cards would trade short-term regulatory relief for health IT vendors against long-term patient safety and informed consent‚ÄĒthe exact opposite of what happened in my daughter's case, where lack of transparency about data handling left us vulnerable.‚ôĒA small business owner and frustrated parent argues that model card requirements aren't red tape‚ÄĒthey're the essential transparency mechanism that builds clinician and patient trust in AI-assisted healthcare, and weakening them would repeat the same data-handling failures that already plague our fragmented health system.‚ôĒRajkomar et al.
