CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0178‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒMark Harris‚ôĒPolicy Center for Maternal Mental Health‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health opposes removing certification requirements for AI-assisted clinical decision support tools, arguing that transparency about model training data, validation evidence, and known limitations is essential ...‚ôĒ**POLICY CENTER FOR MATERNAL MENTAL HEALTH**‚Źé‚ŹéJune 14, 2025‚Źé‚ŹéOffice of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology‚ŹéDepartment of Health and Human Services‚Źé200 Independence Avenue, S.W.‚ŹéWashington, D.C. 20201‚Źé‚ŹéRe: Public Comment on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity (RFI-ONC-2025-0001)‚Źé‚ŹéThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health submits this comment in response to the proposed modifications to health information technology certification criteria, particularly regarding Decision Support Interventions (DSI) and AI-assisted clinical decision-making tools. We focus our remarks on a narrow but critical question: whether removing the current certification requirements for AI model transparency and validation evidence will meaningfully reduce burden, or whether it will instead shift risk from developers onto patients and clinicians in ways that deserve closer examination before proceeding.‚Źé‚Źé**BACKGROUND AND ORGANIZATIONAL PERSPECTIVE**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health represents a network of perinatal psychiatrists, OB-GYNs, maternal medicine specialists, nurse midwives, and patient advocates working across 47 states. Our organization was founded after recognizing a persistent gap in how fragmented maternal care systems fail to identify and respond to maternal mental health crises. We work with individual clinicians, health systems, and regional perinatal quality collaboratives. Last year, we documented 340 cases where maternal mental health conditions went unrecognized or were significantly delayed in diagnosis, contributing to preventable maternal morbidity and mortality.‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be direct about what prompted our focus on AI certification standards. A year ago, I met with a mother in her third postpartum depression crisis. Her OB-GYN was using one EHR system, her psychiatrist another, and neither could access her psychiatric history or the screening tools her midwife had completed during pregnancy. The clinical decision support tools at both locations were pulling from incomplete datasets. What struck me was not that the technology was bad, but that nobody could see what data was going into the algorithms that were supposed to help identify her risk. Her providers were essentially making decisions in the dark, relying on tools whose training datasets and validation populations were completely opaque to them. She ended up in the hospital. She could have been identified sooner with better information flow and more transparent clinical decision support.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is the operational reality that makes the current DSI certification criterion matter. I want to address the RFI's Question 2 directly: whether removing requirements for AI model cards, transparency disclosures, and external validation evidence will reduce regulatory burden while maintaining patient safety and clinical effectiveness.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CURRENT DSI CERTIFICATION REQUIREMENT: WHAT IT ACTUALLY REQUIRES**‚Źé‚ŹéThe existing certification criterion for Decision Support Interventions (21 CFR Part 170, Subpart B, Section 170.315(a)(1)) requires that health IT developers disclose:‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ Intended use and clinical context for which the tool was designed‚Źé‚ÄĘ Target population characteristics, including demographic information and clinical inclusion/exclusion criteria‚Źé‚ÄĘ Known limitations, risks, and contraindications‚Źé‚ÄĘ Training data characteristics, including source datasets, population representativeness, and known biases‚Źé‚ÄĘ External validation evidence, including performance metrics across different patient populations and care settings‚Źé‚ÄĘ Mechanisms for ongoing monitoring and performance degradation detection‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not a heavy lift in the way deregulatory language often implies. It is not requiring that developers conduct exhaustive validation studies for every possible use case. It is requiring transparency. The certification asks: what is this tool designed to do, for whom, under what conditions, and what evidence supports its effectiveness? These disclosures are submitted to the ONC, reviewed by certification bodies, and made available to purchasers, clinicians, and patients.‚Źé‚ŹéThe burden being described in the ASTP proposal appears to conflate two separate things. One is the burden of *developing* a clinically sound AI tool, which is genuinely hard and resource-intensive. The other is the burden of *documenting what you built*, which is administratively tedious but not technically difficult. Removing the certification requirement does not remove the first burden; it simply removes the requirement to be transparent about it.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY MODEL CARDS MATTER IN MATERNAL MENTAL HEALTH SPECIFICALLY**‚Źé‚ŹéMaternal mental health screening and diagnostic support represents a unique use case where AI-assisted decision support has enormous potential but also specific vulnerabilities. Let me explain why the current certification requirements exist, and why removing them creates real clinical risks in this space.‚Źé‚ŹéMaternal depression, anxiety, bipolar disorder, and postpartum psychosis are among the most common and most deadly complications of pregnancy and the postpartum period. Maternal suicide is a leading cause of pregnancy-related death. Yet screening and diagnosis remain inconsistent. Many primary care providers and OB-GYNs do not routinely screen for maternal mental health conditions. When screening does occur, it is often done using validated instruments (Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale, Generalized Anxiety Disorder-7), but the data is frequently not integrated into clinical decision-making.‚Źé‚ŹéClinical decision support tools that help identify maternal mental health risk based on patient history, screening results, and EHR data have genuine value. We have seen tools that flag elevated suicide risk based on screening scores, psychiatric history, and recent life stressors. When clinicians have access to these tools and understand what data they are using, identification improves.‚Źé‚ŹéBut maternal mental health populations have specific characteristics that make algorithmic bias a real concern. Women of color experience higher rates of maternal mortality and maternal mental health mortality. They also report lower rates of screening and diagnosis. If an AI tool is trained predominantly on data from white, insured, suburban populations, it may perform worse for Black women, Hispanic women, and women experiencing poverty. The tool might miss risk signals that are clinically meaningful in these populations. The tool might also generate false positives for certain groups, leading to unnecessary interventions.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current certification requirement for external validation evidence asks: does this tool perform similarly across different racial/ethnic groups, income levels, insurance types, and geographic regions? This is not a theoretical concern. A 2023 study published in JAMA Psychiatry (Petersen et al., Vol. 80, No. 8, pp. 782-791) found that machine learning algorithms trained on predominantly white populations showed significant performance degradation when applied to Black women with perinatal depression. Without knowing the training population, clinicians cannot assess whether the tool is safe to use for their specific patient population.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving the requirement for this kind of transparency does not prevent bias. It prevents clinicians from knowing whether bias exists.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT HAPPENS WHEN CLINICAL DECISION SUPPORT LACKS TRANSPARENCY**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be specific about what removing these requirements means in practice, because it is easy to discuss this in abstract terms. Let me walk through a real scenario that we have seen play out in health systems.‚Źé‚ŹéA large health system in California implemented a maternal mental health risk stratification tool developed by a health IT vendor. The tool was supposed to identify pregnant patients at high risk for postpartum depression based on EHR data and screening responses. The system was implemented across 200+ clinics. For the first year, clinicians used the tool. But many clinicians reported that the tool flagged patients they did not perceive as high-risk, and missed patients they expected it to flag. When the health system asked the vendor for information about the tool's training data, validation performance, and known limitations, the vendor provided only general descriptions. The vendor did not disclose that the tool had been trained predominantly on data from insured patients at academic medical centers, that external validation had been limited to similar populations, and that the tool's performance in safety-net clinics was unknown.‚Źé‚ŹéEighteen months into implementation, the health system discovered that the tool was missing a significant proportion of Latina patients with depression. The tool's risk threshold had been calibrated to the population on which it was trained, and it was not capturing the clinical presentation of depression in this population. Clinicians were relying on the tool to identify risk, and when the tool did not flag patients, they assumed risk was lower than it actually was. Two patients experienced serious adverse outcomes that might have been prevented with better identification.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not a hypothetical scenario. This happened. And when the health system tried to understand what went wrong, they had no transparency into how the tool was built, what data it was trained on, or whether performance differences across populations had been assessed.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the DSI certification requirement is removed, there is no mechanism to prevent this from happening again, and no mechanism to identify it happening. Vendors have no obligation to disclose their training data, their validation populations, or their known limitations. Clinicians purchasing the tool have no basis for assessing its safety or appropriateness for their patient population. Regulators have no way to monitor performance or identify harm.‚Źé‚Źé**CERTIFICATION BURDEN VERSUS SAFETY RISK: A FALSE TRADE-OFF**‚Źé‚ŹéThe deregulatory proposal suggests that removing these certification requirements will reduce burden on developers and accelerate innovation. This framing assumes that the certification process is what prevents innovation, rather than recognizing that clinical validation is what should drive good product development.‚Źé‚ŹéHere is what I think is actually happening. Some vendors are frustrated that the certification process requires them to document what they have built. Some vendors have built tools without adequate validation and do not want to disclose that fact. Some vendors want to move tools to market faster without completing validation studies. The certification requirement creates friction for these vendors. The solution proposed is to remove the requirement.‚Źé‚ŹéBut this solves a problem for vendors at the expense of clinicians and patients. It makes it easier to sell tools that have not been adequately validated. It removes transparency about what clinicians are buying. It shifts the risk of harm from the development process (where it can be managed through good design and validation) onto clinical implementation (where it is much harder to manage).‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be clear that I do not think all AI tools need the same level of validation. A tool that provides educational information to patients has different requirements than a tool that makes diagnostic recommendations. A tool used as a suggestion to clinicians has different requirements than a tool that makes automated decisions. The current certification framework already reflects these distinctions through the concept of "clinical knowledge." The DSI criterion focuses specifically on decision support tools that provide recommendations about patient care, which is appropriate.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat concerns me is the argument that any transparency requirement is automatically an impediment to innovation. This assumes that good products cannot also be transparent about what they do and how they work. In my experience, the opposite is true. Products that are well-designed and well-validated are easier to document transparently because they actually work. Products that are not well-validated are harder to document transparently because the documentation reveals their limitations.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC RISKS IN REMOVING DSI CERTIFICATION WITHOUT REPLACEMENT**‚Źé‚ŹéThe ASTP proposal does not suggest replacing the DSI certification with alternative oversight mechanisms. It suggests simply removing it. This creates several specific risks:‚Źé‚Źé1. **Lack of standardized transparency.** Without a certification requirement, vendors have no obligation to provide any information about how tools are designed, trained, or validated. Some vendors will voluntarily provide detailed documentation. Others will provide minimal information. Clinicians will have no way to know which is which. Purchasers will be unable to compare tools on safety and effectiveness dimensions because vendors will not disclose comparable information.‚Źé‚Źé2. **Loss of accountability for performance degradation.** The current certification includes a requirement that developers establish mechanisms to monitor tool performance over time. This is important because algorithm performance can degrade as patient populations change or as the EHR data that feeds the tool changes. Without this requirement, there is no obligation for vendors to monitor whether their tools are still performing as intended. A tool that works well initially might perform poorly after a few years, and nobody would know.‚Źé‚Źé3. **Absence of post-market surveillance.** The current framework includes mechanisms for ONC to identify and investigate tools that are performing poorly or causing harm. Once certification requirements are removed, there is no post-market surveillance. If a tool is causing harm, there is no systematic way for regulators to know about it, investigate it, or require action.‚Źé‚Źé4. **Inability to identify and address bias.** As I described earlier, algorithmic bias is a significant concern in maternal mental health and in healthcare more broadly. The current certification requirement for external validation evidence is one of the few mechanisms that forces vendors to assess performance across different populations. Removing this requirement means vendors have no obligation to assess or disclose whether their tools work differently for different patients. This is not theoretical; it is a known problem that current requirements help address.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT SHOULD HAPPEN INSTEAD**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be constructive here, because I recognize that the certification process could be streamlined or improved. The RFI asks whether there are ways to reduce burden while maintaining safety. I think there are.‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, the ONC could clarify what "external validation evidence" means in the context of AI tools. The current guidance can be interpreted as requiring large prospective validation studies, which are genuinely resource-intensive. But external validation could also mean validation on hold-out test sets from the training data, or retrospective validation on data from different patient populations, or comparative analysis with existing screening tools. The certification requirement could specify what types of evidence are acceptable, reducing the burden of compliance for developers who understand validation but are uncertain what documentation is required.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, the ONC could create a tiered certification process that reflects the actual risk of different types of clinical decision support tools. A tool that provides general educational information about postpartum depression risk factors has different risk profile than a tool that recommends specific medication dosing. The certification requirement could be proportionate to risk. Developers of lower-risk tools would have lighter documentation requirements.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, the ONC could work with vendors to standardize the format for model documentation. The current requirement allows vendors to document their tools in various ways. Standardized templates (similar to the model cards proposed by Gebru et al. in Communications of the ACM, Vol. 61, No. 10, pp. 56-66) would reduce the burden of documentation while making it easier for clinicians to compare tools.‚Źé‚ŹéFourth, the ONC could establish a process for vendors to request de-identification relief or confidentiality protections for proprietary information. Some vendors are concerned that disclosing details about training data or validation studies reveals proprietary algorithms. The ONC could work with vendors to document what is necessary for safety and clinical decision-making while protecting genuinely proprietary information. This would reduce the friction between transparency and innovation without compromising either.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat I am not proposing is removing the requirement for transparency. Removing transparency does not reduce burden in any meaningful way; it simply shifts burden from vendors (who must document what they built) to clinicians (who must figure out what they are buying) and patients (who are subject to tools they know nothing about).‚Źé‚Źé**THE SPECIFIC CASE FOR RETAINING DSI CERTIFICATION IN MATERNAL MENTAL HEALTH**‚Źé‚ŹéMaternal mental health is a space where clinical decision support tools have enormous potential. Better identification of maternal depression, anxiety, and other conditions could prevent serious morbidity and mortality. Tools that help clinicians recognize risk in populations they typically miss could be genuinely lifesaving.‚Źé‚ŹéBut it is also a space where the risks of inadequate validation and algorithmic bias are high. Maternal mental health conditions are underdiagnosed in women of color, in women experiencing poverty, and in women in rural settings. If AI tools perpetuate or amplify these disparities, they will cause real harm. If tools are implemented without understanding their limitations, they will contribute to worse outcomes.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification requirement is specifically designed to prevent these harms by requiring transparency about what tools do, for whom they work, and what their limitations are. Removing this requirement will not accelerate innovation in maternal mental health. It will accelerate the adoption of tools that may or may not work for the populations that need them most.‚Źé‚ŹéI want to emphasize that I am not opposed to deregulation. I am opposed to deregulation that removes safety mechanisms without replacing them. If there are ways to streamline the certification process or reduce burden, I support exploring those. But the solution cannot be to remove transparency requirements and hope that market forces or ethical vendors will fill the gap. Market forces do not reliably incentivize safety or equity. Ethical vendors should still document their work transparently.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health respectfully urges the ONC to retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion requiring AI model cards, transparency disclosures, and external validation evidence. We recognize that the certification process creates administrative burden for developers. We also recognize that removing the certification requirement would reduce that burden.‚Źé‚ŹéBut we believe that removing the requirement would create much larger burdens for clinicians trying to implement tools responsibly and for patients whose safety depends on clinicians understanding what tools they are using. The certification requirement is not primarily a safety mechanism for patients; it is a transparency mechanism that allows clinicians to make informed decisions about what tools to implement and how to use them appropriately.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the ONC is committed to reducing burden while maintaining safety, we propose exploring streamlined certification approaches, tiered requirements proportionate to risk, standardized documentation formats, and confidentiality protections for genuinely proprietary information. These approaches would address the legitimate concerns about burden without removing the transparency requirements that protect maternal mental health patients.‚Źé‚ŹéWe are grateful for the opportunity to comment and welcome further discussion of how to balance innovation with accountability in AI-assisted maternal healthcare.‚Źé‚ŹéRespectfully submitted,‚Źé‚ŹéMark Harris‚ŹéPolicy Director‚ŹéPolicy Center for Maternal Mental Health‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-07T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-07T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ9‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHospital and health system procurement teams need transparent AI model information to make evidence-based decisions about which tools to adopt and how to implement them safely in their local contexts.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒfrustrated‚ôĒCalifornia‚ôĒpolicy director‚ôĒ48‚ôĒLast year, I sat across from a mother in her third postpartum depression crisis whose OB-GYN and psychiatrist literally couldn't access her fragmented medical history because of how the data systems were walled off‚ÄĒand watching her suffer through repeated diagnostic redundancies while privacy frameworks kept her own providers from talking to each other crystallized something for me about how these access debates play out in real time for people who can least afford delays.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2929
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0179‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDonald Williams‚ôĒCareQuest Institute for Oral Health‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒCareQuest Institute for Oral Health opposes weakening certification criteria for Decision Support Interventions, arguing that transparency requirements for AI model documentation are essential for informed clinical decision-making and patient safe...‚ôĒJune 20, 2025‚Źé‚ŹéOffice of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology‚ŹéDepartment of Health and Human Services‚Źé200 Independence Avenue, SW‚ŹéWashington, DC 20201‚Źé‚ŹéRe: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity‚Źé‚ŹéCareQuest Institute for Oral Health submits this comment on the proposed deregulatory actions affecting health information technology certification criteria and information blocking standards. We write as a research organization working directly with oral health providers and community health centers across rural Vermont and beyond, where we observe firsthand how certification requirements shape the actual implementation of health IT systems in clinical practice.‚Źé‚Źé**THE REAL-WORLD TENSION WE'RE OBSERVING**‚Źé‚ŹéLast year, our team conducted a multi-site analysis of oral health outcome data across three community health centers in rural Vermont. The centers serve overlapping populations, many of whom are uninsured or underinsured with complex medical and dental needs. What we found was instructive: the fragmentation in how each center reported outcomes, managed patient data, and documented clinical decision-making wasn't primarily a result of regulatory burden. It was, in many cases, a direct consequence of insufficient transparency about how their health IT systems made decisions, what data those systems had been trained on, and what risks might exist in relying on algorithmic recommendations without clear visibility into the underlying logic.‚Źé‚ŹéOne center had implemented a screening tool to identify patients at high risk for periodontal disease using machine learning, but the staff using it daily had no documentation of what patient populations the model was validated on, what the false positive rate actually was, or what training data had shaped its recommendations. They were using it because it came with their EHR, but they were essentially flying blind. Another center had integrated a decision support tool for antibiotic prescribing recommendations, again with minimal transparency about its scope or limitations. When we asked the IT director where the model card was, where the intended use statement was, where the external validation data lived, they looked at us blankly. These things didn't exist in any accessible form.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not primarily a story about regulatory burden. This is a story about what happens when we strip away transparency requirements without replacing them with something better. The providers we work with are not opposed to using AI and decision support tools in clinical practice. They want to use them. But they want to know what they're using, and they want some assurance that the tools have been built and validated responsibly.‚Źé‚Źé**DECISION SUPPORT INTERVENTIONS AND THE CERTIFICATION CRITERION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed deregulatory actions include revisions to certification criteria that would weaken or eliminate requirements for AI model transparency in Decision Support Interventions (DSI). Specifically, the current criterion requires health IT developers to provide model cards, intended use statements, target population documentation, known risks, training data characteristics, and evidence of external validation before certification. The rationale offered is that this represents unnecessary burden on developers and slows innovation.‚Źé‚ŹéWe respectfully disagree with the premise. And we want to be clear about why, because it matters for patient safety and for the actual ability of providers to practice evidence-based medicine.‚Źé‚ŹéThe certification criterion for DSI transparency is not an abstract regulatory hurdle. It is, in our view, the minimum documentation required for a clinician to make an informed decision about whether to rely on a tool's recommendation. When a provider is making a clinical decision, they need to know: (1) what population this tool was designed for, (2) what happens if my patient falls outside that population, (3) what are the known failure modes of this tool, (4) how was it validated and in what setting, and (5) what data shaped its recommendations. Without this information, the provider is not making an informed clinical decision. They are guessing.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider the scenario we encountered at one of our partner clinics. A DSI tool recommended against extracting a tooth that a clinician believed needed extraction based on clinical examination. The clinician had no way to assess whether the tool's recommendation was based on population-level data that didn't include patients with the specific presentation in front of them, or whether there was a systematic bias in the training data, or whether the tool had simply been validated in a different clinical context. The clinician proceeded with extraction after clinical judgment, which turned out to be correct. But the fact that they had to override the tool because they couldn't understand its reasoning is exactly the problem that certification criteria are meant to prevent.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving or weakening the DSI certification criterion would make this scenario more common, not less.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS ARE NOT PRIMARILY ABOUT BURDEN**‚Źé‚ŹéThe deregulatory proposal frames DSI certification requirements as a burden on developers. We understand the developer perspective, and we don't dismiss it. Creating model cards and validation documentation requires resources. But we want to point out something that may not be visible from the developer's vantage point: the burden of not having these things falls overwhelmingly on the users and, ultimately, on patients.‚Źé‚ŹéHealth systems and provider organizations that implement DSI tools already spend substantial resources on integration, staff training, and workflow redesign. They also spend resources trying to understand how tools work, what their limitations are, and when not to trust them. These resources are not saved by weakening certification criteria. They are simply pushed downstream and distributed across hundreds or thousands of organizations that have to figure out independently what the developers should have documented in the first place.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not efficient. It is wasteful. It fragments the information about how tools actually perform in different settings, making it harder for the entire field to learn from implementation experience.‚Źé‚ŹéThe 21st Century Cures Act (42 U.S.C. 300jj et seq.) established information blocking rules specifically to promote transparency and interoperability. The implementing regulations in 45 CFR 171 define practices that constitute unreasonable information blocking, with exceptions for practices that protect privacy, security, or safety. We read the DSI certification criterion not as a regulatory burden imposed on developers, but as a reasonable safety practice protected under the Act's framework. It is information that providers need to use health IT safely and effectively.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT TRANSPARENCY ACTUALLY ENABLES**‚Źé‚ŹéWhen we worked with our Vermont clinics, the ones that had invested in understanding their DSI tools, even without perfect documentation from developers, were the ones most able to use them effectively and to know when not to use them. They had asked their vendors hard questions. They had reviewed what validation data existed. They had run their own local validation studies in some cases. And they had built workflows that incorporated clinical judgment rather than replacing it.‚Źé‚ŹéThe clinics that had not done this work were the ones most likely to either blindly follow tool recommendations or ignore them altogether. Neither approach is good. Both represent a failure to integrate technology thoughtfully into clinical practice.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criterion, if properly implemented, would make the informed approach the default rather than the exception. Developers would have to do the work once, comprehensively, and document it clearly. Providers could then use that documentation to make informed decisions about implementation and use. This is not a burden. This is the foundation for responsible AI deployment in healthcare.‚Źé‚ŹéWe also want to note that providing model cards and transparency documentation is increasingly standard practice in AI development across sectors. It is not a novel or particularly onerous requirement. The Algorithmic Accountability Act (proposed but not yet enacted) and various state-level AI transparency laws are moving toward mandating exactly this kind of documentation. The 21st Century Cures Act's implementing regulations at 45 CFR 171.301 already require information blocking exceptions to be justified on grounds of safety. We are simply asking that health IT certification criteria align with these emerging standards and with the Act's stated intent.‚Źé‚Źé**PATIENT SAFETY AND INFORMED CONSENT**‚Źé‚ŹéThere is another dimension to this that we think deserves explicit attention. When providers use DSI tools, patients often do not know they are being used. The decision-making process that results in a clinical recommendation may have been shaped by an algorithmic tool, but the patient is told only the recommendation, not that it came from a tool or how that tool works. This is a de facto informed consent problem.‚Źé‚ŹéPatients have a right to understand how decisions about their care are being made. If a recommendation comes from clinical judgment, that is one thing. If it comes from a tool trained on a particular population or validated in a particular setting, that is different information. We are not arguing that providers should necessarily disclose every tool use to patients in real time, though some transparency advocates would argue for that. But we are saying that providers need to have enough information about tools to make decisions about when disclosure is appropriate and what its scope should be.‚Źé‚ŹéWithout DSI certification criteria requiring transparency, providers have less information about tools, which means patients have less information about how recommendations are being made. This is not a theoretical concern. It is a practical problem that we have observed in clinical practice.‚Źé‚Źé**THE SPECIFIC CRITERION WE RECOMMEND RETAINING**‚Źé‚ŹéTo be specific about our recommendation: we urge HHS and ONC to retain the current certification criterion for DSI transparency, which requires health IT developers to provide the following as a condition of certification:‚Źé‚Źé1. A model card describing the tool's intended use, target population, and known limitations‚Źé2. Documentation of the training data characteristics, including source, size, and any known biases or gaps‚Źé3. Performance metrics and external validation evidence, including performance across different populations‚Źé4. Clear statements about what the tool does and does not do‚Źé5. Information about known risks and failure modes‚Źé6. Any relevant disclaimers or contraindications for use‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not an exhaustive list, and we recognize that specific documentation requirements should be refined in consultation with developers and clinicians. But the principle is clear: before a DSI tool is certified for use in healthcare, there should be transparent, accessible documentation that allows providers to make informed decisions about using it.‚Źé‚Źé**ADDRESSING THE INNOVATION ARGUMENT**‚Źé‚ŹéWe understand that some argue that certification requirements slow innovation. We want to respectfully push back on this framing in two ways.‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, transparency requirements do not prevent innovation. They prevent unsafe or poorly-understood innovation. There is a difference. A developer who has a genuinely innovative tool, validated on appropriate populations with clear documentation of its capabilities, will still be able to get it certified and deployed. The developer whose tool is poorly understood, inadequately validated, or built on biased training data may face barriers, and that is appropriate.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, the experience in other sectors suggests that transparency requirements often accelerate innovation by creating market confidence. When consumers or practitioners understand how a product works and have assurance that it has been properly tested, they are more likely to adopt it. The companies that have invested in transparency around their AI tools, in our observation, have an easier time getting those tools into clinical practice, not a harder time. The burden argument assumes that certification criteria slow adoption. Our experience suggests the opposite.‚Źé‚Źé**INTEROPERABILITY AND DATA FRAGMENTATION**‚Źé‚ŹéWe want to return briefly to the data fragmentation problem that prompted our initial research in Vermont. We mentioned three clinics that could not effectively share outcome data with each other. One reason for this fragmentation was technical and standards-related, which the deregulatory actions appropriately address. But another reason was that each clinic was uncertain about what data it was safe to share, given that some of that data had been shaped by DSI tools with unclear limitations and validation.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen you do not know how a tool works, you cannot confidently share recommendations or predictions it has generated with another organization. You cannot assess whether those recommendations are appropriate in a different clinical context. The DSI certification criterion, by creating transparency, actually enables better interoperability. It allows organizations to understand what data they are sharing and what its limitations are.‚Źé‚Źé**MOVING FORWARD**‚Źé‚ŹéWe recognize that ONC is attempting to balance multiple objectives: reducing regulatory burden, promoting innovation, and ensuring patient safety and data interoperability. These objectives can be compatible, but they require careful judgment about which requirements actually serve each objective and which do not.‚Źé‚ŹéIn our view, the DSI certification criterion requiring transparency is a requirement that serves patient safety and informed clinical decision-making. It is not primarily a burden on developers. It is a foundation for responsible deployment of AI tools in healthcare. Removing or weakening it would not unleash innovation. It would unleash poorly-understood tools into clinical practice, where providers and patients would bear the consequences.‚Źé‚ŹéWe urge HHS and ONC to retain this criterion and, if anything, to strengthen it by clarifying expectations for model documentation and external validation. We are happy to work with the agencies and with developers to refine the specific requirements in ways that are clear and feasible without compromising the core principle of transparency.‚Źé‚ŹéThank you for the opportunity to comment on this important matter.‚Źé‚ŹéRespectfully submitted,‚Źé‚ŹéDonald Williams‚ŹéResearch Director‚ŹéCareQuest Institute for Oral Health‚ŹéEnfield, Vermont‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-26T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-26T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ7‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving DSI transparency requirements creates a dangerous regulatory gap where AI-based clinical decision support tools face fewer disclosure obligations than traditional medical devices, undermining consistent safety standards.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒsupportive‚ôĒVermont‚ôĒresearch director‚ôĒ65‚ôĒLast year, I was analyzing oral health disparities across rural Vermont clinics when we discovered that three community health centers couldn't share patient outcome data with each other due to fragmented reporting requirements‚ÄĒyet simultaneously, we had no visibility into how those same fragmented systems were handling sensitive health information. It crystallized for me the tension we face: the systems keeping our most vulnerable patients invisible also tend to be the ones least equipped to protect their privacy.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2190
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0180‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDavid Allen‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe commenter argues that AI transparency requirements in healthcare should be strengthened rather than weakened, drawing on personal experience with medical information silos to emphasize that patients deserve the same right to understand how AI ...‚ôĒMy wife has seen three different doctors this past year for her arthritis and blood pressure, and I spent two weeks last fall just calling offices trying to get copies of her test results so we could show them all to a new cardiologist. Nobody wanted to share anything with anybody. I understand privacy matters, but I got tired of fighting just to help my own wife keep track of her own health.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what worries me about stripping away transparency rules for AI. When my wife gets a prescription, she can read what's in it and what it does. She should have that same right to know how an AI system works when it's used to make decisions about her care. Doctors need to know it too. Right now I'm already in the dark about too much, and removing requirements for transparency just means hospitals and tech companies can hide how their systems were built without anybody being accountable when something goes wrong.‚Źé‚ŹéI've already seen what happens when people won't share information and nobody's keeping watch. If we take away what little transparency we have for these AI tools, patients like my wife will be even further in the dark. We deserve to understand what's being used to make decisions about our health, the same way we deserve to see our‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-07T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-07T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒlow‚ôĒresigned‚ôĒNorth Carolina‚ôĒoffice manager‚ôĒ77‚ôĒMy wife's been seeing three different doctors for her arthritis and blood pressure, and last year I spent two weeks calling offices trying to get copies of her test results so we could show them all to her new cardiologist.
