CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0181‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒKimberly Perez‚ôĒURAALI Refaluwasch Association‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒURAALI Refaluwasch Association urges ONC to retain AI model card and transparency disclosure requirements for clinical decision support tools, arguing that eliminating these safeguards would disproportionately harm vulnerable populations like undo...‚ôĒRe: RFI on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity (90 Day Comment Period)‚Źé‚ŹéURAALI Refaluwasch Association respectfully submits this comment in response to the above-referenced Request for Information. Our organization has spent the past three years building trust-based health engagement programs in rural New Mexico communities, and we have direct experience with what happens when health data systems prioritize speed and market efficiency over transparency and patient protection. We urge the Office of the National Coordinator and the Assistant Secretary for Technology Policy to retain the current Decision Support Interventions certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures, rather than eliminating these safeguards as part of this deregulatory action.‚Źé‚Źé**WHO WE ARE AND WHAT WE'VE WITNESSED**‚Źé‚ŹéURAALI Refaluwasch Association is a community-based organization serving Bernalillo County and surrounding areas in New Mexico. Our membership includes health advocates, community health workers, undocumented immigrants, people experiencing homelessness, and individuals without stable housing or documentation. We operate three community health screening sites and coordinate care navigation services for populations that have historically been excluded from mainstream health systems. Over the past two years, we have helped coordinate health screenings and basic preventive services for approximately 450 individuals across rural Bernalillo County, with particular focus on populations that face language barriers, immigration-related fear, and distrust of institutions.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat we have learned in this work is not theoretical. Last year, one of our longest-term participants, a 58-year-old woman I'll call Maria, finally agreed to participate in our diabetes screening after two years of relationship-building. She had avoided any health system contact for a decade, terrified of deportation. We spent months assuring her that her information would be safe. She came in, got screened, received her results. One week later, she called me in tears. She had heard through community networks that health information in our county had been accessed by immigration enforcement in an unrelated case. She withdrew her consent for all future care, deleted her contact information from our system, and has not returned. We have not heard from her since.‚Źé‚ŹéThis happened last April. It is now January. We have not been able to rebuild that trust with Maria. And we have watched at least a dozen other individuals in our network become more guarded about their health information after hearing her story. The loss is not just individual. When people withdraw from health systems out of fear, disease goes undetected, complications emerge, and emergency room costs spike. Public health suffers. And the responsibility for that failure belongs, in part, to systems that prioritize data efficiency and market-driven innovation over the explicit, transparent protections that vulnerable populations need to feel safe enough to participate in their own care.‚Źé‚Źé**THE SPECIFIC CONCERN: AI MODEL TRANSPARENCY IN CLINICAL DECISION SUPPORT**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule would eliminate the certification criterion requiring Decision Support Interventions (DSI) to include AI model cards and transparency disclosures about intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. This criterion appears at 45 CFR 170.315(b)(2) and related guidance issued by ONC in 2022. The rationale for elimination appears to be burden reduction on health IT developers. We understand that compliance costs money. But we also understand that removing transparency requirements creates a specific kind of harm that does not appear to be fully accounted for in the cost-benefit analysis.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen a clinician uses an AI-assisted decision support tool, they are making a clinical judgment. That judgment depends on understanding what the tool is doing, what populations it was tested on, what risks or biases it might carry, and what its limitations are. This is not a theoretical principle. It is basic clinical ethics. A provider cannot obtain informed consent from a patient about a treatment recommendation if the provider does not themselves understand the reasoning behind that recommendation. And a provider cannot understand AI-assisted recommendations without knowing how the model was trained, what data it used, and what performance data exists for their specific patient population.‚Źé‚ŹéWe have seen this play out in our own screening programs. We use several health IT systems to track participants and manage their care pathways. In at least two instances, we have encountered clinical decision support tools that made recommendations that seemed inconsistent with a patient's actual clinical picture. When we tried to understand why the tool recommended a particular intervention, we could not get clear information from the vendor about the model's training data or target population. One tool, for example, recommended aggressive hypertension management for an elderly patient with multiple comorbidities, but when we looked at the underlying documentation, it was unclear whether the model had been validated in patients with that particular combination of conditions. We had to rely on the clinician's judgment to override the tool. But we should not have had to guess.‚Źé‚ŹéThis matters especially for populations like ours. Undocumented immigrants, people experiencing homelessness, and unhoused individuals are frequently underrepresented in the datasets used to train AI models. If a model is trained primarily on data from insured, documented populations, and then deployed to serve undocumented or homeless patients, it may perform differently than intended. The model card requirement exists precisely to make this visible. It is not bureaucratic overhead. It is a transparency mechanism that allows clinicians and health systems to assess whether a tool is appropriate for their specific patient population.‚Źé‚Źé**EVIDENCE FROM HEALTH EQUITY LITERATURE AND REGULATORY HISTORY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe concern about AI bias and health equity is not new. The National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine published a comprehensive report in 2022 on "Toward Health Equity in Precision Medicine" that specifically identified the risk of AI and machine learning algorithms amplifying existing disparities in healthcare when training data is not representative of the populations served (National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine, 2022, page 142). The report recommended that health IT systems include explicit mechanisms for transparency and validation across diverse populations.‚Źé‚ŹéSimilarly, the FDA's recent guidance on clinical decision support software (issued in 2021 and updated in 2023) emphasizes the importance of understanding the evidence base for algorithmic recommendations, particularly when those recommendations might affect vulnerable populations. While FDA guidance applies to FDA-regulated software, the underlying principle is sound: clinicians need to understand what they are deploying, and patients need to know what factors are driving recommendations about their care.‚Źé‚ŹéONC itself has published extensive guidance on health IT certification and the role of transparency in building trust in digital health systems. The 2015 ONC Health IT Certification Program Final Rule, published at 80 FR 62602, established the model of certification criteria precisely to ensure that health IT systems met baseline standards for safety, security, and transparency. The decision support criteria were added in subsequent iterations specifically because stakeholders identified concerns about the integration of algorithmic recommendations into clinical workflows without adequate transparency about how those algorithms worked.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving the model card and transparency requirement would be a departure from that established principle. It would say that in the interest of reducing developer burden, we are willing to accept a reduction in clinician and patient visibility into how clinical recommendations are being generated. That is not a neutral tradeoff. It is a choice to prioritize speed and cost efficiency over the transparency mechanisms that patients and providers need to maintain trust in these systems.‚Źé‚Źé**THE SPECIFIC IMPACT ON VULNERABLE POPULATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéWe want to be direct about why this matters for our communities. Undocumented immigrants, people experiencing homelessness, and individuals without stable addresses have experienced decades of medical racism, exploitation, and breach of privacy. The Henrietta Lacks case is not ancient history in these communities. The history of forced sterilizations, unethical research, and unauthorized use of health information is not abstract. When we ask someone without papers to come to a health screening, we are asking them to make themselves visible to a system that has every reason to feel threatening.‚Źé‚ŹéThe only way we have been able to build participation in our programs is by being able to offer explicit, detailed assurances about what will happen to their information. We tell people: your information will be used only for your care. It will not be shared with immigration enforcement. We have specific data protection protocols. We use secure systems. We train our staff. And when people ask us what data the system uses to make recommendations about their health, we need to be able to tell them the truth. We need to be able to say that this tool was tested on people like you, that it works for your conditions, that we have validated it for safety.‚Źé‚ŹéIf we cannot access that information because it is proprietary and not required to be disclosed, we cannot make those assurances. We will have to tell people, "We don't know." And people will not participate. They will not come to screenings. They will not manage their chronic conditions. They will wait until they are in crisis, and then they will show up at the emergency room with complications that could have been prevented. That is not a failure of the people in our community. It is a failure of a system that prioritizes developer convenience over patient safety and health equity.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC OPERATIONAL CONCERNS WITH REMOVING TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéBeyond the equity and trust issues, there are specific operational and clinical governance concerns with removing the model card requirement. When a health system or clinic adopts a new clinical decision support tool, the clinical leadership of that organization has a responsibility to validate that the tool is appropriate for their patient population. That validation process requires access to information about how the model was trained, what populations it was tested on, what the performance metrics are, and what known limitations or risks exist.‚Źé‚ŹéIf that information is not required to be disclosed by the developer, then the health system faces a choice: either conduct expensive and time-consuming independent validation (which many smaller organizations and rural clinics cannot afford), or deploy the tool without understanding its appropriateness for their population. The first option increases costs and delays adoption. The second option increases risk.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not hypothetical. We have a contract with a regional health system to help with community health integration, and we have watched them struggle with exactly this issue. They adopted a care coordination tool that included AI-assisted prioritization of high-risk patients. The tool worked well for their insured, English-speaking population. But when they tried to use it in their community health center serving uninsured and immigrant populations, the tool's recommendations seemed misaligned with actual patient risk. When they asked the vendor for information about how the model was trained and validated, they received a response that essentially said: "That information is proprietary. Trust us." The health system had to hire a data scientist to do independent validation, at a cost of approximately $85,000 over six months. That is the hidden cost of removing transparency requirements. It does not eliminate the need for validation. It just pushes that cost onto health systems and makes it less likely that smaller organizations will be able to do it.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT THE DATA SAYS ABOUT TRUST AND TRANSPARENCY**‚Źé‚ŹéThere is growing evidence that transparency in health IT systems is associated with higher adoption rates and better outcomes. A 2023 study published in the Journal of the American Medical Association found that when clinicians had access to clear documentation about AI model performance and limitations, they were significantly more likely to use the tool appropriately, and less likely to either over-rely on recommendations or dismiss them entirely (Wang et al., JAMA, 2023, 329(18), page 1542-1551). The study looked at 47 different clinical sites and tracked adoption and appropriate use of a clinical decision support tool. Sites that had access to detailed model documentation showed 34 percent higher appropriate use rates than sites that did not.‚Źé‚ŹéSimilarly, research on patient trust in health IT systems shows that transparency about data use and algorithmic decision-making is strongly associated with willingness to participate in health systems and to share health information. A qualitative study by Appelbaum and colleagues at the University of New Mexico (published in Health Affairs in 2022) found that undocumented immigrants in New Mexico were significantly more likely to engage with health systems when those systems could explain, in detail, how their data would be used and protected. The study included interviews with 112 undocumented immigrants about their health IT concerns. When asked what would make them more comfortable with health systems, 91 percent mentioned the need for clear information about how their data would be used, and 78 percent specifically mentioned concerns about algorithmic decision-making.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not about slowing down innovation. This is about building systems that actually work for the populations that need them most.‚Źé‚Źé**THE ALTERNATIVE: STREAMLINED RATHER THAN ELIMINATED REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéWe are not arguing that the current certification process is perfect, or that there are no legitimate concerns about burden on developers. We understand that compliance costs money, and that excessive regulation can slow innovation. But the answer is not to eliminate transparency requirements. The answer is to streamline them in ways that reduce burden without eliminating the information that clinicians and patients need.‚Źé‚ŹéFor example:‚Źé‚Źé1. ONC could establish a standardized template for AI model cards, so that developers are not creating custom documentation for each certification. This would reduce development time while maintaining consistency and usability.‚Źé‚Źé2. ONC could allow developers to submit model cards as part of a broader health IT certification package, rather than as separate documentation, reducing duplication and administrative burden.‚Źé‚Źé3. ONC could establish a tiered approach, where less risky or less complex decision support tools require abbreviated model cards, while higher-risk tools require more detailed documentation.‚Źé‚Źé4. ONC could create a centralized database of model cards that have been submitted and approved, so that health systems can access this information without having to request it from vendors, reducing vendor burden and increasing transparency for users.‚Źé‚Źé5. ONC could work with industry to establish best practices for model card development, so that developers know what they need to document and can do so efficiently.‚Źé‚ŹéThese approaches would maintain the transparency that is essential for patient safety and health equity, while reducing the burden on developers. They are not radical. They reflect approaches that are already being used in other regulated industries, including aviation and pharmaceuticals, where transparency about safety and performance is required, but the process is streamlined to reduce unnecessary burden.‚Źé‚Źé**OUR EXPERIENCE WITH CURRENT CERTIFICATION REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéTo be concrete about this: we have worked with several health IT vendors over the past two years, and we have seen firsthand what happens when transparency requirements exist versus when they don't. When a vendor has to prepare a model card and submit it for certification, they think carefully about what they are building and why. They conduct validation studies. They test their tool on diverse populations. They document known limitations. Yes, this costs money and time. But the result is a better product.‚Źé‚ŹéWe have also seen the inverse: tools that come to market without certification requirements tend to be less well-tested, with less documentation, and with more surprises when they are deployed in real-world settings. We have adopted two clinical decision support tools that were not required to have model cards, and in both cases we discovered performance issues only after deployment, when we had already invested staff time and training in using the tool.‚Źé‚ŹéOne specific example: we adopted a tool for medication management and drug interaction screening about 18 months ago. The vendor assured us it was validated and evidence-based. We deployed it in our three community health centers. Within three months, we discovered that the tool was flagging potential drug interactions at a much higher rate than published clinical evidence would suggest. We were creating unnecessary alerts, which was causing clinician fatigue and leading to decreased reliance on the tool. When we asked the vendor about the training data and validation methodology, we learned that the tool had been trained on a database that overrepresented older, whiter populations, and had not been adequately validated in younger populations or people of color. The vendor eventually provided us with updated guidance on how to configure the tool for our population, but we had already spent time and money on problematic implementation.‚Źé‚ŹéIf that tool had been subject to certification requirements with model cards, we would have had access to that information upfront. We could have made an informed decision about whether to adopt it, or how to configure it appropriately.‚Źé‚Źé**THE EQUITY DIMENSION: WHO BEARS THE BURDEN AND WHO BEARS THE RISK**‚Źé‚ŹéThere is an important equity dimension to this that we want to highlight. The burden of removing transparency requirements falls primarily on large, well-resourced health systems and well-funded clinics that can afford to conduct independent validation of tools. But the risk of deploying poorly-validated tools falls disproportionately on smaller health systems, rural clinics, and community health centers that serve vulnerable populations.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not accidental. It is a structural feature of health IT markets. Large health systems have data science teams and the resources to evaluate and validate tools. They have leverage with vendors and can demand documentation and support. Smaller organizations do not. If certification requirements are eliminated, larger organizations will continue to do the work of validation because they have the capacity and the incentive. But smaller organizations will not, because they cannot afford to. The result is a two-tiered system where large health systems have access to well-validated, transparent tools, and small health systems and community clinics are stuck with tools that may not be appropriate for their populations.‚Źé‚ŹéAnd who works in small health systems and community clinics? Organizations like ours. Health centers that serve uninsured people, undocumented immigrants, people experiencing homelessness. The populations that are most vulnerable to being harmed by poorly-validated AI tools are the same populations that are least likely to have access to organizations with the resources to independently validate those tools.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is the opposite of health equity. This is a policy change that would increase health disparities by design.‚Źé‚Źé**RESPONDING TO THE DEREGULATORY FRAMING**‚Źé‚ŹéWe understand that the proposed rule is framed around deregulation and burden reduction. We are sympathetic to concerns about regulatory burden. But we want to push back on the framing that transparency requirements are "burdens" in a purely negative sense. These requirements serve a function. They protect patients. They ensure that clinicians can make informed decisions. They build trust in health systems.‚Źé‚ŹéYes, they cost money. But the question is not whether they cost money. The question is whether the benefits outweigh the costs. And on that question, we believe the evidence is clear. The benefits in terms of patient safety, health equity, and system trust are significant. The costs in terms of developer burden can be reduced through streamlining, without eliminating the requirements altogether.‚Źé‚ŹéMoreover, the deregulatory framing assumes that removing requirements will necessarily lead to more innovation and better outcomes. But that is not always true. In some cases, transparency requirements actually drive innovation, because they force developers to think carefully about what they are building and to validate their tools rigorously. Removing those requirements might reduce the burden on some developers, but it could also reduce the quality of the tools they produce.‚Źé‚ŹéWe would urge ONC to consider a more nuanced approach: streamline certification requirements where possible to reduce burden, but maintain the core transparency mechanisms that are essential for patient safety and health equity.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéURAALI Refaluwasch Association respectfully urges ONC and the Assistant Secretary for Technology Policy to retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures. This is not a perfect requirement, and there is certainly room for streamlining and improvement. But eliminating it altogether would remove a critical transparency mechanism that patients and clinicians need to make safe, informed decisions about health care.‚Źé‚ŹéThe communities we serve have experienced decades of exclusion, exploitation, and breach of trust in health systems. We have spent three years building relationships and trust with people who have every reason to distrust institutions. We have made specific promises about how their data will be used and protected. We have worked to create health systems that are transparent and accountable. Removing certification requirements for decision support tools would undermine that work. It would make it harder for us to assure people that their information is safe, and that the tools being used to make decisions about their care have been validated and are appropriate for them.‚Źé‚ŹéThat matters. It matters for individual patients, for public health, and for the legitimacy of health systems in communities that have been historically harmed by those systems. We urge you to keep the model card requirement. If there are ways to streamline it to reduce burden on developers, we are happy to discuss those approaches. But transparency about AI in clinical decision-making is not a luxury. It is a necessity.‚Źé‚ŹéThank you for the opportunity to comment.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-02-03T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-03T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ11‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒconcerned‚ôĒNew Mexico‚ôĒcommunity organizer‚ôĒ34‚ôĒLast year, I helped coordinate health screenings at three community centers across rural Bernalillo County, and I watched firsthand how our most vulnerable clients‚ÄĒundocumented immigrants, people experiencing homelessness, those without stable addresses‚ÄĒbecame invisible the moment their data entered fragmented systems with no clear privacy protections. One woman I've worked with for two years finally agreed to get tested for diabetes, but she withdrew consent the next week after hearing through the community grapevine that health information could be shared with immigration enforcement. If privacy safeguards weaken further, we'll lose the trust we've spent years building with populations that already have every reason to distrust institutions.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ3508
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0182‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒSandra Young‚ôĒThe Pharmacy Health Information Technology Collaborative‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Pharmacy Health Information Technology Collaborative opposes the proposed deregulation that would remove certification requirements for Decision Support Interventions in pharmacy systems, arguing that transparency requirements for model cards ...‚ôĒThe Pharmacy Health Information Technology Collaborative submits these comments in response to the proposed deregulatory actions by the Office of the Secretary and the Office of the National Coordinator for Health IT. Our organization brings together pharmacy informaticists, clinical researchers, and health IT specialists working at the intersection of medication management systems and interoperable data exchange. We write from a position of direct experience with the current regulatory framework, having spent considerable time navigating both its protections and its inefficiencies.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PROBLEM WE ENCOUNTERED**‚Źé‚ŹéLast year, while designing a research protocol to examine pharmacy data system interoperability across three major healthcare networks, our team discovered what should have been a straightforward data access problem but turned into a six-month institutional nightmare. Three competing pharmacy vendors had essentially locked their application programming interfaces behind proprietary walls, citing lack of regulatory clarity about what transparency was actually required. We needed basic technical documentation about how these systems handled medication interaction checking, formulary data integration, and clinical decision support logic. Information that should have been publicly available under existing oversight standards was either unavailable or heavily redacted.‚Źé‚ŹéThe FDA's existing guidance on pharmacy informatics systems (FDA 21 CFR Part 11, Electronic Records; Electronic Signatures) establishes baseline requirements for system validation and data integrity. The Office of the National Coordinator's Health IT Certification Program similarly requires vendors to meet specific technical standards and maintain certified status. Yet what we learned was that certification itself had become a checkbox exercise. A system could be certified as interoperable while simultaneously maintaining technical barriers that made actual data exchange difficult. The vendors weren't violating any explicit rule; they were simply operating in a regulatory gray zone where transparency wasn't mandated and market pressure didn't require it.‚Źé‚ŹéOur team eventually obtained the necessary information, but the delay cost us real research time and real money. More importantly, it revealed something deeper about the current regulatory environment: the rules exist, but they lack enforcement teeth. Rather than watching policymakers float the idea of loosening already-insufficient requirements, we need to be having a conversation about making the existing standards actually work.‚Źé‚Źé**DECISION SUPPORT INTERVENTIONS AND PATIENT SAFETY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed deregulation would remove certification requirements for Decision Support Interventions (DSIs) in pharmacy systems, reasoning that market competition will drive vendors toward transparency without regulatory mandate. We respectfully disagree with this approach, particularly as it applies to AI-assisted clinical decision support.‚Źé‚ŹéA Decision Support Intervention in pharmacy context typically refers to algorithmic recommendations about medication selection, dosing adjustments, or clinical alerts. When a pharmacist receives a system recommendation to flag a potential drug-drug interaction, modify a dose based on renal function, or suggest a therapeutic alternative, that recommendation is making a clinical judgment. The pharmacist retains professional responsibility for the final decision, but they are working with information shaped by the system's underlying logic.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current certification criterion for DSIs requires vendors to provide model cards documenting intended use, target population, known limitations, training data characteristics, and external validation results. This is not a trivial requirement, but it is a proportional one. It asks vendors to make transparent what they have built and tested, not to redesign their products or sacrifice competitive advantage.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider a concrete example from recent pharmacy practice: an AI system designed to flag potential opioid overprescribing in a primary care setting. The system was trained primarily on data from adult patients aged 50-75 with chronic pain conditions. It performed well in that population. But when deployed across an entire health system, it began generating false alerts for younger patients with legitimate acute pain needs and for patients with complex pain conditions that didn't fit the training cohort. The pharmacy team had no visibility into these limitations because the vendor claimed the model was proprietary. The result was alert fatigue and clinician override of legitimate safety signals.‚Źé‚ŹéThis scenario is not hypothetical. It reflects problems documented in literature examining clinical decision support in pharmacy (Phansalkar et al., Journal of the American Medical Informatics Association, 2013, examining alert fatigue in computerized physician order entry systems; Steinbrook, New England Journal of Medicine, 2008, on the FDA's approach to software as a medical device). The FDA's own guidance on Clinical Decision Support Software (FDA, 2019) acknowledges that transparency about system design and validation is essential for safe clinical use.‚Źé‚ŹéThe certification criterion requiring DSI model cards directly addresses this problem. It does not prevent vendors from building sophisticated systems. It requires them to document what those systems do, whom they were tested on, and what limitations were identified. A vendor building an opioid-flagging system would still own its proprietary algorithm, but it would need to disclose that the system was trained on a specific demographic and may perform differently outside that population.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT DEREGULATION WOULD MEAN IN PRACTICE**‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving this requirement would create several problems that deserve explicit consideration:‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, pharmacists and prescribers would lose transparency about the decision support they are using. Without model cards, a clinician has no way to know whether a system's recommendation is based on robust evidence, narrow training data, or untested assumptions. The regulatory argument that "the market will incentivize transparency" assumes that purchasers have sufficient bargaining power and technical expertise to demand this information. In pharmacy practice, that is often not true. Many hospital systems and pharmacy chains use bundled systems where DSI modules come as part of larger platforms. Individual pharmacists cannot opt out of the decision support; they can only try to work around it or override it systematically.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, liability and accountability become muddied. If a pharmacist relies on a DSI recommendation and a patient is harmed, the question of whether the system was appropriate for that patient becomes difficult to litigate. The vendor will argue that the pharmacist should have known the system's limitations. The pharmacist will argue that the vendor should have disclosed them. Patients caught in that gap have limited recourse. Maintaining the certification requirement preserves a clear standard: if you are selling a system that makes clinical recommendations, you document what it does.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, pharmacy informatics research becomes more difficult. The Collaborative's work depends on being able to study how systems function in real-world settings. Without transparency requirements, vendors have little incentive to share the data that would allow independent validation of their systems' safety and effectiveness. Published research on pharmacy DSI effectiveness becomes limited to vendor-sponsored studies or to the handful of health systems with sufficient leverage to demand proprietary documentation.‚Źé‚ŹéThe FDA's framework for software as a medical device (FDA, 2021, Proposed Modifications to the Software Precertification Program) and the ONC's Health IT Certification Program both rest on the principle that clinical decision support software should be treated as a regulated medical device when it is intended to provide patient-specific recommendations. This is not a novel position. It reflects the reality that algorithmic recommendations have clinical consequences.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC LANGUAGE TO RETAIN**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Collaborative recommends that the certification criterion for DSIs be retained with its current requirements. Specifically, the rule should continue to require vendors to provide:‚Źé‚Źé1. Intended use and clinical context. What decision is the system designed to support? What is the range of appropriate clinical scenarios for this system?‚Źé‚Źé2. Target population. What patient populations was the system tested on? What demographic, clinical, and genetic characteristics define the primary population?‚Źé‚Źé3. Known risks and limitations. What are the known failure modes? In what scenarios does the system perform poorly? What edge cases were identified during development?‚Źé‚Źé4. Training data characteristics. What data was used to train the model? Over what time period? From how many patients? What data sources contributed?‚Źé‚Źé5. External validation results. Has the system been tested on data not used in training? What were the results? Were there performance differences across subpopulations?‚Źé‚ŹéThese requirements do not demand that vendors open-source their algorithms or reveal proprietary feature engineering. They require transparency about how systems were built and validated. This is a reasonable middle ground between complete deregulation and heavy-handed prescriptive requirements.‚Źé‚Źé**THE RESEARCH PERSPECTIVE**‚Źé‚ŹéOur research at the Collaborative focuses specifically on how pharmacy data systems interact with clinical workflows and how interoperability failures create safety risks. We have conducted studies examining medication error rates in hospitals with different levels of system integration (unpublished data, 2024-2025 academic year), and we have observed that systems with clear documentation of their decision support logic are associated with lower alert override rates and better clinical outcomes.‚Źé‚ŹéOne study we are currently completing examined ten health systems that adopted new pharmacy management systems over the past three years. We interviewed pharmacists about their confidence in system recommendations and their willingness to act on alerts. Health systems that received detailed technical documentation from vendors, including information about how systems handled specific edge cases, showed significantly higher trust in critical alerts. Conversely, systems where vendors provided minimal documentation saw higher override rates even for legitimate safety signals.‚Źé‚ŹéThis finding aligns with broader research on clinical decision support effectiveness. Studies by Kawamoto et al. (Journal of the American Medical Informatics Association, 2005) examining factors that affect the impact of computerized clinical decision support identified system transparency and evidence of validation as key factors in clinician adoption. When clinicians understand how a system works and why it is recommending something, they are more likely to use it appropriately.‚Źé‚Źé**MARKET DYNAMICS AND THE DEREGULATION ARGUMENT**‚Źé‚ŹéThe deregulatory proposal rests partly on the argument that removing certification requirements will reduce vendor burden and accelerate innovation. We acknowledge this concern. Certification processes do create compliance costs. However, the evidence that deregulation drives innovation in pharmacy informatics is limited.‚Źé‚ŹéThe pharmacy software market is actually quite consolidated. Three major vendors dominate hospital pharmacy systems, and their market position is durable because switching costs are high and integration is complex. A small pharmacy informatics startup cannot easily compete with these incumbents. Removing certification requirements does not change this structural reality. It simply removes a requirement that the incumbents can easily meet while potentially disadvantaging smaller vendors who lack the resources to market their products without third-party validation.‚Źé‚ŹéMoreover, innovation in pharmacy informatics over the past decade has been driven largely by academic research and by external pressure from patient safety organizations, not by regulatory relief. The meaningful use requirements that accompanied the Health Information Technology for Economic and Clinical Health (HITECH) Act (42 U.S.C. 17921-17953) actually accelerated certain kinds of innovation, particularly around medication reconciliation and clinical decision support integration.‚Źé‚Źé**MAINTAINING THE INTEGRITY OF CERTIFICATION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Health IT Certification Program has legitimate value. It provides purchasers with assurance that systems meet baseline standards. That assurance is meaningful only if the certification process is rigorous and if the standards themselves are evidence-based. Removing the DSI certification criterion would weaken the program's overall credibility.‚Źé‚ŹéWe recognize that there are aspects of the current certification process that could be improved. The ONC's 2020 Final Rule (85 Fed. Reg. 25642) made changes to reduce burden while maintaining safety and interoperability standards. Rather than abandoning the DSI criterion entirely, a more proportional approach would be to streamline the certification process itself: reduce the documentation burden through standardized templates, allow vendors to submit existing technical documentation if it meets the standard, or create a streamlined pathway for systems that have been independently validated.‚Źé‚Źé**RECOMMENDATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Collaborative respectfully urges the Office of the Secretary and ONC to:‚Źé‚Źé1. Retain the current certification criterion for Decision Support Interventions, including the requirement for model cards and transparency disclosures.‚Źé‚Źé2. Consider streamlining the certification process through standardized templates or documentation frameworks that reduce vendor burden without eliminating transparency requirements.‚Źé‚Źé3. Establish a working group including pharmacy informaticists, patient safety researchers, and health IT vendors to refine the DSI certification standard based on real-world implementation experience.‚Źé‚Źé4. Maintain the principle that clinical decision support software should be treated as a regulated medical device when it provides patient-specific recommendations, consistent with FDA guidance on software as a medical device.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Collaborative stands ready to participate in any stakeholder process examining how to make the certification program more efficient while maintaining its core protective function.‚Źé‚ŹéWe appreciate the opportunity to comment and welcome further dialogue with ONC and CMS about how to balance innovation with patient safety in pharmacy informatics.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-17T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-17T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ6‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving DSI transparency requirements creates a dangerous regulatory gap where AI-based clinical decision support tools face fewer disclosure obligations than traditional medical devices, undermining consistent safety standards.‚ôĒacademic‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒangry‚ôĒWest Virginia‚ôĒpostdoctoral fellow‚ôĒ37‚ôĒWhen I was designing my current research protocol on pharmacy data systems last year, we discovered that three competing vendors had essentially locked their APIs behind proprietary walls‚ÄĒinformation that should have been flagged years ago under existing oversight standards. My team spent six months just trying to get basic interoperability data that would have taken weeks if regulators had teeth to enforce transparency, and now I'm watching policymakers float the idea of loosening those already-insufficient requirements.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2032
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0183‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒSandra Garcia‚ôĒThe Society of Thoracic Surgeons‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Society of Thoracic Surgeons opposes removing AI model card and transparency disclosure requirements from Decision Support Intervention certification standards, arguing that these requirements are essential for clinicians to make informed deci...‚ôĒ**COMMENT TO THE OFFICE OF THE NATIONAL COORDINATOR FOR HEALTH INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY**‚Źé‚Źé**RE: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity**‚Źé‚Źé---‚Źé‚ŹéThe Society of Thoracic Surgeons represents over 1,400 cardiothoracic surgeons across the United States, and we work directly with health systems ranging from rural critical access hospitals to large academic medical centers. Our members perform approximately 700,000 cardiothoracic procedures annually, and they depend on clinical decision support systems that integrate patient data to guide surgical planning, risk assessment, and perioperative management. I'm writing to address the proposed modifications to Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements, specifically the removal of AI model card and transparency disclosure standards.‚Źé‚ŹéWe appreciate the Office of the National Coordinator's commitment to reducing unnecessary regulatory burden. We understand the argument that certification requirements can slow innovation and increase costs for health IT developers. We've heard from our members in smaller health systems that they struggle to adopt new technologies because of implementation complexity and expense. That part of the concern is real, and we don't dismiss it. But I need to be direct about what we've learned over the past eighteen months that makes us uncomfortable with loosening these particular standards.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PROBLEM WE DISCOVERED**‚Źé‚ŹéLast spring, I was reviewing surgical outcome data for our equity initiative when we discovered something troubling. Rural hospitals in underserved counties weren't reporting surgical complications at the same rates as urban centers. Our first assumption was that patient selection or case acuity differed. But when we dug deeper, we found the systems couldn't capture the granular details that larger institutions' EHRs could. A surgeon in a rural setting using a basic clinical decision support tool had no visibility into how that tool was trained, what patient populations it was validated on, or whether its recommendations applied to their patient mix. One hospital in rural Iowa told us they were using a DSI for coronary artery bypass graft risk stratification that was trained primarily on data from high-volume urban centers. They had no way of knowing whether the tool's predictions were accurate for their patient population, which skews older and has higher rates of chronic kidney disease. When we compared their surgical outcomes to the tool's predicted risk, there was a significant discrepancy, but without transparency disclosures, they couldn't even identify why.‚Źé‚ŹéThis wasn't a case of bad faith or negligence. It was a case of a hospital adopting a tool that seemed appropriate, with no mechanism to understand its limitations. If we remove the DSI certification criteria requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures, we'll create more situations exactly like this one. And the patients who suffer most will be the ones in rural areas, in safety-net hospitals, and in underserved communities where health equity is already fragile.‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be clear about what we're not saying. We're not saying that health IT developers should face burdensome, byzantine certification processes. We're not saying that every tweak to a model requires new certifications. We're not opposed to streamlining redundant requirements or removing criteria that don't actually improve safety. But the DSI model card and transparency disclosure requirements are not ornamental. They serve a specific function: they give clinicians the information they need to make informed decisions about whether a tool is appropriate for their patient population and clinical context.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT THE CURRENT REQUIREMENT DOES**‚Źé‚ŹéUnder the current certification criteria (45 CFR 170.315(b)(6)), developers of Decision Support Interventions must provide documentation that includes the intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. This is not a heavy lift. A model card is typically a 5-10 page document. Many developers are already producing them for their own quality assurance and liability purposes. The requirement doesn't mandate a specific format or level of detail that would require months of additional engineering work.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat it does is ensure that when a clinician or health system administrator is evaluating whether to adopt a tool, they have access to the information they need. A cardiothoracic surgeon considering a tool for preoperative risk assessment needs to know: Was this tool trained on a patient population similar to mine? What outcome did it optimize for? What were the external validation results? Were certain subgroups underrepresented in the training data? These aren't academic niceties. They're foundational to informed clinical decision-making.‚Źé‚ŹéI'll give you another concrete example from our membership. One of our members works at a mid-sized hospital in North Carolina that adopted a DSI for predicting acute kidney injury after cardiac surgery. The tool came with a model card showing that it was trained on a predominantly white patient population and had not been externally validated on Black or Hispanic patients. Armed with this information, the surgeon and the hospital's quality team made a deliberate choice: they would use the tool as one input among many, and they would closely monitor whether its predictions were accurate for their increasingly diverse patient population. They collected six months of data and found that the tool consistently underpredicted risk for Black patients. They fed that information back to the developer, who incorporated it into the next iteration. That's how accountability works. That's how equity works. And it's only possible if the model card requirement is in place.‚Źé‚ŹéIf we remove the requirement, that hospital might never know there was a problem. The tool would continue to underpredicting risk for Black patients, and outcomes data might not capture the discrepancy because it's embedded in clinical decision-making that's already happening. The patients wouldn't know they were at higher risk than the tool indicated. The hospital wouldn't have the information to correct course.‚Źé‚Źé**THE BROADER CONTEXT: INFORMATION BLOCKING AND ACCOUNTABILITY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI transparency requirements exist within a broader regulatory framework designed to ensure that health IT serves patients and clinicians, not the other way around. The 21st Century Cures Act Information Blocking Rule (45 CFR 171.100 et seq.) establishes that health IT actors have obligations to provide timely access to health information and to be transparent about how systems work. The DSI certification criteria are one mechanism for making that transparency real in the context of AI-assisted clinical decision-making.‚Źé‚ŹéWe're concerned that removing these criteria sends a signal that transparency in AI is optional, that developers' commercial interests outweigh clinicians' need to understand the tools they're using. We don't think that's the intent of the deregulatory actions, but that's the effect. And the effect cascades downward to the patients who are most vulnerable to errors in clinical decision support.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what keeps me up at night: a clinician using an uncertified DSI for a critical decision, with no documentation of how that tool was built, what it was trained on, or how accurate it is in their patient population. An adverse outcome occurs. The clinician, the patient, and the hospital all want to understand what happened. But there's no model card. There's no documentation of external validation. There's no transparency about training data. The investigation stalls. Liability becomes murky. The clinician loses trust in the tool. The patient doesn't get answers.‚Źé‚Źé**OUR SPECIFIC RECOMMENDATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéWe're asking that the Office of the National Coordinator retain the current DSI certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures. We're not asking for expansion of the requirement. We're not asking for additional layers of certification. We're asking that this one, focused requirement remain in place.‚Źé‚ŹéIf there are aspects of the current requirement that are genuinely burdensome for developers, we'd support refinement. For instance:‚Źé‚Źé- The requirement could specify a maximum length for model cards (e.g., 10 pages) to prevent excessive documentation burden.‚Źé- ONC could provide a standardized template for model cards, so developers don't have to invent new formats.‚Źé- The requirement could explicitly allow for iterative updates to model cards as tools are refined, rather than requiring complete recertification.‚Źé- ONC could develop a safe harbor for tools used in research or limited-scale deployment, with certification required only when tools reach a certain threshold of clinical use.‚Źé‚ŹéThese are reasonable accommodations that would address legitimate developer concerns without gutting the transparency requirement. But removing the requirement entirely would be a mistake.‚Źé‚Źé**EQUITY AND RURAL HEALTH IMPLICATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to circle back to something I mentioned earlier, because it's central to why we're writing this comment. The Society of Thoracic Surgeons has a substantial equity initiative focused on narrowing disparities in cardiothoracic surgery outcomes. We've been tracking this for years. Rural hospitals, safety-net hospitals, and hospitals serving predominantly Black and Hispanic communities consistently have less access to the latest clinical tools, including DSI systems. When they do adopt new tools, they often lack the technical infrastructure and expertise to evaluate them critically. They're relying on the certification process and transparency disclosures to tell them whether a tool is appropriate for their patient population.‚Źé‚ŹéIf we remove the DSI transparency requirements, we're essentially telling those hospitals: "You're on your own. Evaluate these tools however you can, without access to information about how they were built or validated." That's not deregulation. That's abandonment of the equity principle embedded in health IT policy.‚Źé‚ŹéFederal guidance on health equity in health IT (including the HHS Health Equity Action Plan and the ONC Health IT Certification Program guidance) emphasizes the importance of ensuring that health IT doesn't perpetuate or worsen existing disparities. The DSI transparency requirements are one of the mechanisms for doing that. Removing them would be inconsistent with that commitment.‚Źé‚Źé**THE INNOVATION ARGUMENT ISN'T WHAT IT SEEMS**‚Źé‚ŹéWe've read the arguments about how certification requirements slow innovation and increase costs. We've also talked to health IT developers, and the picture is more nuanced than the deregulation narrative suggests. Most developers of serious clinical decision support tools are already producing model cards and transparency documentation because they need it for their own quality assurance, their liability insurance, and their customers' requirements. They're not doing it because ONC requires it. They're doing it because it's good business.‚Źé‚ŹéThe developers who might feel burdened by the requirement are those working on lower-stakes tools or tools with limited validation. And there may be legitimate reasons to create a lighter-touch certification pathway for those tools. But that's different from removing the requirement for all DSI tools, including those used for critical clinical decisions like perioperative risk assessment.‚Źé‚ŹéWe'd also note that "innovation" in health IT has often meant innovation that benefits developers and large health systems, not innovation that benefits patients. The DSI transparency requirement is a modest brake on that. It says: if you're going to offer a tool that influences clinical decision-making, you have to be transparent about how it works and what it's validated for. That's not anti-innovation. That's innovation with accountability.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE'VE HEARD FROM OUR MEMBERS**‚Źé‚ŹéOur members are using DSI tools for several critical functions: risk stratification before surgery, intraoperative decision support, and post-operative complication prediction. Many of them have told us that they value the transparency requirements because they give them confidence that the tools they're using have been vetted. One of our members, a surgeon at a large academic medical center in Boston, told us: "I wouldn't use a tool for surgical risk assessment if I didn't know how it was trained and validated. That's not negotiable for me. If the requirement goes away, I'll just not adopt new tools because I won't know whether they're safe."‚Źé‚ŹéThat's a real cost of deregulation that isn't captured in the regulatory impact analysis. If surgeons stop adopting DSI tools because they don't trust them without transparency information, innovation actually stalls. The tools don't get used. The data doesn't improve. The clinicians and patients don't benefit.‚Źé‚ŹéWe've also heard from health system IT leaders who've told us that they use the DSI certification process as a way to vet tools for their institution. They look at the model card, they evaluate whether the tool's training data is representative of their patient population, and they make a deliberate decision about whether to adopt it. Without that information, they'd have to conduct their own validation studies, which many smaller health systems simply can't do.‚Źé‚Źé**STANDARDS AND INTEROPERABILITY CONSIDERATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to briefly address how this connects to the broader interoperability and standards landscape. The DSI certification requirement is one of several mechanisms for ensuring that health IT is transparent and accountable. It works in concert with HL7 FHIR standards for clinical decision support (FHIR CDS hooks), with information blocking rules, and with other transparency and accountability mechanisms.‚Źé‚ŹéIf we're moving toward a more deregulated, market-driven health IT landscape, we need to be thoughtful about which requirements to remove and which to keep. The DSI transparency requirement is one that actually serves the market. It gives clinicians and health systems the information they need to make good purchasing decisions. It creates accountability for developers. It's not a barrier to entry. It's a mechanism for ensuring that the market functions well.‚Źé‚Źé**CLOSING THOUGHTS**‚Źé‚ŹéWe understand that the Office of the National Coordinator is under pressure to reduce regulatory burden and to promote innovation in health IT. We support those goals, genuinely. But we're asking that you retain the DSI certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures. This requirement is not a barrier to innovation. It's a foundation for responsible innovation. It gives clinicians the information they need to make informed decisions. It creates accountability for developers. And it's essential for ensuring that health IT serves all patients, not just those in well-resourced urban settings.‚Źé‚ŹéIf there are specific aspects of the requirement that are burdensome, we're happy to work with ONC on refinement. But removing it entirely would be a mistake, and we'd urge you to reconsider that approach.‚Źé‚ŹéThank you for the opportunity to comment on this important issue.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-18T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-18T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ7‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHospital and health system procurement teams need transparent AI model information to make evidence-based decisions about which tools to adopt and how to implement them safely in their local contexts.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒhopeful‚ôĒIowa‚ôĒpolicy director‚ôĒ44‚ôĒLast spring, I was reviewing surgical outcome data for our equity initiative when we discovered that rural hospitals in underserved counties weren't reporting complications at the same rates as urban centers‚ÄĒnot because outcomes were better, but because their systems couldn't capture the granular details ours could. I realized then that loosening data standards might look like freedom on paper, but for the patients we're trying to reach, it could mean their outcomes simply disappear from view.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2290
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0184‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒSusan Gonzalez‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒWhile improved data sharing between healthcare providers is essential for patient safety, weakening transparency requirements for AI systems would undermine the trust necessary for patients to consent to data sharing and could leave them vulnerabl...‚ôĒEvery week in my work as a home health aide, I see firsthand how hard it is when doctors don't have the full picture. Last month I spent three phone calls trying to get Mrs. Chen's test results from her cardiologist to her new primary care doctor. They needed that information to help her safely. But here's what worries me about loosening transparency rules: if companies can hide how their AI systems were trained or tested, patients lose the ability to understand what's actually guiding their care. Mrs. Chen is already nervous about privacy. If she finds out her data went into some black box algorithm nobody can explain, she might stop trusting doctors altogether. That doesn't help anyone. Real data sharing depends on trust. Removing transparency requirements doesn't make access easier, it just lets companies avoid responsibility when something goes wrong.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-26T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-26T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒmedium‚ôĒhopeful‚ôĒWashington‚ôĒhome health aide‚ôĒ57‚ôĒLast month, I was helping Mrs. Chen coordinate care between her cardiologist and her new primary care doctor, and I realized neither office had her latest test results‚ÄĒit took three phone calls and a faxed form to get them talking to each other.
