CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0185‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒLinda Wilson‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA retired firefighter argues that proposed deregulation eliminating AI transparency requirements and information-blocking protections would endanger patients by removing documentation standards that ensure clinical decision-support systems are val...‚ôĒ**COMMENT ON PROPOSED DEREGULATION OF HEALTH IT CERTIFICATION AND INFORMATION BLOCKING REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéI'm a 78-year-old retired firefighter from Tulsa, Oklahoma. I spent thirty years running into burning buildings, and in that work I learned something that applies directly to what's happening with these proposed changes to health IT rules: you don't get to call something "freedom" when it means hiding the tools and systems that keep people alive. Last month, my cardiologist ordered cardiac imaging tests that my pulmonologist in Oklahoma City had already completed six months earlier. The two hospital systems couldn't transfer my records between their electronic systems, so I paid out of pocket for duplicate testing and waited an extra week while my arrhythmia went unmonitored. Now I'm hearing that federal regulators want to loosen the rules requiring these doctors to share my data with each other, and I want to be direct: this is recklessness dressed up as deregulation.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed changes would remove certification requirements and transparency standards for AI-based clinical decision support systems, model cards documenting how these algorithms work, and validation evidence showing what populations they were tested on. I understand the argument that regulation creates burden. I also understand that some rules are badly designed. But I need people making these decisions to understand what gets lost when you remove the documentation requirements that currently force developers to prove their systems work before they're used on patients. In firefighting, we don't call equipment inspections a "burden" that slows down our response to emergencies. We call them the foundation of accountability. When you're about to enter a building, you need to know if your ladder is sound, if your air tank was properly filled, if the equipment has been tested under the conditions you're about to face. You need documentation. You need proof. You need to know the limits of what you're carrying into that fire.‚Źé‚Źé**THE REAL COST OF REMOVING TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule would eliminate requirements for health IT developers to provide model cards and other documentation explaining how AI systems make clinical recommendations. Model cards are relatively simple documents that describe what an algorithm was trained on, what populations it was tested on, what its known limitations are, and what risks it poses. They're not bureaucratic theater. They're the equivalent of an equipment inspection report. When my cardiologist recommends a particular treatment based partly on an AI system's analysis of my heart rhythms, I have a right to know whether that system was validated on patients like me, or whether it was built and tested primarily on younger patients without arrhythmia history. That's not asking for unnecessary complexity. That's asking for basic honesty about what we know and don't know about the tool being used to guide my care.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Institute of Medicine's 2015 report on diagnostic errors in clinical practice found that most diagnostic mistakes occur not because individual clinicians are careless, but because systems lack transparency about their limitations and because information gets siloed across different care settings. The proposed changes would make both problems worse. Removing model card requirements means clinicians and patients lose access to documented evidence about how algorithms perform on different patient populations. That's especially dangerous for people like me with multiple conditions. An AI system validated only on generally healthy patients might fail catastrophically when applied to someone with comorbidities. I'm not anti-technology. I'm anti-recklessness. And hiding the documentation that would let my doctors and me understand an algorithm's limitations before it's used to guide my treatment is recklessness.‚Źé‚Źé**INFORMATION BLOCKING AND THE RIGHT TO KNOW WHAT'S IN YOUR OWN SYSTEM**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed changes also seek to weaken information blocking regulations, which currently require healthcare providers to share patient data with other providers and with patients themselves when requested. The justification is that these rules are duplicative or impose unnecessary burden. I've heard arguments that providers should have more flexibility about how and when they share data, and that the current rules create technical requirements that slow down innovation. Let me be clear about what happens when you loosen these rules: you get what I experienced last month. You get fragmented care. You get duplicate testing. You get gaps in monitoring that could cost lives.‚Źé‚ŹéMy cardiologist in Tulsa and my pulmonologist in Oklahoma City are not competing with each other. They're supposed to be treating the same patient, which is me. The information blocking rules exist because healthcare providers have economic incentives to keep patient data locked up in their own systems. If my pulmonologist's hospital system controls my lung imaging data, and my cardiologist's hospital system controls my cardiac data, neither system has much incentive to make it easy for the other to access that information. The patient pays the cost, literally and in terms of health outcomes. Loosening information blocking regulations in the name of reducing "burden" on providers means putting the burden back on patients, and on the clinicians trying to provide coherent care across fragmented systems.‚Źé‚ŹéThe NIST AI Risk Management Framework, Section 4.2, specifically identifies transparency and explainability requirements as non-negotiable elements of responsible AI deployment in high-stakes domains like healthcare. The framework doesn't frame these as optional nice-to-haves. It identifies them as core risk management practices. When you're asking clinicians to use AI systems to make recommendations about patient treatment, you need documented evidence that those systems have been properly validated. You need to know what data they were trained on. You need to know what populations they were tested on. You need to know what they're bad at. That's not regulation for regulation's sake. That's the minimum standard for accountability in a system where the consequences of failure are measured in human lives.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CERTIFICATION QUESTION THAT MATTERS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe RFI asks which certification criteria should be removed or revised as duplicative or unnecessary. I want to be specific about what should not be removed. The certification requirement that health IT systems demonstrate interoperability with other systems, and that they provide clear documentation of their validation testing, is not duplicative. It's foundational. It's the only thing standing between patients and a system where each hospital network locks away patient data for competitive advantage, and where AI tools make clinical recommendations with no documented evidence that they work for patients like you.‚Źé‚ŹéI understand that certification costs money. I understand that meeting these requirements takes time and resources. I also understand that in firefighting, we spend money on equipment checks and training that doesn't directly save a life on any given day. We do it because we know that the cost of not doing it is catastrophic. That's the calculation that should apply here. The cost of a developer having to provide a model card documenting their AI system's training data and known limitations is not comparable to the cost of a patient receiving a treatment recommendation from a black box algorithm that was never validated on people with their condition.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT DEREGULATION SHOULD ACTUALLY MEAN**‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not arguing against all regulatory change. I'm arguing for deregulation that actually protects patients while reducing unnecessary burden on developers and providers. Here's what that would look like: keep the requirements that developers document their systems and that providers share patient data across systems. Reduce the bureaucratic complexity of how those requirements are implemented. Streamline the certification process. Make it faster and cheaper to prove your system works, but don't eliminate the requirement that you prove it at all. Don't eliminate the requirement that you tell clinicians and patients what you know and don't know about your system's limitations.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed changes frame transparency and data sharing as burdens to be minimized. I'm asking you to reframe them as the cost of trust. When I'm lying in a hospital bed with an irregular heartbeat, and a clinician is deciding whether to recommend a medication or procedure based partly on an AI system's analysis, I need to know that system was built and tested responsibly. I need to know my doctor has access to all my medical history, not just the part that's convenient for their hospital network to share. Those aren't oppressive regulations. They're the minimum conditions for responsible medicine.‚Źé‚ŹéI've had a long career in a profession where we take accountability seriously. We know the difference between freedom and recklessness. Removing the documentation and data sharing requirements won't free up innovation. It will free up the ability to hide how systems work and whose data they were built on. That's not progress. That's a step backward.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-03T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-03T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ4‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒurgent‚ôĒOklahoma‚ôĒfirefighter‚ôĒ78‚ôĒLast month, my cardiologist in Tulsa ordered tests my pulmonologist in Oklahoma City had already run six months earlier, but we couldn't get the records transferred between their systems‚ÄĒso I paid for duplicates and waited an extra week while my arrhythmia went unmonitored. Now I'm hearing they might loosen the rules that force these doctors to share my data, and I'm furious: after thirty years running into burning buildings, I know the difference between freedom and recklessness, and making it easier for my doctors to hide my medical history from each other isn't freedom.‚ôĒRemoving model cards and transparency requirements eliminates the accountability mechanism that allows patients and providers to understand how AI systems work before they're used in life-or-death decisions‚ÄĒthis is recklessness, not deregulation. | Healthcare providers need documented evidence of AI validation and known risks the same way firefighters need equipment inspection records; hiding that information puts patients at risk just like hiding a faulty ladder puts firefighters at risk. | Patients have a right to know what data trained an AI system and what populations it was tested on, because an algorithm validated only on younger, healthier patients may fail catastrophically on someone like me with complex comorbidities.‚ôĒLinda frames transparency requirements as non-negotiable safety guardrails, not bureaucratic burden‚ÄĒshe draws a direct parallel between accountability in emergency response and accountability in clinical AI deployment.‚ôĒFDA's 2021 Proposed Regulatory Framework for Modifications to AI/ML-Based Software as a Medical Device | Institute of Medicine report on diagnostic errors in clinical practice (2015) | NIST AI Risk Management Framework, Section 4.2 on transparency and explainability requirements | Journal of Medical Internet Research study on patient trust in AI-assisted diagnosis when model documentation is absent‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1418
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0186‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒCharles Taylor‚ôĒURAALI Refaluwasch Association‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe URAALI Refaluwasch Association opposes the removal of Decision Support Interventions certification requirements for AI model cards and transparency disclosures, arguing that documentation of intended use, target populations, training data char...‚ôĒRe: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity‚ŹéRIN 0945-ZA03‚Źé‚ŹéThe URAALI Refaluwasch Association submits this comment regarding the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria, particularly the requirement for AI model cards and transparency disclosures in clinical decision support systems. We represent patient advocacy organizations and health equity stakeholders across Oklahoma and neighboring states, with direct experience navigating the gap between what regulators assume happens in clinical practice and what actually occurs when families like ours encounter artificial intelligence in healthcare settings.‚Źé‚ŹéOur position is straightforward: the current DSI certification criterion requiring model cards, intended use documentation, target population specifications, known risks disclosure, training data characteristics, and external validation results should remain in place. This is not a call for additional regulation. This is a call to keep guardrails that we have learned, through hard experience, are necessary to protect patients from harm.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PROBLEM WE KNOW FIRSTHAND**‚Źé‚ŹéThree years ago, my daughter was diagnosed with a rare metabolic disorder. The diagnosis itself took fourteen months and involved specialists in three states. What struck me most was not the medical complexity but the information asymmetry. We paid $4,800 out of pocket for genetic sequencing. When we asked the hospital system for access to that data, we were told it would take six to eight weeks. We had to involve a patient advocate and file a formal request under state law before they released what we had funded ourselves. Once we had it, we shared it with metabolic specialists across Oklahoma, Texas, and Kansas. Those specialists helped us understand her condition in ways the original hospital system had not.‚Źé‚ŹéI mention this because I now work in government affairs, and I spend time in meetings with hospital administrators and health IT vendors. I listen to them describe frameworks where AI systems recommend diagnoses or treatment protocols with minimal transparency about how those systems were built, what populations they were tested on, or what risks exist. Some of these vendors are the same entities that made us fight for our own genetic data. The irony is not lost on me.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen I ask about model cards or validation studies, I get responses that range from defensive to evasive. One vendor told me directly that publishing model cards would "compromise competitive advantage." Another said external validation was "not feasible for proprietary systems." What I hear in those conversations is the same logic that delayed my daughter's care: the institution's interests matter more than the patient's right to understand what is being done to them.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY MODEL CARDS AND TRANSPARENCY MATTER IN PRACTICE**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule would remove requirements that developers document intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results (45 CFR 170.315(a)(1) and related guidance in the Federal Register notices from 2020-2024). The stated rationale is reducing burden on developers. I understand burden reduction. I also understand that removing these requirements does not eliminate the need for this information. It simply moves the burden from vendors to patients and clinicians who must now figure out these details on their own, usually after a system has already been deployed.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider what happens without these requirements. A clinical decision support system trained on data from predominantly white, insured populations gets deployed in a rural Oklahoma clinic serving Medicaid patients and Native American communities. No one documents this mismatch. A clinician uses the system to support a diagnosis. The system's recommendations, built on training data that underrepresents this population, are not as accurate for the patients being treated. The clinic has no way to know this is happening because the model card that would have disclosed the training data characteristics is no longer required.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not hypothetical. Literature on algorithmic bias in healthcare consistently documents that AI systems perform differently across demographic groups (Obermeyer et al., Health Affairs, 2019; Gianfrancesco et al., Nature Medicine, 2018; Buolamwini and Gebru, Proceedings of Machine Learning Research, 2018). The solution is not to ignore this problem. The solution is to require documentation so that clinicians can make informed decisions about whether a system is appropriate for their patient population.‚Źé‚ŹéModel cards also create accountability. When a system causes harm, clinicians, patients, and regulators can trace what happened. They can ask: Was this population included in the training data? Was this risk disclosed? Were external validation studies conducted? Without these requirements, the answer is simply unknown. The vendor bears no obligation to have documented anything.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT THE DATA SHOWS ABOUT CLINICAL DECISION SUPPORT**‚Źé‚ŹéThe FDA has overseen clinical decision support systems for years under existing authority. The 21st Century Cures Act (42 U.S.C. 300jj et seq.) explicitly contemplated that certain AI systems would require transparency and validation. The proposed rule does not align with the Cures Act's intent. In fact, the Office of the National Coordinator's own guidance on information blocking and TEFCA (Trusted Exchange Framework and Common Agreement) acknowledges that clinicians need information about data sources and system limitations to make appropriate clinical decisions (ONC guidance documents, 2023-2024).‚Źé‚ŹéWhen ONC surveyed health IT vendors and providers in 2022, a significant portion indicated they lacked confidence in the accuracy of clinical decision support systems across diverse patient populations. That lack of confidence exists because model cards and external validation are sparse. Removing the requirement does not increase confidence. It increases risk while appearing to reduce burden.‚Źé‚ŹéThere is also the question of what happens when a system fails. A hospital in our region deployed a sepsis prediction algorithm without external validation on their specific patient population. The system had high false positive rates. Clinicians learned to ignore its alerts. When the system malfunctioned, it was because no one had documented its limitations. The hospital had to pull the system after six months.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE ARE ASKING FOR**‚Źé‚ŹéThe URAALI Refaluwasch Association asks that the DSI certification criterion remain in effect. Specifically:‚Źé‚Źé- Retain the requirement that developers document intended use, target population, known risks, and training data characteristics‚Źé- Maintain the external validation requirement before systems are deployed in clinical settings‚Źé- Clarify that model cards are not confidential and must be accessible to providers and, upon request, to patients‚Źé- Require that model cards be updated when systems are retrained or when new risks emerge‚Źé- Ensure that information blocking rules continue to apply to access requests for model documentation‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not asking for innovation to stop. It is asking that innovation be transparent. The vendors making the best systems have nothing to hide. The ones resisting model card requirements are the ones where information asymmetry benefits them more than patients.‚Źé‚ŹéMy daughter is doing better now. She has access to her data. We found specialists who understood her condition because we could share genetic information across state lines. But the system that made us fight for that information is the same system deploying AI without explaining how it works.‚Źé‚ŹéI know regulators feel pressure to reduce burden. But burden on vendors should not mean risk to patients. Keeping the DSI transparency requirements is how we ensure that the next family does not have to fight as hard to understand what medicine is doing to them.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-23T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-23T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ4‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒresigned‚ôĒOklahoma‚ôĒdirector of government affairs‚ôĒ51‚ôĒMy daughter was diagnosed with a rare metabolic disorder three years ago, and we spent eighteen months fighting insurance companies and hospital systems for access to her own genetic sequencing data‚ÄĒdata we'd paid for out of pocket. When we finally got it, we could share it with specialists across three states who helped us understand her condition. But sitting in those meetings with hospital administrators lately, I'm watching the same institutions that made us beg for our own medical information now eager to monetize access under looser frameworks, and I keep wondering who pays the real price when the guardrails come down.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1200
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0187‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒPatricia Adams‚ôĒBroadspire, Inc.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ# Abstract‚Źé‚ŹéBroadspire argues that the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion should be retained because it ensures clinicians receive essential information about clinical decision support tools' intended use, target populations, l...‚ôĒ**Broadspire, Inc. Comments on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions**‚Źé‚ŹéBroadspire, Inc. is a health IT compliance and audit firm based in Delaware with fifteen years of experience helping EHR vendors and healthcare providers navigate federal certification requirements. Our team works directly with vendors on Health IT Certification Program compliance, and we've spent the last eighteen months conducting a comprehensive audit of our largest client's decision support and AI-assisted clinical workflow architecture. That work gave us a window into exactly why the current Decision Support Interventions certification criterion exists, and why removing it would be a mistake.‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be straightforward about our perspective here. Broadspire has skin in the game. Our compliance consulting business benefits when certification requirements exist. Reducing those requirements would reduce demand for our services, and I'm not pretending otherwise. But that financial interest actually makes me more careful about how I think through this, not less. The frustration I feel about the uneven playing field in this market is real, and it's separate from the safety question at the center of this rule.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PROBLEM WE'VE OBSERVED IN THE MARKET**‚Źé‚ŹéOver the past three years, we've watched smaller EHR vendors gain market share not because their products are better, but because they've skipped the documentation and validation work that larger vendors like our clients feel obligated to do. It's genuinely unfair. A vendor with 50 employees can't absorb the cost of a third-party validation study the way a vendor with 500 can. The result is that compliance becomes a competitive disadvantage rather than a baseline standard. I get why the deregulatory impulse exists. I've sat in rooms with our clients' product teams and heard them say, plainly, "We could ship this feature three months faster if we didn't have to document every decision tree and flag every known limitation."‚Źé‚ŹéBut here's what I've learned from actually reading through hundreds of EHR implementations and their decision support systems: the reason those requirements exist is not bureaucratic inertia. They exist because clinicians need to know what they're relying on.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THE DSI CERTIFICATION CRITERION MATTERS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Decision Support Interventions criterion, as currently written in 45 CFR 170.315(a)(4), requires vendors to disclose the intended use, target population, known limitations, training data characteristics, and external validation results for clinical decision support tools that use machine learning or other algorithmic approaches. This isn't a trivial checklist. It's the difference between a clinician who knows they're using a system trained on 85% male cardiology patients (and therefore shouldn't be the only source of guidance for women presenting with atypical cardiac symptoms) and a clinician who has no idea.‚Źé‚ŹéLet me be concrete about this. In our client audit last year, we reviewed their sepsis alert algorithm. It's a good system. It catches sepsis earlier than most manual screening approaches. But the training data came primarily from a large academic medical center with specific patient populations and specific laboratory ordering patterns. When that same algorithm was deployed at a rural critical access hospital with a completely different patient demographic and different lab infrastructure, it had a false positive rate of 34 percent. The clinicians at that hospital didn't know that. They discovered it through experience. If they'd had access to the model card that the certification criterion requires, they would have known to configure it differently from the start.‚Źé‚ŹéThat rural hospital isn't some edge case. It's where most Americans actually receive care. And when a clinician has been alerted 34 times in a shift by a system that's wrong nearly a third of the time, they stop using it. That's not innovation. That's waste.‚Źé‚ŹéThe certification criterion requires four specific elements:‚Źé‚Źé1. **Intended use statement**: What clinical problem is this tool designed to solve, and what is it not designed to do?‚Źé2. **Target population specification**: What patient populations was this system validated on, and where is it safe to deploy?‚Źé3. **Known limitations and risks**: What are the failure modes? When does it perform poorly? What biases are present in the training data?‚Źé4. **Training data characteristics and external validation**: How was the system built, and has it been tested outside the development environment?‚Źé‚ŹéThese aren't onerous. They're the minimum information a clinician needs to use a tool responsibly.‚Źé‚Źé**THE SAFETY CASE FOR RETENTION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI criterion is aligned with emerging clinical standards and regulatory guidance on algorithmic transparency. The FDA's recent guidance on clinical decision support software (issued in March 2024, available on the FDA website) emphasizes that developers should document intended use and known limitations. The American Medical Association's Intelligent Health Advisor initiative has called for similar transparency standards. The National Academy of Medicine's 2022 report on algorithmic bias in healthcare specifically recommends that clinical tools using machine learning include documentation of training data characteristics and known performance variations across patient populations.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving this requirement would put the Health IT Certification Program out of step with the clinical field's own emerging consensus about what responsible AI implementation looks like. And it would do so at the exact moment when clinicians are most uncertain about how to evaluate these tools. I've talked to physicians who are deeply concerned about liability if they use an algorithm they don't understand, and I've talked to others who are using algorithms without any real understanding of their limitations because they assume the system has been validated somehow.‚Źé‚ŹéThe certification criterion creates a forcing function for vendors to do the work that good clinical practice requires anyway. If we remove that requirement, we're not reducing burden. We're just moving the burden from vendors (who have the expertise to characterize their systems) to clinicians and hospitals (who have to figure it out by trial and error).‚Źé‚Źé**OUR CONCERN ABOUT THE COMPETITIVE DYNAMICS**‚Źé‚ŹéHere's where I come back to the frustration I mentioned at the start. I know that removing the DSI criterion would help certain vendors compete on price. And I know that some of those vendors are genuinely innovative in other ways. But I'm concerned that removing this particular requirement would create the wrong kind of competitive advantage.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider two hypothetical vendors. Vendor A spends resources on validation, documentation, and transparency because they believe it's right and because the certification criterion requires it. Vendor B cuts those costs and undercuts Vendor A on price. Both sell to hospitals. Vendor A's tools work well because clinicians understand them and can use them appropriately. Vendor B's tools have higher failure rates, but Vendor B doesn't have to tell anyone that, and the failures are diffuse enough that no single hospital feels responsible for reporting them. Which vendor grows faster in a deregulated environment?‚Źé‚ŹéThe answer is obvious. And the result is a market where innovation gets rewarded only if it's profitable to hide problems, not if it's valuable to solve them.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not claiming there's a simple fix to this dynamic. But removing the DSI criterion isn't the solution. It would make it worse.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE'D RECOMMEND INSTEAD**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the intent is to reduce burden on vendors while maintaining patient safety, there are better approaches. The current criterion could be streamlined without being eliminated:‚Źé‚Źé- Allow vendors to submit model cards in a standardized format (following the framework published by Mitchell et al. in 2019, which has become the de facto standard for machine learning documentation). This reduces the burden of creating documentation while maintaining the requirement to create it.‚Źé- For vendors with limited resources, allow third-party validation rather than requiring in-house validation. This would level the playing field for smaller vendors without eliminating the requirement that validation happen.‚Źé- Create a tiered approach where the level of documentation required scales with the clinical risk of the decision support tool. A low-risk alerting system might require less documentation than a high-risk treatment recommendation system.‚Źé- Allow vendors to satisfy the criterion by integrating with existing clinical decision support platforms (like those conforming to HL7 CDS Hooks) that already include transparency mechanisms. This would reduce redundant documentation.‚Źé‚ŹéAny of these approaches would reduce burden while keeping the core requirement intact: clinicians need to know what they're relying on.‚Źé‚Źé**THE BROADER CONTEXT**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to step back and acknowledge something. The deregulatory impulse behind this proposed rule is not wrong. There are certifications and standards that have outlived their usefulness. There are documentation requirements that are genuinely redundant. There are compliance costs that are genuinely high and don't correspond to real safety benefits. I've seen examples of all three.‚Źé‚ŹéBut the DSI criterion is not one of them. And in fact, the timing of this rule concerns me. We're at a moment when AI and machine learning are being integrated into clinical workflows faster than clinicians' understanding of these tools is evolving. The FDA is tightening its oversight of algorithmic clinical tools. The clinical community is developing new standards for transparency. And the proposed rule would move in the opposite direction, removing one of the few places where federal requirements force vendors to document what they're actually building.‚Źé‚ŹéI also want to note something else that concerns me about the deregulatory approach more broadly. The claim in the rule is that reducing certification burden will promote innovation and competition. But the evidence for that claim is mixed. The IT sector has been heavily deregulated since the 1990s, and that deregulation has been associated with consolidation and reduced competition, not increased competition. A handful of very large vendors (Epic, Cerner, now Oracle after the Cerner acquisition) control the vast majority of the EHR market. A smaller vendor might be able to bring a product to market faster if they don't have to certify it, but they're still competing against vendors with massive installed bases, massive resources, and massive network effects. Removing regulatory requirements doesn't change those dynamics. If anything, it might reinforce them by eliminating one of the few places where smaller vendors and larger vendors are required to meet the same standards.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC FEEDBACK ON THE DSI CRITERION**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the agency is committed to streamlining this requirement, Broadspire would offer the following specific feedback:‚Źé‚ŹéThe current language at 45 CFR 170.315(a)(4) requires documentation of "intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation." This is the right set of requirements. But the submission process could be simplified. Currently, vendors must submit separate documentation for each element, and the certification process requires review of each element. A standardized template would reduce submission burden without reducing transparency.‚Źé‚ŹéThe requirement for "external validation" could be clarified to allow for a range of validation approaches. Currently, vendors interpret this to mean a formal third-party study, which is expensive. The rule could specify that external validation includes any evaluation conducted by someone other than the system developer, including health systems that have deployed and evaluated the tool in their own setting. This would allow smaller vendors to leverage their existing customers' experience rather than funding new studies.‚Źé‚ŹéThe requirement could also explicitly allow vendors to satisfy it by reference to published literature. If a vendor is using a validated algorithm that has been published in a peer-reviewed journal, they should be able to cite that publication rather than conducting a new validation study.‚Źé‚ŹéThese changes would reduce burden without eliminating transparency.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT HAPPENS IF THIS REQUIREMENT IS REMOVED**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be honest about what I think happens if the DSI criterion is removed. In the short term, you'll see more vendors bringing decision support tools to market faster, and you'll see prices fall. That's good for competition. But in the medium term, I think you'll see more clinical failures that are attributed to "algorithm error" but that are actually failures of documentation and transparency. You'll see hospitals discovering after deployment that a tool doesn't work well for their specific patient populations. You'll see clinicians losing trust in clinical decision support because they've had bad experiences with poorly understood tools. And you'll see a bifurcated market where large vendors continue to do transparency work because they have the resources and the liability concerns to justify it, while smaller vendors cut corners because they can.‚Źé‚ŹéThe result will be a market that looks less competitive, not more competitive. And it will be one where innovation in transparency and responsible AI development is penalized rather than rewarded.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéBroadspire supports deregulation where it actually reduces burden without compromising safety. We don't support every certification requirement, and we're open to streamlining the ones that don't add value. But the Decision Support Interventions criterion adds real value. It ensures that clinicians have basic information about the tools they're using. It creates a forcing function for vendors to do the validation work that good clinical practice requires. And it creates a level playing field where smaller vendors and larger vendors are held to the same transparency standards.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the goal is to reduce burden on vendors while promoting competition, there are better ways to do it than removing this requirement. Streamline the documentation process. Expand what counts as valid external validation. Create tiered requirements based on clinical risk. Allow third-party validation. But don't remove the requirement entirely.‚Źé‚ŹéThe fact that we've spent eighteen months documenting our client's decision support architecture is expensive and time-consuming. It's also the right thing to do. And it's what we'd recommend to any vendor building clinical AI tools, whether the certification requirement exists or not. The certification criterion just ensures that everyone does it, not just the vendors who can afford to.‚Źé‚Źé---‚Źé‚Źé**Respectfully submitted,**‚Źé‚ŹéPatricia Adams‚ŹéCompliance Officer‚ŹéBroadspire, Inc.‚ŹéWilmington, Delaware‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-30T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-30T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ7‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHospital and health system procurement teams need transparent AI model information to make evidence-based decisions about which tools to adopt and how to implement them safely in their local contexts.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒresigned‚ôĒDelaware‚ôĒcompliance officer‚ôĒ52‚ôĒMy team spent eighteen months documenting our EHR's compliance architecture for a client audit last year, only to watch a smaller competitor undercut us on price because they'd taken shortcuts we couldn't afford to document‚ÄĒit was frustrating to see the playing field wasn't actually level, and now I'm wondering if loosening these requirements will just make that gap wider or finally give us room to compete on actual innovation instead of paperwork.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2258
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0188‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒRichard Gonzalez‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe commenter opposes removing certification requirements for clinical decision support AI systems, arguing that mandatory documentation of model design, validation populations, known risks, and training data characteristics is essential for patie...‚ôĒ**Re: RFI for Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to start with something that happened last year because it keeps coming back to me when I think about this proposed rule. I was working with a client who'd had a stroke, and we spent three weeks calling between hospitals trying to get her imaging records transferred so her neurologist could actually see what they were working with. Three weeks. Her family was terrified we were missing something in the first critical window, and the only reason we finally got those records was because I sat on hold for four hours one afternoon and found someone who would manually pull and fax them. That shouldn't have required luck or persistence. But now I'm reading about removing certification criteria for decision support interventions, and I'm thinking about something else entirely: what happens when the systems get easier to access but nobody has to tell us how they work.‚Źé‚Źé**MY CONCERN WITH REMOVING DSI CERTIFICATION REQUIREMENTS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule suggests removing the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion that currently requires AI model cards, transparency disclosures, and documentation of intended use, target populations, known risks, and training data characteristics. I understand the deregulatory impulse. I know certification takes time and costs money. I've sat in enough meetings listening to health IT developers talk about how compliance slows innovation. But I need to be direct: this is the wrong place to cut corners.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what I've observed in my work. When clinicians use a tool without understanding its limitations, they tend to trust it too much. A neurologist looking at imaging recommendations doesn't necessarily know if that AI was trained mostly on younger populations or predominantly on one demographic group. They don't know if it was validated against the specific patient population sitting in front of them. And if something goes wrong, if there's a missed diagnosis or a treatment recommendation that doesn't fit that particular patient's circumstances, there's no clear record of what the system was designed to do in the first place. That's not just a compliance problem. That's a patient safety problem.‚Źé‚ŹéI think about my client's daughter. She has the same diagnosis her mother had. She's in a complicated situation with her ex-partner, and I worry constantly about what information he could piece together if he knew which providers she was seeing, which treatments she was on. The current rules require some transparency about how data flows and who can access what. But if we strip away the requirements that AI systems document their design and limitations, we also lose one of the few mechanisms for tracking whether a system is being used appropriately or if safeguards are actually working. Transparency isn't just about innovation. It's about accountability when something fails.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY MODEL CARDS AND TRANSPARENCY MATTER FOR PATIENT SAFETY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe certification criterion requiring AI model cards isn't bureaucratic busywork. Let me lay out what's actually being requested and why it matters:‚Źé‚Źé- **Intended use and target population documentation**: A clinical decision support tool trained primarily on data from urban academic medical centers might perform very differently in rural settings or in populations with different baseline health characteristics. If a clinician doesn't know this, they can't adjust their reliance on the tool's recommendations. That's not a theoretical concern. I've watched clinicians make decisions based on what they assumed a tool was designed for, when it was actually built for something narrower.‚Źé‚Źé- **Known risks and limitations**: Every AI system has blindspots. Known risks documentation tells clinicians what those blindspots are. It tells patients what they should know about whether a recommendation came from a validated system or an experimental one. It tells regulators whether a problem is a design issue or a training data issue.‚Źé‚Źé- **Training data characteristics and external validation**: This is where I keep getting stuck. If a system is trained on data that's 70 percent white, 30 percent Black, and 2 percent Hispanic, but it's being deployed in a clinic that's 50 percent Hispanic, we need to know that. We need to know if external validation happened in populations that match the actual patients being treated. Right now, that information exists somewhere. If we remove the requirement to disclose it, it won't exist in any official, auditable form.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not an engineer. I don't write code. But I've spent thirty years working in healthcare, watching how decisions cascade down to patients. The clinicians I work with aren't trying to cut corners. They're trying to help people. But they can only make good decisions if they understand what they're using.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT HAPPENS WITHOUT TRANSPARENCY**‚Źé‚ŹéThere's something I keep thinking about that might sound tangential but isn't. When I was helping my client navigate her care, one of the hospitals refused to release imaging to another hospital because they said it was "for internal use only." The hospital wasn't being malicious. They had liability concerns. They thought releasing the data meant they were endorsing whatever treatment the other hospital recommended. We had to get her permission in writing, get the neurologist to request it directly, basically prove we had a legitimate clinical need. This is exactly the kind of friction that the rule is trying to remove, and I'm not opposed to removing it. Patients should be able to move their information between providers. That's basic good sense.‚Źé‚ŹéBut removing friction from information access without keeping the safeguards that ensure information is used appropriately is like taking the locks off a cabinet because you want people to find the files faster. The problem isn't access. The problem is what happens when you can access something but you don't understand what you're looking at.‚Źé‚ŹéIf I remove the requirement that AI systems document their design, validation, and limitations, I'm not just reducing burden on developers. I'm removing one of the few ways that patients, clinicians, and regulators can verify that a system is doing what it's supposed to do. I'm removing the paper trail that would show, after the fact, whether a system was being used outside its validated population or whether it had known risks that weren't communicated.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT NEEDS TO STAY IN PLACE**‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not arguing against interoperability. I'm not arguing against making it easier for patients to access their records or for clinicians to share information. Those are important. What I'm arguing for is keeping the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion that requires:‚Źé‚Źé1. Clear documentation of what a clinical AI system is designed to do, who it was designed for, and what populations it was validated in.‚Źé‚Źé2. Disclosure of known limitations and risks so that clinicians and patients can factor those into their decision-making.‚Źé‚Źé3. Accessible documentation of training data characteristics so that bias and representativeness can be assessed.‚Źé‚Źé4. Requirement for external validation before deployment, not after, so that we catch problems before they affect real patients.‚Źé‚ŹéThis isn't burdensome in the way that, say, requiring a physical copy of every audit log would be. This is asking developers to document what they should know about their own system. Most responsible developers are doing this anyway. The certification requirement just makes sure it's done consistently and that there's a public record of it.‚Źé‚Źé**ACCOUNTABILITY AND REAL-WORLD CONSEQUENCES**‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what worries me most. If something goes wrong, if a patient is harmed because a clinical decision support system was used outside its validated population or had a known limitation that wasn't disclosed, the only way to understand what happened is to reconstruct it from documentation. If there's no requirement to document it in the first place, that reconstruction becomes almost impossible. You can't hold a system accountable for harm if you've removed the mechanism for understanding how it was supposed to work.‚Źé‚ŹéI work with vulnerable patients. Some of them are in unstable housing situations. Some are dealing with substance use issues. Some are managing multiple chronic conditions. The clinical decisions that affect them often involve AI-assisted tools now. Those tools are making recommendations about medication, about whether someone should be admitted to a hospital, about what treatment options to prioritize. Those decisions matter enormously. I need to know that the person making those recommendations has good information about what the tool is actually designed to do.‚Źé‚ŹéThe certification criterion requiring model cards and transparency documentation is one of the few mechanisms we have for ensuring that information is available. Removing it doesn't make the system more innovative. It just makes it more opaque. And in healthcare, opacity doesn't protect innovation. It enables harm.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm asking you to keep this criterion in place. I'm asking you to require that clinical decision support systems document what they're designed for, who they've been validated in, and what their known limitations are. This isn't about slowing down innovation. It's about making sure that when innovation reaches patients, we understand what we're deploying and why.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-17T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-17T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ4‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒconcerned‚ôĒMississippi‚ôĒsocial worker‚ôĒ63‚ôĒLast year, I helped a client navigate care after a stroke, and we spent three weeks calling between hospitals trying to get her imaging records transferred so her neurologist could see her full history‚ÄĒtime we didn't have. Now that she's stable, I'm relieved that might get easier for the next person, but I'm also thinking about her daughter who has that same diagnosis and lives with an abusive ex; I worry that if safeguards slip, he could more easily access her medical information if he knows which providers she's seeing.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1480
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0189‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDorothy Mitchell‚ôĒDarena Solutions LLC‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒDarena Solutions urges ONC to retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion requiring AI model transparency and documentation, arguing that while compliance burden is real, removing these requirements would shift validation res...‚ôĒ**Darena Solutions LLC**‚ŹéPortland, Oregon‚Źé‚ŹéRe: RFI Response ‚Äď Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions‚Źé‚ŹéThank you for the opportunity to comment on the proposed modifications to the Health IT Certification Program. Darena Solutions has spent the last eight years working directly with health systems, clinics, and EHR vendors on compliance and interoperability challenges. Last year's experience helping a rural clinic in Eastern Oregon transition from a legacy EHR system to a modern platform gave us a concrete, sobering view of how regulatory frameworks‚ÄĒeven well-designed ones‚ÄĒcan inadvertently create switching costs that trap smaller providers in vendor relationships. That project took six months of pure regulatory navigation before the clinic could even begin thinking about patient care improvements. We want to speak directly to one aspect of the proposed rule where we believe the current framework is doing real work that the deregulatory impulse might overlook: the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion and its requirements for AI model transparency.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PROBLEM WE'RE TRYING TO SOLVE**‚Źé‚ŹéThe question of whether to reduce certification burden is not abstract for us. We see the genuine friction that documentation requirements create. Smaller vendors complain constantly that compliance costs eat up resources that could go to product development. Larger health systems tell us that maintaining certification across multiple product lines creates redundant administrative work. These complaints are not frivolous. But we've also watched what happens when transparency requirements disappear or weaken in healthcare settings, and the calculus is more complicated than the deregulatory framework acknowledges.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen we worked with the rural clinic last year, one of the first things their IT director asked us was whether they could trust the clinical decision support tools in their new EHR. The vendor had implemented what looked like an AI-driven medication interaction checker. The clinic's pharmacy director asked a straightforward question: what training data was this built on? What populations was it tested with? Were there known failure modes? The vendor couldn't answer. Not because they were hiding anything, but because the previous system hadn't required them to document it, and they'd inherited the tool from a legacy codebase without that level of transparency. The clinic ended up running their own validation study before they would allow the system to generate alerts. That took three months and cost them about $35,000.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is the human problem we're trying to flag. In a deregulated environment, vendors have no obligation to create model cards, document intended use, describe target populations, or disclose training data characteristics. Providers, especially smaller ones without dedicated data science teams, end up doing that work themselves. Or they don't, and clinical decisions get made on the basis of algorithmic recommendations that nobody fully understands. The burden doesn't disappear; it shifts. And it shifts to the people least equipped to handle it.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THE CURRENT DSI CRITERION MATTERS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current Decision Support Interventions certification criterion requires vendors to document AI and algorithmic tools with specificity: intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. This is not performative compliance. We've reviewed model cards from fifteen different vendors across our portfolio, and they vary wildly in quality. Some are genuinely useful‚ÄĒthey tell you exactly what the algorithm was trained on, what it was validated against, and what the performance looks like in specific subpopulations. Others are thin and evasive. But even thin documentation is better than no documentation.‚Źé‚ŹéThe requirement exists because healthcare is not like other industries. When an algorithm makes a recommendation about patient care, the stakes are different. A clinical decision support tool that works well for white male patients but fails for women or patients over 75 is not just a product quality issue; it's a patient safety issue. A medication interaction checker that was trained on data from a single health system in Massachusetts might perform differently in a rural Oregon clinic serving a different population with different comorbidities. These aren't theoretical concerns. There's published evidence in the medical informatics literature that shows algorithm performance degrades substantially across different care settings and patient populations (Rajkomar et al., "Scalable and accurate deep learning with electronic health records," Nature Medicine, 2018).‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving the requirement to document these characteristics doesn't make the problems go away. It just means providers stop knowing about them until something goes wrong. We're not talking about eliminating a minor paperwork requirement. We're talking about eliminating the one mechanism that forces vendors to think systematically about whether their tools actually work in the settings where they're being deployed.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE'RE ASKING THE AGENCY TO PRESERVE**‚Źé‚ŹéOur recommendation is straightforward: retain the current DSI certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures. We're not arguing that the criterion is perfect. We think it could be refined. We think the requirements could be streamlined to reduce redundant documentation without sacrificing the core information that providers need to make informed decisions about deploying these tools. But we're asking you not to eliminate it entirely.‚Źé‚ŹéThe specific elements that matter most are:‚Źé‚Źé* Intended use documentation. What is this tool designed to do? What is it not designed to do? This sounds simple, but most vendors are vague about this. We've seen vendors deploy tools far beyond their intended scope because the original documentation never clearly bounded the use case.‚Źé‚Źé* Target population specification. What populations was this algorithm developed and validated on? Age ranges, comorbidity profiles, insurance types, racial and ethnic demographics where available‚ÄĒthis matters. A sepsis prediction algorithm trained on ICU patients may not perform well in emergency departments. A readmission risk tool validated in academic medical centers may not work in community health centers.‚Źé‚Źé* Training data characteristics. What data was used to build this? How much data? What time period? Where did it come from? These details let providers assess whether the algorithm is likely to work in their setting.‚Źé‚Źé* Known limitations and risk factors. Every algorithm has failure modes. The requirement to document them forces vendors to think about this systematically rather than discovering problems reactively. It also protects providers by making sure they understand what they're working with.‚Źé‚Źé* External validation results. Has this been tested outside the development setting? With what results? This is the only way to know if performance holds up when the algorithm encounters data it hasn't seen before.‚Źé‚ŹéThese aren't burdensome requirements. They're baseline transparency. A well-organized vendor can produce a useful model card in a few weeks. The burden is real, but it's not crushing. What we're worried about is that in the rush to reduce regulatory burden, the agency will eliminate requirements that providers actually depend on to make safe clinical decisions.‚Źé‚Źé**THE VENDOR ARGUMENT AND WHY IT'S INCOMPLETE**‚Źé‚ŹéWe understand the vendors' position. Compliance costs are real. They reduce resources available for innovation. Smaller vendors face disproportionate burden because they can't amortize compliance costs across as large a product portfolio. These are legitimate business concerns. But the deregulatory frame assumes that removing requirements will lead to more innovation and market competition, and that assumption doesn't hold up well in health IT.‚Źé‚ŹéIn our work with health systems across Oregon and Washington, we've found that smaller providers don't actually make vendor choices based on compliance burden. They make them based on functionality, cost, and implementation support. A small clinic doesn't switch EHRs because a vendor can save them time on model documentation. They switch when they find a system that works better for their patient population and their workflow. What compliance requirements do affect is whether a vendor can enter the market at all. Removing DSI documentation requirements might help a scrappy startup build an AI tool faster, but it won't help them sell it to providers who need to trust that the tool actually works.‚Źé‚ŹéThe other piece the deregulatory argument misses is that vendors benefit from standards, even when those standards create compliance work. A vendor who invests in proper model documentation can market that documentation as a differentiator. They can say to providers, "We've done the validation work. We've documented our tool thoroughly. You can trust it." Right now, that differentiation is limited because all certified vendors have to meet the same standard. If you remove the standard, the vendors with the resources to do thorough documentation still will, and they'll use that as a competitive advantage. The vendors without resources won't, and they'll lose market share. The deregulatory approach assumes that removing standards increases competition. In practice, it often increases concentration by rewarding the vendors who can afford to do things right without being required to.‚Źé‚Źé**REAL-WORLD IMPACT ON SMALLER PROVIDERS**‚Źé‚ŹéThis is where our Eastern Oregon clinic experience becomes relevant. The clinic serves about 8,000 patients, mostly in rural areas outside Pendleton. About 40 percent of their patients are on Medicaid. They have a pharmacy director, two nurse practitioners, and a part-time IT person who spends most of his time keeping the internet working and troubleshooting printer issues. When they evaluated EHR systems, they looked at three options. The decision came down to implementation cost and whether the system could connect to the state's health information exchange.‚Źé‚ŹéThe vendor they chose included a clinical decision support module with a medication interaction checker and a chronic disease management tool. The vendor provided documentation that the tools were certified. What they didn't provide was the model card-level detail about how those tools were built, what data they were trained on, or how they performed in small rural practices. The clinic's medical director asked. The vendor said that documentation wasn't readily available‚ÄĒit existed somewhere in their documentation system, but it wasn't organized in a way they could easily share.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is a real conversation that happened in 2023. The clinic ended up trusting the vendor because the EHR system was otherwise good and they didn't have time to investigate further. But they also didn't use the decision support tools as fully as they could have, because they weren't confident in them. That's not a compliance win. That's a situation where a provider is underutilizing tools that could improve patient care because they don't have enough information to feel confident deploying them.‚Źé‚ŹéIf you remove the DSI documentation requirement, what changes? The vendor has even less incentive to produce documentation. The clinic has even less recourse if they want to understand how the tool works. The gap between what large academic medical centers can do (hire data scientists to validate tools locally) and what small clinics can do (trust the vendor, or don't use the tool) gets wider.‚Źé‚Źé**ALTERNATIVE APPROACHES WORTH CONSIDERING**‚Źé‚ŹéWe're not arguing that the current DSI criterion is optimal. There are ways to streamline it without eliminating it. One approach would be to differentiate requirements based on risk level. A medication interaction checker has higher clinical risk than a patient education tool. Maybe the requirements for model documentation could be scaled to match the risk profile of the tool. Higher-risk tools get more stringent documentation requirements. Lower-risk tools get lighter ones.‚Źé‚ŹéAnother approach would be to create a standardized model card template that vendors could fill out, reducing the work of producing documentation from scratch. The FDA has moved in this direction with their AI guidance documents (FDA, "Good Machine Learning Practice for Medical Device Development," 2021). They don't require vendors to submit lengthy narrative documents; they require structured documentation that can be reviewed efficiently.‚Źé‚ŹéA third option would be to allow vendors to submit model documentation at different levels of detail depending on the scope of use. A tool deployed in a single health system might require less documentation than a tool deployed nationally. This would reduce burden for smaller vendors while maintaining transparency for high-risk, widely-deployed tools.‚Źé‚ŹéThe point is that there are middle grounds between "full DSI certification with all current requirements" and "no documentation requirements at all." The deregulatory proposal seems to assume that only the endpoints matter. In practice, the thoughtful regulatory approach is usually somewhere in the middle.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT DEREGULATION WITHOUT GUARDRAILS LOOKS LIKE**‚Źé‚ŹéWe've been working in health IT long enough to have seen what happens when transparency requirements go away. In the early 2000s, before meaningful use and certified EHR technology, there was less regulation of clinical decision support. What we saw was a lot of tools deployed without validation, algorithms that didn't work well in real-world settings, and providers making clinical decisions based on recommendations they didn't understand. Some of that was fine. Some of it led to patient harm.‚Źé‚ŹéThe reason the DSI criterion exists is that CMS and ONC learned from experience that vendors won't voluntarily document their tools if they're not required to. The certification program created a baseline standard that shifted vendor behavior. We're not saying vendors are dishonest or that they're trying to hide things. We're saying that in the absence of a requirement, vendors optimize for other things: speed to market, cost reduction, feature proliferation. Documentation of how tools actually work and where they might fail gets deprioritized.‚Źé‚ŹéThe deregulatory argument assumes that market competition will create incentives for transparency. Maybe it will, over time. But the transition period‚ÄĒthe years between deregulation and the emergence of market-driven transparency standards‚ÄĒis when patient safety risk concentrates. Providers will deploy tools without full information. Algorithms will be used in populations they weren't validated on. Things will go wrong, sometimes seriously.‚Źé‚ŹéWe're not apocalyptic about this. We're not saying that removing the DSI criterion will cause widespread harm. We're saying that the risk is real, concentrated in smaller providers who don't have the resources to validate tools independently, and not justified by the compliance burden reduction. The burden of producing a model card is not that large. The value of having one is substantial.‚Źé‚Źé**IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the agency decides to keep the DSI documentation requirement, there are practical implementation questions worth thinking through. One is the question of how often documentation needs to be updated. If an algorithm is retrained quarterly with new data, does the model card need to be updated quarterly? That could create genuine burden. A reasonable approach might be to require model card updates when the algorithm's performance changes meaningfully, but allow vendors to define what "meaningfully" means based on their own risk assessment.‚Źé‚ŹéAnother question is how detailed the training data documentation needs to be. If a vendor trained an algorithm on data from 47 health systems across 12 states, do they need to list all 47? Or is a summary sufficient? There's room for streamlining here without sacrificing transparency. Providers don't need to know the name of every institution in the training set. They do need to know the geographic distribution, the population characteristics, and the time period.‚Źé‚ŹéA third consideration is the relationship between DSI documentation and other regulatory requirements. Right now, vendors have to produce clinical validation studies for some tools and model cards for others. There might be opportunities to consolidate documentation requirements so that vendors aren't producing overlapping reports. This would reduce burden without reducing information.‚Źé‚ŹéThese are the kinds of refinements that a thoughtful deregulatory approach might make. Eliminate redundancy. Streamline documentation formats. Differentiate requirements based on risk. But don't eliminate the transparency requirement altogether. The transparency is doing real work.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THIS MATTERS FOR THE BROADER DEREGULATORY AGENDA**‚Źé‚ŹéWe support the general goal of the deregulatory proposal. Health IT is overregulated in some respects. Certification requirements are sometimes redundant. There are opportunities to reduce burden without sacrificing safety. But there's a difference between deregulation that's thoughtful and deregulation that's wholesale.‚Źé‚ŹéThe strongest regulatory requirements are the ones that address real problems. The DSI documentation requirement addresses a real problem: the fact that vendors don't have strong market incentives to validate their tools across different populations and settings, and providers don't have good ways to evaluate whether tools will work for their patients. Removing that requirement doesn't make the problem go away. It just makes it less visible.‚Źé‚ŹéWe've been in health IT long enough to know that the easiest time to build bad incentives into a system is when you're deregulating. Once you remove a requirement, it's very hard to put it back. Vendors adjust their business models around the new environment. Providers adjust their expectations. The status quo ante becomes politically difficult to restore.‚Źé‚ŹéSo our ask is this: think carefully about which requirements are actually creating problems, and which ones are addressing real issues. The DSI documentation requirement is in the second category. It's not perfect. It could be streamlined. But it's doing work that matters. Removing it wholesale is a mistake.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéDarena Solutions works at the intersection of regulatory compliance and health IT implementation. We see the genuine burden that compliance creates. We also see what happens when providers lose the tools they need to make informed decisions about deploying new technology. The decision support interventions certification criterion, specifically the requirement for AI model transparency and documentation, is one of those tools. It's not onerous. The compliance work is real but manageable. The value is substantial, especially for smaller providers who can't afford to validate tools independently.‚Źé‚ŹéWe're not asking the agency to reject the deregulatory proposal. We're asking them to be precise about which requirements are creating unjustified burden, and which ones are addressing real problems. The DSI documentation requirement falls in the second category. We recommend retaining it, with potential streamlining to reduce redundancy and administrative overhead, but without eliminating the core transparency requirements that providers depend on to deploy AI tools safely and effectively.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Eastern Oregon clinic we helped last year ended up implementing the EHR system successfully. They've been using it for about eighteen months now, and it's working well for them. But they're still cautious about the decision support tools, partly because they don't have enough confidence in them. That's the kind of provider hesitation that the DSI documentation requirement is designed to prevent. Removing the requirement won't make that hesitation go away. It will just make it harder for providers to address.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-02-05T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-05T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ9‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHospital and health system procurement teams need transparent AI model information to make evidence-based decisions about which tools to adopt and how to implement them safely in their local contexts.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒsupportive‚ôĒOregon‚ôĒcompliance officer‚ôĒ45‚ôĒWhen we helped a rural clinic in Eastern Oregon migrate off a legacy EHR system last year, their IT director spent six months navigating overlapping certification requirements just to switch vendors‚ÄĒtime and resources that could have gone toward actual patient care improvements instead of regulatory paperwork. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0190‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒGeorge Adams‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒWhile acknowledging the need for improved healthcare data accessibility, the commenter urges caution against deregulating AI transparency requirements in healthcare, arguing that patients need to understand how AI systems work and be assured they ...‚ôĒMy wife Sandra was diagnosed with diabetes last year, and I've spent a lot of time calling between her doctors trying to get records moved around. Last month when we switched to a new specialist, I had to make three separate phone calls just to track down copies of her test results. It took nearly two weeks. I kept thinking how much simpler this would be if I could just pull everything up myself when she needs it.‚Źé‚ŹéBut here's what worries me. Before you start removing rules about how AI systems work in healthcare, I need to understand what I'm actually trusting with my wife's life. When doctors start using AI to make decisions about her diabetes treatment or her test results, I want to know how that system was built and whether it was tested fairly on people like her. Right now if something goes wrong, at least there's a way to look back and see what happened. If you remove the requirements for companies to explain how their AI works and what tests they ran, I won't have any way to know if the recommendation was good or if the system is making mistakes.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not against better technology. I just need to be able to understand it and trust it before doctors use it to care for Sandra.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-02T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-02T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒlow‚ôĒhopeful‚ôĒAlabama‚ôĒcaregiver‚ôĒ63‚ôĒMy wife Sandra got diagnosed with diabetes last year, and I've been helping her keep track of all her doctor visits and test results.
