CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0191‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒMelissa Martinez‚ôĒiPill inc‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒiPill Inc. argues that while certification burden on vendors is real and problematic, eliminating or weakening the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) transparency requirement for AI-based clinical decision support would create serious patient sa...‚ôĒ**COMMENT TO THE OFFICE OF THE NATIONAL COORDINATOR FOR HEALTH INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY**‚Źé‚Źé**RE: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity; Request for Information**‚Źé‚ŹéiPill Inc. appreciates the opportunity to contribute to this important discussion about health IT certification and regulatory burden. We operate as a mid-sized health IT vendor supporting hospital systems, clinics, and integrated delivery networks across the upper Midwest. Our work puts us in direct contact with both the clinical teams who depend on certified EHR systems and the vendor community navigating certification pathways. What I want to address here is not the case for deregulation broadly, but rather the specific question of what should happen to the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion if certification requirements are revised. I believe the answer requires us to think carefully about what happens when we reduce transparency requirements for AI-assisted clinical decision-making.‚Źé‚ŹéLast month, I attended a meeting with our rural clinic partners in North Dakota. One of them, a 40-bed facility that had been with the same EHR vendor for twelve years, received notice that their vendor could no longer afford to maintain certification under the current DSI requirements. The compliance costs had become prohibitive. The clinic's clinical workflows were sound. Their care quality was good. But the vendor couldn't justify the expense for a facility their size, even though larger health systems using the same vendor had no problem absorbing those costs. Within weeks, this clinic faced a transition to a different system that wouldn't integrate with their laboratory equipment, their radiology PACS, or their existing clinical documentation workflows. The transition will take roughly two years. During that time, their clinical teams will be working around a patchwork of manual workarounds and point-to-point interfaces. One of their nurse practitioners called me in tears because she was looking at the prospect of re-learning clinical workflows that had been optimized over a decade.‚Źé‚ŹéI mention this not to argue against all deregulation. I understand the genuine problem of certification burden, and I understand that it falls disproportionately on smaller vendors and smaller healthcare organizations. But I want to be clear about what I am not willing to trade away in pursuit of lighter-touch regulation: I am not willing to see the DSI criterion gutted in a way that removes transparency requirements for AI-based clinical decision support. And I want to explain why, because I think this is a place where the deregulation impulse could produce real harm if we're not careful about what we actually reduce.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CURRENT DSI CRITERION AND WHAT IT REQUIRES**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current Decision Support Interventions certification criterion, as outlined in 45 CFR 170.315(a)(10), requires vendors to maintain and disclose AI model cards containing specific information about intended use, target population, known risks and limitations, characteristics of training data, and results of external validation testing. This is not a paperwork requirement that serves no purpose. It is a requirement that vendors document and make available to purchasers and, ultimately, to clinicians specific information about what their AI system is designed to do, who it was tested on, what it might get wrong, and whether it has been validated outside the original development environment.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen a clinician in a small rural hospital is about to follow a recommendation from a decision support system, they should have access to information about whether that system was validated in a population similar to their own. They should know whether the training data included sufficient representation of the patient populations they treat. They should understand the known failure modes. This is not a regulatory nicety. This is foundational to informed clinical decision-making.‚Źé‚ŹéThe vendors who can most easily absorb certification costs are not, as a rule, the vendors most incentivized to maintain this kind of transparency if they're not required to. There are economic incentives working in the opposite direction. If a vendor has invested heavily in proprietary AI development and has competitive advantage in a particular niche market segment, there is no natural market pressure to disclose the limitations of that system. Clinicians and hospital administrators often lack the technical expertise to demand this information independently. And in the absence of a regulatory requirement, many vendors will simply not provide it, because doing so creates liability exposure and competitive disadvantage.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THIS MATTERS FOR PATIENT SAFETY**‚Źé‚ŹéI need to be direct: I have seen firsthand what happens when clinical decision support systems operate without adequate transparency about their limitations. About three years ago, one of our larger health system clients implemented a sepsis screening algorithm that had been validated primarily in academic medical centers. The system had strong performance in the development cohort. But when deployed in a community hospital setting with a different patient population, a different case mix, and different baseline laboratory practices, the system generated an unusually high false positive rate. Clinicians began to lose confidence in the alerts. The system, which was supposed to improve sepsis detection, ended up creating alert fatigue that actually made it harder to identify real cases.‚Źé‚ŹéThe health system eventually had to hire a data analyst to work backwards through the algorithm's logic, understand where the mismatch between the development cohort and their actual population was occurring, and then configure local overrides. They did this work because they had the resources to do it. A smaller hospital would not have been able to do this. They would have either continued using a system that was generating noise, or they would have disabled the alerts entirely.‚Źé‚ŹéNone of this would have happened if the vendor had been transparent, from the beginning, about the populations in which the algorithm had been tested and validated. If the health system had known upfront that the sepsis screening system had been developed and validated primarily in academic medical centers with certain laboratory capabilities and patient demographics, they could have made an informed decision about whether to implement the system, or how to configure it, or whether to invest in additional validation work before deployment.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is what the current DSI criterion is designed to prevent. It requires that vendors document these things and make them available. And I am concerned that if we eliminate this requirement in pursuit of lighter-touch certification, we will see more of what I just described, not less.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PRESSURE TO REDUCE BURDEN IS REAL, BUT THE ANSWER IS NOT TO ELIMINATE TRANSPARENCY**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to acknowledge something important: the burden of maintaining certification is real, and it does fall more heavily on smaller vendors and smaller providers. The clinic I mentioned at the beginning of this comment was not destroyed by bad regulation. It was destroyed by economic dynamics that made it impossible for their long-time vendor to continue supporting them. That is a problem. But the solution is not to make the DSI criterion weaker. The solution is to think carefully about how to structure that criterion in a way that is proportionate to the size and resources of the vendor, while still maintaining the transparency that clinicians and patients need.‚Źé‚ŹéThere are ways to do this. You could create a tiered approach to DSI certification, where vendors of different sizes have different documentation requirements proportionate to their resources. You could allow vendors to meet the transparency requirement through simplified model cards for smaller systems. You could create a safe harbor for vendors who are using well-established, off-the-shelf AI models that have already been validated in peer-reviewed literature. You could streamline the external validation requirement by allowing vendors to rely on published validation studies rather than requiring original testing for every use case.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat you should not do is eliminate the transparency requirement entirely, or make it optional, or reduce it to the point where vendors have little incentive to document and disclose what their systems actually do.‚Źé‚Źé**THE INTERNATIONAL PRECEDENT AND WHERE EUROPE IS HEADING**‚Źé‚ŹéI mention this because the regulatory conversation in Europe is moving in exactly the opposite direction from what I'm hearing in some of the deregulation proposals. The European Union's proposed AI Act, which is likely to become law in the next year or two, includes requirements for algorithmic transparency and documentation that are significantly more stringent than what we currently have in the U.S. health IT certification framework. The EU is not moving toward less transparency. They are moving toward more.‚Źé‚ŹéI think that should matter to us. Not because we should slavishly follow European regulation, but because the European regulatory bodies have spent considerable time thinking about the risks of AI systems in high-stakes domains like healthcare, and they have concluded that transparency requirements are necessary to manage those risks. If we move in the opposite direction, we will end up with a situation where European health systems have stricter requirements for AI transparency in clinical decision support than American health systems do. That is a peculiar outcome for a country that likes to think of itself as the leader in health IT innovation.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC RECOMMENDATIONS FOR THE DSI CRITERION**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the Office of the National Coordinator is going to consider revisions to the DSI criterion, I would urge you to consider the following approach:‚Źé‚Źé1. Retain the core requirement that vendors document and make available information about intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. Do not eliminate this requirement. Do not make it optional.‚Źé‚Źé2. Create a tiered certification pathway where smaller vendors with limited resources can meet the transparency requirement through simplified model card documentation, as long as the documentation provided is accurate and sufficient for a clinician to understand the basic intended use and known limitations of the system.‚Źé‚Źé3. For vendors using well-established, off-the-shelf AI models that have been extensively validated in published literature, allow them to meet the external validation requirement by referencing published studies rather than conducting original validation testing.‚Źé‚Źé4. Clarify that the external validation requirement can be met through retrospective analysis of performance in real-world settings, not just prospective randomized studies. This will reduce the burden on vendors while still ensuring that systems are actually tested in conditions similar to where they will be deployed.‚Źé‚Źé5. Allow vendors to update their model cards as they learn more about system performance in real-world settings, rather than requiring a complete re-certification process every time new validation data becomes available.‚Źé‚ŹéThese changes would reduce the compliance burden on vendors without eliminating the transparency that clinicians and patients need. They would make it economically feasible for smaller vendors to maintain certification, while still ensuring that the clinical teams using these systems have access to information about what they are actually deploying.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY SMALLER VENDORS SHOULD SUPPORT THIS APPROACH**‚Źé‚ŹéI recognize that some smaller vendors might argue for complete elimination of the DSI transparency requirement, on the grounds that any requirement is a burden. I would respectfully push back on this. If the DSI requirement disappears entirely, what we will see is not a level playing field where all vendors can compete freely. What we will see is a market advantage for large vendors with the resources to develop sophisticated AI systems and keep their inner workings proprietary. Small vendors will have a harder time, not an easier time, competing in that environment.‚Źé‚ŹéConversely, if transparency is required, smaller vendors can compete on the basis of having systems that are simpler, more interpretable, and easier to understand. A small vendor with a straightforward decision support system that is easy to explain and validate has a competitive advantage over a large vendor with a black-box AI system that nobody really understands. Transparency requirements actually level the playing field. They prevent large vendors from using complexity and opacity as a barrier to competition.‚Źé‚Źé**THE REAL BURDEN IS NOT DOCUMENTATION; IT IS VALIDATION**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be precise about where the actual burden lies. The burden is not primarily in documenting what your system does. The burden is in validating that your system actually works the way you say it does, in populations you may not have access to, using data infrastructure you may not have built.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the goal is to reduce burden on vendors, the right place to focus is on simplifying and streamlining the validation requirement, not on eliminating transparency. You could allow vendors to meet the validation requirement through collaborative arrangements with health systems that have data. You could create a shared validation infrastructure where multiple vendors could contribute to validation studies. You could allow vendors to use synthetic data or de-identified data from large datasets to conduct validation testing. You could reduce the statistical rigor requirements for smaller systems serving smaller populations.‚Źé‚ŹéBut do not eliminate the requirement that vendors be transparent about what their systems are and what they have been tested on. That is not a burden that serves no purpose. That is a requirement that serves a fundamental purpose in a healthcare system where clinicians need to make informed decisions about whether to trust algorithmic recommendations.‚Źé‚Źé**THE COST OF GETTING THIS WRONG**‚Źé‚ŹéI keep coming back to the clinic I mentioned at the beginning of this comment. They lost their EHR vendor not because the vendor wanted to abandon them, but because the vendor could not afford to maintain certification. That is a real problem that deserves a real solution.‚Źé‚ŹéBut the solution is not to eliminate the DSI transparency requirement. If we eliminate that requirement, we will create a different problem. We will create a situation where vendors have no incentive to be transparent about the limitations and risks of their AI systems. Where clinicians have no reliable way to understand what they are deploying. Where patients have no assurance that the algorithmic recommendations their doctors are following have been validated in populations similar to their own.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat will happen then is that some vendors will produce excellent, well-validated, carefully transparent systems. And some vendors will produce systems that are less well-validated, that have unknown failure modes, that have not been tested in the populations where they will actually be used. And clinicians and hospital administrators will have no way to tell the difference. The market will not sort this out, because clinicians and administrators lack the technical expertise to evaluate AI systems on their own. Patients will not sort this out, because they do not have access to the information they would need to make informed decisions about their care.‚Źé‚ŹéThe result will be a healthcare system where AI-assisted clinical decision-making is less safe than it is today, not more safe. And that is a real cost that I think we need to be honest about as we consider deregulation.‚Źé‚Źé**A MIDDLE PATH IS POSSIBLE**‚Źé‚ŹéI do not think the choice is between current certification requirements and no certification requirements at all. I think there is a middle path. You can reduce the burden on vendors while maintaining the transparency that clinicians and patients need. You can create a tiered certification system that is proportionate to the size and resources of the vendor. You can streamline the validation requirement while maintaining the transparency requirement. You can simplify the documentation that vendors need to provide while ensuring that the documentation that is provided is accurate and sufficient for informed decision-making.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat you should not do is eliminate transparency. That is not a burden that serves no purpose. That is a requirement that serves a fundamental purpose in a healthcare system where AI systems are increasingly making recommendations that influence clinical decisions and patient outcomes.‚Źé‚ŹéI understand the desire to move quickly and to reduce regulatory burden. I understand that there are genuine problems with the current certification framework. But I would urge the Office of the National Coordinator to think carefully about what happens if we move too far in the direction of deregulation. The clinic in North Dakota that lost their EHR vendor deserves a solution. But that solution should not be to make healthcare AI systems less transparent and less accountable. That solution should be to make the transparency and accountability requirements proportionate to the size and resources of the vendors who have to meet them.‚Źé‚ŹéThank you for considering these comments.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-15T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-15T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ8‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒconcerned‚ôĒNorth Dakota‚ôĒhospital CFO‚ôĒ53‚ôĒLast month, I watched our rural clinic lose its EHR vendor of twelve years because the certification costs had become prohibitive for them‚ÄĒthey couldn't justify the expense for a 40-bed facility, even though their clinical workflows were exemplary. One of our nurse practitioners called me in tears because they were facing a two-year transition to a system that wouldn't integrate with their lab equipment, all because smaller vendors couldn't absorb the compliance burden that the big players treat as a rounding error.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2689
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0192‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDaniel Lopez‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA parent of a child with diabetes opposes the proposed removal of certification and transparency requirements for clinical decision support algorithms, arguing that eliminating mandates for model cards, validation documentation, and disclosure of ...‚ôĒI need to start by saying what's been on my mind since I read through the proposed changes. Three years ago, when my daughter was diagnosed with juvenile diabetes, I learned something that most people don't think about until it happens to them: your medical data can be fragmented across systems in ways that make it nearly impossible to actually use it to keep your child safe. I spent weeks fighting with her pediatrician's office, the endocrinologist, and the hospital system just to get her glucose records in a format I could work with. Three different portals. Three different logins. Records that should have been simple to share were locked in silos.‚Źé‚ŹéThe irony is that back then, I was desperate for better interoperability. I would have done anything to have her data move seamlessly between providers. Now that things are supposedly getting easier, I'm facing a different kind of fear. I'm terrified that making data easier to move around without proper safeguards means someone will scrape her medical history and sell it before she's even old enough to understand what that means. That's not an abstract worry for me. That's my daughter's future.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PROBLEM WITH LOOSENING DECISION SUPPORT SAFEGUARDS**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to focus on one specific part of the proposed deregulatory changes that concerns me deeply: the removal of certification requirements for Decision Support Interventions (DSI). The current rules require AI model cards and transparency disclosures, things like intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation. On the surface, removing these requirements sounds like it's just cutting red tape. But I've watched enough healthcare bureaucracy to know that when you remove transparency requirements, it's rarely because transparency was working too well.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what I'm thinking about. My daughter's endocrinologist uses clinical decision support software to help manage her insulin dosing. It's not the doctor making the decision alone; it's the doctor plus an algorithm. The algorithm was trained on some set of data, with some set of assumptions about how children's bodies work. But I have no idea what that data was. I don't know if it was trained mostly on kids from wealthy backgrounds. I don't know if it was validated on populations that look like mine. I don't know what the algorithm's known limitations are.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule, as I understand it, would let developers skip the certification process that requires them to document these things. That sounds to me like it's moving us backward, not forward. It's moving us from a world where at least there's a paper trail, a set of documented assumptions, toward a world where developers can say "our algorithm works great" without having to show their work.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY TRANSPARENCY MATTERS IN CLINICAL CARE**‚Źé‚ŹéThe FDA already allows certain types of AI in medicine (see 21 CFR 868.1200 for clinical decision support devices), but those pathways still require some level of evidence that the tool actually does what it claims. The proposed rule seems to skip that step. It says that innovation is being held back by the burden of certification. Maybe that's true for some developers. But the burden of a wrong dosing algorithm, or an algorithm that systematically misses certain populations, is not borne by the developer. It's borne by patients.‚Źé‚ŹéI've read through some of the background materials on this, and I keep coming back to one thing: there's an assumption that removing requirements will lead to more competition and lower costs. In theory, that makes sense. In practice, I'm not convinced. When I was fighting to get my daughter's records in a usable format, the barriers weren't because the doctors and hospitals were being innovatively careful. They were siloed because the systems didn't talk to each other, and nobody had an incentive to make them talk. Adding a new, less regulated algorithm to that environment doesn't seem like it fixes the problem.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat I want to see is the opposite direction. Keep the DSI certification requirement. In fact, strengthen it. Here's what I think should be non-negotiable:‚Źé‚Źé- Model cards that document the training data, including demographic breakdowns of who was included and who was excluded‚Źé- Clear disclosure of intended use and target population, not just in regulatory filings but in the actual software interface where doctors see it‚Źé- Documentation of known limitations and failure modes, especially for populations that were underrepresented in training data‚Źé- External validation results from independent testing, not just internal vendor testing‚Źé- A requirement that any algorithm used in pediatric settings be validated specifically on pediatric populations, not just adapted from adult models‚Źé‚ŹéThese aren't burdensome requirements. They're baseline transparency. They're the equivalent of requiring a pharmaceutical company to tell you what's in the drug and what it's been tested on. That's not innovation-killing. That's responsibility.‚Źé‚Źé**THE REAL COST OF DEREGULATION**‚Źé‚ŹéI understand the argument that regulation increases costs and slows down innovation. I'm sympathetic to the idea that some rules are outdated or overly complex. But I want someone to explain to me how removing transparency requirements from clinical decision support algorithms reduces costs for patients. It doesn't. It reduces costs for developers. It shifts the risk from them to us.‚Źé‚ŹéThe 21st Century Cures Act (42 U.S.C. 300jj et seq.) was supposed to make health data more accessible and portable. That's good. But it also included information blocking rules meant to protect patient privacy and safety while data moves around. The current proposal seems to be loosening both the transparency around what algorithms are doing and the safeguards around what can be done with the data once it's accessible.‚Źé‚ŹéThat's a dangerous combination. My daughter's data is more accessible now than it was three years ago, which is progress. But if that accessible data is being fed into algorithms that nobody has to document or validate, and if there's no transparency about what those algorithms are doing, then accessibility becomes a liability instead of a benefit.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not asking for perfection. I know that regulation always has tradeoffs. But the tradeoff here feels backward. We're trading transparency and patient safety for developer convenience. That's not a tradeoff I'm willing to make on behalf of my daughter.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT NEEDS TO STAY**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be clear: I'm not asking for the rule to be abandoned. I'm asking for the DSI certification requirement to remain in place. Keep the model cards. Keep the transparency disclosures. Keep the requirement for external validation. These aren't obstacles to innovation. They're the baseline for responsible innovation.‚Źé‚ŹéThe data portability improvements are good. The interoperability improvements are good. But they only work if the tools being used to interpret and act on that data are transparent and validated. Without that, we're just making it easier for bad algorithms to spread.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-24T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-24T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ3‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒangry‚ôĒMassachusetts‚ôĒstay-at-home parent‚ôĒ50‚ôĒMy daughter was diagnosed with juvenile diabetes three years ago, and I spent months fighting with her pediatrician's office, the endocrinologist, and the hospital system to get her complete glucose records in a format I could actually use‚ÄĒrecords I needed to manage her care properly. When I finally got them, they were a fragmented mess across three different portals with three different logins. Now that things are supposedly easier to move around, I'm terrified some data broker will scrape her medical history and sell it to insurance companies before she's old enough to understand what that means.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1129
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0193‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒRobert Perez‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe commenter opposes proposed deregulation of health IT systems, arguing that clearer and more patient-centered rules‚ÄĒnot looser ones‚ÄĒare needed to ensure transparent AI use in clinical decision-making and reliable access to medical records, as d...‚ôĒ**Comment on HHS ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions**‚Źé‚ŹéMy wife needed to switch cardiologists last year when our insurance coverage changed. Getting her echocardiogram results transferred took three weeks. The old clinic said they were having "technical difficulties," her new appointment kept sliding back, and meanwhile nobody could tell us where her data actually was or who needed to sign off on moving it. I spent those weeks thinking about what would've happened if something had gone wrong during that gap, and I realized how fragile this whole system is when the rules aren't clear about what data belongs to whom and who gets to access it.‚Źé‚ŹéNow I'm seeing this proposed deregulation, and I understand the impulse to reduce burden on developers and providers. Nobody wants unnecessary red tape. But I keep coming back to something that happened during those three weeks: I asked the new cardiologist's office if they could at least see the old echocardiogram results while we waited for the official transfer. They said no, because there wasn't a clear pathway for me, as the patient, to share my own data with them directly. The rules weren't working, but the answer isn't to make the rules looser. It's to make them clearer and more patient-centered.‚Źé‚Źé**THE TRANSPARENCY PROBLEM WITH AI SYSTEMS**‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what worries me more than administrative burden: the comment mentions removing certification requirements for AI systems used in clinical decision-making. I've read enough to know that some hospitals are already using AI tools to help cardiologists interpret imaging and make recommendations. If my wife had been relying on one of those systems and we didn't know how it was trained, what populations it was tested on, or whether it was any good at reading scans in women her age, how would she have made an informed decision about her care?‚Źé‚ŹéThe FDA's 2021 guidance on AI and machine learning in medical devices specifically emphasizes transparency in model development for exactly this reason. Model cards, documentation that explains how a system was built and validated, aren't bureaucratic padding. They're how patients and their doctors actually understand what they're relying on. Removing that requirement doesn't reduce burden on developers. It shifts the risk onto patients who can't make informed decisions without knowing what data trained the system or what it was tested on.‚Źé‚Źé**CLARIFYING ACCESS AND BLOCKING DEFINITIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéOn the information blocking side, I'd ask for something specific: clearer definitions of what "access" means when patients are trying to get their own records. The ONC Health IT Certification Program's 2023 annual report noted real gaps in how different vendors implement patient access rules. Some interpret "access" very narrowly, some broadly. That's why my wife got stuck in limbo.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not saying developers should have no rules. I'm saying the rules need to work for patients first, and for developers second. Removing certification criteria for AI transparency and weakening information blocking safeguards without strengthening them elsewhere will make problems like ours worse, not better. We don't need less oversight. We need oversight that actually protects the people whose lives depend on getting this right.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-03T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-03T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ1‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒsupportive‚ôĒKentucky‚ôĒretired teacher‚ôĒ58‚ôĒLast year when my wife needed to switch cardiologists after our insurance changed, we spent three weeks just trying to get her echocardiogram results transferred‚ÄĒthe new doctor's office said the old clinic was "having technical difficulties," and meanwhile her appointment kept getting pushed back. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0194‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒStephanie Sanchez‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒRemoving AI transparency requirements risks worsening healthcare coordination problems by preventing patients and providers from understanding whether AI systems were tested on relevant populations or have known limitations that could affect care ...‚ôĒMy mom got diagnosed with diabetes last year, and I spent an entire afternoon on hold with her endocrinologist's office just trying to get copies of her lab results to share with her primary care doctor.
