CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0195‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒSandra Nguyen‚ôĒHealthcare Leadership Council‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Healthcare Leadership Council opposes removing certification requirements for clinical AI decision support systems, arguing that transparency disclosures, model cards, and external validation are essential for hospitals to understand system pe...‚ôĒRE: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity (RIN 0945-ZA29)‚Źé‚ŹéThe Healthcare Leadership Council represents 28 member organizations spanning hospitals, health systems, physician groups, health plans, and patient advocacy organizations across the United States. Our members collectively serve more than 150 million patients annually and employ approximately 1.2 million healthcare workers. We appreciate the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory framework, particularly as it relates to artificial intelligence decision support systems in clinical settings.‚Źé‚ŹéWe come to this rulemaking with genuine uncertainty about the right balance. Our members support innovation and understand the regulatory burden that certification requirements impose on developers. But we've also seen what happens when transparency gets stripped away, and I need to be direct about what concerns us. Last year, one of our member organizations discovered that a patient's genetic data had been shared with a third-party AI researcher without meaningful consent. The patient found out through a data breach notice months later. The hospital system didn't even know it had happened until the breach was disclosed. When we talked to the administrators involved, they weren't trying to be reckless. They simply couldn't see what their systems were doing with sensitive information because no one had required anyone to document it. That's the blind spot we're worried about here.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CURRENT CERTIFICATION FRAMEWORK AND DECISION SUPPORT INTERVENTIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe existing certification criteria for health IT systems, established under 45 CFR 170.315 and refined through successive iterations of the ONC Health IT Certification Program, require developers to demonstrate that AI-driven clinical decision support systems include model cards, transparency disclosures, documentation of intended use, target population specifications, known risks, and training data characteristics. These requirements also mandate external validation before deployment. We understand that critics view this as unnecessarily burdensome. The proposed rule would remove or substantially weaken these requirements for Decision Support Interventions, defined in 45 CFR 170.102 as technologies that analyze patient data to provide recommendations to clinicians.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what we're asking the agency to consider: these certification criteria exist because clinical AI is not the same as other software. When a clinician makes a treatment decision based on a recommendation from an AI system, that decision affects a person's body. The stakes are different. Over the past three years, our member hospitals have implemented approximately 87 different AI decision support tools across their networks, ranging from sepsis prediction algorithms to imaging analysis systems. When we surveyed our hospital members about what they needed most from AI developers, the answer wasn't "less regulation." It was "I need to know what this system was trained on, who it was tested with, and what it fails at."‚Źé‚Źé**WHY MODEL CARDS AND TRANSPARENCY DISCLOSURES MATTER IN PRACTICE**‚Źé‚ŹéThe requirement that developers provide AI model cards and transparency disclosures, as currently mandated under the certification criteria, serves three distinct functions that operate at the clinical level. First, these documents allow hospital clinical informatics teams to understand whether a system is appropriate for their patient population. We have a member health system in Maryland with a largely Medicaid-insured population, 62 percent of whom are African American. When that system evaluated an AI sepsis prediction algorithm, the developer's model card revealed that the training dataset was 87 percent white and drawn primarily from academic medical centers. The clinical team could then make an informed decision about whether to implement that system, what populations to prioritize, and what additional monitoring to put in place. Without that documentation, they would have deployed it system-wide. That's not theoretical risk. That's what actually happens.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, transparency disclosures allow clinicians to understand the limitations of the recommendations they receive in real time. If a clinician knows that an AI system was validated only on ICU patients over 65, they can weight that system's recommendation differently when it flags a 35-year-old patient in the emergency department. The Federal Register notice from January 2023 (88 FR 2853) acknowledged this explicitly, noting that clinical decision support systems pose distinct safety risks precisely because their recommendations can bypass or override clinical judgment if the clinician doesn't understand the system's constraints. A clinician who sees a high-confidence recommendation from a black box system may be more likely to follow it than a clinician who knows that system was tested on a different population with different outcomes.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, and this is where accountability comes in, these disclosures create a paper trail. When something goes wrong, when a patient is harmed or when an AI system produces biased recommendations, hospitals need to be able to trace back to what the developer said about the system's performance. In the case I mentioned earlier with the genetic data, part of the problem was that the hospital couldn't even figure out which system had shared the data because no one had documented the system's data-sharing capabilities. Model cards wouldn't have prevented that breach, but they would have made it visible much earlier.‚Źé‚Źé**INFORMATION BLOCKING AND THE LIMITS OF MARKET-DRIVEN TRANSPARENCY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule invokes the deregulatory logic that market competition will incentivize developers to provide transparency voluntarily. The argument goes like this: if hospitals can easily compare AI systems based on their capabilities and limitations, developers who refuse to disclose information will lose market share to developers who do. This assumes that hospitals have the information infrastructure and technical expertise to make those comparisons, and that they have meaningful choice among vendors. Neither assumption holds in practice.‚Źé‚ŹéWe represent 28 member organizations, which collectively contract with hundreds of health IT vendors. What we see in the market is significant consolidation. Three vendors control approximately 55 percent of the hospital EHR market. When a hospital system has already invested millions in infrastructure from a particular vendor, switching to a different vendor because of superior AI transparency is not a realistic option. The transaction costs are enormous. The switching costs are enormous. One of our member health systems spent four years and approximately $47 million implementing an EHR from a major vendor. When that vendor's AI decision support system turned out to have significant limitations for their patient population, they couldn't simply switch to a competitor. They had to work with what they had.‚Źé‚ŹéAdditionally, the assumption that hospitals can easily compare systems relies on developers providing comparable information. Without standards or certification requirements, developers will provide whatever information they think makes their system look good. One developer might highlight external validation results. Another might emphasize the size of its training dataset without mentioning the demographic composition. A third might not disclose known limitations at all. The information asymmetry remains, and hospitals that lack the technical expertise to reverse-engineer an AI system's performance characteristics are left guessing.‚Źé‚ŹéThe information blocking regulations, codified at 42 CFR 171.101 et seq., were designed precisely to address this kind of market failure. The regulations recognize that in healthcare, information asymmetries can cause harm even if no individual actor is behaving maliciously. A developer that declines to disclose training data characteristics isn't necessarily trying to hide a flawed system. They might just see disclosure as a competitive disadvantage. But when multiplied across dozens of vendors and hundreds of hospitals, that aggregated lack of transparency creates a system where no one can see what's actually happening with clinical decision-making.‚Źé‚Źé**THE EQUITY PROBLEM: WHO BEARS THE RISK OF UNVALIDATED SYSTEMS**‚Źé‚ŹéThis is where we need to be honest about distribution of harm. When transparency requirements are removed, the patients most likely to be affected by unvalidated or poorly validated AI systems are the ones least able to advocate for themselves. The literature on algorithmic bias in healthcare is now substantial. Obermeyer et al. (2019) in Science documented how a widely used risk-prediction algorithm systematized racial bias by using healthcare spending as a proxy for health needs, resulting in black patients receiving systematically lower-risk scores. That algorithm was in clinical use in hundreds of hospitals before the bias was discovered. How was it discovered? Because researchers were able to analyze the algorithm's training data and performance across racial groups. If the developer had not disclosed training data characteristics, the bias might never have been identified.‚Źé‚ŹéWe have patient advocates on our board, and they keep coming back to the same point: patients can't consent to something they don't know is happening. If a hospital deploys an AI system without understanding its performance characteristics across different populations, and if that system systematically produces different recommendations for different demographic groups, patients in those groups have no way to know they're being treated differently or to object to it. They don't get to opt out of the algorithm. They just get the recommendation, and many will follow it because they trust their doctor.‚Źé‚ŹéThis isn't an abstract concern. Our member organizations have documented cases where AI decision support systems recommended more intensive treatment for white patients than for black patients with identical clinical presentations. These weren't cases of overt discrimination. These were cases where the system was trained on historical data that reflected existing healthcare disparities, and the system learned to replicate those disparities. The only way to catch these problems is to require developers to document training data characteristics and require hospitals to validate performance across populations. That validation work is expensive and time-consuming. But removing the requirement doesn't make the problem go away. It just makes it invisible.‚Źé‚Źé**BALANCING INNOVATION WITH ACCOUNTABILITY**‚Źé‚ŹéWe genuinely do not think the certification criteria in their current form are perfect. We think there are legitimate ways to streamline them. We think developers should have clear guidance about what model cards need to include so they're not guessing about compliance. We think the timeframes for certification could be more reasonable. But removing the requirement for transparency disclosures and external validation is not the same as streamlining. It's a different choice entirely.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Federal Register notice from March 2022 (87 FR 13746) that established the current certification framework for clinical decision support systems recognized this distinction. The notice stated that certification criteria for clinical AI should be more stringent than criteria for other health IT systems because of the direct impact on clinical decision-making. The agency noted that clinical AI systems differ from traditional decision support tools in that they operate through machine learning models that are not fully interpretable and that their behavior can change as they are updated. That reasoning still applies.‚Źé‚ŹéWe're not asking the agency to prevent innovation. We're asking it to require innovation to be transparent. There's a difference. A developer that has built a robust AI system with good external validation should be proud to say so. A system that performs well across different demographic groups should be publicized as a strength. If removing certification requirements actually does accelerate innovation, then that innovation should be able to withstand transparency requirements. The two aren't mutually exclusive.‚Źé‚Źé**RECOMMENDATION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Healthcare Leadership Council respectfully urges the Office of the Assistant Secretary for Technology Policy and the Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology to retain the current certification criteria requiring Decision Support Interventions to include model cards, transparency disclosures, documentation of intended use, target population specifications, known risks, and training data characteristics. We further urge the agencies to require external validation of AI systems before they are deployed in clinical settings, with particular attention to validating performance across different demographic groups and patient populations.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the agencies are inclined to modify the criteria, we would support a phased approach where developers have a reasonable timeline to comply with enhanced transparency requirements, perhaps with different standards for systems that are intended for advisory purposes versus systems that are integrated into clinical workflows. We would support clearer guidance about what model cards should include. We would support industry working groups to develop standardized formats for transparency disclosures. But we would not support removing these requirements entirely.‚Źé‚ŹéThe stakes in this decision are significant. They're not just about regulatory burden on developers. They're about whether hospitals can actually understand what's happening in their systems, whether clinicians can make informed judgments about AI recommendations, and whether patients in vulnerable populations have any visibility into decisions that affect their care. We've seen what happens when those things aren't transparent. We've learned from it. We're asking the agency to learn from it too.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-22T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-22T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ6‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒconcerned‚ôĒMaryland‚ôĒexecutive director‚ôĒ43‚ôĒLast year, I watched one of our member organizations struggle when a patient's genetic data was inadvertently shared with a third-party researcher without proper consent mechanisms‚ÄĒthe patient only discovered it months later through a data breach notice. That experience crystallized something I'd been sensing in my conversations with hospital administrators and patient advocates: we've built systems so eager to unlock data's potential that we've created blind spots where the most vulnerable populations can't effectively monitor what's happening with their most sensitive information.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2042
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0196‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒRichard Flores‚ôĒCareAtlas Inc.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒCareAtlas argues that the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards should be retained because it ensures clinicians have necessary information about decision support tool limitations and paradoxically h...‚ôĒCareAtlas Inc. submits this comment in response to the proposed modifications to the Health IT Certification Criteria, with specific focus on the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements and their role in ensuring patient safety and clinical accountability.‚Źé‚Źé**ORGANIZATIONAL CONTEXT AND EXPERIENCE**‚Źé‚ŹéCareAtlas Inc. operates as a health IT compliance and analytics firm serving hospital networks, primary care organizations, and health plans across the Southeast. Our work centers on helping healthcare organizations navigate the complex intersection of certification requirements, interoperability standards, and clinical governance. Over the past year, I have personally managed the EHR migration process for a major regional health system, working directly with their clinical leadership, IT teams, and compliance officers. That eight-month process exposed a critical gap: while our certification requirements hadn't substantively changed since the 2015 edition of the ONC Health IT Certification Criteria, the actual problems clinicians faced with decision support tools had evolved dramatically. We now see AI-assisted clinical decision support integrated into workflows in ways that 2015 requirements simply didn't contemplate.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat struck me most during that migration was the disparity in compliance capacity across vendors. Our organization could absorb the cost of building robust certification infrastructure. A smaller competitor, a South Carolina-based vendor with a genuinely better clinical interface for diabetes management, couldn't afford the compliance overhead. They lost contracts not because their product was inferior but because they lacked the resources to document and maintain certification. That outcome bothered me then and still does.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CURRENT DSI CRITERION SERVES A NECESSARY FUNCTION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current Decision Support Interventions certification criterion, codified in 45 CFR 170.315(a)(1), requires vendors to disclose AI model cards containing intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and evidence of external validation. I understand the impulse to reduce certification burden. Compliance is expensive. But I want to be direct: removing or substantially weakening this requirement would be a mistake, and it would be a mistake specifically because it would harm the smaller vendors we're supposedly trying to help.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's why. A hospital's clinical governance committee needs to understand what a decision support tool is actually doing. When an AI system recommends treatment escalation for a sepsis patient, clinicians need to know whether that model was trained on data from teaching hospitals with intensive care expertise, or whether it was trained on a broader population that includes smaller rural facilities. They need to know the known failure modes. They need to know the target population the model was validated against. This isn't regulatory theater. This is basic informed consent translated into the clinical decision-making context.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current model card requirement isn't burdensome in the way a poorly designed certification criterion can be. It asks vendors to document what they should already know about their own products. In our experience helping vendors prepare certification documentation, the organizations that struggled most weren't struggling with the requirement itself. They were struggling because they had never clearly articulated these things internally. Once they did, the documentation followed naturally.‚Źé‚Źé**REMOVAL WOULD DISPROPORTIONATELY HARM SMALLER VENDORS**‚Źé‚ŹéThis is the counterintuitive part, and I think it matters. If the DSI criterion is removed, you don't create a level playing field. You create an information asymmetry that favors large vendors with established relationships to hospital systems. Here's the mechanism: without certification requirements, hospital compliance officers will still need assurance that decision support tools meet some standard. They won't accept lower assurance just because certification is optional. Instead, they'll rely on vendor relationships, brand recognition, and prior contracting history. A large vendor like Cerner or Epic can leverage their existing presence in a hospital system. A smaller vendor has to prove something. Certification criteria, paradoxically, are the tool smaller vendors use to prove it.‚Źé‚ŹéI saw this play out directly. The South Carolina vendor I mentioned earlier actually had better documentation practices than some larger competitors. But without the requirement to submit that documentation as part of a certification package, they had no formal mechanism to demonstrate it to hospital procurement teams. Removing the DSI criterion would have made their position worse, not better.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC RECOMMENDATION: RETAIN THE CURRENT DSI REQUIREMENT WITH MINOR CLARIFICATION**‚Źé‚ŹéCareAtlas recommends that the DSI certification criterion in 45 CFR 170.315(a)(1) be retained in its current form. The model card requirement should remain a mandatory element of certification for any health IT developer integrating clinical decision support, particularly decision support that uses machine learning or algorithmic approaches.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the agency is concerned about compliance burden, there are better approaches than elimination:‚Źé‚Źé- Clarify that model cards may reference external documentation rather than embedding all information in a single submission document. This reduces formatting burden without reducing transparency.‚Źé- Allow vendors to use standardized templates such as the Model Card framework published by Mitchell et al. in 2019, providing a structured approach that vendors can implement without guessing at format.‚Źé- Create a tiered approach where straightforward rule-based decision support (e.g., drug interaction alerts) is exempt, while machine learning-based interventions require the full model card. This targets burden reduction where it's actually justified.‚Źé- Establish a three-year grace period for vendors currently in the market to bring existing products into compliance, rather than grandfathering noncompliant tools indefinitely.‚Źé‚Źé**THE BROADER ECOSYSTEM ARGUMENT**‚Źé‚ŹéStepping back, I think there's a framing issue in the deregulatory conversation. The question isn't whether certification criteria themselves reduce burden. They do. The question is whether removing them actually produces better outcomes for patients, clinicians, and the market.‚Źé‚ŹéOn patient safety, the answer is clearly no. A clinician using a decision support tool without knowing its limitations is worse off than a clinician using the same tool with clear documentation of those limitations. This isn't about compliance for its own sake. It's about informed decision-making in clinical practice.‚Źé‚ŹéOn market competition, the evidence is more mixed than the deregulation framing suggests. Smaller vendors often use certification as a credibility signal precisely because they lack brand recognition. Removing the signal doesn't help them compete. It removes their tool for proving quality.‚Źé‚ŹéOn innovation, I remain unconvinced that the DSI criterion inhibits it. The vendors I work with aren't avoiding innovation because of model card requirements. They're avoiding certain types of innovation because the clinical evidence isn't there yet, or because the liability exposure is unclear. Those are legitimate constraints that certification criteria don't create.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criterion should be retained. It protects patients by ensuring clinicians have the information they need to use decision support appropriately. It protects smaller vendors by providing a transparent mechanism to demonstrate product quality. And it protects the integrity of the health IT market by ensuring that competition occurs on the basis of actual product merit rather than vendor relationships and market position.‚Źé‚ŹéCareAtlas has no financial stake in whether certification requirements are strengthened or weakened. We adapt to whatever regulatory framework exists. But I believe the current DSI requirement is one that should survive this review, even as the agency looks for genuine burden reduction opportunities elsewhere in the certification program.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-16T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-16T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ3‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒresigned‚ôĒNorth Carolina‚ôĒcompliance officer‚ôĒ55‚ôĒWhen we migrated our EHR system last year, I spent eight months navigating certification requirements that hadn't changed since 2015‚ÄĒrequirements that didn't address half the interoperability problems our hospital partners actually faced. Our smaller competitor down in South Carolina simply couldn't afford the compliance infrastructure we'd built, and I watched them lose contracts because of it, not because their product was worse.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1166
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0197‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒRobert Flores‚ôĒThe Pharmacy Health Information Technology Collaborative‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Pharmacy Health Information Technology Collaborative opposes removing Decision Support Interventions certification requirements, arguing that eliminating mandates for AI model cards and transparency disclosures will increase interoperability f...‚ôĒ**The Pharmacy Health Information Technology Collaborative Comments on Proposed Deregulation of Decision Support Interventions Certification Requirements**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Pharmacy Health Information Technology Collaborative (PHITC) appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory actions under the Advancing Secure Technology and Practices (ASTP) initiative. Our organization represents pharmacy networks, health systems, and technology vendors across the western United States who have direct experience implementing electronic health record (EHR) systems and clinical decision support tools. We write to express serious concern about the proposal to remove Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria, specifically the requirements for AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation). Retaining these requirements is essential for patient safety, clinical accountability, and informed decision-making in healthcare settings.‚Źé‚Źé**THE REAL COSTS OF UNCHECKED INTEROPERABILITY FRAGMENTATION**‚Źé‚ŹéOur concern about removing baseline standards is not theoretical. Last year, a regional pharmacy network in Nevada spent eighteen months and substantial resources building an EHR integration system only to discover that their carefully designed data infrastructure could not interoperate with three major hospital systems in the state. The reason was straightforward: there were no baseline standards requiring compatibility. Each hospital system had designed its clinical decision support tools under different regulatory interpretations, creating incompatible data schemas and validation approaches. When the pharmacy network finally mapped what standards should have been mandated from the start, the picture became clear. The fragmentation had not driven innovation. It had created expensive workarounds that patients, providers, and payers all ended up paying for through redundant testing, delayed medication reconciliation, and missed clinical insights.‚Źé‚ŹéThat pharmacy network is now one of our members. Their experience illustrates why removing certification criteria for decision support interventions would likely repeat this pattern at a larger scale. When vendors face no requirement to document their AI models' intended use, target populations, known limitations, or validation methods, the result is not competition on quality or safety. The result is a market where some providers can deploy sophisticated decision support tools while others cannot, not because of clinical efficacy but because of undocumented incompatibilities. Small and mid-sized healthcare organizations, particularly in rural areas and underserved communities, will face higher integration costs and longer implementation timelines. The innovation we actually need is standardized transparency, not its removal.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY DECISION SUPPORT INTERVENTIONS REQUIRE CONTINUED CERTIFICATION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe FDA's existing framework for clinical decision support, outlined in the 2019 guidance document "Clinical Decision Support Software" (FDA, 2019), distinguishes between low-risk informational tools and higher-risk recommendations that directly influence patient care. The certification criterion for DSI in the Health IT Certification Program (45 CFR Part 170, Subpart B) was designed to apply to the latter category. These tools make recommendations about medication dosing, drug interactions, contraindications, and clinical pathways. They influence clinical judgment at the point of care.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current requirement that certified EHR technology include model cards and transparency disclosures serves three distinct functions:‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ It ensures that clinicians understand what population a tool was designed for and whether it applies to their patient. A model trained primarily on data from adult populations may perform poorly in pediatric patients. A model optimized for one demographic group may have higher error rates in others. Without documented intended use and target population, clinicians cannot make informed decisions about whether to trust a recommendation.‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ It creates accountability when adverse events occur. If a patient experiences harm and a decision support tool was involved, healthcare providers and legal teams need to understand what the tool was supposed to do, what data it was trained on, and whether it was used as intended. The absence of documented training data characteristics and validation methods makes root cause analysis impossible.‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ It provides a baseline for interoperability. When vendors document their models using consistent frameworks, health systems can compare tools, understand their limitations, and integrate them into workflows with confidence. Undocumented models become black boxes that cannot be effectively compared or audited.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed deregulation would eliminate these requirements. The stated rationale is that removing certification burden will accelerate innovation and competition. But our experience suggests the opposite. Vendors with resources and market dominance benefit from opacity. They can deploy tools without documentation, creating switching costs for healthcare systems that become dependent on their products. Smaller vendors and open-source projects that operate with full transparency become less competitive because they cannot match the market speed of larger, less-regulated competitors.‚Źé‚Źé**LESSONS FROM EHR CERTIFICATION AND THE CURES ACT**‚Źé‚ŹéThe history of EHR certification under the American Recovery and Reinvestment Act (ARRA) of 2009 and subsequent Cures Act requirements provides instructive precedent. The HITECH Act's EHR Incentive Programs required providers to adopt "certified" EHR technology, which drove significant standardization and interoperability improvements (42 CFR Part 495). The certification process created a common language across vendors. Yes, it imposed compliance costs. But it also enabled data exchange, reduced duplicate testing, and allowed smaller healthcare organizations to adopt sophisticated tools that would otherwise have been inaccessible.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen the Cures Act of 2015 (15 U.S.C. 1320e-3) expanded information blocking regulations and required standardized APIs (45 CFR Part 171), the initial concern was that compliance burden would stifle innovation. In practice, standardization drove innovation in exactly the right direction: new services and tools built on top of standardized data exchange, rather than proprietary integrations that locked customers in. The same dynamic should apply to decision support interventions.‚Źé‚Źé**REAL-WORLD IMPLEMENTATION CONCERNS**‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving DSI certification criteria would create cascading problems in clinical workflows. Consider a concrete example: a hospital pharmacy receives a clinical decision support recommendation about a patient's warfarin dosing. Under current requirements, the pharmacy can review the tool's documentation to understand whether it was validated in patients with renal impairment, whether it accounts for drug interactions with the patient's other medications, and whether it has been tested in the hospital's patient population. If the tool's recommendation conflicts with clinical judgment, the pharmacist can make an informed decision about whether to override it.‚Źé‚ŹéRemove the certification requirement, and none of this information is available. The recommendation appears on the screen with no context about its origins, limitations, or validation. The pharmacist has no way to assess whether the tool is appropriate for this patient. This is not a theoretical concern. The American Society of Health-System Pharmacists (ASHP) has documented cases where uncertified decision support tools have made recommendations that contradict established clinical guidelines (ASHP Guidelines, 2022).‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal to remove these requirements assumes that market forces will ensure quality. But healthcare is not a market where patients or providers can easily switch providers or tools based on poor quality. Once a hospital implements a decision support system, switching costs are high. Clinicians adapt their workflows to the tool. Data integrations are built around it. The incentive to fix problems after implementation is lower than the incentive to cut corners before implementation.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC RECOMMENDATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Pharmacy Health Information Technology Collaborative urges the Office of the National Coordinator and the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services to retain the current DSI certification criteria. We recommend the following:‚Źé‚Źé1. Maintain requirements for documented model cards that specify intended use, target population, known limitations, and training data characteristics (45 CFR 170.315(d)(1)).‚Źé‚Źé2. Require external validation evidence for decision support tools that influence clinical recommendations, particularly those involving medication management, dosing, or contraindication checking.‚Źé‚Źé3. Establish a phased approach for existing deployed tools, allowing vendors 24 months to document their models while requiring certification for all new decision support tools released after the rule's effective date.‚Źé‚Źé4. Create a public registry of certified decision support tools that allows healthcare providers to compare documentation and understand which tools have been validated in specific patient populations.‚Źé‚Źé5. Collaborate with clinical specialty societies (pharmacy, medicine, nursing) to develop standardized templates for model documentation that reduce compliance burden while maintaining transparency.‚Źé‚ŹéThe concern is not that standards are unnecessary. The concern is that removing them will repeat the expensive fragmentation that pharmacy networks like our member organization experienced last year. Healthcare innovation should move toward greater transparency, not less. The future of clinical decision support depends on clinicians understanding and trusting the tools they use. That trust requires documentation, validation, and accountability. These are not regulatory burdens. They are clinical essentials.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-14T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-14T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ4‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHospital and health system procurement teams need transparent AI model information to make evidence-based decisions about which tools to adopt and how to implement them safely in their local contexts.‚ôĒacademic‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒsupportive‚ôĒNevada‚ôĒhealth economist‚ôĒ33‚ôĒLast year, I worked with a regional pharmacy network that spent eighteen months building an EHR integration system only to discover their data couldn't interoperate with three major hospital systems in the state because there were no baseline standards requiring compatibility‚ÄĒthe systems had been designed in silos under different regulatory interpretations. When we finally mapped out what standards *should* have been mandated from the start, it became clear that the fragmentation hadn't driven innovation; it had just created expensive workarounds that patients and providers both paid for.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1369
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0198‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDeborah White‚ôĒXSELL Technologies‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ# Abstract‚Źé‚ŹéWhile acknowledging that current certification requirements create genuine barriers for smaller healthcare vendors, Deborah White argues that ONC should retain Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements for AI mode...‚ôĒDeborah White‚ŹéXSELL Technologies‚ŹéJackson, Mississippi‚Źé‚ŹéRE: RFI Response on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions‚Źé‚ŹéOur clinic's experience migrating EHR systems last year exposed something that doesn't often make it into policy discussions: the tension between making certification requirements lean enough that smaller vendors can actually compete, and keeping them robust enough that patient safety doesn't become a casualty of cost-cutting. I'm writing because the proposed changes to Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria require careful thought before moving forward, particularly around the removal or weakening of AI model transparency requirements.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen we switched platforms, our vendor was technically excellent but couldn't customize workflow fields without losing their certification status. That single constraint cascaded through six months of implementation. We either accepted processes that didn't match how our providers actually work, or we risked non-compliance. I watched our IT director navigate that impossible choice, and it made me understand why smaller vendors are dropping out of healthcare entirely. The compliance burden is real, and it's a genuine problem. But the solution isn't to strip away the requirements that exist because someone got hurt before.‚Źé‚Źé**The Problem We Actually Face**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be clear: I'm not arguing against deregulation broadly. The current certification framework does create barriers. Smaller vendors struggle with the costs of maintaining certified status. Practices like ours face limited choices in the market because many promising companies simply can't afford the certification process. When we were evaluating EHR systems, we looked at eight vendors. Two were dropped from consideration entirely because their products couldn't achieve certification, even though their core functionality was solid. That's a real loss for market competition.‚Źé‚ŹéBut here's what concerns me, and I've thought about this a lot since our implementation: some of the regulatory requirements exist because healthcare learned hard lessons. The DSI certification criterion requiring AI model cards, transparency disclosures around intended use, target population, known risks, and training data characteristics isn't bureaucratic busywork. It's the documentation framework that lets a clinician know whether an algorithm was built for their patient population or for someone else entirely.‚Źé‚ŹéWe're beginning to integrate decision support tools into our workflows. Our vendor is exploring machine learning applications for sepsis prediction and early intervention. It's genuinely useful work. But when I ask about the training data, about whether the model was validated on populations like ours, about what the false positive rate looks like in our patient mix, I'm not asking because I like paperwork. I'm asking because I need to know whether I can trust this tool with my patients, and whether I can explain to my providers why they should or shouldn't follow its recommendations.‚Źé‚Źé**What Happens When Transparency Goes Away**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed approach seems to assume that vendors will voluntarily provide this information anyway. My experience suggests otherwise. When compliance stops being a requirement, it becomes a cost with no offsetting benefit. A vendor with tight margins will cut it. I'm not impugning anyone's motives, but I've been in healthcare administration long enough to know how budgets work. What gets required gets funded. What's voluntary gets deferred.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider what a provider actually needs to know before deploying a decision support tool in clinical practice. According to the FDA's guidance on clinical decision support (2019), and ONC's own Health IT Certification Program guidance under 45 CFR Part 170, transparency about model development, validation, and known limitations is foundational to safe implementation. When we evaluated our sepsis tool, we needed to understand:‚Źé‚Źé- Whether it was trained on data from hospitals, ambulatory clinics, or both‚Źé- What the patient demographics were in the training cohort‚Źé- Whether the model had been tested on populations with similar socioeconomic and racial composition to ours‚Źé- What the actual sensitivity and specificity are in our patient population, not just in research papers‚Źé- What conditions or medications might confound the predictions‚Źé‚ŹéWithout that information, we're essentially asking our providers to use a black box. That's not innovation. It's negligence.‚Źé‚ŹéThe argument for removing these requirements usually rests on two claims. First, that documentation burden is killing smaller vendors. Second, that the market will naturally select for transparency because customers demand it. I think the first claim is partially true and the second is wishful thinking.‚Źé‚Źé**The Real Certification Burden Isn't What We Think**‚Źé‚ŹéThe burden isn't actually the documentation itself. A competent vendor should know how their model was built, what data it was trained on, and how it performs. Writing that down isn't the expensive part. The expensive part is the certification infrastructure, the testing requirements, the audits, the legal review, and the ongoing compliance monitoring. Those costs exist regardless of whether you're documenting a simple rule-based system or a complex neural network.‚Źé‚ŹéIf the goal is to reduce vendor burden, the place to focus is the certification infrastructure itself, not the substantive requirements. You could streamline the testing process, reduce audit frequency for stable products, create tiered certification for different risk levels, or allow vendors to use third-party validation labs. Those changes would actually help smaller vendors without gutting the transparency requirements that providers need.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat you wouldn't do, if you cared about both vendor viability and patient safety, is remove the requirement to document what your tool actually does.‚Źé‚Źé**The Market Won't Solve This**‚Źé‚ŹéThe second argument, that transparency will emerge naturally through market demand, assumes that healthcare IT buyers have enough expertise and leverage to demand rigorous documentation of AI systems. We don't. In our case, we're a 12-provider practice in Mississippi. When we're evaluating a DSI tool, we're not running our own validation studies. We're not hiring data scientists to audit the vendor's model. We're making a decision based on whatever information the vendor chooses to provide, the vendor's reputation, and whether the tool seems to work in our limited testing.‚Źé‚ŹéIf we had more options, maybe we could push back harder on transparency. But in many healthcare markets, there are only two or three vendors offering certain functionalities. Our leverage is minimal. The market dynamics don't support the idea that transparency will emerge naturally. Vendors will provide as much information as they're required to provide, and no more.‚Źé‚Źé**What Retention Looks Like in Practice**‚Źé‚ŹéI'm recommending that ONC retain the DSI certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures, but I'd also recommend thinking about how to make compliance easier for smaller vendors. Here's what I mean:‚Źé‚Źé1. Keep the substantive requirement: vendors must document intended use, target population, known risks, and training data characteristics. This is non-negotiable for patient safety. The FDA requires this for clinical decision support systems, and it's consistent with existing regulatory frameworks like 21 CFR 807.‚Źé‚Źé2. Simplify the compliance process. Rather than requiring vendors to submit detailed technical documentation, allow them to use a standardized template. ONC could publish a model card template that vendors can populate. The template should be straightforward enough that a small vendor with one data scientist can complete it, but comprehensive enough to give providers the information they need.‚Źé‚Źé3. Allow third-party validation. Vendors shouldn't have to conduct all their own testing. They should be able to use independent testing organizations or academic partners to validate their models. This reduces the in-house burden on small vendors while maintaining the integrity of the validation process.‚Źé‚Źé4. Create a tiered approach based on risk. A tool that makes recommendations for routine preventive care has different implications than one that alerts on life-threatening conditions. The documentation requirements could scale with clinical risk. Lower-risk tools might require less extensive validation data.‚Źé‚Źé5. Establish a grace period for existing tools. If a vendor already has a tool on the market that doesn't have full documentation, give them 18 months to get compliant rather than immediately revoking certification. This prevents market disruption while still moving toward the goal.‚Źé‚ŹéThese changes would reduce the burden on smaller vendors without removing the safeguards that healthcare providers actually need.‚Źé‚Źé**The Specific Case for Model Cards**‚Źé‚ŹéModel cards, as defined in the literature (Gebru et al., 2018), are a documented format for reporting key details about machine learning models. They include intended use, performance across different demographic groups, training data composition, and known limitations. For clinical decision support, model cards should be adapted to the healthcare context, but the core idea is sound.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen we're deciding whether to use a tool, a model card would let us quickly understand the scope of its applicability. Our provider population is roughly 35 percent uninsured or Medicaid, 45 percent commercially insured, and 20 percent Medicare. We have high rates of certain chronic conditions because of our patient demographics. A model card would immediately tell us whether the tool was validated on similar populations. If it was trained primarily on data from affluent, insured populations at academic medical centers, we'd know to be cautious about its performance in our setting.‚Źé‚ŹéThat information isn't just academic. It affects clinical decision-making in real time. A sepsis prediction tool that performs well at 95 percent sensitivity in the training population might perform quite differently in our clinic if the training data didn't include patients with our demographic profile. Knowing that, we can adjust our thresholds for using the tool's recommendations. We can do additional manual review in certain cases. We can track whether the tool is actually predicting sepsis accurately in our patient population or whether we need to dial back our reliance on it.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving the requirement to document this information doesn't make the problem go away. It just means we make clinical decisions without knowing the limitations of our tools. That's the opposite of innovation.‚Źé‚Źé**On Compliance Costs and Smaller Vendors**‚Źé‚ŹéI recognize that I'm asking for something that costs money. Creating a model card, documenting training data, running validation studies, these aren't free. But they're also not the primary cost driver for certification. The primary costs are the certification infrastructure: the application fees, the testing and audit requirements, the legal and quality management overhead. Those costs exist whether you're documenting a simple rule or a complex model.‚Źé‚ŹéIf ONC wants to help smaller vendors, the right approach is to reduce the infrastructure costs, not to eliminate the substantive requirements. You could reduce application fees for vendors with less than 10 million in annual revenue. You could streamline the audit process for stable products. You could allow vendors to share validation data across related products rather than requiring separate studies for each one. You could create an expedited pathway for vendors who use third-party validation labs.‚Źé‚ŹéThese changes would actually help smaller vendors compete. Removing the requirement to document how their tools work would just move the problem downstream to healthcare providers, who would then have to do their own validation or simply accept tools they don't understand.‚Źé‚Źé**A Wider Context**‚Źé‚ŹéI'm coming to this from a specific place: I've seen what happens when compliance requirements are poorly designed. Our EHR transition cost us six months and significant resources because the certification framework didn't account for the reality of how small practices actually work. I believe that regulation is often too rigid, and I believe there's real value in thinking about how to make compliance more achievable for smaller vendors.‚Źé‚ŹéBut I also know what it's like to be responsible for patient safety in a healthcare setting. When something goes wrong, when a patient is harmed, the fact that you didn't understand the limitations of your decision support tool because you weren't required to document them is not a defense. It's a failure.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criterion requiring transparency disclosures represents a reasonable balance. It asks vendors to document what they've already built, not to do additional work. It asks providers to understand the tools they're using, not to be expert data scientists. It creates a shared language between vendors and providers about what these tools can and can't do.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving it in the name of deregulation would be a mistake.‚Źé‚Źé**Recommendation**‚Źé‚ŹéXSELL Technologies recommends that ONC retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures, including documentation of intended use, target population, known risks, and training data characteristics. Simultaneously, ONC should streamline the certification process itself to reduce infrastructure costs for smaller vendors, perhaps through the mechanisms I've outlined above. This approach supports both innovation and safety, rather than forcing a choice between them.‚Źé‚ŹéThank you for the opportunity to provide input on this important set of proposed changes.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-13T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-13T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ6‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving DSI transparency requirements creates a dangerous regulatory gap where AI-based clinical decision support tools face fewer disclosure obligations than traditional medical devices, undermining consistent safety standards.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒconcerned‚ôĒMississippi‚ôĒhealthcare administrator‚ôĒ40‚ôĒWhen our clinic switched EHR systems last year, we spent six months navigating certification requirements that seemed designed for hospital networks, not a 12-provider practice‚ÄĒour vendor couldn't even customize basic workflow fields without invalidating their certification status, which meant we either accepted inefficient processes or risked compliance violations. I watched our IT director essentially choose between innovation and safety, and it crystallized for me how these rigid standards, while well-intentioned, have made smaller vendors reluctant to even bid on our contracts because the compliance costs eat up their entire margin.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2074
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0199‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒKaren Harris‚ôĒPolicy Center for Maternal Mental Health‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health opposes the removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements, arguing that transparency documentation of clinical decision support tools' intended use, validation, and limitati...‚ôĒ**POLICY CENTER FOR MATERNAL MENTAL HEALTH**‚Źé**Comment on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health submits this comment regarding the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria from the 2015 Edition Health IT Certification Criteria. We write as an organization whose core mission centers on expanding access to evidence-based maternal mental health screening, diagnosis, and treatment across the United States. Our work brings us into direct contact with the actual consequences of fragmented health information systems and, increasingly, with the potential risks posed by inadequately validated clinical decision support tools deployed in obstetric and postpartum care settings.‚Źé‚ŹéWe have a significant concern about the proposal to remove the requirement that DSI-related health IT modules maintain certification through transparent model cards and validation disclosures. While we recognize the intent behind reducing regulatory burden on developers, this particular deregulatory action poses real risks to maternal mental health outcomes and, more broadly, to the integrity of clinical decision-making in high-stakes healthcare contexts.‚Źé‚Źé**THE REAL PROBLEM WE SEE ON THE GROUND**‚Źé‚ŹéLast spring, our organization worked with a postpartum woman from rural Webster County, Iowa, who experienced a delay in psychiatric treatment because her postpartum depression screening records could not be transferred between her obstetric clinic and her psychiatrist. The clinic director, when questioned, admitted the decision to restrict records sharing was driven not by sound clinical judgment but by what she called "liability anxiety." The staff worried that if something went wrong with a patient's care, the electronic transfer of records might somehow increase exposure. This anxiety, while understandable, had nothing to do with the quality of the information itself. What should have been a routine transfer of the patient's own medical records became an obstacle course. The delay lasted three weeks.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen we began asking other maternal health providers about similar experiences, we heard variations on this story repeatedly. But there was something else we kept encountering: concern about decision support tools that were being used to flag risk in pregnant and postpartum patients without clear documentation of what data those tools had been trained on, who they were designed for, or what their known limitations were.‚Źé‚ŹéOne midwifery clinic in central Iowa told us about a DSI tool their EHR vendor had deployed that flagged certain patients as "high risk" for postpartum depression. When the clinic director asked the vendor for information about the tool's validation, the vendor provided only marketing materials. She wanted to know: Was this tool developed and tested on a population that resembled her patient population? What is the false positive rate? What happens to patients flagged as high risk? The vendor couldn't answer those questions. The clinic was using a tool to inform clinical decision-making about vulnerable women, but no one in that clinic could actually verify that the tool worked as claimed for their specific patient population.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not a theoretical concern. In maternal mental health, the consequences of missed diagnoses or delayed treatment are serious. Untreated postpartum depression is associated with adverse outcomes for mothers, infants, and families. Poor screening and diagnostic practices contribute to the fact that fewer than 15% of women with postpartum depression receive any treatment at all (Wisner et al., Archives of Womens Mental Health, 2013). When clinical decision support tools are deployed without transparency about their development, validation, and known limitations, we lose one of our only mechanisms for ensuring that these tools are actually helping rather than introducing new forms of bias or error into clinical workflows.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY THE DSI CERTIFICATION CRITERION MATTERS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification criterion, established under 45 CFR 170.315(a)(1)(ii) and related sections of the 2015 Edition criteria, requires that health IT modules incorporating clinical decision support functionality include transparent documentation of the tool's intended use, target population, training data characteristics, known risks, and evidence of external validation. This is not burdensome red tape. This is a floor-level expectation that a clinical tool has been documented in ways that allow clinicians to make informed decisions about whether and how to use it.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider what the current criterion actually requires:‚Źé‚Źé- Documentation of the tool's intended use and target population. A DSI tool designed and validated on a predominantly white, middle-class population may perform differently when deployed in a rural, racially diverse, lower-income patient population. Clinicians need to know this.‚Źé‚Źé- Disclosure of training data characteristics. How the tool was developed matters. If a postpartum depression screening tool was trained primarily on data from patients over age 35, it may not perform well for younger mothers. If it was trained on data from hospital settings, it may not generalize to primary care or midwifery practices.‚Źé‚Źé- Documentation of known risks and limitations. Every clinical tool has limitations. The question is whether those limitations are transparent or hidden. A DSI tool with a 20% false positive rate in a specific subpopulation is not inherently bad, but clinicians cannot use it appropriately if they don't know about that limitation.‚Źé‚Źé- Evidence of external validation. The tool needs to have been tested on data beyond the data used to develop it, ideally in settings and populations beyond the original development context. This is basic science.‚Źé‚ŹéThe 2015 Edition criteria established these requirements as a condition of certification. They did not ban DSI tools. They did not require perfection. They required transparency.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal before the agency would remove these requirements entirely. Vendors would no longer be required to provide model cards or validation documentation as a condition of certification. This means that health IT vendors could deploy decision support tools with no documented evidence that the tools work, no disclosure of what populations they were designed for, and no transparency about known limitations or failure modes.‚Źé‚Źé**A WORD ABOUT DEREGULATION AND INNOVATION**‚Źé‚ŹéWe understand the argument for reducing regulatory burden. We are not opposed to innovation. But there is a fundamental difference between removing unnecessary process requirements and removing substantive safeguards that protect against documented harms.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider the difference between these two regulatory moves:‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, suppose the agency required that every DSI tool go through a 12-month pre-deployment review process. This would be a process burden. It would slow innovation. A reasonable deregulatory move would be to streamline that review, perhaps to 6 months or to a risk-based approach where some tools get lighter review than others. That is smart deregulation.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, suppose the agency required that every DSI tool document its intended use, training data, and validation. This is not a process requirement. This is a documentation requirement. It is not asking vendors to do something different with their tools. It is asking them to document what they have already done. Removing this requirement does not speed up innovation. It speeds up deployment of poorly documented tools.‚Źé‚ŹéWe believe the agency is conflating these two things, and that confusion is driving this proposal.‚Źé‚ŹéThe practical reality in maternal health care is that many providers lack the resources to independently validate clinical decision support tools. When a vendor says "our tool helps identify postpartum depression risk," most clinicians have no way to verify that claim. They rely on certification and documentation as a proxy for quality assurance. Remove the certification requirement, and you remove that proxy. You do not create an opportunity for clinicians to become more discerning. You create an opportunity for poorly validated tools to proliferate without anyone knowing.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC RISKS IN THE MATERNAL MENTAL HEALTH CONTEXT**‚Źé‚ŹéMaternal mental health presents particular vulnerabilities to poorly validated decision support tools. Consider why:‚Źé‚ŹéFirst, maternal mental health conditions like postpartum depression, postpartum anxiety, and postpartum psychosis are relatively low-prevalence conditions in the general obstetric population. This creates a base-rate problem. If a screening or risk-stratification tool has a false positive rate above the actual prevalence of the condition, it will generate more false alarms than true cases. Clinicians need to know this. If a tool was developed and validated on a population where 30% of women had postpartum depression (perhaps because it was tested in a clinical research sample of women already seeking mental health care), that tool will behave very differently when deployed in routine obstetric care where the prevalence is closer to 10%. Without transparency about where and how the tool was validated, clinicians cannot account for this.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, maternal mental health screening and diagnosis involves significant racial and socioeconomic disparities in recognition and treatment. Black women are more likely to be screened for postpartum depression but less likely to receive treatment. Rural women and low-income women are less likely to be screened at all. If a DSI tool was developed on data that did not adequately represent these populations, it may perpetuate or amplify existing disparities. We need transparency about who the tool was designed for and how it performs across different populations. The current certification criterion requires this. The proposal would eliminate it.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, the stakes are high. Postpartum psychosis, while rare, is a psychiatric emergency that can result in harm to the mother or infant if not recognized and treated quickly. Postpartum depression is associated with adverse neurodevelopmental outcomes for infants if untreated. A decision support tool that misses cases or generates so many false alarms that clinicians ignore it is not a neutral addition to clinical workflow. It is an active harm.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health has reviewed the Federal Register notice for this proposed rule change (88 FR 22822, April 13, 2023) and the supporting analysis. We found little discussion of the specific vulnerabilities of maternal health care contexts to poorly validated decision support tools. The analysis focuses on general innovation arguments without engaging the question of whether transparency requirements actually slow innovation or whether they serve important safety functions.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT THE EVIDENCE TELLS US**‚Źé‚ŹéThe literature on clinical decision support deployment in healthcare is instructive here. A systematic review by Sittig and colleagues (Journal of the American Medical Informatics Association, 2008) found that one of the most significant predictors of clinician engagement with decision support tools was confidence in the tool's accuracy and relevance to their specific patient population. Tools that lacked transparent documentation of their validation were more likely to be overridden or ignored by clinicians. This is not a failure of the clinicians. It is a rational response to uncertainty.‚Źé‚ŹéThere is also growing literature on algorithmic bias in clinical decision support tools. A widely cited study by Obermeyer and colleagues (Science, 2019) identified racial bias in a commonly used algorithm for allocating healthcare resources. The bias was not intentional, but it was real, and it was only discovered because researchers were able to examine the tool's development and performance data. Without transparency about how tools are developed and validated, these kinds of biases can persist undetected.‚Źé‚ŹéWe are not arguing that the DSI certification criterion is perfect as written. There may be ways to streamline it, to make it more efficient, or to tailor it based on risk levels. But removing it entirely would be a mistake. The requirement serves an important function: it ensures that clinical decision support tools are documented in ways that allow clinicians to use them appropriately and allows researchers to identify problems before those problems cause harm.‚Źé‚Źé**THE SPECIFIC LANGUAGE OF THE CERTIFICATION CRITERION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI criterion (45 CFR 170.315(a)(1)(ii)) requires that:‚Źé‚Źé- The health IT module must include a model card or equivalent documentation that discloses the tool's intended use, target population, and known limitations.‚Źé- The module must include documentation of the data used to develop the tool, including the source, size, and demographic characteristics of the development dataset.‚Źé- The module must include evidence of external validation, defined as testing on data not used in model development, ideally in settings and populations beyond the original development context.‚Źé- The module must include documentation of performance metrics, including sensitivity, specificity, and predictive values, stratified by demographic subgroups where relevant.‚Źé‚ŹéNone of these requirements are unreasonable. None of them prevent vendors from developing and deploying decision support tools. All of them serve important functions in ensuring that tools are used appropriately.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal would remove all of these requirements as a condition of certification. Vendors would still be free to provide such documentation if they chose to, but there would be no requirement to do so. In practice, this means that documentation would be provided selectively, if at all. Competitive pressure would favor faster deployment over better documentation. The race to market would accelerate. Documentation would slow down deployment, so vendors would skip it.‚Źé‚Źé**OUR SPECIFIC RECOMMENDATION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health recommends that the agency retain the DSI certification criterion as currently written in 45 CFR 170.315(a)(1)(ii). If the agency believes that the criterion should be modified, we recommend that any modifications be limited to clarifications that make the requirement more efficient to implement, not to eliminating the substantive requirement for transparency.‚Źé‚ŹéSpecifically, we would support:‚Źé‚Źé- Clarification of what constitutes acceptable "external validation" so that vendors understand what evidence they need to provide.‚Źé- A tiered approach where the level of documentation required is proportionate to the clinical risk posed by the tool. A tool used for low-stakes administrative decisions might require less documentation than a tool used for clinical diagnosis.‚Źé- Specification of acceptable formats for model cards and documentation, potentially aligning with emerging standards like the Hugging Face model card format or the Datasheets for Datasets framework developed by Gebru and colleagues.‚Źé- A reasonable timeline for vendors to provide documentation, perhaps 90 days from the date of certification request, with allowance for extension if the vendor is conducting additional validation studies.‚Źé‚ŹéBut the core requirement should remain: health IT modules that incorporate clinical decision support functionality should be certified only if they include transparent documentation of their intended use, target population, development data characteristics, known limitations, and evidence of external validation.‚Źé‚Źé**ADDRESSING THE INFORMATION BLOCKING QUESTION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposal also raises questions about how information blocking regulations interact with DSI certification. Under 45 CFR 171.103, actors in the health IT ecosystem are prohibited from information blocking, meaning they cannot prevent or delay the exchange of health information without legitimate reasons. One concern raised by some vendors is that transparency requirements for DSI tools might be seen as creating information blocking obligations for vendors to disclose proprietary information about their algorithms.‚Źé‚ŹéWe want to be clear: we are not arguing that vendors should be forced to disclose proprietary algorithmic details or training data that would compromise trade secrets. The DSI certification criterion does not require that. It requires disclosure of the tool's intended use, target population, and known limitations. This information is not proprietary. It is not a trade secret. It is a basic description of what the tool is designed to do and who it is designed for.‚Źé‚ŹéA vendor can disclose the intended use and target population of a tool without disclosing the specific features used in the algorithm or the exact coefficients in a logistic regression model. A vendor can describe the demographic characteristics of the training dataset without disclosing the identities of patients or the specific clinical sites where the tool was developed. The certification criterion requires transparency, not proprietary disclosure.‚Źé‚Źé**THE STAKES FOR MATERNAL MENTAL HEALTH**‚Źé‚ŹéWe want to be direct about why we are commenting on this particular rule. Maternal mental health is a public health crisis in the United States. Approximately one in seven women experience postpartum depression. Fewer than 15% receive any treatment. The consequences are serious: maternal suicide is a leading cause of pregnancy-related death. Postpartum depression is associated with adverse neurodevelopmental outcomes for infants. The economic costs are substantial.‚Źé‚ŹéOne of the tools we have to address this crisis is better screening and diagnosis. Decision support tools that help clinicians identify women at risk for postpartum depression could be valuable. But only if those tools are validated, transparent, and appropriately used.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen we remove the requirement that these tools be certified based on transparent documentation of their development and validation, we are saying that speed to market matters more than confidence in the tool's accuracy. We are saying that vendor convenience matters more than clinician confidence. We are saying that we are willing to accept the risk that poorly validated tools might be deployed in clinical settings serving vulnerable women.‚Źé‚ŹéWe do not think that is the right trade-off.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health respectfully urges the agency to retain the DSI certification criterion as currently written in 45 CFR 170.315(a)(1)(ii). Clinical decision support tools that help clinicians identify postpartum depression, postpartum anxiety, and other maternal mental health conditions can be important tools for improving outcomes. But those tools must be developed, validated, and deployed with transparency about their intended use, target populations, known limitations, and evidence of external validation.‚Źé‚ŹéRemoving the certification requirement would not speed innovation in any meaningful way. It would remove a safeguard that protects clinicians and patients from poorly validated tools. In the context of maternal mental health, where the stakes are high and the existing disparities in recognition and treatment are substantial, this would be a mistake.‚Źé‚ŹéWe thank the agency for the opportunity to comment and for considering the specific concerns of maternal health care providers and the women and families they serve.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-29T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-29T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ9‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHospital and health system procurement teams need transparent AI model information to make evidence-based decisions about which tools to adopt and how to implement them safely in their local contexts.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒangry‚ôĒIowa‚ôĒpolicy director‚ôĒ51‚ôĒLast spring, I sat with a young mother from rural Webster County who'd been denied access to her own postpartum depression screening records because the clinic claimed sharing them with her psychiatrist would violate some compliance threshold‚ÄĒrecords that should have been routine to transfer. CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0201‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒJessica Lewis‚ôĒCollege of American Pathologists (CAP)‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe College of American Pathologists opposes the removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements, arguing that transparency disclosures about AI model training, validation, and performance are essential for clinical acco...‚ôĒ**COMMENT TO THE OFFICE OF THE NATIONAL COORDINATOR FOR HEALTH INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY AND THE CENTERS FOR MEDICARE & MEDICAID SERVICES**‚Źé‚Źé**Re: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity**‚Źé‚ŹéThe College of American Pathologists (CAP) appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory framework. The CAP represents nearly 18,000 physicians in laboratory medicine and pathology across the United States and maintains active certification and accreditation programs affecting thousands of clinical laboratories. Our comments focus specifically on the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria and the implications for patient safety, clinical accountability, and data quality in AI-assisted diagnostic workflows.‚Źé‚ŹéThe experience that prompted this submission comes directly from our research collaboration last spring. While reviewing standardized quality metrics from a multi-state pathology study, the CAP research team discovered that three independent rural Nebraska laboratories had been absorbed by a regional health system consolidator. Within weeks of acquisition, their entire information technology infrastructure was replaced. This single transition rendered eight years of historical quality data inaccessible and incomparable to national benchmarks we had been tracking since 2017. The consolidator's new system did not maintain backward compatibility with the previous vendor's data formats, and the original laboratory directors had minimal control over the transition timeline or specifications. The research impact was immediate: we lost the ability to monitor whether these facilities maintained their diagnostic accuracy thresholds, and the national dataset became incomplete. This is not an edge case. Laboratory consolidation has accelerated significantly, with the American Clinical Laboratory Association reporting that the number of independent clinical laboratories has declined by roughly 30% over the past decade (Federal Register, Vol. 88, No. 156, August 2023).‚Źé‚ŹéWhat concerns the CAP is not consolidation itself, but the loss of transparency and accountability mechanisms that should accompany it, especially as artificial intelligence tools become embedded in diagnostic decision-making. The proposed removal of DSI certification requirements would eliminate one of the few regulatory anchors requiring developers and deploying organizations to disclose how their AI models were trained, what populations they were validated on, what known risks exist, and what the intended clinical use case actually is. These requirements are not burdensome paperwork. They are the foundation of informed clinical decision-making.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CURRENT DSI CERTIFICATION REQUIREMENT AND WHAT IT REQUIRES**‚Źé‚ŹéUnder the current 21 CFR Part 170.315(a)(1), health information technology systems that include clinical decision support features must meet specific certification criteria. These include transparency disclosures about model cards, training data characteristics, external validation results, target population specifications, and known limitations or risks.
