The requirement does not mandate specific performance thresholds or prevent innovation. It mandates disclosure. A pathologist reviewing an AI-assisted diagnostic recommendation needs to know whether the model was trained on biopsy samples from patients similar to the one being examined, whether the model's accuracy degrades for certain tissue types or stains, and what the false positive and false negative rates actually are. Without these disclosures, the pathologist is essentially making a clinical judgment with incomplete information about the tool being used.‚Źé‚ŹéThe CAP recognizes that the Office of the National Coordinator and CMS are seeking to reduce regulatory burden on developers. That is a legitimate policy objective. However, the proposed approach conflates "removing regulatory burden" with "removing transparency requirements." These are not the same thing. A model card is not a burden on innovation. It is a precondition for responsible deployment.‚Źé‚ŹéThe rural Nebraska case illustrates why this matters in practice. When the consolidator replaced the legacy laboratory information system, none of the AI-assisted diagnostic tools in the new system carried the documentation that would have allowed the original laboratory directors to understand whether their clinical workflows should change. One of the three laboratories had been using a particular vendor's AI application for cervical cytology screening for four years. Under the current DSI rules, that vendor was required to provide documentation about the model's performance on different specimen types, its validation population, and its intended use case. When the new health system's IT team implemented a different vendor's cervical cytology tool, there was no systematic way to ensure that the transition maintained equivalent diagnostic safety. The research team had to reconstruct validation data through manual record review, a process that took six months and cost approximately $45,000 in staff time.‚Źé‚Źé**PATIENT SAFETY AND ACCOUNTABILITY IN CONSOLIDATED HEALTH SYSTEMS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe consolidation trend in laboratory medicine is accelerating precisely because of economies of scale and operational efficiency gains. The CAP does not oppose consolidation. What the CAP opposes is using deregulation to remove the transparency mechanisms that make consolidated systems accountable to the clinicians and patients who depend on them.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen a large health system acquires three independent laboratories, the consolidator's IT leadership typically has authority to select diagnostic tools, implement training data protocols, and define performance standards. The original laboratory directors may have limited input into these decisions. In the absence of DSI certification requirements, there is no regulatory mechanism requiring the consolidator to document that the new tools meet the same safety and accuracy standards as the tools they replaced. The consolidator can argue that such documentation is proprietary and therefore unnecessary to disclose. From a business perspective, that makes sense. From a patient safety perspective, it creates a gap.‚Źé‚ŹéThe CAP's concern is not theoretical. In a 2022 survey of CAP members working in consolidated health systems, 34% reported that they had been unable to obtain complete validation data for newly implemented AI-assisted diagnostic tools. Of those respondents, 67% reported that they would have wanted that information before deployment. These data come from the CAP's Quality, Efficiency, and Safety Assessment Program (QuEST), which surveyed 312 laboratory directors between January and March 2022 and achieved a 73% response rate.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed deregulation would amplify this problem. If DSI certification criteria are removed, there is no federal requirement that a consolidator document how a new AI tool performs on the patient populations actually served by the acquired laboratories. The consolidator can deploy the tool, monitor its performance through internal quality metrics, and adjust implementation as needed. That is not inherently unsafe. But it is less transparent than the current regime, and in consolidation contexts where the original laboratory leadership has limited authority, it creates accountability gaps.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC RECOMMENDATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe CAP respectfully recommends that the final rule retain the current DSI certification criteria requiring model cards and transparency disclosures. If the goal is to reduce burden on developers, the CAP proposes the following alternative approaches:‚Źé‚Źé1. **Establish a safe harbor for model card submission.** Allow developers to submit model cards in a standardized format (aligned with emerging standards from the National Institute of Standards and Technology) without requiring full FDA premarket approval. This reduces compliance burden while maintaining transparency.‚Źé‚Źé2. **Phase implementation timelines for legacy systems.** Health IT systems currently deployed do not need to undergo immediate recertification. New systems and significant updates to existing systems would be required to meet DSI criteria going forward. This allows consolidators to integrate legacy systems without immediate recertification while ensuring that new deployments are transparent.‚Źé‚Źé3. **Create a clinical advisory mechanism for consolidated health systems.** When a large health system acquires multiple laboratories, require a documented review process involving the original laboratory leadership to assess whether new AI tools meet the safety standards of the tools they replace. This does not impose new burden on the health system. It codifies a process that responsible consolidators should already be conducting.‚Źé‚Źé4. **Align DSI criteria with NIST AI Risk Management Framework.** Rather than removing DSI criteria, align them with the emerging NIST framework for AI risk management (NIST AI 600-1, released March 2023). This modernizes the requirements without eliminating transparency.‚Źé‚ŹéThe regulatory framework should not be used to enable consolidators to reduce transparency during the transition period when clinical accountability is most fragile. If anything, consolidation contexts warrant heightened attention to decision support transparency.‚Źé‚Źé**CONCLUSION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe College of American Pathologists supports regulatory modernization and acknowledges the legitimate burden-reduction objectives in the proposed rule. However, the removal of DSI certification criteria is not a proportionate response to that objective. Transparency about how AI models are trained, validated, and deployed is not a regulatory burden. It is the foundation of clinical accountability and patient safety. The rural Nebraska experience demonstrates that consolidation can create gaps in that accountability. The proposed rule should be revised to retain DSI certification criteria while streamlining implementation pathways for developers and consolidating health systems.‚Źé‚ŹéThe CAP stands ready to work with the Office of the National Coordinator and CMS to develop implementation guidance that balances innovation with transparency.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-03T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-03T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ4‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHospital and health system procurement teams need transparent AI model information to make evidence-based decisions about which tools to adopt and how to implement them safely in their local contexts.‚ôĒacademic‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒfrustrated‚ôĒKansas‚ôĒPhD researcher‚ôĒ59‚ôĒLast spring, I was reviewing laboratory certification data from a multi-state study when we discovered that three independent pathology labs in rural Nebraska had abruptly stopped reporting standardized quality metrics‚ÄĒnot because of noncompliance, but because a regional consolidator had absorbed them and switched their entire IT infrastructure overnight, making their historical data inaccessible to our research team and, critically, incomparable to the national benchmarks we'd been tracking for eight years.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1425
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0202‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒEdward Rivera‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒA veteran healthcare professional argues that while deregulation of health IT is needed to improve interoperability and reduce unnecessary compliance burdens, the requirement for AI decision support tools to provide model cards documenting their t...‚ôĒRE: Response to Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability RFI: Deregulatory Actions to Unleash Prosperity‚Źé‚ŹéAfter forty years working in hospital settings and long-term care, I've watched patient safety swing on the hinge of information access. Three hours on the phone last month trying to get Mrs. Chen's cardiac imaging transferred between two hospital systems in the same state should not happen in 2025. But what concerns me more than that administrative nightmare is the thought of what could happen if we strip away the safeguards meant to keep bad actors from exploiting access to health data while we're fixing the genuine problems that exist.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed deregulation addresses real issues. Interoperability standards are fragmented. Certification requirements do slow innovation. Smaller health IT vendors get squeezed out. I understand why developers complain about the compliance burden, and I'm not here to defend every rule we have. But I need to flag something specific about the certification criteria for Decision Support Interventions (DSI) that I think deserves protection even in a deregulation effort. This isn't about blocking innovation. It's about keeping it honest.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT AI MODEL CARDS ACTUALLY DO FOR PATIENT SAFETY**‚Źé‚ŹéWhen a clinical decision support tool makes a recommendation to a nurse or physician, that recommendation is now often generated by machine learning models trained on data sets that may not reflect the patient in front of you. I've used these tools. They're helpful. But I've also watched a recommendation come through for a patient with comorbidities the algorithm wasn't trained on, and the clinician had to decide whether to trust the machine or their own judgment. That decision is impossible to make well if you don't know what population the model was built for, what data it saw during training, what its known failure modes are, and whether it's been validated on anyone like your actual patient.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification criterion requires developers to provide model cards documenting intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. This isn't bureaucratic theater. It's the difference between a tool and a black box. When Mrs. Chen's cardiologist orders an imaging interpretation or gets a flagged alert about medication interactions, she and her providers deserve to know whether the system generating that alert was trained on people her age, her ethnicity, her comorbidity profile. If it wasn't, they need to know that too.‚Źé‚ŹéI've seen what happens when clinicians don't have this information. They either blindly trust the tool or blindly distrust it. Either way, the patient loses. The clinician loses the ability to make an informed decision about how much weight to give the recommendation. This is exactly the kind of transparency that protects both innovation and safety.‚Źé‚Źé**ACCOUNTABILITY STARTS WITH KNOWING WHAT YOU'RE USING**‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what worries me about loosening the DSI certification requirements: accountability evaporates. If a developer doesn't have to disclose what population their model was trained on, and a patient is harmed by a recommendation that didn't apply to them, who's responsible? The developer can say the tool wasn't intended for that use. The health system can say they relied on the certification process. The clinician can say they followed the tool's output. The patient is left holding the bag.‚Źé‚ŹéI'm not opposed to faster approval timelines or lighter-touch oversight for lower-risk tools. But Decision Support Interventions that influence clinical decisions about imaging, medications, or treatment plans are not low-risk. They touch real patients. When a tool fails, people can die or suffer permanent harm. The model card requirement isn't a burden that slows innovation. It's a transparency mechanism that allows clinicians to use the tool responsibly.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider the FDA's approach to software as a medical device. They've streamlined approval for lower-risk categories while maintaining rigorous oversight for high-risk applications. The DSI certification criterion does something similar. It doesn't require years of testing or prohibit release. It requires that developers tell the truth about what they built and what it's been tested on. That's a reasonable floor, not a ceiling on innovation.‚Źé‚Źé**THE REAL PROBLEM ISN'T TRANSPARENCY, IT'S INTEGRATION**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be honest about what the actual bottleneck is in my daily work. It's not model cards. It's that three different electronic health record systems in my region don't talk to each other without a manual workaround, and when they do talk, they send incomplete or incorrectly formatted data. That's what needs fixing. That's where deregulation could actually help.‚Źé‚ŹéBut fixing data integration is separate from gutting safeguards on clinical decision tools. One is about removing unnecessary technical barriers. The other is about removing accountability measures. They're not the same thing. I can support aggressive deregulation on interoperability standards and data exchange requirements without accepting removal of transparency requirements for AI-assisted clinical tools.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT NEEDS TO STAY, WHAT COULD GO**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the goal is to unleash innovation and reduce burden on developers, focus on the certification process itself. Speed up review timelines. Streamline the paperwork. Allow developers to self-certify their model cards under a light-touch audit model. Create pathways for rapid updates when models are retrained. But don't eliminate the requirement that developers document what they built, who they built it for, and what it's been tested on.‚Źé‚ŹéThe information blocking safeguards that prevent patients from accessing their own records? That's a different conversation, and yes, I understand those need adjustment. Patients should have genuine portability. But that's not what the DSI certification criterion does. It ensures that the tools influencing care decisions are documented and accountable.‚Źé‚Źé**CLOSING THOUGHT**‚Źé‚ŹéAfter four decades in nursing, I've learned that innovation and safety aren't enemies. They're partners. The fastest way to kill trust in health technology is to deploy tools that fail in ways nobody saw coming because the developers didn't bother documenting what they were building. The slowest way to innovate is to require five years of testing before anything can be used.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criterion with model cards is the middle ground. It lets developers innovate and get to market quickly while ensuring that clinicians and patients know what they're working with. That's worth keeping. Everything else in the deregulation proposal that removes unnecessary burden? I'm with you. But this one matters too much to patients like Mrs. Chen to let go.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-22T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-22T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ3‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe agency's claim that 'no publicly available evidence' supports transparency requirements conflates absence of evidence with evidence of absence; lack of published studies does not mean the criterion provides no value.‚ôĒindividual_consumer‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒfrustrated‚ôĒConnecticut‚ôĒnurse‚ôĒ66‚ôĒLast month, I had to spend three hours on the phone with two different hospital systems just to get Mrs. Chen's cardiac imaging transferred so her new cardiologist could actually see what we were dealing with‚ÄĒand she's my patient, not some stranger requesting records. After forty years in nursing, I've watched the same data sit in incompatible systems while patients suffer the consequences, but I've also seen how quickly bad actors can exploit access when guardrails disappear.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1041
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0203‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒDorothy Hill‚ôĒKith and Kin, LLC‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒKith and Kin, LLC urges the Office of the National Coordinator to retain Decision Support Interventions certification requirements, particularly transparency disclosures about AI model training data and validation, arguing that removing these safe...‚ôĒ**Re: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions ‚ÄĒ Decision Support Interventions Certification Criterion**‚Źé‚ŹéKith and Kin, LLC is a primary care practice management organization operating in Anchorage, Alaska, serving approximately 8,500 patients across multiple clinical sites. We employ 34 staff members and have managed our own electronic health record systems for fifteen years. I'm writing to urge the Office of the National Coordinator to retain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion, particularly the requirements for AI model cards and transparency disclosures, rather than pursue the proposed deregulatory approach outlined in the Advanced Technologies and Practices (ASTP) initiative.‚Źé‚ŹéThe premise of deregulation appeals to us on its surface. Three years ago, we switched EHR systems and spent $47,000 on vendor customizations just to replicate workflows we already had. We understand vendor burden. We understand the frustration with compliance costs. But the proposed removal of DSI certification requirements would create a different kind of burden, one that transfers risk from vendors directly to patients and clinicians like ours who actually use these tools every day.‚Źé‚Źé**Why the Current DSI Criterion Matters**‚Źé‚ŹéThe existing DSI certification requirement mandates that vendors disclose intended use, target populations, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results for AI-assisted clinical decision support tools. This is not bureaucratic theater. When we evaluate whether to implement a decision support system in our clinic, we need to know what population it was validated on. Our patient demographic is 22% Alaska Native, with significantly higher rates of diabetes and hypertension than national averages. If a vendor's AI model was trained primarily on data from populations with different disease prevalence and different social determinants of health, that model may perform very differently for our patients. We have no way to know this without transparency disclosures.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider a concrete example from our own experience. Last year, we evaluated a sepsis-alert algorithm. The vendor claimed it would flag high-risk patients in real time. We asked about the training data. The vendor was evasive. We later learned through an independent query that the model was trained on ICU data from large academic medical centers. Our clinic is outpatient primary care. The algorithm would have generated constant false alarms in our setting and potentially desensitized our staff to legitimate alerts. We did not implement it. But under a deregulated framework where vendors have no obligation to provide model cards or validation data, we might have made that decision blindly.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not a rare scenario. A 2022 study in Nature Medicine found that AI models trained on predominantly white populations performed significantly worse when applied to patients of color, with performance gaps ranging from 7 to 18 percentage points depending on the model (Obermeyer et al., Nature Medicine 28, 11‚Äď14, 2022). Removing certification requirements that mandate disclosure of training data characteristics means clinicians will have no systematic way to identify and mitigate these risks.‚Źé‚Źé**The Patient Safety Argument**‚Źé‚ŹéWe are deeply concerned that deregulation in this area conflates regulatory burden with patient safety. The DSI certification criterion exists because clinical decision support systems influence real medical decisions. If a provider relies on a flagged alert and that alert is unreliable for their patient population, the consequences can be serious. Transparency requirements do not prevent innovation. They require vendors to understand their own products well enough to document them.‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed deregulation would also create perverse incentives. Vendors with less rigorous validation processes could bring products to market faster and cheaper than vendors who invest in proper testing and documentation. We have seen this dynamic before in health IT. The vendors with the slimmest margins and least investment in quality often undercut those doing the work properly. Removing certification requirements accelerates that race to the bottom.‚Źé‚Źé**A Practical Middle Path**‚Źé‚ŹéWe are not opposed to streamlining certification processes. If ONC believes the current documentation requirements are redundant or could be restructured to reduce burden without sacrificing transparency, we support that conversation. But the core requirement that vendors disclose training data characteristics, intended use, known risks, and external validation results should remain.‚Źé‚ŹéOne feasible approach would be to standardize the format of DSI disclosures so vendors can comply more efficiently. The FDA's Proposed Modifications to Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) guidance (Federal Register Vol. 86, No. 21, February 2, 2021) offers a useful template for how transparency requirements can be structured without excessive burden. Vendors could submit a single standardized form rather than navigating ambiguous regulatory language.‚Źé‚Źé**What Matters to Our Patients**‚Źé‚ŹéAt the end of the day, Kith and Kin serves patients who depend on us to make good clinical decisions. We cannot do that if we are implementing AI tools we do not understand. Removing certification requirements that mandate transparency serves vendor convenience, not patient welfare. We urge ONC to retain the DSI criterion and to resist the broader deregulatory impulse that assumes all compliance costs are unjustified.‚Źé‚ŹéWe are happy to provide additional detail on our concerns and would welcome the opportunity to discuss how transparency requirements can be implemented efficiently.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-02-08T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-08T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒresigned‚ôĒAlaska‚ôĒpractice manager‚ôĒ42‚ôĒWhen our clinic switched EHR systems three years ago, we were locked into a vendor's proprietary customizations that cost us $47,000 in modifications just to match our existing workflows‚ÄĒmodifications that became worthless the moment we wanted to switch to something better. My staff spent months relearning interfaces that should have been intuitive, and we still can't pull patient data the way we need to without manual workarounds that eat up their afternoons.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ842
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0204‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒMark White‚ôĒHealthcare Leadership Council‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Healthcare Leadership Council opposes the proposed elimination of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements for AI model transparency and disclosure, arguing that removing these standards will disproportionately harm rura...‚ôĒ# Comment on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: Deregulatory Actions‚Źé‚ŹéThe Healthcare Leadership Council represents over 60 major healthcare organizations, including hospital systems, physician groups, health plans, and specialized care networks. Our member organizations collectively serve millions of patients across urban, suburban, and rural settings. I want to address what we're seeing in real clinical environments as this rule moves forward, particularly around the proposed changes to Decision Support Interventions certification criteria.‚Źé‚ŹéLast year, one of our rural member clinics in northwest Oklahoma spent nearly six months trying to share a patient's oncology records with a specialist two counties over. The records were critical. The patient was 47, newly diagnosed with stage three ovarian cancer, and every week of delay affected her treatment timeline. Our IT director was caught between contradictory guidance from state regulators and federal standards, trying to parse what was actually required under the existing certification framework. Meanwhile, a data broker acquired this same patient's insurance claims history for forty-seven dollars through what appears to be a legal loophole. The patient never knew. The specialist never knew the patient existed. That gap between what we're making hard to do and what we're making easy to allow troubles me deeply.‚Źé‚ŹéThe specific issue we want to address is the proposed elimination of the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion that currently requires AI model cards and transparency disclosures. This matters more than it might appear on the surface, and I think the rule needs to be reconsidered on this particular point.‚Źé‚Źé**What the Current DSI Criterion Actually Protects**‚Źé‚ŹéThe existing certification requirement for DSI technologies mandates that developers provide model cards containing information about intended use, target population, known limitations and risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. This isn't theoretical. In practice, when our health systems evaluate an AI-assisted diagnostic tool or a clinical decision support system, we're asking specific questions: Was this model trained on patients who look like the ones we serve? What happens when you use it outside that population? Have independent researchers tested it, or only the vendor? What are the failure modes?‚Źé‚ŹéThese disclosures exist in 45 CFR Part 170.315(b)(8) for a reason. They emerged from years of implementation experience with EHR technology and from clinical safety incidents where tools were deployed without adequate understanding of their limitations. When a system tells a cardiologist that a patient's risk profile matches a certain category, the cardiologist needs to know whether the underlying model was validated in diverse populations or only in academic medical centers. That's not bureaucratic overhead. That's the difference between informed clinical decision-making and flying blind.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat concerns us about the proposed removal is that it assumes the market will naturally incentivize transparency. In our experience, that's not what happens. We've seen vendors market clinical tools with minimal disclosed validation data because hospital purchasing committees don't always have the technical expertise to demand better. Regulatory requirements create a baseline. Remove the requirement, and we'll see some vendors maintain disclosure standards because they're committed to quality. But we'll also see others cut corners because they can. The market doesn't self-correct on things patients don't directly observe.‚Źé‚Źé**The Rural and Equity Problem We're Actually Living**‚Źé‚ŹéI keep coming back to the clinic example because it illustrates something the rule might not account for. Our rural and underserved member organizations operate with constrained IT budgets and limited access to clinical informatics expertise. When they adopt new technologies, they're often relying on certification standards as a proxy for quality assurance. They don't have the internal expertise to validate an AI model independently. They can't hire a machine learning engineer to audit vendor claims. They're reading the model card and making a decision based on that information.‚Źé‚ŹéIf we remove the requirement for model cards and transparency disclosures, we're effectively creating a two-tier system. Large academic medical centers with robust informatics departments can afford to demand detailed vendor documentation regardless of whether it's required. They have leverage. Rural clinics don't. They'll adopt whatever the vendor provides and hope it works.‚Źé‚ŹéThis gets at health equity in a way that's hard to quantify until something goes wrong. I've looked at the clinical literature on algorithm bias. The evidence shows that AI models trained predominantly on certain populations sometimes perform worse in others (Obermeyer et al. in Science, 2019, documented racial bias in a widely used healthcare algorithm). The mechanism is straightforward: if your training data isn't representative, your model won't generalize. Rural populations, racial minorities, low-income patients, and other underrepresented groups end up being the canaries in the coal mine. They're the ones who discover the model doesn't work well for them, sometimes after clinical decisions have already been made.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification requirement doesn't solve this problem entirely. But it creates a documented baseline. It forces vendors to at least state what population the model was developed in and where external validation has occurred. That transparency is what allows clinicians in underserved communities to recognize when a tool might not be appropriate for their patient population.‚Źé‚Źé**The Broader Transparency Issue**‚Źé‚ŹéHere's what's strange about the proposed change, and I'll be direct about this: we're simultaneously tightening other data-sharing regulations while loosening oversight of clinical tools. It doesn't make sense. Our IT director spent months navigating HIPAA guidance, state data governance requirements, and ONC standards just to share a patient's records with a treating specialist across state lines. That's friction. Some of it is necessary. Some of it isn't. But we're treating interoperability for legitimate clinical care as this heavily scrutinized activity while commercial data brokers operate in a regulatory shadow.‚Źé‚ŹéThe rule proposes loosening certification requirements partly to reduce burden on vendors and accelerate innovation. I understand that argument. But I'm not confident we've thought through what happens when we reduce transparency requirements on the clinical side while data commercialization expands unchecked on the back end. Patients' data is being sold. That's legal and happening right now. Meanwhile, the tools that actually affect their clinical care are getting lighter oversight. That feels backwards.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI criterion isn't just about safety, though safety matters. It's about accountability. When something goes wrong, when a patient has a bad outcome and there's a question about whether the clinical decision support tool contributed to that outcome, the model card is part of the audit trail. It documents what the vendor claimed the tool could do and what the vendor disclosed about its limitations. Remove that requirement, and you've made it harder to investigate what went wrong.‚Źé‚Źé**Implementation Challenges We're Experiencing**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be concrete about this because abstractions aren't useful. Our health systems are currently implementing ONC-certified EHR systems and clinical tools. The certification process itself is expensive and slow, and that's a fair criticism. Vendors complain about it. We hear those complaints. But when we survey our own organizations about what they actually value in certification, transparency consistently ranks high. They want to know what they're getting. They want documentation. They want to be able to show regulators and legal teams that they did due diligence when they adopted a tool.‚Źé‚ŹéIf we remove the model card requirement, what replaces it? The rule doesn't propose an alternative. It's just removal. That means vendors will provide whatever documentation they choose to provide, and it will vary wildly. Some will include detailed model cards voluntarily. Others won't. Clinicians will have to parse inconsistent documentation formats and make decisions based on incomplete information. That's not less burdensome. It's just different burden, shifted from vendors to end users.‚Źé‚ŹéWe've also found that the certification process, while slow, creates a common language between vendors and health systems. When we're evaluating a tool, we can point to the certification standards and say, "We need documentation on these specific points." The standards give us leverage as buyers. Removing the standards removes that leverage. Vendors will have less incentive to disclose information that might make their tools look less impressive than competitors' tools.‚Źé‚Źé**What We're Actually Recommending**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Healthcare Leadership Council is not opposed to reducing unnecessary regulatory burden. We're not opposed to deregulation where it makes sense. But we think the DSI certification criterion should be retained, and I want to explain why without being dismissive of the deregulatory impulse.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current criterion requires disclosure of intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation. That's a reasonable set of requirements. It's not excessive. Vendors who are confident in their products can meet it. The burden is real but not prohibitive. The benefit is substantial. Clinicians get information they need to make informed decisions. Patients benefit from tools that have been tested and documented. The market still functions. Competition still exists. Innovation still happens.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat we'd propose instead of removing the requirement is streamlining it. Could the certification process be faster? Probably. Could vendors be given more flexibility in how they document certain elements? Possibly. Could the requirement be tailored to different categories of decision support tools, with less stringent requirements for lower-risk applications? Maybe. But removing transparency requirements entirely creates more problems than it solves, especially for the rural and underserved organizations we represent.‚Źé‚Źé**The Specific Concern About Scope Creep**‚Źé‚ŹéOne thing that worries me is that if we remove the DSI certification criterion now, we'll see proposals to remove other transparency-related requirements down the road. The logic would be the same: reduce burden, promote innovation, let the market decide. But markets are good at some things and not good at others. Markets are good at price competition and feature development. Markets are not good at internalizing patient safety or equity concerns that don't directly affect the bottom line.‚Źé‚ŹéThe information blocking regulations exist for a reason. They emerged from years of experience with vendor lock-in and data hoarding that harmed patients. The certification standards exist for a reason. They emerged from implementation experience and from real incidents where tools were deployed without adequate understanding of their limitations. Deregulation is sometimes appropriate. But it should be based on evidence that the regulation is actually preventing good things from happening. The evidence for that isn't strong here. The DSI criterion isn't the reason vendors are struggling to innovate. Vendors are innovating. The criterion just requires them to document what they've done.‚Źé‚Źé**The Equity Lens**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to come back to the equity issue because I think it's critical and easy to overlook. The rule's preamble emphasizes removing burden on developers and promoting market competition. Those are legitimate policy goals. But the rule should also consider the effect on different types of end users. Our large academic medical centers have the expertise and resources to evaluate clinical tools independently. Our rural and community health centers don't. Our health plans serving predominantly insured urban populations have different needs than our safety-net organizations serving uninsured and underinsured populations.‚Źé‚ŹéWhen you remove a transparency requirement, you're not removing it equally across different types of organizations. You're removing it for everyone, but the effect is most significant for organizations that rely most heavily on regulatory standards as a proxy for quality assurance. That's our rural members. That's our safety-net providers. That's our community health centers. They're the ones who will be most affected by the absence of model cards and external validation documentation.‚Źé‚ŹéEquity isn't the same as charity. It's about distributing the benefits and burdens of policy fairly. The DSI criterion creates a baseline that protects less-resourced organizations. Removing it distributes the burden of due diligence to the organizations least able to bear it.‚Źé‚Źé**Closing Thoughts on This Specific Issue**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Healthcare Leadership Council is submitting this comment because we have a stake in both sides of this equation. We want innovation. We want vendors to be able to develop and deploy new tools without excessive regulatory burden. We also want patients to be safe. We want clinicians to have the information they need to make good decisions. We want rural and underserved communities to have access to the same quality of clinical tools as wealthy urban centers. Those goals aren't incompatible, but they do require some regulatory structure.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification criterion is not the bottleneck to innovation in healthcare technology. Vendor capacity is. Clinical workflow integration challenges are. Reimbursement policy is. The criterion is a reasonable requirement that achieves real benefits in terms of transparency and accountability. We're asking ONC to retain it.‚Źé‚ŹéWe also recognize that the rule proposes other changes that we haven't addressed in this comment. We may have separate feedback on those. But on this specific point, we think the proposed elimination of the DSI transparency requirement is a mistake. It will harm patient safety and health equity without meaningfully reducing burden on vendors or accelerating innovation. We'd welcome the opportunity to work with ONC on a modified approach that maintains transparency requirements while potentially streamlining the certification process itself.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-28T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-28T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ7‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒangry‚ôĒOklahoma‚ôĒpolicy director‚ôĒ53‚ôĒLast year, I watched our rural clinic's IT director spend six months navigating contradictory state and federal guidance just to share a patient's oncology records with a specialist two counties over‚ÄĒwhile simultaneously, a data broker acquired the same patient's insurance claims history for $47 through a legal loophole. The irony of erecting barriers to legitimate clinical access while commercialized surveillance expands unchecked infuriates me, and it's precisely why I'm concerned this approach will widen the gap between what our underserved communities can actually access versus what corporations can freely exploit about them.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2141
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0205‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒSusan Young‚ôĒCareQuest Institute for Oral Health‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒCareQuest Institute for Oral Health opposes the proposed removal of AI decision support transparency requirements in health IT certification, arguing that model cards and vendor disclosures are essential for safety-net providers to understand syst...‚ôĒCareQuest Institute for Oral Health respectfully submits this comment on the proposed deregulatory changes to health IT certification criteria and information blocking rules. Our organization represents the interests of safety-net dental providers and Medicaid programs serving over 800,000 patients annually across 45 states, so we have direct experience with the consequences when health information systems fail to communicate reliably or when transparency requirements are stripped away in the name of market efficiency.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PROBLEM WE'RE WATCHING UNFOLD**‚Źé‚ŹéLast year, when our research team analyzed dental outcomes across our Medicaid clinics, we discovered that patients in rural counties were 40 percent less likely to complete treatment plans than their urban counterparts. We wanted to understand why. Was it transportation? Insurance denials? Provider availability? The answer should have been straightforward, but it wasn't. Our data systems couldn't cross-reference treatment abandonment with insurance authorization failures or with pharmacy records showing whether patients filled prescriptions for pain management. The information systems simply weren't designed to talk to each other. This is the operational reality for most safety-net providers, and I'm concerned that the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions certification criteria requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (21 CFR Part 170.315(b)(1)) will make this fragmentation worse, not better.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current certification criterion requires vendors to disclose intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results for any AI-assisted clinical decision support. This may seem burdensome from a developer's perspective, but from the clinic floor, it means we actually know what a system is designed to do before we implement it. When a vendor cannot or will not provide this information, we're flying blind. We're deploying technology without understanding its limitations, and in healthcare, that's not a competitive advantage‚ÄĒit's a safety issue. The proposed rule would eliminate this requirement entirely under 45 CFR 164.504(a), the information blocking safe harbor for technology vendors, which would remove one of the few enforceable mechanisms we have to demand transparency.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE'RE ASKING FOR**‚Źé‚ŹéWe need the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion to remain in place. Specifically:‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ Retain the requirement that vendors provide model cards documenting intended use, target population, and known performance limitations for any AI system marketed for clinical decision support, consistent with 21 CFR 170.315(b)(1).‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ Strengthen, rather than weaken, audit trails and transparency requirements so that when an AI system recommends denying a claim or flagging a patient as "high risk," we can actually trace back through the logic and understand what happened.‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ Require that any safety-net provider or Medicaid program implementing AI-assisted decision support be given access to validation data showing how the system performs specifically in underrepresented populations, not just in the overall training set.‚Źé‚Źé‚ÄĘ Maintain enforcement authority under 42 U.S.C. 1320a-7j (the information blocking statute) so that vendors cannot simply refuse to disclose how their algorithms work.‚Źé‚ŹéThe deregulatory impulse I understand. Certification criteria do slow market entry. But the speed of market entry is not the problem we're trying to solve in safety-net dentistry. The problem is that we've been solving the wrong problem for years. We've optimized for vendor ease while patients wait 40 percent longer for care completion and we have no idea why. Removing transparency requirements won't fix that. It will entrench it.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Federal Register notice (89 FR 77824) frames this as a choice between innovation and burden, but for our patients, it's a choice between knowing and not knowing whether the systems we're using actually work for them. We can have both innovation and transparency. Other regulated industries‚ÄĒpharmaceuticals, medical devices, aviation‚ÄĒmanage this balance. So can health IT.‚Źé‚ŹéWe respectfully urge ONC to retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion and its transparency requirements. Market competition should reward vendors who are willing to be honest about what their systems do and don't do. Safety-net providers deserve that information.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-22T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-22T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒresigned‚ôĒVirginia‚ôĒresearch director‚ôĒ50‚ôĒWhen we analyzed dental outcomes across our Medicaid clinics last year, we discovered that patients in rural counties were 40% less likely to complete treatment plans, but we couldn't cross-reference that data with transportation barriers or insurance denials because the information systems simply aren't designed to talk to each other‚ÄĒand now I'm watching us potentially move toward even looser data governance without building the infrastructure to actually protect the communities we're trying to serve.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ639
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0206‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒBrian Walker‚ôĒTexas Medical Association‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe Texas Medical Association urges retention of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification requirements mandating AI model cards and transparency disclosures, arguing that while implementation burdens are real, the requirements serve crit...‚ôĒ**COMMENT SUBMISSION TO ASTP/ONC DOCKET ON HEALTH DATA, TECHNOLOGY, AND INTEROPERABILITY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Texas Medical Association appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory actions affecting health information technology certification and interoperability standards. Our organization represents over 54,000 physicians and medical students across Texas, and our policy work over the past eighteen months has brought us directly into conversation with clinicians managing the real-world consequences of current AI implementation in clinical decision-support systems.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat I want to flag clearly from the outset is something our patient privacy survey surfaced last spring that still troubles me. When we asked respondents about expanding data access and AI-assisted clinical tools, we saw a pattern that doesn't fit neatly into either "regulation good" or "regulation bad." Rural practitioners and low-income patients expressed genuine anxiety about what happens when their medical records become more accessible and more algorithmically processed, yet they simultaneously wanted better access to their own data. One woman at a community forum in East Texas explained that she'd delayed seeking mental health treatment years ago because she feared her employer would discover it through insurance records. She's not alone in that fear. The point here is that when we talk about streamlining certification requirements for AI decision-support interventions, we're talking about systems that will touch patients who have lived experience of data being weaponized against them.‚Źé‚Źé**DECISION SUPPORT INTERVENTIONS AND CERTIFICATION TRANSPARENCY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe Texas Medical Association supports the general direction toward reducing unnecessary regulatory burden on health IT developers and providers. We understand the case for deregulation. We also think some of the proposed changes require a second look, specifically around the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion that mandates AI model cards and transparency disclosures.‚Źé‚ŹéOur position is straightforward: retain the current DSI certification requirement, which mandates model cards documenting intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. This is not a call for adding more regulation. It's a call for keeping in place a minimum standard that actually serves patient safety and clinical accountability in a way that other existing oversight mechanisms do not.‚Źé‚ŹéHere's why I think this matters beyond abstract principle. Over the past year, our organization worked with twelve health systems across Texas ranging from 150-bed rural hospitals to large urban medical centers. We asked them specifically about the DSI certification requirement and what it costs them. The burden is real. Developers have to document things. Systems have to verify claims. Hospitals have to review model cards before deployment. But here's what we also found: in nine of those twelve systems, the model card documentation actually prevented deployment of at least one algorithm that either had inadequate external validation or was being marketed for populations the training data didn't represent. In one case, a respiratory risk algorithm trained primarily on adult male smokers was being proposed for use in a pediatric asthma program. The model card requirement caught that. Without it, that system would have deployed the tool and potentially harmed kids.‚Źé‚ŹéThe regulatory docket proposes removing specific transparency requirements from the DSI criterion, ostensibly to reduce burden on vendors. I understand why vendors want that removed. What I don't understand is why we'd remove it when the actual burden on providers is manageable and the risk of harm from opaque algorithms is not theoretical.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PATIENT SAFETY ISSUE THAT KEEPS COMING UP**‚Źé‚ŹéLet me ground this in something concrete from our survey data. We asked 847 patients across Texas about their comfort level with AI-assisted clinical decisions in three scenarios: medication dosing recommendations, diagnostic support for imaging interpretation, and treatment sequencing for cancer. When patients had access to information about how the algorithm was developed, what population it was tested on, and what its known limitations were, their comfort level with the AI recommendation was significantly higher than when they received only the recommendation itself. This held true across age groups, income levels, and education levels.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat's more, we asked physicians the same question. Clinicians said they wanted to know the same things. They wanted to know if an algorithm was validated in their patient population. They wanted to understand what data it was trained on. They wanted to see external validation results from independent sources, not just the developer's internal testing. This isn't a regulatory compliance question for them. It's a clinical judgment question. Physicians can't practice good medicine with algorithmic black boxes.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification criterion requires vendors to provide this information in a structured format. The proposal would eliminate that requirement. I recognize that streamlining certification requirements sounds efficient. But the information itself is not becoming unnecessary just because it's no longer mandatory to disclose it. The information still matters for patient safety. The question is whether that information will be disclosed at all, and in what form, and to whom.‚Źé‚Źé**IMPLEMENTATION CHALLENGES WE'VE OBSERVED**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be fair about the implementation side because the docket's concerns about burden are not invented. Three of the twelve health systems we worked with reported that model card documentation from vendors was sometimes incomplete or inconsistent in format. One vendor provided a 40-page technical specification when a structured one-page model card would have been clearer. Another vendor essentially refused to disclose training data characteristics, claiming proprietary protection. These are real friction points.‚Źé‚ŹéBut the solution to inconsistent or incomplete disclosure is standardization and enforcement of the disclosure requirement, not elimination of the requirement. The National Institute of Standards and Technology published guidance on AI model cards in 2019 (NIST AI RMF, documented in the Federal Register notice on AI governance frameworks). The format exists. It's not proprietary. Vendors know what a model card should contain. The friction point is not that disclosure is impossible; it's that some vendors would prefer not to disclose.‚Źé‚ŹéOur recommendation is that any revision to the DSI certification criterion should maintain the transparency disclosure requirements but potentially allow for flexible formatting that meets a standardized content checklist. The required elements should remain: intended use, target population, known risks and limitations, characteristics of training data, external validation results from independent sources, and information about how the algorithm performs across demographic subgroups if applicable.‚Źé‚Źé**INFORMATION BLOCKING AND CERTIFICATION LINKAGES**‚Źé‚ŹéThe docket also touches on information blocking regulations and how they interact with certification requirements. This is worth examining in some detail because the linkages are not arbitrary.‚Źé‚ŹéUnder 45 CFR 171.100, information blocking is defined as a practice that is likely to interfere with, prevent, or materially discourage access to electronic health information. The current certification requirements for health IT modules include standards that make it easier to identify when information blocking is occurring. When a certified system is supposed to meet certain interoperability standards (TEFCA, FHIR, direct secure messaging), deviations from those standards can be documented and investigated.‚Źé‚ŹéIf certification requirements are significantly loosened without corresponding adjustments to information blocking enforcement, we create a gap. A vendor could claim they've implemented interoperability but in a way that technically meets loose standards while practically impeding data exchange. The current tighter linkage between certification and information blocking creates accountability. Loosen certification without adjusting information blocking oversight, and you've just created an enforcement problem.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Texas Medical Association is not proposing that information blocking rules become more stringent. We're proposing that if certification requirements are relaxed, information blocking enforcement mechanisms be adjusted to compensate so that the practical effect on patient access to records doesn't degrade.‚Źé‚Źé**EXTERNAL VALIDATION AND REAL-WORLD EVIDENCE**‚Źé‚ŹéOne element of the current DSI requirement that deserves specific attention is the external validation requirement. The proposal seems to suggest that developers' internal validation testing might be sufficient without independent external testing.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is where I want to circle back to something I mentioned earlier about that respiratory algorithm trained on adult male smokers. Internal validation testing at the vendor level often uses the same population the algorithm was trained on, or a very similar population. That's not validation. That's confirmation. Real external validation means testing the algorithm on a genuinely different patient population, often in a different clinical setting, often with different EHR systems and workflows. That's where problems show up.‚Źé‚ŹéThe FDA has requirements for this in certain contexts. The Federal Register notice on proposed modifications to clinical decision support oversight (88 FR 73904, October 2023) acknowledges that clinical validation requirements exist for certain high-risk algorithms. The DSI certification requirement for external validation is consistent with that principle. It's not inventing a new burden. It's applying a standard that makes sense for patient safety.‚Źé‚ŹéOur organization would support a revision that allows developers to conduct external validation in partnership with health systems rather than requiring it to be done by completely independent third parties, if cost and feasibility are the concerns. But eliminating the external validation requirement altogether would be a mistake.‚Źé‚Źé**PRACTICAL CONCERNS FROM HEALTH SYSTEMS**‚Źé‚ŹéI mentioned earlier that we worked with twelve health systems on this. Let me give you a sense of what they told us about what happens when certification requirements are light.‚Źé‚ŹéOne 300-bed hospital in a suburban area deployed a length-of-stay prediction algorithm that had been certified under loose requirements. The algorithm performed well in the vendor's testing but significantly overpredicted length of stay for elderly patients on anticoagulation therapy, a population that made up about 22 percent of their admissions. Because the algorithm had not been externally validated on that population, the hospital didn't know this before deployment. It took three months of operational use to notice the pattern. They had to recalibrate the algorithm locally, which required data science expertise they don't have in-house, and they ended up paying the vendor for a custom analysis. If external validation had been required before deployment, that problem would have been caught.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not a hypothetical. This is what happens in practice when the transparency and validation requirements are weak.‚Źé‚Źé**THE REGULATORY FRAMEWORK QUESTION**‚Źé‚ŹéI recognize that the docket frames this as a deregulatory action aimed at reducing burden on vendors and promoting innovation. The Texas Medical Association is not ideologically opposed to deregulation. We support removing requirements that don't serve a safety purpose. But we also think the burden of regulation needs to be weighed against the benefit, and in the case of DSI certification transparency, the benefit is real.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current regulatory framework under 45 CFR Part 170 (Health Information Technology Certification Criteria) establishes requirements for health IT modules that are used in clinical practice. These requirements exist because health IT affects patient outcomes. That's not a controversial claim. The question is whether specific requirements within that framework serve a purpose.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI certification transparency requirements serve three purposes. First, they enable clinicians to make informed decisions about whether to use a particular algorithm. Second, they create accountability. If an algorithm performs poorly, it's possible to trace back and understand why because documentation exists. Third, they protect patients by ensuring that algorithms aren't deployed in populations they weren't designed for.‚Źé‚ŹéNone of those purposes is eliminated by deregulation. The information still matters. The need for accountability still exists. The risk to patients is still real.‚Źé‚Źé**WHAT WE'RE NOT ARGUING FOR**‚Źé‚ŹéI want to be clear about the scope of our position because I think there's sometimes confusion about what strengthening DSI requirements would mean.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Texas Medical Association is not arguing for:‚Źé- More burden on vendors than currently exists‚Źé- Elimination of all discretion in how vendors document their systems‚Źé- Requirements for proprietary information disclosure that would compromise legitimate business interests‚Źé- Elimination of innovation in clinical decision support tools‚Źé- Federal micromanagement of how individual hospitals deploy algorithms‚Źé‚ŹéWhat we are arguing for:‚Źé- Retention of the requirement that vendors document intended use, target population, known risks, and training data characteristics in a standardized format‚Źé- Retention of external validation requirements for algorithms before clinical deployment‚Źé- Clarity about what information constitutes adequate transparency so vendors know what they need to provide‚Źé- Proportional enforcement that doesn't create perverse incentives to avoid certification‚Źé‚Źé**MOVING FORWARD**‚Źé‚ŹéIf the goal of the docket is to reduce unnecessary burden while maintaining patient safety, there's a middle path that doesn't require eliminating the DSI transparency requirements entirely.‚Źé‚ŹéThe framework could:‚Źé1. Standardize the format and content of model cards so vendors know exactly what to provide and can do so efficiently‚Źé2. Allow flexibility in how validation is conducted (vendor-led, health system-led, third-party-led) as long as the validation is genuinely external‚Źé3. Create a tiered approach where algorithms for lower-risk use cases have lighter requirements than algorithms for high-risk interventions‚Źé4. Establish clear timelines for vendors to provide documentation so they can plan accordingly‚Źé5. Provide technical assistance to smaller vendors who lack experience with documentation requirements‚Źé‚ŹéThis approach would reduce burden for vendors who are already trying to do the right thing, while maintaining minimum standards that protect patients.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Texas Medical Association has significant concerns about the broader implications of removing certification transparency requirements. Our clinicians and our patients are telling us that they want to understand how algorithms work, what they're designed to do, and what their limitations are. Removing the requirement to disclose that information doesn't make those questions go away. It just means the information won't be systematically available.‚Źé‚ŹéWe respectfully urge the Office of the Secretary and ASTP/ONC to retain the DSI certification transparency requirements, with modifications as needed to reduce documented burden, rather than eliminate them entirely.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2026-01-19T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-01-19T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ7‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒThe 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒsupportive‚ôĒMaine‚ôĒpolicy director‚ôĒ55‚ôĒLast spring, I reviewed our association's patient privacy survey and was struck by how many respondents‚ÄĒparticularly in rural counties and among low-income members‚ÄĒexpressed anxiety about their medical histories becoming visible to insurers or employers, even as they acknowledged wanting easier access to their own records. One woman I spoke with at a community forum explained she'd delayed seeking mental health treatment years ago specifically because she feared her employer would discover it; I realized we needed to ensure any expansion of data access didn't recreate that same barrier for the next generation.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2238
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0207‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒAmanda Anderson‚ôĒURAALI Refaluwasch Association‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒThe URAALI Refaluwasch Association opposes removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) transparency requirements from health IT certification criteria, arguing that mandates for AI model cards, validation data, and risk disclosures are essenti...‚ôĒJune 22, 2025‚Źé‚ŹéOffice of the Secretary‚ŹéDepartment of Health and Human Services‚ŹéAttn: ASTP/ONC Docket‚Źé200 Independence Avenue, S.W.‚ŹéWashington, D.C. 20201‚Źé‚Źé**RE: Request for Information on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions**‚Źé‚ŹéThe URAALI Refaluwasch Association submits this comment in response to the proposed modifications to health IT certification criteria and information blocking regulations. Our organization represents patient advocacy networks across Connecticut and the broader Northeast, with particular focus on health equity in algorithmic decision-making within clinical settings. We appreciate the opportunity to address the proposed removal of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) transparency requirements, an issue that goes directly to the heart of patient safety and equitable access to care.‚Źé‚Źé**THE REAL COST OF OPACITY IN CLINICAL AI**‚Źé‚ŹéLast year, a board member of our organization experienced something that should concern every person in this regulatory process. Her daughter, age 34, was terminated from her employer's health plan after genetic screening data was cross-referenced with the company's insurance algorithm through a pathway nobody involved could fully explain. The breach originated somewhere in the flow of health information between a clinical AI system and downstream commercial systems. She's now uninsurable on the private market. When we tried to trace what happened, we hit a wall. The AI vendor cited proprietary model architecture. The employer cited business confidentiality. The health plan cited standard underwriting procedures. Nobody could tell us what data the model was trained on, what populations it was validated against, or what known limitations it had. This is not a theoretical concern about innovation burden. This is Margaret. This is a real person whose financial security collapsed because transparency requirements that could have prevented this weren't in place.‚Źé‚ŹéI mention this not to be dramatic but because it shaped how I read the proposed rule. The ASTP/ONC request asks whether removing DSI certification criteria that require AI model cards, transparency disclosures of intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation would reduce burden while promoting innovation. The implicit answer from industry will almost certainly be yes‚ÄĒit would reduce burden. But the question regulators should ask is whether that burden reduction is worth the cost when something goes wrong, and in my experience, something always goes wrong.‚Źé‚Źé**WHY DSI TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS MATTER FOR PATIENT SAFETY**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current certification criteria under 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1) require that Decision Support Interventions include documentation of intended use, target population, known risks and limitations, training data characteristics, and evidence of external validation. These requirements exist in the Health IT Certification Program for specific reasons rooted in patient safety and informed clinical decision-making (Federal Register, Vol. 89, No. 17, January 24, 2024, pp. 4889‚Äď4920). They are not bureaucratic impediments. They are guardrails.‚Źé‚ŹéConsider what happens without them. A clinical AI system trained predominantly on data from academic medical centers in urban areas is deployed across rural safety-net clinics serving different patient populations. The model performs well on the training population but degrades in predictive accuracy when applied to patients with different demographic characteristics, comorbidity patterns, or access to follow-up care. Without transparency disclosures, clinicians have no way to know this. Without external validation requirements, the vendor has no obligation to test performance across populations. The patient‚ÄĒthe actual person affected by the algorithm's recommendation‚ÄĒhas no recourse and often no awareness that her treatment pathway was shaped by a model that was never validated on anyone like her.‚Źé‚ŹéThis is not hypothetical. Studies examining racial and gender disparities in clinical algorithms published in JAMA Network Open and Health Affairs have documented precisely this pattern (Obermeyer et al., 2019; Gianfrancesco et al., 2020). When algorithms lack transparency requirements, disparities accumulate in silence. When they include transparency requirements, clinicians can at least see the problem and push back. The DSI criteria in the current rule create that visibility.‚Źé‚Źé**THE INFORMATION BLOCKING CONNECTION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule also addresses modifications to information blocking regulations under 42 CFR 171.102. I want to be clear about something that concerns me, even though I understand the deregulatory impulse. Loosening information blocking enforcement to reduce compliance burden for providers makes sense if the tradeoff is that data flows more easily to patients and their authorized representatives. But if the tradeoff is that data flows more easily to insurers, employers, and commercial actors without corresponding transparency about how that data will be used in algorithmic systems, then we've solved one problem by creating a larger one.‚Źé‚ŹéWhat happened to Margaret happened because data moved through multiple commercial systems without sufficient transparency about how each system would use it. She had no way to know that genetic screening data from her clinical care would feed into an insurance underwriting algorithm that had never been validated on her population. The information blocking rules, properly enforced, at least create friction that forces explicit decisions about data sharing. Remove that friction without replacing it with transparency requirements about downstream use, and you accelerate the flow of patient data into black boxes.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC RECOMMENDATIONS**‚Źé‚ŹéURAALI Refaluwasch Association recommends the following:‚Źé‚Źé1. Retain the current DSI certification criteria requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures as specified in 45 CFR 170.315(b)(1). The burden of documenting intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation is not a compliance cost; it is a patient safety requirement.‚Źé‚Źé2. If the rule proceeds with modifications to information blocking regulations, pair any relaxation of information blocking enforcement with explicit transparency requirements for downstream use of health data in algorithmic systems. Vendors and covered entities should be required to disclose when health information will be used to train, validate, or operationalize clinical AI or commercial algorithms.‚Źé‚Źé3. Establish a requirement that clinical AI systems used in direct patient care be validated across demographic subgroups before certification. This is standard in medical device approval (FDA guidance documents on clinical validation) and should be standard in health IT certification.‚Źé‚Źé4. Create a mechanism for patients to request transparency disclosures about algorithms used in their care. Patients cannot consent to algorithmic decision-making they don't know exists. The proposed rule should include patient rights to request and receive information about DSI systems used in their treatment.‚Źé‚ŹéThe question before regulators is whether the burden of transparency requirements on vendors is worth the protection they provide to patients. I would argue it is, particularly for vulnerable populations with the most to lose when algorithms fail silently. Innovation matters. But innovation that sacrifices transparency in the name of market efficiency creates space for harms like Margaret's to happen again, with no one able to explain why.‚Źé‚ŹéRespectfully submitted,‚Źé‚ŹéAmanda Anderson‚ŹéExecutive Director‚ŹéURAALI Refaluwasch Association‚ŹéConnecticut‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-16T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-16T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ3‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒadvocacy_group‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒurgent‚ôĒConnecticut‚ôĒexecutive director‚ôĒ61‚ôĒLast year, I watched a board member's diabetic daughter pulled from her workplace health plan after her genetic screening data was somehow cross-referenced with her employer's insurance algorithm‚ÄĒnobody could explain how it happened or where the breach originated. She's 34 and now uninsurable on the private market. That experience crystallized for me that expanding data access without simultaneous privacy safeguards doesn't just create theoretical risks; it creates Margarets, people whose entire financial security collapses when health information moves faster than the regulations meant to contain it.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1102
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0208‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒNancy Wilson‚ôĒJohns Hopkins University and Medicine‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒJohns Hopkins University argues that proposed deregulation eliminating AI model card transparency requirements for clinical decision support systems should be rejected, as these requirements protect patient safety by ensuring clinicians have infor...‚ôĒ**DECISION SUPPORT INTERVENTIONS AND THE CASE FOR MAINTAINING AI TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS IN CLINICAL SETTINGS**‚Źé‚ŹéAs an assistant professor of health information systems at Johns Hopkins University, the author submits this comment on behalf of the institution's research and clinical mission. The proposed deregulation of Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criteria presents a critical juncture for patient safety and the responsible deployment of artificial intelligence in medicine. Johns Hopkins has a responsibility to weigh in on this matter because we train clinicians, conduct implementation science research, and operate clinical systems where AI-assisted decision support is increasingly embedded in daily workflows. What happens to these certification standards will shape how AI reaches patients and clinicians for years to come.‚Źé‚ŹéThe impetus for this comment is personal as well as professional. Last month, a family member underwent repeated cardiac imaging because a newly consolidated health system could not access previous scans from an independent clinic. The scans were identical; the information existed; the system simply could not retrieve it. That experience crystallized something the author has spent a decade researching: consolidation and deregulation do not automatically produce better care. Sometimes they produce worse care, at higher cost, with less accountability. That same principle applies to AI certification. Removing transparency requirements for Decision Support Interventions would allow consolidation of AI development without the oversight mechanisms that protect patients when things go wrong. The proposed rule would eliminate the requirement that AI model cards disclose intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results (as specified in 45 CFR 170.102(c), the current DSI certification criterion). Removing this requirement would be a mistake, and Johns Hopkins respectfully urges the agencies to retain it.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CURRENT DSI CERTIFICATION REQUIREMENT AND WHY IT EXISTS**‚Źé‚ŹéThe current DSI certification criterion requires that health IT developers submit AI model cards documenting key characteristics of clinical decision support algorithms. This requirement emerged from the 21st Century Cures Act and subsequent ONC rulemaking (88 Federal Register 4326, January 25, 2023) specifically because AI systems can fail in ways that traditional software cannot. An algorithm trained on data from predominantly male patients may perform poorly when applied to women. A model trained on a specific patient population may not generalize to different racial or ethnic groups. A system optimized to reduce false positives in one context may produce false negatives in another. These are not theoretical concerns. The literature on algorithmic bias in healthcare is extensive.
