A 2019 study in Nature Medicine found that a widely used algorithm for allocating healthcare resources systematically underestimated the healthcare needs of Black patients, leading to less aggressive preventive care (Obermeyer et al., Nature Medicine 25, 1118-1124, 2019). A 2021 analysis in JAMA found that commercial sepsis prediction algorithms performed differently across racial groups, with implications for treatment decisions (Gianfrancesco et al., JAMA Internal Medicine 181, 1611-1619, 2021). These are not outliers. They represent the normal state of AI systems deployed without adequate transparency and validation.‚Źé‚ŹéThe DSI model card requirement addresses this by requiring developers to disclose what their system was designed to do, who it was tested on, what it might get wrong, and how it was validated outside the development environment. This is not an onerous burden. It is documentation that any responsible AI developer should produce anyway. The requirement does not mandate specific algorithms, prohibit innovation, or prevent competition. It simply requires transparency. When a clinician uses AI to recommend a dosing decision or a diagnostic test, that clinician should know whether the system was validated in a population similar to the patient in front of them. A cardiologist should know if a chest pain risk model was trained primarily on data from younger men. A hospitalist should know if a sepsis alert was less accurate in Black patients. Removing the model card requirement would eliminate these disclosures, leaving clinicians and patients without information they need to make informed decisions.‚Źé‚Źé**PATIENT SAFETY, ACCOUNTABILITY, AND THE LIMITS OF MARKET-DRIVEN INNOVATION**‚Źé‚ŹéThe proposed rule assumes that removing certification requirements will spur innovation and competition. This assumption deserves scrutiny. In some markets, deregulation does increase competition. In healthcare, however, deregulation has often produced consolidation, not competition. The author's recent research on health information system adoption (published in Health Affairs in 2024) found that smaller health systems and independent clinics are increasingly unable to compete with large integrated systems when it comes to AI and interoperability, not because of regulatory burden but because of the cost and complexity of developing or integrating these systems. Removing certification requirements will not change this dynamic. A large integrated health system with resources to develop proprietary AI systems will benefit from the removal of transparency standards because it can deploy systems without external scrutiny. A smaller clinic or independent provider will struggle even more because they will have fewer options and less information about the systems they are forced to adopt.‚Źé‚ŹéMore fundamentally, the DSI certification requirement serves a function that markets alone cannot provide: accountability. When a patient is harmed by an AI system that was not validated for their population, who is responsible? If the model card has been removed, the paper trail of what the developer knew about the system's limitations is gone. The clinician cannot claim they were unaware of a known limitation if the model card never disclosed it. The developer cannot claim the system was not designed for a particular use case if they never documented what it was designed for. The model card requirement is a form of accountability infrastructure. Removing it is equivalent to allowing manufacturers to sell medications without labeling the side effects or contraindications. Innovation in that context is not progress; it is risk-shifting onto patients.‚Źé‚Źé**EXTERNAL VALIDATION AND THE REAL-WORLD DEPLOYMENT PROBLEM**‚Źé‚ŹéOne of the most important elements of the current DSI requirement is external validation. The model card must document not only how the system performed during development but how it performed when tested on data the developers did not see, ideally in a different health system or patient population. This is a standard practice in the machine learning literature and in clinical research. A drug is not approved based only on the manufacturer's internal trials; the FDA requires independent verification. A medical device is not approved based only on the developer's own testing; the FDA requires external validation data. Yet the proposed rule would eliminate this requirement for AI-assisted clinical decision support. Why? The rationale offered in the ASTP/ONC notice (89 Federal Register [page number to be confirmed in final rule]) is that external validation is burdensome and slows deployment. This is true. It is also true that external validation catches problems that internal testing misses. A model that performs well on a developer's own data may perform poorly in a different health system, with different electronic health record systems, different patient populations, and different clinical workflows. Without external validation, these problems are discovered only after the system is deployed at scale, affecting real patients.‚Źé‚ŹéThe author's institution has experience with this problem. Johns Hopkins operates multiple clinical sites with different patient demographics and clinical workflows. When Johns Hopkins implements a clinical decision support system, the system sometimes performs differently across sites. A risk prediction model trained on data from Hopkins' main hospital may not perform as well at a community hospital site. An alert system optimized for the main hospital's workflow may fire at different rates at satellite locations. These differences are discoverable through testing but only if someone actually tests the system before deploying it at scale. The external validation requirement in the DSI criterion is designed to prevent the problem of deploying systems that work in development but fail in practice. Removing it would shift risk from developers onto patients and clinicians.‚Źé‚Źé**THE CASE FOR RETAINING THE REQUIREMENT**‚Źé‚ŹéJohns Hopkins University and Medicine respectfully urges the Department of Health and Human Services and the Office of the National Coordinator to retain the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion requiring AI model cards with disclosure of intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. This requirement does not impede innovation. It requires transparency. Transparency is not the enemy of competition; it is the foundation of informed decision-making, both for clinicians adopting systems and for patients whose care those systems will influence.‚Źé‚ŹéThe deregulation agenda is motivated by the belief that removing requirements will accelerate innovation and lower costs. In some contexts, this is true. In the deployment of AI systems in clinical settings, however, the relevant question is not whether deregulation will accelerate innovation but whether it will improve patient care. A faster deployment of a less transparent, less validated system is not progress. The cost savings realized by eliminating validation requirements will be offset by the cost of managing adverse events that could have been caught through external validation. The competitive advantage gained by developers who can skip the validation step will be offset by the liability exposure when systems fail in ways that documented validation would have revealed.‚Źé‚ŹéJohns Hopkins is prepared to work with ONC and CMS on streamlining the validation process, reducing unnecessary burden, and accelerating the deployment of safe, effective clinical decision support systems. But this work should proceed by making validation easier and faster, not by eliminating it. The commitment to patient safety and clinician autonomy that the DSI requirement represents is too important to abandon in pursuit of deregulation as an end in itself.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-12T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-12T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ5‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒRemoving transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.‚ôĒacademic‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒhopeful‚ôĒSouth Dakota‚ôĒassistant professor‚ôĒ61‚ôĒMy daughter called me last month in tears because her cardiologist's office‚ÄĒpart of a newly consolidated health system‚ÄĒcouldn't access her previous imaging from the independent clinic where she'd been treated for two years, forcing her to repeat expensive scans that her insurance won't cover twice. As someone who spends my days designing studies on health information systems, I found myself on the other side of the research question, watching firsthand how the very consolidation trends we're supposed to be analyzing play out in real family crises.‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTrue‚ôĒ‚ôĒ1573
CMS-2025-0050-SYNTH-0209‚ôĒCMS-2025-0050‚ôĒRobert Hernandez‚ôĒDoximity, Inc.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒDoximity argues that while the current Decision Support Interventions certification requirement for AI/ML-based clinical decision support creates genuine compliance burdens for smaller vendors, eliminating it entirely would compromise patient safe...‚ôĒ**RE: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity (RFI)**‚Źé‚ŹéOn behalf of Doximity, Inc., I am submitting this comment in response to the Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology's request for information regarding proposed modifications to the 21st Century Cures Act certification requirements. Doximity operates one of the largest digital health networks in the United States, connecting over two million healthcare professionals across all fifty states. As a Chief Medical Officer, I have direct visibility into how our clinical users depend on health IT systems that are both innovative and trustworthy. I want to address a specific concern: the proposal to relax certification requirements for Decision Support Interventions, particularly those involving artificial intelligence and machine learning algorithms.‚Źé‚Źé**THE PROBLEM WE NEED TO SOLVE CAREFULLY**‚Źé‚ŹéI understand the impulse behind the deregulatory approach. I watched it firsthand last month when a small vendor we had been piloting with decided to shut down their EHR innovation work entirely. They were drowning in certification costs. The compliance documentation effort exceeded their actual product development budget. It was frustrating because their clinical creativity was genuine, their team was talented, and the market could have benefited from their work. At the same time, I have watched that same company's decision paralyze our clinicians who had started integrating their decision support tools into their workflows. When the vendor withdrew, we had to scramble to manage patient safety concerns during the transition. That experience taught me something important: the burden is real, but so is the risk if we reduce oversight without thinking through what we are giving up.‚Źé‚ŹéThe current Decision Support Interventions certification criterion, codified in 45 CFR 170.315(a)(4), requires vendors to document model cards with information about intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and external validation results. This requirement exists because AI-assisted clinical decision-making is fundamentally different from traditional health IT. A drug interaction checker operates on deterministic logic. An AI model that recommends treatment escalation for sepsis operates on patterns learned from data, and those patterns can fail in ways we do not always anticipate. In 2023, when the FDA published guidance on modifications to AI/ML-based software as a medical device, they emphasized the importance of transparency and ongoing monitoring specifically because the risk profile of these systems evolves (FDA Guidance on Modifications to AI/ML-Based SaMD, January 2023). Our clinical teams need to understand what they are working with.‚Źé‚Źé**WHERE THE CURRENT FRAMEWORK HAS VALUE**‚Źé‚ŹéLet me be direct: I do not think the answer is to eliminate the DSI certification criterion. I think the answer is to make compliance more achievable for smaller vendors while keeping the substance of what we are trying to accomplish. The model card requirement, for example, does not require vendors to conduct original research or hire armies of data scientists. It requires transparency about what was done, what the limitations are, and what the clinical team should watch for. That is basic professional responsibility in healthcare. When I review our own decision support implementations, I want to know: What patient populations were included in the training data? Were there systematic differences between the population we trained on and the population we are using this in now? What happened when the model was tested on external data? These are not bureaucratic questions. They are the questions a responsible clinician asks before adopting any new tool.‚Źé‚ŹéThe Federal Register notice for the proposed rule (88 FR 41350) suggested that smaller vendors find the current certification pathway prohibitively expensive. I believe that is true, and it is a genuine problem. But the solution should not be to remove the requirement for transparency about AI model characteristics. The solution should be to streamline the pathway to compliance. For instance, vendors could be allowed to use industry-standard model card templates, perhaps aligned with work from organizations like the Partnership on AI or the National Institute of Standards and Technology. Vendors could be permitted to leverage external validation studies conducted by other organizations rather than being required to conduct their own. There are ways to reduce the compliance burden without reducing the clinical safety assurance.‚Źé‚Źé**SPECIFIC CONCERNS ABOUT REMOVING THIS REQUIREMENT**‚Źé‚ŹéIf we eliminate or substantially weaken the DSI certification criterion, several things happen. First, clinicians lose a structured source of information about how AI systems work. Right now, when a health system adopts a new decision support tool, the certification process creates a paper trail. The vendor has documented what they know and what they do not know. That documentation becomes part of the clinical governance process. Without it, clinicians are left to trust vendor marketing materials or to conduct their own validation work in parallel. Smaller health systems often lack the resources to do that kind of validation. Larger health systems can hire informaticists and data scientists to audit tools. Smaller clinics cannot. Removing the requirement for model transparency widens that gap.‚Źé‚ŹéSecond, we lose a mechanism for accountability. If a clinical decision support system produces consistently biased recommendations for a particular population, how do we find out? We find out through clinical incident reporting, through adverse events, or through the kind of diligent post-implementation monitoring that good health systems do. But the certification requirement creates a baseline expectation: vendors have thought about bias, they have looked for it, they have documented what they found. That baseline exists in writing. Without it, vendors have no obligation to think about bias at all, and health systems have no standard framework for understanding whether a vendor has done so.‚Źé‚ŹéThird, there is a competitive dynamic I worry about. Large health IT vendors with established market share can afford to maintain high internal standards for AI safety even without a regulatory requirement. They have compliance teams, quality assurance processes, and reputational concerns. Smaller vendors who are trying to break into the market may cut corners on safety documentation if it is not required. In a deregulated environment, the vendors who invest in transparency might be undercut by vendors who do not. That is not a theoretical concern. It is exactly what happened in other regulated industries when oversight was relaxed. The vendors who maintained standards were penalized competitively.‚Źé‚Źé**A MIDDLE GROUND APPROACH**‚Źé‚ŹéWhat I want to propose is this: maintain the core requirement that vendors provide model card documentation for AI-based decision support, but streamline the implementation pathway. Specifically, ONC could adopt a tiered approach. For lower-risk applications (decision support that provides information to clinicians but does not directly trigger clinical actions), vendors could use a simplified model card template. For higher-risk applications (automated clinical alerts, dosing recommendations, patient triage algorithms), vendors would use a more detailed template. Both tiers would require documentation of intended use, target population, and known limitations. But the burden would be proportionate to the risk.‚Źé‚ŹéAdditionally, ONC could establish a registry of pre-approved external validation studies. If a vendor's AI model has been validated by a reputable research institution or by another health system and that validation has been published or made available through the registry, the vendor should not need to conduct their own validation. This would reduce duplication of effort while maintaining assurance that models have been tested beyond the training data.‚Źé‚ŹéFinally, ONC could work with vendors and health systems to develop open-source tools that make model card documentation easier. Right now, each vendor creates their own documentation format. If there were a standardized, user-friendly template tool, the compliance burden would drop significantly. This approach mirrors what CMS has done with quality reporting measures. They provide standardized eCQM specifications, which reduces the burden on vendors and providers while maintaining data quality.‚Źé‚Źé**THE BROADER CONTEXT**‚Źé‚ŹéI recognize that the deregulatory agenda has legitimate merit in some areas. Reducing unnecessary standards duplication, streamlining interoperability requirements, and allowing vendors more flexibility in how they meet clinical outcomes makes sense. Health IT innovation has been constrained by accumulated regulatory complexity. But AI decision support is not an area where we should move quickly toward less oversight. It is an area where we should move toward smarter oversight. The distinction matters.‚Źé‚ŹéONC has established itself as a thoughtful regulator precisely because it listens to industry feedback and adjusts requirements when they are genuinely burdensome while protecting patient safety. The DSI criterion, as currently written, can be made more efficient without being eliminated. I would urge the agency to consider that path forward rather than removing the requirement entirely.‚Źé‚ŹéThe healthcare professionals who use our platforms trust us to build systems that are safe, effective, and transparent. That trust is not negotiable, and it should not be sacrificed in the name of deregulation. The vendor I mentioned at the beginning, the one that shut down their innovation work, did so partly because of compliance costs. But they also did so because they could not compete with larger vendors in a deregulated market. If we want to see small vendors thrive and innovate, we need to reduce barriers. Removing oversight of AI decision support is not the right barrier to remove. Making compliance achievable for smaller vendors is the right goal, and there are ways to get there that do not require us to give up on transparency and safety.‚ôĒ‚ôĒ2025-12-21T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-21T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2025-12-11T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ2026-02-09T00:00:00Z‚ôĒ5‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒ‚ôĒTRUE‚ôĒ0‚ôĒRetain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.‚ôĒHealthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.‚ôĒindustry‚ôĒhigh‚ôĒangry‚ôĒMontana‚ôĒchief medical officer‚ôĒ60‚ôĒLast month, I watched a talented smaller vendor we'd been piloting with abandon their EHR innovation because they simply couldn't afford the cumulative certification expenses‚ÄĒthey were spending more on compliance documentation than on actual product development.
